topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Solar Energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Energy** is the radiant light and heat emitted by the **Sun**, harnessed through two principal conversion pathways: **Photovoltaic (PV) conversion** (direct electricity generation via the photovoltaic effect in semiconductors) and **Solar Thermal conversion** (heat capture for thermal storage, mechanical work, or thermodynamic cycles).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** course *PEPOT757 – Polymers & Photovoltaic Technology*, **Solar Energy** is treated as the **primary energy input** to every photovoltaic device. The photovoltaic industry is, fundamentally, the science of *efficiently capturing, converting, and stabilizing* this incoming electromagnetic radiation into usable electrical energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Solar energy fundamentals are introduced here to establish the *quantitative boundary conditions* (irradiance, spectrum, air mass, geometry) under which any subsequent photovoltaic device must operate. Every equation that follows in Modules 2–5 is parameterized against these boundary values.

## 1.2 The Sun as an Energy Source — Quantitative Description

The Sun behaves approximately as a **blackbody radiator** at an effective surface temperature of **T ≈ 5778 K** (often rounded to **5800 K** for engineering estimation).

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effective Surface Temperature | T | 5778 | K |
| Solar Radius | R⊙ | 6.957 × 10⁸ | m |
| Sun-Earth Mean Distance | d | 1.496 × 10¹¹ | m |
| Total Radiated Power | L⊙ | 3.828 × 10²⁶ | W |

## 1.3 The Solar Constant (I₀) — Most Critical KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Constant (I₀):** The total solar irradiance (integrated over all wavelengths) incident on a surface oriented **perpendicular to the Sun's rays** at the **top of Earth's atmosphere** (i.e., outside the atmosphere, Air Mass Zero — **AM0**), at the **mean Sun–Earth distance**, averaged over one year.
> **Standard Value: I₀ ≈ 1361 W/m²** (accepted modern value; older KTU textbooks may list 1366 W/m² or 1353 W/m²).

### 1.3.1 Derivation Insight (Why this specific number?)

The Sun emits **L☉ = 3.828 × 10²⁶ W** isotropically. At a spherical shell of radius d (Sun–Earth distance), this power is distributed uniformly over a sphere of surface area 4πd².

$$I_0 = \frac{L_\odot}{4\pi d^2}$$

$$I_0 = \frac{3.828 \times 10^{26}}{4\pi \times (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2}$$

$$I_0 \approx 1361 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant ≠ Constant forever:** The term "solar constant" is a historical misnomer. I₀ varies by ±3.4 % over the year due to Earth's elliptical orbit (perihelion in January: ~1414 W/m²; aphelion in July: ~1322 W/m²). This is precisely why photovoltaic datasheets always specify a standard test condition (STC) irradiance of **1000 W/m²** (not 1361 W/m²) — because the *real, ground-level* radiation under AM1.5 is much smaller.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Solar Energy in Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Cosmic Showerhead:** Imagine the Sun is a giant **showerhead** floating in space, spraying packets of light-energy (photons) in every direction. Earth is a tiny ball placed 150 million kilometers away, catching a small fraction of that spray. The solar constant (1361 W/m²) is like measuring how much water lands on a 1 m × 1 m tray held perpendicular to the spray, *before* the atmosphere dampens it. When that tray passes under a cloud (atmosphere), the water weakens — that's why we use **1000 W/m²** as a realistic, daylit, ground-level benchmark.

## 1.5 The Solar Spectrum — From AM0 to AM1.5G

As sunlight traverses Earth's atmosphere, it is **attenuated** and **spectrally filtered** by scattering (Rayleigh & Mie) and absorption (by O₃, H₂O, CO₂). Different atmospheric path lengths therefore produce **different spectra**, denoted by the **Air Mass (AM)** number.

| Air Mass | Definition | Typical Application | Total Irradiance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AM0** | Outside atmosphere (space) | Satellite PV cells | **1361 W/m²** |
| **AM1** | Sun directly overhead (zenith) | Equatorial, midday | ~1050 W/m² |
| **AM1.5G** | Sun at 48.2° from zenith | Standard terrestrial PV (KTU/industry) | **1000 W/m²** |
| **AM1.5D** | Direct beam only | Concentrator PV | ~900 W/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'G' in AM1.5G means 'Global'** (direct + diffuse). **AM1.5D** is the **Direct** component only. **AM1.5** is shorthand for the standard terrestrial spectrum with sun at zenith angle θz ≈ 48.19°, giving a slant path length of 1.5 × the vertical atmosphere thickness.

## 1.6 Why Solar Energy Matters for Photovoltaics

The **spectral distribution**, **intensity**, and **angle of incidence** of solar radiation collectively determine:
- The **short-circuit current (Iₛc)** of a solar cell (proportional to photon flux above bandgap)
- The **fill factor (FF)** and **open-circuit voltage (Vₒc)** (dependent on total irradiance and temperature)
- The **energy yield (kWh)** over the lifetime of a module

Thus, *every* downstream equation in PV technology is referenced to the **AM1.5G, 1000 W/m² standard**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Geometry — The Earth's Coordinate System

To predict the position of the Sun at any time and location on Earth, we use three angles:

### 2.1.1 Declination Angle (δ)
The angle between the Earth's equatorial plane and the line connecting the Earth and Sun centers. It varies from **−23.45°** (Winter Solstice, Dec 21) to **+23.45°** (Summer Solstice, Jun 21).

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

where **n** = day of the year (Jan 1 = 1, Dec 31 = 365).

### 2.1.2 Hour Angle (ω)
The angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian. **ω = 0° at solar noon**, **negative before noon**, **positive after noon**.

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{solar} - 12)$$

where **tₛolar** is the local solar time in hours. Note: **15° per hour** because Earth rotates 360° in 24 h.

### 2.1.3 Latitude (φ)
The angular location north (+) or south (−) of the equator.

### 2.1.4 Zenith Angle (θz) and Altitude Angle (α)
- **Zenith angle (θz):** Angle between the Sun and the local vertical.
- **Altitude angle (α):** Angle between the Sun and the local horizontal. **α + θz = 90°**.

### 2.1.5 Solar Azimuth Angle (γs)
Horizontal angle measured from North to the projection of the Sun's rays on the horizontal plane.

## 2.2 The Air Mass (AM) Formula — Critical Derivation

The **Air Mass** is defined as the ratio of the actual path length of sunlight through the atmosphere to the **minimum (vertical) path length** (Sun at zenith).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Mass Equation:**
> $$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)}$$

For the standard KTU terrestrial spectrum: θz = 48.19° → cos(48.19°) ≈ 0.6664 → **AM = 1/0.6664 ≈ 1.5** ✓

A more refined (Kasten & Young 1989) formula accounting for atmospheric curvature:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z) + 0.50572 \times (96.07995 - \theta_z)^{-1.6364}}$$

## 2.3 Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface (I₀ₕ)

The extraterrestrial irradiance on a horizontal surface at day **n** is:

$$I_{0h} = I_0 \times \left[1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)\right] \times \cos(\theta_z)$$

## 2.4 Solar Radiation on a Tilted Surface — The Transposition Equation

A photovoltaic module mounted at tilt angle **β** from the horizontal receives **total irradiance (I_T)** decomposed into three components:

$$I_T = I_b \cdot R_b + I_d \cdot R_d + (I_b + I_d) \cdot R_r$$

where:
- **I_b** = direct (beam) irradiance on horizontal
- **I_d** = diffuse irradiance on horizontal
- **R_b** = ratio of beam on tilted to beam on horizontal
- **R_d** = ratio of diffuse on tilted to diffuse on horizontal (often ≈ (1 + cos β)/2 for isotropic sky)
- **R_r** = ground-reflected component (often ≈ ρ(1 − cos β)/2, ρ = albedo)

## 2.5 Peak Sun Hours (PSH) — A KTU Favourite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peak Sun Hours** is defined as the equivalent number of hours per day during which solar irradiance is constant at **1000 W/m²** such that the total daily energy equals the actual daily insolation.
> $$\text{PSH} = \frac{\text{Daily Insolation (Wh/m}^2\text{)}}{\text{1000 W/m}^2}$$

Example: If a location receives 5500 Wh/m²/day → PSH = **5.5 h/day**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Solar Energy

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_0 = \dfrac{L_\odot}{4\pi d^2}$ | Solar constant from stellar luminosity | W/m² |
| 2 | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360}{365}(284+n)\right]$ | Solar declination angle | degrees, n = day of year |
| 3 | $\omega = 15^\circ (t_{solar} - 12)$ | Hour angle | degrees |
| 4 | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ | Air Mass (simple) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z + 0.50572(96.07995-\theta_z)^{-1.6364}}$ | Air Mass (Kasten-Young, accurate) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | Zenith angle from spherical trig | dimensionless |
| 7 | $I_{0h} = I_0\left[1+0.033\cos\!\left(\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)\right]\cos\theta_z$ | Extraterrestrial on horizontal | W/m² |
| 8 | $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d R_d + (I_b+I_d)R_r$ | Total on tilted surface | W/m² |
| 9 | $\text{PSH} = \dfrac{H_{day}}{1000}$ | Peak Sun Hours | hours |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{G \cdot A}$ | PV conversion efficiency | dimensionless, G = irradiance (W/m²), A = area (m²) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are the *backbone of every PV system design software* (PVsyst, SAM, RETScreen). The **tilt angle β ≈ |φ|** rule (set module tilt equal to site latitude) optimizes annual energy capture — a derivation you'll see in SECTION 3.

## 2.7 Spectral Distribution & Photovoltaic Relevance

The terrestrial solar spectrum (AM1.5G) is described by the **ASTM G173 standard** and is characterized by:
- **~5 % UV** (300–400 nm)
- **~43 % Visible** (400–700 nm)
- **~52 % Near-Infrared** (700–2500 nm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photon Energy Threshold:** For a semiconductor with bandgap **E_g**, only photons with **hν ≥ E_g** (i.e., λ ≤ 1240/E_g nm) contribute to photocurrent. Photons below this threshold transmit through; above it, the excess energy is lost as heat. This is the **detailed-balance (Shockley-Queisser) limit** and is the fundamental thermodynamic bound for a single-junction solar cell.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Air Mass (AM) from Geometry

### Step 1 — Setup

Consider the Earth as a sphere of radius **R** with a thin atmospheric layer. The Sun's rays traverse this atmosphere along a slant path **L** when the Sun is at zenith angle **θz**.

### Step 2 — Geometric Relation

For a Sun located at "infinity" (parallel rays), the geometry yields:

$$L \cos\theta_z = h$$

where **h** is the effective vertical atmospheric thickness (when Sun is overhead).

### Step 3 — Definition of Air Mass

By definition:

$$AM \equiv \frac{L}{h} = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$

### Step 4 — Verification (Standard Case)

For the standard AM1.5 spectrum:

$$\cos(48.19^\circ) = 0.6664 \implies AM = \frac{1}{0.6664} = 1.5006 \approx 1.5 \;\; \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Solar Constant from Stellar Luminosity

### Step 1 — Sun's Power Emission

The Sun's effective surface temperature **T = 5778 K**, radius **R⊙ = 6.957 × 10⁸ m**.

$$L_\odot = 4\pi R_\odot^2 \cdot \sigma T^4$$

where σ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴**.

### Step 2 — Numerical Substitution

$$L_\odot = 4\pi (6.957 \times 10^8)^2 \cdot (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (5778)^4$$

$$L_\odot = 4\pi \times 4.840 \times 10^{17} \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.119 \times 10^{15}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluation

$$L_\odot \approx 3.828 \times 10^{26} \; \text{W}$$

### Step 4 — Distribution at Earth

$$I_0 = \frac{L_\odot}{4\pi d^2} = \frac{3.828 \times 10^{26}}{4\pi (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2}$$

$$I_0 \approx 1361 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This derivation shows that any alteration to Sun–Earth distance (e.g., the elliptical ±1.7 % variation) directly scales the solar constant. The **eccentricity correction factor** $\left[1 + 0.033 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)\right]$ in Equation 7 above quantifies exactly this.

## 3.3 Derivation: Zenith Angle from Spherical Trigonometry

### Step 1 — Coordinate System

Let the observer be at latitude **φ** on a tilted Earth. The Sun is at declination **δ** and hour angle **ω**.

### Step 2 — Apply the Cosine Rule for a Spherical Triangle

The vertices of the spherical triangle are: **Zenith**, **Pole**, and **Sun**. The sides opposite these vertices are:
- Side opposite Zenith = **θz** (the zenith angle — what we want)
- Side opposite Pole = **90° − δ**
- Side opposite Sun = **90° − φ**

The angle at the **Pole** vertex is the hour angle **ω**.

### Step 3 — Apply Spherical Cosine Rule

$$\cos\theta_z = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos\omega$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Using $\cos(90°-x) = \sin x$ and $\sin(90°-x) = \cos x$

$$\boxed{\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example

For Trivandrum (φ = +8.5°) on the equinox (δ = 0°) at solar noon (ω = 0°):

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(8.5^\circ)\sin(0^\circ) + \cos(8.5^\circ)\cos(0^\circ)\cos(0^\circ)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = 0 + (0.989)(1)(1) = 0.989 \implies \theta_z = 8.5^\circ$$

This confirms that near the equator, at noon on equinox, the Sun is almost overhead — a critical design insight for **Kerala-based PV installations**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Optimal Tilt Angle for Annual Energy Capture

### Step 1 — Problem Statement

For a fixed-tilt module at angle **β** (south-facing in the Northern Hemisphere), find **β** that maximizes annual energy capture.

### Step 2 — Beam Component on Tilted Surface

The beam component ratio:

$$R_b = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z}$$

where **θ** is the angle of incidence on the tilted surface:

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega$$

### Step 3 — Annual Average

For a flat-earth, daily-averaged analysis (neglecting diffuse for this analytical purpose), integrating over the day from sunrise to sunset, the optimal tilt satisfies:

$$\frac{d}{d\beta}\int_{\text{year}} \bar{R}_b \, dn = 0$$

### Step 4 — Result (Standard Engineering Approximation)

$$\boxed{\beta_{opt} \approx |\phi| - 15^\circ \; \text{(summer-heavy load)}, \quad \beta_{opt} \approx |\phi| \; \text{(annual)}, \quad \beta_{opt} \approx |\phi| + 15^\circ \; \text{(winter-heavy load)}}$$

For **Kerala (φ ≈ 8–13° N)**, optimal annual tilt ≈ **10–13°**. This is small because Kerala is near the equator — modules are nearly flat!

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Influence on PV Performance

| Variable | Symbol | Effect on Iₛc | Effect on Vₒc | Effect on P_max |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase irradiance (G) | ↑ G | ∝ G (linear) | ∝ ln(G) (logarithmic) | ∝ G (approx.) |
| Increase temperature | ↑ T | Slight ↑ (E_g decreases) | ↓ (~−2 mV/°C/cell) | ↓ significantly |
| Increase Air Mass | ↑ AM | ↓ (less blue/UV) | Slight ↓ | ↓ |
| Increase cloudiness | ↑ Diffuse % | ↓ (diffuse is less intense) | Slight ↓ | ↓ |
| Increase module tilt to latitude | ↑ β to φ | Depends on season | Depends | Maximizes annual |

## 3.6 Energy Yield Calculation — Worked Example

A 1 kW (STC-rated) PV system in Kerala has the following parameters:
- Module efficiency at STC: η = 18 %
- Module area: A = 1 kW / (1000 W/m² × 0.18) = **5.56 m²**
- Annual average daily PSH for Kerala: ≈ **4.5–5.0 h/day**
- System derate factor (dust, temperature, wiring losses): **0.80**

### Step 1 — Daily Energy Output

$$E_{day} = P_{STC} \times \text{PSH} \times \text{Derate Factor}$$

$$E_{day} = 1 \times 5.0 \times 0.80 = 4.0 \; \text{kWh/day}$$

### Step 2 — Annual Energy Yield

$$E_{year} = 4.0 \times 365 = 1460 \; \text{kWh/year}$$

This is the standard range for a **1 kW residential rooftop system in Kerala** — a number every KTU graduate should be able to reproduce.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
