# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Solar Radiation Components on a Tilted Module

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Sun - 5778 K Blackbody"] --> nodeB["Extraterrestrial Radiation - AM0 - 1361 W per sq m"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Atmospheric Attenuation - Scattering plus Absorption"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Terrestrial Global Radiation - AM1.5G"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Direct Beam - Ib"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Diffuse Sky - Id"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Ground Reflected - Ig"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Tilted PV Module - Angle beta"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["Total Irradiance IT - equals IbRb plus IdRd plus IgRr"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Photocurrent Generation - h nu greater than Eg"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Electrical Output - DC Power"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Solar Geometry Reference

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["Observer at Latitude phi"] --> node2["Zenith Angle theta z"]
    node1 --> node3["Altitude Angle alpha"]
    node1 --> node4["Azimuth Angle gamma s"]
    node5["Sun Position"] --> node6["Declination delta"]
    node5 --> node7["Hour Angle omega"]
    node6 --> node2
    node7 --> node2
    node6 --> node8["Air Mass AM"]
    node2 --> node8
    node8 --> node9["Spectral Distribution"]
    node9 --> node10["Photon Flux on PV Cell"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Solar Energy Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Solar Resource - Irradiance and Spectrum"] --> s2["Module Level - Photovoltaic Conversion"]
    s2 --> s3["DC Power Generation"]
    s3 --> s4["Power Electronics - MPPT and Inverter"]
    s4 --> s5["AC Power Output"]
    s5 --> s6a["Grid Connected Mode"]
    s5 --> s6b["Standalone with Battery"]
    s5 --> s6c["Hybrid with Diesel Backup"]
    s6a --> s7["Load or Utility Grid"]
    s6b --> s7
    s6c --> s7
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Air Mass Concept (Geometric Block View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    g1["Sun at Zenith theta z equals 0"] --> g2["AM equals 1 - Vertical Path"]
    g3["Sun at theta z equals 30 deg"] --> g4["AM equals 1.1547"]
    g5["Sun at theta z equals 48.19 deg"] --> g6["AM equals 1.5 - Standard"]
    g7["Sun at theta z equals 60 deg"] --> g8["AM equals 2.0"]
    g9["Sun at theta z equals 90 deg"] --> g10["AM equals Infinity - Sunrise or Sunset"]
    g1 --> g3 --> g5 --> g7 --> g9
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Photon to Watt

```mermaid
graph TD
    p1["Photon h nu strikes semiconductor"] --> p2{"h nu greater than Eg"}
    p2 -- "Yes" --> p3["Electron Hole Pair Generated"]
    p2 -- "No" --> p4["Photon Lost - Heat or Transmit"]
    p3 --> p5["Charge Separation by p-n Junction"]
    p5 --> p6["Photocurrent Iph Collected"]
    p6 --> p7["Terminal Voltage V across Load"]
    p7 --> p8["DC Power equals Iph times V"]
    p8 --> p9["Fill Factor and Conversion Efficiency eta"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for KTU students:** Mermaid diagram 1 maps the *physical path* of solar radiation from emission to electricity. Mermaid diagram 4 visualizes how the **Air Mass number** increases geometrically as the Sun moves away from zenith — this is the *single most important* atmospheric parameter for PV system sizing in Kerala, where the Sun spends most of the year between AM1 and AM2.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** [3 Marks]

**Define Solar Constant. Mention its standard value and explain why the standard test condition (STC) irradiance for PV modules is fixed at 1000 W/m² instead of 1361 W/m².**

**Model Answer:**
The **Solar Constant (I₀)** is the total solar irradiance incident per unit area perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the top of Earth's atmosphere (AM0), at mean Sun–Earth distance. **Standard value: I₀ ≈ 1361 W/m²**.

The **STC irradiance of 1000 W/m²** is chosen because:
1. **1361 W/m² is the extraterrestrial value**, never observed at ground level.
2. **Atmospheric attenuation** (Rayleigh scattering, Mie scattering, absorption by O₃/H₂O/CO₂) reduces the actual ground-level peak to ≈ 1000 W/m².
3. **AM1.5G** is the internationally standardized terrestrial spectrum (ASTM G173), with **1000 W/m²** total irradiance.
4. Using a single, repeatable benchmark enables **fair comparison** of PV modules across manufacturers worldwide.

---

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** [3 Marks]

**Define Air Mass (AM). Why is AM1.5 chosen as the standard terrestrial spectrum for PV module characterization?**

**Model Answer:**
**Air Mass (AM)** is the ratio of the actual path length of sunlight through the atmosphere to the vertical path length (Sun at zenith). Mathematically, **AM = 1/cos(θz)**.

AM1.5 is the standard because:
1. It represents the **average atmospheric path length** for mid-latitude locations (≈ 48.2° latitude) where the majority of global PV deployment occurs.
2. It corresponds to a **realistic, sunlit, ground-level spectrum** — not the space spectrum (AM0) or the equator-at-noon spectrum (AM1).
3. It provides **reproducibility** for measuring Iₛc, Vₒc, and η in laboratory settings.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 1):** Students often lose 2–3 marks in *every* question by:
> 1. Forgetting to specify the **AM** value when citing the 1000 W/m² STC.
> 2. Using **1366 W/m²** without stating it as the *older* accepted value (1361 W/m² is current).
> 3. Skipping the **declination equation** derivation when asked about seasonal variation.
> 4. Mixing up **solar time** and **local standard time** in the hour angle equation.
> Always cite **AM1.5G, 1000 W/m², 25 °C** as the *complete* STC triplet.

---

### Question B-A. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** [14 Marks — Internal Choice Option A]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the components of **solar radiation** reaching the Earth's surface. Define the **Solar Constant** and derive its value from first principles using the Sun's luminosity and Earth–Sun distance.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Define **Air Mass (AM)**. Derive the relation **AM = 1/cos(θz)**. A PV module is installed at a location where the Sun's zenith angle is **60°**. Calculate: **(i)** the Air Mass number, and **(ii)** the corresponding spectral classification (AM0, AM1, AM1.5, AM2, or other).

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Diagram:** Refer to Mermaid Diagram 1 in SECTION 4.

**Definition of Solar Constant:** Solar Constant is the total solar irradiance on a unit surface oriented perpendicular to the Sun's rays, located at the top of Earth's atmosphere, at mean Sun–Earth distance. **I₀ ≈ 1361 W/m²**.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: The Sun's luminosity is given by Stefan-Boltzmann law:

$$L_\odot = 4\pi R_\odot^2 \sigma T^4$$

Step 2: Substitute R⊙ = 6.957 × 10⁸ m, T = 5778 K, σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴:

$$L_\odot = 4\pi (6.957 \times 10^8)^2 (5.67 \times 10^{-8})(5778)^4 \approx 3.828 \times 10^{26} \; \text{W}$$

Step 3: This power is distributed over a sphere of radius d = 1.496 × 10¹¹ m:

$$I_0 = \frac{L_\odot}{4\pi d^2} = \frac{3.828 \times 10^{26}}{4\pi (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2} \approx 1361 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Definition:** Air Mass is the ratio of slant path length to vertical path length of sunlight through the atmosphere.

**Derivation (see SECTION 3.1):** AM = 1/cos(θz).

**Calculations:**

(i) For θz = 60°:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(60^\circ)} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2.0$$

(ii) **Spectral Classification: AM2.0** — corresponds to Sun at 60° from zenith; commonly occurs in morning/evening hours or in winter at mid-to-high latitudes.

---

### Question B-B. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** [14 Marks — Internal Choice Option B]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain **solar geometry** with reference to: declination angle (δ), hour angle (ω), latitude (φ), zenith angle (θz), and altitude angle (α). Derive the **declination angle formula** and compute δ for **March 21** and **December 21**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Air Mass concept** and the **AM1.5G standard terrestrial spectrum**. A 1 kW PV system is installed in **Kerala (latitude ≈ 10° N)**. The annual average **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** for this location is **5.0 h/day**. The system derate factor is **0.80**. Calculate the **expected annual energy yield** in kWh.

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Solar Geometry Definitions:**
- **φ (latitude):** angular position N/S of equator.
- **δ (declination):** angle between Sun–Earth line and equatorial plane.
- **ω (hour angle):** angular displacement of Sun east/west of local meridian; ω = 0° at solar noon, ±15° per hour.
- **θz (zenith angle):** angle from local vertical to Sun.
- **α (altitude angle):** angle from local horizontal to Sun; α = 90° − θz.

**Declination Derivation:**

The Earth's axis is tilted 23.45° relative to the orbital plane. The declination varies sinusoidally over the year:

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

**Computation:**

For **March 21** (n = 80; Jan=31, Feb=28, Mar 21 → 31+28+21 = 80):

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 80)\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin(359.18^\circ) \approx 0^\circ$$

For **December 21** (n = 355):

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 355)\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin(629.59^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \sin(269.59^\circ) \approx -23.45^\circ$$

> This confirms: **March 21 = Spring Equinox (δ = 0°)**; **December 21 = Winter Solstice (δ = −23.45°)**.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Air Mass Concept** (see SECTION 1.5 and 2.2): AM = 1/cos(θz); AM1.5G is the standard terrestrial spectrum with 1000 W/m² total irradiance.

**Annual Energy Yield Calculation:**

Step 1 — Daily Energy:

$$E_{day} = P_{STC} \times \text{PSH} \times \eta_{derate}$$

$$E_{day} = 1 \; \text{kW} \times 5.0 \; \text{h/day} \times 0.80 = 4.0 \; \text{kWh/day}$$

Step 2 — Annual Energy:

$$E_{year} = 4.0 \times 365 = \mathbf{1460 \; \text{kWh/year}}$$

Step 3 — Specific Yield:

$$Y_{specific} = \frac{E_{year}}{P_{STC}} = \frac{1460}{1} = 1460 \; \text{kWh/kWp}$$

This specific yield of ~1460 kWh/kWp/year is **typical for Kerala rooftop PV** and matches MNRE benchmark data.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solar Energy**

- ☑ **Solar Constant** I₀ = **1361 W/m²** at AM0; older texts use 1366 W/m².
- ☑ **STC**: **AM1.5G, 1000 W/m², 25 °C cell temperature** — the universal benchmark for PV modules.
- ☑ **Air Mass** AM = 1/cos(θz); **AM1.5** corresponds to θz ≈ **48.19°**.
- ☑ **Declination angle** δ ranges from **−23.45°** (Dec 21) to **+23.45°** (Jun 21); zero at equinoxes.
- ☑ **Hour angle** ω = 15° × (solar time − 12); positive afternoon, negative morning.
- ☑ **Zenith angle formula**: cos(θz) = sin(φ)sin(δ) + cos(φ)cos(δ)cos(ω).
- ☑ **Optimal annual tilt** β_opt ≈ **|φ|**; for Kerala, β_opt ≈ **10–13°**.
- ☑ **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** = Daily Insolation (Wh/m²) ÷ 1000.
- ☑ **Kerala-specific** PSH ≈ **4.5–5.0 h/day**; specific yield ≈ **1400–1500 kWh/kWp/year**.
- ☑ **Solar spectrum** ASTM G173: ~5 % UV, ~43 % Visible, ~52 % NIR.
- ☑ **Photocurrent** requires **hν ≥ E_g**; λ_threshold = **1240/E_g(nm)**.
- ☑ **Albedo (ρ)** for typical ground ≈ 0.2; fresh snow ≈ 0.8.
- ☑ **Conversion efficiency** η = P_out / (G × A).
- ☑ **Always cite complete STC** (AM1.5G + 1000 W/m² + 25 °C) in numerical answers to avoid losing marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Neutrinos
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Neutrinos

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Solar Neutrinos** are **electron-flavor neutrinos ($\nu_e$)** produced as a direct, inescapable by-product of the **thermonuclear fusion reactions** occurring in the core of the Sun. They are elementary, electrically neutral, weakly interacting leptons that travel essentially at the **speed of light ($c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s)** and reach Earth roughly **8 minutes 20 seconds** after being emitted from the solar core, since they escape the dense solar interior almost unimpeded (mean free path $\gg$ solar radius).

Solar neutrinos are fundamentally a signature of the **proton-proton (pp) chain** (dominant, $\approx 99\%$) and the **Carbon-Nitrogen-Oxygen (CNO) cycle** ($\approx 1\%$ in our Sun), which together are the two principal mechanisms of stellar nucleosynthesis in low-to-medium mass stars like the Sun ($M_\odot = 1.989 \times 10^{30}$ kg).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ghost Messengers" of the Sun

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine the Sun as a giant, roaring furnace deep inside an opaque steel vault. Photons of light get bounced, absorbed, and re-emitted millions of times — it can take them **~100,000 to 170,000 years** to escape the solar radiative zone. **Solar neutrinos, however, behave like silent ghosts — they walk straight out of the vault within seconds, carrying unaltered, real-time information about what is happening *right now* inside the core.** They are, in essence, the only direct probe of the solar interior available to experimental physics.

This is precisely why solar neutrinos are studied — they provide the most **direct, model-independent verification** of stellar fusion theory and quantum field theory beyond the Standard Model.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (KTU Reference)

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Solar mass | $M_\odot$ | $\mathbf{1.989 \times 10^{30}}$ kg |
| Solar luminosity (photon) | $L_\odot$ | $\mathbf{3.828 \times 10^{26}}$ W |
| Core temperature | $T_c$ | $\mathbf{1.57 \times 10^7}$ K |
| Core density | $\rho_c$ | $\mathbf{1.62 \times 10^5}$ kg/m$^3$ |
| Total pp-chain $\nu_e$ flux at Earth | $\Phi_\nu$ | $\mathbf{6.0 \times 10^{10}}$ cm$^{-2}$ s$^{-1}$ |
| Neutrino mass upper bound (eV) | $m_\nu$ | $\mathbf{< 1.1}$ eV (total) |
| Fermi coupling constant | $G_F$ | $\mathbf{1.166 \times 10^{-5}}$ GeV$^{-2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Source Mechanisms — Where the Neutrinos Come From

### 2.1.1 The Proton-Proton (pp) Chain (Dominant, $\approx 99\%$)

The pp-chain is a series of fusion steps in which hydrogen is converted into helium, releasing energy ($\approx 26.732$ MeV per final $^4$He) and neutrinos at several branches:

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step logic of why neutrinos appear:**
> 1. Two protons must overcome the **Coulomb barrier** via quantum tunneling to fuse.
> 2. To conserve **lepton number, charge, and energy**, one proton must $\beta^+$-decay into a neutron, emitting a **positron ($e^+$)** and an **electron-neutrino ($\nu_e$)** *simultaneously* with fusion.
> 3. The neutrino is the **only** particle that escapes directly; everything else is thermalized into photons.

Key neutrino-producing reactions:

$$
p + p \rightarrow d + e^+ + \nu_e \quad (E_{\nu}^{\max} = 0.420 \text{ MeV})
$$

$$
p + e^- + p \rightarrow d + \nu_e \quad (\text{pep reaction}, E_\nu = 1.442 \text{ MeV})
$$

$$
{}^7\text{Be} + e^- \rightarrow {}^7\text{Li} + \nu_e \quad (E_\nu = 0.862 \text{ MeV},\ 0.384 \text{ MeV})
$$

$$
{}^8\text{B} \rightarrow {}^8\text{Be}^* + e^+ + \nu_e \quad (E_{\nu}^{\max} = 14.06 \text{ MeV})
$$

### 2.1.2 The CNO Cycle ($\approx 1\%$, dominant in stars $>1.3\,M_\odot$)

Uses C, N, O as catalysts:

$$
{}^{13}\text{N} \rightarrow {}^{13}\text{C} + e^+ + \nu_e \quad (E_{\nu}^{\max} = 1.199 \text{ MeV})
$$

$$
{}^{15}\text{O} \rightarrow {}^{15}\text{N} + e^+ + \nu_e \quad (E_{\nu}^{\max} = 1.732 \text{ MeV})
$$

## 2.2 Neutrino Oscillations (Why Solar Neutrinos "Disappear")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea:** A neutrino produced as $\nu_e$ in the Sun does *not* remain $\nu_e$ during its $1.5 \times 10^{11}$ m journey to Earth. Quantum mechanically, it is a coherent superposition of mass eigenstates $\nu_1, \nu_2, \nu_3$, and these mass eigenstates evolve with different phases, so flavor composition oscillates.

The flavor eigenstates are related to mass eigenstates by the **PMNS (Pontecorvo–Maki–Nakagawa–Sakata) matrix** $U$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} \nu_e \\ \nu_\mu \\ \nu_\tau \end{pmatrix} = U \begin{pmatrix} \nu_1 \\ \nu_2 \\ \nu_3 \end{pmatrix}, \quad U = U_{23} U_{13} U_{12}
$$

The **standard 2-flavor vacuum oscillation probability** (KTU high-yield):

$$
P(\nu_e \rightarrow \nu_\mu) = \sin^2(2\theta) \sin^2\!\left(\frac{1.27\,\Delta m^2\, L}{E}\right)
$$

where $L$ is in meters, $E$ in MeV, $\Delta m^2$ in eV$^2$ (this 1.27 arises from natural-unit conversion: $\hbar c \approx 197$ MeV·fm).

## 2.3 The MSW (Mikheyev–Smirnov–Wolfenstein) Effect

When neutrinos travel through dense matter, **coherent forward scattering off electrons** modifies the effective mixing angle. The effective mixing angle $\theta_M$ in matter of electron number density $N_e$ is:

$$
\sin^2(2\theta_M) = \frac{\sin^2(2\theta)}{\left[\cos(2\theta) - \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,G_F\, N_e\, E}{\Delta m^2}\right]^2 + \sin^2(2\theta)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Resonance condition** (where the denominator of $\cos 2\theta$ vanishes) occurs when $2\sqrt{2}\,G_F\, N_e\, E \cos(2\theta) = \Delta m^2 \cos(2\theta)$ — at this point, $\sin^2(2\theta_M) = 1$ and flavor conversion becomes **maximal**. This is exactly what happens adiabatically in the solar core for $\nu_e$ with energies above $\sim 2$ MeV.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\Phi_\nu = \frac{L_\odot \, f_\nu}{\langle E_\nu \rangle \, 4\pi d^2}$ | Neutrino flux at Earth | cm$^{-2}$ s$^{-1}$ |
| $P_{\text{osc}} = \sin^2(2\theta)\sin^2\!\left(\frac{1.27\,\Delta m^2 L}{E}\right)$ | 2-flavor vacuum oscillation | dimensionless |
| $L_{\text{osc}} = \frac{4\pi E}{\Delta m^2} \approx 2.48 \frac{E[\text{MeV}]}{\Delta m^2[\text{eV}^2]}$ km | Oscillation length | km |
| $\sin^2(2\theta_M)$ (MSW) | Effective mixing in matter | dimensionless |
| $Q_{\text{pp}} = 26.732$ MeV | Energy per $^4$He (pp chain) | MeV |
| $\langle E_\nu \rangle_{^8\text{B}} \approx 6.5$ MeV | Mean $^8$B neutrino energy | MeV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Although neutrinos are not directly used in photovoltaic (PV) devices, **understanding the solar energy generation mechanism is essential for designing PV systems that match the solar spectrum (AM1.5G) which results from these same fusion processes.** The $L_\odot$ that PV modules intercept is the photon counterpart of the $L_\nu$ that neutrino detectors measure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Vacuum Oscillation Probability $P(\nu_e \to \nu_\mu)$

We start with the flavor basis $\nu_e, \nu_\mu$ as superpositions of mass eigenstates $\nu_1, \nu_2$:

$$
|\nu_e(0)\rangle = \cos\theta\,|\nu_1\rangle + \sin\theta\,|\nu_2\rangle
$$

$$
|\nu_\mu(0)\rangle = -\sin\theta\,|\nu_1\rangle + \cos\theta\,|\nu_2\rangle
$$

This is the 2-flavor PMNS rotation by angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Time evolution of mass eigenstates.** Each mass eigenstate propagates with its own rest-energy eigenvalue:

$$
|\nu_i(t)\rangle = e^{-i E_i t / \hbar}\,|\nu_i(0)\rangle, \quad E_i = \sqrt{p^2 c^2 + m_i^2 c^4} \approx E + \frac{m_i^2 c^4}{2E}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $E_2 - E_1$.** Using $L \approx ct$ (ultra-relativistic):

$$
E_2 - E_1 \approx \frac{\Delta m^2 c^4}{2E}, \quad \Delta m^2 \equiv m_2^2 - m_1^2
$$

**Step 3 — Express $|\nu_e(t)\rangle$.** Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|\nu_e(t)\rangle &= \cos\theta\,e^{-iE_1 t/\hbar}\,|\nu_1\rangle + \sin\theta\,e^{-iE_2 t/\hbar}\,|\nu_2\rangle \\
&= e^{-iE_1 t/\hbar}\!\left[\cos\theta\,|\nu_1\rangle + \sin\theta\,e^{-i\Delta m^2 c^4 t/(2E\hbar)}\,|\nu_2\rangle\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Project onto $\nu_\mu$.** Using $\langle\nu_\mu|\nu_1\rangle = -\sin\theta$ and $\langle\nu_\mu|\nu_2\rangle = \cos\theta$:

$$
\langle\nu_\mu|\nu_e(t)\rangle = e^{-iE_1 t/\hbar}\!\left[-\sin\theta\cos\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta\,e^{-i\phi}\right]
$$

where the dynamical phase is $\phi = \dfrac{\Delta m^2 c^4 L}{2E\hbar c}$.

**Step 5 — Compute the probability.** The squared modulus gives:

$$
P(\nu_e \to \nu_\mu) = \left|\langle\nu_\mu|\nu_e(t)\rangle\right|^2 = \sin^2(2\theta)\,\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Convert $\phi$ to convenient units.** Using $\hbar c = 197.327$ MeV·fm:

$$
\frac{\phi}{2} = \frac{\Delta m^2 [\text{eV}^2] \cdot L[\text{m}]}{4 E[\text{MeV}]} \cdot \frac{(\hbar c)^2 \cdot 10^{15}}{\hbar c} \Rightarrow 1.27\,\frac{\Delta m^2 L}{E}
$$

**Final KTU-ready expression:**

$$
\boxed{P(\nu_e \to \nu_\mu) = \sin^2(2\theta)\,\sin^2\!\left(1.27\,\frac{\Delta m^2 L}{E}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Setting $L = L_{\text{osc}}/2$ gives the first maximum. The phenomenon is purely quantum — no classical analog exists.

## 3.2 Derivation: Solar Neutrino Flux at Earth

The total solar photon luminosity is $L_\odot = 3.828 \times 10^{26}$ W. Every 4 protons consumed release $Q = 26.732$ MeV of energy, of which $\sim 2\%$ is carried by neutrinos. The pp-chain rate of $^4$He formation in the Sun:

$$
\dot{N}_{^4\text{He}} = \frac{L_\odot}{Q - L_\nu/Q \cdot Q} = \frac{0.98\, L_\odot}{Q}
$$

Plugging values:

$$
\dot{N}_{^4\text{He}} = \frac{0.98 \times 3.828 \times 10^{26}}{26.732 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 8.78 \times 10^{37}\ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

Each $^4$He corresponds to **2 pp neutrinos**, so the total $\nu_e$ production rate is:

$$
\dot{N}_\nu = 2\,\dot{N}_{^4\text{He}} \approx 1.76 \times 10^{38}\ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

The Earth is at $d = 1\,\text{AU} = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m, so the flux is:

$$
\Phi_\nu = \frac{\dot{N}_\nu}{4\pi d^2} = \frac{1.76 \times 10^{38}}{4\pi (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2} \approx 6.25 \times 10^{14}\ \text{m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}
$$

Converting to cm$^{-2}$ s$^{-1}$:

$$
\boxed{\Phi_\nu \approx 6.0 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}}
$$

This means **about 60 billion solar neutrinos pass through every cm$^2$ of your body every second**, with virtually no interaction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The pp-Chain Neutrino Production Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["P1 P PLUS P"] --> B["DEUTERON D E PLUS NEUTRAL V"]
    C["P PLUS P PLUS E"] --> B
    B --> D["HELIUM 3 PLUS GAMMA"]
    D --> E["HELIUM 4 PLUS 2P LOOP END"]
    D --> F["BERYLLIUM 7 PLUS GAMMA"]
    F --> G["LITHIUM 7 PLUS NEUTRAL V BE BRANCH"]
    F --> H["BORON 8 PLUS GAMMA"]
    H --> I["BERYLLIUM 8 STAR PLUS E PLUS NEUTRAL V B8 BRANCH"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the three principal branches of the pp-chain: **pp-I (dominant, no high-energy $\nu$)**, **pp-II (produces $^7$Be $\nu$ at 0.862 MeV)**, and **pp-III (produces $^8$B $\nu$ up to 14 MeV)**. Only the latter two contribute the high-energy neutrinos detectable by water-Cherenkov experiments.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Solar Neutrino "Problem" and Its Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STANDARD SOLAR MODEL 1960S"] -->|PREDICTS 35 SNU| P1["HOMESTAKE MINE EXPERIMENT 1968"]
    P1 -->|MEASURES 8 SNU| S2["SOLAR NEUTRINO PROBLEM"]
    S2 -->|PROMPT HINT| A1["NEUTRINO OSCILLATION HYPOTHESIS"]
    S2 -->|ALTERNATIVE| A2["STELLAR MODEL REVISION REJECTED"]
    A1 --> T1["SNO 2001 MEASURES NC AND CC"]
    T1 --> T2["TOTAL FLUX MATCHES SSM"]
    T2 --> R1["OSCILLATION CONFIRMED"]
    A1 --> T3["KAMIOKANDE 1996 REAL TIME"]
    T3 --> R1
    R1 --> P2["PMNS MATRIX PHYSICS BEYOND SM"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Detection Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUN["SOLAR CORE FUSION"] -->|EMITTED| NEB["NEUTRINO BEAM MIXED FLAVORS"]
    NEB --> D1["RADIOCHEMICAL CL 37 AR 37 HOMESTAKE"]
    NEB --> D2["WATER CHERENKOV E SCATTER KAMIOKANDE SUPER K"]
    NEB --> D3["HEAVY WATER D2O SNO NC PLUS CC"]
    NEB --> D4["SCINTILLATOR BX BORAGErino GALLEX GNO SAGE"]
    D1 -->|SNU| RES["OSCILLATION PARAMETERS THETA12 DELTA M21 SQUARED"]
    D2 --> RES
    D3 --> RES
    D4 --> RES
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define solar neutrinos. Why are they important for solar physics?**
*Model Answer (3 marks):*
Solar neutrinos are electron-flavor neutrinos ($\nu_e$) produced as by-products of thermonuclear fusion reactions in the solar core, primarily the **proton-proton (pp) chain** and the **CNO cycle**. They are important because, unlike photons, they escape the solar interior almost instantaneously (within $\sim 8.3$ min) without scattering, providing a **direct, real-time probe of the core** where fusion occurs. (3 marks)

**Q2. What is meant by the "solar neutrino problem"?**
*Model Answer (3 marks):*
The solar neutrino problem refers to the long-standing discrepancy (observed from 1968 to 2001) between the **predicted electron-neutrino flux** from the Standard Solar Model (SSM), e.g., $\sim 35$ SNU at Homestake, and the **experimentally measured flux**, which was only $\sim 1/3$ of the predicted value. The problem was ultimately resolved by the discovery of **neutrino flavor oscillations** — solar $\nu_e$ convert into $\nu_\mu$ and $\nu_\tau$ en route to Earth. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the 2-flavor vacuum oscillation probability for solar neutrinos. **(7 marks)**

*Step-by-step Model Solution:*
1. Express the flavor eigenstates as superpositions of mass eigenstates: $|\nu_e\rangle = \cos\theta|\nu_1\rangle + \sin\theta|\nu_2\rangle$ and $|\nu_\mu\rangle = -\sin\theta|\nu_1\rangle + \cos\theta|\nu_2\rangle$. **(1 mark)**
2. Each mass eigenstate evolves as $e^{-iE_i t/\hbar}$ with $E_i \approx E + m_i^2 c^4/(2E)$. **(1 mark)**
3. Compute the phase difference $\phi = (E_2 - E_1)t/\hbar = \dfrac{\Delta m^2 c^4 L}{2E\hbar c}$. **(1 mark)**
4. Compute $\langle\nu_\mu|\nu_e(t)\rangle = \sin\theta\cos\theta(e^{-i\phi} - 1)$ (up to global phase). **(2 marks)**
5. Take the modulus squared: $P = \sin^2(2\theta)\sin^2(\phi/2)$. **(1 mark)**
6. Convert units: $P = \sin^2(2\theta)\sin^2\!\left(1.27\,\Delta m^2 L / E\right)$ with $L$ in m, $E$ in MeV, $\Delta m^2$ in eV$^2$. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** Explain the MSW effect and its significance for solar neutrino oscillations. **(7 marks)**

*Step-by-step Model Solution:*
1. Neutrinos propagating through matter undergo **coherent forward scattering** off electrons (only $\nu_e$ feels this via charged-current). **(1 mark)**
2. This adds a matter potential $V = \sqrt{2}\,G_F N_e$ to the Hamiltonian, modifying the effective mixing. **(1 mark)**
3. The effective mixing angle in matter satisfies: $\sin^2(2\theta_M) = \dfrac{\sin^2(2\theta)}{[\cos(2\theta) - 2\sqrt{2}G_F N_e E/\Delta m^2]^2 + \sin^2(2\theta)}$. **(2 marks)**
4. **Resonance** occurs at the density where $\cos(2\theta) = 2\sqrt{2}G_F N_e E/\Delta m^2$, giving $\theta_M = \pi/4$ and maximal conversion. **(1 mark)**
5. In the adiabatically-evolving solar density profile, neutrinos produced at higher density than the resonance pass through it and emerge predominantly as $\nu_2$. **(1 mark)**
6. This explains the **energy-dependent suppression** observed by SNO/Super-K for high-energy $^8$B neutrinos and the **day-night asymmetry**. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the proton-proton (pp) chain with its three branches, listing the neutrino sources and their energies. **(7 marks)**

*Step-by-step Model Solution:*
1. **Primary step:** $p + p \to d + e^+ + \nu_e$ (continuum, $E_{\max} = 0.420$ MeV). **(1 mark)**
2. $d + p \to {}^3\text{He} + \gamma$, then ${}^3\text{He} + {}^3\text{He} \to {}^4\text{He} + 2p$ (closes **pp-I branch**, no high-E $\nu$). **(1 mark)**
3. **pp-II branch:** ${}^3\text{He} + {}^4\text{He} \to {}^7\text{Be} + \gamma$, then ${}^7\text{Be} + e^- \to {}^7\text{Li} + \nu_e$ (mono-energetic, 0.862 MeV [90%], 0.384 MeV [10%]), then ${}^7\text{Li} + p \to 2\,{}^4\text{He}$. **(2 marks)**
4. **pp-III branch:** ${}^7\text{Be} + p \to {}^8\text{B} + \gamma$, then ${}^8\text{B} \to {}^8\text{Be}^* + e^+ + \nu_e$ (continuum up to 14.06 MeV). **(1 mark)**
5. The **pep reaction** $p + e^- + p \to d + \nu_e$ produces mono-energetic 1.442 MeV neutrinos. **(1 mark)**
6. **Energy bookkeeping:** $\sim$ 26.732 MeV per $^4$He; of this, $\sim$ 2% ($\sim$ 0.6 MeV) is carried by neutrinos that escape, the rest thermalizes into photons. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** With neat schematics, describe the Homestake, Kamiokande, and SNO experiments and their roles in resolving the solar neutrino problem. **(7 marks)**

*Step-by-step Model Solution:*
1. **Homestake (1968, Davis, USA):** Used 615 tons of C$_2$Cl$_4$ (perchloroethylene). Reaction: $\nu_e + {}^{37}\text{Cl} \to {}^{37}\text{Ar} + e^-$. Measured $\sim 2.56$ SNU vs. SSM prediction $\sim 8$ SNU — the **original deficit**. (Radiochemical, threshold 0.814 MeV.) **(2 marks)**
2. **Kamiokande / Super-Kamiokande (Japan):** 50,000-ton water Cherenkov detector. Reaction: $\nu_e + e^- \to \nu_e + e^-$ (elastic scattering). Directional correlation with the Sun confirmed solar origin. Threshold $\sim 5$ MeV. Confirmed deficit, but with **real-time, imaging** detection. **(2 marks)**
3. **SNO (Sudbury, Canada, 2001):** Used 1,000 tons of **heavy water D$_2$O**. Three channels:
   - CC: $\nu_e + d \to p + p + e^-$ (sensitive to $\nu_e$ only),
   - NC: $\nu_x + d \to p + n + \nu_x$ (sensitive to **all flavors**),
   - ES: $\nu_x + e^- \to \nu_x + e^-$. **(2 marks)**
4. SNO showed that the **NC flux matched SSM predictions**, proving the missing $\nu_e$ had oscillated into $\nu_\mu$ and $\nu_\tau$. This **definitively resolved** the solar neutrino problem and earned the 2015 Nobel Prize (Kajita & McDonald, shared with atmospheric $\nu$ oscillations). **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing $\nu_e$ flux with total $\nu$ flux:** Many students write $6 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-2}$s$^{-1}$ as "neutrino flux" without specifying that this is the *pp* $\nu_e$ flux. The $^8$B flux is $\sim 5 \times 10^6$ cm$^{-2}$s$^{-1}$, four orders of magnitude smaller.
> 2. **Mixing up SNU and counts:** 1 SNU = $10^{-36}$ captures per target atom per second. Forgetting this unit costs full marks on numerical questions.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\nu_e$-only CC channel in SNO:** Students often say "SNO detected all flavors" — the **NC** channel does, not the CC. The CC measures only $\nu_e$, which is *why* the deficit appeared there.
> 4. **Sign error in MSW denominator:** The matter correction has a *minus* sign $\cos(2\theta) - 2\sqrt{2}G_F N_e E/\Delta m^2$. Writing $+$ instead will fail the resonance-condition check.
> 5. **Skipping the phase unit conversion:** Always show the explicit step from $\hbar c$ to obtain the 1.27 prefactor — direct jump to the boxed formula loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Solar neutrinos** are $\nu_e$ from pp-chain and CNO-cycle fusion in the solar core.
- **Total flux at Earth** $\approx \mathbf{6 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}}$ (pp neutrinos dominant).
- **Two production regimes:** continuous spectrum (pp, $^8$B, CNO) and mono-energetic lines (pep, $^7$Be).
- **Energy of neutrinos:** pp $\le 0.420$ MeV; $^7$Be $= 0.862$ MeV; $^8$B $\le 14.06$ MeV; CNO $\le 1.74$ MeV.
- **Solar neutrino problem:** Observed $\nu_e$ flux was $\sim 1/3$ of SSM prediction (1968–2001).
- **Resolution:** Neutrino flavor oscillations governed by the PMNS matrix and the MSW effect in matter.
- **Oscillation formula:** $P = \sin^2(2\theta)\sin^2(1.27\,\Delta m^2 L/E)$ — must know the **1.27** unit conversion.
- **Mixing parameters:** $\theta_{12} \approx 33.4^\circ$, $\Delta m^2_{21} \approx 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ eV$^2$ — the *solar* parameters.
- **MSW resonance** explains why high-E $^8$B $\nu_e$ are more suppressed than low-E pp $\nu_e$.
- **Key experiments:** Homestake (radiochemical, 1968), Kamiokande/Super-K (water Cherenkov), SNO (heavy water, 3 channels), Gallex/SAGE (Ga, low-threshold radiochemical), Borexino (real-time sub-MeV spectroscopy).
- **Connection to PV technology:** Although neutrinos are not harvested, the **same pp-chain physics** dictates the **solar spectrum (AM1.5G)** that PV modules convert. Understanding solar energy generation grounds the engineering rationale for photovoltaic device design.
- **Nobel Prize 2015:** Kajita (Super-K, atmospheric $\nu$) and McDonald (SNO, solar $\nu$) for the discovery of neutrino oscillations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiant Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Radiant Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Radiant Power** (also called **Radiant Flux**, denoted by the symbol $\Phi$ or $P$) is defined as the total quantity of electromagnetic energy (light, heat, UV, IR, etc.) emitted, transferred, or received per unit time. It is a fundamental radiometric quantity that quantifies the rate at which radiant energy flows.

$$P = \frac{dQ}{dt}$$

where:
- $P$ = Radiant Power (in **Watts, W**)
- $Q$ = Radiant Energy (in **Joules, J**)
- $t$ = Time (in **seconds, s**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):** *Radiant power is the time rate of flow of radiant energy. It is the energy per unit time radiated, reflected, transmitted, or received in the form of electromagnetic waves. Its SI unit is the watt (W = J·s⁻¹).*

In the context of **photovoltaic technology**, radiant power from the Sun (a.k.a. solar radiant power or solar irradiance when normalized to area) is the primary input energy that a PV cell converts into useful electrical energy.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water hose** connected to a tap:
- The **water** flowing out is analogous to **radiant energy** ($Q$).
- The **rate at which water pours out per second** (litres/second) is analogous to **radiant power** ($P$).
- If you spread that water flow over a wide **bigger bucket (larger area)**, the *intensity* per unit area drops — exactly as **irradiance** ($G$) relates to radiant power.

**Plain-English Statement:**
> "Radiant power is simply *how fast* light energy is being delivered — like measuring the flow rate of a stream, but instead of water, you're measuring packets of electromagnetic energy called photons."

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants Used in Photovoltaics & Radiometry:**

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar Constant (AM 0) | $G_{sc}$ | **1361 W·m⁻²** | Total radiant power density at Earth's outer atmosphere |
| Peak AM1.5G Irradiance | $G$ | **1000 W·m⁻²** | Standard terrestrial test condition (STC) for PV modules |
| Planck Constant | $h$ | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** | Quantum of radiant energy per photon |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J·K⁻¹** | Governs blackbody spectral distribution |
| Speed of Light | $c$ | **2.998 × 10⁸ m·s⁻¹** | Connects wavelength and frequency |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Radiant Power

To fully understand radiant power in PV systems, we must examine its hierarchical decomposition:

### A. Spectral Radiant Power
Different wavelengths carry different amounts of energy. The Sun does not emit a single wavelength but a continuum.

$$P_{\lambda} = \frac{dP}{d\lambda} \quad [\text{W·nm}^{-1}]$$

The **total** radiant power is recovered by integrating across the spectrum:

$$P = \int_{0}^{\infty} P_{\lambda}\, d\lambda$$

### B. From Radiant Power to Irradiance
A PV cell of finite area $A$ intercepts radiant power distributed over its surface. The *intensity* per unit area is **Irradiance** $G$:

$$G = \frac{P}{A} \quad [\text{W·m}^{-2}]$$

This is the metric PV engineers actually care about because it tells us *how much* solar energy a square metre of panel receives.

### C. Blackbody Foundation (Why the Sun Radiates)
The Sun behaves approximately as a **blackbody** at temperature $T \approx 5778$ K. Stefan–Boltzmann's Law states:

$$P = \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

where $\sigma$ = **5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** (Stefan–Boltzmann constant).

### D. Photon Energy Link
Each photon of frequency $\nu$ carries quantum energy:

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Therefore, the number of photons per second in a radiant power stream is:

$$N_{photons} = \frac{P}{E_{photon}} = \frac{P\lambda}{hc}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Board Exams — Memorize This Table.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = dQ/dt$ | Fundamental definition of radiant power | W |
| 2 | $P = \int P_{\lambda}\, d\lambda$ | Total power from spectral density | W |
| 3 | $G = P/A$ | Irradiance (power per unit area) | W·m⁻² |
| 4 | $E_{photon} = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | Energy of a single photon | J |
| 5 | $N_{photons} = P\lambda/(hc)$ | Photon flux from radiant power | photons·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $P = \sigma A T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann (blackbody emission) | W |
| 7 | $I_\lambda(\lambda,T) = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}\right)-1\right]^{-1}$ | Planck's spectral radiance | W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·m⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\lambda_{peak}\cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | Wien's displacement law | m·K |
| 9 | $G_{AM1.5G} = 1000$ | Standard PV test irradiance | W·m⁻² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When writing formulas, always state **what each variable represents and its SI unit** before plugging values. Examiners reward clarity in unit-handling.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Relevance of Radiant Power |
|---|---|
| **PV Module Sizing** | Determines number of panels needed for required kW output |
| **Solar Thermal Collectors** | Input energy for flat-plate and concentrator designs |
| **Satellite Power Budgets** | AM0 spectrum defines space-grade solar arrays |
| **Building-Integrated PV (BIPV)** | Glazing polymers are rated by their radiant power transmittance |
| **Concentrator Photovoltaics (CPV)** | Uses concentrated radiant flux > 100× to boost efficiency |
| **Agricultural Photovoltaics** | Spectral partitioning of radiant power for crop + electricity |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Solar Radiant Power Incident on a Horizontal PV Surface

### Given:
- Standard terrestrial AM1.5G spectral irradiance data $G_\lambda(\lambda)$
- Surface area $A$ of the PV module
- We wish to find: Total radiant power $P$ intercepted.

### Step 1 — Start from the definition of irradiance

$$G_\lambda(\lambda) = \frac{dG}{d\lambda} \quad [\text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·nm}^{-1}]$$

Here $G_\lambda(\lambda)$ is the *spectral irradiance* — radiant power density per unit wavelength interval.

### Step 2 — Recover total irradiance by integration over the solar spectrum

$$G_{total} = \int_{0}^{\infty} G_\lambda(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$

Inserting the standard AM1.5G value (which has already been integrated and tabulated):

$$G_{total} = 1000 \;\; \text{W·m}^{-2}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by the module area to obtain radiant power

$$P = G_{total} \cdot A$$

### Step 4 — Convert to kilowatt form for PV rating

$$P_{\text{kW}} = \frac{G_{total} \cdot A}{1000}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A rooftop PV array has dimensions 10 m × 5 m. Under standard test conditions (STC), find the radiant power incident on it.

**Solution:**

$$A = 10 \times 5 = 50 \;\; \text{m}^2$$

$$P = G \cdot A = 1000 \;\text{W·m}^{-2} \times 50 \;\text{m}^2 = 50{,}000 \;\text{W}$$

$$P = 50 \;\text{kW}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Photons in Monochromatic Radiant Power

### Given:
- Radiant power $P = 10$ W
- Wavelength $\lambda = 600$ nm (orange light)
- Constants: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

### Step 1 — Compute energy of one photon

$$E_{photon} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{600 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$E_{photon} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{6 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.313 \times 10^{-19} \;\text{J}$$

### Step 2 — Compute photon rate

$$N_{photons} = \frac{P}{E_{photon}} = \frac{10}{3.313 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$N_{photons} = 3.018 \times 10^{19} \;\text{photons/s}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stefan–Boltzmann Power from a Solar-Disk Blackbody

### Step 1 — Surface area of the Sun

$$A_{sun} = 4\pi r_{sun}^2 = 4\pi (6.96 \times 10^8)^2 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$A_{sun} = 6.087 \times 10^{18} \;\text{m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply Stefan–Boltzmann

$$P_{sun} = \sigma A_{sun} T^4$$

$$P_{sun} = (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(6.087 \times 10^{18})(5778)^4$$

$$P_{sun} = (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(6.087 \times 10^{18})(1.116 \times 10^{15})$$

$$P_{sun} = 3.85 \times 10^{26} \;\text{W}$$

This is the **total radiant power output of the Sun** — a classic derivation that examiners love to test.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Irradiance at Earth's Orbit (Solar Constant)

### Step 1 — Spread $P_{sun}$ over a sphere of radius $r_{au} = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m

$$A_{sphere} = 4\pi r_{au}^2 = 4\pi (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2 = 2.812 \times 10^{23} \;\text{m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Divide

$$G_{sc} = \frac{P_{sun}}{A_{sphere}} = \frac{3.85 \times 10^{26}}{2.812 \times 10^{23}}$$

$$G_{sc} \approx 1369 \;\text{W·m}^{-2} \;\;(\approx 1361 \;\text{W·m}^{-2} \text{ measured})$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU asks this:** It builds intuition that "solar constant" isn't a constant in the strict sense — it varies with Earth's orbital eccentricity, hence the small spread between 1361–1369 W·m⁻².

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Radiant Power Hierarchy

| Level | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Radiant Energy | $Q$ | — | J |
| 1 | **Radiant Power** | $P$ | $dQ/dt$ | W |
| 2 | Irradiance | $G$ | $P/A$ | W·m⁻² |
| 3 | Spectral Irradiance | $G_\lambda$ | $dG/d\lambda$ | W·m⁻²·nm⁻¹ |
| 4 | Photon Flux | $N$ | $P/(h\nu)$ | photons·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | Photon Flux Density | $N_{ph}$ | $G/(h\nu)$ | photons·s⁻¹·m⁻² |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Flow: From Sun to Solar Cell

The following Mermaid block shows the **block-level functional architecture** mapping how radiant power flows from the solar source down to the PV cell output, with energy losses at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sun Surface T 5778 K] --> A2[Blackbody Emits Total Radiant Power 3.85e26 W]
    A2 --> A3[Propagation Across 1 AU Distance]
    A3 --> A4[Solar Constant 1361 W per sq m at AM0]
    A4 --> A5[Atmospheric Attenuation and Scattering]
    A5 --> A6[Ground Level Irradiance AM1.5G 1000 W per sq m]
    A6 --> A7[PV Module Intercepts Radiant Power P G times A]
    A7 --> A8[Photon Absorption in Semiconductor]
    A8 --> A9[Electron Hole Pair Generation]
    A9 --> A10[Separation by p n Junction Electric Field]
    A10 --> A11[DC Electrical Power Output to Load]
    A11 --> A12[Inverter Converts DC to AC]
    A12 --> A13[AC Power Delivered to Grid or Load]
```

## 4.2 Conversion Map: Radiant Power → Photon Flux → Electrical Power

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Input Radiant Power P in W] --> B2[Divide by Photon Energy h nu]
    B2 --> B3[Photon Flux N photons per sec]
    B3 --> B4[Multiply by Active Area A]
    B4 --> B5[Incident Photon Flux Density per sq m]
    B5 --> B6[Multiply by Quantum Efficiency QE]
    B6 --> B7[Electron Hole Pairs Generated]
    B7 --> B8[Multiply by Charge q and Voltage V]
    B8 --> B9[Electrical Output Power P out in W]
```

## 4.3 Spectral Decomposition Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Full Solar Spectrum 280 nm to 2500 nm] --> C2[UV Region 280 to 400 nm 5 percent]
    C1 --> C3[Visible Region 400 to 700 nm 43 percent]
    C1 --> C4[IR Region 700 to 2500 nm 52 percent]
    C2 --> C5[Filtered by Glass Encapsulant and EVA Polymer]
    C3 --> C6[Strongly Absorbed by c Si Bandgap 1.12 eV]
    C4 --> C7[Partially Absorbed Weak Contribution]
    C5 --> C8[Final Useful Radiant Power to PV Cell]
    C6 --> C8
    C7 --> C8
```

## 4.4 Air Mass & Spectral Shift Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Sunlight Enters Atmosphere] --> D2[Path Length Determined by Solar Zenith Angle theta]
    D2 --> D3[Air Mass AM equals 1 over cos theta]
    D3 --> D4[Shorter Path AM1 Direct Normal]
    D3 --> D5[Longer Path AM1.5 Standard Test]
    D3 --> D6[Very Long Path AM5 or Higher]
    D4 --> D7[High Radiant Power and Blue Rich Spectrum]
    D5 --> D8[Reduced Radiant Power and Red Shifted]
    D6 --> D9[Heavily Attenuated Spectrum]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams are designed as **block-functional** representations suitable for KTU board answers where drawing complex physical schematics in exam halls is impractical. Each block can be expanded with 1–2 lines of explanatory text to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define radiant power. State its SI unit and the physical significance in photovoltaic systems.**

**Model Answer:**

Radiant power, denoted by $\Phi$ or $P$, is defined as the time rate of flow of radiant energy.

$$P = \frac{dQ}{dt}$$

Its **SI unit is the watt (W = J·s⁻¹)**.

**Physical significance in PV systems:**
- Radiant power from the Sun is the **primary energy input** to a photovoltaic module.
- It is typically expressed per unit area as *irradiance* $G$ in W·m⁻².
- Under STC, the standard reference value is **$G = 1000$ W·m⁻²** (AM1.5G spectrum).
- The radiant power incident on a module of area $A$ is $P = G \times A$.
- It determines the **maximum theoretical electrical output** of the PV system, limited by the Shockley–Queisser efficiency bound.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between radiant power and irradiance. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Radiant Power ($P$) | Irradiance ($G$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Total radiant energy per unit time | Radiant power per unit area |
| Formula | $P = dQ/dt$ | $G = P/A$ |
| SI Unit | Watt (W) | Watt per square metre (W·m⁻²) |
| Area dependence | Independent of surface area | Depends on surface area |
| Example | The Sun emits total radiant power $\approx 3.85 \times 10^{26}$ W | Solar constant at Earth's orbit = 1361 W·m⁻² |

---

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the hierarchical classification of radiometric quantities leading to the definition of radiant power. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon PV module of dimensions 1.6 m × 1.0 m operates under STC (AM1.5G, 1000 W·m⁻²). Calculate:
- (i) The total radiant power incident on the module.
- (ii) The number of photons per second striking the module at $\lambda = 700$ nm.
- (iii) The short-circuit current if quantum efficiency = 0.85 (Assume each photon generates 1 electron). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Hierarchical classification of radiometric quantities:**

1. **Radiant Energy ($Q$):** The fundamental quantity — total electromagnetic energy in joules.
2. **Radiant Power / Flux ($P = dQ/dt$):** Energy flow rate in watts.
3. **Irradiance ($G = P/A$):** Power per unit area, in W·m⁻².
4. **Spectral Irradiance ($G_\lambda$):** Irradiance per unit wavelength interval.
5. **Photon Flux ($N$):** Number of photons per second, $N = P/(h\nu)$.
6. **Photon Flux Density:** Photon flux per unit area.

A neat hierarchy diagram (drawn as a pyramid or vertical flow) earns full marks. The key relationship is:

$$G \xrightarrow{\times A} P \xrightarrow{\div h\nu} N_{photons}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Total radiant power:**

$$A = 1.6 \times 1.0 = 1.6 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$P = G \cdot A = 1000 \times 1.6 = 1600 \;\text{W}$$

**(ii) Photon flux at 700 nm:**

$$E_{photon} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{700 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$E_{photon} = 2.839 \times 10^{-19} \;\text{J}$$

$$N = \frac{P}{E_{photon}} = \frac{1600}{2.839 \times 10^{-19}} = 5.635 \times 10^{21} \;\text{photons/s}$$

**(iii) Short-circuit current:**

Assuming full photon energy is in this wavelength band (a simplification for the exam):

$$N_{e} = N \cdot QE = 5.635 \times 10^{21} \times 0.85 = 4.79 \times 10^{21} \;\text{electrons/s}$$

$$I_{sc} = N_{e} \cdot q = (4.79 \times 10^{21})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})$$

$$I_{sc} = 0.766 \;\text{A}$$

For full spectrum consideration, the actual $I_{sc}$ would be higher; the exam simplification is acceptable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the total radiant power emitted by the Sun using Stefan–Boltzmann law. Use this to calculate the solar constant at Earth's mean orbital radius. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how atmospheric air mass affects the radiant power reaching a PV module. How does polymer encapsulation modify this? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Stefan–Boltzmann derivation:**

The Sun is approximated as a blackbody at temperature $T = 5778$ K. The total radiant power emitted is:

$$P_{sun} = \sigma \cdot A_{sun} \cdot T^4$$

where $A_{sun} = 4\pi r_{sun}^2 = 4\pi (6.96 \times 10^8)^2 = 6.087 \times 10^{18}$ m².

$$P_{sun} = (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(6.087 \times 10^{18})(5778)^4$$

$$P_{sun} = 3.85 \times 10^{26} \;\text{W}$$

**Solar constant derivation:**

At Earth's orbital distance $r_{au} = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m, this power spreads over a sphere:

$$G_{sc} = \frac{P_{sun}}{4\pi r_{au}^2} = \frac{3.85 \times 10^{26}}{4\pi (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2}$$

$$G_{sc} = \frac{3.85 \times 10^{26}}{2.812 \times 10^{23}} \approx 1369 \;\text{W·m}^{-2}$$

This matches the measured value of **1361 W·m⁻²** within acceptable tolerance for board exams.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Effect of Air Mass on radiant power:**

- **Air Mass (AM):** $AM = 1/\cos(\theta_z)$, where $\theta_z$ is the solar zenith angle.
- **AM0:** Outer atmosphere, no attenuation → 1361 W·m⁻²
- **AM1.5:** Sun at 48.2° elevation → 1000 W·m⁻² (standard for PV testing)
- Higher AM → longer atmospheric path → greater scattering and absorption by air molecules, dust, water vapor → reduced radiant power reaching the module.
- Spectral composition also shifts toward red/IR at high AM (Rayleigh scattering removes blue light preferentially).

**Polymer encapsulation effects:**

- Polymers like **EVA (ethylene-vinyl acetate)** and **Tedlar®** are used as encapsulants.
- They provide:
  1. **Optical coupling:** Anti-reflective coatings + refractive index matching reduce reflection losses (improving transmitted radiant power).
  2. **UV filtering:** Some polymers absorb UV below 380 nm, protecting the cell but reducing high-energy photons.
  3. **Mechanical/environmental protection** without significantly attenuating visible spectrum.
  4. Typically transmit **> 90%** of radiant power in the 400–1100 nm range.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always append W, W·m⁻², or photons·s⁻¹. Unit errors = −1 to −2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing radiant power with irradiance** — Examiners explicitly test this in Part A. Draw a clear table.
> 3. **Skipping substitution steps in numerical problems** — Show each numerical step; partial credit is given only for visible work.
> 4. **Using wrong Stefan–Boltzmann constant** — Use $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴, not e or k_B.
> 5. **Omitting the "physical significance" line** — In definition questions, KTU 2024 scheme demands a 1-line application note (e.g., "in PV, this is the energy input...").
> 6. **Incorrect AM formula** — Use $AM = 1/\cos(\theta)$, NOT $AM = \cos(\theta)$. Common sign error.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions** — When assuming blackbody Sun, explicitly write "assuming the Sun behaves as a blackbody."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 30-Second Revision Card for KTU 2024 — Radiant Power**

- ✅ **Radiant Power ($P$):** Energy per unit time; SI unit = **Watt (W)**; formula $P = dQ/dt$.
- ✅ **Irradiance ($G$):** Power per unit area; SI unit = **W·m⁻²**; formula $G = P/A$.
- ✅ **Solar Constant (AM0):** **1361 W·m⁻²** — radiant power density at Earth's outer atmosphere.
- ✅ **Standard Test Condition (STC):** **AM1.5G, 1000 W·m⁻², 25 °C**.
- ✅ **Stefan–Boltzmann:** $P = \sigma A T^4$, $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.
- ✅ **Photon Energy:** $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$.
- ✅ **Photon Flux:** $N = P / (hc/\lambda) = P\lambda / (hc)$.
- ✅ **Sun's Surface Temperature:** $\approx 5778$ K (Blackbody assumption).
- ✅ **Sun's Total Radiant Power:** $\approx 3.85 \times 10^{26}$ W.
- ✅ **Air Mass:** $AM = 1/\cos(\theta_z)$; higher AM = lower radiant power at ground.
- ✅ **Polymer Encapsulants (EVA):** Transmit > 90% of usable radiant power; filter harmful UV.
- ✅ **Wien's Law:** $\lambda_{peak} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K (Sun's peak $\approx 502$ nm).
- ✅ **Units Hierarchy:** J → W → W·m⁻² → W·m⁻²·nm⁻¹ → photons·s⁻¹.
- ✅ **Exam Mantra:** Always write formula → define variables → substitute → state answer with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Solar Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Types of Solar Cells # ☀️ Types of Solar Cells — Core Foundation & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solar Cell Classification (KTU Module 1.3 Definition):** > A *solar cell* (or *photovoltaic cell*) is a semiconductor p–n junction device that converts incident photons (from solar radiation) directly into electrical energy via the *photovoltaic effect*. Solar cells are classified into **three technological generations** based on the *active absorber material*, *device architecture*, and *manufacturing process maturity*: > > 1. **First Generation (1G):** Wafer-based crystalline silicon (c-Si) cells — *monocrystalline silicon (mono-Si)* and *polycrystalline / multicrystalline silicon (multi-Si / poly-Si)*. > 2. **Second Generation (2G):** Thin-film cells — *Amorphous Silicon (a-Si)*, *Cadmium Telluride (CdTe)*, *Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS)*, and *Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)*. > 3. **Third Generation (3G):** Emerging / next-generation cells — *Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSC)*, *Perovskite Solar Cells (PSC)*, *Organic Photovoltaics (OPV)*, *Quantum Dot Solar Cells (QDSC)*, and *Multi-junction / Tandem cells*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **☕ The "Coffee Mug" Analogy:** > Imagine sunlight as a shower of coins (photons) falling on a "piggy bank" (the solar cell). A **First-Generation (1G) cell** is like a *thick, heavy ceramic piggy bank* — it is rigid, expensive to make, but very reliable. A **Second-Generation (2G) cell** is like a *flexible, thin plastic money pouch* — cheaper, can be rolled out on rooftops, but absorbs coins less efficiently. A **Third-Generation (3G) cell** is like a *smart digital wallet* that uses new "sensing" technology to catch even tiny coins — extremely cheap, printable like newspaper ink, but still under research refinement. Each generation is essentially a *trade-off between efficiency, cost, and flexibility*. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following **bold** constants and metrics are universally used by KTU examiners and the photovoltaic industry: > > - **Solar constant (AM 1.5G irradiance):** **G = 1000 W/m²** (Standard Test Conditions, STC) > - **Bandgap energy of crystalline Si:** **E_g(Si) ≈ 1.12 eV** at **300 K** > - **Bandgap of GaAs:** **E_g(GaAs) ≈ 1.42 eV** at **300 K** > - **Bandgap of CdTe:** **E_g(CdTe) ≈ 1.45 eV** at **300 K** > - **Bandgap of CIGS:** **E_g(CIGS) ≈ 1.0 – 1.7 eV** (tunable) > - **Bandgap of perovskite (CH₃NH₃PbI₃):** **E_g ≈ 1.55 eV** at **300 K** > - **Schockley–Queisser limit (single-junction, 1 sun):** **η_max ≈ 33.7 %** > - **Planck's constant:** **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** > - **Elementary charge:** **q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering > [!NOTE] > From a KTU board-exam and industry perspective, the *types of solar cells* constitute the **backbone of every photovoltaic (PV) system** — from rooftop solar panels (1G/2G dominant) to flexible wearables and space satellites (3G emerging). Engineers must be able to **select, design, and justify** a cell type based on the application: terrestrial power generation, building-integrated PV (BIPV), space-grade solar arrays, or low-cost indoor energy harvesting. # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Generational Classification Logic (Structured Breakdown) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Module 1.3 Core Principle:** The classification is *technological* and *historical*, not just material-based. Each generation represents a paradigm shift in *how sunlight is absorbed* and *how electricity is extracted*. ### **Generation 1 — Wafer-Based Crystalline Silicon (c-Si)** - **Material:** Single-crystal or multi-crystal Si wafers (thickness ≈ **160 – 200 µm**). - **Process:** Czochralski (CZ) pulling for mono-Si; directional solidification / casting for multi-Si. - **Efficiency (lab/record):** **Mono-Si ≈ 26.7 %**; **Multi-Si ≈ 24.4 %** (NREL certified). - **Commercial module efficiency:** **15 % – 22 %**. - **Pros:** High efficiency, mature technology, 25–30 year lifespan, abundant raw material (silicon). - **Cons:** High production cost (energy-intensive), rigid, indirect bandgap → requires thick absorber. - **Application:** Rooftop and utility-scale solar farms. ### **Generation 2 — Thin-Film Technologies** - **Material thickness:** Typically **1 – 10 µm** active layer (≈ 100× thinner than 1G). - **Substrate:** Glass, metal foil, or polymer. - **Efficiency (lab):** CdTe ≈ **22.1 %**; CIGS ≈ **23.4 %**; a-Si ≈ **14.0 %**. - **Pros:** Lower material cost, flexible, better performance under diffuse light / high temperature. - **Cons:** Lower efficiency per m², some toxicity (Cd), light-induced degradation (a-Si, Staebler–Wronski effect). - **Application:** BIPV, large utility-scale, portable chargers. ### **Generation 3 — Emerging / Advanced Technologies** - **Goal:** Combine high efficiency with low-cost solution-processable fabrication. - **Key technologies:** - **DSSC (Grätzel cell, 1991):** Uses dye + TiO₂ + liquid electrolyte; lab efficiency ≈ **13 %**. - **Perovskite Solar Cells (PSC):** Methylammonium lead halide absorber; lab efficiency **> 26 %** (NREL 2024). - **Organic PV (OPV):** Polymer/fullerene bulk heterojunctions; lab efficiency ≈ **19 %**. - **Quantum Dot Solar Cells (QDSC):** Tunable bandgap via size effect; lab efficiency ≈ **19 %**. - **Multi-junction / Tandem cells:** Stacked sub-cells with different E_g; lab efficiency > **47 %** (concentrator). ## 2.2 The 'Why' & 'How' Behind Photovoltaic Action > [!NOTE] > **Why different bandgaps?** > The Sun emits a broad spectrum (UV → IR). A solar cell only absorbs photons with energy **hν ≥ E_g**. If E_g is too large, low-energy photons are wasted. If E_g is too small, high-energy photons thermalize. **Optimum E_g for a single-junction under AM 1.5G ≈ 1.34 eV** (this is why GaAs, CdTe, and perovskite perform well). > > **How a p–n junction separates carriers:** > Photons generate electron–hole pairs (EHP) in the depletion region. The built-in electric field sweeps electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side, producing photocurrent **I_L**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-yield formulas for Module 1 — Types of Solar Cells:** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Parameters / Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Photocurrent from incident power | $I_{L} \;=\; q \cdot A \cdot G \cdot \Phi_{QE}$ | $q$ = charge, $A$ = area (m²), $G$ = irradiance (W/m²), $\Phi_{QE}$ = quantum efficiency | Estimating short-circuit current | | 2 | Open-circuit voltage (ideal) | $V_{OC} \;=\; \dfrac{n k_{B} T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{L}}{I_{0}} + 1\right)$ | $n$ = ideality factor, $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant, $T$ = temperature (K), $I_0$ = reverse saturation current | Maximum voltage output | | 3 | Fill Factor (FF) | $FF \;=\; \dfrac{V_{MP} \cdot I_{MP}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$ | $V_{MP}, I_{MP}$ = voltage and current at max power point | Cell quality metric | | 4 | Power Conversion Efficiency | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | $P_{in} = G \cdot A$ (incident solar power) | Compare cell technologies | | 5 | Bandgap–wavelength relation | $E_{g} \;=\; \dfrac{h c}{\lambda_{c}}$ | $h c \approx 1240 \; \text{eV·nm}$ | Cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c$ | | 6 | Cutoff wavelength | $\lambda_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{1240}{E_{g}\,\text{[eV]}} \;\text{nm}$ | Direct numerical substitution | Determine absorption range | | 7 | Spectral mismatch | $\eta_{\text{spectral}} \;\propto\; \int_{0}^{\lambda_{c}} \Phi_{\odot}(\lambda)\,d\lambda$ | $\Phi_\odot$ = solar spectral photon flux | Why 1.34 eV is optimum | | 8 | Schockley–Queisser limit | $\eta_{max}^{\text{SQ}} \approx 33.7\%$ at $E_{g}\!\approx\!1.34\;\text{eV}$ | Single-junction, 1-sun, AM 1.5G | Theoretical efficiency ceiling | | 9 | Multi-junction stacking | $\eta_{\text{tandem}} > \eta_{\text{single}}$ for $N \geq 2$ sub-cells | Series-connected with tunnel junctions | Tandem / III-V cells | | 10 | Temperature coefficient (Si) | $\dfrac{\partial \eta}{\partial T} \approx -0.45 \,\%/\text{K}$ | Relative to STC (25 °C) | Real-world derating | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Terrestrial power plants:** Use 1G mono-Si for highest $/Watt efficiency. - **Flexible electronics & wearables:** Use 2G thin-film or 3G OPV. - **Space satellites:** Use 3G multi-junction GaInP/GaAs/Ge (radiation-hard, > 30 %). - **Building facades (BIPV):** Use semi-transparent perovskite or DSSC windows. - **Indoor IoT sensors:** Use 3G DSSC / OPV tuned for indoor LED spectrum (low light, high V_OC). # 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > All derivations below are *exhaustive*, every algebraic transition is explicitly shown — as required by the KTU-Premier-Engine V10 protocol. ## 3.1 Derivation: Photon Energy → Cutoff Wavelength Relation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goal:** Derive the relation between semiconductor bandgap $E_g$ and the longest wavelength $\lambda_c$ of photon that can be absorbed. **Step 1 — Energy of a single photon (Planck–Einstein relation):** $$E_{\text{photon}} \;=\; h \nu \;=\; \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$ **Step 2 — Condition for absorption in a semiconductor:** For an incident photon to excite an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, its energy must be **at least equal to the bandgap energy**: $$E_{\text{photon}} \;\geq\; E_{g}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the photon energy expression:** $$\dfrac{h c}{\lambda} \;\geq\; E_{g}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the maximum wavelength (cutoff wavelength):** At the *equality* condition ($\lambda = \lambda_c$), the photon has *just enough* energy to be absorbed. Re-arranging: $$\lambda_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{h c}{E_{g}}$$ **Step 5 — Insert numerical constants in electron-volt units:** Using $h c = 1.986 \times 10^{-25}\;\text{J·m}$ and $1\;\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\;\text{J}$: $$h c \;=\; \dfrac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \;\text{eV·m} \;\approx\; 1.240 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{eV·m}$$ Converting to nanometres ($1\;\text{m} = 10^{9}\;\text{nm}$): $$h c \;\approx\; 1240\;\text{eV·nm}$$ **Step 6 — Final engineering formula:** $$\boxed{\;\lambda_{c}\,[\text{nm}] \;=\; \dfrac{1240}{E_{g}\,[\text{eV}]}\;}$$ > **Interpretation logic:** *The smaller the bandgap, the longer the wavelength the cell can absorb — and the more of the solar spectrum is captured.* This is why **CdTe (1.45 eV)** absorbs visible light strongly, while **GaAs (1.42 eV)** also captures red photons, and **amorphous Si (≈ 1.7 eV)** absorbs mostly blue/green. ## 3.2 Derivation: Power Conversion Efficiency (η) of a Solar Cell **Step 1 — Define incident optical power:** The Sun provides $G = 1000\;\text{W/m}^2$ at AM 1.5G. For a cell of active area $A$: $$P_{\text{in}} \;=\; G \cdot A$$ **Step 2 — Define maximum electrical power output:** The cell delivers power $P = V \cdot I$. Under load, the operating point $(V, I)$ lies on the I–V curve. The **maximum power point** is $(V_{MP}, I_{MP})$: $$P_{\max} \;=\; V_{MP} \cdot I_{MP}$$ **Step 3 — Relate $V_{MP}, I_{MP}$ to $V_{OC}, I_{SC}$ via the Fill Factor:** The ratio of the maximum rectangle (under the I–V curve) to the ideal rectangle is the *Fill Factor*: $$FF \;\equiv\; \dfrac{V_{MP}\cdot I_{MP}}{V_{OC}\cdot I_{SC}}$$ $$\Longrightarrow \; P_{\max} \;=\; FF \cdot V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}$$ **Step 4 — Define efficiency:** $$\eta \;\equiv\; \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \;=\; \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}{G \cdot A}$$ **Step 5 — Special case: short-circuit current density (commonly tabulated):** Since $I_{SC} = J_{SC} \cdot A$, the area cancels: $$\boxed{\;\eta \;=\; \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC}}{G}\;}$$ > **Interpretation logic:** *Efficiency depends on three intrinsic cell parameters — V_OC, J_SC, FF — and the one external parameter, irradiance G.* This formula is the **single most important equation** for comparing solar-cell technologies (used in every NREL chart). ## 3.3 Derivation: V_OC in Terms of Photocurrent and Saturation Current **Step 1 — Ideal diode equation (Shockley):** $$I \;=\; I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{n k_{B} T}\right) - 1\right] - I_{L}$$ **Step 2 — At open circuit (I = 0, V = V_OC):** $$0 \;=\; I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q V_{OC}}{n k_{B} T}\right) - 1\right] - I_{L}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange:** $$I_{L} \;=\; I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q V_{OC}}{n k_{B} T}\right) - 1\right]$$ **Step 4 — Take logarithm of both sides:** $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{L}}{I_{0}} + 1\right) \;=\; \dfrac{q V_{OC}}{n k_{B} T}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for V_OC:** $$\boxed{\;V_{OC} \;=\; \dfrac{n k_{B} T}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{L}}{I_{0}} + 1\right)\;}$$ > **Interpretation logic:** *V_OC grows logarithmically with light intensity (I_L) and is degraded by leakage current (I_0).* This is why passivation (reducing I_0) is the holy grail of 1G silicon research. ## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style Numerical) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A monocrystalline silicon solar cell has $V_{OC} = 0.72\;\text{V}$, $I_{SC} = 3.5\;\text{A}$, and $FF = 0.78$. The cell area is $A = 0.015\;\text{m}^2$ (≈ 15 cm × 10 cm) and is illuminated at AM 1.5G. Calculate the **efficiency**. **Step 1 — Incident power:** $$P_{\text{in}} \;=\; G \cdot A \;=\; 1000 \cdot 0.015 \;=\; 15\;\text{W}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum power:** $$P_{\max} \;=\; FF \cdot V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \;=\; 0.78 \cdot 0.72 \cdot 3.5$$ $$P_{\max} \;=\; 0.78 \cdot 2.52 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 1.9656\;\text{W}$$ **Step 3 — Efficiency:** $$\eta \;=\; \dfrac{1.9656}{15} \;\approx\; 0.1310$$ $$\boxed{\;\eta \;\approx\; 13.1\%\;}$$ > This value is realistic for a **commercial-grade mono-Si cell**. Premium cells (PERC, HJT, TOPCon) reach **22 – 24 %** in mass production. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield Summary) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Big-4" Cell Technologies Side-by-Side** — frequently asked in KTU Module-1 questions. | Property | **Mono-Si (1G)** | **Multi-Si (1G)** | **CdTe (2G)** | **CIGS (2G)** | **Perovskite (3G)** | **GaAs MJ (3G)** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Bandgap (eV) | 1.12 | 1.12 | 1.45 | 1.0 – 1.7 | 1.55 | 1.42 + others | | Lab η (%) | 26.7 | 24.4 | 22.1 | 23.4 | 26.7 | 47.6 (concentrator) | | Module η (%) | 19–22 | 16–18 | 18–19 | 14–17 | 15–22 (proto) | 30–35 | | Thickness | 160–200 µm | 160–200 µm | 2–8 µm | 1–3 µm | 0.3–0.6 µm | 5–10 µm | | Cost (relative) | High | Med | Low | Med–High | Very Low | Very High | | Stability | 25–30 yr | 25–30 yr | 20–25 yr | 15–20 yr | < 5 yr (moisture) | > 20 yr (space) | | Toxicity | None | None | **Cd (toxic)** | None | **Pb (toxic)** | As (toxic) | | Flexibility | Rigid | Rigid | Flexible | Flexible | Flexible | Rigid | | Best Use | Rooftop, utility | Utility | Utility, BIPV | BIPV, portable | Research, tandem | Space, CPV | # 🧩 Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generational Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Photovoltaic Solar Cells]:::root --> B[1st Generation
