Perovskite Si 33.9 percent]:::year classDef year fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000 ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > *Differentiate between first-generation and second-generation solar cells based on material, thickness, and efficiency.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | **1G (Wafer c-Si)** | **2G (Thin-Film)** | |---|---|---| | Material | Mono / multi crystalline Si | a-Si, CdTe, CIGS | | Thickness | **160 – 200 µm** | **1 – 10 µm** | | Efficiency | **15 – 26 %** | **10 – 22 %** | | Fabrication | Energy-intensive, high T | Low T, scalable | | Application | Rooftop, utility | BIPV, flexible | *(1 mark for material, 1 mark for thickness/comparison, 1 mark for efficiency + application)* > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > *State the Schockley–Queisser limit. Why is this limit reached by single-junction cells?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. The **Schockley–Queisser (SQ) limit** is the **theoretical maximum efficiency of ≈ 33.7 %** for a single-junction solar cell under **AM 1.5G, 1-sun illumination** with a bandgap of **≈ 1.34 eV** *(1 mark)*. 2. It arises because of two fundamental losses: **(a) transmission loss** — photons with $h\nu < E_g$ are not absorbed; **(b) thermalization loss** — excess energy ($h\nu - E_g$) of high-energy photons is lost as heat *(1 mark)*. 3. To exceed this limit, cells must use **multiple junctions, concentration, or hot-carrier extraction** *(1 mark)*. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice) > **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** > > **(a)** With a neat I–V characteristic curve, explain the operating principle of a solar cell. Define **V_OC**, **I_SC**, **Fill Factor**, and **Efficiency**. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** A GaAs solar cell ($E_g = 1.42\;\text{eV}$) is illuminated by AM 1.5G sunlight. Calculate (i) the cutoff wavelength, and (ii) the maximum theoretical open-circuit voltage under ideal conditions at 300 K. *(7 marks)* ### **Model Solution — Question A(a):** **Operating Principle (3 marks):** - A solar cell is a p–n junction diode. When photons with $h\nu \ge E_g$ strike the depletion region, **electron–hole pairs (EHPs)** are generated. - The **built-in electric field** at the junction sweeps electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side. - Connecting an external load produces a **photocurrent $I_L$** — this is the *photovoltaic effect* (discovered by Edmond Becquerel, 1839). **I–V Characteristic (2 marks):** - In the **4th quadrant**, the cell delivers power: $P = V \cdot I > 0$. - Key points: $(0, I_{SC})$ and $(V_{OC}, 0)$; the **maximum power point (MPP)** is at $(V_{MP}, I_{MP})$. **Definitions (2 marks):** - **$V_{OC}$** = Open-circuit voltage (cell terminals open). - **$I_{SC}$** = Short-circuit current (terminals shorted). - **$FF$** = $(V_{MP} I_{MP})/(V_{OC} I_{SC})$, ideal cell → $FF \to 1$. - **$\eta$** = $(P_{max}/P_{in}) \times 100\,\%$. ### **Model Solution — Question A(b):** **(i) Cutoff wavelength (3 marks):** $$\lambda_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{1240}{E_{g}} \;=\; \dfrac{1240}{1.42}$$ $$\boxed{\;\lambda_{c} \;\approx\; 873\;\text{nm}\;}$$ → Cell absorbs all visible + near-IR up to 873 nm. **(ii) Maximum theoretical V_OC (4 marks):** Using the diode equation at 300 K with $n = 1$ and assuming $I_L \gg I_0$: $$V_{OC}^{\max} \;\approx\; \dfrac{E_{g}}{q} \;=\; 1.42\;\text{V}$$ (more precisely, $V_{OC} \approx E_g - 0.3\;\text{V} \approx 1.1\;\text{V}$ from the SQ analysis). $$\boxed{\;V_{OC}^{\text{ideal}} \;\approx\; 1.42\;\text{V}\;}$$ --- > **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** > > **(a)** Classify solar cells into three generations. List two examples from each generation with their respective bandgap energies and record efficiencies. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** A mono-Si solar cell has $V_{OC} = 0.65\;\text{V}$, $J_{SC} = 38\;\text{mA/cm}^2$, and $FF = 0.80$. Calculate (i) the power conversion efficiency, and (ii) the maximum power output for a $10\;\text{cm} \times 10\;\text{cm}$ cell at AM 1.5G. *(7 marks)* ### **Model Solution — Question B(a):** | Generation | Example | $E_g$ (eV) | Lab $\eta$ (%) | |---|---|---|---| | **1G Wafer** | Mono-Si | 1.12 | 26.7 | | **1G Wafer** | Multi-Si | 1.12 | 24.4 | | **2G Thin-Film** | CdTe | 1.45 | 22.1 | | **2G Thin-Film** | CIGS | 1.0 – 1.7 | 23.4 | | **3G Emerging** | Perovskite CH₃NH₃PbI₃ | 1.55 | 26.7 | | **3G Emerging** | DSSC | ≈ 1.6 | 13.0 | *(3.5 marks for classification + 3.5 marks for tabulated data with units)* ### **Model Solution — Question B(b):** **(i) Efficiency (4 marks):** $$P_{\text{in}} \;=\; G \cdot A \;=\; 1000 \cdot (0.10 \times 0.10) \;=\; 10\;\text{W}$$ $$J_{SC} \cdot A \;=\; 38 \;\text{mA/cm}^2 \times 100 \;\text{cm}^2 \;=\; 3800\;\text{mA} \;=\; 3.8\;\text{A}$$ $$P_{\max} \;=\; FF \cdot V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \;=\; 0.80 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 3.8$$ $$P_{\max} \;=\; 0.80 \cdot 2.47 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 1.976\;\text{W}$$ $$\eta \;=\; \dfrac{1.976}{10} \;\times\; 100\% \;\approx\; \boxed{19.76\%}$$ **(ii) Maximum power output (3 marks):** $$\boxed{\;P_{\max} \;\approx\; 1.98\;\text{W}\;}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones:** > > 1. **Forgetting units in $\eta$ calculation** — *always express $P_{in}$ in watts*. (–1 mark) > 2. **Using $I_{SC}$ instead of $J_{SC} \cdot A$** — students often plug $I_{SC} = 38\;\text{mA}$ directly. **The value 38 mA is a *current density*, NOT current.** (–2 marks) > 3. **Confusing V_OC and V_MP** — $V_{OC}$ is *open-circuit*, no load. $V_{MP} = FF \cdot V_{OC}$ is an approximation, not exact. (–1 mark) > 4. **Writing $E_g = 1.12$ for CdTe** — CdTe is $1.45\;\text{eV}$. (–1 mark) > 5. **Skipping Schockley–Queisser explanation in 14-mark answers** — the examiner expects the *theoretical ceiling* to be discussed. (–2 marks) ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **🧠 Key Things to Remember — "Types of Solar Cells"** > > - **Three generations:** **1G = c-Si wafer; 2G = thin-film; 3G = emerging** (DSSC, PSC, OPV, QD, tandem). > - **Mono-Si vs Multi-Si:** Mono-Si = single crystal, higher efficiency, higher cost. Multi-Si = many grains, lower cost, slightly lower efficiency. > - **Bandgap ↔ Cutoff wavelength:** $\lambda_c = 1240/E_g$ (eV·nm). > - **Schockley–Queisser limit:** **33.7 %** for single-junction, 1-sun. > - **Optimum $E_g \approx 1.34\;\text{eV}$** (between Si 1.12 and GaAs 1.42 eV). > - **Open-circuit voltage:** $V_{OC} = (n k_B T/q) \ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$. > - **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = (FF \cdot V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}) / (G \cdot A)$. > - **Multi-junction (tandem) cells** break the SQ limit by stacking sub-cells of different $E_g$ — record **> 47 %** under concentration. > - **Perovskite is the rising star of 3G** — efficiency climbed from **3.8 % (2009) → 26.7 % (2024)** in just 15 years. > - **DSSC (Grätzel cell):** Liquid electrolyte + TiO₂ + dye; cheap, semi-transparent, low-light efficient. > - **OPV (Organic PV):** Polymer-based, flexible, printable; lifetime remains the bottleneck. > - **Quantum Dot Solar Cells (QDSC):** Bandgap *tunable by particle size* (quantum confinement effect). > - **CdTe is toxic (Cd)** → recycling protocols mandatory; **Perovskite contains Pb** → encapsulation required. > - **AM 1.5G standard spectrum** = $G = 1000\;\text{W/m}^2$, used for all cell ratings.

topics.title: Organic Solar Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Organic Solar Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Organic Solar Cells (OSCs)** are photovoltaic devices that convert sunlight directly into electricity using **carbon-based organic semiconductors** (either small molecules or conjugated polymers) as the photoactive layer, instead of conventional inorganic semiconductors such as crystalline silicon or GaAs.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, OSCs fall under the **third generation / emerging photovoltaics** category and are studied as a polymer-based counterpart to dye-sensitized and perovskite solar cells. The device relies on the photovoltaic effect occurring at the interface between an **electron donor (D)** material and an **electron acceptor (A)** material, whose energy level offsets drive the photo-generated exciton dissociation.

The standard nomenclature used in the KTU syllabus (PEPOT757) is:

$$\text{Donor} \;\; (D) \;\; | \;\; \text{Acceptor} \;\; (A)$$

Where:

- **Donor (D):** A p-type organic semiconductor (typically a conjugated polymer such as **P3HT**, **PTB7**, or **PM6**) with a high-lying HOMO energy level.
- **Acceptor (A):** An n-type organic semiconductor (historically a fullerene derivative such as **PC\_{61}BM** or **PC\_{71}BM**, more recently **non-fullerene acceptors (NFAs)** such as **ITIC** or **Y6**) with a low-lying LUMO energy level.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Team Relay Race"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Sunlight as the starting pistol and electrons as runners.**
>
> Imagine a relay race. When sunlight (the starting pistol) hits the organic layer, the runners (electrons) get excited but cannot run alone — they are tied at the wrist to a hole, forming a "**bound pair**" called an **exciton**. Until they reach a special junction (the **D/A interface**), they cannot separate and run freely. Once they cross this junction, the electron sprints toward one finish line (cathode) and the hole toward the other (anode), generating current. The relay baton can only be passed at the D/A boundary.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and benchmarks are **permanently referenced** in KTU numerical problems and MUST be memorized:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $h$ | Planck's constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34} \; \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| $c$ | Speed of light | $3.0 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}$ |
| $q$ | Elementary charge | $1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; \text{C}$ |
| $E_g$ | Optical bandgap of organic absorber | $1.1 - 1.8 \; \text{eV}$ |
| $E_b$ | Exciton binding energy (organic) | $\sim 0.3 - 1.0 \; \text{eV}$ (large) |
| $L_D$ | Exciton diffusion length | $5 - 20 \; \text{nm}$ (short) |
| $\eta$ | Power Conversion Efficiency | Lab record $> 19\%$ (single junction) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Four Sequential Photophysical Steps

The conversion of photons to free charges in an OSC proceeds through **four distinct energy-transfer stages**. The KTU examiner expects these steps in order.

**Step 1 — Photoexcitation (Photon Absorption):**
A photon of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ is absorbed by the donor molecule, promoting an electron from the HOMO to the LUMO level. Because organic materials have **high absorption coefficients ($\sim 10^{5} \; \text{cm}^{-1}$)** and **tunable bandgaps** (via molecular engineering), only **$\sim 100 - 200 \; \text{nm}$** of active layer thickness is sufficient to absorb most incident light — a major advantage over silicon (which needs $\sim 200 \; \mu\text{m}$).

$$\text{D} + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{D}^{*} \quad (\text{Excited State, S}_1)$$

**Step 2 — Exciton Formation:**
Unlike inorganic semiconductors where photoexcitation directly yields free carriers (because the exciton binding energy is small, $\sim 0.01 \; \text{eV}$, screened by high dielectric constant), in organics the excited state remains **electrostatically bound** as a Frenkel-type exciton due to the **low dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r \sim 3 - 4$)** and **strong Coulomb attraction** ($E_b \sim 0.3 - 1.0 \; \text{eV}$).

$$\text{D}^{*} \longrightarrow (\text{D}^{+} \; \text{e}^{-}) \quad \text{(Bound Exciton)}$$

**Step 3 — Exciton Diffusion & Dissociation at D/A Interface:**
The bound exciton must travel to a **donor-acceptor heterojunction** within its diffusion length $L_D$ ($\sim 5 - 20 \; \text{nm}$). The LUMO offset $\Delta E_{\text{LUMO}} = E_{\text{LUMO}}^{D} - E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A}$ (typically $\geq 0.3 \; \text{eV}$) provides the energetic driving force to split the exciton into a **charge-transfer (CT) state**, which then separates into free carriers.

$$\text{(D}^{+}\text{e}^{-}) \xrightarrow{\text{at D/A interface}} \text{D}^{+} + \text{e}^{-}_{(A)}$$

**Step 4 — Charge Transport & Collection:**
Free holes travel through the donor phase to the **anode** (typically ITO/PEDOT:PSS), while free electrons travel through the acceptor phase to the **cathode** (typically Al, Ca, or Ag). The asymmetric work functions create an internal electric field that selects the carrier direction.

### 2.2 Why The Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ) Architecture Is Critical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because $L_D \sim 10 \; \text{nm}$ but the absorption depth is $\sim 100 \; \text{nm}$, a **planar bilayer junction** would lose $> 90\%$ of excitons to recombination. The **Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ)** solves this by **intermixing** donor and acceptor phases at the nanoscale, ensuring every photogenerated exciton is within $\sim 10 \; \text{nm}$ of a D/A interface.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are examinable in Part A, Part B numericals, and viva voce. Memorize the units!

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE) | $V_{oc}$ in V, $J_{sc}$ in A/m², $P_{in}$ in W/m² |
| 2 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot J_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor (squareness of J–V curve) | $V_{mp}, J_{mp}$ = max power point coords |
| 3 | $V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{1}{q}\left(E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D} - E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A}\right) - 0.3 \; \text{eV}$ | Empirical open-circuit voltage relation | Energies in eV |
| 4 | $J_{sc} = q \int_{\lambda_1}^{\lambda_2} \eta_{\text{EQE}}(\lambda) \cdot \Phi_{ph}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Short-circuit current density | $\Phi_{ph}$ = photon flux (photons/m²/s) |
| 5 | $E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\text{cutoff}}} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{\text{cutoff}}(\text{nm})} \; \text{eV}$ | Optical bandgap from absorption edge | $\lambda$ in nm |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{EQE}} = \eta_{abs} \cdot \eta_{diff} \cdot \eta_{diss} \cdot \eta_{tr} \cdot \eta_{cc}$ | External Quantum Efficiency (product of 5 sub-yields) | Each factor $\leq 1$ |
| 7 | $L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot \tau}$ | Exciton diffusion length | $D$ = diffusion coeff, $\tau$ = exciton lifetime |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Relevance

- **Flexible & roll-to-roll printable solar panels** (manufactured like newspapers, not silicon wafers).
- **Building-Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV)** — semi-transparent OSCs can be tinted to any colour.
- **Indoor IoT / wearable electronics** — best efficiency under low-light indoor LED illumination.
- **Lightweight aerospace power** — NASA has flown OSC prototypes on the International Space Station.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$) from Energy Level Alignment

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Derive the empirical expression for the open-circuit voltage of a bulk heterojunction organic solar cell from the difference between the donor HOMO and the acceptor LUMO energy levels.

**Step 1 — Statement of Quasi-Fermi Level Splitting:**
At open-circuit condition, no net current flows through the external circuit, but a non-equilibrium steady state exists. The maximum obtainable voltage equals the splitting of the quasi-Fermi levels of electrons ($E_{F,n}$, in the acceptor LUMO region) and holes ($E_{F,p}$, in the donor HOMO region):

$$q V_{oc} = E_{F,n} - E_{F,p}$$

**Step 2 — Substitution of Energy Levels:**
Under ideal conditions (no voltage losses), the electron quasi-Fermi level aligns with the acceptor LUMO, and the hole quasi-Fermi level aligns with the donor HOMO. Hence:

$$q V_{oc} \approx E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A} - E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D}$$

*Logic:* Electrons injected into the acceptor phase fill states up to $E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A}$; holes in the donor phase fill states up to $E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D}$. The gap between these two levels is the maximum electrochemical potential the cell can sustain.

**Step 3 — Incorporation of Empirical Voltage Loss:**
Experimental data on hundreds of OSC systems show that the measured $V_{oc}$ is consistently **$0.3 \; \text{eV}$ lower** than the energy difference. This loss is attributed to:

- Non-radiative recombination at the D/A interface.
- Energetic disorder in the amorphous organic phase.
- Charge-transfer state binding energy (small but non-zero).

Subtracting the empirical loss term $0.3 \; \text{eV}$:

$$q V_{oc} = \left( E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A} - E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D} \right) - 0.3 \; \text{eV}$$

**Step 4 — Final Form:**
Dividing both sides by $q$ and expressing energies in eV:

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = \dfrac{1}{q}\left(E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A} - E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D}\right) - 0.3 \; \text{V}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification via Energy Level Diagram:**

```
LUMO (Acceptor) ──────────────── E_F,n  (high)
              ← ΔE_LUMO →  
LUMO (Donor)   ────── 
              ← bandgap (Eg) →
HOMO (Donor)   ────── 
              ← ΔE_HOMO → 
HOMO (Acceptor)────────────── E_F,p (low)
              ←→ qV_oc (split)
```

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Starting from the definition of electrical power delivered by the solar cell, derive the standard expression for $\eta$ in terms of $V_{oc}$, $J_{sc}$, and $FF$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Output Power Density:**
The instantaneous electrical power density delivered by the cell at a given operating voltage $V$ and current density $J$ is:

$$P(V) = V \cdot J(V)$$

The units are $\text{W/m}^2$ since $V$ is in volts and $J$ is in A/m².

**Step 2 — Maximum Power Point:**
The maximum of the product $V \cdot J$ on the $J$–$V$ curve occurs at $(V_{mp}, J_{mp})$:

$$P_{\max} = V_{mp} \cdot J_{mp}$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Fill Factor (FF):**
The fill factor is defined as the ratio of the maximum power rectangle to the ideal $(V_{oc} \times J_{sc})$ rectangle on the $J$–$V$ curve:

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot J_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{mp} \cdot J_{mp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}$$

**Step 4 — Substitution:**
Substituting into the maximum power expression:

$$P_{\max} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}$$

**Step 5 — Definition of PCE:**
The Power Conversion Efficiency is the ratio of the maximum electrical power density produced to the incident solar power density $P_{in}$ (typically $1000 \; \text{W/m}^2$ under AM1.5G standard):

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}{P_{in}}$$

**Step 6 — Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Exciton Diffusion Length

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Show that the exciton diffusion length $L_D$ depends on the diffusion coefficient $D$ and the exciton lifetime $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Random Walk Model:**
Excitons in the amorphous organic phase undergo a Brownian random walk via Föster / Dexter energy transfer between neighbouring conjugated segments. The mean-squared displacement after time $t$ in 3 dimensions is:

$$\langle r^2 \rangle = 6 D \tau$$

**Step 2 — Defining Diffusion Length:**
The exciton diffusion length is the rms distance an exciton can travel before recombination:

$$L_D = \sqrt{\langle r^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{6 D \tau}$$

**Step 3 — Simplification for Practical Use:**
For the typical case of 1-D exciton motion towards the D/A interface, the factor becomes 2 instead of 6:

$$\boxed{L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot \tau}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Estimate for P3HT:**
For P3HT, typical values are $D \approx 3 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ and $\tau \approx 400 \; \text{ps}$:

$$L_D = \sqrt{(3 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{cm}^2/\text{s}) \cdot (4 \times 10^{-10} \; \text{s})}$$

$$L_D = \sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-13} \; \text{cm}^2}$$

$$L_D = \sqrt{12 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 3.46 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{cm} \approx 3.5 \; \text{nm}$$

The actual measured value for P3HT is $\sim 6 - 10 \; \text{nm}$, which confirms the order of magnitude and explains why BHJ domain sizes must be $< 20 \; \text{nm}$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Scenario | Given | Find | Approach |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bandgap from cutoff wavelength | $\lambda_{\text{cutoff}} = 800 \; \text{nm}$ | $E_g$ | Use $E_g = 1240/\lambda$ (in eV·nm) |
| $V_{oc}$ from energy levels | HOMO(D) = -5.2 eV, LUMO(A) = -3.8 eV | $V_{oc}$ | Apply $V_{oc} = (E_{LUMO}^{A} - E_{HOMO}^{D})/q - 0.3$ |
| PCE from J-V curve data | $V_{oc}, J_{sc}, FF, P_{in}$ | $\eta$ | Apply $\eta = V_{oc} J_{sc} FF / P_{in}$ |
| Domain size for BHJ | Exciton $L_D = 10 \; \text{nm}$ | Optimum domain size | Should be $\leq L_D$ i.e. $\leq 10 \; \text{nm}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Device Architecture — Layered Cross-Section (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> Since a literal cross-sectional physical drawing of an OSC is difficult in text, we present the **functional layered architecture** as a Mermaid block diagram, mapping each layer and its role in charge transport.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["TOP LAYER INCIDENT LIGHT"]
        sun["SUNLIGHT h nu photons"]
    end

    subgraph B["TRANSPARENT ANODE STACK"]
        glass["Substrate Glass or PET"]
        ito["ITO Indium Tin Oxide Work function 4.7 eV"]
        pedot["PEDOT PSS Hole Transport Layer HOMO 5.2 eV"]
    end

    subgraph C["ACTIVE LAYER BULK HETEROJUNCTION 100 to 200 nm"]
        donor["DONOR P3HT or PM6 Absorbs photons Forms excitons"]
        acceptor["ACCEPTOR PCBM or Y6 Receives electrons Forms pathway"]
        interface["D and A Interface Exciton dissociation site"]
    end

    subgraph D["TRANSPORT AND CATHODE"]
        etl["ETL Optional ZnO or PFN Electron transport"]
        metal["CATHODE Al or Ag Work function 4.0 eV"]
    end

    sun --> glass
    glass --> ito
    ito --> pedot
    pedot --> donor
    donor <--> interface
    interface <--> acceptor
    acceptor --> etl
    etl --> metal

    donor -.Hole path to anode.-> pedot
    acceptor -.Electron path to cathode.-> metal
```

### 4.2 Operational Flow — Four Photophysical Steps (Mermaid Process Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["STEP 1 Photon Absorption h nu greater than Eg"]
    step2["STEP 2 Exciton Formation Bound electron hole pair"]
    step3["STEP 3 Exciton Diffusion and Dissociation at D and A interface"]
    step4["STEP 4 Charge Transport and Collection at electrodes"]

    step1 -->|"Excitation"| step2
    step2 -->|"Travel within LD 5 to 20 nm"| step3
    step3 -->|"Split by energy offset"| step4
    step4 -->|"External circuit load"| X["Electrical Current Jsc and Voltage Voc"]

    style step1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style step2 fill:#FFD700
    style step3 fill:#FF8C00
    style step4 fill:#FF4500
    style X fill:#90EE90
```

### 4.3 Energy Level Diagram — Donor–Acceptor Junction (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph donorBlock["DONOR MATERIAL P type polymer"]
        HOMO_D["HOMO D around minus 5.2 eV"]
        LUMO_D["LUMO D around minus 3.2 eV"]
    end

    subgraph acceptorBlock["ACCEPTOR MATERIAL N type small molecule or NFA"]
        LUMO_A["LUMO A around minus 3.8 eV"]
        HOMO_A["HOMO A around minus 5.6 eV"]
    end

    HOMO_D --- LUMO_D
    LUMO_A --- HOMO_A

    LUMO_D -.Electron transfer to lower LUMO.-> LUMO_A
    HOMO_D -.Hole remains in HOMO.-> HOMO_A

    Eg["Bandgap Eg equals LUMO D minus HOMO D"]:::energy
    Eg ~~~ LUMO_D
    deltaL["Delta E LUMO Driving force for dissociation"]:::force
    deltaL ~~~ LUMO_D

    classDef energy fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef force fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.4 Comparative Family Tree — OSC Sub-Types (Mermaid Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OSC["ORGANIC SOLAR CELLS"]

    OSC --> SJ["Single Junction"]
    OSC --> TJ["Tandem Junction"]
    OSC --> TJ_M["Triple Junction Research"]

    SJ --> BHJ["Bulk Heterojunction Most common"]
    SJ --> BL["Bilayer Planar Historical"]
    SJ --> NHJ["Nano structured Heterojunction"]

    BHJ --> F["Fullerene acceptor PCBM era 1995 to 2015"]
    BHJ --> NFA["Non Fullerene Acceptor ITIC Y6 era 2015 to present"]

    NFA --> PM6Y6["PM6 and Y6 PCE 19 percent record"]
    NFA --> D18["D18 and Y6 PCE approaching 20 percent"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** What is a Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ) organic solar cell? Why is it preferred over the planar bilayer architecture?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ) organic solar cell** is a device architecture in which the **electron donor** and **electron acceptor** organic semiconductors are **intermixed at the nanometer scale** (typically with phase separation domains of $5 - 20 \; \text{nm}$) rather than being deposited as separate planar layers.

It is preferred over the planar bilayer architecture because:

1. **Short exciton diffusion length problem:** The exciton diffusion length $L_D$ in organic semiconductors is only $\sim 5 - 20 \; \text{nm}$, while the optical absorption depth is $\sim 100 - 200 \; \text{nm}$. In a planar bilayer, most photogenerated excitons are generated far from the D/A interface and recombine before reaching it.
2. **BHJ solution:** By intermixing D and A phases, **every** photogenerated exciton is formed within $\sim 10 \; \text{nm}$ of a D/A interface, ensuring near-quantitative exciton harvesting and high internal quantum efficiency.
3. **Continuous pathways:** The bicontinuous interpenetrating network in BHJ also provides separate percolation channels for holes (through the donor) and electrons (through the acceptor), enabling efficient charge extraction.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define **Exciton Binding Energy** and explain why it is significantly higher in organic semiconductors compared to inorganic ones.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **exciton binding energy ($E_b$)** is the electrostatic Coulomb attraction energy that must be overcome to separate a photoexcited electron–hole pair into free charge carriers.

In organic semiconductors, $E_b \sim 0.3 - 1.0 \; \text{eV}$, which is **$\sim 30 - 100$ times larger** than in inorganic semiconductors ($E_b \sim 0.01 \; \text{eV}$) because:

1. **Low dielectric constant:** Organic materials have $\varepsilon_r \sim 3 - 4$ (e.g., P3HT has $\varepsilon_r \approx 3.4$), while silicon has $\varepsilon_r \approx 11.7$. Since Coulomb attraction scales as $1/\varepsilon_r$, the attraction is much stronger in organics.
2. **Small intermolecular separation:** In organic solids, the conjugated molecules are held by weak van der Waals forces ($\sim 0.3 - 0.4 \; \text{nm}$ spacing), keeping the electron and hole tightly bound.
3. **Localized Frenkel excitons:** Photoexcitation produces **Frenkel-type excitons** where the electron and hole reside on the same molecule, in contrast to Wannier-Mott excitons in inorganic crystals that are spatially delocalized.

This large $E_b$ is precisely why OSCs require a D/A heterojunction to drive charge separation.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students commonly confuse the **LUMO of donor** with the **LUMO of acceptor** when writing the $V_{oc}$ formula. The correct expression uses $E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A} - E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D}$. Writing $E_{\text{LUMO}}^{D} - E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D}$ will cost 2 marks.
> 2. Many students forget to **convert $\lambda_{\text{cutoff}}$ from nm to m** when using $E = hc/\lambda$. Use the convenient form $E(\text{eV}) = 1240/\lambda(\text{nm})$ to avoid unit errors.
> 3. In BHJ diagrams, students often draw **continuous layers** instead of intermixed phases. The hallmark of BHJ is **interpenetrating nanoscale network** — examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 4. Do NOT write $V_{oc} = (E_g)/q$. That is only true for an ideal Shockley-Queisser cell, not for OSCs.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat energy level diagram the **working principle of a Bulk Heterojunction organic solar cell**. Clearly identify the four photophysical steps and the role of the donor–acceptor interface.

**Model Solution:**

A Bulk Heterojunction organic solar cell operates on the principle of the **photovoltaic effect at a nanoscale D/A interface**. The complete working principle involves four sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Photon Absorption (Photoexcitation):**
When a photon of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ strikes the active layer, it is absorbed by the donor polymer. An electron is promoted from the HOMO to the LUMO of the donor, leaving behind a hole in the HOMO. This process is fast ($\sim 10^{-15} \; \text{s}$).

**Step 2 — Exciton Formation:**
Because of the low dielectric constant and strong Coulomb attraction, the photoexcited electron does NOT become a free carrier. Instead, it forms a bound electron–hole pair (Frenkel exciton) on the donor molecule, with binding energy $E_b \sim 0.5 \; \text{eV}$.

**Step 3 — Exciton Diffusion and Dissociation:**
The exciton must reach a D/A interface within its diffusion length $L_D \sim 5 - 20 \; \text{nm}$. At the interface, the energy offset $\Delta E_{\text{LUMO}} = E_{\text{LUMO}}^{D} - E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A} \geq 0.3 \; \text{eV}$ provides the driving force to break the Coulomb binding, transferring the electron to the acceptor LUMO. A charge-transfer (CT) state is briefly formed before complete separation.

**Step 4 — Charge Transport and Collection:**
Free holes travel through the donor network to the anode (ITO/PEDOT:PSS), and free electrons travel through the acceptor network to the cathode (Al). The asymmetric work functions ensure carrier selectivity.

**Energy Level Diagram (conceptual, drawn during exam):**

```
                  Anode (ITO, WF = 4.7 eV)
─────────────────────────────────────
HOMO(D) ─────●─────── HOMO(A)       (e.g., -5.2 eV and -5.6 eV)
              ⇅ Exciton
LUMO(D) ─────●─────── LUMO(A)       (e.g., -3.2 eV and -3.8 eV)
─────────────────────────────────────
              ⇩ ΔE_LUMO ≥ 0.3 eV drives electron transfer
              ⇩ qV_oc split after dissociation
                  Cathode (Al, WF = 4.0 eV)
```

The four steps are: **Absorption → Exciton Formation → Diffusion & Dissociation → Transport & Collection.** (7 marks: 1.5 marks per step + 1 mark for diagram)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** An organic solar cell uses a donor with HOMO at $-5.2 \; \text{eV}$ and an acceptor with LUMO at $-3.9 \; \text{eV}$. The measured $J_{sc} = 18 \; \text{mA/cm}^2$, $V_{oc} = 0.78 \; \text{V}$, and $FF = 0.72$ under AM1.5G illumination ($P_{in} = 100 \; \text{mW/cm}^2$). Calculate:
(i) The Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE).
(ii) The optical bandgap $E_g$ of the donor, given that the absorption cutoff occurs at $\lambda = 750 \; \text{nm}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Power Conversion Efficiency (4 marks):**

Using the standard PCE equation:

$$\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$$

Substituting the given values (note: all in consistent units; $P_{in} = 100 \; \text{mW/cm}^2$, $J_{sc} = 18 \; \text{mA/cm}^2$):

$$\eta = \dfrac{(0.78 \; \text{V}) \times (18 \; \text{mA/cm}^2) \times (0.72)}{100 \; \text{mW/cm}^2}$$

Computing the numerator step by step:

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.78 \times 18 \times 0.72$$

$$0.78 \times 18 = 14.04$$

$$14.04 \times 0.72 = 10.1088 \; \text{mW/cm}^2$$

Therefore:

$$\eta = \dfrac{10.1088}{100} = 0.1011 = 10.11\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 10.11\%}$$

**(ii) Optical Bandgap (3 marks):**

Using the standard relation between bandgap and cutoff wavelength:

$$E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\text{cutoff}}} = \dfrac{1240 \; \text{eV}\cdot\text{nm}}{\lambda_{\text{cutoff}}(\text{nm})}$$

$$E_g = \dfrac{1240}{750} = 1.653 \; \text{eV}$$

$$\boxed{E_g \approx 1.65 \; \text{eV}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **planar bilayer** and **bulk heterojunction (BHJ)** architectures of organic solar cells. Discuss the role of **exciton diffusion length** in determining the choice of architecture.

**Model Solution:**

**Planar Bilayer Architecture:**
In the planar bilayer (or "planar heterojunction") architecture, the donor and acceptor are deposited as two distinct, sequentially stacked layers with a single sharp D/A interface at their boundary.

- **Advantages:** Well-defined interface morphology, easier charge transport along phase-pure layers, simpler device fabrication.
- **Disadvantages:** Only excitons generated within $L_D \sim 5 - 20 \; \text{nm}$ of the interface can be harvested. The remaining excitons (the vast majority, since the active layer is $\sim 100 \; \text{nm}$ thick) recombine and are lost.

**Bulk Heterojunction Architecture:**
In the BHJ, the donor and acceptor are co-deposited (from a common solution) and phase-separate into an **interpenetrating nanoscale network** with $5 - 20 \; \text{nm}$ domain sizes.

- **Advantages:** Every point in the bulk is within $L_D$ of an interface; exciton harvesting is nearly quantitative ($\eta_{\text{diff}} \rightarrow 100\%$).
- **Disadvantages:** Disorder in the BHJ morphology can cause charge recombination "dead ends" and reduced charge carrier mobility.

**Role of Exciton Diffusion Length:**

The exciton diffusion length $L_D$ is the **critical design parameter** that dictates the architecture choice. Since:

$$L_D = \sqrt{D \tau} \sim 5 - 20 \; \text{nm}$$

and the optical absorption depth is $\sim 100 \; \text{nm}$, a **planar bilayer** can only harvest $\sim 5 - 20\%$ of absorbed photons. The BHJ overcomes this geometric mismatch by minimizing the distance from any exciton generation site to the nearest D/A interface, making it the **standard architecture** in modern OSCs.

| Feature | Planar Bilayer | Bulk Heterojunction |
|---|---|---|
| D/A Interface area | Small (planar) | Very large (3-D network) |
| Exciton harvesting efficiency | $5 - 20\%$ | $\sim 90 - 100\%$ |
| Charge transport | Phase-pure (good) | Tortuous (can be limiting) |
| PCE achieved historically | $\sim 1\%$ | $> 19\%$ |
| Year introduced | 1986 (Tang) | 1995 (Heeger / Friend) |

(7 marks: 3 marks comparison + 2 marks for $L_D$ explanation + 2 marks for table)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bulk heterojunction organic solar cell is fabricated with a P3HT:PC\_{61}BM active layer. The donor P3HT has HOMO at $-4.9 \; \text{eV}$ and the acceptor PC\_{61}BM has LUMO at $-3.7 \; \text{eV}$. The measured open-circuit voltage under illumination is $V_{oc} = 0.58 \; \text{V}$.
(i) Verify whether the observed $V_{oc}$ is consistent with the empirical relation $V_{oc} = (E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A} - E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D})/q - 0.3 \; \text{V}$.
(ii) Comment on the charge-transfer driving force and whether this material combination is suitable for photovoltaic operation.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Verification of Open-Circuit Voltage (3 marks):**

Applying the empirical relation:

$$V_{oc}^{\text{theory}} = \dfrac{1}{q}\left(E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A} - E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D}\right) - 0.3 \; \text{V}$$

Substituting the energy level values (note: $E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A} = -3.7 \; \text{eV}$ and $E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D} = -4.9 \; \text{eV}$):

$$V_{oc}^{\text{theory}} = \dfrac{1}{1}\left[(-3.7) - (-4.9)\right] - 0.3$$

$$V_{oc}^{\text{theory}} = \dfrac{1.2}{1} - 0.3$$

$$V_{oc}^{\text{theory}} = 1.2 - 0.3 = 0.9 \; \text{V}$$

But the **measured** value is $V_{oc}^{\text{measured}} = 0.58 \; \text{V}$.

The discrepancy is $0.9 - 0.58 = 0.32 \; \text{eV}$, which is **larger than the typical $0.3 \; \text{eV}$ empirical loss**. The observed $V_{oc}$ is **lower than predicted**, indicating that this device suffers from **additional non-radiative recombination losses, energetic disorder, or shunt leakage**. The values are therefore **inconsistent** — the simple empirical formula over-estimates $V_{oc}$ by $\sim 0.32 \; \text{eV}$.

(To accept the simple empirical formula, the measured loss should be approximately $0.3 \; \text{eV}$; here it is $0.32 \; \text{eV}$, borderline but considered consistent by some examiners. Students should justify either interpretation.)

**(ii) Charge-Transfer Driving Force and Suitability (4 marks):**

The **LUMO offset** driving force for exciton dissociation is:

$$\Delta E_{\text{LUMO}} = E_{\text{LUMO}}^{D} - E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A}$$

For P3HT, $E_{\text{LUMO}}^{D} \approx -3.2 \; \text{eV}$; for PC\_{61}BM, $E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A} = -3.7 \; \text{eV}$:

$$\Delta E_{\text{LUMO}} = (-3.2) - (-3.7) = +0.5 \; \text{eV}$$

This is **well above the minimum $0.3 \; \text{eV}$ required** for efficient exciton dissociation — the system is energetically favorable for charge transfer.

Similarly, the **HOMO offset**:

$$\Delta E_{\text{HOMO}} = E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D} - E_{\text{HOMO}}^{A} = (-4.9) - (-6.1) = +1.2 \; \text{eV}$$

provides sufficient driving force for hole transfer.

**Conclusion:** The P3HT:PC\_{61}BM blend **is suitable for photovoltaic operation**, with both $\Delta E_{\text{LUMO}}$ and $\Delta E_{\text{HOMO}}$ exceeding the $0.3 \; \text{eV}$ threshold. The reduced $V_{oc}$ compared to the theoretical maximum is due to non-radiative voltage losses — a well-known limitation of fullerene-based systems that motivated the development of **non-fullerene acceptors (NFAs)** such as ITIC and Y6.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT757 — Module 1: Organic Solar Cells**

- **Definition:** OSCs use carbon-based organic semiconductors (polymers or small molecules) as the photoactive layer. (Not inorganic, not dye-sensitized, not perovskite.)
- **Architecture hierarchy:** Bilayer (historical) → **BHJ (current standard)** → Tandem (advanced).
- **Four operational steps:** Absorption → Exciton Formation → Diffusion & Dissociation → Charge Transport & Collection. (Always write in this order in exam.)
- **Exciton binding energy:** $E_b \sim 0.3 - 1.0 \; \text{eV}$ in organics (much larger than inorganics because $\varepsilon_r$ is small).
- **Exciton diffusion length:** $L_D \sim 5 - 20 \; \text{nm}$ — this is **why BHJ exists**.
- **BHJ domain size rule:** Phase separation domain $\leq L_D$ (i.e., $\leq 10 - 20 \; \text{nm}$).
- **D/A interface role:** LUMO offset $\geq 0.3 \; \text{eV}$ drives electron transfer from donor to acceptor.
- **Open-circuit voltage:** $V_{oc} \approx (E_{\text{LUMO}}^{A} - E_{\text{HOMO}}^{D})/q - 0.3 \; \text{V}$.
- **PCE formula:** $\eta = V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF / P_{in}$.
- **Fill Factor:** $FF = V_{mp} J_{mp} / (V_{oc} J_{sc})$, lies between 0.5 and 0.85 typically.
- **Bandgap shortcut:** $E_g (\text{eV}) = 1240 / \lambda_{\text{cutoff}}(\text{nm})$.
- **External Quantum Efficiency:** $\eta_{EQE} = \eta_{abs} \cdot \eta_{diff} \cdot \eta_{diss} \cdot \eta_{tr} \cdot \eta_{cc}$ — all five factors $\leq 1$.
- **Materials:** Donor (P3HT, PTB7, PM6, D18) ; Acceptor (PC\_{61}BM, PC\_{71}BM, ITIC, Y6).
- **Modern record:** PCE $> 19\%$ for single-junction OSCs using non-fullerene acceptors (PM6:Y6 family).
- **Standard test condition:** AM1.5G spectrum, $P_{in} = 100 \; \text{mW/cm}^2$, $T = 25 \; ^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never confuse LUMO(D) with LUMO(A) in $V_{oc}$ derivation.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never draw the BHJ as continuous layers — must show intermixed phases.
- **Units check:** $\lambda$ in nm for $E_g = 1240/\lambda$ formula; convert all energies to eV before applying $V_{oc}$ relation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Band Gap and Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Band Gap and Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Band Gap

In solid-state physics, the **Band Gap Energy (Eg)** of a semiconductor is defined as the minimum energy required to excite an electron from the top of the **Valence Band (VB)** to the bottom of the **Conduction Band (CB)**, thereby creating a free electron in the conduction band and a hole in the valence band. This energy threshold represents a "forbidden zone" where no allowed electronic states exist.

Mathematically, it is the energy difference between the conduction band minimum (Ec) and the valence band maximum (Ev):

$$E_g = E_c - E_v$$

The standard unit is **electron-volt (eV)**, where **1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Band gap is the *single most critical material parameter* governing photovoltaic performance. It dictates which portion of the solar spectrum a material can absorb and directly limits the maximum theoretical conversion efficiency of any single-junction solar cell.

## 1.2 The Staircase Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

Imagine a building with two floors connected by a staircase:
- The **ground floor** = Valence Band (electrons bound to atoms)
- The **first floor** = Conduction Band (electrons free to move and conduct electricity)
- The **staircase** = The Band Gap

If the staircase has **10 steps (Eg = 1.1 eV, like Silicon)**, even moderately energetic photons (sunlight) can push electrons up. If the staircase has **20 steps (Eg = 2.0 eV, like some organic polymers)**, only the most energetic photons (blue/UV light) can climb it — most light is wasted. If the staircase has only **5 steps (Eg = 0.5 eV, like Germanium)**, almost all light climbs up easily, but each excited electron releases very little energy when it falls back — *you get a small voltage*. 

**The "Goldilocks problem"**: There exists an *optimum* band gap (≈ 1.34 eV) that balances absorption of sunlight with output voltage, giving the highest possible efficiency.

## 1.3 Types of Band Gaps

| Band Gap Type | Momentum Requirement | Recombination Behavior | Representative Materials |
|---------------|----------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Direct Band Gap** | No phonon needed for transition | Strong absorption; efficient light emission | GaAs, CdTe, CIGS, Perovskites |
| **Indirect Band Gap** | Phonon (lattice vibration) required | Weaker absorption; longer lifetimes | Silicon (Si), Germanium (Ge) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Phonon-assisted transitions** in indirect band gap materials (like Si) require simultaneous interaction with a lattice vibration, which reduces the absorption coefficient by ~10³ compared to direct band gap semiconductors. This is why Si solar cells must be relatively thick (180–300 μm) while thin-film direct band gap cells need only 1–3 μm of active material.

## 1.4 Photon–Band Gap Interaction

A photovoltaic material absorbs a photon **only if** the photon's energy (hν) equals or exceeds the band gap:

$$h\nu \geq E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{absorption occurs}$$

$$h\nu < E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{photon passes through (transmission)}$$

The corresponding **absorption cut-off wavelength** is:

$$\lambda_{cutoff} = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1240 \text{ eV·nm}}{E_g \text{ (eV)}}$$

This relationship is fundamental for designing tandem (multi-junction) solar cells, where the top cell has a wide Eg and the bottom cell has a narrower Eg to capture different spectral regions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Photovoltaic Trade-off

The conversion efficiency (η) of a solar cell is governed by a fundamental trade-off between two competing loss mechanisms, both determined by Eg:

### Loss Mechanism 1: Transmission Loss (Sub-bandgap photons)
- Photons with energy **hν < Eg** cannot be absorbed.
- A material with **Eg too large** → loses a large portion of the solar spectrum.
- Fraction of usable solar photons increases as Eg decreases.

### Loss Mechanism 2: Thermalization Loss (Excess photon energy)
- Photons with energy **hν > Eg** are absorbed, but the excess energy (hν − Eg) is lost as heat via carrier thermalization within picoseconds.
- A material with **Eg too small** → loses a large amount of energy per absorbed photon.

**The maximum efficiency** corresponds to the Eg that optimally balances these two losses.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).** Master each symbol, unit, and boundary condition.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E_g = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy – band gap equivalence | eV or J |
| 2 | $\lambda_{cutoff} = \dfrac{1240}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | Cut-off wavelength for absorption | nm |
| 3 | $P_{in} = \displaystyle\int_0^\infty S(\lambda)\, d\lambda$ | Total incident solar power (AM 1.5G = 1000 W/m²) | W/m² |
| 4 | $J_{sc} = q\displaystyle\int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{sun}(E)\, dE$ | Short-circuit current density (photon flux × q) | A/m² |
| 5 | $J_0 = q\displaystyle\int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{bb}(T_c, E)\, dE$ | Reverse saturation current (radiative recombination) | A/m² |
| 6 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{k_B T_c}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{sc}}{J_0}+1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage from diode equation | V |
| 7 | $V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{E_g}{q} - \dfrac{k_B T_c}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_{sun}}{\Omega_c}\right)$ | Upper bound of Voc (radiative limit) | V |
| 8 | $P_{max} = J_{mp} \times V_{mp}$ | Maximum power point (where dP/dV = 0) | W/m² |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{J_{sc}\,V_{oc}\,FF}{P_{in}}$ | Power conversion efficiency | dimensionless (or %) |
| 10 | $FF = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc}+0.72)}{v_{oc}+1}$ | Empirical Fill Factor (FF vs. normalized Voc) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_{SQ} \approx 33.7\% \text{ at } E_g \approx 1.34 \text{ eV}$ | **Shockley–Queisser detailed balance limit** | % |

Where:
- **h** = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **c** = Speed of light = **3.0 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **k_B** = Boltzmann's constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **q** = Elementary charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **T_c** = Cell operating temperature = **300 K**
- **T_s** = Sun's blackbody temperature = **5778 K**
- **Φ_sun(E)** = Solar photon flux (AM 1.5G spectrum)
- **Φ_bb(T_c, E)** = Blackbody photon flux at cell temperature

## 2.3 The Shockley–Queisser Detailed Balance Principle

In 1961, **William Shockley and Hans-Joachim Queisser** established the foundational limit for single-junction solar cells. Their assumptions form the basis of all modern efficiency analysis:

1. The cell absorbs every photon with **hν ≥ Eg** (step function absorption).
2. The only recombination pathway is **radiative** (every absorbed photon eventually emits one photon back).
3. The cell emits photons as a **blackbody at T_c = 300 K** into a cone of solid angle 2π steradians.
4. The Sun is treated as a **blackbody at T_s = 5778 K**, emitting into a small solid angle (≈ 6.85 × 10⁻⁵ sr) as seen from Earth.

**The key engineering insight:** Even under these ideal assumptions, the maximum efficiency is **not 100%** — it is bounded by the second law of thermodynamics and the spectral mismatch between the sun's 5778 K emission and the cell's 300 K environment.

## 2.4 Real-World Significance

| Application | Implication of Band Gap Choice |
|-------------|---------------------------------|
| **Space photovoltaics** | Triple-junction GaInP/GaAs/Ge (Eg = 1.86 / 1.42 / 0.67 eV) reaches η > 40% |
| **Terrestrial Si cells** | Eg = 1.12 eV gives η ≈ 25% practical (vs. 33% SQ limit) |
| **Indoor PV (IoT)** | Lower Eg (~1.0 eV) optimal for LED spectra (mostly 400–700 nm) |
| **Building-integrated PV** | Semi-transparent wide-Eg perovskites (1.7–2.3 eV) for colored windows |
| **Tandem cells** | 1.68 eV wide-gap perovskite over 1.12 eV Si → 29.8% record efficiency |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Photon Energy to Band Gap Wavelength Conversion

**Starting Point:** The energy of a single photon is given by the Planck–Einstein relation:

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the threshold condition** for absorption. A photon is absorbed if and only if its energy equals or exceeds the band gap:

$$E_{photon} \geq E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{hc}{\lambda} \geq E_g$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the cut-off wavelength.** Rearranging to isolate λ (the longest wavelength absorbed):

$$\lambda \leq \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

$$\lambda_{cutoff} = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical constants** in convenient units. Using h = 4.136 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV·s and c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s = 3 × 10¹⁷ nm/s:

$$\lambda_{cutoff} = \frac{(4.136 \times 10^{-15} \text{ eV·s})(3 \times 10^{17} \text{ nm/s})}{E_g \text{ (eV)}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify** the leading constant:

$$\lambda_{cutoff} = \frac{1240.8 \text{ eV·nm}}{E_g \text{ (eV)}} \text{ nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This compact form is universally used in KTU numerical problems. **Memorize: 1240 eV·nm / Eg(eV) = λ_cutoff(nm).**

**Worked Example:** For Silicon (Eg = 1.12 eV):

$$\lambda_{cutoff} = \frac{1240}{1.12} \approx 1107 \text{ nm}$$

This means Si absorbs all visible light and part of the near-infrared, but not mid/far-IR.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Theoretical Efficiency as a Function of Band Gap (Shockley–Queisser Limit)

We derive the **efficiency vs. Eg curve** for an ideal single-junction solar cell under AM 1.5G illumination.

### Step A — Define the input solar power

Treat the Sun as a blackbody at temperature **T_s = 5778 K**, with spectral photon flux (Planck's law per unit area per unit energy):

$$\Phi_{sun}(E) = \frac{2\pi}{\hbar^3 c^2} \cdot \frac{E^2}{e^{E/(k_B T_s)} - 1}$$

The total incident power per unit area on a cell facing the Sun:

$$P_{in} = \int_0^\infty E \cdot \Phi_{sun}(E)\, dE = \sigma T_s^4 \cdot \Omega_{sun}$$

where $\sigma$ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant and $\Omega_{sun}$ is the solid angle subtended by the Sun (≈ 6.85 × 10⁻⁵ sr). Numerically, **P_in = 1000 W/m²** for AM 1.5G.

### Step B — Calculate the short-circuit current density

Each absorbed photon generates one electron–hole pair, contributing charge q. The current density is the integral of photon flux above the band gap, multiplied by q:

$$J_{sc}(E_g) = q \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{sun}(E)\, dE$$

**Interpretation:** As Eg increases, the lower limit of integration moves to higher energies → fewer photons absorbed → J_sc **decreases monotonically** with Eg.

### Step C — Calculate the radiative saturation current

The cell re-emits photons as a blackbody at T_c = 300 K into 2π steradians. The corresponding emitted photon flux is:

$$\Phi_{cell}(E) = \frac{2\pi}{\hbar^3 c^2} \cdot \frac{E^2}{e^{E/(k_B T_c)} - 1}$$

The corresponding reverse saturation current is:

$$J_0(E_g) = q \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{cell}(E)\, dE$$

**Interpretation:** As Eg increases, the exponential cutoff is harsher → J_0 **decreases exponentially** with Eg.

### Step D — Determine the open-circuit voltage

Setting the net current density to zero (J = 0) in the diode equation $J = J_{sc} - J_0(e^{qV/k_B T_c} - 1)$:

$$V_{oc}(E_g) = \frac{k_B T_c}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}(E_g)}{J_0(E_g)} + 1\right)$$

**Interpretation:** Because J_0 decreases exponentially with Eg, V_oc **increases monotonically** with Eg (and asymptotes toward Eg/q as Eg → ∞).

### Step E — Apply the upper-bound approximation for V_oc

In the radiative limit, the cell's emitted photon current exactly balances the absorbed solar photon current at V_oc. Using solid-angle arguments:

$$V_{oc} \approx \frac{E_g}{q} - \frac{k_B T_c}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{\Omega_{sun}}{\Omega_c}\right) = \frac{E_g}{q} - 0.30 \text{ V}$$

The logarithmic term contributes a fixed offset (≈ 0.30 V at 300 K), showing **V_oc is essentially proportional to Eg**.

### Step F — Construct the efficiency

At the maximum power point, define the **normalized voltage** $v_{oc} = qV_{oc}/(k_B T_c)$. The empirical fill factor is:

$$FF(v_{oc}) = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

**The final efficiency:**

$$\eta(E_g) = \frac{J_{sc}(E_g) \cdot V_{oc}(E_g) \cdot FF(v_{oc})}{P_{in}}$$

### Step G — Result: The Shockley–Queisser Curve

Substituting Steps B–F, the efficiency becomes an explicit function of Eg. Numerical evaluation yields:

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} \approx 33.7\% \quad \text{at} \quad E_g^{opt} \approx 1.34 \text{ eV}}$$

For a more rigorous treatment with full Planck-spectrum integration and the AM 1.5G spectrum (not just blackbody Sun), the optimum shifts slightly to **Eg ≈ 1.34 eV with η ≈ 33.7%** (Shockley–Queisser, 1961).

**Physical interpretation of the optimum:**
- For **Eg < 1.34 eV**: Thermalization losses dominate; absorbed photons have too much excess energy wasted as heat.
- For **Eg > 1.34 eV**: Transmission losses dominate; high-energy photons in the visible are not absorbed.
- At **Eg ≈ 1.34 eV**: A balance is struck — corresponds to the GaAs band gap (1.42 eV), explaining why high-efficiency single-junction cells often use GaAs.

### Step H — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A solar cell with Eg = 1.5 eV is illuminated by AM 1.5G sunlight (P_in = 1000 W/m²). Given that the absorbed photon current density is J_sc = 32 mA/cm² and the saturation current is J_0 = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ A/cm² at T = 300 K, calculate the open-circuit voltage, fill factor, and efficiency.

**Solution:**

(i) Open-circuit voltage:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0} + 1\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.02585 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{32 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-14}}\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.02585 \cdot \ln(3.2 \times 10^{11}) = 0.02585 \cdot 26.79$$

$$V_{oc} \approx 0.693 \text{ V}$$

(ii) Normalized voltage:

$$v_{oc} = \frac{q V_{oc}}{k_B T} = \frac{0.693}{0.02585} \approx 26.8$$

(iii) Fill Factor:

$$FF = \frac{26.8 - \ln(26.8 + 0.72)}{26.8 + 1} = \frac{26.8 - 3.314}{27.8} = \frac{23.486}{27.8} \approx 0.845$$

(iv) Power output:

$$P_{max} = J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF = 32 \times 0.693 \times 0.845 = 18.74 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$

(v) Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{18.74 \text{ mW/cm}^2}{100 \text{ mW/cm}^2} = 0.1874 \approx 18.7\%$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Open-Circuit Voltage from a Single Diode

Starting from the principle of detailed balance at V = V_oc, the cell's emitted photon flux (per unit area) integrated above Eg must equal the absorbed solar flux:

$$\int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{cell}(E) e^{qV_{oc}/k_B T_c} dE = \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{sun}(E) dE$$

**Step 1** — Factor the exponential out of the integral (approximation, valid when the dominant contribution is near Eg):

$$e^{qV_{oc}/k_B T_c} \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{cell}(E) dE = \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{sun}(E) dE$$

**Step 2** — Recognize that $\int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{cell}(E) dE$ is proportional to the cell's blackbody emission per unit solid angle times 2π sr, and $\int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{sun}(E) dE$ is the same quantity for the Sun times its solid angle $\Omega_{sun}$:

$$e^{qV_{oc}/k_B T_c} \cdot 2\pi \cdot N_{bb}(T_c, E_g) = \Omega_{sun} \cdot N_{bb}(T_s, E_g)$$

**Step 3** — Solve for V_oc:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{k_B T_c}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{\Omega_{sun} \cdot N_{bb}(T_s, E_g)}{2\pi \cdot N_{bb}(T_c, E_g)}\right)$$

**Step 4** — Take the limit where the cell's emission is dominated by energies near Eg (Würfel's generalized Planck law):

$$N_{bb}(T, E) \propto E^2 \exp(-E/k_B T)$$

**Step 5** — Substitute the boundary condition $E = E_g$ and take the logarithm:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{k_B T_c}{q}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{\Omega_{sun}}{2\pi}\right) + \frac{E_g}{k_B T_c}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_c}-\frac{1}{T_s}\right)^{-1}\cdot(\text{terms})\right]$$

**Step 6** — Final simplified form (Shockley–Queisser upper bound):

$$\boxed{V_{oc}^{max} \approx \frac{E_g}{q} - \frac{k_B T_c}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{\Omega_{sun}}\right) \approx \frac{E_g}{q} - 0.30 \text{ V}}$$

This is why **Voc loss ≈ 0.30 V is unavoidable** in any single-junction cell operating at 300 K under unconcentrated sunlight.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Table of Real Materials

| Material | Eg (eV) | Type | η (practical) | η (SQ limit) | Loss (%) |
|----------|---------|------|----------------|---------------|----------|
| Ge | 0.66 | Indirect | ~5% | ~30% | High |
| Si (crystalline) | 1.12 | Indirect | 26.7% | 33% | Modest |
| InGaAs | 1.42 | Direct | ~32% (tandem) | 33% | Minimal |
| GaAs | 1.42 | Direct | 29.1% | 33% | Small |
| CdTe | 1.45 | Direct | 22.1% | 32% | Moderate |
| CIGS | 1.10–1.70 | Direct | 23.35% | 32% | Moderate |
| Perovskite (CH₃NH₃PbI₃) | 1.55 | Direct | 26.7% | 31% | Modest |
| Organic (P3HT:PCBM) | 1.7–2.0 | Direct | ~19% | ~25% | High |
| Amorphous Si (a-Si:H) | 1.7 | Quasi-direct | 14% | ~25% | High |

> [!NOTE]
> The **gap between practical and SQ limit** is called the "detailed balance deficit." It is largest for materials with non-radiative recombination (Auger, defect-mediated, surface recombination), transport losses, and series resistance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Energy Band Structure of a Semiconductor

The following block diagram illustrates the energy landscape of a direct band gap semiconductor under illumination, showing the photon absorption and electron–hole pair generation process that ultimately leads to photocurrent.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["VALENCE BAND (Ev)"]:::vb
    nodeB["BAND GAP (Eg) - FORBIDDEN ZONE"]:::bg
    nodeC["CONDUCTION BAND (Ec)"]:::cb
    nodeD["Incident Photon (h nu >= Eg)"]:::photon
    nodeE["Free Electron (e-)"]:::elec
    nodeF["Hole (h+)"]:::hole
    nodeG["Thermalization Loss (Excess Energy as Heat)"]:::heat
    nodeH["Photon Transmitted (h nu less than Eg)"]:::lost
    nodeI["Photon to Electricity Conversion"]:::good

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeD -- "h nu greater than Eg absorbed" --> nodeA
    nodeD -- "h nu less than Eg transmitted" --> nodeH
    nodeA -- "electron jumps to CB" --> nodeC
    nodeC -- "excess energy dissipated as heat" --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeI

    classDef cb fill:#E27D4A,stroke:#9C4A1F,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px

    classDef good fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#1F8A6F,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Flow of Photovoltaic Conversion

This diagram shows the causal chain from incident solar spectrum to useful electrical output, including all band gap-dependent loss mechanisms.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sun["SOLAR SPECTRUM AM 1.5G"]:::input
    bandA["Eg = 0.5 to 1.0 eV Narrow Gap"]:::narrow
    bandB["Eg = 1.0 to 1.5 eV Optimum Gap"]:::opt
    bandC["Eg greater than 1.5 eV Wide Gap"]:::wide
    therm["THERMALIZATION LOSS Heat"]:::loss
    trans["TRANSMISSION LOSS Pass-through"]:::loss
    out["ELECTRICAL POWER Output"]:::power
    sq["SQ Limit 33.7 percent at 1.34 eV"]:::limit

    sun --> bandA
    sun --> bandB
    sun --> bandC
    bandA --> therm
    bandA --> out
    bandB --> out
    bandB --> sq
    bandC --> trans
    bandC --> out
    therm --> out
    trans --> out

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Efficiency vs. Band Gap Trade-off (Conceptual Map)

The block below maps the three regimes of photovoltaic operation dictated by Eg, the dominant loss mechanism in each, and the engineering response.

```mermaid

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the term **band gap energy** of a semiconductor. Explain why it is the most important parameter governing the efficiency of a photovoltaic cell.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The band gap energy (Eg) of a semiconductor is the minimum energy required to excite an electron from the valence band to the conduction band. It is the energy difference between the bottom of the conduction band (Ec) and the top of the valence band (Ev):

$$E_g = E_c - E_v$$

**Importance for PV efficiency:**

1. **Spectral matching:** Only photons with energy hν ≥ Eg are absorbed. A larger Eg means a smaller fraction of the solar spectrum is utilized (transmission loss).
2. **Voltage generation:** The open-circuit voltage Voc is fundamentally limited by Eg/q (Voc ≈ Eg/q − 0.30 V). A larger Eg gives a higher output voltage.
3. **Trade-off:** The maximum efficiency occurs at an optimum Eg ≈ 1.34 eV (Shockley–Queisser limit ≈ 33.7%), which balances these two competing effects.
4. **Material selection:** Eg determines which semiconductor (Si = 1.12 eV, GaAs = 1.42 eV, perovskite = 1.55 eV) is best suited for a given application.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Distinguish between **direct band gap** and **indirect band gap** semiconductors. Give one example of each, and state which type is more suitable for thin-film solar cells.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Direct Band Gap | Indirect Band Gap |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Band structure | CB minimum and VB maximum occur at the **same k-vector** | CB minimum and VB maximum occur at **different k-vectors** |
| Transition | Electron transition requires **no phonon** | Electron transition requires **phonon assistance** |
| Absorption coefficient | **High** (α ≈ 10⁴–10⁶ cm⁻¹) | **Low** (α ≈ 10²–10³ cm⁻¹) |
| Required thickness | Thin (1–3 μm sufficient) | Thick (180–300 μm required) |
| Example | GaAs, CdTe, CIGS, Perovskite | Si, Ge |
| PV application | Thin-film cells, tandem cells | Wafer-based crystalline Si cells |

**Conclusion:** Direct band gap semiconductors are more suitable for **thin-film solar cells** because their high absorption coefficient allows the active layer to be made very thin, reducing material cost and enabling flexible substrates.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q:** With the help of the **Shockley–Queisser detailed balance limit**, derive the expression for the maximum theoretical efficiency of a single-junction solar cell as a function of band gap energy. Show that the optimum band gap is approximately 1.34 eV. Calculate the cut-off wavelength for a solar cell with Eg = 1.45 eV. State the assumptions of the model.

**OR**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Shockley–Queisser Limit (7 Marks)

**Assumptions of the model:**
1. The active layer is thick enough to absorb every photon with hν ≥ Eg (step-function absorption).
2. The only recombination pathway is **radiative** (no Auger, no defect-mediated recombination).
3. The Sun behaves as a blackbody at T_s = 5778 K, emitting into the solid angle subtended at Earth (Ω_sun ≈ 6.85 × 10⁻⁵ sr).
4. The solar cell emits photons as a blackbody at T_c = 300 K into a 2π sr hemisphere.

**Step 1 — Photocurrent density:** Each absorbed photon above Eg contributes one electron of charge q:

$$J_{sc} = q \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{sun}(E) \, dE$$

**Step 2 — Saturation current density:** The cell's blackbody emission above Eg gives:

$$J_0 = q \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{bb}(T_c, E) \, dE$$

**Step 3 — Open-circuit voltage:** Setting J = 0 in the diode equation:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{k_B T_c}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0} + 1\right)$$

Using the Würfel generalized Planck law and approximating the dominant emission near E = Eg, the upper bound is:

$$V_{oc} \approx \frac{E_g}{q} - \frac{k_B T_c}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{\Omega_{sun}}\right) \approx \frac{E_g}{q} - 0.30 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Fill Factor and Efficiency:** Using the empirical formula:

$$FF = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}, \quad v_{oc} = \frac{qV_{oc}}{k_B T_c}$$

$$\eta(E_g) = \frac{J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical result:** Substituting the AM 1.5G spectrum and T_c = 300 K:

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} \approx 33.7\% \quad \text{at} \quad E_g^{opt} \approx 1.34 \text{ eV}}$$

The behavior of η vs. Eg shows:
- η → 0 as Eg → 0 (no voltage)
- η → 0 as Eg → ∞ (no current)
- η peaks at Eg ≈ 1.34 eV

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Q:** For a solar cell with Eg = 1.45 eV (e.g., CdTe), calculate the cut-off wavelength.

**Solution:**

The cut-off wavelength is the longest wavelength of light that can be absorbed:

$$\lambda_{cutoff} = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

Substituting h = 4.136 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV·s, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, and Eg = 1.45 eV:

$$\lambda_{cutoff} = \frac{(4.136 \times 10^{-15} \text{ eV·s})(3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s})}{1.45 \text{ eV}}$$

$$\lambda_{cutoff} = \frac{1.2408 \times 10^{-6} \text{ eV·m}}{1.45 \text{ eV}} = 8.56 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{cutoff} \approx 856 \text{ nm}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The cell absorbs all visible light (400–700 nm) and the near-infrared up to 856 nm. Photons with λ > 856 nm (mid-IR) are transmitted and lost. The maximum theoretical Voc is:

$$V_{oc}^{max} \approx \frac{1.45}{1} - 0.30 = 1.15 \text{ V}$$

This is consistent with the Shockley–Queisser limit, where CdTe (Eg = 1.45 eV) has a theoretical efficiency of approximately 32%.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q:** (a) Describe the two main loss mechanisms that prevent 100% photovoltaic conversion efficiency, and explain how they are governed by the band gap energy. (b) A solar cell has J_sc = 30 mA/cm², J_0 = 1.0 × 10⁻¹² A/cm², and FF = 0.82 at 300 K. Calculate its open-circuit voltage, power density, and efficiency under AM 1.5G illumination (P_in = 100 mW/cm²).

**OR**

#### Part (a) — Loss Mechanisms (7 Marks)

Two fundamental loss mechanisms limit the conversion efficiency of a single-junction solar cell:

**1. Transmission Loss (Sub-bandgap photons):**
- Photons with energy **hν < Eg** cannot be absorbed and pass through the cell.
- Fraction of transmitted power increases as Eg increases.
- A material with Eg = 2.0 eV transmits ~70% of solar power.
- **Governing equation:** Fraction transmitted = $\int_0^{E_g} E \cdot \Phi_{sun}(E) dE \big/ \int_0^\infty E \cdot \Phi_{sun}(E) dE$

**2. Thermalization Loss (Excess photon energy):**
- Photons with energy **hν > Eg** are absorbed, but the excess energy (hν − Eg) is converted to heat within ~picoseconds via carrier–phonon scattering.
- Fraction of energy lost to thermalization increases as Eg decreases.
- A material with Eg = 0.5 eV thermalizes ~50% of the absorbed energy.
- **Governing equation:** Thermalized fraction = $\int_{E_g}^{\infty} (h\nu - E_g) \Phi_{sun}(E) dE \big/ \int_{E_g}^{\infty} h\nu \cdot \Phi_{sun}(E) dE$

**How band gap governs these losses:**
- Increasing Eg → ↓ thermalization loss, ↑ transmission loss.
- Decreasing Eg → ↑ thermalization loss, ↓ transmission loss.
- The **balance** between these opposing trends gives the optimum at Eg ≈ 1.34 eV (SQ limit, η ≈ 33.7%).

**Additional losses** (not from band gap): reflection, series resistance, recombination, contact shadowing.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** J_sc = 30 mA/cm² = 0.030 A/cm²; J_0 = 1.0 × 10⁻¹² A/cm²; T = 300 K; FF = 0.82; P_in = 100 mW/cm².

**(i) Open-circuit voltage:**

$$V_{oc} = \frac{k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0} + 1\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.02585 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{0.030}{1.0 \times 10^{-12}} + 1\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.02585 \cdot \ln(3.0 \times 10^{10})$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.02585 \cdot 24.12 = 0.624 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Maximum power density:**

$$P_{max} = J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF = 0.030 \cdot 0.624 \cdot 0.82 = 0.01535 \text{ W/cm}^2 = 15.35 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$

**(iii) Power conversion efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{15.35 \text{ mW/cm}^2}{100 \text{ mW/cm}^2} = 0.1535 = 15.35\%$$

**Summary table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| J_sc | 30.0 mA/cm² |
| V_oc | 0.624 V |
| FF | 0.82 |
| P_max | 15.35 mW/cm² |
| **η** | **15.35%** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 ESE Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Wrong units on λ_cutoff:** Many students write λ in meters and forget to convert to nm. Always write the final answer as **λ_cutoff (nm) = 1240 / Eg (eV)**. Carrying units throughout earns 1 full mark.
>
> 2. **Confusing E_g with photon energy:** A common error is writing E = hν and substituting ν in Hz while the answer is expected in eV. Always state the conversion: **1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**.
>
> 3. **Shockley–Queisser derivation skips the radiative limit:** A 7-mark derivation question demands that you explicitly state the four assumptions. Writing the J_sc and J_0 integrals **without assumptions** will lose 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing V_oc with E_g/q:** V_oc is *not* equal to Eg/q — there is always a logarithmic offset (≈ 0.30 V at 300 K). Writing V_oc = Eg/q is a 1-mark penalty.
>
> 5. **Numerical error in FF formula:** Many students use the wrong empirical form. The **correct one** is $FF = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc}+0.72)}{v_{oc}+1}$, **not** the simpler form $FF \approx 0.85$.
>
> 6. **Missing the 1240 factor:** Memorizing 1240 eV·nm is essential. Students who try to derive from h and c in SI units waste 3–4 minutes and often make arithmetic errors.
>
> 7. **Sign of V_oc:** V_oc is always **positive** by definition. If you get a negative value, your J_0 > J_sc — check the calculation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 1: Band Gap & Efficiency):**

### Core Concepts (Definition-level mastery required)
- ☑ Band gap Eg = Ec − Ev; measured in eV; represents forbidden energy region.
- ☑ Direct vs. indirect band gap; transition rules; absorption coefficient difference (~10³).
- ☑ Cut-off wavelength: **λ_cutoff (nm) = 1240 / Eg (eV)** — instant numerical tool.
- ☑ Photon absorption condition: hν ≥ Eg.
- ☑ The two fundamental loss mechanisms: **Transmission (low hν) + Thermalization (excess hν)**.
- ☑ The two losses are governed by a single parameter — **band gap Eg**.

### Numerical Mastery (Problem-solving required)
- ☑ E = hc/λ = 1240/λ(nm) eV.
- ☑ V_oc = (kT/q) ln(J_sc/J_0 + 1) ≈ Eg/q − 0.30 V (radiative limit).
- ☑ FF formula: $FF = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc}+0.72)}{v_{oc}+1}$.
- ☑ η = (J_sc · V_oc · FF) / P_in.
- ☑ P_in (AM 1.5G) = 1000 W/m² = 100 mW/cm².

### Key Numerical Constants (No memory device — memorize!)
- ☑ h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s = 4.136 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV·s.
- ☑ c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s = 3 × 10¹⁷ nm/s.
- ☑ k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K = 8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K.
- ☑ q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.
- ☑ k_B T / q at 300 K = 0.02585 V.
- ☑ T_sun = 5778 K; T_cell = 300 K.
- ☑ hc = 1240 eV·nm (compact constant).

### The Shockley–Queisser Highlights
- ☑ SQ limit: **η_max ≈ 33.7% at Eg ≈ 1.34 eV** (single junction, unconcentrated, 300 K).
- ☑ Assumptions: step-function absorption, radiative-only recombination, blackbody Sun and cell.
- ☑ Implies **V_oc loss ≈ 0.30 V is unavoidable** at 300 K.
- ☑ The SQ limit is the **theoretical maximum for single-junction cells**; multi-junction and concentration can surpass it.

### Material Band Gaps (Frequently asked)
- ☑ Si = 1.12 eV (indirect); practical η ≈ 26.7%.
- ☑ GaAs = 1.42 eV (direct); practical η ≈ 29.1%.
- ☑ CdTe = 1.45 eV (direct); practical η ≈ 22.1%.
- ☑ CIGS = 1.10–1.70 eV (direct, tunable); practical η ≈ 23.35%.
- ☑ Perovskite CH₃NH₃PbI₃ = 1.55 eV (direct); practical η ≈ 26.7%.
- ☑ Amorphous Si = 1.7 eV (quasi-direct); practical η ≈ 14%.
- ☑ Ge = 0.66 eV (indirect); rarely used alone.

### Common KTU 2024 ESE Question Patterns
- ☑ "Define band gap and explain its role in PV efficiency" (3 marks).
- ☑ "Distinguish direct vs. indirect band gap with examples" (3 marks).
- ☑ "Derive the Shockley–Queisser limit" (7 marks).
- ☑ "Calculate λ_cutoff for Eg = X eV" (3–4 marks).
- ☑ "Calculate V_oc, FF, η given J_sc, J_0, T" (7 marks).
- ☑ "Explain the transmission and thermalization losses" (7 marks).

### Last-Memory Points
- ☑ The **optimum band gap is 1.34 eV**, NOT 1.1 eV (Si) — many students write this incorrectly.
- ☑ For a single-junction cell, **SQ limit is 33.7%**, not 30% or 40%.
- ☑ The Shockley–Queisser limit assumes **300 K cell temperature**; higher T → lower efficiency.
- ☑ Concentration (multi-sun illumination) breaks the SQ assumption and can yield higher efficiency.
- ☑ Multi-junction (tandem) cells exceed SQ because each junction has its own optimized Eg.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photogenerated Current Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Photogenerated Current Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Photogenerated Current Density** (denoted as **$J_{ph}$** or **$J_L$**) is the current density produced in a photovoltaic device due to the absorption of incident photons and the subsequent collection of the resulting photogenerated electron–hole pairs at the external electrodes of the solar cell.

Mathematically, it represents the total charge carrier flux per unit cross-sectional area generated by photon absorption, integrated over the active absorption region of the device:

$$J_{ph} = q \int_{0}^{W} G(x) \, dx$$

where:
- **$q$** = elementary charge = **$1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, C$**
- **$G(x)$** = generation rate of electron–hole pairs at depth $x$ (units: $cm^{-3} s^{-1}$)
- **$W$** = width of the depletion/absorption region

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** At short-circuit condition (i.e., $V = 0$), the **entire** photogenerated current density becomes available as the short-circuit current density: **$J_{sc} = J_{ph}$**. This is a high-yield relation frequently asked in university examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a wide rooftop (the **solar cell**) collecting rainwater. Raindrops fall from the sky (**photons**) onto the rooftop, and each drop that hits is converted into a measurable electric signal. The **rate** at which water flows out of the rooftop's drainage pipes (the **external circuit**) is analogous to the photogenerated current density.

- The **intensity of rainfall** is like the photon flux (incident power, $P_{in}$).
- The **size of the rooftop** corresponds to the active area $A$.
- The **amount of water collected per second** per square meter of rooftop = **photogenerated current density** ($A/m^2$).

If the rain is heavy and the rooftop is large, the flow rate is high — this corresponds to a higher $J_{ph}$. Similarly, brighter sunlight and a more efficient absorber produce a greater photogenerated current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Photogenerated Current Density vs. Photocurrent:** The two terms are often used interchangeably in KTU texts. Strictly, $J_{ph}$ is the *internal* generation current density, while $J_L$ often refers to the *light-generated current* in the equivalent circuit. For Module 1, treat them as identical.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Elementary charge | $q$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, C$ |
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $3 \times 10^{8} \, m/s$ |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, J \cdot s$ |
| Standard solar input (AM1.5G) | $P_{in}$ | $1000 \, W/m^2$ |
| Typical $J_{sc}$ for Si solar cell | $J_{sc}$ | $\approx 35\text{–}42 \, mA/cm^2$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The photogenerated current density emerges from a sequence of physical phenomena:

1. **Photon Incidence:** Photons from sunlight strike the semiconductor surface with energy $E = h\nu$.
2. **Photon Absorption:** If $E \geq E_g$ (bandgap), the photon is absorbed, transferring its energy to a valence electron.
3. **Electron–Hole Pair Generation:** The excited electron jumps to the conduction band, leaving a hole in the valence band. This creates an **EHP** (electron–hole pair).
4. **Carrier Separation:** The built-in electric field at the p–n junction sweeps electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side.
5. **Collection at Electrodes:** Carriers reaching the contacts contribute to external current flow.
6. **Current Density Formation:** The integrated flux of collected carriers per unit area yields $J_{ph}$.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why $E \geq E_g$?** Photons with energy less than the bandgap cannot bridge the valence-to-conduction band gap; they pass through or generate heat.
- **Why built-in field?** Without an electric field, generated EHPs would recombine before being collected, contributing nothing to current.
- **Why integration over depth?** Photon flux decays exponentially with depth (Beer–Lambert law), so generation rate is a function of position $x$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G(x) = \alpha \cdot \Phi_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$ | Generation rate at depth $x$ (Beer–Lambert) | $cm^{-3} s^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $J_{ph} = q \int_{0}^{W} G(x) \, dx$ | General definition of $J_{ph}$ | $A/m^2$ |
| 3 | $J_{ph} = q \, G_0 \, \dfrac{1 - e^{-\alpha W}}{\alpha}$ | $J_{ph}$ for exponentially absorbed light | $A/m^2$ |
| 4 | $J_{ph} = q \, \Phi_0 \, \left(1 - e^{-\alpha W}\right)$ | Simplified form (when $G_0 = \alpha \Phi_0$) | $A/m^2$ |
| 5 | $J_{ph} = q \, \Phi_0$ | Limit when $\alpha W \gg 1$ (complete absorption) | $A/m^2$ |
| 6 | $J_{sc} = J_{ph}$ | Short-circuit equality | $A/m^2$ |
| 7 | $\Phi_0 = \dfrac{P_{in}(1 - R)}{h\nu}$ | Incident photon flux with reflectance $R$ | $cm^{-2} s^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $\eta_{ext} = \dfrac{J_{sc}}{q \Phi_{inc}}$ | External quantum efficiency (EQE) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $J_{ph} = J_0 \left(e^{qV/kT} - 1\right) + J_{L}$ | Total cell current (superposition principle) | $A/m^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** The symbol $\Phi$ denotes photon flux density (number of photons per unit area per unit time). It must **not** be confused with work function $\phi$ or quantum efficiency $\eta$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The photogenerated current density is the **primary performance metric** for evaluating solar cell output before voltage considerations. Engineers use $J_{ph}$ to:

- Benchmark different absorber materials (Si, GaAs, CdTe, perovskites, polymer blends).
- Calculate the **maximum achievable $J_{sc}$** for bandgap tuning studies.
- Design anti-reflection coatings (since reflectance $R$ directly reduces $\Phi_0$).
- Optimize active layer thickness $W$ in organic/polymer solar cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photogenerated Current Density Under Uniform Generation

**Assumption:** Light is uniformly absorbed, so $G(x) = G_0$ (constant across the active layer).

**Starting Equation:**

$$J_{ph} = q \int_{0}^{W} G(x) \, dx$$
