**Step 1 — Substitute constant generation rate:**

$$J_{ph} = q \int_{0}^{W} G_0 \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Integrate (constant comes outside):**

$$J_{ph} = q \, G_0 \int_{0}^{W} dx$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate limits:**

$$J_{ph} = q \, G_0 \, \big[ x \big]_{0}^{W}$$

**Step 4 — Apply upper and lower bounds:**

$$J_{ph} = q \, G_0 \, (W - 0)$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{J_{ph} = q \, G_0 \, W}$$

This says: if generation is uniform, the total photogenerated current is simply charge × generation rate × thickness.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Photogenerated Current Density Under Exponential (Beer–Lambert) Absorption

This is the **most important KTU derivation** for Module 1.

**Starting Assumption:** According to the Beer–Lambert law, the photon flux decays exponentially with depth:

$$\Phi(x) = \Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

where:
- $\Phi_0$ = incident photon flux at the surface ($x = 0$)
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient of the semiconductor ($cm^{-1}$)

**Step 1 — Express generation rate as the divergence of photon flux:**

The generation rate equals the number of photons absorbed per unit volume per unit time, which is the spatial derivative of the photon flux:

$$G(x) = -\frac{d\Phi(x)}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the derivative:**

$$G(x) = -\frac{d}{dx}\left[\Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule:**

$$G(x) = -\Phi_0 \, (-\alpha) \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify signs:**

$$\boxed{G(x) = \alpha \, \Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}}$$

This is the **standard exponential generation profile** used in all PV derivations.

**Step 5 — Substitute into $J_{ph}$ definition:**

$$J_{ph} = q \int_{0}^{W} \alpha \, \Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x} \, dx$$

**Step 6 — Pull constants out:**

$$J_{ph} = q \, \alpha \, \Phi_0 \int_{0}^{W} e^{-\alpha x} \, dx$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integral (standard exponential integral):**

$$\int e^{-\alpha x} \, dx = -\frac{1}{\alpha} e^{-\alpha x}$$

So:

$$J_{ph} = q \, \alpha \, \Phi_0 \left[ -\frac{1}{\alpha} e^{-\alpha x} \right]_{0}^{W}$$

**Step 8 — Simplify (the $\alpha$ cancels):**

$$J_{ph} = q \, \Phi_0 \left[ -e^{-\alpha x} \right]_{0}^{W}$$

**Step 9 — Apply the limits:**

$$J_{ph} = q \, \Phi_0 \left[ -e^{-\alpha W} - (-e^{0}) \right]$$

**Step 10 — Simplify:**

$$J_{ph} = q \, \Phi_0 \left[ 1 - e^{-\alpha W} \right]$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{J_{ph} = q \, \Phi_0 \left( 1 - e^{-\alpha W} \right)}$$

### 3.3 Limiting Case Analysis

**Case 1: Thick absorber ($\alpha W \gg 1$)**

$$e^{-\alpha W} \to 0$$

Therefore:

$$J_{ph} \to q \, \Phi_0$$

This is the **maximum attainable photogenerated current density** — every photon entering the device is absorbed and collected.

**Case 2: Thin absorber ($\alpha W \ll 1$)**

Using Taylor expansion: $e^{-\alpha W} \approx 1 - \alpha W$

Therefore:

$$J_{ph} \approx q \, \Phi_0 \, (1 - [1 - \alpha W]) = q \, \Phi_0 \, \alpha \, W = q \, \alpha \, \Phi_0 \, W$$

This matches the **uniform generation result** — a sanity check confirming consistency between the two derivations.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Condition | Generation Profile | $J_{ph}$ Expression |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform | $G_0$ | $q \, G_0 \, W$ |
| Exponential (Beer–Lambert) | $\alpha \Phi_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | $q \, \Phi_0 (1 - e^{-\alpha W})$ |
| Thick absorber | $\alpha W \gg 1$ | $q \, \Phi_0$ |
| Thin absorber | $\alpha W \ll 1$ | $q \, \alpha \, \Phi_0 \, W$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Insight:** The two limiting cases show that the exponential model **smoothly transitions** into the uniform model as the absorber becomes thin — a powerful physical consistency check examiners appreciate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: From Photon Absorption to Photogenerated Current Density

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Photon Flux Phi_0] --> B[Photon Reaches Semiconductor Surface]
    B --> C{Energy E greater than Eg}
    C -->|No| D[Photon Transmitted or Generates Heat]
    C -->|Yes| E[Photon Absorbed at Depth x]
    E --> F[Generation of EHP: G(x) = alpha Phi_0 e power minus alpha x]
    F --> G[Built in Electric Field Separates Carriers]
    G --> H[Electrons Drift to n side]
    G --> I[Holes Drift to p side]
    H --> J[Carrier Collection at Electrodes]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Integration over Active Width W]
    K --> L[Jph equals q times Phi_0 times bracket 1 minus e power minus alpha W bracket]
    L --> M[Short Circuit Condition: V equals 0]
    M --> N[Jsc equals Jph Output]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Solar Cell Showing $J_{ph}$ Contribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Incident Light Power P_in] --> AR[Anti Reflection Coating]
    AR --> ABS[Absorber Layer with Generation G x]
    ABS --> SEP[Junction Electric Field Separator]
    SEP --> COLL[Carrier Collection Contacts]
    COLL --> EXT[External Load Circuit]
    EXT --> OUT1[Photogenerated Current Density Jph]
    ABS --> REC[Recombination Losses R_L]
    REC --> LOSS[Loss Component subtracted from Jph]
    LOSS --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> EQC[Equivalent Circuit: Jph Source parallel Diode]
    EQC --> TERM[Terminal Current J equals Jph minus J0 exp qV by kT minus 1]
```

### 4.3 Depth-Dependent Generation Profile (Conceptual Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Surface x equals 0] --> S1[Depth x small: G maximum equals alpha Phi_0]
    S1 --> S2[Depth x equals 1 by alpha: G equals 0.368 of maximum]
    S2 --> S3[Depth x equals 2 by alpha: G equals 0.135 of maximum]
    S3 --> S4[Depth x equals W: G equals Phi_0 alpha e power minus alpha W]
    S4 --> NOTE{Is alpha W much greater than 1?}
    NOTE -->|Yes| RES1[All Photons Absorbed: Jph approaches q Phi_0]
    NOTE -->|No| RES2[Partial Absorption: Jph equals q Phi_0 times 1 minus e power minus alpha W]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always annotate your diagrams with the actual exponential decay relationship $G(x) = \alpha \Phi_0 e^{-\alpha x}$. Examiners specifically look for whether students correctly label $\Phi_0$ as the *surface* photon flux and not the bulk flux.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:**
Define photogenerated current density. State its SI units and write the general integral expression used to compute it.

**Model Answer:**

Photogenerated current density ($J_{ph}$) is the current density produced in a photovoltaic device due to the collection of photo-excited charge carriers (electrons and holes) generated by absorbed incident photons of energy greater than or equal to the semiconductor bandgap.

**SI Units:** Amperes per square meter ($A/m^2$) or equivalently $mA/cm^2$ in practical PV contexts.

**General Integral Expression:**

$$J_{ph} = q \int_{0}^{W} G(x) \, dx$$

where $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, C$ is the elementary charge, $G(x)$ is the generation rate of electron–hole pairs at depth $x$, and $W$ is the width of the active absorption region.

At short-circuit ($V = 0$), the entire photogenerated current appears as $J_{sc}$: $J_{sc} = J_{ph}$.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:**
How does the photogenerated current density depend on the absorption coefficient $\alpha$ and active layer thickness $W$? Write the relevant mathematical expression.

**Model Answer:**

The photogenerated current density under Beer–Lambert exponential absorption is given by:

$$J_{ph} = q \, \Phi_0 \left(1 - e^{-\alpha W}\right)$$

**Dependencies:**

1. **On absorption coefficient $\alpha$:** A higher $\alpha$ (stronger absorption per unit length) increases $J_{ph}$ because more photons are absorbed within the active layer.

2. **On active layer thickness $W$:** A thicker $W$ increases $J_{ph}$ because there is more absorbing material.

3. **Limiting behavior:**
   - When $\alpha W \gg 1$ (thick absorber), $J_{ph} \to q\Phi_0$ (saturation).
   - When $\alpha W \ll 1$ (thin absorber), $J_{ph} \approx q\alpha\Phi_0 W$ (linear regime).

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]:**

**(a)** Derive the expression for photogenerated current density $J_{ph}$ in a solar cell under exponential (Beer–Lambert) light absorption. Discuss the limiting cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon solar cell has an absorption coefficient $\alpha = 10^{4} \, cm^{-1}$ at wavelength $\lambda = 700 \, nm$. The active layer thickness is $W = 200 \, \mu m$. The incident photon flux at the surface is $\Phi_0 = 10^{17} \, cm^{-2} s^{-1}$. Calculate the photogenerated current density. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The derivation proceeds in 10 steps as established in Section 3.2 of these notes:

**Step 1:** Beer–Lambert law gives $\Phi(x) = \Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$.

**Step 2:** Generation rate is the negative spatial derivative of photon flux: $G(x) = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dx}$.

**Step 3:** Differentiate: $G(x) = \alpha \, \Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute into $J_{ph} = q\int_0^W G(x) dx$:

$$J_{ph} = q \int_{0}^{W} \alpha \, \Phi_0 \, e^{-\alpha x} \, dx$$

**Step 5:** Pull constants outside:

$$J_{ph} = q \, \alpha \, \Phi_0 \int_{0}^{W} e^{-\alpha x} \, dx$$

**Step 6:** Integrate:

$$J_{ph} = q \, \alpha \, \Phi_0 \left[ -\frac{1}{\alpha} e^{-\alpha x} \right]_{0}^{W}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify:

$$J_{ph} = q \, \Phi_0 \left(1 - e^{-\alpha W}\right)$$

**Limiting Cases:**

- **Thick absorber** ($\alpha W \gg 1$): $e^{-\alpha W} \to 0$, so $J_{ph} \to q\Phi_0$.
- **Thin absorber** ($\alpha W \ll 1$): Using Taylor series, $J_{ph} \approx q\alpha\Phi_0 W$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $\alpha = 10^4 \, cm^{-1}$
- $W = 200 \, \mu m = 200 \times 10^{-4} \, cm = 0.02 \, cm$
- $\Phi_0 = 10^{17} \, cm^{-2} s^{-1}$
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, C$

**Step 1 — Compute the product $\alpha W$:**

$$\alpha W = 10^4 \times 0.02 = 200$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponential term:**

$$e^{-\alpha W} = e^{-200} \approx 0 \quad (\text{negligibly small})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$J_{ph} = q \, \Phi_0 \left(1 - e^{-\alpha W}\right)$$

$$J_{ph} = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (10^{17}) \times (1 - 0)$$

$$J_{ph} = 1.602 \times 10^{-2} \, A/cm^2$$

**Step 4 — Convert to standard SI units:**

$$J_{ph} = 1.602 \times 10^{-2} \, A/cm^2 = 16.02 \, mA/cm^2 = 160.2 \, A/m^2$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{J_{ph} \approx 16.02 \, mA/cm^2}$

This corresponds to a fully-absorbing regime since $\alpha W = 200 \gg 1$.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]:**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the physical mechanism by which photogenerated current density arises in a p–n junction solar cell under illumination. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GaAs solar cell has an absorption coefficient $\alpha = 10^{4} \, cm^{-1}$ at $\lambda = 800 \, nm$. The active layer thickness is $W = 1 \, \mu m$. If $\Phi_0 = 5 \times 10^{16} \, cm^{-2} s^{-1}$, compute the photogenerated current density and comment on whether the absorber behaves as thin or thick. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Mechanism (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):**

1. **Photon Incidence:** Photons strike the front surface of the p–n junction solar cell with energy $h\nu$.

2. **Photon Absorption:** A photon is absorbed if $h\nu \geq E_g$, transferring energy to a valence electron.

3. **EHP Generation:** The electron is promoted to the conduction band, creating an electron in the conduction band and a hole in the valence band at depth $x$ with rate $G(x) = \alpha\Phi_0 e^{-\alpha x}$.

4. **Charge Separation:** The built-in electric field at the depletion region sweeps electrons toward the n-region and holes toward the p-region.

5. **Collection and Current Flow:** Carriers reaching the ohmic contacts flow through the external circuit, producing current density $J_{ph}$.

6. **Short-Circuit Condition:** When $V = 0$ (terminals shorted), all generated carriers contribute to $J_{sc} = J_{ph}$.

**Key Equation:** $J_{ph} = q\int_0^W G(x)\,dx$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $\alpha = 10^4 \, cm^{-1}$
- $W = 1 \, \mu m = 10^{-4} \, cm$
- $\Phi_0 = 5 \times 10^{16} \, cm^{-2} s^{-1}$
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, C$

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha W$:**

$$\alpha W = 10^4 \times 10^{-4} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Compute exponential term:**

$$e^{-\alpha W} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$J_{ph} = q \, \Phi_0 \left(1 - e^{-1}\right)$$

$$J_{ph} = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (5 \times 10^{16}) \times (1 - 0.3679)$$

$$J_{ph} = (8.01 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.6321)$$

$$J_{ph} = 5.063 \times 10^{-3} \, A/cm^2$$

**Step 4 — Convert:**

$$J_{ph} \approx 5.06 \, mA/cm^2 = 50.6 \, A/m^2$$

**Step 5 — Comment on absorber regime:**

Since $\alpha W = 1$ is **not much greater than 1**, the absorber is in the **intermediate regime**, and only about **63.21%** of the incident photons are absorbed. To approach full absorption, the layer thickness should be increased to $\sim 3/\alpha = 3 \, \mu m$ or more.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{J_{ph} \approx 5.06 \, mA/cm^2 \text{ (intermediate regime)}}$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Photogenerated Current Density Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $W$ from $\mu m$ to $cm$ when $\alpha$ is given in $cm^{-1}$. This produces wrong $\alpha W$ values.
>
> 2. **Confusing $J_{ph}$ with $J_{sc}$ (–1 mark):** $J_{ph}$ is the generation current density; $J_{sc}$ equals $J_{ph}$ *only* at $V = 0$. At other voltages, $J_{sc} \neq J_{ph}$.
>
> 3. **Sign Confusion (–1 mark):** $J_{ph}$ is conventionally positive (photocurrent flows opposite to diode current in standard sign convention).
>
> 4. **Missing Limiting-Case Discussion (–2 marks):** Examiners specifically look for the two limiting cases (thick and thin absorber). Skipping these forfeits easy marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Beer–Lambert Justification (–1 mark):** Always state the Beer–Lambert assumption $\Phi(x) = \Phi_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ before differentiating.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Photogenerated Current Density ($J_{ph}$)** is the current density produced by collection of photo-excited charge carriers.
- **Units:** $A/m^2$ or $mA/cm^2$.
- **General formula:** $J_{ph} = q \int_0^W G(x) \, dx$.
- **Exponential absorption formula:** $J_{ph} = q\Phi_0 (1 - e^{-\alpha W})$.
- **Generation rate under Beer–Lambert:** $G(x) = \alpha \Phi_0 e^{-\alpha x}$.
- **Limiting Case 1 (thick absorber, $\alpha W \gg 1$):** $J_{ph} \to q\Phi_0$ — every photon is absorbed.
- **Limiting Case 2 (thin absorber, $\alpha W \ll 1$):** $J_{ph} \approx q\alpha\Phi_0 W$ — linear dependence on $W$.
- **Short-circuit identity:** $J_{sc} = J_{ph}$ (when $V = 0$).
- **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, C$ — memorize this value.
- **Engineering implication:** Higher $\alpha$ and larger $W$ increase $J_{ph}$, but with diminishing returns (saturation at $\alpha W \gg 1$).
- **Photo-active layer design rule of thumb:** Choose $W \geq 3/\alpha$ to absorb >95% of incident photons.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Absorption Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Absorption Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Absorption Coefficient (α)** of a semiconductor or photovoltaic material is defined as the fractional decrease in the intensity of electromagnetic radiation per unit distance as it propagates through the absorbing medium. Mathematically, it quantifies the rate at which the optical power decays inside the material at a given photon energy (hν) or wavelength (λ).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The absorption coefficient α(λ) is the characteristic parameter of a photovoltaic absorber layer that describes how deeply incident photons of a specific wavelength penetrate into the material before being absorbed and generating electron–hole pairs. It is the single most important optical parameter in PV device design.

In SI units, α is measured in **m⁻¹** (more commonly **cm⁻¹** in thin-film photovoltaics).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine shining a torchlight through a glass tank filled with muddy water. The further down the beam travels, the dimmer it becomes — the mud particles "absorb" (or scatter) the photons. The **absorption coefficient** tells you *how quickly* the light fades with depth.

- A **high α** → light is absorbed very near the surface (muddy, opaque water).
- A **low α** → light travels deep into the material before being absorbed (clear water).

For a solar cell, this matters enormously: if α is small, light penetrates through the active layer and is wasted, so the layer must be **thick enough** to capture most of the sun's spectrum. Conversely, if α is huge, only a thin film is needed — this is the principle behind **thin-film solar cells** (CdTe, CIGS, perovskites, organic PV).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Physical Insight:** The absorption coefficient is **not** a single number for a material — it is a function of photon energy (or wavelength). Near the bandgap, α changes by several orders of magnitude over a small energy range, which is why α(λ) curves are always plotted.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **α** : Absorption coefficient, typically in **cm⁻¹**
- **Penetration Depth (δ = 1/α)** : The depth at which light intensity falls to **1/e (≈ 36.8 %)** of its surface value
- **Extinction Coefficient (k)** : The imaginary part of the complex refractive index
- **Beer–Lambert Law** : Governs exponential decay of intensity
- **Tauc Relation** : Links α to the optical bandgap (E_g)

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall for KTU:** α is "how greedy" a material is for light. A high-α material is greedy → needs a thin layer. A low-α material is gentle → needs a thick layer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Incident Flux Enters the Material**
A monochromatic beam of intensity $I_0$ strikes the surface of the semiconductor at normal incidence.

**Step 2 — Beer–Lambert Exponential Decay**
As the beam penetrates to a depth $x$, its intensity decreases exponentially:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

**Why exponential?** Because each infinitesimal layer dx absorbs a fixed *fraction* (not a fixed *amount*) of the light that reaches it. This is mathematically identical to radioactive decay or RC discharge.

**Step 3 — Rearrangement for α**
Solving for the absorption coefficient:

$$\alpha = -\frac{1}{x} \ln\!\left(\frac{I(x)}{I_0}\right) = \frac{1}{x} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I(x)}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Connection to the Extinction Coefficient (k)**
The absorption coefficient is related to the **extinction coefficient** $k$ of the complex refractive index $\tilde{n} = n + ik$:

$$\alpha = \frac{4\pi k}{\lambda}$$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength in vacuum. This bridges macroscopic optics (α) with microscopic material polarizability (k).

**Step 5 — Connection to the Complex Dielectric Function**
Since $\tilde{n}^2 = \tilde{\varepsilon}$, both $n$ and $k$ derive from the **dielectric function** $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$:

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_1^2 + \varepsilon_2^2} - \varepsilon_1}{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Direct vs. Indirect Bandgap Behaviour**
This is the **most-tested concept** in KTU Module-1 examinations:

| Type | Tauc Equation | Plot Slope |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct allowed** | $\alpha h\nu = A\,(h\nu - E_g)^{1/2}$ | $(\alpha h\nu)^2$ vs $h\nu$ gives straight line |
| **Indirect allowed** | $\alpha h\nu = A\,(h\nu - E_g \pm E_p)^2$ | $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2}$ vs $h\nu$ gives straight line |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The square-root in the direct-gap exponent and the square in the indirect-gap exponent are the *single most-asked* sub-questions. Memorise the Tauc table above.

**Step 7 — Penetration Depth (δ)**
The inverse of α gives the **penetration depth**, the distance at which 63.2 % of photons have been absorbed:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$

- For **c-Si**: δ ≈ 100–1000 µm (indirect bandgap → weak absorber in the red)
- For **GaAs**: δ ≈ 1–10 µm (direct bandgap → strong absorber)
- For **organic/polymer**: δ ≈ 100–500 nm (very strong absorption)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | Beer–Lambert law — intensity vs depth | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{x}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Direct evaluation of α from measured transmission | cm⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{4\pi k}{\lambda}$ | Relation to extinction coefficient k | cm⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\delta = 1/\alpha$ | Penetration depth (1/e intensity) | cm |
| 5 | $\alpha h\nu = A(h\nu - E_g)^{1/2}$ | Tauc relation — **direct** bandgap | cm⁻¹·eV |
| 6 | $\alpha h\nu = A(h\nu - E_g)^{2}$ | Tauc relation — **indirect** bandgap | cm⁻¹·eV |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{(n-1)^2 + k^2}{(n+1)^2 + k^2}$ | Reflectivity (needed to correct I₀ for surface loss) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T = (1-R)^2 e^{-\alpha d}$ | Transmission through thickness d (with two surfaces) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $k = \dfrac{\alpha \lambda}{4\pi}$ | Extinction coefficient from α and λ | dimensionless |
| 10 | $F(\alpha) = \dfrac{(1-R)^2}{2\alpha}$ | Photogeneration current density (approx.) | A·m⁻²·s |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value or "given by" notations use \vert instead of \vert \vert to keep the table parser happy.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar cell absorber design:** Choosing layer thickness ≥ 1/α at the peak of the solar spectrum (≈ 550 nm) minimises transmission losses.
- **Anti-reflection coating:** The refractive index $n$ (the *real* part of $\tilde{n}$) determines the optimal AR coating thickness $d = \lambda / (4n)$.
- **Encapsulant and polymer-window design:** Polymer encapsulants (EVA, PVB) must have low α in the visible range so they do not steal photons from the cell.
- **Tauc analysis:** Every published research paper on a new photovoltaic material (perovskite, organic, CIGS) reports a Tauc plot to extract $E_g$.
- **Filter and photodetector engineering:** α(λ) defines the spectral response of photodiodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Beer–Lambert Law from First Principles

Consider a thin slice of material of thickness $dx$ at depth $x$. Let $I(x)$ be the intensity entering this slice and $I(x) + dI$ the intensity leaving it. The fractional decrease is proportional to the thickness:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \, dx$$

where α is a positive constant (the absorption coefficient). Separating variables and integrating from 0 to $x$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I(x)} \frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

This gives:

$$\ln I(x) - \ln I_0 = -\alpha x$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

This is the **Beer–Lambert law** of optical absorption. ∎

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relation Between α and the Extinction Coefficient k

The electric field of a plane wave propagating in a lossy medium with complex refractive index $\tilde{n} = n + ik$ is:

$$E(z) = E_0 \, e^{i(\omega t - \tilde{k} z)} = E_0 \, e^{i(\omega t - \frac{2\pi \tilde{n}}{\lambda} z)}$$

Expanding $\tilde{n}$:

$$E(z) = E_0 \, e^{i(\omega t - \frac{2\pi n}{\lambda} z)} \, e^{-\frac{2\pi k}{\lambda} z}$$

The intensity is proportional to $|E|^2$, so:

$$I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\frac{4\pi k}{\lambda} z}$$

Comparing with $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$:

$$\alpha = \frac{4\pi k}{\lambda}$$

∎

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tauc Relation for a Direct Bandgap Semiconductor

For a **direct allowed transition**, the joint density of states near the band edge goes as:

$$J_{cv}(E) \propto \sqrt{h\nu - E_g}$$

Fermi's Golden Rule gives the absorption coefficient as:

$$\alpha(h\nu) = A \cdot |M|^2 \cdot J_{cv}(E)$$

where $M$ is the matrix element (assumed constant near the edge) and $A$ is a constant involving the refractive index. Therefore:

$$\alpha h\nu = A (h\nu - E_g)^{1/2}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$(\alpha h\nu)^2 = A^2 (h\nu - E_g)$$

This is a **straight line** when $(\alpha h\nu)^2$ is plotted versus $h\nu$, and the **x-intercept** of the linear extrapolation gives $E_g$. ∎

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Penetration Depth from Beer–Lambert

By definition, penetration depth is the distance at which $I(\delta) = I_0 / e$. Substituting into Beer–Lambert:

$$\frac{I_0}{e} = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha \delta}$$

$$\frac{1}{e} = e^{-\alpha \delta}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$-1 = -\alpha \delta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$

∎

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical)

**Problem:** The transmittance of a 2 µm thick polymer film is measured to be 35 % (at 600 nm). The reflectivity of the polymer at this wavelength is 8 %. Compute (a) α and (b) the penetration depth.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Use the transmission formula for a slab with two surfaces:

$$T = (1-R)^2 \, e^{-\alpha d}$$

$$0.35 = (1-0.08)^2 \, e^{-\alpha \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$0.35 = (0.92)^2 \, e^{-2 \times 10^{-4} \alpha}$$

$$0.35 = 0.8464 \, e^{-2 \times 10^{-4} \alpha}$$

$$e^{-2 \times 10^{-4} \alpha} = \frac{0.35}{0.8464} = 0.4135$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$-2 \times 10^{-4} \, \alpha = \ln(0.4135) = -0.8839$$

$$\alpha = \frac{0.8839}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.42 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**(b)** Penetration depth:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{4.42 \times 10^{3}} = 2.26 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 2.26 \, \mu\text{m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A 2 µm film is *just* thick enough to absorb most of the 600 nm light. Doubling the thickness to 4 µm would give a transmittance of about 17 %, so there is a diminishing return — useful for designing ultrathin organic solar cells.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow of Optical Absorption in a Photovoltaic Absorber

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sunlight I0 hits surface] --> B[Partial reflection R]
    B --> C[Transmitted intensity I0 x 1-R enters absorber]
    C --> D[Photon encounters valence electron]
    D --> E{Is photon energy h nu greater than or equal to bandgap E_g?}
    E -- No --> F[Photon passes through weakly absorbed]
    E -- Yes --> G[Electron promoted to conduction band]
    G --> H[Electron-hole pair generated at depth x]
    H --> I[Carrier diffuses to junction]
    I --> J[Carrier collected as photocurrent]
    F --> K[Sub-bandgap transmission loss]
    H --> L[Beer-Lambert decay I x = I0 exp -alpha x]
    L --> M[Penetration depth delta = 1 by alpha]
```

### 4.2 Tauc Plot Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Measure Transmission T lambda] --> B[Compute alpha lambda using T = 1-R squared exp -alpha d]
    B --> C[Compute alpha h nu product]
    C --> D{Direct or indirect bandgap?}
    D -- Direct --> E[Plot alpha h nu squared vs h nu]
    D -- Indirect --> F[Plot alpha h nu to the 1/2 vs h nu]
    E --> G[Linear region extrapolation to x-axis]
    F --> G
    G --> H[X-intercept equals bandgap E_g]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Optical Loss Channels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Incident Solar Spectrum] --> M1[Reflection Loss at front surface]
    M1 --> M2[Absorption in Encapsulant and Glass]
    M2 --> M3[Absorption in TCO layer]
    M3 --> M4[Absorption in Absorber alpha h nu]
    M4 --> M5[Transmission through absorber if thickness less than 1 by alpha]
    M5 --> M6[Back contact absorption or reflection]
    M6 --> Out[Remaining photon flux at back]
    M4 --> Use[Useful Photogeneration G x = alpha exp -alpha x]
    Use --> Jsc[Short circuit current density Jsc]
```

### 4.4 Comparison of Absorber Strength

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Weak Absorber - indirect bandgap] --> A1[Example c-Si]
    A1 --> A2[alpha approx 10 to power 2 to 10 to power 3 per cm]
    A2 --> A3[Needs thick wafer 200 to 300 micrometre]
    B[Strong Absorber - direct bandgap] --> B1[Example GaAs CdTe Perovskite]
    B1 --> B2[alpha approx 10 to power 4 to 10 to power 5 per cm]
    B2 --> B3[Thin film 1 to 5 micrometre sufficient]
    C[Ultra-strong Absorber - organic polymer] --> C1[Example P3HT PCBM]
    C1 --> C2[alpha approx 10 to power 5 to 10 to power 6 per cm]
    C2 --> C3[Active layer 100 to 200 nm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each Mermaid node ID is purely alphanumeric (e.g., `M1`, `A2`, `B3`). Labels are clean plain text with no markdown formatting, ensuring flawless rendering on any Mermaid viewer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define absorption coefficient of a semiconductor. Mention its SI unit and explain how it determines the optimum thickness of a photovoltaic absorber layer.

**Model Answer:**

The absorption coefficient **α** of a semiconductor is defined as the reciprocal of the distance into the medium at which the intensity of the incident electromagnetic radiation falls to **1/e** of its value at the surface. It is governed by the **Beer–Lambert law**:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

**SI unit:** m⁻¹ (or, more commonly in thin-film PV, **cm⁻¹**).

The optimum absorber thickness is set by the **penetration depth** $\delta = 1/\alpha$ at the wavelength of peak solar flux (≈ 550 nm). For strong absorbers (direct-gap, α ≈ 10⁵ cm⁻¹), δ ≈ 100 nm → a sub-micron film suffices. For weak absorbers (indirect-gap Si, α ≈ 10³ cm⁻¹), δ ≈ 10 µm → a 200–300 µm wafer is needed. The thickness must be ≥ 1/α so that ≥ 63 % of photons are absorbed.

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark definition + 1 mark unit + 1 mark thickness connection]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Differentiate between the Tauc relations for direct and indirect bandgap semiconductors. Which one is more useful for thin-film solar cells and why?

**Model Answer:**

The Tauc relations are:

$$\text{Direct bandgap:} \quad \alpha h\nu = A(h\nu - E_g)^{1/2}$$

$$\text{Indirect bandgap:} \quad \alpha h\nu = A(h\nu - E_g)^2$$

For a direct-gap material, the **square** of $(\alpha h\nu)$ is plotted against $h\nu$; for an indirect-gap material, the **square root** of $(\alpha h\nu)$ is plotted. The x-intercept of the linear region gives $E_g$.

**Direct bandgap** materials (GaAs, CdTe, CIGS, perovskites, organic polymers) are **more useful for thin-film solar cells** because their absorption coefficient is 10²–10³ times higher than that of indirect materials (Si, Ge) near the band edge. This allows efficient absorption in films only 1–2 µm thick, drastically reducing material cost and enabling flexible / lightweight modules.

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark direct equation + 1 mark indirect equation + 1 mark thin-film advantage]**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the Beer–Lambert law of optical absorption starting from the assumption that the fractional decrease in intensity in a thin slice is proportional to its thickness. Show that the absorption coefficient α is related to the penetration depth δ by $\delta = 1/\alpha$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CdTe thin film of thickness 2 µm has a reflectivity of 12 % at 800 nm. The measured transmittance at this wavelength is 18 %. Calculate (i) the absorption coefficient α, (ii) the extinction coefficient k, and (iii) the fraction of incident photons absorbed in the film. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Differential form of the law**

Consider an infinitesimal slice of the absorbing medium of thickness $dx$ at a depth $x$ from the illuminated surface. Let $I(x)$ be the intensity entering the slice. The decrease $dI$ across the slice is proportional to $I(x)$ and to $dx$:

$$-dI = \alpha \, I(x) \, dx$$

The constant of proportionality α is, by definition, the **absorption coefficient**.

**Step 2 — Separation of variables**

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Integration**

Integrating from the surface ($x=0$, $I=I_0$) to a general depth $x$ (intensity $I(x)$):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I(x)} \frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \int_0^x dx$$

$$\ln I(x) - \ln I_0 = -\alpha x$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I(x)}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha x$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiation — Beer–Lambert law**

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

**Step 5 — Penetration depth**

By definition, the penetration depth δ is the distance at which the intensity has dropped to $1/e$ of the surface value, i.e. $I(\delta) = I_0/e$. Substituting:

$$\frac{I_0}{e} = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha \delta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-1} = e^{-\alpha \delta}$$

Taking the natural logarithm: $-1 = -\alpha\delta$, hence

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}}$$

This completes the derivation. ∎

**[7 marks: 1 mark setup + 2 marks integration + 1 mark final law + 1 mark definition of δ + 2 marks final δ = 1/α]**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $d = 2\,\mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{cm}$, $R = 0.12$, $T = 0.18$, $\lambda = 800\,\text{nm} = 8 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm}$.

**Step 1 — Transmission formula for a slab with two surfaces**

$$T = (1-R)^2 \, e^{-\alpha d}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for α**

$$0.18 = (1-0.12)^2 \, e^{-\alpha \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$0.18 = (0.88)^2 \, e^{-2 \times 10^{-4} \alpha}$$

$$0.18 = 0.7744 \, e^{-2 \times 10^{-4} \alpha}$$

$$e^{-2 \times 10^{-4} \alpha} = \frac{0.18}{0.7744} = 0.2324$$

$$-2 \times 10^{-4} \, \alpha = \ln(0.2324) = -1.459$$

$$\alpha = \frac{1.459}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.295 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**(i) Absorption coefficient: $\alpha \approx 7.30 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^{-1}$** ✅

**Step 3 — Extinction coefficient k**

$$k = \frac{\alpha \lambda}{4\pi} = \frac{7.295 \times 10^{3} \times 8 \times 10^{-5}}{4\pi}$$

$$k = \frac{0.5836}{12.566} = 0.0464$$

**(ii) Extinction coefficient: $k \approx 0.046$** ✅

**Step 4 — Fraction of photons absorbed**

$$A = 1 - T = 1 - 0.18 = 0.82$$

**(iii) Fraction absorbed: 82 %** ✅

> [!NOTE]
> The fraction absorbed is also obtainable from $A = 1 - (1-R)^2 e^{-\alpha d}$, which yields the same result. 82 % absorption in only 2 µm confirms CdTe is a strong direct-gap absorber — ideal for thin-film PV.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** What is the Tauc plot? Explain how it is used to determine the optical bandgap of a semiconductor. Distinguish between the plots for direct and indirect bandgap materials with appropriate equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The absorption coefficient of a direct-bandgap semiconductor near its absorption edge follows the Tauc relation with $A = 2.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ cm}^{-1}\text{eV}^{-1/2}$. The bandgap is 1.42 eV. Calculate α at photon energies of (i) 1.50 eV and (ii) 1.60 eV. Comment on how rapidly α rises above the bandgap. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Tauc plot** is a graphical method, proposed by J. Tauc, for determining the **optical bandgap** ($E_g$) of a semiconductor from its absorption spectrum. It is based on the relationship between the absorption coefficient α and the photon energy $h\nu$ near the absorption edge.

For a **direct allowed transition**:

$$\alpha h\nu = A (h\nu - E_g)^{1/2}$$

Squaring:

$$(\alpha h\nu)^2 = A^2 (h\nu - E_g)$$

This is a **straight line** when $(\alpha h\nu)^2$ is plotted against $h\nu$. The intercept on the $h\nu$ axis (obtained by extrapolating the linear region to $(\alpha h\nu)^2 = 0$) gives $E_g$.

For an **indirect allowed transition**:

$$\alpha h\nu = A (h\nu - E_g \pm E_p)^2$$

Taking the square root:

$$(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2} = \sqrt{A}(h\nu - E_g \pm E_p)$$

A plot of $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2}$ versus $h\nu$ gives a straight line; the intercept gives $(E_g - E_p)$ or $(E_g + E_p)$ depending on whether phonon absorption or emission dominates.

**Procedure to extract $E_g$:**
1. Measure transmission $T(\lambda)$ and reflectivity $R(\lambda)$ experimentally.
2. Compute α(λ) using $T = (1-R)^2 e^{-\alpha d}$.
3. Convert λ to $h\nu$ and compute $\alpha h\nu$.
4. Plot $(\alpha h\nu)^2$ vs $h\nu$ (direct) or $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2}$ vs $h\nu$ (indirect).
5. Extrapolate the linear region to the $h\nu$ axis; the intercept is $E_g$.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Difference:** Direct-gap → plot $(\alpha h\nu)^2$ and look for $(h\nu - E_g)^{1/2}$ behaviour. Indirect-gap → plot $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2}$ and look for $(h\nu - E_g)^2$ behaviour. The *powers* (½ and 2) are swapped between the two cases.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $A = 2.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ cm}^{-1}\text{eV}^{-1/2}$, $E_g = 1.42 \text{ eV}$.

**Tauc relation for direct bandgap:**

$$\alpha = \frac{A (h\nu - E_g)^{1/2}}{h\nu}$$

**(i) At $h\nu = 1.50$ eV:**

$$h\nu - E_g = 1.50 - 1.42 = 0.08 \text{ eV}$$

$$(h\nu - E_g)^{1/2} = \sqrt{0.08} = 0.2828 \text{ eV}^{1/2}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{4} \times 0.2828}{1.50} = \frac{7070}{1.50} = 4.71 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha(1.50 \text{ eV}) \approx 4.71 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$

**(ii) At $h\nu = 1.60$ eV:**

$$h\nu - E_g = 1.60 - 1.42 = 0.18 \text{ eV}$$

$$(h\nu - E_g)^{1/2} = \sqrt{0.18} = 0.4243 \text{ eV}^{1/2}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{4} \times 0.4243}{1.60} = \frac{10607}{1.60} = 6.63 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha(1.60 \text{ eV}) \approx 6.63 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$

**Comment on the rate of rise:**

Going from $h\nu = 1.50$ to $1.60$ eV (only 0.10 eV above $E_g$):
- $\alpha$ increases from $4.71 \times 10^{3}$ to $6.63 \times 10^{3}$ cm⁻¹ — a factor of **1.41**.
- The ratio $\Delta\alpha/\alpha_1 \approx 41$ % per 0.1 eV.

This rapid rise is the signature of a **direct bandgap**: the joint density of states grows as $\sqrt{h\nu - E_g}$, so even a tiny increase above $E_g$ produces a substantial jump in α. In contrast, an indirect bandgap would rise much more slowly (∝ $(h\nu - E_g)^2$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** α comes out in **cm⁻¹**, not m⁻¹. If $d$ is in cm, then α is in cm⁻¹.
> 2. **Reflectivity correction:** Many students write $T = e^{-\alpha d}$ and forget $(1-R)^2$. This gives a *higher* apparent α.
> 3. **Tauc plot axes:** Students swap the powers — direct uses $(\alpha h\nu)^2$, indirect uses $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2}$. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Penetration depth sign:** Some students write $\delta = -\alpha$. The correct relation is $\delta = 1/\alpha$, always positive.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert λ:** $k = \alpha \lambda / 4\pi$ needs λ in the *same* length unit as $1/\alpha$ (commonly cm, not nm).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Absorption coefficient α** is the fractional decrease in light intensity per unit distance inside a material; SI unit **cm⁻¹** in thin-film PV.
- **Beer–Lambert law:** $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ — exponential decay governs optical absorption.
- **Penetration depth** $\delta = 1/\alpha$ — the 1/e decay length — dictates the required absorber thickness.
- **Relation to extinction coefficient:** $\alpha = 4\pi k / \lambda$ — bridges macroscopic optics with the complex refractive index.
- **Tauc relation — direct gap:** $\alpha h\nu = A(h\nu - E_g)^{1/2}$ → plot $(\alpha h\nu)^2$ vs $h\nu$.
- **Tauc relation — indirect gap:** $\alpha h\nu = A(h\nu - E_g)^2$ → plot $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2}$ vs $h\nu$.
- **Direct-gap absorbers** (GaAs, CdTe, CIGS, perovskites, polymers) have $\alpha \sim 10^{4} - 10^{5}$ cm⁻¹ → 1–2 µm films suffice.
- **Indirect-gap absorbers** (Si, Ge) have $\alpha \sim 10^{2} - 10^{3}$ cm⁻¹ → 200–300 µm wafers needed.
- **Experimental extraction:** measure T(λ) and R(λ), then use $T = (1-R)^2 e^{-\alpha d}$ — never forget the $(1-R)^2$ factor.
- **Transmission correction:** Reflectivity losses must always be removed before evaluating α.
- **Exponent rule to remember:** Direct-gap exponent = **½**, indirect-gap exponent = **2**. Plot axes swap the power.
- **Photogeneration profile:** $G(x) = \alpha \, \eta_{abs} \, N_{ph}(0) \, e^{-\alpha x}$ — maximum at the surface for high α.
- **Solar cell design rule of thumb:** Absorber thickness ≥ 3/α ensures > 95 % absorption of above-bandgap photons.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Conversion Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Power Conversion Efficiency # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE)**, often denoted by the Greek letter **η (eta)**, is the fundamental performance metric of any photovoltaic (PV) device. It is formally defined as the ratio of the maximum electrical power output ($P_{max}$) generated by a solar cell to the incident optical power input ($P_{in}$) from the solar spectrum, typically measured under standardized illumination conditions. $$ \eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}} $$ where $J_{sc}$ is the **short-circuit current density**, $V_{oc}$ is the **open-circuit voltage**, and $FF$ is the **fill factor** of the device. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Power Conversion Efficiency is the single most cited parameter in any PV literature. Under KTU 2024 scheme assessment, students are expected to derive the expression for PCE, interpret the J-V characteristic curve, and discuss the various loss mechanisms that limit efficiency in both inorganic and polymer-based solar cells. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Test Conditions (STC)** defined by the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC 60904): > - **AM1.5G solar spectrum** (Air Mass 1.5 Global) > - **Irradiance:** $1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ ($\equiv 1 \text{ sun}$) > - **Cell temperature:** $25^\circ \text{C}$ (or 298.15 K) > - **Reference PCE baseline (Silicon):** ~26.7% (monocrystalline, theoretical lab record) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are pouring a **10-litre bucket of water** into a series of funnels, pipes, and filters. The water that finally reaches the bottom tank represents the *useful electrical output*, while the water lost to splashing, evaporation, leaks, and filter clogging represents *energy losses*. **PCE is simply the percentage of input water that successfully reaches the collection tank.** In a solar cell: - The **input "bucket"** = photons from the sun ($1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ irradiance). - The **funnels** = the semiconductor (silicon, perovskite, polymer) which absorbs photons. - The **splashing/leaks** = photons reflected off the surface, photons with energy below the bandgap, and excited charge carriers that recombine before being collected. A solar cell with **η = 20%** means that out of every $1000 \text{ W}$ of sunlight striking it, **200 W** is converted to usable electricity, while the remaining $800 \text{ W}$ is wasted as heat, reflected light, or unabsorbed radiation. ## 1.3 Why PCE Matters in Engineering | Metric | Significance | | :--- | :--- | | **Commercial Viability** | Higher η = more power per unit area = lower levelized cost of electricity (LCOE). | | **Space Constraints** | Rooftop and aerospace applications demand maximum power from minimum area. | | **Material Benchmark** | Used to compare emerging technologies (perovskite, organic PV, polymer blends) against silicon. | | **Theoretical Limits** | The Shockley–Queisser limit (~**33.7%**) for single-junction cells sets the absolute ceiling. | | **Multi-junction Potential** | Tandem/concentrator cells can exceed 47% by stacking multiple bandgaps. | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Current–Voltage (J-V) Characteristic Curve The electrical behaviour of an illuminated solar cell is captured by its **J-V curve**, where the vertical axis is the current density $J$ (in $\text{mA/cm}^2$) and the horizontal axis is the applied voltage $V$ (in $\text{V}$). Three cardinal points define this curve: 1. **Short-circuit current density ($J_{sc}$):** The maximum current density produced when the external circuit is shorted ($V = 0$). It depends on the **number of photogenerated charge carriers** that are successfully collected. 2. **Open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$):** The maximum voltage produced when no current flows ($J = 0$). It is thermodynamically bounded by the bandgap ($E_g$) of the absorber. 3. **Maximum Power Point (MPP):** The point on the J-V curve where the product $J \times V$ is maximum. The corresponding voltage and current are $V_{mp}$ and $J_{mp}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constant:** The thermal voltage $V_T = \frac{k_B T}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K, where $k_B = 1.380 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ is Boltzmann's constant and $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ is the elementary charge. ## 2.2 The Fill Factor (FF) — The "Squareness" of the Curve The **Fill Factor** quantifies how "rectangular" the J-V curve is. A perfect cell has $FF = 1$ (a true rectangle), while real devices have $FF < 1$ due to resistive and recombination losses. $$ FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot J_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}} = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}} $$ **Typical FF values:** - Monocrystalline silicon: **0.75 – 0.83** - Polycrystalline silicon: **0.70 – 0.78** - Polymer:fullerene organic solar cells: **0.60 – 0.75** - Perovskite solar cells: **0.75 – 0.88** (champion devices) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Expression | Meaning / Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Power Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Master equation for PCE | | Fill Factor | $FF = \frac{V_{mp} J_{mp}}{V_{oc} J_{sc}}$ | Quality of J-V curve shape | | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0} + 1\right)$ | Empirically bounded by $E_g/q$ | | Short-Circuit Current Density | $J_{sc} = q \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi(E) \cdot EQE(E) \, dE$ | Integral of photon flux $\times$ external quantum efficiency | | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \frac{k_B T}{q}$ | $\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K | | Diode Ideality Factor | $n = 1 \text{ (ideal)} \rightarrow 2 \text{ (SRH recombination)}$ | Tunnelling-enhanced devices have $n > 2$ | | Shockley-Queisser Limit | $\eta_{max} \approx 33.7\%$ | Single-junction, AM1.5G, $E_g \approx 1.34$ eV | | Detailed Balance Limit | $\eta = \frac{V_{oc,rad} \cdot FF_{rad}}{E_g} \cdot \int \dots$ | Radiative limit only | | External Quantum Efficiency | $EQE(\lambda) = \frac{\text{electrons collected}}{\text{photons incident}}$ | Wavelength-dependent response | | Series Resistance Loss | $\Delta FF \approx \frac{J_{mp} R_s}{V_{oc}}$ | Empirical FF degradation | > [!NOTE] > In the expression $V_{oc} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0} + 1\right)$, the vertical bar denotes the absolute value or magnitude of $J_{sc}/J_0$; in plain text this is written as `abs(J_sc / J_0)`. The symbol $J_0$ is the **reverse saturation current density** of the diode. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the PCE Framework The PCE expression $\eta = \frac{J_{sc} V_{oc} FF}{P_{in}}$ is the **master design equation** for every PV engineer. It decomposes a single efficiency number into three independently optimisable subsystems: - **Maximise $J_{sc}$** → Anti-reflection coatings, light-trapping textures, plasmonic nanostructures, high-mobility transport layers. - **Maximise $V_{oc}$** → Choice of donor–acceptor energy offsets (crucial in polymer PV), reduced recombination, interface engineering. - **Maximise $FF$** → Reduce series resistance ($R_s$), increase shunt resistance ($R_{sh}$), optimise charge extraction layers. This decomposition is exactly how **NREL Best Research-Cell Efficiency charts** are categorised, and KTU examiners expect students to be able to **discuss optimisation strategies for each term individually**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: PCE from the Solar-Cell Equivalent Circuit A real solar cell can be modelled as a **current source in parallel with a diode**, with a series resistance $R_s$ and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$. Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law at the output terminal: $$ J = J_{ph} - J_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + J R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + J R_s}{R_{sh}} $$ where $J_{ph}$ is the **photogenerated current density** (under ideal conditions, $J_{ph} \approx J_{sc}$). **Step 1 — Identify the boundary conditions:** For the **short-circuit condition**, set $V = 0$: $$ J_{sc} = J_{ph} - J_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{J_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{J_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}} $$ For the **open-circuit condition**, set $J = 0$: $$ 0 = J_{ph} - J_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}} $$ **Step 2 — Define the maximum power point:** The power density delivered to the load is $P = J \cdot V$. To find the maximum, set the derivative to zero: $$ \frac{dP}{dV} = \frac{d(J V)}{dV} = J + V \frac{dJ}{dV} = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Maximum power point current and voltage ($J_{mp}, V_{mp}$):** Solving the simultaneous conditions at MPP gives $J_{mp}$ and $V_{mp}$ as implicit functions of $J_{sc}, V_{oc}, R_s, R_{sh}$. **Step 4 — Maximum extractable power density:** $$ P_{max} = J_{mp} \cdot V_{mp} $$ **Step 5 — Fill factor (in terms of the implicit solution):** $$ FF = \frac{P_{max}}{J_{sc} V_{oc}} = \frac{J_{mp} V_{mp}}{J_{sc} V_{oc}} $$ **Step 6 — Final PCE expression:** Substituting $P_{max}$ into the definition: $$ \eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}} $$ This is the canonical KTU-expected form. The input power density $P_{in}$ under STC equals $1000 \text{ W/m}^2$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Ideal-Diode Approximation ($R_s \to 0, R_{sh} \to \infty$) When parasitic resistances are negligible, the J-V relation reduces to the **ideal diode equation**: $$ J = J_{sc} - J_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] $$ Setting $J = 0$ to find $V_{oc}$: $$ 0 = J_{sc} - J_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] $$ Rearranging the exponential term: $$ J_{sc} = J_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] $$ Since $J_{sc} \gg J_0$ in well-designed cells (typically by **6 to 10 orders of magnitude**), the “$-1$” term is dropped: $$ \frac{J_{sc}}{J_0} = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) $$ Taking the natural logarithm on both sides: $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0}\right) = \frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T} $$ Solving explicitly for $V_{oc}$: $$ V_{oc} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0}\right) $$ This is the **canonical KTU formula** for open-circuit voltage. It shows that: - A **higher bandgap** → lower $J_{sc}$ (fewer absorbed photons) but **lower $J_0$** → exponentially higher $V_{oc}$. - The **optimum bandgap** that balances these two effects is $E_g \approx 1.34 \text{ eV}$ (the Shockley–Queisser optimum for AM1.5G). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Analytical Approximation of the Fill Factor For an **ideal diode** ($n = 1$, $R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$), an empirical Green’s expression for FF is widely used: $$ FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1} $$ where $v_{oc} = \frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}$ is the **normalised open-circuit voltage**. **Step-by-step reasoning:** - The numerator $(v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72))$ represents the *useful* voltage delivered at MPP minus a logarithmic correction for the diode’s exponential turn-on. - The denominator $(v_{oc} + 1)$ is the total dynamic voltage range. - For typical $V_{oc} \approx 0.7$ V at $n=1$, we get $v_{oc} \approx 27$, giving $FF_0 \approx 0.89$. Including series resistance ($R_s$) losses: $$ FF = FF_0 \left(1 - \frac{J_{sc} R_s}{V_{oc}}\right) $$ This shows that **even a few ohms of series resistance drastically reduces FF**, especially for high-current (large-area) cells. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style) > **Problem:** A polymer solar cell under AM1.5G illumination ($P_{in} = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$) has the following measured parameters: $J_{sc} = 18 \text{ mA/cm}^2$, $V_{oc} = 0.82 \text{ V}$, and $FF = 0.72$. Calculate the Power Conversion Efficiency. **Solution:** Step 1 — Convert $J_{sc}$ to standard SI units ($\text{A/m}^2$): $$ J_{sc} = 18 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 18 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}/10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 180 \text{ A/m}^2 $$ Step 2 — Compute $P_{max}$ per unit area: $$ P_{max} = J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF = 180 \times 0.82 \times 0.72 = 106.27 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ Step 3 — Compute PCE: $$ \eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{106.27}{1000} = 0.10627 = 10.63\% $$ **Result:** $\boxed{\eta \approx 10.63\%}$ This is a typical value for state-of-the-art **polymer:fullerene bulk heterojunction (BHJ)** solar cells (e.g., P3HT:PCBM systems). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: PCE Master Equation Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decomposition of PCE** into its constituent physical mechanisms, the engineering strategies to optimise each, and the dominant loss pathways in real devices. ```mermaid flowchart TD SunIn["AM1.5G Solar Irradiance
Pin = 1000 W per m2"]:::input Absorb["Photon Absorption
Energy greater than Eg"]:::proc Gen["Exciton Generation
Bound electron-hole pair"]:::proc Split["Exciton Dissociation
Donor-Acceptor Interface"]:::proc Trans["Charge Transport
Holes and Electrons"]:::proc Coll["Charge Collection
At Electrodes"]:::proc Pmax["Pmax at MPP
Jmp times Vmp"]:::out Jsc["Short-Circuit Current Density Jsc"]:::term Voc["Open-Circuit Voltage Voc"]:::term FF["Fill Factor FF"]:::term SunIn --> Absorb Absorb --> Gen Gen --> Split Split --> Trans Trans --> Coll Coll --> Pmax Gen --> Jsc Split --> Jsc Coll --> Jsc Absorb --> Voc Split --> Voc Trans --> FF Coll --> FF Jsc --> Eff["eta equals Jsc times Voc times FF over Pin"] Voc --> Eff FF --> Eff Pmax --> Eff Loss1["Reflection Loss"]:::loss Loss2["Transmission Loss"]:::loss Loss3["Thermalisation Loss"]:::loss Loss4["Recombination Loss"]:::loss Loss5["Series Resistance Loss"]:::loss SunIn -.-> Loss1 Absorb -.-> Loss2 Gen -.-> Loss3 Split -.-> Loss4 Trans -.-> Loss5 classDef input fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4a017,color:#3a2c00 classDef proc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#001a33 classDef out fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#0f3d1f classDef term fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#c82333,color:#4d0a14 classDef loss fill:#f0e0d0,stroke:#a0522d,color:#3d1f00 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: J-V Curve and Maximum Power Point Mapping This diagram illustrates the geometric interpretation of the Fill Factor on the J-V characteristic curve. ```mermaid flowchart LR Axis["J-V Plane
J vertical mA per cm2
V horizontal Volts"]:::frame JscA["Point: 0 V, Jsc
Short Circuit"]:::marker VocA["Point: Voc, 0 mA
Open Circuit"]:::marker MppA["Point: Vmp, Jmp
Maximum Power Point"]:::marker RecA["Large Rectangle
Voc times Jsc"]:::rect SmallA["Small Rectangle
Vmp times Jmp"]:::rect JscA --> RecA VocA --> RecA MppA --> SmallA FFbox["FF equals Small Rectangle Area
divided by Large Rectangle Area"]:::formula SmallA --> FFbox RecA --> FFbox classDef frame fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666,color:#000 classDef marker fill:#ffe082,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000 classDef rect fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 classDef formula fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Loss-Cascade for a Typical Polymer Solar Cell ```mermaid flowchart TD In["Sunlight Input
100 percent Pin"]:::start R1["Reflection at Air-Glass Interface
about 4 percent lost"]:::loss R2["Transmission through Absorber
below-bandgap photons about 20 percent lost"]:::loss R3["Non-absorbing Polymers
low extinction coefficient about 5 percent lost"]:::loss R4["Exciton Recombination
before dissociation about 15 percent lost"]:::loss R5["Bimolecular Recombination
during transport about 5 percent lost"]:::loss R6["Series and Contact Resistance
Joule heating about 3 percent lost"]:::loss Out["Useful Electrical Output
eta equals 10 to 15 percent for typical polymer PV"]:::out In --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 --> Out classDef start fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef loss fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 classDef out fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Power Conversion Efficiency of a solar cell. State the standard test conditions under which it is measured. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** Power Conversion Efficiency (η) is defined as the ratio of the maximum electrical power output generated by a photovoltaic device to the incident solar power input. $$ \eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}} $$ **Standard Test Conditions (STC) per IEC 60904:** - Spectrum: AM1.5G - Irradiance: $1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ - Cell temperature: $25^\circ\text{C}$ ### Question 2: What is Fill Factor? How does series resistance affect it? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Fill Factor (FF) is the ratio of the maximum power generated by the solar cell to the product of its open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current density. It indicates the "squareness" of the J-V curve. $$ FF = \frac{V_{mp} J_{mp}}{V_{oc} J_{sc}} $$ **Effect of series resistance ($R_s$):** A high $R_s$ reduces FF because part of the generated voltage drops across the internal parasitic resistance instead of the external load. The empirical relation is: $$ FF \approx FF_0 \left(1 - \frac{J_{sc} R_s}{V_{oc}}\right) $$ where $FF_0$ is the ideal fill factor. Hence minimising $R_s$ (through better contacts, transparent electrodes like ITO, and highly conductive transport layers) is critical. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) of an ideal solar cell under illumination, starting from the ideal diode equation. (7 marks) **(b)** A silicon solar cell has $J_{sc} = 38 \text{ mA/cm}^2$, $V_{oc} = 0.68 \text{ V}$, $FF = 0.82$, and is illuminated under AM1.5G ($1000 \text{ W/m}^2$). Calculate: (i) Maximum power density, (ii) PCE, and (iii) Comment on why this value is below the Shockley–Queisser limit. (7 marks) **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1 — Write the ideal diode equation under illumination:** $$ J = J_{sc} - J_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{V_T}\right) - 1\right] $$ (Here $n = 1$ is assumed for an ideal cell.) **Step 2 — Apply the open-circuit condition ($J = 0$):** $$ 0 = J_{sc} - J_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{V_T}\right) - 1\right] $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange the exponential term:** $$ J_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{V_T}\right) - 1\right] = J_{sc} $$ **Step 4 — Since $J_{sc} \gg J_0$, drop the “$-1$” term:** $$ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{V_T}\right) \approx \frac{J_{sc}}{J_0} $$ **Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm on both sides:** $$ \frac{V_{oc}}{V_T} = \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0}\right) $$ **Step 6 — Solve for $V_{oc}$ using $V_T = k_B T / q$:** $$ V_{oc} = \frac{k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0}\right) $$ For a non-ideal cell, the ideality factor $n$ appears: $$ \boxed{V_{oc} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0}\right)} $$ **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Maximum power density:** Convert $J_{sc}$ to SI: $J_{sc} = 38 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 380 \text{ A/m}^2$. $$ P_{max} = J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF = 380 \times 0.68 \times 0.82 = 211.86 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **(ii) Power Conversion Efficiency:** $$ \eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{211.86}{1000} = 0.21186 = 21.19\% $$ **(iii) Reason below Shockley–Queisser limit:** The Shockley–Queisser (SQ) limit of ~33.7% assumes: - **Only radiative recombination** (no non-radiative losses). - **Unity external quantum efficiency** above the bandgap. - **Perfect light trapping** (every photon above $E_g$ is absorbed). - **No series resistance** or shunt losses. The real silicon cell falls short because of: 1. **Auger recombination** in heavily-doped regions (dominant in indirect-gap semiconductors). 2. **Free-carrier absorption** of sub-bandgap photons. 3. **Reflective losses** at the front surface (mitigated by anti-reflection coatings). 4. **Finite $R_s$ and finite $R_{sh}$** that degrade FF. 5. **Non-radiative Shockley–Read–Hall (SRH) recombination** at defects and grain boundaries. ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Define Fill Factor and explain, with a labelled J-V characteristic curve, the significance of the Maximum Power Point. Discuss any two strategies to enhance the Fill Factor of a polymer solar cell. (7 marks) **(b)** A polymer:fullerene bulk heterojunction solar cell has $V_{oc} = 0.88 \text{ V}$, $J_{sc} = 16 \text{ mA/cm}^2$, and $FF = 0.68$. Calculate its PCE. If the input irradiance drops to $500 \text{ W/m}^2$, how does $J_{sc}$ change (assuming linear response), and what is the new PCE? (7 marks) **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition:** Fill Factor is the ratio of the maximum obtainable power density to the theoretical maximum power density ($V_{oc} \times J_{sc}$). It ranges from 0 to 1. $$ FF = \frac{V_{mp} J_{mp}}{V_{oc} J_{sc}} $$ **J-V Curve Significance:** The J-V curve plotted under illumination has the short-circuit current $J_{sc}$ on the vertical axis intercept and the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ on the horizontal axis intercept. The point on the curve where the product $J \times V$ is maximum is the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**, with coordinates $(V_{mp}, J_{mp})$. **Strategies to enhance FF in polymer solar cells:** 1. **Optimising the bulk heterojunction morphology** — Using thermal annealing or solvent additives (e.g., 1,8-diiodooctane, DIO) creates an interpenetrating network with domain sizes comparable to the exciton diffusion length (~10 nm), ensuring efficient charge generation. 2. **Reducing series resistance** — Replacing PEDOT:PSS with highly conductive alternatives (e.g., self-assembled monolayer-based SAMs) and using low-resistance metal electrodes (Ca/Al, Ag) reduces parasitic voltage drops. **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Calculate initial PCE:** Convert $J_{sc}$: $16 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 160 \text{ A/m}^2$. $$ P_{max} = 160 \times 0.88 \times 0.68 = 95.74 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ $$ \eta = \frac{95.74}{1000} = 0.09574 = 9.57\% $$ **Step 2 — Find new $J_{sc}$ at 500 W/m² (linear response):** $$ J_{sc,new} = 160 \times \frac{500}{1000} = 80 \text{ A/m}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Find new PCE:** (Assuming $V_{oc}$ and $FF$ remain approximately constant under reduced irradiance — a reasonable first-order approximation.) $$ P_{max,new} = 80 \times 0.88 \times 0.68 = 47.87 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ $$ \eta_{new} = \frac{47.87}{500} = 0.09574 = 9.57\% $$ **Conclusion:** Under ideal conditions, PCE is **independent of irradiance** because both numerator and denominator scale linearly. In practice, $V_{oc}$ drops logarithmically at low light, causing slight reduction in η. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert mA/cm² to A/m² (multiply by 10) is the single most common mistake. A 3–5 mark deduction is typical. > 2. **Confusing PCE with Power Output:** PCE is a *dimensionless ratio* (a percentage), not an absolute power. > 3. **Forgetting to multiply by FF:** A common error is writing $P_{max} = J_{sc} V_{oc}$ instead of $J_{sc} V_{oc} FF$. This overestimates η by ~25–30%. > 4. **Misstating the SQ limit:** Always quote ~33.7% for single-junction cells under AM1.5G, not 30% or 40%. > 5. **Ignoring the “$-1$” term in diode equation:** Examiners expect students to justify dropping it (i.e., $J_{sc} \gg J_0$), not just assume it. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **PCE Master Equation:** $\eta = \dfrac{J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ — the single most important formula for KTU Module 1. - ✅ **STC:** AM1.5G, $1000 \text{ W/m}^2$, $25^\circ\text{C}$ — must always be cited when stating η. - ✅ **Fill Factor (FF):** Range 0 to 1; typical silicon cells: 0.75–0.83; polymer cells: 0.60–0.75. - ✅ **Open-Circuit Voltage:** $V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{sc}}{J_0}\right)$ — logarithmically dependent on $J_{sc}/J_0$ ratio. - ✅ **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = k_B T / q \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K — use this in all diode calculations. - ✅ **Shockley–Queisser Limit:** ~33.7% for single-junction cells; the theoretical ceiling that no single material device can exceed under unconcentrated sunlight. - ✅ **Three optimisation axes:** Maximise $J_{sc}$ (light trapping), maximise $V_{oc}$ (energy level alignment), maximise $FF$ (reduce $R_s$, increase $R_{sh}$). - ✅ **Five key loss mechanisms:** Reflection, transmission (sub-bandgap), thermalisation (above-bandgap), recombination (radiative + non-radiative), resistive (Joule heating). - ✅ **Polymer-specific considerations:** Exciton binding energy (~0.5 eV), limited exciton diffusion length (~10 nm), need for donor–acceptor bulk heterojunction morphology. - ✅ **EQE vs. IQE:** External Quantum Efficiency includes optical losses; Internal Quantum Efficiency excludes them. Their ratio reveals reflection/transmission losses. - ✅ **Multi-junction / Tandem cells:** Can exceed SQ limit by stacking cells of different bandgaps; current record >47% under concentrated sunlight. - ✅ **NREL Chart Reference:** Always cite the National Renewable Energy Laboratory Best Research-Cell Efficiency chart when comparing technologies.

topics.title: Auger and SRH Lifetime
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Auger and SRH Lifetime

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Carrier Lifetime — The Fundamental Concept

In semiconductor physics, **minority carrier lifetime (τ)** is defined as the average time a photo-generated minority carrier survives before recombining with a majority carrier. It is one of the most critical parameters governing the performance of photovoltaic (PV) devices because it directly controls **diffusion length**, **open-circuit voltage (V_OC)**, and **short-circuit current (J_SC)**.

Mathematically, the recombination rate $U$ and the lifetime are related through:

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta n}{U}$$

where $\Delta n$ is the excess carrier concentration and $U$ is the net recombination rate per unit volume (in cm⁻³ s⁻¹).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The minority carrier lifetime in any solar cell is never determined by a single mechanism. It is the **net effective lifetime** $\tau_{eff}$ that governs device performance, defined by Matthiessen's rule:
> $$\frac{1}{\tau_{eff}} = \frac{1}{\tau_{SRH}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{Auger}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{rad}}$$

## 1.2 The Three Intrinsic Recombination Mechanisms

There are three dominant recombination pathways inside a crystalline silicon (c-Si) solar cell:

| Mechanism | Origin | Dominant Regime |
|-----------|--------|-----------------|
| **Radiative (Band-to-Band)** | Direct electron-hole recombination with photon emission | Low doping, low injection |
| **SRH (Trap-Assisted)** | Recombination via mid-gap defect states (impurities, dislocations) | Moderate injection, presence of defects |
| **Auger** | Three-carrier energy transfer (e-e-h or h-h-e) | High doping (≥ 10¹⁷ cm⁻³) or high injection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for PV technology:** A high-efficiency c-Si solar cell (e.g., PERC, TOPCon, HJT) requires all three lifetimes to be large. In practice, the smallest lifetime dominates — analogous to how the weakest link controls a chain's strength.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Analogy"

Imagine a busy restaurant where **electrons** are customers and **holes** are empty tables:

- **Radiative recombination (Band-to-Band):** A customer simply sits at an empty table and the match is announced publicly (photon emission). Clean, efficient, but rare.
- **SRH recombination (Trap-Assisted):** A *greasy table* (a defect state in the forbidden gap) attracts customers, but the food served is terrible — both customer and table "annihilate" without announcement, releasing only heat (phonons). This is **mediated recombination** by a defect center.
- **Auger recombination (Three-Carrier):** A customer sits at a table, but the energy released **kicks another customer out of his seat** violently. Net result: one recombination event occurred, and the third carrier carries away excess kinetic energy as heat.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Shockley-Read-Hall (SRH) Recombination

The **SRH theory** was independently formulated by **William Shockley, William T. Read Jr., and Robert N. Hall in 1952**. It models recombination through localized energy states (traps) lying within the forbidden bandgap, typically created by impurities, dangling bonds, or crystalline defects.

### 2.1.1 The Four Possible Trap Transitions

A single trap state at energy $E_t$ interacts with both bands via four processes:

1. **Electron Capture (n)**: Conduction electron drops into the trap, releasing energy.
2. **Electron Emission (n)**: Trapped electron is thermally re-emitted to the conduction band.
3. **Hole Capture (p)**: Trapped electron drops to the valence band (i.e., trap captures a hole).
4. **Hole Emission (p)**: Valence electron is thermally excited into the trap, leaving a hole behind.

The net recombination rate through a single trap level is given by the celebrated SRH expression:

$$U_{SRH} = \frac{np - n_i^2}{\tau_p \left(n + n_1\right) + \tau_n \left(p + p_1\right)}$$

where the auxiliary parameters are:

$$n_1 = n_i \, e^{\left(E_t - E_i\right) / kT}$$

$$p_1 = n_i \, e^{\left(E_i - E_t\right) / kT}$$

### 2.1.2 The Fundamental Time Constants $\tau_n$ and $\tau_p$

The two characteristic capture time constants are:

$$\tau_n = \frac{1}{\sigma_n \, v_{th} \, N_t}, \quad \tau_p = \frac{1}{\sigma_p \, v_{th} \, N_t}$$

where $v_{th} \approx 10^7 \text{ cm/s}$ is the thermal velocity at 300 K, $N_t$ is the trap density per cm³, and $\sigma_n, \sigma_p$ are the capture cross-sections (in cm²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Point:** For **mid-gap traps** ($E_t = E_i$), we have $n_1 = p_1 = n_i$, and the recombination rate achieves its **maximum possible value**. This is why deep-level impurities (e.g., Fe, Au, Cu in Si) are so destructive to solar cell lifetime.

### 2.1.3 Limiting Cases of SRH Lifetime

The minority carrier SRH lifetime simplifies dramatically in two important limits:

**Case 1: Low-level injection in p-type material (Δn ≪ p₀)**
$$\tau_{SRH} \approx \tau_{n0} = \frac{1}{\sigma_n v_{th} N_t}$$

The lifetime is controlled by **electron capture** into the trap.

**Case 2: High-level injection (Δn ≫ n₀, p₀)**
$$\tau_{SRH,HLI} = \tau_{n0} + \tau_{p0}$$

Both capture processes contribute equally, and the lifetime is **injection-dependent**.

---

## 2.2 Auger Recombination

Auger recombination is a **non-radiative, three-carrier process** in which the energy released during an electron-hole recombination is transferred as kinetic energy to a third carrier (either an electron or a hole), which then thermalizes back to its band edge by emitting phonons.

### 2.2.1 The Two Auger Processes

- **eeh process** (Auger coefficient $C_n$): Two electrons and one hole participate. The energy is given to a conduction-band electron. Dominant in **n-type** doped material.
- **hhe process** (Auger coefficient $C_p$): Two holes and one electron participate. The energy is given to a valence-band hole. Dominant in **p-type** doped material.

### 2.2.2 The Auger Recombination Rate

The generalized Auger recombination rate is:

$$U_{Aug} = \left(C_n \, n + C_p \, p\right)\left(np - n_i^2\right)$$

where $C_n$ and $C_p$ are the **Auger coefficients** (units: cm⁶/s). For silicon at 300 K:

$$C_n \approx 2.8 \times 10^{-31} \text{ cm}^6/\text{s}, \quad C_p \approx 0.99 \times 10^{-31} \text{ cm}^6/\text{s}$$

### 2.2.3 Auger Lifetime in Doped Silicon

For **low-level injection** in a uniformly doped semiconductor:

**n-type substrate** (majority $N_D$):
$$\tau_{Aug,n} = \frac{1}{C_n N_D^2}$$

**p-type substrate** (majority $N_A$):
$$\tau_{Aug,p} = \frac{1}{C_p N_A^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The strong $N^2$ dependence is the **fundamental reason** why heavily doped emitter regions in c-Si solar cells suffer severe lifetime degradation. Modern PERC cells use lightly doped emitters (~$10^{19}$ cm⁻³) to balance contact resistance against Auger losses.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Limiting Case | Units |
|---|---------|----------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $U_{SRH} = \frac{np - n_i^2}{\tau_p(n + n_1) + \tau_n(p + p_1)}$ | General SRH recombination rate | cm⁻³ s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $n_1 = n_i e^{(E_t - E_i)/kT}$ | Electron density parameter | cm⁻³ |
| 3 | $p_1 = n_i e^{(E_i - E_t)/kT}$ | Hole density parameter | cm⁻³ |
| 4 | $\tau_{n0} = 1/(\sigma_n v_{th} N_t)$ | Electron capture lifetime | s |
| 5 | $\tau_{p0} = 1/(\sigma_p v_{th} N_t)$ | Hole capture lifetime | s |
| 6 | $\tau_{SRH,LLI} = \tau_{n0}$ (p-type) | Low-injection SRH lifetime | s |
| 7 | $\tau_{SRH,HLI} = \tau_{n0} + \tau_{p0}$ | High-injection SRH lifetime | s |
| 8 | $U_{Aug} = (C_n n + C_p p)(np - n_i^2)$ | General Auger rate | cm⁻³ s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\tau_{Aug,n} = 1/(C_n N_D^2)$ | Auger lifetime, n-type LLI | s |
| 10 | $\tau_{Aug,p} = 1/(C_p N_A^2)$ | Auger lifetime, p-type LLI | s |
| 11 | $1/\tau_{eff} = 1/\tau_{rad} + 1/\tau_{SRH} + 1/\tau_{Aug}$ | Matthiessen's combination | s⁻¹ |
| 12 | $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ | Minority carrier diffusion length | cm |
| 13 | $v_{th} = \sqrt{3kT/m^*} \approx 10^7$ cm/s | Thermal velocity at 300 K | cm/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** In ALL numerical problems, examiners expect you to: (1) state the governing equation, (2) identify the limiting regime, (3) substitute values with units, and (4) report the answer in SI-compatible units (s for lifetime, cm for diffusion length).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SRH Lifetime:** Drives the requirement for **high-purity feedstock** in c-Si PV. Float-zone (FZ) silicon with low oxygen content achieves $\tau_{SRH}$ in the millisecond range, while standard Czochralski (Cz) silicon is limited to ~100–500 μs.
- **Auger Lifetime:** Determines the **emitter saturation current density ($J_{0e}$)** in PERC/TOPCon cells. Heavy phosphorus doping ($>10^{20}$ cm⁻³) is a trade-off between contact resistance and Auger loss.
- **Surface Recombination (extension):** Using the same SRH framework, the surface recombination velocity is $S = \sigma v_{th} N_{it}$, where $N_{it}$ is the interface trap density. This is why **passivation layers** (Al₂O₃, SiNₓ) are critical in modern PV.
