## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a hot carrier in a semiconductor. Why are hot carriers important for next-generation photovoltaic cells?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A hot carrier is a photogenerated charge carrier (electron or hole) whose kinetic energy lies *significantly above* the conduction-band minimum $E_C$ (or below the valence-band maximum $E_V$) immediately after photoexcitation. The carrier population is described by a carrier temperature $T_{HC}$ satisfying $T_{HC} \gg T_{L}$.
- **(1 mark)** Such carriers exist for a brief time (typically 0.1–10 ps in bulk semiconductors) before relaxing to the band edge via phonon emission.
- **(1 mark)** Hot carriers are important because their *excess energy* (above $E_g$) is otherwise lost to thermalization. If this energy is extracted as useful electrical work (via Energy-Selective Contacts) before cooling, the Shockley–Queisser limit of 33.7% can be broken, with theoretical HCSC efficiencies reaching 65.8% (Ross–Nozik, 1982).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**
*List and briefly explain any TWO strategies used to slow hot-carrier cooling in potential HCSC absorber materials.*

**Model Answer (3 marks = 1.5 + 1.5):**
- **(Strategy 1 – 1.5 marks):** *Quantum Confinement / Phonon Bottleneck:* By reducing the absorber dimension to a quantum dot (radius $L \leq 5$ nm in InAs), the electronic level spacing $\Delta E_n$ becomes larger than the LO-phonon energy $\hbar\omega_{LO}$. The "Kohn–Sham gap" in the phonon density of states suppresses the dominant Fröhlich (polar-optical) relaxation channel, extending $\tau_{cool}$ from $\sim 0.1$ ps to $\sim 1$–$10$ ns.
- **(Strategy 2 – 1.5 marks):** *Hot-Phonon Bottleneck:* Under intense photoexcitation, the population of LO phonons is non-thermal. These "hot phonons" are reabsorbed by carriers before decaying into acoustic phonons (since LO→acoustic phonon decay is a slow, anharmonic process). The result is a quasi-equilibrium between carriers and LO phonons, effectively slowing net carrier cooling. This is significant in GaAs, InP, and 2D perovskites.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls in HCSC Questions**
> 1. **Confusing $T_{HC}$ with $T_{sun}$:** Many students write $\eta_C = 1 - T_{cell}/T_{sun}$ (Carnot limit) instead of the *carrier* Carnot expression $\eta = 1 - T_{cell}/T_{HC}$. The relevant temperature is the **hot-carrier temperature**, not the Sun's.
> 2. **Ignoring the energy-selective contact requirement:** A full-mark answer on HCSCs must mention *both* the slowed cooling **and** the energy-selective extraction. Mentioning only one will cost **at least 3 marks**.
> 3. **Conflating Auger cooling with phonon cooling:** Auger is a *carrier–carrier* process; it dominates at high injection but does not directly transfer energy to the lattice. The relevant loss to the lattice is *phonon* emission, not Auger.
> 4. **Quoting 65.8% without the Ross–Nozik reference:** Examiners expect the attribution *"Ross–Nozik 1982 detailed-balance analysis"*; un-attributed numerical claims lose marks.
> 5. **Drawing the ESC incorrectly:** The DBRTS has *two* barriers and a *single* well — not one barrier — and the transmission window must be drawn at the resonant well level, not at the conduction band edge.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Hot Carrier Cell Physics**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the principle of operation of a hot carrier solar cell. With the aid of an energy-band sketch, describe how the energy-selective contact enables quasi-monoenergetic carrier extraction. Why is a *narrow* energy window $\Delta E$ required at the contact?*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Principle of Operation — 3 marks:**
A hot carrier solar cell consists of an absorber in which photogenerated electron–hole pairs retain a fraction of their excess photon energy as *kinetic* energy above the band edge. This non-equilibrium carrier distribution is described by a temperature $T_{HC}$ well above the lattice temperature $T_L$. To convert this stored kinetic energy into useful electrical work, the carriers must be extracted *before* they thermalize via phonon emission. The two governing design principles are:
- **(a)** Suppression of carrier–phonon coupling (achieving long $\tau_{cool}$).
- **(b)** Energy-selective extraction through resonant-tunnelling contacts (achieving short $\tau_{ext}$).

The condition $\tau_{ext} < \tau_{cool}$ must be satisfied.

**(ii) Energy-Selective Contact (DBRTS) — 3 marks:**
A Double-Barrier Resonant Tunnelling Structure (DBRTS) consists of:
- A thin (1–2 nm) insulator/semiconductor **barrier 1** (e.g., AlAs on GaAs).
- A **quantum well** of 2–5 nm (e.g., GaAs) — supports a quasi-bound state at energy $E_{well} = E_C + \delta$ above the conduction band edge of the absorber.
- A thin (1–2 nm) **barrier 2** (symmetric to barrier 1).
- A contact metal / n⁺ semiconductor.

A carrier approaching the contact from the absorber tunnels through barrier 1 *only* if its energy lies within $\pm \Delta E/2$ of $E_{well}$. The transmission coefficient is:

$$T(E) = \frac{\Gamma^2}{(E - E_{well})^2 + \Gamma^2}$$

where $\Gamma$ is the resonance half-width. Outside this window, $T(E) \to 0$ and the carrier is **reflected back** into the absorber — giving the contact its energy-selectivity.

**(iii) Why a narrow window? — 1 mark:**
A narrow $\Delta E$ ensures that:
- The extracted carrier distribution is *quasi-monoenergetic*, maximizing the voltage per extracted carrier.
- The *entropy* of the carrier distribution is minimized, increasing the free-energy conversion efficiency (analogous to a reversible heat engine).
- Cold (already-thermalized) carriers near $E_C$ are *not* extracted, preventing short-circuit-like current loss.

**(b) [7 marks]** *The detailed-balance efficiency of a single-junction solar cell is limited to 33.7%. Using the Ross–Nozik analysis, explain how a hot carrier absorber at $T_{HC} = 2000$ K can in principle reach 49.5% efficiency. Compute the optimum bandgap at this temperature using the approximate relation $E_{g,opt} \approx 0.50 \cdot (1 + T_{HC}/T_{sun})^{-1} \cdot E_{sun,peak}$. Take $T_{sun} = 5778$ K and the peak photon energy $E_{sun,peak} = 1.40$ eV.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Ross–Nozik Framework — 4 marks:**
In the standard Shockley–Queisser detailed-balance model, the absorber is assumed to be in *internal thermal equilibrium with the lattice at $T_L$*. This means every absorbed photon of energy $h\nu > E_g$ is *immediately* thermalized to $E_g$, losing the excess $(h\nu - E_g)$ as heat. This thermalization loss is the *largest* single loss mechanism in single-junction cells.

The Ross–Nozik (1982) model **relaxes** the assumption of instantaneous thermalization. The absorber is treated as a *non-equilibrium thermodynamic system* at an *effective* temperature $T_{HC} > T_L$. The carrier population follows:

$$f_{HC}(E) = \frac{1}{e^{(E - \mu_{HC})/k_B T_{HC}} + 1}$$

and emits photons from the absorber surface at rate:

$$\dot{N}_{rec} = \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \frac{2\pi E^2}{\hbar^3 c^2}\cdot\frac{1}{e^{(E - \mu_{HC})/k_B T_{HC}} - 1}\,dE$$

Because $T_{HC}$ is much greater than $T_L = 300$ K, the chemical potential $\mu_{HC}$ can in principle be *larger* than $E_g$ (in conventional cells it is limited to $\mu \leq E_g$). Hence the open-circuit voltage:

$$V_{OC} = \frac{\mu_{HC}}{q} > \frac{E_g}{q}$$

This increased $V_{OC}$ is precisely why the efficiency climbs. Numerically, optimizing $E_g$ and $\mu_{HC}$ for a fixed $T_{HC} = 2000$ K yields $\eta_{max} \approx 49.5\%$ — a 15.8 percentage-point improvement over the SQ limit.

**(ii) Optimum Bandgap Calculation — 3 marks:**

Given: $T_{HC} = 2000$ K, $T_{sun} = 5778$ K, $E_{sun,peak} = 1.40$ eV.

$$E_{g,opt} \approx \frac{0.50 \cdot E_{sun,peak}}{1 + T_{HC}/T_{sun}}$$

Compute the temperature ratio:

$$\frac{T_{HC}}{T_{sun}} = \frac{2000}{5778} = 0.346$$

Substitute:

$$E_{g,opt} \approx \frac{0.50 \times 1.40 \text{ eV}}{1 + 0.346} = \frac{0.700 \text{ eV}}{1.346}$$

$$\boxed{E_{g,opt} \approx 0.52 \text{ eV}}$$

This matches the Ross–Nozik tabulated value (~0.55 eV for $T_{HC} = 2000$ K) within the approximation's tolerance. The result is consistent with the physical intuition that **hot-carrier operation shifts the optimum bandgap toward the infrared**, where the *bulk* of the solar spectrum's energy resides.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Phonon Bottleneck & Quantum Confinement**

**(a) [7 marks]** *What is the "phonon bottleneck" effect? Derive the critical quantum-dot radius $L_{crit}$ below which an InAs quantum dot exhibits a phonon bottleneck. Given $m^\ast = 0.023\,m_0$ and $\hbar\omega_{LO} = 30$ meV for InAs, evaluate $L_{crit}$.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Phonon Bottleneck Concept — 3 marks:**
In bulk polar semiconductors, hot electrons relax to the band edge by emitting **Longitudinal-Optical (LO) phonons** of fixed energy $\hbar\omega_{LO} \approx 20$–$40$ meV. This process is extremely fast ($\sim 100$ fs) because the density of states for LO phonons is sharply peaked at $\hbar\omega_{LO}$ (the *Fröhlich* interaction).

In a *quantum dot* of sufficiently small size, the electronic states are *quantized* with spacing:

$$\Delta E_{n,n+1} = \frac{(2n+1)\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m^\ast L^2}$$

If $\Delta E_{n,n+1} > \hbar\omega_{LO}$, then the electron *cannot* emit a single LO phonon (energy conservation forbids it), and is forced to use a much slower multi-phonon (acoustic) pathway. This *gating* of the dominant relaxation channel is called the **phonon bottleneck**. Effective relaxation times can lengthen from $\sim 0.1$ ps to $\sim 1$–$10$ ns.

**(ii) Derivation of $L_{crit}$ — 3 marks:**
Set the *lowest* electronic level spacing equal to $\hbar\omega_{LO}$. The lowest spacing corresponds to $n = 1$:

$$\Delta E_{1,2} = \frac{(2 \cdot 1 + 1)\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m^\ast L^2} = \frac{3 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m^\ast L^2}$$

Setting $\Delta E_{1,2} = \hbar\omega_{LO}$:

$$\frac{3 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m^\ast L_{crit}^2} = \hbar \omega_{LO}$$

Solving for $L_{crit}$:

$$L_{crit} = \pi \hbar \sqrt{\frac{3}{2 m^\ast \hbar \omega_{LO}}}$$

For a more conservative threshold one often uses the *single-particle* spacing ($n = 1 \to \infty$ analogy, or the spacing between $n=1$ and $n=\infty$), giving:

$$L_{crit} = \pi \hbar \sqrt{\frac{1}{2 m^\ast \hbar \omega_{LO}}}$$

**(iii) Numerical Evaluation — 1 mark:**

Constants:
- $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $m^\ast = 0.023 \cdot m_0 = 2.10 \times 10^{-32}$ kg
- $\hbar\omega_{LO} = 30$ meV $= 4.80 \times 10^{-21}$ J

$$L_{crit} = \pi \cdot 1.055 \times 10^{-34} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{2 \cdot 2.10 \times 10^{-32} \cdot 4.80 \times 10^{-21}}}$$

Denominator inside the square root:

$$2 \cdot 2.10 \times 10^{-32} \cdot 4.80 \times 10^{-21} = 2.016 \times 10^{-52}$$

Inverse:

$$\frac{1}{2.016 \times 10^{-52}} = 4.96 \times 10^{51}$$

Square root:

$$\sqrt{4.96 \times 10^{51}} = 7.04 \times 10^{25}$$

Multiply by $\pi \hbar$:

$$L_{crit} = 3.14 \cdot 1.055 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 7.04 \times 10^{25} \approx 2.46 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L_{crit} \approx 25 \text{ nm (diameter) or radius} \approx 12.5 \text{ nm}}$$

(Note: A more conservative estimate using $\Delta E_{1,2}$ instead gives $L_{crit} \approx 4$–$5$ nm radius, consistent with the experimental reports of phonon bottleneck in InAs QDs smaller than 5 nm radius.)

**(b) [7 marks]** *Discuss the role of Energy-Selective Contacts (ESCs) in hot carrier solar cells. Compare ESCs with conventional ohmic contacts in terms of (i) current extraction, (ii) voltage, and (iii) entropy of the extracted carrier distribution. Why is a DBRTS preferred over a single-barrier structure?*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Role of ESCs — 2 marks:**
An ESC is the *extraction port* of an HCSC. Its purpose is to *filter* carriers by energy: only those with kinetic energy in a narrow window $\Delta E$ above the band edge are allowed to leave the absorber. This is essential because:
- It enforces the *quasi-monoenergetic* extraction required for high voltage.
- It *prevents* cold (thermalized) carriers from leaving, which would otherwise create a low-voltage short-circuit and erase the hot-carrier benefit.
- It *localizes* the carriers in the absorber long enough for the hot-carrier distribution to be re-established by carrier–carrier scattering.

**(ii) Comparison Table — 3 marks:**

| Property | Conventional Ohmic Contact | Energy-Selective Contact (DBRTS) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Current extraction** | All carriers — both hot and cold — extracted equally; ohmic loss at metal/semiconductor interface. | Only hot carriers with $E \in [E_{well} - \Delta E/2,\ E_{well} + \Delta E/2]$ extracted; cold carriers reflected. |
| **(ii) Voltage** | $V_{OC} \leq E_g / q$ (limited by the energy gap). | $V_{OC} \approx (E_g + \delta)/q$ — the *resonant well energy* is added to the chemical potential, boosting $V_{OC}$. |
| **(iii) Entropy of extracted distribution** | High — carriers extracted over a wide energy range $\sim k_B T_L$ near band edge. | Low — narrow $\Delta E \ll k_B T_{HC}$ ensures reversible (low-entropy) extraction, preserving free energy. |

**(iii) Why DBRTS preferred over single barrier — 2 marks:**
- A **single barrier** would tunnel carriers over a broad energy range, behaving effectively as a leaky ohmic contact — no energy selectivity.
- A **double barrier with a quantum well** creates a *resonant* transmission peak: $T(E)$ has a Lorentzian shape with FWHM $= 2\Gamma$, where $\Gamma$ is the resonance width. At resonance $E = E_{well}$, $T \to 1$; off-resonance, $T$ decays as $1/(E - E_{well})^2$. The double barrier thus produces an *extremely narrow* transmission window, with high contrast between on- and off-resonance transmission.
- The double barrier also *localizes* the wavefunction inside the well, reducing the off-resonant tunnelling probability further (tunnelling amplitude falls off exponentially through *each* barrier).
- Symmetric DBRTS designs additionally eliminate the charge-buildup effects seen in asymmetric structures.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HOT CARRIER CELLS — THE 12-POINT EXAM REVISION SHEET**

1. **Definition:** Hot carrier = charge carrier with kinetic energy ≫ $k_B T_L$ above the band edge; described by hot-carrier temperature $T_{HC} \gg T_L$.
2. **The problem solved:** Overcomes the dominant *thermalization loss* $(h\nu - E_g)$ in single-junction cells, breaking the 33.7% Shockley–Queisser limit.
3. **Ultimate HCSC efficiency:** 65.8% (1-sun, Ross–Nozik 1982); 86.8% (max-concentration).
4. **Two engineering requirements (must mention both):** (a) Suppressed carrier–phonon cooling ($\tau_{cool}$ large), and (b) Energy-selective contacts ($\tau_{ext}$ small).
5. **Cooling mechanisms (in order of speed):** Fröhlich LO-phonon (100 fs) → Auger (1 ps) → acoustic phonon (10 ps).
6. **Phonon bottleneck:** Achieved by quantum confinement when $\Delta E_n > \hbar\omega_{LO}$ — typically $L \leq 5$ nm radius in InAs QDs.
7. **Hot-phonon bottleneck:** Under intense photoexcitation, non-thermal LO-phonon population re-energizes carriers before anharmonic decay to acoustic phonons.
8. **Energy Selective Contact (ESC):** Double-Barrier Resonant Tunnelling Structure (DBRTS) with quasi-bound state at $E_{well}$, transmission $T(E) = \Gamma^2 / [(E - E_{well})^2 + \Gamma^2]$.
9. **Key relation — extraction condition:** $\tau_{ext} < \tau_{cool}$; in practice $\tau_{ext} < 1$ ps and $\tau_{cool} > 1$ ns desired.
10. **Ross–Nozik optimum bandgap:** $E_{g,opt}$ *decreases* as $T_{HC}$ rises (shifts toward IR); at $T_{HC} = 2000$ K, $E_{g,opt} \approx 0.5$–$0.55$ eV.
11. **Open-circuit voltage enhancement:** $V_{OC} = \mu_{HC}/q > E_g/q$ in HCSC, since $\mu_{HC}$ can exceed $E_g$ when $T_{HC} \gg T_L$.
12. **Promising HCSC absorber materials:** InAs QDs, InN, GaAs/AlAs DBRTS, 2D perovskites, MoS₂ monolayers, halide perovskite nanocrystals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photovoltaic Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Photovoltaic Modules # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Photovoltaic Module? A **Photovoltaic (PV) Module** is the fundamental, factory-assembled, weather-sealed building block of a solar power system. It consists of a series/parallel electrical interconnection of multiple individual **PV cells** (typically **36, 60, 72, or 144 cells**), encapsulated between a transparent front cover (usually low-iron tempered glass), an encapsulant film (EVA — Ethylene Vinyl Acetate, a polymer directly tied to our course curriculum), and a polymer-based backsheet (TPT/TPE/KPK), all framed within an aluminum profile for mechanical rigidity and outdoor mounting. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** > A *photovoltaic module* is a packaged, environmentally protected, interconnected assembly of solar cells, designed to serve as the smallest field-replaceable unit in a PV installation. Multiple modules form a **panel/array**. ### Hierarchy of a PV System The structure follows a strict cascading logic: $$ \text{1 Cell} \rightarrow \text{1 Module (Panel)} \rightarrow \text{1 String} \rightarrow \text{1 Array} $$ - **Cell** — A single p–n junction semiconductor device (typically crystalline silicon, ~156 mm × 156 mm or 182 mm × 182 mm in modern modules). - **Module** — An encapsulated package of cells wired in series (to raise voltage) and sometimes in parallel (to raise current). - **String** — Modules connected in **series** to reach the inverter's MPPT window (commonly 600 V – 1500 V DC). - **Array** — Multiple parallel strings forming the complete DC power-generating field. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Battery Pack of Solar" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a single solar cell as a *single AA battery* producing only ~0.5 V — far too weak to light a bulb. Just as we stack batteries in a flashlight to step up the voltage, we **solder solar cells in series inside a module** to obtain a usable DC voltage (typically **30–50 V open-circuit** for a 60-cell module). The encapsulant glass + EVA + backsheet acts like the *plastic shrink-wrap* that protects the battery pack from rain, dust, and physical damage. ### Why Not Use a Single Big Cell? A single crystal of silicon as large as a module (~1.6 m × 1 m) is **technically impossible and prohibitively expensive** to grow. Therefore, the industry grows standardized wafers (~180 mm square), fabricates individual cells, and then **modularly interconnects** them. The PV module is, in essence, a *distributed, scalable, and manufacturable solution* to the size constraint of monocrystalline silicon ingots. ## 1.3 Standard Module Parameters (Industry Benchmarks) | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (c-Si 60-cell) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Rated Power | $P_{max}$ | **250 – 400** | W | | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc}$ | **37 – 41** | V | | Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc}$ | **8 – 11** | A | | Voltage at MPP | $V_{mp}$ | **30 – 34** | V | | Current at MPP | $I_{mp}$ | **7.5 – 10** | A | | Module Efficiency | $\eta$ | **15 – 22** | % | | NOCT | $T_{NOCT}$ | **45 ± 2** | °C | Standard Test Conditions (STC): Irradiance **G = 1000 W/m²**, Cell Temperature **T = 25 °C**, Air Mass **AM 1.5**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Any I-V characteristic reported on a module datasheet is at **STC**. Field performance is always lower because cells operate at **NOCT (Nominal Operating Cell Temperature ≈ 45 °C)** under actual sunlight, not 25 °C. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Crystalline-Silicon Module A modern c-Si PV module is a **laminated sandwich** of seven distinct layers, listed from top (sun-facing) to bottom: 1. **Tempered Low-Iron Glass Front Cover** (~3.2 mm thick) — Provides mechanical protection, ≥ 91 % transmittance in the 400–1100 nm band, and hail/load resistance. 2. **Upper Encapsulant (EVA Film)** — Cross-linkable **Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate** (~0.45 mm) that bonds glass to cells, provides optical coupling, UV/moisture barrier, and electrical insulation. 3. **Solar Cells** — Mono- or multi-crystalline silicon wafers with screen-printed silver front contacts and aluminum back-surface field. 4. **Lower Encapsulant (EVA Film)** — Same as upper; provides symmetric lamination. 5. **Backsheet** — Multi-layer polymer film (commonly **TPT** = Tedlar / PET / Tedlar, or newer **KPE / KPK** variants with polyolefin) providing electrical insulation, UV protection, and moisture barrier. 6. **Junction Box** — Weather-sealed (IP67) box on the rear, containing **bypass diodes** and termination cables with **MC4 connectors**. 7. **Aluminum Frame** — Anodized Al extrusion providing structural strength and grounding continuity. > [!NOTE] > **Course Linkage (PEPOT757):** The encapsulant **EVA** and the backsheet polymers (Tedlar, PET, polyolefins) are precisely the *polymer* components of the module — directly tying Module 2 to the polymers portion of our syllabus. ## 2.2 Electrical Interconnection of Cells Cells inside a module are connected in **series strings** (to add voltages) and these strings are then interconnected through **bypass diodes** in a specific pattern. ### 2.2.1 Series Connection For $N_s$ identical cells in series: $$ V_{module} = N_s \cdot V_{cell} $$ $$ I_{module} = I_{cell} $$ The voltage adds; the current remains constant. A **60-cell module** typically has 3 substrings of 20 cells each. ### 2.2.2 Parallel Connection (rare within a module) $$ V_{module} = V_{cell} $$ $$ I_{module} = N_p \cdot I_{cell} $$ Parallel connection within a single module is uncommon for c-Si because parallel mismatch is more catastrophic than series mismatch. However, **thin-film modules (CIGS, CdTe)** frequently use series–parallel hybrid wiring. ## 2.3 Fill Factor and Module Efficiency — The Two Performance Metrics The **Fill Factor (FF)** is the *figure of merit* of the I-V curve's "squareness": $$ FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} $$ Typical FF for a good c-Si cell: **0.75 – 0.82**. The **Module Conversion Efficiency** is: $$ \eta_{module} = \frac{P_{max}}{G_{STC} \cdot A_{module}} = \frac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{1000 \cdot A_{module}} $$ where $G_{STC} = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ and $A_{module}$ is the total aperture area in m². ## 2.4 The Two Diode Problem — Real Module vs. Ideal Cell A real module deviates from the ideal single-diode model due to **parasitic resistances**: - **Series Resistance ($R_s$)** — Caused by contact resistance at the silver fingers, busbars, tabbing ribbons, and cell-to-string solder joints. $R_s$ **decreases the slope of the I-V curve near $V_{oc}$** and directly reduces FF. - **Shunt Resistance ($R_{sh}$)** — Caused by manufacturing defects, edge shunts, and crystal dislocations. Low $R_{sh}$ **tilts the I-V curve near $I_{sc}$** and also reduces FF. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Mantra:** *"$R_s$ kills the voltage corner; $R_{sh}$ kills the current corner."* Examiners frequently ask this in Part A. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Maximum power delivered at MPP | W | | 2 | $FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Squareness of the I-V curve | Dimensionless | | 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A}$ | Conversion efficiency | % | | 4 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{sc}}{I_o} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage (single-diode model) | V | | 5 | $V_{module} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$ | String voltage from series cells | V | | 6 | $P_{module} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot P_{cell}$ | Power of a module (parallel branches) | W | | 7 | $T_{cell} = T_{amb} + \left(\dfrac{NOCT - 20}{0.8}\right) \cdot G$ | Cell temperature under field operation | °C | | 8 | $P_{field} = P_{STC}\!\left[1 + \gamma_{pmp}(T_{cell}-25)\right] \cdot \dfrac{G}{1000}$ | Power output corrected for T and G | W | | 9 | $E_{annual} = \eta \cdot A \cdot H$ | Annual energy yield of module | kWh | | 10 | $V_{th} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K | Thermal voltage | V | Where: $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**, $q$ = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**, $n$ = ideality factor (1–2), $\gamma_{pmp}$ = temperature coefficient (typically **−0.4 %/°C** for c-Si), and $H$ = annual in-plane irradiance (kWh/m²/yr). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Photovoltaic modules are the core workhorse of: - **Utility-Scale Solar Farms** (1 MW – 1 GW): Hundreds of thousands of modules in series–parallel arrays feeding central inverters. - **Rooftop Residential Systems** (3 kW – 10 kW): 10–25 modules feeding string inverters. - **Building-Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV)**: Modules replace roof tiles or façade glass — a major emerging *polymer-substrate* application. - **Spacecraft & Satellites**: High-efficiency multi-junction modules with 30–40 % efficiency, protected by polymer cover glasses. > [!NOTE] > **Why This Topic Matters in KTU Exams:** Photovoltaic modules are a direct application of semiconductor physics, electrochemistry, and polymer encapsulation — making them a *high-weightage* topic that integrates Module 1 (PV cell physics) with Module 2 (module engineering). # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Expression for Fill Factor and Maximum Power Point ### Step 1 — Start from the Single-Diode Equation The terminal current of a PV cell under illumination is governed by the single-diode model: $$ I = I_{ph} - I_o \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_{th}}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}} $$ where $V_{th} = kT/q$. ### Step 2 — Apply the Two Boundary Conditions At **short-circuit** ($V = 0$): assuming $R_s \ll R_{sh}$, $$ I_{sc} \approx I_{ph} $$ At **open-circuit** ($I = 0$): rearranging, $$ 0 = I_{ph} - I_o \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_{th}}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}} $$ If $R_{sh}$ is large (ideal case), the last term is negligible, giving: $$ I_{ph} \approx I_o \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_{th}}\right) \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_{oc} \approx n V_{th} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_o}\right) $$ This is the **fundamental expression for $V_{oc}$** — a board-favorite result. ### Step 3 — Express the Output Power The instantaneous electrical power delivered by the cell/module is: $$ P = V \cdot I $$ Substituting the diode current expression: $$ P(V) = V \cdot I_{ph} - V \cdot I_o \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_{th}}\right) - 1 \right] - V \cdot \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Maximum Power Condition At the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**, the derivative of power with respect to voltage vanishes: $$ \frac{dP}{dV}\bigg|_{MPP} = 0 $$ Using the product rule, $P = V \cdot I$, so: $$ \frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{dI}{dV}\bigg|_{MPP} = -\frac{I_{mp}}{V_{mp}} $$ This is the **maximum-power-point condition** — the slope of the I-V curve at the MPP must be the negative of the I/V ratio at that point. ### Step 5 — Define the Fill Factor By definition, $$ FF \equiv \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} $$ For an *ideal* cell ($R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$), the empirical expression (Green, 1981) is: $$ FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1} $$ where $v_{oc} = V_{oc}/(n V_{th})$ is the *normalized open-circuit voltage*. ### Step 6 — Real-Cell Correction for Series Resistance A first-order correction accounting for $R_s$ gives: $$ FF = FF_0 \left(1 - \frac{R_s I_{sc}}{V_{oc}}\right) $$ This shows that **FF decreases linearly with $R_s I_{sc}/V_{oc}$** — a key KTU design takeaway. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern) > **Problem:** A PV module consists of **36 series-connected crystalline silicon cells**, each with the following parameters at STC: $V_{oc,cell} = 0.62$ V, $I_{sc,cell} = 3.5$ A, Fill Factor = **0.78**, Cell area = **156 cm²** (i.e., 15.6 cm × 15.6 cm pseudo-square). Calculate: > **(a)** The module's open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current, and maximum power. > **(b)** The module's conversion efficiency. Assume the active area equals the cell area × number of cells. ### Solution **Part (a) — Module Voltages, Current, and Power** Step 1: With all 36 cells in series, voltages add while current stays the same: $$ V_{oc,module} = N_s \cdot V_{oc,cell} = 36 \times 0.62 $$ $$ V_{oc,module} = 22.32 \text{ V} $$ Step 2: Current remains the same as a single cell: $$ I_{sc,module} = I_{sc,cell} = 3.5 \text{ A} $$ Step 3: Compute maximum power using the Fill Factor: $$ P_{max,module} = FF \cdot V_{oc,module} \cdot I_{sc,module} $$ $$ P_{max,module} = 0.78 \times 22.32 \times 3.5 $$ $$ P_{max,module} = 60.93 \text{ W} $$ **Part (b) — Module Conversion Efficiency** Step 1: Total active area: $$ A = N_s \cdot A_{cell} = 36 \times (156 \text{ cm}^2) $$ $$ A = 36 \times 156 = 5616 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.5616 \text{ m}^2 $$ Step 2: Incident solar power at STC: $$ P_{in} = G \cdot A = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2 \times 0.5616 \text{ m}^2 = 561.6 \text{ W} $$ Step 3: Efficiency: $$ \eta = \frac{P_{max,module}}{P_{in}} = \frac{60.93}{561.6} $$ $$ \eta = 0.1085 = 10.85 \,\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Interpretation:** This efficiency (~10.85 %) is typical of an *older-generation poly-c-Si 36-cell module*. Modern PERC and TOPCon modules exceed 21 % because the cell's $V_{oc}$ has been pushed above ~0.72 V and FF beyond 0.82, while reducing $R_s$ losses. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Effect of $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ on Module Performance | Parameter Affected | Effect of Increasing $R_s$ | Effect of Decreasing $R_{sh}$ | |---|---|---| | Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$ | Almost unchanged | Almost unchanged | | Short-Circuit Current $I_{sc}$ | Unchanged (slightly) | Slightly reduced | | Maximum Power $P_{max}$ | **Strongly decreased** | **Strongly decreased** | | Fill Factor $FF$ | Decreased (kills voltage corner) | Decreased (kills current corner) | | Slope of I-V at $V_{oc}$ | Becomes finite (less steep) | Mostly unaffected | | Slope of I-V at $I_{sc}$ | Mostly unaffected | Becomes more negative (tilts down) | | Series Resistance Target | $R_s < 0.5 \text{ }\Omega\text{.cm}^2$ | $R_{sh} > 1000 \text{ }\Omega\text{.cm}^2$ | --- ## 3.4 Hot-Spot Formation & Bypass Diode Mitigation — Analytical View When one cell in a series string is **partially shaded**, it becomes reverse-biased and dissipates power as heat rather than generating it — this is a **hot spot**. **Derivation of Power Dissipated by a Shaded Cell:** In a string of $N$ cells with one cell shaded, the unshaded cells continue to drive current $I$ through the shaded cell. The shaded cell's terminal voltage becomes negative (acting as a load). The power it dissipates is: $$ P_{shaded} = I \cdot \vert V_{shaded}\vert \approx I \cdot (V_{string} - (N-1) V_{good,cell}) $$ If this power exceeds the shaded cell's thermal dissipation limit, **irreversible damage** (melted solder, cracked wafer, or even fire) occurs. **Mitigation — Bypass Diodes:** A bypass diode (typically a Schottky diode) is connected *anti-parallel* to a substring of 18–24 cells. When the substring is shaded, the bypass diode becomes forward-biased and routes the string current around the substring, limiting the voltage across the shaded cells to one diode drop (~0.7 V). The power dissipated in the shaded cells is then: $$ P_{shaded,protected} = I \cdot V_{f,diode} \approx I \cdot 0.7 \text{ V} $$ This is typically 10–20× less than the unprotected case, eliminating the hot-spot hazard. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A typical 60-cell module has **3 bypass diodes**, each protecting a substring of 20 cells. A 72-cell module has 3 bypass diodes (24 cells each). Loss of one bypass diode disables one-third of the module. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchy from Cell to Array ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Single PV Cell
Voc 0.6 V, Isc 8 A] --> B[Module Panel
36 or 60 or 72 cells in series] B --> C[String
Multiple modules in series] C --> D[Array
Multiple strings in parallel] D --> E[DC Combiner Box
with fuses and SPD] E --> F[Inverter MPPT
DC to AC conversion] F --> G[AC Distribution
Grid or Load] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Layered Cross-Section of a c-Si Module ```mermaid flowchart TB L1["Layer 1 Tempered Low Iron Glass 3.2 mm"] L2["Layer 2 Upper EVA Encapsulant 0.45 mm"] L3["Layer 3 Solar Cells Si Wafers 180 to 200 micrometer"] L4["Layer 4 Lower EVA Encapsulant 0.45 mm"] L5["Layer 5 Backsheet TPT or KPE Polymer"] L6["Layer 6 Junction Box with Bypass Diodes"] L7["Layer 7 Anodized Aluminum Frame"] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 L5 -.attached to.-> L6 L5 -.encased in.-> L7 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Series-Parallel Cell Interconnection with Bypass Diodes ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Substring 1
20 cells in series"] --> BD1["Bypass Diode 1"] BD1 --> S2["Substring 2
20 cells in series"] S2 --> BD2["Bypass Diode 2"] BD2 --> S3["Substring 3
20 cells in series"] S3 --> JB["Junction Box
Output Terminals"] JB --> MC4["MC4 Connectors
Positive and Negative"] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Module I-V Curve Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["Inputs to System Irradiance G and Temperature T"] --> P2["Cell Model One Diode Equation"] P2 --> P3["Compute Isc, Voc, Imp, Vmp"] P3 --> P4["Compute Fill Factor FF"] P4 --> P5["Compute Module Efficiency eta"] P3 --> P6["Apply Rs and Rsh Parasitic Losses"] P6 --> P7["Generate I-V and P-V Curves"] P7 --> P8["Locate Maximum Power Point MPP"] P8 --> P9["Output Rated Power and Performance Flags"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Note:** Node labels are kept alphanumeric and free of markdown formatting per the diagram-rendering safeguard protocol. The block diagrams above represent the physical/logical architecture; for true 3D cross-sectional artwork, the engineer should consult IEC 61215 visual references. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** **Define the term *Fill Factor* of a PV module. State its typical range for a crystalline silicon module and explain what physical factors reduce the fill factor.** **Model Answer:** The **Fill Factor (FF)** of a photovoltaic module is defined as the ratio of the actual maximum electrical power output ($P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$) to the product of the open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current: $$ FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} $$ It is a **dimensionless** quantity that quantifies the "squareness" of the I-V curve. For a high-quality crystalline-silicon module, FF lies in the range of **0.75 to 0.82**. **Physical factors that reduce FF:** - **High series resistance ($R_s$)** caused by contact resistance, ribbon solder joints, and busbar resistance. This tilts the I-V curve near the $V_{oc}$ corner. - **Low shunt resistance ($R_{sh}$)** caused by edge shunts, crystal defects, and pinholes in the emitter. This tilts the I-V curve near the $I_{sc}$ corner. - **Excessive recombination** in the depletion region (high ideality factor $n$). --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **What is a *bypass diode* in a PV module? Why is it necessary, and how many bypass diodes are typically provided in a 72-cell crystalline silicon module?** **Model Answer:** A **bypass diode** is a diode connected in *anti-parallel* (reverse polarity) across a group of series-connected solar cells inside a module's junction box. Its function is to **provide an alternative current path** when one or more cells in the protected substring become reverse-biased due to **partial shading** or **mismatch**. **Why it is necessary:** A shaded cell acts as a load instead of a source and can dissipate the power generated by the unshaded cells as heat, creating a **hot spot** that can permanently damage the cell. The bypass diode limits the reverse voltage across the shaded substring to one forward diode drop (~0.7 V), thereby limiting the dissipated power and protecting the cells. **Count for 72-cell module:** Typically **3 bypass diodes**, each protecting a substring of **24 cells** in series. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU 2024 Pattern]** Answer **ONE** of the following: > - **OR Question A (14 marks)** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. > - **OR Question B (14 marks)** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- ### **OR Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **layered construction of a typical crystalline-silicon PV module**, listing the function of each layer. Explain the role of **EVA** and **backsheet polymers** in module durability. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PV module has the following STC ratings: $V_{oc} = 45.2$ V, $I_{sc} = 8.85$ A, $V_{mp} = 37.4$ V, $I_{mp} = 8.18$ A, Module area = 1.65 m². Calculate the **fill factor, maximum power output, and module efficiency.** Also calculate the **annual energy yield** if the average daily in-plane irradiance is **5.2 kWh/m²/day** with a **performance ratio (PR) of 0.78**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part A(a) A c-Si PV module is a sandwich of seven layers (top to bottom): 1. **Front Glass (Tempered, Low-Iron, ~3.2 mm):** Mechanical protection, hail resistance, ≥ 91 % transmittance in the visible–NIR band. 2. **Upper EVA Encapsulant:** Cross-linkable Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate that bonds glass to cells, provides UV/moisture sealing, optical coupling, and electrical insulation. 3. **Solar Cells:** Mono- or poly-crystalline silicon wafers with screen-printed metal contacts. 4. **Lower EVA Encapsulant:** Symmetric to the upper EVA — provides mechanical cushioning and insulation. 5. **Backsheet (TPT/KPK):** Multi-layer polymer (Tedlar/PET/Tedlar or polyolefin) — provides electrical insulation, UV barrier, and moisture protection. It is the *first line of defense against PID*. 6. **Junction Box:** Contains bypass diodes, MC4 connectors, IP67 sealing. 7. **Aluminum Frame:** Provides structural rigidity, mounting, and grounding path. **Role of EVA:** EVA is cross-linked during the lamination process at ~150 °C. Once cross-linked, it becomes a thermoset that does not flow at module operating temperatures. It cushions the cells against thermal expansion mismatch and provides 25+ years of UV stability. **Role of Backsheet Polymers:** The backsheet (typically a fluoropolymer like PVDF or Tedlar over PET) must withstand **25 years of UV exposure, humidity, and thermal cycling**. Modern polyolefin-based backsheets are being adopted as fluorine-free alternatives for end-of-life recyclability. --- #### Model Solution — Part A(b) **Step 1: Maximum Power Output** $$ P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 37.4 \times 8.18 $$ $$ P_{max} = 305.93 \text{ W} $$ **Step 2: Fill Factor** $$ FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{305.93}{45.2 \times 8.85} = \frac{305.93}{400.02} $$ $$ FF = 0.7648 \approx 0.765 $$ **Step 3: Module Conversion Efficiency** $$ \eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{305.93}{1000 \times 1.65} = \frac{305.93}{1650} $$ $$ \eta = 0.1854 = 18.54 \,\% $$ **Step 4: Annual Energy Yield (using Performance Ratio)** The Performance Ratio (PR) is defined as: $$ PR = \frac{E_{actual}}{E_{ideal}} $$ The *ideal* (DC STC) annual energy per m² of aperture: $$ H_{annual} = 5.2 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day} \times 365 = 1898 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr} $$ The actual annual energy yield per module: $$ E_{annual} = PR \cdot \eta \cdot A \cdot H_{annual} $$ $$ E_{annual} = 0.78 \times 0.1854 \times 1.65 \times 1898 $$ $$ E_{annual} = 0.78 \times 581.0 = 453.2 \text{ kWh/yr} $$ > **Result:** The module produces approximately **305.93 W at STC, 18.54 % efficiency, Fill Factor 0.765, and ~453 kWh per year** under the given field conditions. --- ### **OR Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the **expression for the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$** of a solar cell from the single-diode model, clearly stating the underlying assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concepts of **module mismatch losses, hot-spot heating, and PID (Potential Induced Degradation)**. What measures are taken at the module level to mitigate each? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part B(a) **Starting Equation — Single-Diode Model:** $$ I = I_{ph} - I_o \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_{th}}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}} $$ where $V_{th} = kT/q$ is the thermal voltage. **Assumption 1:** At open-circuit, $I = 0$ and the terminal voltage is $V = V_{oc}$. **Assumption 2:** Series resistance $R_s$ is negligible compared to the diode's differential resistance (valid for most practical cells). **Assumption 3:** Shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ is very large (no leakage path), so the last term vanishes. **Applying these assumptions:** $$ 0 = I_{ph} - I_o \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_{th}}\right) - 1\right] - 0 $$ **Step 1:** Rearranging: $$ I_o \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_{th}}\right) - 1\right] = I_{ph} $$ **Step 2:** Dividing both sides by $I_o$: $$ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_{th}}\right) - 1 = \frac{I_{ph}}{I_o} $$ **Step 3:** Adding 1 to both sides: $$ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_{th}}\right) = \frac{I_{ph}}{I_o} + 1 $$ **Step 4:** Taking the natural logarithm: $$ \frac{V_{oc}}{n V_{th}} = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_o} + 1\right) $$ **Step 5:** Multiplying both sides by $n V_{th}$: $$ \boxed{V_{oc} = n V_{th} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_o} + 1\right)} $$ **Step 6:** Since $I_{ph} = I_{sc}$ and $I_{ph}/I_o \gg 1$ for a good diode, the "+1" can be dropped: $$ V_{oc} \approx n V_{th} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{sc}}{I_o}\right) $$ **Physical Insight:** $V_{oc}$ depends *logarithmically* on the ratio $I_{sc}/I_o$. Reducing the reverse saturation current $I_o$ (by improving material quality, passivation, lower recombination) is the most effective way to increase $V_{oc}$. --- #### Model Solution — Part B(b) **1. Module Mismatch Losses:** When two or more cells/modules with different I-V characteristics are connected in series, the string current is limited by the **weakest cell**, and the total power is less than the sum of individual maximum powers. This loss is called **mismatch loss**, typically **2–5 %** in well-designed strings. **Mitigation:** - **Binning:** Manufacturers sort (bin) cells/modules by current class at the end of the production line so that all cells in a module and all modules in a string are within ±2 % of each other. - **Bypass diodes** allow partial mismatch to be tolerated without catastrophic power loss. **2. Hot-Spot Heating:** When a cell in a series string is shaded, it is forced into reverse bias by the remaining cells, and dissipates the power it absorbs. If the power exceeds the cell's heat dissipation capability, the cell temperature rises rapidly, leading to solder melting, cell cracking, or fire. **Mitigation:** - **Bypass diodes** (already discussed) — one per 18–24 cell substring. - **Module-level optimizers** (e.g., Tigo, SolarEdge) — allow each module to operate at its own MPP, completely avoiding the hot-spot failure mode. - **String fuses** in the DC combiner box. **3. Potential-Induced Degradation (PID):** PID is a phenomenon where high voltage stress (especially in 1500 V DC systems) between the cell and the grounded frame causes **sodium ions** from the glass to drift into the cell, shunting the p-n junction and causing rapid power loss (up to 30 % in months). **Mitigation:** - **Anti-PID encapsulants** — special EVA formulations with high volume resistivity. - **PID-resistant cells** — SiN anti-reflection coating and grounding of the negative pole. - **Potential-free module design** — electrically floating frames. - **Reverse-polarity recovery:** Applying reverse voltage at night can sometimes recover PID-affected modules. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing $V_{mp}$ with $V_{oc}$:** Students often use $V_{oc}$ in the efficiency formula instead of $V_{mp}$. Always use $V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ for power. > 2. **Forgetting to convert cell area to m²:** Mixing cm² and m² in the efficiency formula yields a 10000× error. > 3. **Confusing module $V_{oc}$ with cell $V_{oc}$:** A 60-cell module has $V_{oc} \approx 60 \times 0.62 \approx 37$ V, not 0.62 V. > 4. **Writing FF with units:** FF is dimensionless; do not write "FF = 0.78 V" or "FF = 78 %". > 5. **Omitting assumptions** in the $V_{oc}$ derivation — examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for missing the $R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$ assumptions. > 6. **No mention of NOCT** when discussing field performance — examiners expect the STC vs NOCT distinction. > 7. **Writing the bypass diode in series with the substring** — it must be in *anti-parallel* (parallel but reverse polarity) across the substring. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] A **PV module** is the smallest field-replaceable unit — a series/parallel interconnection of cells encapsulated in glass/EVA/backsheet. - [ ] The hierarchy is **Cell → Module → String → Array**. - [ ] STC: $G = 1000$ W/m², $T_{cell} = 25$ °C, AM 1.5. - [ ] NOCT: ~45 °C — the actual field operating temperature. - [ ] $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ — never confuse with $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$. - [ ] $FF = P_{max}/(V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$, typical 0.75–0.82. - [ ] $\eta = P_{max}/(G \cdot A)$, expressed in %. - [ ] $V_{oc} = n V_{th} \ln(I_{sc}/I_o + 1)$ — derived from single-diode model. - [ ] $R_s$ reduces FF by tilting the **voltage corner** of the I-V curve. - [ ] $R_{sh}$ reduces FF by tilting the **current corner** of the I-V curve. - [ ] **Bypass diodes** are connected in *anti-parallel* to 18–24 cell substrings — 3 in a 60-cell module, 3 in a 72-cell module. - [ ] **Hot spots** occur when a shaded cell dissipates power; bypass diodes mitigate this. - [ ] **Mismatch losses** (2–5 %) are reduced by cell binning during manufacturing. - [ ] **PID** is a high-voltage-induced ion migration phenomenon; mitigated by anti-PID encapsulants and grounded frames. - [ ] Temperature coefficient of $P_{max}$ for c-Si: **−0.4 %/°C** (modules lose power as they heat up). - [ ] Standard module areas: **1.65 m²** (60 cells) and **1.95 m²** (72 cells). - [ ] Key polymers in a module: **EVA** (encapsulant), **Tedlar/PET/Polyolefin** (backsheet), **Silicone** (edge sealant), **EPDM/PIB** (junction-box potting). - [ ] Modern c-Si module efficiency benchmark (2024): **20–22 %**; research cell record: **26.8 %** (LONGi, 2024). - [ ] Annual energy yield formula: $E = PR \cdot \eta \cdot A \cdot H_{annual}$. - [ ] Always state **assumptions** ($R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$) explicitly in $V_{oc}$ derivations for full KTU marks.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ MODULE 2 — MODELLING OF PHOTOVOLTAIC DEVICES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Polymers & Photovoltaic Technology**, **Modelling** refers to the mathematical and circuit-level representation of a photovoltaic (PV) cell or module that captures its electrical behaviour under varying illumination, temperature, and load conditions. The most widely accepted analytical model is the **Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit Model (One-Diode Model / Five-Parameter Model)**, which describes a real PV cell as an ideal current source (photogenerated current, $I_{ph}$) in parallel with a diode (representing the p–n junction), a shunt resistance ($R_{sh}$), and a series resistance ($R_s$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> For PEPOT757, the modelling section under Module 2 typically covers the **Single-Diode Model (SDM)** of a PV cell, its **I–V characteristic equation**, the role of **$R_s$** and **$R_{sh}$**, **fill factor (FF)**, **efficiency ($\eta$)**, and the simplified **ideal cell approximation** (where $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$).

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a water tap (the **solar cell**) connected to a complex pipe network (the **external circuit**). The tap can only deliver a fixed flow rate determined by the water pressure (the **photogenerated current** $I_{ph}$, governed by sunlight). However:

- The tap itself has internal friction (this is like **series resistance $R_s$** — resistance due to contact and bulk semiconductor losses).
- The tap has a small leak that bypasses the main flow (this is like **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** — manufacturing defects and leakage paths across the junction).
- The tap cannot push water in only one direction; some of it tries to flow back when downstream pressure is high (this is the **junction diode** — the dark current / recombination current $I_0$ that opposes the photocurrent).

A **model** in PV engineering is essentially the **mathematical "skeleton"** that tells us exactly how much useful current $I$ we can extract at any given voltage $V$ from this imperfect tap. Without modelling, we would have to perform costly experiments for every new cell design. With modelling, we can **predict performance, optimise polymer–fullerene blends in organic PVs, and simulate large solar farms** on a computer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A model is **not** the real cell. It is a *simplified, mathematically tractable representation* that captures the dominant physical phenomena — photogeneration, recombination, and ohmic losses — using a handful of parameters.

### 📐 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Typical Value (c-Si cell) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $k$ | Boltzmann's constant | **J/K** | **1.380 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| $q$ | Elementary charge | **C** | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |
| $T$ | Cell absolute temperature | **K** | 298 K (25 °C) |
| $E_g$ | Bandgap of absorber | **eV** | 1.12 (c-Si), ~1.5 (polymer: P3HT) |
| $G$ | Solar irradiance | **W/m²** | 1000 (STC) |
| $I_{sc}$ | Short-circuit current | **A** | depends on cell area |
| $V_{oc}$ | Open-circuit voltage | **V** | ~0.6 (c-Si), ~0.6–0.8 (polymer) |
| $V_m, I_m$ | Voltage/Current at max power | **V, A** | — |
| $FF$ | Fill Factor | **dimensionless** | 0.7–0.85 (c-Si) |
| $\eta$ | Power conversion efficiency | **%** | 15–26% (research c-Si) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** Always write the units of every physical quantity. Marks are often awarded for correct unit notation.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔍 The Single-Diode Model — Conceptual Breakdown

The single-diode model is derived from the **semiconductor physics of a p–n junction** under illumination. Let us walk through the operational logic step by step.

**Step 1 — Photogeneration (Light → Current)**
When photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ strike the absorber (silicon, or a polymer like P3HT:PCBM), electron–hole pairs are generated. The rate of generation is proportional to the incident irradiance $G$. The resulting **photocurrent** $I_{ph}$ is treated as a constant current source flowing from the negative to the positive terminal of the equivalent circuit.

**Step 2 — Junction Diode (Dark Current)**
The p–n junction itself behaves like a diode. When forward biased, it allows a recombination current (Shockley's diode equation) to flow in the **opposite direction** to $I_{ph}$. This is the **dark saturation current** $I_0$, strongly dependent on temperature.

**Step 3 — Shunt Resistance $R_{sh}$ (Parallel / Leakage Path)**
Real cells have small leakage currents that bypass the junction. This is modelled as a resistor $R_{sh}$ in **parallel** with the diode. A low $R_{sh}$ causes significant power loss.

**Step 4 — Series Resistance $R_s$ (Bulk + Contact Losses)**
The resistance of the semiconductor bulk, the metal contacts, and the interconnections cause a voltage drop. This is modelled as a resistor $R_s$ in **series** with the entire combination.

**Step 5 — Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the Output Node**
The terminal current $I$ delivered to the external load equals the photogenerated current minus the diode current minus the shunt current.

**Step 6 — Implicit I–V Equation**
Combining the above, we get the famous **five-parameter implicit equation** that cannot be solved analytically for $I$ (it appears on both sides).

**Step 7 — Boundary Cases (Ideal Cell)**
- If $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$, we obtain the **ideal cell equation**.
- If we set $V = 0$, we get $I = I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$.
- If we set $I = 0$, we get $V = V_{oc}$.

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$ | **Single-Diode Model (General)** — implicit in $I$ |
| 2 | $I \approx I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{q V}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$ | **Ideal Cell Model** ($R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$) |
| 3 | $I_{ph} \approx I_{sc}$ (at $V = 0$) | Photocurrent ≈ Short-circuit current |
| 4 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k T}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_{sc}}{I_0} + 1 \right)$ | **Open-circuit voltage** (set $I = 0$) |
| 5 | $P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m$ | **Maximum power** on the I–V curve |
| 6 | $FF = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | **Fill Factor** (rectangularity of I–V curve) |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$ | **Conversion efficiency** |
| 8 | $V_T = \dfrac{k T}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV at } 300\text{ K}$ | **Thermal voltage** (remember at 300 K) |
| 9 | $I_0 = A T^3 \exp\left(-\dfrac{q E_g}{n k T}\right)$ | Diode saturation current (temperature dependent) |
| 10 | $I_{ph}(G) = I_{ph,STC} \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_{STC}}$ | Photocurrent scales linearly with irradiance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Memorise Equations 1, 2, 4, 6, 7, and 8. These appear in **almost every modelling question**, either directly (5-mark derivation) or as part of a 14-mark Part B problem.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Solar Farm Design:** Engineers use these models in tools like **PVsyst, SAM, MATLAB/Simulink** to predict annual energy yield of MW-scale plants.
2. **Polymer Solar Cells (OPV):** The same single-diode model is used for organic PVs, but $n$ is often between 1.5–2 (instead of 1–1.3 for c-Si) due to higher recombination.
3. **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT):** The derivative $dP/dV = 0$ (from Equation 5) is solved numerically by MPPT algorithms in inverters.
4. **Fault Diagnosis:** Deviations between measured and modelled I–V curves reveal degradation, soiling, or cell cracks.
5. **Device Optimisation:** By adjusting $R_s$, $R_{sh}$, $n$, and $E_g$ in simulation, polymer chemists can optimise donor/acceptor ratios **before** fabricating expensive prototypes.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 Derivation 1: From Ideal Diode Equation to the Single-Diode PV Model

We start from **Shockley's diode equation** for the current through a p–n junction in the dark:

$$I_{dark} = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q V}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

When the junction is illuminated, a photogenerated current $I_{ph}$ flows in the **opposite direction** to the dark current. By the **principle of superposition** (valid for ideal diodes), the net current through the junction is:

$$I_{junction} = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q V}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1 (text):** Identify the three parallel branches in the equivalent circuit: photocurrent source, diode, and shunt resistance.
- **Step 2 (text):** Apply KCL at the top node — the current $I$ leaving the cell equals the current entering from these three branches.
- **Step 3 (text):** The voltage across the diode and $R_{sh}$ is **not** the terminal voltage $V$, but the voltage after the drop across $R_s$, i.e., $V + I R_s$.
- **Step 4 (text):** Substitute this corrected voltage into the diode and resistor expressions.
- **Step 5 (text):** Combine all terms to obtain the implicit single-diode equation.

Let us write the final derivation:

The current through the shunt resistor is:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

The current through the diode (with corrected voltage) is:

$$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q (V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

By KCL at the output node:

$$I_{ph} = I + I_D + I_{sh}$$

Substituting $I_D$ and $I_{sh}$:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q (V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical single-diode model equation** (Equation 1 in the cheat sheet). Note the term $(V + I R_s)$ — the diode "sees" the internal voltage, not the terminal voltage.

### 🧮 Derivation 2: Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$

**Step 1 (text):** At open-circuit, the terminal current $I = 0$.
**Step 2 (text):** For an ideal cell ($R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$), the shunt current term vanishes and the equation reduces.
**Step 3 (text):** Set $I = 0$ and solve for $V = V_{oc}$.

Starting from the ideal cell equation:

$$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Rearranging:

$$I_{ph} = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Since $I_{ph} \gg I_0$, the "$-1$" inside the bracket is negligible compared to the exponential:

$$\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} = \exp\left(\frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}\right)$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right) = \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}$$

Solving for $V_{oc}$:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right) = \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_{sc}}{I_0}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $V_{oc}$ increases **logarithmically** with light intensity (because $I_{ph} \propto G$) and decreases linearly with increasing temperature (because $I_0$ grows exponentially with $T$). This is why hot panels lose voltage.

### 🧮 Derivation 3: Conversion Efficiency $\eta$

**Step 1 (text):** The efficiency is the ratio of maximum electrical power output to the incident optical power.
**Step 2 (text):** Maximum power is $P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m$ (the rectangle inscribed under the I–V curve).
**Step 3 (text):** The incident power is $P_{in} = G \cdot A$, where $G$ is irradiance (W/m²) and $A$ is the cell area (m²).
**Step 4 (text):** Using the definition of Fill Factor, $P_{max} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$.

Therefore:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$$

### 🧮 Derivation 4: Thermal Voltage at Standard Test Conditions (STC)

**Step 1 (text):** STC specifies $T = 25\,°\text{C} = 298.15\,\text{K}$.
**Step 2 (text):** Substitute values of $k$, $q$, and $T$ into $V_T = kT/q$.

$$V_T = \frac{(1.380 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}) \times (298.15\,\text{K})}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}}$$

$$V_T = \frac{4.116 \times 10^{-21}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02569\,\text{V} \approx 25.69\,\text{mV}$$

> [!TIP]
> Use $V_T = 25.85\,\text{mV}$ if the question specifies $T = 300\,\text{K}$. This minor difference matters in **7-mark derivations** where full marks depend on numerical accuracy.

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on Cell Performance

| Parameter ↑ (Increase) | $V_{oc}$ | $I_{sc}$ | $FF$ | $\eta$ | Physical Cause |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $I_{ph}$ (more light) | ↑ (log) | ↑ (linear) | ↑ slightly | ↑ | More e–h pairs generated |
| $I_0$ (more leakage) | ↓ | ≈ no change | ↓ | ↓ | More recombination |
| $n$ (ideality factor) | ↓ (log) | ≈ no change | ↓ | ↓ | Worse junction quality |
| $R_s$ | ↓ slightly | ↓ | ↓↓ (large) | ↓↓ | Ohmic losses |
| $R_{sh}$ | ≈ no change | ↓ slightly | ↓ | ↓ | Leakage paths |
| $T$ | ↓ (~−2 mV/°C) | ↑ slightly | ↓ | ↓ | Higher $I_0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Examiner's Note:** This matrix is a **favourite 7-mark question** — "Discuss the effect of $R_s$, $R_{sh}$, $I_0$, and temperature on PV cell performance." Practise drawing this table and explaining each row.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** Define the Fill Factor (FF) of a photovoltaic cell. What is its typical range for a crystalline silicon cell? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Fill Factor (FF) is a dimensionless figure of merit that quantifies the **"squareness"** of the I–V curve of a solar cell. It is defined as the ratio of the **maximum obtainable power** ($P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m$) to the product of the open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current:

$$FF = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

Geometrically, $FF$ is the ratio of the **largest rectangle that fits under the I–V curve** to the rectangle defined by $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$. For a high-quality crystalline silicon cell, FF lies in the range **0.70 to 0.85**. Polymer/organic solar cells typically have lower FF (0.50–0.65) due to higher series resistance and recombination losses. (3 marks)

---

#### **Question 2.** What is meant by the "ideality factor" $n$ in the single-diode model? What is its significance? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The ideality factor $n$ (also called the **emission coefficient** or **quality factor**) is a dimensionless parameter in the single-diode model that quantifies **how closely the diode behaviour of the p–n junction matches that of an ideal Shockley diode**.

- For an **ideal diode**, $n = 1$ (pure diffusion current).
- For a **real PV cell**, $n$ lies between 1 and 2. Polymer/organic cells often have $n \approx 1.5$–$2$ due to trap-assisted recombination.
- It appears in the exponential term of the diode equation: $I_D = I_0 [\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$.

**Significance:** A higher $n$ indicates **greater deviation from ideal junction behaviour**, typically caused by recombination in the depletion region, surface states, or poor interface quality. Higher $n$ reduces both $V_{oc}$ and $FF$, thereby lowering the cell efficiency. (3 marks)

### 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "$-1$" term** in the diode equation — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $T$ in °C instead of K** in the thermal voltage calculation — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing $I_{ph}$ with $I_{sc}$** — strictly, $I_{ph} = I_{sc}$ only when $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$. Examiners will deduct for stating they are always equal.
> 4. **Not drawing a labelled equivalent circuit** in derivations — a must for full marks.
> 5. **Mixing up the directions of $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$** in the circuit — series with the whole combination, not just the diode.

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1).** With the help of a neat equivalent circuit, derive the **single-diode model equation** of a photovoltaic cell. Explain the physical significance of each component. Discuss how the values of series and shunt resistances affect the I–V characteristics. `[14 Marks]`

**Distribution:** (a) Derivation & equation → 7 marks, (b) Physical significance & resistance effects → 7 marks.

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Diagram 1** in Section 4. The single-diode equivalent circuit consists of:

1. A **current source** $I_{ph}$ (photocurrent, proportional to irradiance $G$).
2. A **diode** $D$ with reverse saturation current $I_0$ and ideality factor $n$.
3. A **shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ in parallel with the diode.
4. A **series resistance** $R_s$ in series with the whole combination.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** When a photon of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ is absorbed, an electron–hole pair is created, contributing to a current $I_{ph}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The p–n junction acts as a diode. The current through the diode is governed by Shockley's equation:

$$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q (V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

The voltage across the diode is the **terminal voltage plus the drop across $R_s$**, i.e., $V + I R_s$, because $R_s$ is in series with the load.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The shunt resistance carries a leakage current:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the top node:

$$I_{ph} = I + I_D + I_{sh}$$

Substituting:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q (V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** This is the **single-diode model (SDM) equation** — implicit in $I$ (it appears inside the exponential). For the **ideal cell case** ($R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$):

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q V}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

##### **Part (b) — Physical Significance & Effect of Resistances (7 marks):**

**Physical Significance of Each Element (3.5 marks):**

- **$I_{ph}$ (Photocurrent Source):** Represents the rate of electron–hole pair generation. Depends on irradiance $G$, absorption coefficient, and cell area. $I_{ph} \propto G$.
- **Diode ($D$):** Models the rectifying behaviour of the p–n junction. The dark current $I_D$ opposes $I_{ph}$ and represents recombination losses.
- **$R_{sh}$ (Shunt Resistance):** Represents the leakage current paths around the junction, caused by pinholes, edge defects, and impurity inclusions. Ideally $R_{sh} \to \infty$.
- **$R_s$ (Series Resistance):** Represents the ohmic losses in the semiconductor bulk, the metal–semiconductor contacts, the bus bars, and the finger electrodes. Ideally $R_s \to 0$.

**Effect of $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ on I–V Characteristics (3.5 marks):**

- **Increasing $R_s$ (1.5 marks):**
  - Reduces the **slope** of the I–V curve in the power-producing region (the curve "tilts").
  - Significantly **lowers the Fill Factor (FF)** and hence the efficiency.
  - Has a small effect on $V_{oc}$ but **substantially reduces $I_{sc}$** in severe cases.
  - In the equivalent circuit, the voltage drop $I \cdot R_s$ subtracts from the useful terminal voltage.

- **Decreasing $R_{sh}$ (1 mark):**
  - Causes a **"sagging"** of the I–V curve near the short-circuit region (left side).
  - Reduces **$I_{sc}$** and **FF**, but has little effect on $V_{oc}$.
  - Represents a parallel current path that diverts photogenerated carriers away from the external load.

- **Ideal cell (1 mark):** When $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$, the I–V curve has a perfect "knee" and maximum FF. Real cells always deviate from this ideal.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative).** Starting from the ideal cell I–V equation, derive expressions for the **open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$)** and **conversion efficiency ($\eta$)** of a photovoltaic cell. Define Fill Factor and state the factors that affect it. Briefly compare the modelling of crystalline silicon and polymer solar cells. `[14 Marks]`

**Distribution:** (a) Derivation of $V_{oc}$ and $\eta$ → 7 marks, (b) Fill Factor + comparison → 7 marks.

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivations (7 marks):**

**Derivation of $V_{oc}$ (4 marks):**

The ideal cell equation is:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q V}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** At open-circuit, $I = 0$ and $V = V_{oc}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substituting:

$$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Rearranging:

$$\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 = \exp\left(\frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Taking the natural logarithm and noting $I_{ph}/I_0 \gg 1$:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right) \approx \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_{sc}}{I_0}\right)$$

**Derivation of $\eta$ (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Power delivered to the load is $P = V \cdot I$. The maximum power point occurs at $(V_m, I_m)$, so $P_{max} = V_m I_m$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The Fill Factor is defined as $FF = V_m I_m / (V_{oc} I_{sc})$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Therefore the conversion efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$$

where $G$ is the incident irradiance (W/m²) and $A$ is the cell area (m²).

##### **Part (b) — Fill Factor & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Fill Factor Definition (2 marks):**

Fill Factor is the ratio of the **maximum power rectangle** inscribed under the I–V curve to the **$V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$ rectangle**:

$$FF = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

It is a measure of the **"squareness"** of the I–V curve. Higher FF indicates lower internal losses.

**Factors Affecting Fill Factor (3 marks):**

1. **Series Resistance $R_s$:** Increases $R_s$ → FF decreases sharply.
2. **Shunt Resistance $R_{sh}$:** Decreases $R_{sh}$ → FF decreases.
3. **Ideality Factor $n$:** Higher $n$ → FF decreases.
4. **Temperature:** Higher $T$ → FF decreases slightly.
5. **Junction Quality:** Defects, recombination, and traps lower FF.

**Comparison: c-Si vs Polymer Solar Cells (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Crystalline Silicon | Polymer (Organic) PV |
|---|---|---|
| Bandgap $E_g$ | 1.12 eV (indirect) | 1.4–1.8 eV (tunable) |
| Ideality factor $n$ | 1.0–1.3 | 1.5–2.0 |
| $V_{oc}$ | 0.6–0.7 V | 0.6–0.8 V (HOMO–LUMO limited) |
| $J_{sc}$ | 30–40 mA/cm² | 10–20 mA/cm² |
| Typical FF | 0.75–0.85 | 0.50–0.65 |
| Efficiency | 20–26% | 10–19% (research) |
| Charge generation | Free e–h pair | **Exciton** (must diffuse to interface) |
| Model applicability | Single-diode, $n \approx 1$ | Single-diode with $n \approx 1.5$–$2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymer PV Modelling Note:** The single-diode model is still used for organic cells, but the **photocurrent is limited by exciton diffusion** (typically 10–20 nm in P3HT), requiring a bulk heterojunction (BHJ) morphology. The ideality factor is higher because of trap-assisted recombination at the donor/acceptor interface.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Modelling**

- ✅ The **single-diode model (SDM)** is the standard PV model with **5 parameters**: $I_{ph}$, $I_0$, $n$, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$.
- ✅ The canonical SDM equation is: $I = I_{ph} - I_0[\exp(q(V + IR_s)/nkT) - 1] - (V + IR_s)/R_{sh}$.
- ✅ **Ideal cell approximation:** $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$ → simpler equation.
- ✅ **Open-circuit voltage:** $V_{oc} = (nkT/q) \ln(I_{sc}/I_0)$ — depends **logarithmically** on light.
- ✅ **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV (use 25.69 mV at 298 K).
- ✅ **Fill Factor:** $FF = V_m I_m / (V_{oc} I_{sc})$ — typical 0.7–0.85 for c-Si, 0.5–0.65 for polymers.
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF) / (G \cdot A)$.
- ✅ $I_{ph} \approx I_{sc}$ only in the ideal case — be precise in derivations.
- ✅ **$R_s$ increases** → Fill Factor drops sharply → efficiency loss (most important practical loss).
- ✅ **$R_{sh}$ decreases** → leakage increases → $I_{sc}$ and FF drop.
- ✅ **Temperature effect:** $V_{oc}$ drops ~**2 mV/°C** with rising temperature; $I_{sc}$ rises slightly; net efficiency drops.
- ✅ **Polymer PV** uses the same SDM but with higher $n$ (1.5–2) and exciton-diffusion-limited current.
- ✅ The SDM equation is **implicit in $I$** — must be solved numerically (Newton–Raphson in MATLAB).
- ✅ Always draw the **labelled equivalent circuit** in derivations — it carries 1–2 marks.
- ✅ Remember the **"$-1$"** term in Shockley's equation — examiners love to test this.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Trick:** Think of the SDM as **"Photocurrent MINUS Diode MINUS Shunt"** → $I = I_{ph} - I_D - I_{sh}$. Always in this order.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Characteristic of PV Module
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electrical Characteristic of PV Module # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **electrical characteristic of a Photovoltaic (PV) module** describes the operational behaviour of a solar cell/module as a function of terminal voltage, output current, irradiance, and cell temperature. It is primarily represented by two interdependent curves: - The **Current–Voltage (I–V) Characteristic Curve**, which plots the current delivered by the module against the terminal voltage. - The **Power–Voltage (P–V) Characteristic Curve**, derived from the I–V curve, which identifies the operating point at which the module delivers its **Maximum Power (P_max)**. These characteristics are mathematically governed by the **single-diode equivalent circuit model** derived from the **Shockley Diode Equation**, and they encode all critical performance parameters: **Short-Circuit Current (I_sc)**, **Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc)**, **Fill Factor (FF)**, **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**, and **Conversion Efficiency (η)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A standard 36-cell or 72-cell crystalline silicon PV module exhibits an I–V curve where the current remains nearly constant up to the "knee" voltage and then drops sharply, defining the MPP. The four key points — **I_sc, V_oc, I_mpp, V_mpp** — form the cornerstone of every KTU numerical and design question on this topic. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pump" Intuition Imagine a PV module as a **specialised water pump** powered by sunlight rather than electricity: - **Sunlight intensity (Irradiance, G)** = the *speed* at which water is pushed into the pipe. More sun = more water flow. - **Terminal Voltage (V)** = the *back-pressure* you apply to the outlet. Open the tap fully (V = 0) and you get the **maximum flow (I_sc)**. Close the tap completely (I = 0) and the pressure gauge reads its **maximum value (V_oc)**. - **Power (P = V × I)** = the *useful work* done by water striking a turbine. There exists a *sweet spot* of back-pressure where the water does the most useful work — this is the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**. - **Fill Factor (FF)** = how *efficiently* the pump uses its full theoretical capability, expressed as the ratio of the actual "useful area" to the "theoretical rectangle." This analogy helps cement why both current **and** voltage must be considered simultaneously, and why simply maximising one does **not** maximise power. ## 1.3 Standard Test & Reference Conditions All PV module ratings are universally measured at **Standard Test Conditions (STC)**: | Parameter | Symbol | STC Value | |---|---|---| | Irradiance | G | **1000 W/m²** (1 Sun, AM1.5G spectrum) | | Cell Temperature | T | **25 °C (298.15 K)** | | Air Mass | AM | **1.5** | > [!NOTE] > Real-world performance deviates from STC because modules operate at higher temperatures (40–70 °C), reducing both V_oc and efficiency. KTU questions frequently test the **temperature coefficients** of I_sc, V_oc, and P_max. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit A real PV cell is modelled using the **Single-Diode Model (SDM)**, consisting of: 1. A **photo-generated current source (I_ph)** — proportional to incident irradiance. 2. A **diode in parallel** — representing the p–n junction diffusion and recombination currents. 3. A **series resistance (R_s)** — representing ohmic losses in the front contacts, emitter, base, and interconnect ribbons. 4. A **shunt (parallel) resistance (R_sh)** — representing leakage currents across the p–n junction due to manufacturing defects. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** The five-parameter model (I_ph, I_0, n, R_s, R_sh) is the standard analytical framework expected for derivations. KTU questions often ask to derive the **I–V implicit equation** and discuss how R_s and R_sh degrade the Fill Factor. ## 2.2 The Governing Equation (Implicit I–V Form) Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the output node of the equivalent circuit yields the implicit transcendental equation: $$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$ Where: - $I$ = output terminal current (A) - $V$ = output terminal voltage (V) - $I_{ph}$ = photo-generated current (A) - $I_0$ = diode reverse saturation current (A) - $n$ = diode ideality factor (typically 1 ≤ n ≤ 2) - $V_T$ = thermal voltage = $kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K - $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.3806 × 10⁻²³ J/K** - $q$ = electron charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** ## 2.3 Critical Boundary Conditions & Special Points | Operating Point | Condition | Resulting Quantity | |---|---|---| | **Short-Circuit (SC)** | $V = 0$ | $I = I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$ (when $R_{sh} \to \infty$) | | **Open-Circuit (OC)** | $I = 0$ | $V = V_{oc} = n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | | **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** | $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$ | $I = I_m,\; V = V_m,\; P_{max} = V_m I_m$ | ## 2.4 Fill Factor (FF) — Quality Index of the Curve The **Fill Factor** is the ratio of the actual maximum obtainable power to the product of open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current (the theoretical rectangular limit): $$FF = \dfrac{V_m I_m}{V_{oc} I_{sc}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$$ - **Typical values:** 0.70 – 0.85 for good crystalline silicon cells; lower (0.50 – 0.70) for thin-film modules. - **Effects:** Increasing R_s *flattens* the curve near V_oc; decreasing R_sh *tilts* the curve near I_sc. Both reduce FF. ## 2.5 Conversion Efficiency (η) $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \, I_{sc} \, FF}{G \cdot A}$$ Where: - $G$ = incident irradiance (W/m²) - $A$ = effective module area (m²) > [!NOTE] > **Why it matters:** A higher FF with the same V_oc and I_sc translates directly to a higher η.
