KTU 2024 questions often ask students to compare two modules and justify which is "better" using FF and η. ## 2.6 Effect of Irradiance (G) and Temperature (T) ### Effect of Irradiance: - $I_{sc}$ is **directly proportional** to G: $I_{sc} \propto G$ - $V_{oc}$ increases *logarithmically* with G: $V_{oc} \propto \ln(G)$ ### Effect of Temperature: - $I_{sc}$ increases *slightly* with temperature: $\dfrac{\partial I_{sc}}{\partial T} \approx +0.05\%/\text{°C}$ - $V_{oc}$ **decreases significantly** with temperature: $\dfrac{\partial V_{oc}}{\partial T} \approx -0.35\%/\text{°C}$ - Therefore, $P_{max}$ **decreases** with rising temperature. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---| | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[e^{(V+IR_s)/nV_T} - 1\right] - \dfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Single-diode I–V model | A, V | | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage | V (≈ 25.85 mV @ 300 K) | | $V_{oc} = nV_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage | V | | $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$ | Short-circuit current (ideal) | A | | $FF = \dfrac{V_m I_m}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor | Dimensionless | | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{G \cdot A}$ | Conversion efficiency | % | | $I_{sc,new} = I_{sc,STC} \times \dfrac{G_{new}}{1000}$ | Irradiance scaling of I_sc | A | | $V_{oc,new} = V_{oc,STC} + N_s \cdot \dfrac{\partial V_{oc}}{\partial T}(T_{new} - 25)$ | Temperature correction of V_oc | V | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc) We begin with the single-diode equation from §2.2. At the open-circuit condition, the terminal current $I = 0$ and the terminal voltage equals $V_{oc}$. Substituting: $$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}$$ **Step 1 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:** $$I_{ph} + I_0 = I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) + \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the standard engineering approximation $R_{sh} \to \infty$ (shunt path is negligible for an ideal cell):** The $\dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}$ term drops out, leaving: $$I_{ph} + I_0 = I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Divide both sides by $I_0$:** $$\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:** $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right) = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $V_{oc}$:** $$\boxed{V_{oc} = n V_T \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)}$$ **Interpretation:** Because $I_{ph} \gg I_0$ (by 6–10 orders of magnitude), the "+1" inside the logarithm is mathematically negligible. V_oc is therefore dominated by the **logarithmic ratio** of light-generated current to reverse saturation current. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fill Factor (FF) from First Principles The Fill Factor is *defined* as the ratio of the actual MPP rectangle area to the bounding rectangle defined by I_sc and V_oc. **Step 1 — Express P(V) using the load-line form:** For a given operating voltage V, the current is I(V). The instantaneous power delivered is: $$P(V) = V \cdot I(V)$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate P(V) with respect to V to locate the maximum:** $$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = I(V) + V \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0$$ **Step 3 — At the MPP, this yields the necessary condition:** $$\dfrac{dI}{dV}\bigg|_{V_m} = -\dfrac{I_m}{V_m}$$ This is a numerical condition that, combined with the implicit I–V equation, must be solved iteratively (Newton–Raphson). For an *idealised* cell (R_s = 0, R_sh → ∞), the empirical approximation is: $$FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1} \quad \text{where} \quad v_{oc} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}$$ **Step 4 — The final FF definition:** $$\boxed{FF = \dfrac{V_m I_m}{V_{oc} I_{sc}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Efficiency (η) from STC Ratings **Step 1 — Power input to the module area A at STC:** $$P_{in} = G_{STC} \times A = 1000 \times A \;\; (\text{W})$$ **Step 2 — Maximum electrical power output at STC:** $$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio:** $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{1000 \cdot A}$$ **Step 4 — Express in percentage form:** $$\boxed{\eta \, (\%) = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{10 \cdot A}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-world derating:** Manufacturers report STC efficiencies of 18–22% for mono-Si modules. Field efficiency is typically 3–5 percentage points lower due to temperature, dust, spectral mismatch, and DC-AC inverter losses. --- ## 3.4 Derivative Analysis: Effect of Series Resistance R_s Differentiating the single-diode equation with respect to V and evaluating at the short-circuit point shows that R_s has its dominant effect *near the V_oc knee*: $$\dfrac{\partial I}{\partial V}\bigg|_{V=0} = -\dfrac{1}{R_s} \quad \text{(approximately, for small R_sh)}$$ A high R_s **flattens** the I–V curve near V_oc, reducing V_m (and therefore P_max) without significantly affecting I_sc. This is the classic symptom of poor solder joints or high-resistivity emitter layers. ## 3.5 Derivative Analysis: Effect of Shunt Resistance R_sh A low R_sh acts as a *current leak* across the junction, **tilting** the I–V curve near I_sc and reducing I_m without significantly affecting V_oc. This is the classic symptom of manufacturing-induced shunts (e.g., pinholes in thin-film modules, edge isolation failures). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *direction* of R_s and R_sh effects. Memorise: **R_s → affects V_oc side; R_sh → affects I_sc side.** The Fill Factor is degraded by both, but in *different regions* of the curve. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit (Block-Level Functional Architecture) This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the topology of the single-diode model, the current flow paths, and the inter-element relationships governing the I–V characteristic. ```mermaid graph LR G((Incident
Irradiance G)) --> A[Photocurrent
Source Iph] A --> N1((Node A)) N1 --> D[Diode D
Ideality Factor n] N1 --> Rsh[Shunt Resistance
Rsh] N1 --> Rs[Series Resistance
Rs] Rs --> N2((Output Node B)) N2 --> L((External Load
V and I)) Rsh --> N2 D --> N1 A -- Iph --> N1 ``` **Node Interpretation:** - **Node A** is the internal junction node where KCL is applied. - **Node B** is the external terminal node accessible to the load. - **Three parallel current paths** leave Node A: (1) through the diode, (2) through R_sh, and (3) into the series resistance toward the load. ## 4.2 I–V and P–V Curve Topology — Block Architecture The I–V and P–V characteristic curves are best visualised as a *flow of operating states* that a PV module traverses as the load impedance varies from zero (short circuit) to infinity (open circuit). ```mermaid graph TD A[Start: Load = 0
Short Circuit] --> B[V = 0
I = Isc
P = 0] B --> C[Load Increasing
V Rising Slowly] C --> D[Knee Region
V approx Vm
I approx Im] D --> E[Maximum Power Point
P = Pmax
FF computed] E --> F[Load Increasing Further
V Rising Sharply] F --> G[Open Circuit
I = 0
V = Voc
P = 0] G --> H[End] D -.-> P1[P-V Curve Peak
Pmax] B -.-> Q1[I-V Curve Y-Intercept
Isc] G -.-> Q2[I-V Curve X-Intercept
Voc] ``` **Curve Topology Notes:** - The **I–V curve** is monotonically decreasing: starts at I_sc on the Y-axis and ends at V_oc on the X-axis. - The **P–V curve** starts at zero, rises to a single maximum (P_max), and returns to zero. The MPP is at the apex. - The product $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$ defines a *bounding rectangle*; the actual operating rectangle ($V_m \times I_m$) is inscribed within it. The **ratio of their areas = FF**. ## 4.3 Effect of Irradiance on I–V Curve (Block Comparison) ```mermaid graph LR S1[High Irradiance
G = 1000 W per m2
High Isc High Voc] --> P1[I-V Curve Right Up] S2[Medium Irradiance
G = 500 W per m2
Half Isc Slightly Lower Voc] --> P2[I-V Curve Middle] S3[Low Irradiance
G = 200 W per m2
Low Isc Low Voc] --> P3[I-V Curve Left Down] P1 --> CMP[Key Insight: Isc scales linearly with G
Voc scales logarithmically with G] P2 --> CMP P3 --> CMP ``` > [!NOTE] > At very low irradiance (e.g., 200 W/m²), the I–V curve shrinks primarily because I_sc collapses. The voltage drop is comparatively minor. This is why partially shaded or cloudy conditions disproportionately reduce the *current* delivered by a module. ## 4.4 Effect of Temperature on I–V Curve (Block Comparison) ```mermaid graph LR T1[Low Cell Temperature
T = 25 C
High Voc Normal Isc] --> R1[I-V Curve Tall and Right] T2[Nominal Cell Temperature
T = 45 C
Slightly Lower Voc] --> R2[I-V Curve Standard] T3[High Cell Temperature
T = 75 C
Lower Voc Slightly Higher Isc] --> R3[I-V Curve Compressed Right] R1 --> OBS[Key Insight: Voc decreases with temperature
Isc increases marginally
Pmax decreases overall] R2 --> OBS R3 --> OBS ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Hot Climate Design Note:** For every 1 °C rise above 25 °C, a typical crystalline silicon module loses approximately **0.4–0.5% of its rated power**. KTU design problems often require computing the expected power output at a specified ambient temperature by adding the *NOCT (Nominal Operating Cell Temperature)* offset to the ambient. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, May Attempt]** **Define Fill Factor of a solar cell. Mention its typical value range for a crystalline silicon cell.** **Model Answer:** The Fill Factor (FF) of a solar cell is defined as the ratio of the maximum obtainable power to the product of the open-circuit voltage (V_oc) and the short-circuit current (I_sc): $$FF = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$ It is a **dimensionless quantity** that quantifies the "squareness" of the I–V curve. For a high-quality crystalline silicon cell, FF typically ranges between **0.70 and 0.85**. Lower values (0.50 – 0.70) are observed in thin-film and amorphous silicon modules due to higher recombination and series resistance losses. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **Differentiate between the effects of series resistance (R_s) and shunt resistance (R_sh) on the I–V curve of a PV module.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Series Resistance (R_s) | Shunt Resistance (R_sh) | |---|---|---| | **Origin** | Ohmic losses in front contacts, emitter, base, ribbons | Leakage current paths across the p–n junction | | **Affected region of I–V curve** | Near the **open-circuit (V_oc)** point — flattens the curve | Near the **short-circuit (I_sc)** point — tilts the curve | | **Effect on I_sc** | Negligible | Significantly reduces I_sc | | **Effect on V_oc** | Significantly reduces V_oc | Negligible | | **Effect on FF** | Decreases | Decreases | | **Typical cause** | Poor solder joints, high-resistivity emitter | Pinholes, edge shunts, manufacturing defects | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Provided:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Sample Set 2]** **(a)** Derive the I–V characteristic equation of a photovoltaic cell from the single-diode equivalent circuit model. Clearly state the significance of each term. (7 Marks) **(b)** A crystalline silicon PV module has the following specifications at STC: - Open-circuit voltage, V_oc = **37.5 V** - Short-circuit current, I_sc = **8.75 A** - Voltage at maximum power point, V_m = **30.5 V** - Current at maximum power point, I_m = **8.20 A** - Module area, A = **1.60 m²** Calculate: (i) Fill Factor, (ii) Maximum power output, (iii) Conversion efficiency, (iv) Comment on the quality of the cell. (7 Marks) --- #### 📝 Model Solution to Question A: **(a) Derivation of the I–V equation:** **Step 1 — Equivalent circuit components:** The single-diode model consists of a photocurrent source I_ph (driven by incident light), a diode D with ideality factor n, a series resistance R_s, and a shunt resistance R_sh, all connected as in §4.1. **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the internal node:** The total current generated by the source equals the sum of the diode current, the shunt current, and the load current: $$I_{ph} = I_D + I_{sh} + I$$ **Step 3 — Express the diode current using the Shockley Diode Equation:** $$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_D}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right]$$ Where $V_D$ is the voltage across the diode, related to the terminal voltage V by: $$V_D = V + I R_s$$ **Step 4 — Express the shunt current using Ohm's Law:** $$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute and rearrange to obtain the standard I–V equation:** $$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$ **Significance of each term:** - $I_{ph}$ — Light-generated current; proportional to irradiance. - $I_0 \exp(\cdots)$ — Diode forward current; represents recombination and diffusion losses. - $\dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ — Shunt leakage current; represents manufacturing defects. - $R_s$ — Series resistance loss; reduces FF near V_oc. - $R_{sh}$ — Shunt resistance loss; reduces FF near I_sc. **(b) Numerical Computation:** **(i) Fill Factor:** $$FF = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{30.5 \times 8.20}{37.5 \times 8.75} = \dfrac{250.10}{328.125} = 0.762$$ **(ii) Maximum Power Output:** $$P_{max} = V_m \times I_m = 30.5 \times 8.20 = 250.10 \text{ W}$$ **(iii) Conversion Efficiency:** $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \times A} = \dfrac{250.10}{1000 \times 1.60} = \dfrac{250.10}{1600} = 0.1563 = 15.63\%$$ **(iv) Quality comment:** A Fill Factor of **0.762** falls within the typical crystalline-silicon range (0.70 – 0.85), and an efficiency of **~15.6%** is acceptable for a standard multicrystalline silicon module (though slightly below modern monocrystalline benchmarks of 19–22%). The cell is of **good commercial quality** with low parasitic losses. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular]** **(a)** With the help of neat I–V and P–V characteristic curves, explain the following terms: (i) Short-circuit current, (ii) Open-circuit voltage, (iii) Maximum power point, (iv) Fill Factor. State how each is affected by a change in (i) irradiance and (ii) cell temperature. (7 Marks) **(b)** A 72-cell crystalline silicon PV module is rated at 320 W (STC) with V_oc = 45.5 V, I_sc = 9.20 A, V_m = 37.0 V, I_m = 8.65 A. If the irradiance drops to 600 W/m² and the cell temperature rises to 60 °C, estimate the new power output. Use the following temperature coefficients: α(I_sc) = +0.05 %/°C, β(V_oc) = −0.32 %/°C, and the power temperature coefficient γ = −0.40 %/°C. (7 Marks) --- #### 📝 Model Solution to Question B: **(a) Conceptual explanation:** **(i) Short-circuit current (I_sc):** The current delivered when the terminals are shorted (V = 0). It appears as the **Y-intercept** of the I–V curve. Numerically, it is approximately equal to the photo-generated current I_ph. **(ii) Open-circuit voltage (V_oc):** The terminal voltage when the load is disconnected (I = 0). It appears as the **X-intercept** of the I–V curve. It is governed by $V_{oc} = n V_T \ln(I_{ph}/I_0 + 1)$. **(iii) Maximum Power Point (MPP):** The single operating point on the I–V curve where the product V × I is maximised. It is the **apex of the P–V curve**. The coordinates are (V_m, I_m) and the power is P_max. **(iv) Fill Factor (FF):** The ratio of the MPP rectangle area to the bounding rectangle: $FF = P_{max}/(V_{oc} I_{sc})$. **Effects of irradiance change (↓G):** - $I_{sc}$ ↓ **linearly** with G (e.g., 600/1000 → I_sc drops to ~60% of STC value). - $V_{oc}$ ↓ **logarithmically** (small drop, e.g., ~5% for 40% G drop). - $P_{max}$ ↓ roughly in proportion to G. **Effects of cell temperature change (↑T):** - $I_{sc}$ ↑ *slightly* (≈ +0.05 %/°C). - $V_{oc}$ ↓ **significantly** (≈ −0.32 %/°C). - $P_{max}$ ↓ at approximately −0.40 %/°C (i.e., higher T = lower power). **(b) Numerical estimation under new conditions:** **Step 1 — Irradiance correction to I_sc:** $$I_{sc,new} = I_{sc,STC} \times \dfrac{G_{new}}{G_{STC}} = 9.20 \times \dfrac{600}{1000} = 5.52 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Temperature correction to V_oc:** Temperature rise: $\Delta T = 60 - 25 = 35$ °C $$V_{oc,new} = V_{oc,STC} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{\beta}{100} \times \Delta T \right] = 45.5 \left[ 1 + (-0.0032)(35) \right]$$ $$V_{oc,new} = 45.5 \times [1 - 0.112] = 45.5 \times 0.888 = 40.40 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Temperature correction to maximum power:** $$P_{max,new} = P_{max,STC} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{\gamma}{100} \times \Delta T \right] \times \dfrac{G_{new}}{G_{STC}}$$ $$P_{max,new} = 320 \times [1 - 0.004 \times 35] \times \dfrac{600}{1000}$$ $$P_{max,new} = 320 \times [1 - 0.14] \times 0.6 = 320 \times 0.86 \times 0.6$$ $$P_{max,new} = 320 \times 0.516 = 165.12 \text{ W}$$ **Result:** Under the new conditions (G = 600 W/m², T = 60 °C), the module delivers approximately **165 W**, which is **~51.6%** of its STC rating — a typical mid-day, hot-summer, partly-cloudy operating point. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PV Electrical Characteristics Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in the V_oc equation** — Always write $\ln(I_{ph}/I_0 + 1)$, not $\ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$. Even though numerically minor, KTU examiners deduct marks for incomplete expressions. > 2. **Unit confusion on η** — Efficiency is *always* expressed as a percentage. If you compute 0.1563, write **15.63%**. A bare decimal is marked wrong. > 3. **Confusing R_s and R_sh effects** — R_s affects the V_oc side; R_sh affects the I_sc side. Mixing these is the single most common conceptual error. > 4. **Forgetting to apply BOTH irradiance AND temperature corrections** in Part B numerics. They are *multiplicative* effects, not additive. > 5. **Mixing up V_T formula** — Use $V_T = kT/q$ with T in **Kelvin**, not Celsius. $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ The **single-diode model** is the universal analytical framework: $I = I_{ph} - I_0[\exp((V+IR_s)/nV_T) - 1] - (V+IR_s)/R_{sh}$. - ✅ Three critical points on the I–V curve: **I_sc** (V=0), **V_oc** (I=0), **MPP** (dP/dV = 0). - ✅ **Fill Factor (FF)** = ratio of actual MPP rectangle to bounding rectangle; range 0.50–0.85 depending on cell technology. - ✅ **Efficiency (η)** = $P_{max}/(G \cdot A)$; measured at STC (1000 W/m², 25 °C, AM1.5G). - ✅ **I_sc ∝ G** (linear); **V_oc ∝ ln(G)** (logarithmic). - ✅ **I_sc ↑ slightly** with T; **V_oc ↓ significantly** with T; net: **P_max ↓** with T. - ✅ **R_s** → flattens curve near V_oc; **R_sh** → tilts curve near I_sc. Both reduce FF. - ✅ **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K (T must be in Kelvin). - ✅ For numerical problems, always apply irradiance and temperature corrections **multiplicatively**. - ✅ Memorise the empirical FF₀ formula for ideal cells: $FF_0 = (v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72))/(v_{oc} + 1)$. - ✅ Standard module efficiency benchmarks: mono-Si (19–22%), poly-Si (15–18%), CdTe thin-film (16–19%), a-Si (6–9%).

topics.title: Double Diode Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Double Diode Model # ⚡ Double Diode Model of a Photovoltaic Cell ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Double Diode Model (DDM)** of a photovoltaic (PV) cell is an enhanced equivalent circuit representation that employs **two parallel-connected diodes** to accurately model the current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of a real solar cell. Unlike the single diode model which attributes all non-ideal behavior to a single junction, the double diode model segregates the dark current into two physically distinct mechanisms: - **Diode 1 (D₁)** → Models the **diffusion current** in the quasi-neutral regions (p-type and n-type bulk) of the p-n junction, with an ideality factor of **n₁ = 1**. - **Diode 2 (D₂)** → Models the **recombination current** in the space-charge (depletion) region of the junction, with an ideality factor of **n₂ = 2**. The model is mathematically expressed by the double-exponential current equation solved implicitly for terminal current. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The double diode model is essential for **high-precision modeling** of photovoltaic devices, particularly under **low-illumination conditions** and **low open-circuit voltages** where recombination losses dominate. It is the standard reference model cited in IEEE and IEC 60904 photovoltaic characterization standards. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Leak Bucket" Imagine a water tank (representing the PV cell) being filled by a pump (photogenerated current, **Iₚₕ**): - **Water exits through two separate holes** instead of one: - **Hole 1 (D₁)** is a smooth, well-engineered orifice → small, predictable leakage representing normal diffusion loss. - **Hole 2 (D₂)** is a rough, irregular crack in the tank's middle section → represents unwanted recombination loss occurring *within* the junction's active region itself. The **net useful water** (load current **I**) = Water pumped in − Leakage through Hole 1 − Leakage through Hole 2 − Side leakage (shunt loss). > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** The second diode becomes increasingly significant at **low forward bias voltages** (near the maximum power point region), where recombination in the depletion region is the dominant loss mechanism. This is precisely why the double diode model is superior to the single diode model in curve-fitting accuracy. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value | |--------|----------|----------------| | $q$ | Electronic charge | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** | | $k$ | Boltzmann constant | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | | $T$ | Absolute temperature | **300 K (room temperature)** | | $V_t$ | Thermal voltage = $kT/q$ | **≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Equivalent Circuit Topology The double diode model consists of the following components connected as follows: 1. **Current Source (Iₚₕ)** → Represents the **photogenerated (light-generated) current**, dependent on incident solar irradiance and cell area. 2. **Diode D₁ (Diffusion Diode)** → Placed in parallel with current source; forward bias opposes Iₚₕ. 3. **Diode D₂ (Recombination Diode)** → Placed in parallel with D₁; captures space-charge region recombination. 4. **Series Resistance (Rₛ)** → Represents ohmic losses in the bulk semiconductor, front/back metal contacts, and interconnects. 5. **Shunt Resistance (Rₛₕ)** → Represents the parallel leakage paths across the p-n junction due to manufacturing defects and edge effects. ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Current Flow Analysis **Step 1:** Photons with energy $E \geq E_g$ strike the semiconductor and generate electron-hole pairs. **Step 2:** The light-generated current $I_{ph}$ flows from the n-region to the p-region through the external load. **Step 3:** The total current $I$ delivered to the load is reduced by two internal diode currents ($I_{d1}$ and $I_{d2}$) and one shunt leakage current ($I_{sh}$). **Step 4:** The series resistance $R_s$ accounts for the voltage drop between the junction and the external terminals. **Step 5:** The terminal voltage $V$ is therefore less than the junction internal voltage $V_j$ due to ohmic loss: $V_j = V + I \cdot R_s$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description / Physical Meaning | Units | |---|--------------------|--------------------------------|-------| | 1 | $I = I_{ph} - I_{d1} - I_{d2} - I_{sh}$ | **Node equation** by KCL at top node | A | | 2 | $I_{d1} = I_{o1}\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n_1 V_t}\right) - 1\right]$ | **Diode 1 current** (diffusion loss) | A | | 3 | $I_{d2} = I_{o2}\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n_2 V_t}\right) - 1\right]$ | **Diode 2 current** (recombination loss) | A | | 4 | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$ | **Shunt (leakage) current** through Rₛₕ | A | | 5 | $V_t = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | **Thermal voltage** at temperature T | V | | 6 | $I_{ph} \approx I_{ph,STC} \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_{STC}}$ | **Photocurrent** scaling with irradiance G | A | | 7 | $n_1 = 1$ | Ideality factor for **diffusion** | — | | 8 | $n_2 = 2$ | Ideality factor for **recombination** | — | | 9 | $I_{sc} = I_{ph}$ (when $V = 0$) | Short-circuit current condition | A | | 10 | $V_{oc} = n_1 V_t \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_{o1}} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage (dominated by D₁) | V | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The double diode model is the **gold standard** for parameter extraction algorithms (e.g., Lambert W-function based methods, numerical iterative solvers) used in commercial PV simulation tools like **MATLAB/Simulink (Simscape Electrical)**, **PVsyst**, **SABER**, and **SPICE**. It enables accurate prediction of system performance under partial shading and varying irradiance conditions. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Double Diode Model Equation ### Starting Point — Single Diode Shockley Equation The ideal p-n junction diode current follows the **Shockley diode equation**: $$ I_d = I_o \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_j}{n V_t}\right) - 1\right] $$ where $V_j$ is the voltage across the junction depletion region. ### Step 1: Modifying for Internal Voltage Drop In a real solar cell, the actual junction voltage $V_j$ is greater than the externally measurable terminal voltage $V$ due to the voltage drop across the series resistance $R_s$ caused by the load current $I$: $$ V_j = V + I R_s $$ This is a **critical** substitution. Substituting into Shockley's equation: $$ I_{d1} = I_{o1}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n_1 V_t}\right) - 1\right] $$ ### Step 2: Introducing the Second Diode for Recombination The single diode equation assumes all recombination occurs in the neutral regions. However, significant recombination occurs **within the depletion region itself** (Sah-Noyce-Shockley theory, 1957). This mechanism has an ideality factor of $n_2 = 2$: $$ I_{d2} = I_{o2}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n_2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] $$ ### Step 3: Adding Shunt Leakage Path Real p-n junctions exhibit finite shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ due to manufacturing defects. Using Ohm's law, the shunt leakage current is: $$ I_{sh} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}} $$ ### Step 4: Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) At the top node of the equivalent circuit, **KCL** dictates that the photogenerated current splits into four branches: $$ I_{ph} = I + I_{d1} + I_{d2} + I_{sh} $$ Rearranging for the terminal current $I$: $$ \begin{aligned} I &= I_{ph} - I_{d1} - I_{d2} - I_{sh} \\[6pt] I &= I_{ph} - I_{o1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n_1 V_t}\right) - 1\right] \\[4pt] &\quad - I_{o2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n_2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] \\[4pt] &\quad - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **complete implicit double diode model equation** of a PV cell. ## 3.2 Analytical Boundary Conditions (Limiting Cases) ### Case A — Short Circuit Condition ($V = 0$) $$ \begin{aligned} I &= I_{ph} - I_{o1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{I R_s}{V_t}\right) - 1\right] - I_{o2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{I R_s}{2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{I R_s}{R_{sh}} \end{aligned} $$ Assuming $I_{sc} R_s \ll V_t$ and $R_s \ll R_{sh}$: $$ \boxed{I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}} $$ ### Case B — Open Circuit Condition ($I = 0$) $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= I_{ph} - I_{o1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{V_t}\right) - 1\right] - I_{o2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}} \end{aligned} $$ Solving for $V_{oc}$: $$ \boxed{V_{oc} \approx n_1 V_t \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph} + I_{o2} - V_{oc}/R_{sh}}{I_{o1}}\right)} $$ ### Case C — Ideal Cell Limit ($R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$) $$ \begin{aligned} I = I_{ph} - I_{o1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{V_t}\right) - 1\right] - I_{o2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Single Diode Model | Double Diode Model | |---------|-------------------|--------------------| | Number of diodes | 1 | 2 | | Ideality factors | 1 (fixed) | $n_1 = 1$, $n_2 = 2$ | | Parameters to extract | 5 | 7 | | Accuracy at low V | Moderate | **High** | | Recombination modeled | Only diffusion | Diffusion + depletion region | | Computational complexity | Low | **Higher** | | KTU exam relevance | Foundational | **Frequently asked (14-mark)** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Equivalent Circuit — Double Diode PV Cell Model ```mermaid graph TB nodeA["Light Source
Photon Flux G"] -.->|"Generates Iph"| nodeB["Current Source Iph
Photogenerated Current"] nodeB --- nodeC["Node A
Internal Junction"] nodeC --- nodeD["Diode D1
Diffusion Current
n1 = 1"] nodeC --- nodeE["Diode D2
Recombination Current
n2 = 2"] nodeC --- nodeF["Resistor Rsh
Shunt Resistance
Leakage Path"] nodeC -->|"Voltage V + I*Rs"| nodeG["Series Resistance Rs
Ohmic Contact Loss"] nodeG -->|"Terminal Voltage V
Load Current I"| nodeH["External Load
Terminal + -"] nodeH -->|"Return Path"| nodeI["Ground Node
Bottom Reference"] nodeI --- nodeD nodeI --- nodeE nodeI --- nodeF ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["Photon Input
G W/m2"] --> nodeB["Light Generation Stage
Iph = q.G.A.eta"] nodeB --> nodeC["Internal Junction Node
KCL Application Point"] nodeC --> nodeD["Branch 1
Diffusion Diode D1"] nodeC --> nodeE["Branch 2
Recombination Diode D2"] nodeC --> nodeF["Branch 3
Shunt Resistor Rsh"] nodeC --> nodeG["Branch 4
Series Resistor Rs"] nodeG --> nodeH["Terminal Output
Voltage V, Current I"] nodeD --> nodeI["Loss Summation
Id1 + Id2 + Ish"] nodeE --> nodeI nodeF --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ["Net Load Current
I = Iph - Sum Losses"] ``` ## 4.3 I–V Characteristic Curve Annotation ```mermaid graph TB A["Open Circuit
Voc, I = 0"] --> B["Maximum Power Point
Pm = Vm x Im"] B --> C["Knee Region
Diffusion Dominant"] C --> D["Short Circuit
Isc, V = 0"] D --> E["Linear Region
Rsh Dominant"] E --> A F["Diode D1 Controls
High Voltage Region"] -.-> A G["Diode D2 Controls
Low Voltage Region
Recombination"] -.-> C ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In the I–V curve, Diode D₁ dominates the **high-voltage (near V_oc)** region with its exponential current rise, while Diode D₂ dominates the **low-to-mid voltage (knee/Maximum Power Point)** region — exactly where recombination in the depletion region becomes the controlling loss mechanism. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP] **Distinguish between the single diode model and the double diode model of a solar cell. Why is the double diode model preferred for accurate simulation?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Aspect | Single Diode Model | Double Diode Model | |--------|-------------------|--------------------| | Diodes used | One | Two | | Loss mechanisms | Diffusion only | Diffusion + Recombination | | Ideality factor | Variable n (typically 1–1.5) | $n_1 = 1$ (fixed), $n_2 = 2$ (fixed) | | Accuracy | Moderate | **High**, especially at low illumination | **Reason for preference (1 mark):** The double diode model accounts for **space-charge region recombination losses** which dominate under low irradiance and at the maximum power point, providing superior curve fitting and more accurate prediction of PV system performance in real field conditions. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP] **Define the term "ideality factor" and state its significance in the context of the double diode model.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): The **ideality factor** ($n$) is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies how closely the diode behavior matches that of an ideal p-n junction diode as predicted by the Shockley equation. **Significance in double diode model (2 marks):** - For **Diode D₁** (diffusion-dominated current): $n_1 = 1$, indicating ideal diffusion. - For **Diode D₂** (recombination-dominated current): $n_2 = 2$, indicating non-ideal recombination in the depletion region. - The combination of these two specific ideality factors enables the model to capture **both physical loss mechanisms simultaneously**, providing accurate I–V characteristics across the full operating voltage range. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 2]** — *14 Marks* **(a)** Draw the equivalent circuit of the double diode model of a photovoltaic cell and label all components clearly. Derive the corresponding current-voltage (I–V) characteristic equation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A silicon solar cell has the following parameters at 300 K: $I_{ph} = 3.0 \text{ A}$, $I_{o1} = 1 \times 10^{-10} \text{ A}$, $I_{o2} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ A}$, $R_s = 0.01 \ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 300 \ \Omega$. Compute the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$. Given: $V_t = 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Equivalent Circuit and Derivation (7 marks):** **Circuit diagram (2 marks):** The equivalent circuit consists of: - A current source $I_{ph}$ (photogenerated current) - Two diodes D₁ and D₂ connected in **parallel** with opposite polarity to the current source - A shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ in parallel with the diodes - A series resistance $R_s$ in series with the output **Derivation (5 marks):** Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law** at the top node: $$ I_{ph} = I + I_{d1} + I_{d2} + I_{sh} $$ where: $$ I_{d1} = I_{o1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n_1 V_t}\right) - 1\right] $$ $$ I_{d2} = I_{o2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n_2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] $$ $$ I_{sh} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}} $$ Final equation: $$ \begin{aligned} I = I_{ph} - I_{o1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n_1 V_t}\right) - 1\right] - I_{o2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n_2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}} \end{aligned} $$ --- **(b) Numerical Computation of $V_{oc}$ (7 marks):** **Given:** $I_{ph} = 3.0$ A, $I_{o1} = 10^{-10}$ A, $I_{o2} = 10^{-7}$ A, $R_s = 0.01\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 300\ \Omega$, $V_t = 25.85$ mV. **Step 1:** Under open-circuit condition, $I = 0$: $$ 0 = I_{ph} - I_{o1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{V_t}\right) - 1\right] - I_{o2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}} $$ **Step 2:** Substituting $V_j = V_{oc} + 0 \cdot R_s = V_{oc}$: $$ 0 = 3.0 - 10^{-10}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{0.02585}\right) - 1\right] - 10^{-7}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{0.0517}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V_{oc}}{300} $$ **Step 3:** Initial estimate ignoring D₂ and shunt: $$ V_{oc} \approx V_t \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_{o1}}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{3.0}{10^{-10}}\right) $$ $$ V_{oc} \approx 0.02585 \times \ln(3 \times 10^{10}) $$ $$ V_{oc} \approx 0.02585 \times 24.12 \approx 0.6235 \text{ V} $$ **Step 4:** Including shunt correction: $\Delta V = \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}} \times \text{slope}^{-1}$. With $I_{ph} = 3$ A and $R_{sh} = 300\ \Omega$: $$ \text{Shunt drop} = \frac{0.6235}{300} \approx 0.00208 \text{ A (negligible compared to } I_{ph}) $$ **Step 5:** Refined estimate: $$ \boxed{V_{oc} \approx 0.62 \text{ V}} $$ --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 2 Alternative]** — *14 Marks* **(a)** Explain the physical significance of the two diodes in the double diode model of a solar cell. Discuss why one diode has ideality factor 1 and the other has ideality factor 2. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expressions for short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$) and open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) from the double diode model. State the key assumptions made. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Physical Significance of the Two Diodes (7 marks):** **Diode D₁ — Diffusion Diode (3.5 marks):** In a p-n junction under forward bias, minority carriers (electrons in p-region, holes in n-region) diffuse across the junction and recombine in the quasi-neutral bulk regions. This **diffusion current** follows the ideal Shockley equation with ideality factor $n_1 = 1$. The associated saturation current $I_{o1}$ is typically very small (10⁻⁸ to 10⁻¹² A for silicon) because it depends on the square of intrinsic carrier concentration. $$ I_{d1} = I_{o1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_j}{V_t}\right) - 1\right] $$ **Diode D₂ — Recombination Diode (3.5 marks):** Inside the space-charge (depletion) region, mobile charge carriers are swept away rapidly by the strong built-in electric field. However, electrons and holes can recombine via **trap-assisted (Shockley-Read-Hall) recombination** within this region. This recombination current has a different voltage dependence, characterized by ideality factor $n_2 = 2$ (proposed by **Sah, Noyce, and Shockley, 1957**): $$ I_{d2} = I_{o2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_j}{2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] $$ **Why $n_2 = 2$?** The factor of 2 in the exponent arises because the recombination rate in the depletion region depends linearly on the concentration of both electrons and holes, while the diffusion current depends only on the minority carrier concentration gradient — a fundamental result of semiconductor device physics. --- **(b) Derivations of $I_{sc}$ and $V_{oc}$ (7 marks):** **Short-Circuit Current $I_{sc}$ — Derivation (3.5 marks):** Under short-circuit condition, $V = 0$. The general equation reduces to: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{sc} &= I_{ph} - I_{o1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{V_t}\right) - 1\right] - I_{o2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}} \end{aligned} $$ **Key Assumption 1:** For a well-designed cell, $I_{sc} R_s \ll V_t$, so the exponential terms ≈ 0. **Key Assumption 2:** $R_s \ll R_{sh}$, so the last term is negligible. Therefore: $$ \boxed{I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}} $$ **Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$ — Derivation (3.5 marks):** Under open-circuit, $I = 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= I_{ph} - I_{o1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{V_t}\right) - 1\right] - I_{o2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{2 V_t}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}} \end{aligned} $$ **Key Assumption:** Since $\exp(V_{oc}/V_t) \gg 1$, the "−1" terms and the small shunt current are dropped. Also, $I_{o1} \ll I_{ph}$, and the D₂ exponential is much smaller than D₁ exponential at typical $V_{oc}$ values. Rearranging: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{oc} &= n_1 V_t \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph} - V_{oc}/R_{sh}}{I_{o1}} + 1\right) \end{aligned} $$ For ideal shunt ($R_{sh} \to \infty$): $$ \boxed{V_{oc} \approx V_t \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_{o1}}\right)} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $n_1$ factor in the formula sheet** — Students often write $V_{oc} = V_t \ln(I_{ph}/I_{o1})$ without explicitly mentioning that $n_1 = 1$ has been substituted. Always state the **general form first**, then substitute. > 2. **Sign convention errors** — In the equivalent circuit, the diode currents **subtract** from $I_{ph}$. Forgetting the negative sign yields an incorrect (larger than $I_{ph}$) terminal current. **Always derive via KCL carefully.** > 3. **Mixing up ideality factors** — Writing $n_2 = 1$ for diffusion or $n_1 = 2$ for recombination leads to full mark deduction in conceptual questions. > 4. **Missing the "$−1$" in Shockley equation** — The "−1" arises from the reverse saturation current contribution. Dropping it loses 1 mark in derivations. > 5. **Forgetting the implicit nature** — The double diode equation is **implicit in I** (I appears on both sides). Mentioning this is required for full marks on derivations. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ The **Double Diode Model** uses two parallel diodes: **D₁ (diffusion, $n_1 = 1$)** and **D₂ (recombination, $n_2 = 2$)**. - ✅ The **master equation** is: $I = I_{ph} - I_{d1} - I_{d2} - I_{sh}$ — derived using **KCL**. - ✅ Diode D₂ dominates at **low forward bias / near MPP** — this is why DDM is more accurate than SDM. - ✅ Thermal voltage $V_t = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at **T = 300 K** — memorize this value. - ✅ Under short-circuit: $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$ (when $R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$). - ✅ Under open-circuit: $V_{oc} \approx V_t \ln(I_{ph}/I_{o1})$ — only D₁ controls $V_{oc}$. - ✅ Seven parameters in DDM: $I_{ph}, I_{o1}, I_{o2}, R_s, R_{sh}, n_1, n_2$ (last two fixed). - ✅ Real-world application: PV system simulation tools (MATLAB/Simulink, PVsyst, SPICE). - ✅ Originating theory: **Sah-Noyce-Shockley (1957)** — frequently cited in KTU answer keys. - ✅ Always state **physical reasoning** alongside formulas in KTU exams for full marks.

topics.title: Single Diode Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Single Diode Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Single Diode Model (SDM)**, also known as the *one-diode equivalent circuit model*, is the most widely used lumped-parameter equivalent circuit representation of a photovoltaic (PV) cell. It mathematically describes the non-linear current–voltage (I–V) characteristics of a real solar cell by idealizing the cell as a current source (representing the photo-generated current) in parallel with a diode (representing the p–n junction) and two parasitic resistances — a **series resistance (Rs)** and a **shunt resistance (Rsh)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Single Diode Model):**
> The single diode model is the foundational analytical tool used for performance prediction, I–V curve fitting, and Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) algorithm design in all PV systems.

### 1.2 Constitutive Equation (Implicit Form)

The generalized implicit current equation governing the single diode model is:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

where every term has a precise physical meaning discussed in Section 2.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water pump system** connected to a pipe network:

| PV Component | Water Analogy | Function |
|--------------|---------------|----------|
| **Current Source (Iph)** | A mechanical pump pushing water | Represents light-generated charge carriers |
| **Diode (D)** | A one-way check valve | Allows current flow in only one direction (junction behavior) |
| **Series Resistance (Rs)** | Pipe friction along the main line | Represents resistive losses in contacts & bulk semiconductor |
| **Shunt Resistance (Rsh)** | A leak hole in the pipe wall | Represents manufacturing defects causing leakage current |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a "single" diode?** Because in this simplified model, all recombination losses (in the bulk, depletion region, and surfaces) are lumped into a *single* diode with an effective ideality factor $n$. More advanced models (e.g., the two-diode model) separate these recombination pathways.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Boltzmann Constant**: $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- **Electronic Charge**: $q = 1.602176634 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Thermal Voltage**: $V_T = k_B T / q \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at $T = 300\ \text{K}$
- **Standard Test Conditions (STC)**: Irradiance $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, Temperature $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$ ($25^\circ\text{C}$), Air Mass 1.5G spectrum.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Equivalent Circuit Architecture

The single diode model represents the PV cell as a **five-parameter equivalent circuit**:

1. **Photogenerated Current Source ($I_{ph}$)** — A dependent current source proportional to incident solar irradiance $G$ and cell area $A$.
2. **Diode (D)** — Models the rectifying behavior of the p–n junction under dark conditions.
3. **Series Resistance ($R_s$)** — Models ohmic losses in the front metallic grid, emitter, base, and back contact.
4. **Shunt Resistance ($R_{sh}$)** — Models leakage currents across the p–n junction due to manufacturing defects.
5. **Ideality Factor ($n$)** — A dimensionless quality factor (typically $1 \leq n \leq 2$) representing how closely the diode behavior matches an ideal diode ($n=1$).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Why a current source?** Photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ generate electron–hole pairs; the rate of generation is governed by the incident photon flux $\Rightarrow$ acts as a current source.
- **Why a diode?** The semiconductor junction intrinsically behaves as a diode — it conducts current readily under forward bias but blocks reverse current, mimicking Shockley's diode equation.
- **Why $R_s$ in series?** Real semiconductors and metal contacts have finite resistivity; this opposes useful current flow $\Rightarrow$ placed in series with the load.
- **Why $R_{sh}$ in parallel?** Crystal defects and edge shunts create alternate paths for current to bypass the junction $\Rightarrow$ placed in parallel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ideal Single Diode Model vs Real Model:**
> - **Ideal**: $R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$, $n = 1$
> - **Real (Practical)**: $R_s > 0$, $R_{sh}$ finite (large), $1 < n < 2$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = k_B T / q$ | V | $\approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at $25^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 2 | Photogenerated Current | $I_{ph} = \left[ I_{sc,STC} + K_i (T - T_{STC}) \right] \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_{STC}}$ | A | $K_i$ = temp. coeff. of $I_{sc}$ |
| 3 | Diode Saturation Current | $I_0 = I_{0,STC} \left(\dfrac{T}{T_{STC}}\right)^3 \exp\!\left[\dfrac{q E_g}{n k_B}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{STC}} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$ | A | Strongly temperature-dependent |
| 4 | Single Diode I–V Equation | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$ | A | **Implicit** in $I$ (transcendental) |
| 5 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc} \approx n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | V | Set $I = 0$, neglect $R_s$, $R_{sh}$ |
| 6 | Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$ | A | Set $V = 0$ (approx. for $R_s \ll$, $R_{sh} \gg$) |
| 7 | Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | dimensionless | Typically $0.6 - 0.85$ |
| 8 | Cell Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{P_{in}}$ | dimensionless (or %) | $P_{in} = G \cdot A$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The single diode model is the workhorse used by manufacturers in datasheets, by MPPT charge controllers for real-time tracking, by PV system simulators (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink, PSpice), and by grid-tied inverter firmware for fault diagnosis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Single Diode I–V Equation (Kirchhoff's Current Law)

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the top node of the equivalent circuit.**

The photo-generated current $I_{ph}$ entering the node splits into three paths: (i) through the diode $I_D$, (ii) through the shunt resistance $I_{sh}$, and (iii) through the external load (terminal current $I$).

$$
I_{ph} = I_D + I_{sh} + I
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Shockley Diode Equation.**

The current through the diode depends on the voltage across its terminals. The diode voltage equals the terminal voltage $V$ plus the voltage drop across the series resistance $I R_s$ (because $R_s$ is in series with the load path).

$$
I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law across the Shunt Resistance.**

The voltage across $R_{sh}$ is also $V + I R_s$ (since both $R_{sh}$ and the (diode+load) branch share the same pair of nodes).

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the KCL equation.**

$$
I_{ph} = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] + \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}} + I
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the canonical form of the single diode model.**

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation** of the single diode model. It is *implicit* in $I$ because $I$ appears on both sides of the equation inside the exponential and the shunt term.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$)

**Step 1 — Apply the open-circuit condition:** At open circuit, the terminal current $I = 0$ (no external load).

**Step 2 — Substitute $I = 0$ into the governing equation:**

$$
0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 3 — For a high-quality cell where $R_{sh}$ is very large, neglect the term $V_{oc}/R_{sh}$:**

$$
I_{ph} \approx I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Since $I_{ph} \gg I_0$, the "$-1$" term is negligible:**

$$
I_{ph} \approx I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{oc}$ by taking natural logarithm on both sides:**

$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) = \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}
$$

$$
\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T} = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{oc} \approx n V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Short-Circuit Current ($I_{sc}$)

**Step 1 — Apply the short-circuit condition:** Terminal voltage $V = 0$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $V = 0$ into the governing equation:**

$$
I_{sc} = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 3 — For $R_s \ll$ and $R_{sh} \gg$, both correction terms vanish:**

$$
I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}\,}
$$

This is why the datasheet value of $I_{sc}$ is commonly used as a direct estimator of the photogenerated current in parameter-extraction algorithms.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Maximum Power Point (MPP) Condition

**Step 1 — The output power is** $P = V \cdot I$.

**Step 2 — At the maximum power point, $dP/dV = 0$:**

$$
\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the single diode equation with respect to $V$** (treat $I$ as a function of $V$):

$$
\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{\dfrac{I_0}{n V_T}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) + \dfrac{1}{R_{sh}}}{1 + \dfrac{I_0 R_s}{n V_T}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) + \dfrac{R_s}{R_{sh}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Imposing $dP/dV = 0$ at $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ yields the implicit transcendental MPP condition:**

$$
I_{mp} = \frac{V_{mp} \left[ \dfrac{I_0}{n V_T}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{mp} + I_{mp} R_s}{n V_T}\right) + \dfrac{1}{R_{sh}} \right]}{1 + \dfrac{I_0 R_s}{n V_T}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{mp} + I_{mp} R_s}{n V_T}\right) + \dfrac{R_s}{R_{sh}}}
$$

This is solved **numerically** (Newton–Raphson or Lambert W function) in engineering practice, since an analytical closed-form solution does not exist.

---

### 3.5 Temperature & Irradiance Dependence (Linearized Approximations)

**For $I_{ph}$:**

$$
I_{ph}(G, T) = \left[ I_{sc,STC} + K_i (T - T_{STC}) \right] \cdot \frac{G}{G_{STC}}
$$

**For $V_{oc}$:**

$$
V_{oc}(G, T) = V_{oc,STC} + K_v (T - T_{STC}) + n V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{G}{G_{STC}}\right)
$$

**For $I_0$:**

$$
I_0(T) = I_{0,STC} \left(\frac{T}{T_{STC}}\right)^3 \exp\!\left[\frac{q E_g}{n k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_{STC}} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Practical Insight:** $I_{sc}$ increases *weakly* with temperature (positive $K_i$), but $V_{oc}$ *decreases* with temperature (negative $K_v$). Therefore, **PV cell efficiency drops by roughly $0.4 - 0.5\%$ per °C rise in cell temperature** — a critical design consideration for tropical Kerala climates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single Diode Equivalent Circuit (Functional Block Architecture)

Because physical circuit schematics with electronic symbols are difficult to render cleanly in Mermaid, the diagram below maps the **functional signal-flow architecture** of the single diode model — tracing how photogenerated current splits among the diode branch, the shunt branch, and the external load branch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeLight[Incident Photon Flux G]:::input
    nodeSrc[Photocurrent Source Iph]:::src
    nodeKCL{KCL Node Top}:::junction
    nodeDiode[Diode Branch ID]:::branch
    nodeShunt[Shunt Branch Ish]:::branch
    nodeLoad[Load Branch I Out]:::branch
    nodeRs[Series Resistance Rs]:::loss
    nodeRsh[Shunt Resistance Rsh]:::loss
    nodeV[V T Across Cell]:::output
    nodeMPPT[MPPT Tracker]:::sink

    nodeLight --> nodeSrc
    nodeSrc --> nodeKCL
    nodeKCL --> nodeDiode
    nodeKCL --> nodeShunt
    nodeKCL --> nodeLoad
    nodeDiode -. Ohmic drop I Rs .-> nodeRs
    nodeShunt -. Parallel loss .-> nodeRsh
    nodeLoad --> nodeV
    nodeV --> nodeMPPT

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef junction fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#F08080,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000
    classDef sink fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#191970,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow (Five-Parameter Extraction Pipeline)

The diagram below maps how the **five unknown parameters** of the single diode model are extracted from the experimentally measured I–V curve (datasheet-based analytical method).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMeas[Measured I-V Curve]:::input
    nodeIsc[Identify Isc]:::step
    nodeVoc[Identify Voc]:::step
    nodeImp[Identify Imp Vmp]:::step
    nodeRs0[Compute Rs from dV at Voc]:::calc
    nodeRsh0[Compute Rsh from dI at Isc]:::calc
    nodeFF[Compute Fill Factor FF]:::calc
    nodeI0[Iterate n and I0]:::calc
    nodeFinal[Five Parameter Set]:::output

    nodeMeas --> nodeIsc
    nodeMeas --> nodeVoc
    nodeMeas --> nodeImp
    nodeIsc --> nodeRsh0
    nodeVoc --> nodeRs0
    nodeImp --> nodeFF
    nodeFF --> nodeI0
    nodeRsh0 --> nodeI0
    nodeRs0 --> nodeI0
    nodeI0 --> nodeFinal

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping of Components

| Block Diagram Node | Physical Realization | Governing Law |
|--------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Photocurrent Source Iph | Photogeneration of e-h pairs by sunlight | Photocurrent model |
| Diode Branch ID | p-n junction dark current | Shockley equation |
| Shunt Branch Ish | Manufacturing defect leakage | Ohm's law |
| Series Resistance Rs | Metal contacts + bulk resistance | Ohm's law |
| KCL Top Node | Charge conservation at junction | Kirchhoff's current law |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the single diode model of a solar cell. List its five parameters.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **single diode model** is an equivalent circuit representation of a photovoltaic cell that idealizes the cell as a constant current source (the photogenerated current) in anti-parallel with a diode, with a series resistance $R_s$ and a shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

**The five parameters are:**
1. **$I_{ph}$** — Photogenerated current (A)
2. **$I_0$** — Diode reverse saturation current (A)
3. **$n$** — Diode ideality factor (dimensionless)
4. **$R_s$** — Series resistance ($\Omega$)
5. **$R_{sh}$** — Shunt resistance ($\Omega$)

**Mark Distribution (Valuator Pattern):**
- Definition (1 mark) + Listing 5 parameters (2 marks, 0.4 per correct parameter).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the single diode model considered "implicit" in current $I$?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The governing equation of the single diode model is:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

The terminal current $I$ appears on **both sides of the equation** — explicitly on the left-hand side and *inside* both the exponential term and the shunt-ohmic term on the right-hand side. Because $I$ is enclosed inside a transcendental function (the exponential), the equation **cannot be algebraically solved for $I$ in closed form**. Numerical iterative methods (Newton–Raphson, fixed-point iteration, or the Lambert W function) are required to obtain $I$ for a given $V$. This transcendental coupling makes the equation *implicit* in $I$.

**Mark Distribution (Valuator Pattern):**
- I on both sides — 1 mark
- Exponential (transcendental) nature — 1 mark
- Numerical methods needed — 1 mark

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the I–V characteristic equation of a solar cell using the single diode equivalent circuit. State all the assumptions made in the derivation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A crystalline silicon PV cell has the following measured parameters at STC: $I_{ph} = 3.2\ \text{A}$, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}$, $n = 1.30$, $R_s = 0.02\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250\ \Omega$, $T = 300\ \text{K}$. Calculate the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ and short-circuit current $I_{sc}$ of the cell. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:** [1 Mark]
1. The diode follows the Shockley ideal diode equation (no generation/recombination in depletion region separately modeled).
2. $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ are lumped, constant, and temperature-independent.
3. $I_{ph}$ depends only on irradiance and temperature (no spectral dependence).
4. The "$-1$" term in the Shockley equation is retained (no approximation made in the full form).

**Derivation using KCL at the top node:** [4 Marks]

Step 1: The current from the photo-source splits into the diode, the shunt resistance, and the external load:

$$
I_{ph} = I_D + I_{sh} + I
$$

Step 2: The voltage across the diode is $V + I R_s$ (terminal voltage plus ohmic drop across $R_s$):

$$
I_D = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right]
$$

Step 3: The current through $R_{sh}$ is given by Ohm's law:

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

Step 4: Substituting and rearranging:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

This is the **single diode I–V equation**. [2 Marks for correctly stating the final canonical form]

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Given:** $I_{ph} = 3.2\ \text{A}$, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}$, $n = 1.30$, $R_s = 0.02\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250\ \Omega$, $T = 300\ \text{K}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the thermal voltage:** [1 Mark]

$$
V_T = \frac{k_B T}{q} = \frac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.586 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{V} \approx 25.86\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_{oc}$ (open-circuit condition $I = 0$):** [3 Marks]

$$
V_{oc} \approx n V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} = \frac{3.2}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.133 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\ln(2.133 \times 10^{9}) = \ln(2.133) + 9 \ln(10) = 0.758 + 20.723 = 21.481
$$

$$
n V_T = (1.30)(0.02586) = 0.03362\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{oc} \approx (0.03362)(21.481) = 0.722\ \text{V}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $I_{sc}$ (short-circuit condition $V = 0$):** [3 Marks]

Substituting $V = 0$ in the governing equation:

$$
I_{sc} = I_{ph} - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

For small $R_s = 0.02\ \Omega$, the exponential correction is negligible:
- $\dfrac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T} \approx \dfrac{(3.2)(0.02)}{(1.30)(0.02586)} \approx 1.90$ — this is non-trivial, so we iterate.

**Iteration 1:** Assume $I_{sc}^{(0)} = 3.20\ \text{A}$.

$$
I_{sc}^{(1)} = 3.20 - 1.5\times 10^{-9}\left[e^{1.90} - 1\right] - \frac{(3.20)(0.02)}{250}
$$

$$
e^{1.90} \approx 6.686, \quad 1.5\times 10^{-9} \cdot 5.686 = 8.53 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A (negligible)}
$$

$$
I_{sc}^{(1)} = 3.20 - 8.53\times 10^{-9} - 2.56 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.1997\ \text{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{sc} \approx 3.20\ \text{A}}
$$

The diode and shunt corrections are negligibly small relative to $I_{ph}$, confirming $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a labeled equivalent circuit, explain the physical significance of each of the five parameters in the single diode model of a PV cell. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What is the fill factor (FF) of a solar cell? A solar cell has $V_{oc} = 0.65\ \text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 3.5\ \text{A}$, $V_{mp} = 0.52\ \text{V}$, and $I_{mp} = 3.10\ \text{A}$. Compute the fill factor, maximum power, and conversion efficiency if the cell area is $A = 150\ \text{cm}^2$ and the incident irradiance is $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — Physical Significance [7 Marks]

**Equivalent Circuit Description:** [1 Mark]

The single diode model equivalent circuit consists of a photocurrent source $I_{ph}$ in parallel with a diode $D$, with a series resistance $R_s$ in the main current path and a shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ in parallel with the diode.

**Five Parameters and Their Physical Significance:** [6 Marks, 1.2 each]

1. **$I_{ph}$ (Photogenerated Current):** Represents the rate of electron–hole pair generation by incident photons of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$. It is directly proportional to the incident solar irradiance $G$ and the cell area $A$. Symbolically, $I_{ph} \propto G \cdot A$.

2. **$I_0$ (Reverse Saturation Current):** Represents the small reverse-bias leakage current that flows through the diode under dark conditions. It originates from thermally generated minority carriers in the quasi-neutral regions. $I_0$ is strongly temperature-dependent and typically of order $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-12}\ \text{A}$ for crystalline silicon.

3. **$n$ (Ideality Factor):** A dimensionless quality factor that quantifies how closely the real diode approaches ideal Shockley behavior. $n = 1$ corresponds to pure diffusion current (ideal), while $n = 2$ corresponds to pure recombination in the depletion region. For real PV cells, $1 < n < 2$.

4. **$R_s$ (Series Resistance):** Represents the cumulative ohmic losses due to (i) front metallic grid contact resistance, (ii) emitter and base bulk resistances, (iii) back-contact resistance, and (iv) interconnection tab resistance. Higher $R_s$ degrades the fill factor and reduces maximum power output.

5. **$R_{sh}$ (Shunt Resistance):** Represents the loss of photocurrent through alternate conduction paths created by manufacturing defects — such as crystal dislocations, edge shunts, and pinholes in the emitter. A low $R_{sh}$ sharply reduces $V_{oc}$ and the fill factor, especially at low irradiance.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Fill Factor and Efficiency [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition of Fill Factor:** [1 Mark]

The fill factor (FF) is the ratio of the maximum obtainable power to the product of the open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current:

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}
$$

It is a measure of the "squareness" of the I–V curve.

**Step 2 — Numerical Calculation of FF:** [2 Marks]

$$
FF = \frac{(0.52\ \text{V})(3.10\ \text{A})}{(0.65\ \text{V})(3.5\ \text{A})} = \frac{1.612}{2.275} = 0.7086
$$

$$
\boxed{FF \approx 0.709 \text{ (or } 70.9\% \text{)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Power:** [2 Marks]

$$
P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = (0.52)(3.10) = 1.612\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{max} = 1.612\ \text{W}}
$$

**Step 4 — Conversion Efficiency:** [2 Marks]

The input optical power is $P_{in} = G \cdot A$.

First, convert area: $A = 150\ \text{cm}^2 = 150 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 = 0.015\ \text{m}^2$.

$$
P_{in} = (1000\ \text{W/m}^2)(0.015\ \text{m}^2) = 15\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{1.612}{15} = 0.1075
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \approx 10.75\%}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversions:** Many students lose 1–2 marks by failing to convert cell area from $\text{cm}^2$ to $\text{m}^2$ in efficiency calculations. Always do the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Implicit Equation Handling:** Do NOT attempt to algebraically solve the single diode equation for $I$. State clearly that the equation is implicit and that numerical iteration (Newton–Raphson) is used. Examiners award marks for explicitly recognizing the implicit nature.
> 3. **Assumption Statements:** When deriving $V_{oc}$, always state the assumption $R_{sh} \to \infty$ *before* simplifying the equation. Omitting this step costs at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Sign Convention:** Ensure that the direction of $I_{ph}$, $I_D$, $I_{sh}$, and terminal $I$ is correctly indicated in the KCL diagram; wrong sign convention leads to a wrong final equation and complete loss of derivation marks.
> 5. **Ideality Factor Range:** Always state the valid range of $n$ ($1 \leq n \leq 2$) when defining the ideality factor. Writing just "$n$ is a constant" loses 0.5 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] The **single diode model** has **five parameters**: $I_{ph}, I_0, n, R_s, R_{sh}$.
- [ ] The governing equation is **implicit in $I$** because $I$ appears inside the exponential term.
- [ ] The **thermal voltage** $V_T = k_B T / q \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at $25^\circ\text{C}$.
- [ ] **$I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$** (short-circuit current ≈ photogenerated current).
- [ ] **$V_{oc} \approx n V_T \ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$** (logarithmic dependence on the ratio of currents).
- [ ] **Fill Factor** $FF = V_{mp} I_{mp} / (V_{oc} I_{sc})$ quantifies I–V curve "squareness".
- [ ] **Efficiency** $\eta = P_{max} / P_{in} = V_{oc} I_{sc} FF / (G \cdot A)$.
- [ ] **Series resistance $R_s$** primarily affects the slope of the I–V curve near $V_{oc}$.
- [ ] **Shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** primarily affects the slope of the I–V curve near $I_{sc}$.
- [ ] **Ideality factor** $n = 1$ is ideal; $n = 2$ indicates dominant depletion-region recombination.
- [ ] **Temperature effect:** $V_{oc}$ decreases with temperature (~ $-2\ \text{mV/°C per cell}$); $I_{sc}$ increases marginally.
- [ ] **Irradiance effect:** $I_{sc}$ scales linearly with $G$; $V_{oc}$ scales logarithmically with $G$.
- [ ] The equation **cannot be solved algebraically** — Newton–Raphson iteration or Lambert W function is used.
- [ ] For **ideal cell** assumption: $R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$, $n = 1$.
- [ ] The single diode model is the **foundation** of all PV system simulators and MPPT algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Model Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of photovoltaic (PV) technology, **Model Parameters** refer to the set of physical and electrical quantities that mathematically describe the operation of a solar cell using an equivalent circuit. The standard approach is the **Single-Diode Model (SDM)** or the more accurate **Two-Diode Model (TDM)**. These models replicate the current–voltage (I–V) characteristic of a real photovoltaic device by combining an ideal current source, one or two diodes, and two parasitic resistances.

The five fundamental model parameters of the **Single-Diode Model** are:

| Symbol | Parameter |
|---|---|
| $I_L$ | Photo-generated current (light current) |
| $I_0$ | Diode reverse saturation current |
| $n$ | Diode ideality factor |
| $R_s$ | Series resistance |
| $R_{sh}$ | Shunt (parallel) resistance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Focus: Module 2 emphasizes the **single-diode five-parameter model** as the canonical descriptor of a PV cell. Mastery of these five parameters is mandatory because they appear in every Part B question on device characterization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a water tap connected to a tank:

- The **current source ($I_L$)** is the water pressure pushing water out — this is the useful sunlight-driven current.
- The **diode** is a small leak in the tank — current "leaks" backward across the p–n junction. $I_0$ is the leak rate; $n$ describes *how non-ideal* the leak is (a perfect diode has $n = 1$, a leaky real one has $n > 1$).
- The **series resistance ($R_s$)** is the narrow pipe leading to the tap — it chokes the current, especially when you want maximum flow.
- The **shunt resistance ($R_{sh}$)** is a crack in the tank wall — current is diverted away from the output, reducing useful power.

A good solar cell = high $I_L$, low $I_0$, $n \approx 1$, low $R_s$, high $R_{sh}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants You Must Memorize:**
> - Boltzmann constant: $k = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - Elementary charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
> - Thermal voltage at 300 K: $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85 \; mV}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit

The five-parameter equivalent circuit models a real solar cell as:

- A **light-generated current source** $I_L$ proportional to incident photon flux.
- A **p–n junction diode** (representing diffusion + recombination in the depletion region).
- A **series resistance** $R_s$ (contact resistance, bulk resistance, metal-semiconductor interface losses).
- A **shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ (leakage paths, manufacturing defects, edge shunts).

Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the output node gives the iconic implicit I–V equation of a PV cell.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Implicit I–V characteristic (SDM) | — |
| 2 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage | $\approx 25.85$ mV @ 300 K |
| 3 | $I_{sc} = I \big\vert_{V=0} \approx I_L$ | Short-circuit current | — |
| 4 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{n V_T}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) \approx n V_T \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage | — |
| 5 | $FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{V_{mpp} \cdot I_{mpp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor | 0.7 – 0.85 |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Power conversion efficiency | — |
| 7 | $I_{mpp} \approx I_L \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{V_{mpp}/(nV_T)}\right]$ | Current at maximum power point | — |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dP}{dV}\bigg\vert_{V=V_{mpp}} = 0$ | Condition for maximum power | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Board Standard):**
> - Photocurrent $I_L$ flows **outward** from the cell (delivered to load).
> - Load current $I$ is drawn **out** of the positive terminal.
> - Voltage $V$ is positive across the terminals under illumination.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Model parameters are extracted experimentally from measured I–V curves and used for:

1. **Performance prediction** of PV modules under varying irradiance and temperature.
2. **Loss diagnostics** — quantifying how much $R_s$ degrades fill factor vs. how much $R_{sh}$ degrades open-circuit voltage.
3. **Mismatch analysis** in series/parallel arrays.
4. **MPPT algorithm tuning** in inverter design.
5. **Quality control** in solar cell manufacturing lines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Implicit Single-Diode I–V Equation

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit branches.**

The output node has three parallel branches: the current source $I_L$, the diode $D$, and the shunt resistor $R_{sh}$. The series resistor $R_s$ lies between the output node and the external terminal.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the internal node (after $R_s$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current through diode: } I_D &= I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_D}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] \\[4pt]
\text{Current through } R_{sh} &: I_{sh} = \dfrac{V_D}{R_{sh}} \\[4pt]
\text{Terminal voltage: } V &= V_D - I R_s \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_D = V + I R_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate currents.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= I_D + I_{sh} + I \\[4pt]
I_L &= I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] + \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}} + I
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for the load current $I$.**

$$
\boxed{\,I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}\,}
$$

This is the canonical KTU board equation. It is **implicit** in $I$ (appears on both sides) and must be solved numerically (Newton–Raphson) for arbitrary operating points.

## 3.2 Derivation of Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$

**Step 1 — Boundary condition:** At open circuit, $I = 0$ and $V = V_{oc}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the simplification $R_{sh} \to \infty$.**

For an ideal cell the shunt leakage is negligible, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] \\[4pt]
I_L &= I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the exponential.**

$$
\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) = \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1
$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm.**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{oc} = n V_T \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) \;\approx\; n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The approximation $V_{oc} \approx n V_T \ln(I_L/I_0)$ holds because for a good cell $I_L / I_0 \gg 1$ (typically $10^6$ to $10^{10}$). The $+1$ inside the logarithm is mathematically necessary but numerically invisible — examiners, however, will accept either form.

## 3.3 Derivation of Short-Circuit Current $I_{sc}$

**Step 1 — Boundary condition:** At short circuit, $V = 0$ and $I = I_{sc}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $V = 0$ into the governing equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc} &= I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the engineering assumption $R_s \to 0$.**

For a well-made cell the term $I_{sc} R_s \ll n V_T$, so the diode term vanishes:

$$
\boxed{\,I_{sc} \approx I_L\,}
$$

This is why $I_{sc}$ is linearly proportional to irradiance and is the easiest parameter to measure directly.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Fill Factor (FF) — Green’s Empirical Approximation

**Step 1 — Define normalized voltage.**

$$
v_{oc} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Green’s empirical expression.**

$$
\boxed{\,FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}\,}
$$

This is the fill factor of an *ideal* cell (no parasitic resistances).

**Step 3 — Correct for parasitic resistances.**

$$
FF = FF_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{R_s I_{sc}}{V_{oc}}\right) \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh} I_{sc}}\right)
$$

The first bracketed term penalizes $R_s$, the second penalizes low $R_{sh}$.

## 3.5 Derivation of Maximum Power Point Condition

**Step 1 — Define output power.**

$$
P = V \cdot I = V \left\{ I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}} \right\}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the derivative to zero.**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dV}\bigg\vert_{V=V_{mpp}} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — After differentiation (assuming $R_{sh} \to \infty$ for simplicity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= I + V \dfrac{dI}{dV} \\[4pt]
\dfrac{dI}{dV} &= -\dfrac{I_0}{n V_T} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting back and solving for $V_{mpp}$ requires Lambert W function:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{mpp} \approx V_{oc} - n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{mpp} + I_{mpp} R_s}{n V_T} + 1\right)\,}
$$

In KTU exams, the iterative Lambert W form is rarely demanded — students should be able to **state the condition $dP/dV = 0$** and **numerically locate the MPP** from a given I–V curve.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on $I_{sc}$ | Effect on $V_{oc}$ | Effect on FF | Effect on $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $I_L \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (logarithmically) | slight $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
| $I_0 \uparrow$ | (negligible) | $\downarrow$ (logarithmically) | (negligible) | $\downarrow$ |
| $n \uparrow$ | (negligible) | $\uparrow$ (linearly) | $\downarrow$ | trade-off |
| $R_s \uparrow$ | (negligible) | (negligible) | $\downarrow\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ |
| $R_{sh} \uparrow$ | (negligible) | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Diode Model — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IL["Current Source IL\n(Photogenerated)"]:::source
    RS["Series Resistor Rs\n(Contact + Bulk Loss)"]:::loss
    D["Diode D\n(p-n Junction)"]:::diode
    RSH["Shunt Resistor Rsh\n(Leakage Path)"]:::loss
    OUT["Output Terminal\nV, I to Load"]:::out

    IL -->|Internal Node A| RS
    D -->|Parallel Branch| RS
    RSH -->|Parallel Branch| RS
    RS --> OUT

    classDef source fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#F4B084,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef diode fill:#A9D08E,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#9DC3E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the schematic:**

- The **current source** $I_L$ sits at the heart of the model, mimicking sunlight → current conversion.
- The **diode branch** represents the p–n junction's rectification behavior.
- The **shunt resistor** $R_{sh}$ runs in parallel with the diode, modeling real-world leakage.
- The **series resistor** $R_s$ sits between this parallel combination and the external terminals.

## 4.2 Two-Diode Model — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IL2["Current Source IL"]:::source
    D1["Diode D1\nDiffusion Current\nIdeality Factor n1"]:::diode
    D2["Diode D2\nRecombination Current\nIdeality Factor n2"]:::diode
    RSH2["Shunt Resistor Rsh"]:::loss
    RS2["Series Resistor Rs"]:::loss
    OUT2["Output Terminal V, I"]:::out

    IL2 --> RS2
    D1 -->|Parallel to internal node| RS2
    D2 -->|Parallel to internal node| RS2
    RSH2 -->|Parallel to internal node| RS2
    RS2 --> OUT2

    classDef source fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#F4B084,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef diode fill:#A9D08E,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#9DC3E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the schematic:**

- $D_1$ models the **diffusion current** in the quasi-neutral regions ($n_1 = 1$).
- $D_2$ models **recombination current** in the depletion region ($n_2 = 2$).
