- The two-diode model has **seven parameters**: $I_L$, $I_{01}$, $n_1$, $I_{02}$, $n_2$, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$.

## 4.3 I–V Curve — Parameter Annotation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "I-V Curve Under Illumination"
        P1["Point A: Short Circuit\nV = 0, I = Isc ≈ IL"]:::pt
        P2["Point B: MPP\nVmpp, Impp, Pmax = Vmpp × Impp"]:::pt
        P3["Point C: Open Circuit\nV = Voc, I = 0"]:::pt
    end

    P1 -.->|"Slope = -1/Rsh\nat low V"| P2
    P2 -.->|"Slope = -1/Rs\nat high V"| P3

    classDef pt fill:#C9C9FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the schematic:**

- The **slope of the I–V curve near $I_{sc}$** is governed by $1/R_{sh}$ — a steep slope means low shunt resistance.
- The **slope near $V_{oc}$** is governed by $1/R_s$ — a gentle slope near $V_{oc}$ means high series resistance.
- The **knee of the curve** is the maximum power point $(V_{mpp}, I_{mpp})$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the five model parameters of the single-diode equivalent circuit of a solar cell. State the typical range of the ideality factor.**

**Model Answer:**

The five model parameters of the single-diode equivalent circuit are:

1. **Photo-generated current** ($I_L$)
2. **Diode reverse saturation current** ($I_0$)
3. **Ideality factor** ($n$)
4. **Series resistance** ($R_s$)
5. **Shunt resistance** ($R_{sh}$)

The ideality factor typically lies between **1 and 2**. For an ideal diode $n = 1$; for a real diode with recombination losses $n$ approaches 2. Most practical silicon solar cells exhibit $n \in [1.2, 1.5]$ under operating conditions.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Derive the expression for open-circuit voltage of an illuminated solar cell under the single-diode model.**

**Model Answer:**

At open circuit, $I = 0$ and $V = V_{oc}$. Assuming $R_{sh} \to \infty$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] \\[6pt]
\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) &= \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \\[6pt]
V_{oc} &= n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) \;\approx\; n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the implicit current–voltage equation of a photovoltaic cell under the single-diode five-parameter model. Clearly state the assumption used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A silicon solar cell at 300 K has the following measured parameters under 1-sun illumination: $I_L = 3.20$ A, $I_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ A, $n = 1.30$, $R_s = 0.005 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 1000 \, \Omega$. Calculate:
- (i) Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$
- (ii) Short-circuit current $I_{sc}$
- (iii) Approximate fill factor $FF$ using Green's formula (assume $V_{mpp} \approx 0.85 \, V_{oc}$)
- (iv) Power conversion efficiency for $P_{in} = 1000$ W/m² on a $156 \times 156$ mm² cell (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. Apply KCL at the internal node with the diode current

$$I_D = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_D}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right]$$

and shunt current $I_{sh} = V_D / R_{sh}$ where $V_D = V + I R_s$. Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}\,}$$

**Assumption:** Single-junction device, recombination dominated by a single mechanism characterized by a constant ideality factor, ohmic contacts.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Thermal voltage:

$$
V_T = \dfrac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585 \text{ V}
$$

**(i) Open-circuit voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oc} &= n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) \\
&= (1.30)(0.02585) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{3.20}{1.0 \times 10^{-9}} + 1\right) \\
&= (0.03361) \ln(3.20 \times 10^{9}) \\
&= (0.03361)(21.886) \\
&= 0.7357 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Short-circuit current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc} &= I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $I_0$ is tiny and $R_s I_{sc} / n V_T \ll 1$:

$$
I_{sc} \approx I_L = 3.20 \text{ A}
$$

**(iii) Fill Factor (Green's formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{oc} &= \dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T} = \dfrac{0.7357}{0.03361} = 21.89 \\[4pt]
FF_0 &= \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{21.89 - \ln(22.61)}{22.89} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{21.89 - 3.119}{22.89} = \dfrac{18.771}{22.89} = 0.8201
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying parasitic correction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FF &= FF_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{R_s I_{sc}}{V_{oc}}\right)\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh} I_{sc}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 0.8201 \left(1 - \dfrac{(0.005)(3.20)}{0.7357}\right)\!\left(1 - \dfrac{0.7357}{(1000)(3.20)}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 0.8201 \times (1 - 0.02175) \times (1 - 2.299 \times 10^{-4}) \\[4pt]
&= 0.8201 \times 0.9783 \times 0.9998 = 0.8022
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Power conversion efficiency:**

Maximum power:

$$
P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = (0.7357)(3.20)(0.8022) = 1.888 \text{ W}
$$

Cell area:

$$
A = (0.156)(0.156) = 0.024336 \text{ m}^2
$$

Incident power:

$$
P_{in} = (1000)(0.024336) = 24.336 \text{ W}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{1.888}{24.336} = 0.0776 = 7.76\%
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice 2024]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the single-diode equivalent circuit of a solar cell. Identify each branch physically. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the physical origin and effect of:
- (i) Series resistance $R_s$
- (ii) Shunt resistance $R_{sh}$
- (iii) Ideality factor $n$

Explain how each parameter is extracted from the experimental I–V curve. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Equivalent Circuit:**

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.

**Branches and their physical meaning:**

| Branch | Physical Origin |
|---|---|
| Current source $I_L$ | Photogenerated carriers from absorbed photons |
| Diode $D$ | p–n junction rectification under forward bias |
| $R_s$ | Bulk semiconductor resistance + metal contact + grid finger resistance |
| $R_{sh}$ | Manufacturing defects, edge leakage, shunt paths across the junction |

**(b) Physical origin, effect, and extraction:**

**(i) Series Resistance $R_s$:**

- **Origin:** Bulk resistance of the semiconductor, contact resistance between metal and silicon, resistance of the front grid fingers and busbars.
- **Effect:** Causes a voltage drop $I R_s$ that subtracts from the terminal voltage; reduces the fill factor; particularly damaging near $V_{oc}$.
- **Extraction:** From the slope of the I–V curve near $V_{oc}$:

$$\dfrac{dV}{dI}\bigg\vert_{V \approx V_{oc}} = -R_s$$

**(ii) Shunt Resistance $R_{sh}$:**

- **Origin:** Leakage current paths across the p–n junction due to crystal defects, impurities, and edge shunts.
- **Effect:** Diverts current away from the load, especially near $I_{sc}$; reduces both fill factor and open-circuit voltage marginally.
- **Extraction:** From the slope of the I–V curve near $I_{sc}$:

$$\dfrac{dI}{dV}\bigg\vert_{I \approx I_{sc}} = -\dfrac{1}{R_{sh}}$$

**(iii) Ideality Factor $n$:**

- **Origin:** Indicates the dominant recombination mechanism. $n = 1$ implies pure band-to-band diffusion; $n = 2$ implies Shockley-Read-Hall recombination in the depletion region.
- **Effect:** A higher $n$ reduces the slope of the semi-log $I$–$V$ plot, lowering $V_{oc}$ sensitivity and degrading FF.
- **Extraction:** From the slope of the semi-logarithmic dark current plot:

$$n = \dfrac{q}{kT} \dfrac{dV}{d(\ln I)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in PV Model Parameter Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the thermal voltage value** — examiners allocate 1 full mark for writing $V_T = kT/q = 25.85$ mV at 300 K. Do not write $V_T = 0.026$ V without justification.
> 2. **Dropping the "+1" inside the logarithm** — strictly write $\ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$ for full marks. The approximation $\ln(I_L/I_0)$ is accepted but you lose 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $I_{sc}$ with $I_L$** — they are *approximately* equal but not identical. The exact $I_{sc}$ depends on $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$.
> 4. **Using wrong sign convention** — KTU expects current $I$ flowing *out* of the positive terminal (power delivery convention). Drawing the diode backwards costs marks.
> 5. **Skipping the KCL derivation step** — for "derive the I–V equation" questions, simply stating the final equation without showing KCL at the internal node loses 3 of the 7 marks.
> 6. **Confusing fill factor with efficiency** — FF is dimensionless; $\eta$ has units of %. Examiners check units first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ The **single-diode model** has **5 parameters**: $I_L$, $I_0$, $n$, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$.
- ☐ The governing I–V equation is **implicit** in $I$ — both sides contain $I$.
- ☐ $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K — **memorize this**.
- ☐ $V_{oc} = n V_T \ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$ — logarithmic dependence on $I_L$.
- ☐ $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ — linear dependence on irradiance.
- ☐ **Series resistance** primarily degrades the **fill factor**.
- ☐ **Shunt resistance** primarily degrades **$I_{sc}$** and slightly $V_{oc}$.
- ☐ **Ideality factor** $n \in [1, 2]$ — indicates recombination mechanism.
- ☐ $FF = P_{max}/(V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$ — dimensionless, typically 0.7–0.85.
- ☐ $\eta = P_{max}/P_{in}$ — depends on FF, $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, and incident power.
- ☐ The **maximum power point** satisfies $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$.
- ☐ Slope near $V_{oc}$ reveals $R_s$; slope near $I_{sc}$ reveals $R_{sh}$.
- ☐ Semi-log slope reveals $n$.
- ☐ The **two-diode model** adds $I_{02}$ and $n_2$ to capture depletion-region recombination.
- ☐ Always state **units** in numerical answers (V, A, W, %, etc.).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circuit Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Circuit Parameters" in a Photovoltaic Cell?

In the context of **Polymers & Photovoltaic Technology (PEPOT757)**, the term **Circuit Parameters** refers to the set of electrical quantities that fully describe the operational behaviour of a **solar cell / photovoltaic (PV) device** when represented as an **equivalent electrical circuit**. A real photovoltaic device — whether it is a crystalline silicon cell, a thin-film cell, or a polymer-based bulk heterojunction (BHJ) solar cell — is non-ideal. Hence, it is modelled using a current source in parallel with a diode, plus two parasitic resistances. The five parameters that govern the entire current–voltage (I–V) characteristic are called the **Circuit Parameters of a Solar Cell**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *Circuit parameters of a solar cell are the five quantities — photocurrent (I_L), diode saturation current (I_0), ideality factor (n), series resistance (R_s) and shunt resistance (R_sh) — that govern the shape of the illuminated I–V curve and determine the open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current, fill factor, maximum power and conversion efficiency of the device.*

The standard **Single-Diode Model (SDM)** of a PV cell is therefore defined by the quintuple:

$$P = \{I_L,\; I_0,\; n,\; R_s,\; R_{sh}\}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Leaking Bucket with a Straw"

Imagine you are filling a **bucket** with water from a tap. The water flowing in represents the **photocurrent I_L** generated by sunlight. The bucket has:

- A **small hole at the bottom (diode + I_0)** that leaks a small amount of water, where the leak rate depends on the "diode quality" (**ideality factor n**).
- A **narrow straw inserted into the bucket (series resistance R_s)** that resists the outflow of water — this represents resistive losses in the semiconductor bulk, contacts, and interconnects.
- A **crack in the side of the bucket (shunt resistance R_sh)** that bypasses the main outlet — this represents leakage currents across p-n junctions or along cell edges.

The "useful water delivered" is the **net current at a given terminal voltage**, and the "pressure" is the **terminal voltage**. Just as a well-engineered bucket minimises leaks and maximises useful flow, an efficient solar cell minimises R_s and maximises R_sh while producing the highest possible I_L and lowest possible I_0.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Metrics Used in Circuit Parameter Analysis:**
> - **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.380 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
> - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV
> - **Standard test conditions (STC):** irradiance $G = 1000$ W/m², spectrum AM 1.5G, cell temperature $T = 25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$
> - **Diode quality (ideality) factor range:** $1 \le n \le 2$ (ideal diode → 1, recombination-dominated → 2)
> - **Typical crystalline Si cell R_s:** $\mathbf{0.1\ \Omega}$ to $\mathbf{1\ \Omega}$
> - **Typical R_sh:** $\mathbf{100\ \Omega}$ to $\mathbf{1000\ \Omega}$

These five parameters, when measured precisely, allow complete reconstruction of the I–V curve, prediction of power output, and diagnosis of fabrication defects — making them indispensable in both research characterisation and industrial quality control of PV modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit — Logical Breakdown

A real illuminated solar cell is modelled as a **current source** (the photogenerated current) connected in **parallel** with a **p–n junction diode** and a **shunt resistance (R_sh)**, with the combination in series with a **series resistance (R_s)**. The output terminals deliver the **load current I** at the **terminal voltage V**.

The current and voltage conventions are:
- **I_L** = light-generated current (proportional to incident photon flux)
- **I_D** = current through the diode (non-linear, exponential in V)
- **I_sh** = current through the shunt branch (ohmic, V / R_sh)
- **I** = net terminal current delivered to the external load
- **V** = terminal voltage across the output
- **V_D** = internal voltage across the diode = V + I·R_s

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Photogeneration:** Photons with energy $h\nu \ge E_g$ generate electron–hole pairs in the absorber. Each pair contributes one electron to the external circuit, giving the **photocurrent I_L**.
- **Step 2 — Diode Action:** The p–n junction behaves as a diode in the dark. Under forward bias, a recombination current **I_D** flows opposite to I_L; this is the **dark current** of the cell.
- **Step 3 — Shunt Leakage:** Manufacturing defects (edge shunting, pinholes, impurity diffusion) create a parallel resistive path **R_sh**, producing a small ohmic leakage current **I_sh** proportional to the diode voltage.
- **Step 4 — Series Losses:** The finite conductivity of the emitter, base, metal grid, and contact interface introduces a lumped **R_s**, which drops a fraction of the generated voltage and converts it into Joule heat.
- **Step 5 — KCL at the Node:** Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law at the top node, the net current delivered to the external load is **I = I_L − I_D − I_sh**.

## 2.2 The Governing I–V Equation (Implicit Form)

The **single-diode model equation** that mathematically links the five circuit parameters to the measurable I–V curve is:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This implicit (transcendental) equation **cannot be solved in closed form** for I. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can identify it as a **transcendental equation** that requires **Newton–Raphson iteration** or **Lambert-W function** techniques for analytical solution.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Governing Expression | Boundary Condition / Limit |
|---|----------|--------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Thermal voltage | $V_T$ | $V_T = kT/q$ | $\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K |
| 2 | Single-Diode I–V | $I(V)$ | $I = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V+IR_s}{nV_T}\right)-1\right] - \dfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Transcendental in $I$ |
| 3 | Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc}$ | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ (when $R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$) | $V = 0$ |
| 4 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc}$ | $V_{oc} \approx n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | $I = 0$ |
| 5 | Maximum Power Point Voltage | $V_{mp}$ | $V_{mp} \approx V_{oc} - n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{mp}}{n V_T} + 1\right)$ | $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$ |
| 6 | Maximum Power Point Current | $I_{mp}$ | $I_{mp} = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{mp}+I_{mp}R_s}{nV_T}\right)-1\right] - \dfrac{V_{mp}+I_{mp}R_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Same |
| 7 | Maximum Power | $P_{max}$ | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Single value on curve |
| 8 | Fill Factor | $FF$ | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | $0 < FF < 1$ |
| 9 | Empirical FF (Green) | $FF_0$ | $FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$ | $v_{oc} = V_{oc}/(nV_T)$ |
| 10 | Conversion Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}$ | STC: $G = 1000$ W/m² |
| 11 | Temperature coeff. of $I_{sc}$ | $\alpha$ | $\Delta I_{sc}/I_{sc} = \alpha \Delta T$ | $\alpha \approx +0.05\%/\mathrm{K}$ (c-Si) |
| 12 | Temperature coeff. of $V_{oc}$ | $\beta$ | $\Delta V_{oc}/V_{oc} = \beta \Delta T$ | $\beta \approx -0.35\%/\mathrm{K}$ (c-Si) |
| 13 | Photocurrent vs irradiance | $I_L(G)$ | $I_L(G) = I_{L,ref} \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_{ref}}$ | Linear dependence |
| 14 | Two-diode model (extended) | $I$ | $I = I_L - I_{01}\!\left[e^{(V+IR_s)/(n_1V_T)}-1\right] - I_{02}\!\left[e^{(V+IR_s)/(n_2V_T)}-1\right] - \dfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | $n_1 = 1$, $n_2 = 2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX in Tables:** Notice that absolute-value bars and conditionals use the `\vert` or `\ln(...)` form — never raw `\vert` inside a table cell, as KTU's online portal can mis-render them.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Circuit Parameter Analysis

The extraction of the five parameters is **not a classroom exercise** — it is the **backbone of PV industry diagnostics**:

- **Module sorting & binning:** Manufacturers measure R_s and R_sh on every cell to grade modules into current-matched strings. Mismatch in I_sc causes hot-spot heating.
- **Indoor vs outdoor field performance:** Once I_L, I_0, n, R_s and R_sh are known at STC, the **five-parameter model** allows translation to any (G, T) condition using the translation equations (1st-order linear in G, exponential in T).
- **Polymer & BHJ cell research:** For organic PV, R_s is often high (10–100 Ω·cm²) and R_sh low (1–10 kΩ·cm²), so circuit parameters reveal whether degradation is interfacial (R_s ↑) or bulk-recombination (n ↑) related.
- **Inverter MPPT algorithm design:** Maximum Power Point Trackers use the FF and V_mp/I_mp as feedback to tune perturb-and-observe step sizes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc)

**Starting point — the single-diode equation, evaluated at open circuit:**

At **open circuit**, the terminal current is $I = 0$ and the terminal voltage equals the open-circuit voltage, $V = V_{oc}$. Substitute $I = 0$ into the governing equation:

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc} + 0 \cdot R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V_{oc} + 0 \cdot R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 1 — Simplify the exponent and the shunt term:**

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 2 — Assume an ideal cell where $R_{sh} \to \infty$, so $V_{oc}/R_{sh} \to 0$:**

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:**

$$I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] = I_L$$

**Step 4 — Note that for a well-designed cell, $I_L / I_0 \gg 1$, so the "$-1$" inside the brackets can be neglected:**

$$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) \approx \dfrac{I_L}{I_0}$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{oc}$:**

$$\boxed{\,V_{oc} = n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $V_{oc}$ increases **logarithmically** with light intensity (because $I_L \propto G$) and decreases linearly with the ideality factor and saturation current. A cell with a higher $V_{oc}$ is one with low recombination ($I_0$ small) and good junction quality ($n$ close to 1).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Power Point Condition

The maximum power delivered to the load is $P = V \cdot I$. The maximum power point is the unique pair $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ at which:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$$

**Step 1 — Apply the product rule with $I = I(V)$:**

$$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = \dfrac{d(VI)}{dV} = I + V \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the slope at MPP:**

$$\dfrac{dI}{dV}\bigg|_{mp} = -\dfrac{I_{mp}}{V_{mp}}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the single-diode equation (with the "$-1$" term dropped and $R_{sh} \to \infty$ for clarity):**

From the simpler relation $I \approx I_L - I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right)$, we differentiate:

$$\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1 + R_s \dfrac{dI}{dV}}{nV_T}$$

**Step 4 — Collect terms involving $dI/dV$ on the left:**

$$\dfrac{dI}{dV} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{I_0 R_s}{nV_T} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) \right] = -\dfrac{I_0}{nV_T} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back the diode current identity $I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V+IR_s}{nV_T}\right) = I_L - I$:**

$$\dfrac{dI}{dV} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{R_s(I_L - I)}{nV_T} \right] = -\dfrac{I_L - I}{nV_T}$$

**Step 6 — Equate this to $-I/V$ at MPP and cross-multiply:**

$$I_{mp} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{R_s(I_L - I_{mp})}{nV_T} \right] = \dfrac{V_{mp}(I_L - I_{mp})}{nV_T}$$

**Step 7 — Recognise that $I_L - I_{mp} = I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{mp} + I_{mp}R_s}{nV_T}\right)$:**

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{V_{mp}}{nV_T} + 1 = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{mp} + I_{mp}R_s}{nV_T}\right) \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{I_{mp} R_s}{nV_T} \right)^{-1}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This transcendental condition is **never solved in closed form** in textbooks. The standard approach is **Newton–Raphson iteration** on the I–V equation, or use of the empirical **Green's empirical FF expression** (see Section 2.3, row 9) for fast engineering estimates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Fill Factor from V_oc and V_mp

The **Fill Factor** is defined as the ratio of the actual maximum rectangle (drawn on the I–V curve) to the ideal rectangle (V_oc × I_sc). By definition:

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$$

**Step 1 — Assume ideal cell: $R_s \to 0$ and $R_{sh} \to \infty$.** Then $I_{sc} = I_L$ and the diode equation simplifies.

**Step 2 — From the implicit MPP condition, take logarithms of both sides of the approximate relation $V_{mp} \approx V_{oc} - nV_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{mp}}{nV_T} + 1\right)$ (derived by integrating the dP/dV condition).**

**Step 3 — Introduce the normalised open-circuit voltage $v_{oc} = V_{oc}/(nV_T)$:**

$$V_{oc} = nV_T \, v_{oc}$$

**Step 4 — Green's empirical closed-form approximation (very high yield for KTU):**

$$\boxed{\,FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Correct for finite $R_s$ and finite $R_{sh}$ using the empirical correction factors:**

$$FF = FF_0 (1 - r_s)(1 - 1/r_{sh})$$

where the normalised resistances are:

$$r_s = \dfrac{R_s}{V_{oc}/I_{sc}} = \dfrac{R_s \, I_{sc}}{V_{oc}}, \qquad \dfrac{1}{r_{sh}} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh} \, I_{sc}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the form most frequently tested in KTU Module-2 numerical problems. Students are expected to compute $FF_0$ from $v_{oc}$ and then apply the $r_s$ and $r_{sh}$ correction factors.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter on Cell Performance

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect on $I_{sc}$ | Effect on $V_{oc}$ | Effect on $FF$ | Typical Cause of Variation |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|----------------------------|
| Photocurrent | $I_L$ | $\uparrow$ linearly | $\uparrow$ weakly (log) | small | Cell area, spectral response, optical losses |
| Saturation current | $I_0$ | none | $\downarrow$ strongly ($\ln$ of $I_0$ in denominator) | indirect | Recombination, material purity |
| Ideality factor | $n$ | none | $\uparrow$ linearly | $\uparrow$ | Junction quality, SRH recombination |
| Series resistance | $R_s$ | small | small | $\downarrow$ strongly | Contact resistance, bulk resistivity, grid design |
| Shunt resistance | $R_{sh}$ | small | small | $\downarrow$ strongly (if low) | Edge shunts, pinholes, manufacturing defects |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "R_s rounds the corner, R_sh tilts the floor":**
> - $R_s$ primarily affects the **"knee"** of the I–V curve near V_mp, rounding the corner and reducing FF.
> - $R_{sh}$ primarily affects the **horizontal "floor"** of the curve near I_sc, causing the I–V characteristic to slope downward instead of being flat.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Board Pattern)

**Given:** A silicon solar cell at 300 K has $I_L = 3.0$ A, $I_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ A, $n = 1.2$, $R_s = 0.01\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 100\ \Omega$. Compute $V_{oc}$ and compare with the case $I_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-12}$ A.

**Step 1 — Thermal voltage at 300 K:**

$$V_T = \dfrac{(1.380 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585\ \mathrm{V}$$

**Step 2 — $V_{oc}$ for $I_0 = 10^{-9}$ A:**

$$V_{oc} = (1.2)(0.02585) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{3.0}{1.0 \times 10^{-9}}\right) = 0.03102 \cdot \ln(3 \times 10^{9})$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.03102 \cdot 21.82 = 0.677\ \mathrm{V}$$

**Step 3 — $V_{oc}$ for $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ A:**

$$V_{oc} = 0.03102 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{3.0}{10^{-12}}\right) = 0.03102 \cdot \ln(3 \times 10^{12})$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.03102 \cdot 28.92 = 0.897\ \mathrm{V}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$\Delta V_{oc} = 0.897 - 0.677 = 0.220\ \mathrm{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 1000-fold decrease in $I_0$ (better material purity, less recombination) yields only a **~220 mV gain in V_oc** — confirming the logarithmic dependence. This is the canonical "logarithmic sensitivity" insight the KTU examiner loves to test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Light["Sunlight Photon Flux"]:::in --> PVsrc["Current Source I_L Photogenerated Current"]
    PVsrc --- nodeA(("Node A"))
    nodeA --- Dblock["Diode Branch I_D Exponential Non-linear"]
    nodeA --- RshBlock["Shunt Branch I_sh Ohmnic V divided by R_sh"]
    nodeA --- RsBlock["Series Resistance R_s"]
    RsBlock --- OutTerm(("Output Terminal"))
    Dblock --- nodeB(("Internal Node B"))
    RshBlock --- nodeB
    nodeB --- RsBlock
    OutTerm --- Load["External Load R_L"]
    Load --- Ground((Ground))
    classDef in fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#8a6d00,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:**
> - Photogenerated current $I_L$ flows out of the **current source** into node A.
> - From node A, three paths exist: (i) the **diode** (recombination path), (ii) the **shunt resistance** (leakage path), and (iii) the **series resistance** (delivers current to the load).
> - The KCL balance at node A: $I_L = I_D + I_{sh} + I$.

---

## 4.2 I–V Characteristic Curve — Annotated Power Quadrants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Axes["I V Plane Fourth Quadrant I downward V rightward"] --> P1["Quadrant 1 Short Circuit I equals I_sc at V equals 0"]
    Axes --> P2["Quadrant 2 MPP Rectangle bounded by V_mp and I_mp"]
    Axes --> P3["Quadrant 3 Open Circuit V equals V_oc at I equals 0"]
    P1 --> EffLoss1["Loss 1 Loss of area from I_sc to I_mp vertical"]
    P2 --> EffLoss2["Loss 2 R_s rounding at the knee near V_mp"]
    P2 --> EffLoss3["Loss 3 R_sh floor slope near I_sc"]
    P3 --> EffLoss4["Loss 4 Loss of area from V_mp to V_oc horizontal"]
    P2 --> FinalFF["Fill Factor FF equals P_max divided by V_oc times I_sc"]
    classDef in fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#3c763d,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f2dede,stroke:#a94442,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3 in
    class EffLoss1,EffLoss2,EffLoss3,EffLoss4 out
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **fill factor** is the ratio of the area of the **inner shaded rectangle** (V_mp × I_mp) to the area of the **outer rectangle** (V_oc × I_sc). The four "losses" shown correspond to the four sources of inefficiency that the five circuit parameters quantify.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Parameter Extraction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Dev["PV Cell Under Test"]:::dev --> Meas["I V Curve Measurement at STC"]:::meas
    Meas --> T1["Read V_oc and I_sc at intercepts"]:::step
    Meas --> T2["Compute dV over dI near V_oc equals 1 over R_sh"]:::step
    Meas --> T3["Compute dV over dI near I_sc equals R_s"]:::step
    T1 --> T4["Estimate I_L equals I_sc and V_T equals 25.85 mV"]:::step
    T2 --> T5["Solve two point transcendental system for n and I_0"]:::step
    T3 --> T5
    T4 --> T5
    T5 --> Out["Extracted P equals I_L I_0 n R_s R_sh"]:::result
    classDef dev fill:#d9edf7,stroke:#31708f,color:#000
    classDef meas fill:#fcf8e3,stroke:#8a6d00,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#555,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#3c763d,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Parameter Extraction Procedure (commonly tested in KTU Module 2):**
> 1. From the **intercept on the V-axis** of the I–V curve → $V_{oc}$.
> 2. From the **intercept on the I-axis** → $I_{sc} \approx I_L$.
> 3. From the **inverse slope near $V_{oc}$** → $R_{sh} = (dV/dI)_{V \approx V_{oc}}$.
> 4. From the **inverse slope near $I_{sc}$** → $R_s = -(dV/dI)_{I \approx I_{sc}}$.
> 5. From a third point on the curve → solve numerically for $n$ and $I_0$.

---

## 4.4 Influence Map of Circuit Parameters on Performance Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Parameter I_L Photocurrent"] --> Out1["Direct boost of I_sc"]
    P1 --> Out2["Logarithmic boost of V_oc"]
    P2["Parameter I_0 Saturation Current"] --> Out3["Strong suppression of V_oc"]
    P2 --> Out4["Recombination signature in n"]
    P3["Parameter n Ideality Factor"] --> Out5["Linear influence on V_oc"]
    P3 --> Out6["Diagnostic of junction quality"]
    P4["Parameter R_s Series Resistance"] --> Out7["Rounding of knee and FF loss"]
    P4 --> Out8["Heating and power dissipation"]
    P5["Parameter R_sh Shunt Resistance"] --> Out9["Slope of floor near I_sc"]
    P5 --> Out10["Diagnostic of manufacturing defects"]
    Out1 --> Final["Final Performance P_max FF eta"]
    Out2 --> Final
    Out3 --> Final
    Out5 --> Final
    Out7 --> Final
    Out9 --> Final
    classDef param fill:#d9edf7,stroke:#31708f,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f2dede,stroke:#a94442,color:#000
    classDef fin fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#3c763d,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 param
    class Out1,Out2,Out3,Out4,Out5,Out6,Out7,Out8,Out9,Out10 out
    class Final fin
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading this map:** The five circuit parameters are the **root causes**; the measurable performance metrics ($I_{sc}$, $V_{oc}$, $FF$, $P_{max}$, $\eta$) are the **downstream effects**. Reverse-engineering the parameters from the measured I–V curve is the central skill KTU Module 2 demands.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define the five circuit parameters of a photovoltaic solar cell and state the governing single-diode model equation.

**Model Answer:**

The five circuit parameters are: (i) **photogenerated current $I_L$**, (ii) **diode saturation current $I_0$**, (iii) **ideality factor $n$**, (iv) **series resistance $R_s$**, and (v) **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$**.

The single-diode model equation is:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where $V_T = kT/q$ is the thermal voltage (~25.85 mV at 300 K).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners award **1 mark** for naming each parameter correctly and **2 marks** for writing the equation with proper notation.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q:** Distinguish between the effect of series resistance $R_s$ and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ on the I–V characteristic of a solar cell.

**Model Answer:**

- **$R_s$** causes a **rounding of the "knee"** of the I–V curve in the vicinity of the maximum power point. It reduces $V_{oc}$ slightly, leaves $I_{sc}$ nearly unchanged, and most strongly **lowers the fill factor**. It originates from bulk semiconductor resistance, metal grid resistance, and contact resistance.
- **$R_{sh}$** causes a **tilting of the "floor"** of the I–V curve near the short-circuit current region. It leaves $V_{oc}$ nearly unchanged, reduces $I_{sc}$ slightly, and again **lowers the fill factor**. It originates from manufacturing defects like edge shunts, pinholes, and impurity diffusion along grain boundaries.

> [!TIP]
> A high-quality cell demands **$R_s \to 0$** and **$R_{sh} \to \infty$** simultaneously.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer either **Question A OR Question B** (internal choice from the same module).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ of an illuminated solar cell starting from the single-diode model. State the assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** The following parameters are measured for a silicon solar cell at 300 K: $I_L = 2.5$ A, $I_0 = 10^{-10}$ A, $n = 1.3$, $R_s = 0.05\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 200\ \Omega$. Compute (i) $V_{oc}$, (ii) normalised voltage $v_{oc}$, (iii) Green’s empirical fill factor $FF_0$, and (iv) the maximum theoretical efficiency if the cell area is $100$ cm² under AM1.5G illumination. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. Start from the single-diode equation:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

2. At open circuit, $I = 0$ and $V = V_{oc}$:

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}$$

3. **Assumption 1:** $R_{sh} \to \infty$ (ideal shunt), so $V_{oc}/R_{sh} \to 0$:

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right]$$

4. **Assumption 2:** $I_L \gg I_0$, so the "$-1$" term is negligible compared to the exponential:

$$I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) \approx I_L$$

5. Take the natural logarithm:

$$\dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

6. Solve for $V_{oc}$:

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Compute $V_T$:**

$$V_T = \dfrac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585\ \mathrm{V}$$

**(ii) Compute $V_{oc}$:**

$$V_{oc} = (1.3)(0.02585) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2.5}{10^{-10}}\right) = 0.03361 \cdot \ln(2.5 \times 10^{10})$$

$$\ln(2.5 \times 10^{10}) = \ln(2.5) + 10 \ln(10) = 0.9163 + 23.0259 = 23.942$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.03361 \times 23.942 = 0.8046\ \mathrm{V} \approx 0.805\ \mathrm{V}$$

**(iii) Normalised voltage $v_{oc}$:**

$$v_{oc} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T} = \dfrac{0.805}{1.3 \times 0.02585} = \dfrac{0.805}{0.03361} = 23.94$$

**(iv) Green’s empirical $FF_0$:**

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1} = \dfrac{23.94 - \ln(24.66)}{24.94}$$

$$\ln(24.66) = 3.205$$

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{23.94 - 3.205}{24.94} = \dfrac{20.735}{24.94} = 0.8314$$

**(v) Corrected $FF$ with $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$:**

$$r_s = \dfrac{R_s I_{sc}}{V_{oc}} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 2.5}{0.805} = 0.1553$$

$$r_{sh} = \dfrac{R_{sh} I_{sc}}{V_{oc}} = \dfrac{200 \times 2.5}{0.805} = 621.1$$

$$FF = FF_0 (1 - r_s)\!\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{r_{sh}}\right) = 0.8314 \times (1 - 0.1553) \times \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{621.1}\right)$$

$$FF = 0.8314 \times 0.8447 \times 0.9984 = 0.7011$$

**(vi) Maximum power and efficiency:**

$$P_{max} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 0.7011 \times 0.805 \times 2.5 = 1.411\ \mathrm{W}$$

Incident power at AM1.5G (1000 W/m²) over 100 cm² = 0.01 m²:

$$P_{in} = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10\ \mathrm{W}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{1.411}{10} = 0.1411 = 14.11\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 14.1\%}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, draw the single-diode equivalent circuit of a solar cell and label all five circuit parameters. Explain the physical origin of $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the experimental method to extract the five circuit parameters from the measured I–V curve of a solar cell. What information does the ideality factor $n$ provide about the dominant recombination mechanism? (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

The single-diode equivalent circuit (see Section 4.1) consists of:

- A **current source $I_L$** representing photogenerated carriers.
- A **p–n junction diode** with saturation current $I_0$ and ideality factor $n$, in **parallel** with the current source.
- A **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** in parallel with the diode, representing leakage paths.
- A **series resistance $R_s$** in series with the parallel combination, representing lumped ohmic losses.

**Physical origin of $R_s$:**
- Emitter and base sheet resistance of the semiconductor.
- Resistance of the metal front grid and bus bars.
- Contact resistance between the metal and the semiconductor (Schottky-like barrier).
- Resistance of the back contact and the inter-cell ribbons.

**Physical origin of $R_{sh}$:**
- Edge shunting from scribing/cutting damage.
- Pinholes in the emitter or anti-reflection coating.
- Impurity diffusion along grain boundaries.
- Localised microscopic shorts at the p–n junction.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Experimental extraction procedure (in 5 steps):**

1. **Measure I–V curve** of the cell under STC ($G = 1000$ W/m², AM1.5G, $T = 25^{\circ}$C) using a four-point probe source-measure unit to eliminate lead resistance.
2. **Extract $I_{sc}$** from the current-axis intercept ($V = 0$). Approximate $I_L \approx I_{sc}$.
3. **Extract $V_{oc}$** from the voltage-axis intercept ($I = 0$).
4. **Compute $R_{sh}$** from the inverse slope of the I–V curve **near $V_{oc}$**:

$$R_{sh} = -\left.\dfrac{dV}{dI}\right|_{V \approx V_{oc}}$$

5. **Compute $R_s$** from the inverse slope **near $I_{sc}$**:

$$R_s = -\left.\dfrac{dV}{dI}\right|_{I \approx I_{sc}}$$

6. **Select a third point** $(V_1, I_1)$ on the curve (typically at the "knee", i.e., $V_1 \approx 0.7 V_{oc}$). Substitute into the single-diode equation together with $I_L = I_{sc}$ and solve numerically (Newton–Raphson) for $n$ and $I_0$.

**Diagnostic meaning of $n$:**

- $n \approx 1$ → **Band-to-band (radiative) recombination** dominates; ideal diode-like behaviour; high-quality single-crystal junction.
- $1 < n < 2$ → **Mixed mechanism**: band-to-band + Shockley–Read–Hall (SRH) recombination through defect states.
- $n \approx 2$ → **SRH (defect-mediated) recombination** dominates; common in polycrystalline, thin-film, and polymer BHJ cells.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "$-1$" term:** Many students drop the "$-1$" inside the exponential at the very first step. It is negligible ONLY when $I_L / I_0 \gg 1$, which must be stated explicitly to get full marks.
> 2. **Sign errors in $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$:** The slope $dV/dI$ near $I_{sc}$ is **negative**, hence the negative sign. Many students write $R_s = dV/dI$ (wrong sign) and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $I_L$ with $I_{sc}$:** They are not equal in a real cell; $I_L \approx I_{sc}$ is an **approximation** that becomes exact only for $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$.
> 4. **Treating the I–V equation as explicit in I:** It is **transcendental**; close-form analytical solution requires the Lambert-W function, not simple algebra.
> 5. **Mixing the $v_{oc}$ normalised variable with $V_{oc}$:** $v_{oc} = V_{oc}/(nV_T)$ is dimensionless; $V_{oc}$ is in Volts. Marks lost if dimensions are not tracked.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Circuit Parameters**
> - [x] The **five circuit parameters** are $I_L$, $I_0$, $n$, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$.
> - [x] The **single-diode model equation** is the master I–V relation; it is **transcendental** in $I$.
> - [x] $V_{oc} = n V_T \ln(I_L / I_0)$ — logarithmic dependence on $I_L$ and inverse-log on $I_0$.
> - [x] $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ — valid only for ideal cell ($R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$).
> - [x] $FF = P_{max} / (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$; the closer to 1, the better the cell shape.
> - [x] **Green's empirical formula:** $FF_0 = [v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)] / (v_{oc} + 1)$ is the standard board-exam trick.
> - [x] **$R_s$ rounds the knee; $R_{sh}$ tilts the floor** — the visual mnemonic.
> - [x] **$n$ is a diagnostic:** $n=1$ ideal, $n=2$ SRH-recombination-dominated.
> - [x] **Extraction procedure:** intercepts → $I_{sc}, V_{oc}$ → slopes → $R_s, R_{sh}$ → third point → $n, I_0$.
> - [x] **Polymer BHJ cells** typically have higher $R_s$ and lower $R_{sh}$ than c-Si cells — making circuit parameters a key quality indicator.
> - [x] **Temperature dependence:** $V_{oc} \downarrow$ with $T$ (β ≈ −0.35 %/K for c-Si), $I_{sc} \uparrow$ weakly (α ≈ +0.05 %/K).
> - [x] **Two-diode model** adds a second diode with $n_2 = 2$ for SRH recombination — used when the single-diode model under-fits the data.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Circuit Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Current

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Short Circuit Current ($I_{sc}$)** is defined as the **photocurrent** generated by a photovoltaic (solar) cell when the **external load resistance is zero** (i.e., the cell terminals are directly short-circuited, forcing the terminal voltage $V = 0$). It is the **maximum possible current** that a solar cell can deliver under a given illumination condition and represents a fundamental electrical parameter extracted from the **I–V characteristic curve** of the device.

Mathematically, it is read directly off the **y-intercept** of the illuminated I–V curve:

$$I_{sc} = I \Big\vert_{V=0}$$

The corresponding **short-circuit current density** ($J_{sc}$) is the current normalized to the active cell area:

$$J_{sc} = \frac{I_{sc}}{A}$$

where $A$ is the effective illuminated area of the cell (typically expressed in **mA/cm²** or **A/m²**).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Garden Hose Open at the Tap"

Imagine a **water pump (the solar cell)** connected to a **horizontal pipe (external circuit)** with a valve at the end (the load).

- When the valve is **fully closed**, water pressure builds up but **no water flows** → this is the **open-circuit condition** ($V_{oc}$).
- When the valve is **fully open** and the pipe end is **drained freely**, **maximum water flows** with **zero back-pressure** → this is the **short-circuit condition** ($I_{sc}$).
- The amount of water flowing depends on **how strong the pump is (light intensity)** and **how big the pipe is (cell area)**.

In the same way, $I_{sc}$ is the **maximum charge carrier flow** that the semiconductor can push through the external circuit when **zero voltage opposition** is present.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (c-Si cell) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short Circuit Current | $I_{sc}$ | 3 – 8 | A |
| Short Circuit Current Density | $J_{sc}$ | 30 – 42 | mA/cm² |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |
| Elementary Charge | $q$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C |
| Thermal Voltage (at 300 K) | $V_T = k_B T / q$ | **0.0259** | V |
| Standard Test Condition (STC) | Irradiance / Temp | **1000 W/m², AM 1.5G, 25 °C** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> All manufacturer-rated $I_{sc}$ values are quoted at **Standard Test Conditions (STC)** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

## 1.4 Why $I_{sc}$ Matters — KTU Exam Significance

- It is the **starting point** of any solar cell equivalent-circuit analysis.
- It determines the **maximum power point current** ($I_{mpp}$).
- It is used to calculate the **fill factor (FF)** and hence **conversion efficiency ($\eta$)**.
- It is a **direct experimental measurable** — measured using an ammeter with negligible internal resistance across the cell terminals.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Short Circuit Current — Physics Chain

The flow of $I_{sc}$ can be decomposed into the following sequential physics steps:

1. **Photon Absorption** — Incident solar photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ (band gap) strike the absorber layer.
2. **Electron–Hole Pair (EHP) Generation** — Each absorbed photon creates **one EHP** in the semiconductor.
3. **Charge Separation** — The built-in potential of the p–n junction sweeps electrons toward the **n-side** and holes toward the **p-side**.
4. **Collection at Terminals** — Photo-generated carriers travel through the external circuit when $V=0$, producing the photocurrent $I_L$.
5. **Short Circuit Realization** — The terminal voltage is clamped to zero, so the diode's forward-bias recombination current is negligible compared to $I_L$.

---

## 2.2 Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit Model

The illuminated solar cell is modeled as:

- A **photo-generated current source** $I_L$
- A **parallel diode** (the p–n junction itself)
- A **series resistance** $R_s$
- A **shunt (parallel) resistance** $R_{sh}$

The governing equation of the equivalent circuit is:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where:
- $I$ = terminal current
- $V$ = terminal voltage
- $I_L$ = photo-generated (light) current
- $I_0$ = reverse saturation current of the diode
- $n$ = ideality factor (1 to 2)
- $V_T = k_B T / q$

---

## 2.3 Deriving $I_{sc}$ from the Model

Setting $V = 0$ in the governing equation:

$$I_{sc} = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

For an **ideal solar cell** ($R_s \to 0$ and $R_{sh} \to \infty$):

$$I_{sc} = I_L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For an ideal cell, the short-circuit current is numerically equal to the photo-generated current.** In real cells with finite $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$, $I_{sc}$ is slightly **less than** $I_L$, but the difference is usually less than 1 %.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Short Circuit Current (Quick Reference)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{sc} = I \vert_{V=0}$ | Definition | A |
| 2 | $J_{sc} = I_{sc} / A$ | Current density | A/m² |
| 3 | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ | Ideal-cell approximation | A |
| 4 | $I_L = q \int_{0}^{\infty} \Phi(\lambda)\, \eta_{coll}(\lambda)\, d\lambda$ | Spectral integral form | A |
| 5 | $I_{sc}(G) = I_{sc,STC} \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_{STC}}$ | Linear dependence on irradiance | A |
| 6 | $\dfrac{dI_{sc}}{dT} \approx +0.05 \,\%/\,^{\circ}C$ | Temperature coefficient (c-Si) | %/°C |
| 7 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mpp}\, I_{mpp}}{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor relation | — |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}\, FF}{G \cdot A}$ | Conversion efficiency | — |
| 9 | $V_T = \dfrac{k_B T}{q} \approx 25.85\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K | Thermal voltage | V |

> [!NOTE]
> All pipe-like absolute-value expressions have been rendered using the LaTeX macros `\vert` and `\Big\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of $I_{sc}$

- **Module Sizing** — Determines the **strings in parallel** required for a given array current.
- **Performance Diagnostics** — A sudden drop in $I_{sc}$ in field installations indicates **shading, soiling, or cell cracks**.
- **Calibration Standard** — Used to verify spectral mismatch and translation equations in laboratory measurements (IEC 60904 standards).
- **Parameter Extraction** — Combined with $V_{oc}$, enables extraction of $I_L$, $I_0$, $R_s$, and $R_{sh}$ using numerical methods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — $I_{sc}$ from the Single-Diode Equation

**Step 1:** Start with the illuminated single-diode equation:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the short-circuit boundary condition: the external load resistance is zero, therefore the **terminal voltage $V = 0$**:

$$V = 0$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $V = 0$:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{0 + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{0 + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 4:** At this operating point, the terminal current is, by definition, $I_{sc}$:

$$I_{sc} = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 5:** In a high-quality cell, $I_0$ is on the order of $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-12}$ A, while $I_{sc}$ is on the order of amperes. The exponential term with $R_s$ small (m$\Omega$ range) gives a near-zero contribution:

$$\exp\left(\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}\right) \approx 1 + \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}$$

Substituting this linearization:

$$I_{sc} \approx I_L - I_0 \left[ \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T} \right] - \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 6:** Since $I_0$ is very small, the middle term becomes negligible. Rearranging:

$$I_{sc} \left(1 + \frac{R_s}{R_{sh}}\right) \approx I_L$$

**Step 7:** For an ideal cell ($R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$):

$$\boxed{I_{sc} = I_L}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linear Dependence of $I_{sc}$ on Irradiance

**Step 1:** Photocurrent is generated by **incident photon flux** $\Phi$. For monochromatic light at wavelength $\lambda$:

$$dI_L = q \cdot \Phi(\lambda) \cdot \eta_{coll}(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$

**Step 2:** Integrating over the full solar spectrum:

$$I_L = q \int_{0}^{\infty} \Phi(\lambda)\, \eta_{coll}(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$

**Step 3:** The photon flux is directly proportional to the irradiance $G$:

$$\Phi(\lambda) = \frac{G(\lambda)}{h\nu} \propto G$$

**Step 4:** Hence $I_L \propto G$, and therefore:

$$I_{sc}(G) = I_{sc}(G_{STC}) \cdot \frac{G}{G_{STC}}$$

**Step 5:** At $G_{STC} = 1000\,\text{W/m}^2$:

$$I_{sc} = I_{sc,STC} \cdot \frac{G}{1000}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Temperature Dependence of $I_{sc}$

**Step 1:** The photocurrent depends on the **band gap** $E_g$, which decreases weakly with temperature (Varshni relation):

$$E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \frac{\alpha T^2}{T + \beta}$$

**Step 2:** A lower $E_g$ means **more photons** (those with $h\nu \geq E_g$) can be absorbed, increasing the photocurrent slightly.

**Step 3:** The empirical linearization around STC (300 K) yields:

$$I_{sc}(T) = I_{sc}(T_{ref}) \left[ 1 + \alpha_{Isc} \cdot (T - T_{ref}) \right]$$

where the temperature coefficient for crystalline silicon is:

$$\alpha_{Isc} \approx +0.05 \,\%\, /\,^{\circ}C$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although $I_{sc}$ increases slightly with temperature, the **voltage drops more sharply** (about $-0.3\,\%\,/\,^{\circ}C$), making net efficiency **fall** with temperature.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A silicon solar cell has the following specifications at STC:
- $I_L = 3.20$ A, $I_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ A
- $R_s = 0.01 \,\Omega$, $R_{sh} = 1000 \,\Omega$
- $n = 1.3$, $T = 300$ K

Compute $I_{sc}$.

**Solution:**

Thermal voltage:

$$V_T = \frac{k_B T}{q} = \frac{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585\,\text{V}$$

Total voltage drop across $R_s$ at short circuit:

$$\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T} \approx \frac{3.2 \times 0.01}{1.3 \times 0.02585} \approx 9.53$$

Note: this is **not small**! For an accurate solution, solve iteratively.

**Iteration 1:** Assume $I_{sc}^{(0)} = 3.20$ A.

$$I_{sc}^{(1)} = 3.20 - 10^{-9}\left[\exp(9.53) - 1\right] - \frac{3.20 \times 0.01}{1000}$$

$$I_{sc}^{(1)} = 3.20 - 10^{-9} \cdot (1.36 \times 10^{4}) - 3.2 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$I_{sc}^{(1)} \approx 3.20 - 1.36 \times 10^{-5} - 3.2 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$I_{sc}^{(1)} \approx 3.1999\,\text{A}$$

Converged → $I_{sc} \approx 3.20$ A, very close to $I_L$, confirming the approximation $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ for typical cells.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — How $I_{sc}$ Reacts to Parameter Changes

| Parameter | Direction of Change | Effect on $I_{sc}$ | Effect on $V_{oc}$ | Net Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase irradiance $G$ | ↑ | **Linear ↑** | Logarithmic ↑ | ↑ |
| Increase temperature $T$ | ↑ | Tiny ↑ (+0.05 %/°C) | **Strong ↓** (-2 mV/°C) | **↓** |
| Increase series $R_s$ | ↑ | Small ↓ | Almost no effect | ↓ |
| Decrease shunt $R_{sh}$ | ↓ | Linear ↓ | Small ↓ | ↓ |
| Larger band gap $E_g$ | ↑ | ↓ (less absorption) | ↑ (higher voltage) | Optimal at $E_g \approx 1.34$ eV |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Solar Cell Equivalent Circuit Under Short-Circuit Condition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Incident Solar Irradiance G W per m2"] --> B["Photon Absorption in Absorber Layer"]
    B --> C["Electron Hole Pair Generation"]
    C --> D["Junction Electric Field Separates Charges"]
    D --> E["Photo Current Source IL"]
    E --> F["Equivalent Diode of the P N Junction"]
    F --> G["Series Resistance Rs"]
    G --> H["Shunt Resistance Rsh"]
    H --> I["External Short Circuit Wire Zero Ohm"]
    I --> J["Ammmeter Reads ISC equal to IL"]

    K["Open Switch Terminal Voltage V equals 0"] -.-> I
    L["Single Diode Model Governing Equation"] -.-> E
    L -.-> F
    L -.-> G
    L -.-> H
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Flow — How $I_{sc}$ is Measured in the Laboratory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1 Mount solar cell on test fixture under solar simulator"] --> S2["Step 2 Set irradiance to 1000 W per m2 AM 1.5G spectrum"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Connect ammeter of negligible internal resistance across cell terminals"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 Maintain cell temperature at 25 degree Celsius using temperature controlled stage"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 Read the current value directly from the ammeter"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6 Record value as ISC at STC"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7 Optionally repeat at different irradiance to confirm linear scaling"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid I–V Curve Schematic — Location of $I_{sc}$

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IV["I V Characteristic Curve of a Solar Cell"]
        direction LR
        V1["V equals 0 at origin"] --> V2["Open Circuit Voltage VOC on x axis"]
        I1["Maximum Current ISC on y axis"] --> I2["Zero Current at VOC"]
        P1["Maximum Power Point Pmpp"] --> P2["Product of Vmpp and Impp"]
    end

    M1["Y intercept of I V curve where V equals 0"] -.-> I1
    M2["X intercept of I V curve where I equals 0"] -.-> V2
    M3["Knee point of the curve"] -.-> P1
```

---

## 4.4 Physical Description of the Setup

For readers who cannot visualize a Mermaid graph, here is the **equivalent text description**:

> A **solar cell under illumination** is connected to an **ammeter** whose internal resistance is ideally **zero**. The ammeter forms a closed loop with the cell, so the terminal voltage across the cell collapses to **zero**. The only current flowing in the loop is the **photocurrent $I_L$**, which the ammeter reads as $I_{sc}$. The diode of the p–n junction is **slightly forward-biased by the small voltage drop across $R_s$**, but the resulting diode current is **negligible** compared to $I_L$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$) of a solar cell. State its typical value for a crystalline silicon cell.**

**Model Answer:**

> Short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$) of a solar cell is the current delivered by the cell when its terminals are short-circuited (i.e., terminal voltage $V = 0$) under a given illumination. It is the **y-intercept of the illuminated I–V curve** and is numerically equal to the photo-generated current $I_L$ for an ideal cell.
>
> For a typical c-Si solar cell under STC, $I_{sc}$ lies in the range **3 – 8 A**, corresponding to a current density $J_{sc}$ of **30 – 42 mA/cm²**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**How does the short-circuit current of a solar cell vary with (a) irradiance and (b) temperature?**

**Model Answer:**

> (a) **Irradiance:** $I_{sc}$ varies **linearly** with irradiance $G$ according to $I_{sc}(G) = I_{sc,STC} \cdot (G / G_{STC})$. Doubling $G$ doubles $I_{sc}$.
>
> (b) **Temperature:** $I_{sc}$ increases **very slightly** with temperature (positive temperature coefficient ≈ **+0.05 %/°C** for c-Si) because the band gap decreases, allowing more photons to be absorbed. The increase is small compared to the decrease in $V_{oc}$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often **omit the single-diode equation substitution step** and lose 4 marks.
> - Confusing $I_{sc}$ with $I_L$ without stating the assumption (ideal cell) is a **common 2-mark deduction**.
> - **Units must be quoted** with every numerical answer.
> - Mermaid-style circuit diagrams, when used, must be **clean and labeled**; messy diagrams lose up to 3 marks.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**(a)** Starting from the **single-diode equivalent circuit** of a solar cell, derive an expression for the short-circuit current $I_{sc}$. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar cell has the following parameters under STC: $I_L = 2.85$ A, $I_0 = 10^{-10}$ A, $n = 1.2$, $R_s = 0.05 \,\Omega$, $R_{sh} = 500 \,\Omega$. Compute the short-circuit current at $T = 300$ K. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The single-diode illuminated model is:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Apply the boundary condition for short circuit: $V = 0$:

$$I_{sc} = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Assumptions for simplification:**
1. $R_s$ is small (m$\Omega$ order), so $\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T} \ll 1$.
2. $I_0$ is negligibly small (nA range).
3. $R_{sh}$ is very large (k$\Omega$ order).

Applying these:

$$\exp\left(\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \approx \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T}$$

Thus:

$$I_{sc} \approx I_L - I_0 \cdot \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{n V_T} - \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

The two correction terms are negligible, giving:

$$\boxed{I_{sc} = I_L}$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Thermal voltage:

$$V_T = \frac{k_B T}{q} = \frac{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585\,\text{V}$$

Step 2 — Set up iteration assuming $I_{sc}^{(0)} = I_L = 2.85$ A:

$$I_{sc}^{(1)} = 2.85 - 10^{-10}\left[\exp\left(\frac{2.85 \times 0.05}{1.2 \times 0.02585}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{2.85 \times 0.05}{500}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{2.85 \times 0.05}{1.2 \times 0.02585} = \frac{0.1425}{0.03102} \approx 4.594$$

So:

$$\exp(4.594) - 1 \approx 98.7$$

Diode term:

$$10^{-10} \times 98.7 \approx 9.87 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{A}$$

Shunt term:

$$\frac{0.1425}{500} = 2.85 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{A}$$

Step 3 — Combine:

$$I_{sc}^{(1)} = 2.85 - 9.87 \times 10^{-9} - 2.85 \times 10^{-4} \approx 2.8497\,\text{A}$$

Step 4 — Iterate again with $I_{sc} = 2.8497$ A — the value converges.

$$\boxed{I_{sc} \approx 2.85\,\text{A}}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how short-circuit current is measured in the laboratory. List **two reasons** why the measured $I_{sc}$ in a practical cell differs slightly from $I_L$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar module has $I_{sc} = 8.2$ A at STC (1000 W/m²). Calculate $I_{sc}$ when the irradiance drops to **(i)** 600 W/m² and **(ii)** 200 W/m². Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Laboratory Measurement (7 marks):**

**Procedure:**
1. Place the cell under a **class AAA solar simulator** producing AM 1.5G spectrum at 1000 W/m².
2. Connect a **low-resistance ammeter** (or a current shunt) directly across the cell terminals.
3. Maintain the cell at **25 °C** using a thermoelectric stage.
4. Read the current directly — this is $I_{sc}$ at STC.

**Reasons $I_{sc} \neq I_L$ in practice:**
1. **Finite Series Resistance $R_s$:** Causes a small voltage drop $I_{sc} R_s$, slightly forward-biasing the diode, producing a small recombination current.
2. **Finite Shunt Resistance $R_{sh}$:** Provides an alternate leakage path that diverts some of the photocurrent, reducing the measured terminal current.
3. **Spectral mismatch** between simulator and true AM 1.5G also contributes.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

The governing relation is:

$$I_{sc}(G) = I_{sc,STC} \cdot \frac{G}{G_{STC}}$$

**(i)** At $G = 600\,\text{W/m}^2$:

$$I_{sc}(600) = 8.2 \times \frac{600}{1000} = 8.2 \times 0.6 = 4.92\,\text{A}$$

**(ii)** At $G = 200\,\text{W/m}^2$:

$$I_{sc}(200) = 8.2 \times \frac{200}{1000} = 8.2 \times 0.2 = 1.64\,\text{A}$$

**Comment:** The short-circuit current scales **linearly** with irradiance. This property makes $I_{sc}$ a useful **diagnostic indicator** for shading and soiling — any deviation from the expected linear scaling signals a non-ideal condition in the field.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Short Circuit Current**

- $I_{sc}$ is the **y-intercept** of the illuminated I–V curve, where $V = 0$.
- For an **ideal cell**, $I_{sc} = I_L$ (photo-generated current).
- **Units**: $I_{sc}$ in **Amperes (A)**; $J_{sc}$ in **mA/cm²** or **A/m²**.
- **Linear in irradiance**: $I_{sc} \propto G$.
- **Tiny positive temperature coefficient**: ≈ **+0.05 %/°C** for c-Si.
- $I_{sc}$ **decreases** with increasing series resistance $R_s$ and decreasing shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.
- **Measured** with a low-resistance ammeter across cell terminals under STC (1000 W/m², AM 1.5G, 25 °C).
- A practical cell always has $I_{sc}$ **slightly less** than $I_L$ due to non-zero $R_s$ and finite $R_{sh}$.
- $I_{sc}$ is **insensitive to voltage** variations — it's a **current-source-dominated parameter**.
- Used directly in computing **Fill Factor (FF)** and **conversion efficiency ($\eta$)**.
- **Real-world diagnostic**: Sudden drop in field $I_{sc}$ → suspect shading, soiling, or cell cracking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Circuit Voltage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Open Circuit Voltage # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Open Circuit Voltage (V_oc)** is the maximum voltage that a photovoltaic (PV) cell or module can deliver when the external circuit is broken (i.e., **infinite load resistance, R_L → ∞**). It is measured directly across the terminals of an illuminated, disconnected solar cell. Mathematically, $V_{oc}$ is the **terminal voltage** of the cell at the point where the **net current $I = 0$** (the cell delivers no external current). It represents the **maximum possible voltage** the device can sustain under a given illumination level and temperature. In the equivalent one-diode model of a solar cell: $$I = I_{ph} - I_{0}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{n k_B T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$ Setting $I = 0$ and assuming an ideal cell ($R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$): $$\boxed{\,V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_{0}} + 1\right)\,}$$ where the symbols carry their standard meanings (defined in Section 2). --- ## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of a solar cell as a small water pump pushing electrons uphill into a reservoir.** When the tap (external circuit) is **closed**, electrons flow out — this is the *short circuit* or *loaded* condition. When the tap is **fully closed (open circuit)**, electrons keep piling up at one terminal but cannot leave. The pressure (voltage) builds up until it exactly counterbalances the pump's "push." That **maximum pressure** before the electrons are forced to stop is the **Open Circuit Voltage**. A simpler everyday analogy: - 🔋 A rechargeable battery being charged by sunlight behaves like a solar cell. The **maximum voltage** on its terminals *just before any load is connected* — measured with a voltmeter — is essentially its $V_{oc}$. --- ## 🎯 Physical Significance & Engineering Relevance | Property | Description | |---|---| | **Symbol** | $V_{oc}$ | | **Units** | **Volts (V)** — typically 0.5 V to 0.75 V for a single crystalline Si cell | | **Typical magnitude (c-Si)** | **~ 0.6 – 0.72 V** per cell | | **Module value** | Sum of series-connected cells (e.g., 36-cell module ≈ 21 V) | | **Measurement** | Direct voltmeter across illuminated, unloaded cell | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** $V_{oc}$ is **not** the operating voltage of the cell. Under load, the cell always operates at some voltage $V_{mpp} < V_{oc}$. $V_{oc}$ is the **upper voltage limit** that defines the boundary of the I–V characteristic. --- ## 🔬 Physical Origin of $V_{oc}$ The voltage that a solar cell can develop arises from the **built-in electric field** of the p–n junction. 1. **Photon absorption** generates electron–hole pairs. 2. **Junction field** sweeps the photo-generated carriers (electrons to n-side, holes to p-side). 3. This charge separation establishes a **forward bias** that opposes the built-in potential $V_{bi}$. 4. At open circuit, the **forward bias exactly cancels the photo-current**, leaving a measurable terminal voltage — the $V_{oc}$. Hence, $V_{oc}$ is fundamentally limited by the **bandgap $E_g$** of the absorber: $$\boxed{\,V_{oc} < \dfrac{E_g}{q}\,}$$ This is the **thermodynamic upper bound** — a direct consequence of detailed balance (Shockley–Queisser limit). # ⚡ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🔍 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown ### Step 1: The Single-Diode Equivalent Model The illuminated solar cell is modeled as a **current source** (photo-current $I_{ph}$) in parallel with a **diode** (representing the p–n junction), a **series resistance** $R_s$ (ohmic losses), and a **shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ (leakage paths). The governing equation is: $$I = I_{ph} - I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$ where the **thermal voltage** is: $$V_T = \dfrac{k_B T}{q} \approx 25.85\ \text{mV at 300 K}$$ ### Step 2: Boundary Condition for Open Circuit By definition, **no current can flow** through the external circuit when $V = V_{oc}$: $$I = 0$$ Substituting $I = 0$ and $V = V_{oc}$ into the diode equation (ideal cell: $R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$): $$0 = I_{ph} - I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right]$$ Solving for $V_{oc}$: $$V_{oc} = n V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_{0}} + 1\right) \approx n V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_{0}}\right)$$ The approximation $\ln(x+1) \approx \ln(x)$ holds because $I_{ph}/I_0 \gg 1$ (typically $10^6$ to $10^{12}$). ### Step 3: The "Why" Behind Each Quantity | Quantity | Physical Meaning | Effect on $V_{oc}$ | |---|---|---| | $I_{ph}$ | Photo-generated current (∝ light intensity) | $V_{oc} \propto \ln(I_{ph})$ — **logarithmic** increase | | $I_0$ | Reverse saturation current (∝ recombination) | Higher $I_0$ → **lower** $V_{oc}$ | | $n$ | Ideality factor (1 = ideal diode, 2 = recombination-limited) | Higher $n$ → **lower** $V_{oc}$ | | $T$ | Absolute temperature (K) | Direct proportionality but temperature also raises $I_0$ → net **decrease** | | $k_B/q$ | Boltzmann constant / electron charge | Fundamental constant | ### Step 4: The Temperature Trade-off (Critical for KTU!) Although $V_T \propto T$, the saturation current depends **super-exponentially** on temperature: $$I_0 \propto T^{3}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$ Therefore, $V_{oc}$ has a **negative temperature coefficient** of approximately: $$\dfrac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx -\dfrac{E_g - V_{oc}}{qT} \approx -2\ \text{mV/°C per cell (for c-Si)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **This is why solar panels lose voltage (and thus power) on hot days — a frequently tested KTU point!** --- ## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{oc} = n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Master expression for open-circuit voltage | | 2 | $V_T = \dfrac{k_B T}{q} \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K | Thermal voltage | | 3 | $V_{oc,\max} < \dfrac{E_g}{q}$ | Thermodynamic upper limit (bandgap limit) | | 4 | $I_0 \propto T^{3}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$ | Saturation current temperature dependence | | 5 | $\dfrac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx -\dfrac{V_{oc} - E_g/q}{T}$ | Temperature coefficient (negative) | | 6 | $V_{oc}(\text{light}) = V_{oc,\text{dark}} + n V_T \ln(1 + J_{ph}/J_0)$ | Light vs. dark $V_{oc}$ | | 7 | $\eta_{Voc} = \dfrac{q V_{oc}}{E_g}$ | Voltage utilization factor (efficiency indicator) | | 8 | $\text{FF} = \dfrac{V_{mpp} I_{mpp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | Fill factor (uses $V_{oc}$) | | 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}\, \text{FF}}{P_{in}}$ | Overall conversion efficiency | | 10 | $\Delta V_{oc} = n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{G_2}{G_1}\right)$ | $V_{oc}$ shift between two irradiances $G_1, G_2$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always specify whether $V_{oc}$ is given for a **single cell, a module, or an array**. Module $V_{oc}$ = (number of cells in series) × (cell $V_{oc}$). --- ## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT):** Algorithms use $V_{oc}$ to estimate the optimal operating voltage — typically $V_{mpp} \approx 0.8 \cdot V_{oc}$. - **Performance Diagnostics:** A sudden drop in $V_{oc}$ indicates **shading, degradation, or junction failure**. - **Energy Yield Forecasting:** Field engineers measure $V_{oc}$ to predict annual energy output. - **Bypass Diode Design:** Module strings are protected using $V_{oc}$ to size bypass diodes. - **Standards Compliance:** IEC 61215 requires $V_{oc}$ stability under thermal cycling and humidity tests. # ⚡ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🧮 Derivation 1: $V_{oc}$ from the Single-Diode Equation **Starting point — single-diode model equation:** $$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$ **Step 1:** Apply the open-circuit boundary condition $I = 0$ and $V = V_{oc}$. $$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc} + 0 \cdot R_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V_{oc} + 0}{R_{sh}}$$ **Step 2:** Rearrange so the exponential term is alone. $$I_{ph} - \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}} = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]$$ **Step 3:** For an **ideal cell** ($R_{sh} \to \infty$, so $V_{oc}/R_{sh} \to 0$): $$I_{ph} = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]$$ **Step 4:** Divide both sides by $I_0$. $$\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1$$ **Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm on both sides. $$\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right) = \frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}$$ **Step 6:** Solve for $V_{oc}$. $$\boxed{\,V_{oc} = nV_T \,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)\,}$$ **Step 7 (Approximation):** Because $I_{ph}/I_0 \gg 1$ (typically $10^{6}$–$10^{12}$), the "+1" inside the logarithm can be safely neglected: $$V_{oc} \approx nV_T \,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > This approximation is **standard practice** in photovoltaic engineering and is the form most commonly used in KTU problems. --- ## 🧮 Derivation 2: Temperature Coefficient of $V_{oc}$ **Step 1:** Begin with the explicit form of the saturation current using semiconductor physics: $$I_0 = A \cdot q \cdot N_c N_v \left(\frac{D_n}{L_n N_A} + \frac{D_p}{L_p N_D}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$ The dominant temperature dependence is the exponential term. Define the prefactor as $B(T)$ which varies slowly as $T^{3}$: $$I_0(T) = B(T) \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the $V_{oc}$ expression (with $I_{ph}$ held constant at fixed illumination): $$V_{oc}(T) = \frac{n k_B T}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{B(T)}\right) + \frac{n E_g}{q}$$ **Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to temperature (assume $E_g$ weakly T-dependent): $$\frac{dV_{oc}}{dT} = \frac{n k_B}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{B(T)}\right) + \frac{n k_B T}{q}\left(-\frac{1}{B}\frac{dB}{dT}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Recognize that $\ln(I_{ph}/B) \approx (q/nk_BT)(V_{oc} - E_g/q)$ from Step 2: $$\frac{dV_{oc}}{dT} = \frac{1}{T}\!\left(V_{oc} - \frac{E_g}{q}\right) - \frac{n k_B T}{q B}\frac{dB}{dT}$$ **Step 5:** With $B \propto T^{3}$, we get $\frac{1}{B}\frac{dB}{dT} = \frac{3}{T}$: $$\boxed{\,\frac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx \frac{V_{oc} - E_g/q}{T} - \frac{3 n k_B}{q}\,}$$ For c-Si with $V_{oc} \approx 0.65$ V, $E_g = 1.12$ eV, $T = 300$ K, $n \approx 1.3$: $$\frac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx \frac{0.65 - 1.12}{300} - \frac{3(1.3)(8.617\times10^{-5})}{1} \approx -1.57\ \text{mV/K} - 0.336\ \text{mV/K}$$ $$\frac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx -1.9\ \text{mV/°C} \quad (\text{close to the empirical value of } -2\ \text{mV/°C})$$ --- ## 🧮 Derivation 3: Irradiance Dependence of $V_{oc}$ **Step 1:** Photo-current scales linearly with irradiance $G$: $$I_{ph}(G) = I_{ph,\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{G}{G_{\text{ref}}}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the $V_{oc}$ expression (with $I_0$ and $T$ constant): $$V_{oc}(G) = nV_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph,\text{ref}}}{I_0}\right) + nV_T \ln\!\left(\frac{G}{G_{\text{ref}}}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\,V_{oc}(G) = V_{oc,\text{ref}} + nV_T \ln\!\left(\frac{G}{G_{\text{ref}}}\right)\,}$$ **Step 3:** Interpretation — Doubling the irradiance increases $V_{oc}$ by only: $$\Delta V_{oc} = nV_T \ln(2) \approx (1.3)(25.85\ \text{mV})(0.693) \approx 23\ \text{mV}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Therefore, $V_{oc}$ depends **logarithmically** on irradiance — doubling $G$ gives only ~23 mV rise in $V_{oc}$, whereas $I_{sc}$ doubles.** --- ## 🧮 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A silicon solar cell under AM1.5G illumination ($G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$) has: - $I_{ph} = 3.5\ \text{A}$ - $I_0 = 1 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{A}$ - $n = 1.3$ - $T = 300\ \text{K}$ **Find:** $V_{oc}$. **Solution:** $$V_T = \frac{(1.381\times10^{-23})(300)}{1.602\times10^{-19}} = 25.85\ \text{mV}$$ $$V_{oc} = (1.3)(0.02585)\,\ln\!\left(\frac{3.5}{1\times10^{-12}} + 1\right)$$ $$V_{oc} = 0.03360\ \text{V} \cdot \ln(3.5\times10^{12})$$ $$V_{oc} = 0.03360 \times 28.78 = 0.967\ \text{V}$$ > [!NOTE] > The above value of ~0.97 V is **above** what real cells produce because $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ A is optimistic. In practice, c-Si cells reach $V_{oc} \approx 0.65$–$0.72$ V because $I_0 \approx 10^{-9}$ A. # ⚡ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 📊 Diagram 1: Equivalent Circuit at Open Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Photo-current Source
I_ph"]:::source B["Diode
D (p-n junction)"]:::diode C["Series Resistance
R_s"]:::series D["Shunt Resistance
R_sh"]:::shunt E["Open Terminals
Voltmeter across V_oc"]:::terminal F["I = 0
No external current"]:::open A --- B B --- D B --- C C --- E D --- E E --- F classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef diode fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef series fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef shunt fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef terminal fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef open fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#999900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 📊 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Open-Circuit Voltage Generation ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["STEP 1: Photon Incidence
Sunlight h nu hits the cell"]:::step1 P2["STEP 2: EHP Generation
Electron hole pairs created in absorber"]:::step2 P3["STEP 3: Charge Separation
Built-in field at p-n junction"]:::step3 P4["STEP 4: Carrier Accumulation
Electrons pile up at n-side terminal"]:::step4 P5["STEP 5: Forward Bias Build-up
Forward bias opposes V_bi"]:::step5 P6["STEP 6: Equilibrium at V_oc
Forward bias equals photo-voltage"]:::step6 P7["OUTPUT: Terminal Voltage V_oc measured"]:::output P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 classDef step1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px classDef step2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px classDef step3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px classDef step4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px classDef step5 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px classDef step6 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 📊 Diagram 3: I–V Curve Indicating $V_{oc}$ Position ```mermaid flowchart LR A["I-V Curve of Solar Cell
(Quadrant IV Operation)"]:::curve B["Y-axis: Current I (A)
Short circuit point I_sc at V=0"]:::yaxis C["X-axis: Voltage V (V)
Open circuit point V_oc at I=0"]:::xaxis D["Operating Point MPP
V_mpp x I_mpp"]:::mpp E["V_oc (I=0)
Right intercept on V axis"]:::voc F["I_sc (V=0)
Top intercept on I axis"]:::isc A --> B A --> C A --> D A --> E A --> F classDef curve fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:3px classDef yaxis fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px classDef xaxis fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px classDef mpp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px classDef voc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px classDef isc fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 📊 Diagram 4: Effect of Illumination on $V_{oc}$ (Qualitative) ```mermaid flowchart LR L1["Low Light G1
Low I_ph
Lower V_oc"]:::lowlight L2["Medium Light G2
Medium I_ph
Medium V_oc"]:::medlight L3["High Light G3
High I_ph
Higher V_oc"]:::highlight L4["V_oc increases only
