logarithmically with G"]:::note L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 classDef lowlight fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px classDef medlight fill:#FFD700,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px classDef highlight fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px classDef note fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px ``` # ⚡ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Define Open Circuit Voltage (V_oc) of a solar cell. State the expression relating V_oc to photo-current and reverse saturation current.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Open Circuit Voltage is the voltage measured across the terminals of an illuminated solar cell when **no external load is connected** (terminal current $I = 0$). It represents the **maximum voltage** deliverable by the cell under given illumination and temperature conditions. **Expression (2 marks):** From the single-diode model with $I = 0$: $$V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right) \approx \dfrac{n k_B T}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$$ where $n$ is the ideality factor, $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, $q$ is electron charge, $I_{ph}$ is the photo-generated current, and $I_0$ is the reverse saturation current. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]** **Why does V_oc have a negative temperature coefficient? Explain briefly.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** As temperature increases, two competing effects occur (1 mark): 1. The **thermal voltage** $V_T = k_B T / q$ increases, which by itself would raise $V_{oc}$ (1 mark). 2. However, the **reverse saturation current** $I_0$ increases **super-exponentially** with temperature: $$I_0 \propto T^3 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$ This increase in $I_0$ dominates, lowering the ratio $I_{ph}/I_0$ inside the logarithm and therefore reducing $V_{oc}$ (1 mark). The net effect is a negative temperature coefficient of approximately **–2 mV/°C per cell** for crystalline silicon. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage of an ideal p–n junction solar cell from the single-diode model. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A crystalline silicon solar cell has the following parameters at 300 K under AM1.5G: - Photo-current density $J_{ph} = 35\ \text{mA/cm}^2$ - Reverse saturation current density $J_0 = 1 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{A/cm}^2$ - Ideality factor $n = 1.2$ - Cell area $A = 100\ \text{cm}^2$ Calculate: (i) The thermal voltage $V_T$. (ii) The open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$. (iii) The maximum theoretical $V_{oc}$ if $E_g = 1.12$ eV. Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with the single-diode equation for a solar cell under illumination: $$I = I_{ph} - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** State the assumptions: - Series resistance $R_s = 0$ (no ohmic losses). - Shunt resistance $R_{sh} = \infty$ (no leakage). - Uniform illumination and temperature. - Single-exponential diode. **Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply open-circuit condition $I = 0$, $V = V_{oc}$: $$0 = I_{ph} - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Rearrange: $$I_{ph} = I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Take logarithm: $$\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T} = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Final expression: $$\boxed{V_{oc} = nV_T \,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Approximate for $I_{ph}/I_0 \gg 1$: $$V_{oc} \approx nV_T \,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks) **(i) Thermal voltage (2 marks):** $$V_T = \frac{k_B T}{q} = \frac{(1.381\times10^{-23})(300)}{1.602\times10^{-19}} = 25.85\ \text{mV}$$ **(ii) Open-circuit voltage (3 marks):** $$V_{oc} = nV_T \,\ln\!\left(\frac{J_{ph}}{J_0} + 1\right) = (1.2)(0.02585)\,\ln\!\left(\frac{35\times10^{-3}}{1\times10^{-11}} + 1\right)$$ $$V_{oc} = 0.03102 \times \ln(3.5\times10^{9}) = 0.03102 \times 22.67 = 0.703\ \text{V}$$ **(iii) Bandgap-limited maximum (2 marks):** $$V_{oc,\max} = \frac{E_g}{q} = 1.12\ \text{V}$$ **Comment:** The calculated $V_{oc}$ (0.703 V) is significantly **less than** the bandgap limit (1.12 V). The difference arises from: - **Recombination losses** (captured in $I_0$). - **Entropy loss** during carrier thermalization (Shockley–Queisser detailed balance). - **Non-radiative recombination** reduces $V_{oc}$ below the radiative limit. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]` **(a)** Discuss the physical factors that limit the open-circuit voltage of a solar cell. Explain why $V_{oc}$ cannot exceed $E_g/q$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A solar module has 36 series-connected silicon cells. Each cell has $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V at 25°C. If the temperature rises to 65°C, estimate the new $V_{oc}$ of the cell and the module. Given: temperature coefficient of $V_{oc} = -2\ \text{mV/°C per cell}$. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Physical Limits of $V_{oc}$ (7 marks) 1. **Bandgap limit (2 marks):** The energy per absorbed photon is at most $E_g$. Converting this to electrical energy gives at most $E_g/q$ volts. Detailed balance (Shockley–Queisser) shows that radiative recombination alone enforces $V_{oc} < E_g/q$. 2. **Recombination losses (2 marks):** Both **radiative** and **non-radiative** recombination contribute to $I_0$. Every recombination event consumes a photon or carrier that could have contributed to voltage: - Radiative recombination → $I_{0,\text{rad}}$ (fundamental, unavoidable) - Auger recombination → dominant in heavily doped Si - SRH (Shockley–Read–Hall) recombination → defect-mediated, dominant in low-quality material 3. **Ideality factor (1 mark):** $V_{oc} \propto 1/n$. SRH recombination in the depletion region gives $n = 2$, halving the voltage contribution per thermal voltage unit. 4. **Temperature (1 mark):** Increased temperature raises $I_0$ super-exponentially, lowering $V_{oc}$. 5. **Junction design (1 mark):** Poor passivation, surface recombination, contact resistance, and bandgap narrowing all reduce $V_{oc}$. --- #### Part (b) — Temperature Effect Numerical (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Temperature rise: $$\Delta T = 65 - 25 = 40\ \text{°C}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Change in cell $V_{oc}$: $$\Delta V_{oc,\text{cell}} = (-2\ \text{mV/°C})(40\ \text{°C}) = -80\ \text{mV} = -0.080\ \text{V}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** New cell $V_{oc}$: $$V_{oc,\text{cell}}(65°\text{C}) = 0.65 - 0.080 = 0.570\ \text{V}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Module $V_{oc}$ at 25°C: $$V_{oc,\text{module}}(25°\text{C}) = 36 \times 0.65 = 23.4\ \text{V}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Module $V_{oc}$ at 65°C: $$V_{oc,\text{module}}(65°\text{C}) = 36 \times 0.570 = 20.52\ \text{V}$$ **Conclusion:** The module loses **2.88 V** of open-circuit voltage when the temperature rises by 40°C — a critical design consideration for hot climates and rooftop PV systems. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the ideality factor $n$** — students often write $V_{oc} = V_T \ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$ without $n$. This is correct **only for an ideal diode** ($n=1$). Always include $n$ unless explicitly told otherwise. > > 2. **Confusing cell $V_{oc}$ with module $V_{oc}$** — examiners award 1–2 marks just for correctly stating the series-cell multiplication. > > 3. **Sign error in temperature coefficient** — $dV_{oc}/dT$ is **negative**. Writing $-2$ mV/°C as $+2$ mV/°C loses credit. > > 4. **Using Celsius instead of Kelvin** — $V_T$ requires absolute temperature in Kelvin. > > 5. **Not justifying approximations** — if you approximate $\ln(x+1) \approx \ln(x)$, write the **condition** $I_{ph}/I_0 \gg 1$. > > 6. **Mixing up $V_{oc}$ and $V_{mpp}$** — $V_{oc}$ is the open-circuit point; $V_{mpp}$ is the maximum-power operating point. They are **not equal** ($V_{mpp} \approx 0.8 V_{oc}$). > > 7. **Missing bandgap limit** — any derivation question worth ≥10 marks expects a final note on $V_{oc} < E_g/q$. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist:** > > - **$V_{oc}$** is the voltage across an **unloaded, illuminated** solar cell. > - **Master formula:** $V_{oc} = nV_T \ln(I_{ph}/I_0 + 1) \approx nV_T \ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$. > - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T \approx 25.85$ mV. > - **Upper bound:** $V_{oc} < E_g / q$. > - **Logarithmic with light:** Doubling irradiance adds only ~23 mV. > - **Negative temperature coefficient:** ~ –2 mV/°C for c-Si. > - **Module $V_{oc}$:** Sum of series-connected cell $V_{oc}$ values. > - **Depends strongly on $I_0$:** Lower recombination → higher $V_{oc}$. > - **Ideality factor:** $n=1$ (radiative), $n=2$ (SRH-limited). > - **Measurement:** Direct voltmeter across illuminated terminals. > - **Used in MPPT:** $V_{mpp} \approx 0.8 V_{oc}$. > - **Diagnostic value:** Sudden $V_{oc}$ drop indicates shading, soiling, or cell damage. > - **Standard test conditions (STC):** $G = 1000$ W/m², AM1.5G, $T = 25$°C. > - **Voc appears in efficiency formula:** $\eta = (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot \text{FF}) / P_{in}$.

topics.title: Loading Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Loading Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Loading conditions** in the context of **Polymers & Photovoltaic (PV) Technology** refer to the **complete set of mechanical, thermal, environmental, and operational stresses** that a polymer component or a PV module experiences during its service life. These loads dictate material selection, geometry design, durability testing, and lifetime prediction of polymeric encapsulants, backsheets, frames, adhesives, and the overall PV laminate.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, loading conditions are formally classified under **Module 2** as the external inputs that drive the **viscoelastic, elastic, and plastic response** of polymers used in photovoltaic systems. The principal categories recognized by international standards (IEC 61215, IEC 61730, ASTM D638, ASTM D790) are:

1. **Mechanical Loading** — Tension, compression, shear, bending, torsion.
2. **Thermal Loading** — Steady-state, cyclic, and gradient (ΔT) loading.
3. **Environmental Loading** — Wind, snow, hail, humidity, UV, chemical.
4. **Dynamic / Time-Dependent Loading** — Creep, stress relaxation, fatigue, impact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Loading conditions form the *input boundary conditions* for the constitutive equations governing polymer behavior (Hookean, viscoelastic Maxwell/Kelvin-Voigt models) and for the design verification tests specified in **IEC 61215:2021** for crystalline silicon PV modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a **rubber band tied to a small solar panel** on a rooftop.

- When the wind blows (a **mechanical load**), the rubber stretches — that is **tensile loading**.
- When the sun comes out and then disappears behind a cloud, the panel heats and cools — that is **thermal cyclic loading**.
- When the panel sits on the roof for 20 years under its own weight, it slowly sags — that is **creep loading**.
- When hailstones hit the surface — that is **impact loading**.

Each of these is a "loading condition," and the polymer materials inside the panel (encapsulant like **EVA — Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate**, backsheet like **PVDF** or **TPT**, sealant like **silicone**) must **survive all of them simultaneously** for 25+ years. *Loading conditions* is therefore the vocabulary engineers use to **describe the total stress environment** a material must endure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** Think of loading conditions as the "weather forecast" for materials. Just as a civil engineer studies rain, wind, and earthquakes before designing a bridge, a PV engineer must catalogue every possible load before choosing a polymer formulation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard SI units** are used throughout the analysis of loading conditions:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Stress | σ | **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²** |
| Strain | ε | **Dimensionless** (m/m) |
| Young's Modulus | E | **GPa** for polymers |
| Shear Modulus | G | **GPa** |
| Force | F | **Newton (N)** |
| Pressure (wind/snow) | P | **Pa** or **kPa** |
| Temperature | T | **Kelvin (K)** or **°C** |
| Time | t | **Seconds (s)** |
| Strain rate | ε̇ | **s⁻¹** |
| Creep compliance | J(t) | **Pa⁻¹** (1/GPa) |

**Universal constants used:** Gravitational acceleration **g = 9.81 m/s²**, Universal Gas Constant **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Loading Conditions — Structured Logic

Loading conditions are broadly classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A: By Time-Dependence
- **Static (Quasi-static) Loading** — Load applied slowly or held constant.
- **Dynamic Loading** — Load varies with time (cyclic, impact, random).

### Axis B: By Physical Nature
- **Mechanical** (force-induced)
- **Thermal** (temperature-induced)
- **Environmental** (weather/chemical-induced)
- **Combined / Coupled** (thermo-mechanical, hygro-mechanical)

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Each Loading Type

### 2.2.1 Tensile Loading
- A uniaxial force pulls the material apart along one axis.
- Governed by: $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ where $A_0$ is the original cross-section.
- For polymers: response is **viscoelastic** — depends on strain rate and temperature.
- Engineering relevance: PV backsheets and frame members are predominantly loaded in tension by wind uplift.

### 2.2.2 Compressive Loading
- Force pushes material together; $\sigma < 0$.
- Critical for module mounting clamps and racking bolts.
- Polymer foam-based thermal insulators in BIPV (Building-Integrated PV) primarily experience compression.

### 2.2.3 Shear Loading
- Force parallel to the surface: $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$.
- Critical at **encapsulant–glass interface** and **adhesive bondlines** in laminate PV modules.
- Shear stress in PV laminate is given by: $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A}$ where V is the transverse load.

### 2.2.4 Bending (Flexural) Loading
- Combination of tension and compression across the cross-section.
- Module deflection under wind/snow load is a bending problem.
- Flexural stress: $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ where M is the bending moment, y is the distance from neutral axis, I is the second moment of area.

### 2.2.5 Torsional Loading
- Twisting moment about the longitudinal axis.
- Relevant for tracker drives and rotating PV mounts.
- Shear stress due to torsion: $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ where T is torque, r is radial distance, J is polar moment of inertia.

### 2.2.6 Creep Loading (Time-Dependent)
- Constant stress → continuously increasing strain over time.
- Described by **creep compliance** J(t) = ε(t)/σ.
- Critical for polymer encapsulants operating at elevated temperatures (60–85 °C in modules).

### 2.2.7 Stress Relaxation
- Constant strain → stress decays over time.
- Critical for pre-tightened bolted polymer mounts.

### 2.2.8 Fatigue Loading (Cyclic)
- Repeated sub-failure stress cycles → progressive damage.
- Quantified by **S-N curve** (Stress vs Number of cycles to failure).
- Wind-induced vibration is a major fatigue source in PV installations.

### 2.2.9 Impact Loading
- Sudden, high-strain-rate load (e.g., hailstone strike).
- Strain rate typically > 10 s⁻¹.
- Polymers transition from ductile to brittle under high strain rates.

### 2.2.10 Thermal Loading
- Steady-state (e.g., 85 °C operational): causes differential expansion.
- Thermal cycling (e.g., −40 °C to +85 °C, 200 cycles per IEC 61215).
- Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) mismatch drives **thermomechanical fatigue**.

### 2.2.11 Environmental Loading
- **Wind pressure:** P_wind = ½ · ρ_air · v² (Bernoulli's dynamic pressure).
- **Snow load:** ρ_snow · g · h (pressure by snow depth).
- **Humidity / Moisture ingress:** drives hydrolytic degradation of EVA.
- **UV radiation:** photochemical degradation of polymer chains.

## 2.3 Constitutive Models for Polymer Response

### 2.3.1 Hooke's Law (Linear Elastic Limit)
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

### 2.3.2 Maxwell Model (Viscoelastic — Series Spring + Dashpot)
$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

### 2.3.3 Kelvin-Voigt Model (Viscoelastic — Parallel Spring + Dashpot)
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$$

### 2.3.4 Standard Linear Solid (Zener) Model
Combines Maxwell and Kelvin-Voigt elements to capture both instantaneous elasticity and delayed viscoelastic response.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU ESE — Memorize these tables.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A$ | Normal stress | Pa |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Young's Modulus | Pa |
| 4 | $\tau = F/A$ | Shear stress | Pa |
| 5 | $G = \tau / \gamma$ | Shear Modulus | Pa |
| 6 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} / \varepsilon_{\text{ax}}$ | Poisson's ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma = M \cdot y / I$ | Flexural stress (bending) | Pa |
| 8 | $\tau_{\max} = 1.5 \cdot V/A$ | Max shear in rectangular section | Pa |
| 9 | $\tau = T \cdot r / J$ | Torsional shear stress | Pa |
| 10 | $P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot v^2$ | Wind pressure | Pa |
| 11 | $P_{\text{snow}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Snow pressure | Pa |
| 12 | $\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal strain | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress (constrained) | Pa |
| 14 | $J(t) = \varepsilon(t) / \sigma_0$ | Creep compliance | Pa⁻¹ |
| 15 | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | Stress relaxation (Maxwell) | Pa |
| 16 | $E(t) = \sigma_0 / \varepsilon(t)$ | Relaxation modulus | Pa |
| 17 | $\tan \delta = E'' / E'$ | Loss tangent (damping) | dimensionless |
| 18 | $W_{f} = \sigma_a \cdot N_f^b$ | Coffin-Manson fatigue relation | mixed |
| 19 | $T_g$ | Glass transition temperature | K or °C |
| 20 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \cdot e^{-Q/RT} \cdot \sigma^n$ | Arrhenius creep rate | s⁻¹ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PV Module Design:** Engineers use wind/snow load data (per ASCE 7 / IS 875) combined with **IEC 61215** mechanical load test (2400 Pa wind + 5400 Pa snow safety factor) to size module frames, glass thickness, and polymer backsheet mechanical ratings.
- **Polymer Compounding:** Knowing the strain rate and temperature window allows compounders to set the **crosslink density** of EVA encapsulant to balance creep resistance and processability.
- **Failure Analysis:** Yellowing, delamination, and backsheet cracking in field-aged modules are almost always traced back to a specific loading condition (UV + thermal cycling + mechanical creep).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** When answering any question on polymer behavior, *always* mention that polymer response is **time, temperature, and rate dependent** — this single sentence can earn 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wind Pressure on a PV Module Surface

The dynamic pressure exerted by a fluid (air) moving at velocity *v* against a flat surface is derived from Bernoulli's principle.

**Step 1:** Bernoulli's equation between a free-stream point (1) and a stagnation point on the module surface (2):
$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2$$

**Step 2:** At the stagnation point on the surface, the fluid velocity becomes zero: $v_2 = 0$. At the free stream, $P_1$ is the static atmospheric pressure and $v_1 = v$.

$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2 = P_2 + 0$$

**Step 3:** The *dynamic* pressure (the additional load on the module) is the difference:
$$P_{\text{dyn}} = P_2 - P_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2$$

**Step 4:** For air at sea level, $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225$ kg/m³, and design wind velocity is typically *v* = 30 m/s (≈ 108 km/h, severe storm).

**Step 5:** Substitute numerical values:
$$P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (30)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900$$

$$P_{\text{wind}} = 551.25 \text{ Pa} \approx 551 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to the more common module-test unit of Pascals (1 Pa = 1 N/m²) and compare with IEC 61215 test load of 2400 Pa. This wind speed alone gives only ~551 Pa, but **gust factors** (typically 1.5–2.5) and the **shape coefficient** $C_f$ (1.3–2.0 for tilted panels) amplify the design load to ~2400 Pa, matching the standard test.

$$P_{\text{design}} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot C_f \cdot G$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Creep Strain Using the Kelvin-Voigt Model

The Kelvin-Voigt model places a spring (E) and dashpot (η) in **parallel**, so both experience the same strain:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 1:** Apply a constant stress $\sigma_0$ at $t = 0$ (creep loading). The differential equation becomes:

$$\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange into standard linear form:

$$\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \dfrac{E}{\eta} \cdot \varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 3:** This is a first-order linear ODE. Let $\tau = \eta / E$ (the **retardation time**).

**Step 4:** Solve using integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\left( \varepsilon \cdot e^{t/\tau} \right) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot e^{t/\tau}$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from 0 to *t*:

$$\varepsilon(t) \cdot e^{t/\tau} - \varepsilon(0) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot \tau \cdot \left( e^{t/\tau} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 6:** Apply initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$ (no instantaneous strain in Kelvin-Voigt — this is a model limitation):

$$\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

**Step 7:** The **creep compliance** is therefore:
$$J(t) = \dfrac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \dfrac{1}{E} \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

**Step 8:** Limiting behavior:
- As $t \to 0$: $J(0) = 0$ (instantaneous elastic response of pure spring is missing in KV — fix is to add Maxwell in series, i.e., the Standard Linear Solid model).
- As $t \to \infty$: $J(\infty) = 1/E$ (fully relaxed spring strain).

**Step 9:** Physical interpretation: For EVA encapsulant in a field-aged module, $\tau$ may be on the order of **hours to days** at 85 °C, which is why long-term creep must be modeled, not short-term elasticity alone.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Stress from CTE Mismatch in a PV Laminate

A PV module consists of stacked layers: **glass / EVA / Si-cell / EVA / backsheet**. Each layer has a different coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE). On cooling from the lamination temperature (~150 °C) to room temperature, differential contraction generates **thermally induced stress**.

**Step 1:** For a bilayer (simplified to glass + backsheet) bonded together, the force balance at the interface requires that the total force = 0 (no external load):

$$\sigma_g \cdot A_g + \sigma_b \cdot A_b = 0$$

**Step 2:** Strain compatibility at the interface:

$$\varepsilon_g - \alpha_g \cdot \Delta T = \varepsilon_b - \alpha_b \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3:** For symmetric (equal area) layers, $A_g = A_b$, so $\sigma_g = -\sigma_b$. Using Hooke's law in the strains:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_g}{E_g} - \alpha_g \cdot \Delta T = -\dfrac{\sigma_g}{E_b} - \alpha_b \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\sigma_g$:

$$\sigma_g \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{E_g} + \dfrac{1}{E_b} \right) = (\alpha_b - \alpha_g) \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\sigma_g = \dfrac{(\alpha_b - \alpha_g) \cdot \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_g} + \dfrac{1}{E_b}}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example:
- Glass: $\alpha_g = 9 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $E_g = 70$ GPa
- Backsheet (PVDF): $\alpha_b = 120 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $E_b = 2$ GPa
- $\Delta T = -125$ °C (cooling from 150 °C to 25 °C)

**Step 6:** Compute the denominator:

$$\dfrac{1}{E_g} + \dfrac{1}{E_b} = \dfrac{1}{70} + \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.01429 + 0.5 = 0.5143 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

**Step 7:** Numerator:
$$(\alpha_b - \alpha_g) \cdot \Delta T = (120 - 9) \times 10^{-6} \times (-125) = 1.3875 \times 10^{-2} \text{ /°C} \cdot \text{°C} = -0.013875$$

**Step 8:** Final stress on glass:
$$\sigma_g = \dfrac{-0.013875}{0.5143} = -0.02698 \text{ GPa} \approx -27 \text{ MPa (compression on glass)}$$

**Step 9:** The EVA encapsulant (low $E$, high compliance) accommodates this differential strain through **shear deformation** at the interface, which is precisely why EVA is the industry-standard encapsulant — its low modulus at lamination temperature allows stress relaxation during the lamination process itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This thermal stress is *additive* to wind and snow mechanical loads, which is why IEC 61215 requires combined **mechanical + thermal cycling** tests (TC50, DH1000, HF10 sequences).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Loading Type vs Polymer Response

| Loading Type | Governing Equation | Polymer Material Property Tested | Critical for PV Component |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Tensile strength, Young's modulus | Backsheet, frame |
| Compressive | $\sigma = -F/A$ | Compressive modulus | Mounting clamps |
| Shear | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Shear strength | EVA/glass interface |
| Bending | $\sigma = My/I$ | Flexural strength | Module glass |
| Torsional | $\tau = Tr/J$ | Torsional modulus | Tracker drives |
| Creep | $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_0/E)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Creep compliance J(t) | Encapsulant |
| Stress relaxation | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ | Relaxation modulus E(t) | Bolted joints |
| Fatigue | $N_f = a \cdot \sigma_a^{-b}$ | S-N curve | Wind-vibrated mounts |
| Impact | $\sigma = \rho c \Delta v$ | Impact toughness (Izod/Charpy) | Front glass |
| Thermal | $\varepsilon = \alpha \Delta T$ | CTE | All laminate layers |
| Wind | $P = 0.5 \rho v^2$ | Pressure resistance | Module surface |
| Snow | $P = \rho g h$ | Load-bearing capacity | Module surface |
| UV | Photon flux $\phi$ | UV degradation kinetics | Backsheet, EVA |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Taxonomy of Loading Conditions on a PV Module

A block-level functional architecture mapping the *sources* of loading to the *material responses* and the *failure modes* they induce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EXTERNAL LOAD SOURCES] --> B[Mechanical Loads]
    A --> C[Thermal Loads]
    A --> D[Environmental Loads]
    A --> E[Operational Loads]

    B --> B1[Wind pressure]
    B --> B2[Snow weight]
    B --> B3[Seismic vibration]
    B --> B4[Hail impact]
    B --> B5[Installer handling]

    C --> C1[Steady state temperature 85C]
    C --> C2[Thermal cycling 40C to 85C]
    C --> C3[Gradient across laminate]
    C --> C4[Lamination cooling]

    D --> D1[Humidity and moisture]
    D --> D2[UV radiation]
    D --> D3[Chemical mist salt acid]
    D --> D4[Dust and soiling]

    E --> E1[Electrical bias]
    E --> E2[Potential induced degradation]
    E --> E3[Hot spot heating]

    B1 --> F[MATERIAL RESPONSES]
    B2 --> F
    B3 --> F
    B4 --> F
    B5 --> F
    C1 --> F
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> F1[Elastic deformation]
    F --> F2[Plastic deformation]
    F --> F3[Viscoelastic creep]
    F --> F4[Stress relaxation]
    F --> F5[Fatigue damage]
    F --> F6[Brittle fracture]
    F --> F7[Chemical degradation]

    F1 --> G[FAILURE MODES]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    F7 --> G

    G --> G1[Backsheet cracking]
    G --> G2[Encapsulant delamination]
    G --> G3[Cell microcracks]
    G --> G4[Junction box failure]
    G --> G5[EVA yellowing and browning]
    G --> G6[Glass breakage]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Layered Cross-Section of a PV Module Showing Load Transfer Path

A schematic of the laminate stack and how wind/snow loads are transmitted from the front glass through each polymer layer to the frame and mounting structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Top layer: Tempered glass 3.2 mm] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2: EVA encapsulant front 0.45 mm] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3: Crystalline silicon cell 200 um] --> L4
    L4[Layer 4: EVA encapsulant rear 0.45 mm] --> L5
    L5[Layer 5: Polymer backsheet PVDF or TPT 0.3 mm] --> L6
    L6[Layer 6: Aluminum frame and sealant silicone] --> L7
    L7[Layer 7: Mounting structure and racking]

    L1 -. Wind snow load enters .-> MECH[MECHANICAL PATH]
    L5 -. CTE mismatch .-> THERM[THERMAL PATH]
    L2 -. Adhesion bond .-> SHEAR[SHEAR PATH]
    L3 -. Cell stress .-> BEND[BENDING PATH]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Viscoelastic Constitutive Models (Schematic of Mechanical Analogs)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[POLYMER VISCOELASTIC MODELS] --> A1[Maxwell Model]
    A --> A2[Kelvin-Voigt Model]
    A --> A3[Standard Linear Solid Model]

    A1 --> A1D[Spring E and Dashpot eta in SERIES]
    A1D --> A1E[Captures stress relaxation and fluid flow]
    A1E --> A1F[Fails to predict creep recovery]

    A2 --> A2D[Spring E and Dashpot eta in PARALLEL]
    A2D --> A2E[Captures creep and delayed elasticity]
    A2E --> A2F[Fails to predict instantaneous elastic strain]

    A3 --> A3D[Maxwell arm in series with Kelvin-Voigt arm]
    A3D --> A3E[Captures full viscoelastic spectrum]
    A3E --> A3F[Industry standard for polymer encapsulants]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Tree for Selecting Polymer Test Based on Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START: Identify dominant loading] --> Q1{Is the load constant in time?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Is strain constant or stress constant?}
    Q2 -- Stress constant --> CR[CREEP TEST ASTM D2990]
    Q2 -- Strain constant --> SR[STRESS RELAXATION TEST ASTM D6048]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{Is the load cyclic?}
    Q3 -- YES --> FT[FATIGUE TEST ASTM D7791]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Is the load sudden impact?}
    Q4 -- YES --> IM[IMPACT TEST Izod Charpy ASTM D256 D6110]
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5{Is the load temperature driven?}
    Q5 -- YES --> TM[THERMAL MECHANICAL TEST DMA TMA IEC 61215]
    Q5 -- NO --> ST[STATIC TENSILE OR FLEXURAL ASTM D638 D790]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** In board exams, a single labeled flow diagram of load classification (Diagram 1) can earn **3–4 marks** if drawn cleanly. Practice drawing it from memory.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "loading conditions" as applied to a photovoltaic module. List four major categories of loading. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]

**Model Answer:**

> Loading conditions refer to the complete spectrum of mechanical, thermal, environmental, and operational stresses experienced by a polymer material or PV module during its service life. The four major categories are:
> 1. **Mechanical loading** — tension, compression, shear, bending, torsion (e.g., wind, snow).
> 2. **Thermal loading** — steady-state, cyclic, and gradient temperature variations.
> 3. **Environmental loading** — humidity, UV radiation, chemical exposure, dust.
> 4. **Dynamic/time-dependent loading** — creep, stress relaxation, fatigue, impact.
>
> Together these define the design and qualification envelope per standards such as **IEC 61215** and **IEC 61730**. **[3 marks]**

### Question 2: Differentiate between creep and stress relaxation in polymers. State the constitutive equation for the Kelvin-Voigt model. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Creep | Stress Relaxation |
|---|---|---|
| Maintained quantity | Constant **stress** | Constant **strain** |
| Observed quantity | Strain increases with time | Stress decreases with time |
| Loading type | Sustained load (e.g., weight) | Fixed deformation (e.g., stretched band) |
| Test standard | ASTM D2990 | ASTM D6048 |

Kelvin-Voigt constitutive equation:
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

Under constant stress $\sigma_0$, the creep strain evolves as:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} \left( 1 - e^{-Et/\eta} \right)$$

** [3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [14 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Classify the various loading conditions encountered by a polymer-based photovoltaic module during its service life. Describe in detail the wind and snow loading with their governing equations. **[7 marks]**
> **(b)** A crystalline silicon PV module of dimensions 1.6 m × 1.0 m is mounted on a rooftop. The design wind velocity is 30 m/s. Compute the design wind pressure on the module assuming a shape coefficient $C_f = 1.5$ and a gust factor $G = 2.0$. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Comment on the result with respect to the IEC 61215 design load of 2400 Pa. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Classification of Loading Conditions** (write as a labeled diagram or bulleted list):

1. **Mechanical Loads**
   - Static: dead weight of module, snow weight.
   - Quasi-static: wind pressure.
   - Dynamic: gust, seismic, hail impact, handling.
2. **Thermal Loads**
   - Steady-state operating temperature (~85 °C cell).
   - Diurnal/seasonal cycling (–40 °C to +85 °C).
   - Lamination cooling from 150 °C.
   - Spatial gradient across the laminate.
3. **Environmental Loads**
   - Humidity (85 % RH per IEC 61215 DH test).
   - UV-A + UV-B exposure.
   - Salt mist (coastal), ammonia (agricultural), acid rain.
   - Dust and soiling (adds mass, causes hotspots).
4. **Operational Loads**
   - Electrical bias (system voltage stress → PID).
   - Hot-spot heating.
   - Tracker actuation torque.

**Wind Loading (Bernoulli's principle):**
$$P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot v^2$$

This is the **dynamic pressure** of air. On a tilted module, the effective pressure is modified by a **shape/force coefficient** $C_f$ (typically 1.3 to 2.0). For design, a **gust factor** *G* (1.5 to 2.5) is applied to account for transient fluctuations:
$$P_{\text{design}} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot C_f \cdot G$$

**Snow Loading (hydrostatic analogy):**
$$P_{\text{snow}} = \rho_{\text{snow}} \cdot g \cdot h$$

Typical values: $\rho_{\text{snow}} = 100$ to $500$ kg/m³ depending on density, $h$ = design snow depth (m), $g = 9.81$ m/s². The IEC 61215 mechanical load test uses **5400 Pa** as the safety-margin equivalent of severe snow load.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $v = 30$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_f = 1.5$, $G = 2.0$

**Step 1:** Compute the basic dynamic wind pressure.
$$P_{\text{base}} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot (30)^2$$
$$P_{\text{base}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 900 = 551.25 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the shape coefficient and gust factor.
$$P_{\text{design}} = P_{\text{base}} \cdot C_f \cdot G = 551.25 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 2.0$$
$$P_{\text{design}} = 551.25 \cdot 3.0 = 1653.75 \text{ Pa} \approx 1654 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with IEC 61215 design load of 2400 Pa.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Calculated design wind pressure | **1654 Pa** |
| IEC 61215 wind test load | **2400 Pa** |
| Margin (IEC − Calculated) | **746 Pa (~31 %)** |

**Step 4:** Commentary.

The calculated design pressure (1654 Pa) is *below* the IEC 61215 wind test load of 2400 Pa. This **safety margin of ~31 %** is intentional in the standard to account for:
- Higher wind speeds at certain geographic sites (e.g., 35–40 m/s cyclonic winds).
- Combined loading (wind + snow acting simultaneously, which IEC 61215 covers via the 5400 Pa mechanical test).
- Aging of sealant and degradation of mechanical fasteners over 25 years.
- Edge effects and pressure non-uniformity.

**Conclusion:** The module is **adequately designed** for the specified wind condition, with the IEC 61215 standard providing a robust safety envelope. The 2400 Pa is the design verification load, not the expected service load. **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B: [14 Marks] — Internal Choice Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **Maxwell** and **Kelvin-Voigt** viscoelastic models for polymers. Derive the creep strain expression for the Kelvin-Voigt model under constant stress. **[7 marks]**
> **(b)** An EVA encapsulant is subjected to a constant tensile stress of 0.5 MPa at 50 °C. Its elastic modulus is 5 MPa and viscosity is $2 \times 10^7$ Pa·s. Compute: (i) the retardation time $\tau$, (ii) the creep strain after 100 hours, and (iii) the asymptotic maximum strain. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Maxwell Model:** Spring (E) and dashpot (η) in **series**.
- Diagram: [Spring]—[Dashpot]
- Strain is additive: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}} + \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}$
- Strain rate: $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$
- **Captures:** Stress relaxation, viscous flow.
- **Fails to capture:** Creep recovery (instantaneous elastic response is missing in the dashpot after unloading).

**Kelvin-Voigt Model:** Spring (E) and dashpot (η) in **parallel**.
- Diagram: [Spring | Dashpot]
- Stress is additive: $\sigma = \sigma_{\text{spring}} + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}$
- Constitutive equation: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$
- **Captures:** Creep (delayed elasticity) and recovery.
- **Fails to capture:** Instantaneous elastic strain on loading.

**Derivation of Creep Strain under constant $\sigma_0$:**

Step 1: Governing equation: $\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$

Step 2: Rearrange: $\dot{\varepsilon} + (E/\eta)\varepsilon = \sigma_0 / \eta$

Step 3: Define retardation time $\tau = \eta / E$:
$$\dot{\varepsilon} + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\tau} = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

Step 4: Solve with integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$:
$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\varepsilon \cdot e^{t/\tau}\right) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot e^{t/\tau}$$

Step 5: Integrate from 0 to *t*, with $\varepsilon(0) = 0$:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

This is the **creep strain** of the Kelvin-Voigt model.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $\sigma_0 = 0.5$ MPa = $0.5 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $E = 5$ MPa = $5 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $\eta = 2 \times 10^7$ Pa·s
- $t = 100$ hours = $100 \times 3600 = 3.6 \times 10^5$ s

**Step (i): Retardation time**
$$\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^7}{5 \times 10^6} = 4 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step (ii): Creep strain at t = 100 hours**

Compute the dimensionless ratio $t/\tau$:
$$\dfrac{t}{\tau} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^5}{4} = 9 \times 10^4$$

Since $t/\tau = 90{,}000 \gg 1$, the exponential term $e^{-90000} \approx 0$ (effectively zero).

$$\varepsilon(100 \text{ hr}) = \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^6} \cdot (1 - 0) = 0.1 = 10\%$$

**Step (iii): Asymptotic maximum strain**
$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \varepsilon(t \to \infty) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} = \dfrac{0.5}{5} = 0.1 = 10\%$$

**Conclusion:** The EVA has already reached **asymptotic saturation** well before 100 hours (since $\tau = 4$ s is very small compared to 100 hr). The maximum achievable strain under this load is **10 %**, which is within the elastic limit of EVA (typically 100–300 % elongation at break). The material will not fail by creep at this stress level, but the design margin must consider the additional thermal cycles and UV exposure. **[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in creep problems** — Always write Pa and s explicitly; do not leave numbers "bare." *Penalty: up to 1 mark.*
> 2. **Confusing *retardation time* (Kelvin-Voigt) with *relaxation time* (Maxwell).** Retardation time governs **creep**; relaxation time governs **stress decay**.
> 3. **Using $\Delta T$ in Kelvin when CTE is given in °C⁻¹.** Since CTE has the same numerical value in K⁻¹ and °C⁻¹, this is acceptable — but the *magnitude* and *sign* of $\Delta T$ must reflect the actual cooling (negative for cool-down).
> 4. **Omitting $C_f$ and $G$ in wind-load problems.** The bare Bernoulli pressure is only the *dynamic* pressure. A 2-mark penalty applies if these coefficients are not introduced.
> 5. **Not relating the answer back to a PV-specific component** (encapsulant, backsheet, glass). KTU examiners reward "applied" answers; pure textbook derivations without PV context lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Loading conditions** = all mechanical, thermal, environmental, and operational stresses on a polymer / PV module.
- **Five canonical load types** to remember: Tension, Compression, Shear, Bending, Torsion.
- **Three time-dependent loads** to remember: Creep, Stress Relaxation, Fatigue.
- **Key governing equations** (must memorize):
  - $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (Hooke's Law)
  - $\tau = G\gamma$ (Shear)
  - $P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \rho v^2 C_f G$
  - $P_{\text{snow}} = \rho g h$
  - $\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = \alpha \Delta T$
  - Kelvin-Voigt creep: $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_0 / E)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$
  - Maxwell relaxation: $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$
- **Retardation time** $\tau = \eta / E$ — measures how fast the polymer "catches up" to applied stress.
- **IEC 61215 design load** = **2400 Pa (wind) and 5400 Pa (snow/mechanical safety)**.
- **Glass transition temperature $T_g$** dictates the polymer state: glassy (brittle) below $T_g$, rubbery (compliant) above $T_g$.
- **Polymers are viscoelastic** — their response depends on **time, temperature, and strain rate** (the "Holy Trinity" of polymer mechanics).
- **CTE mismatch** between layers is the primary driver of **thermomechanical fatigue** in PV laminates.
- **EVA encapsulant** is chosen for its low modulus at lamination temperature (~150 °C) and compliance at operating temperature.
- **Creep compliance** $J(t) = \varepsilon(t) / \sigma_0$ has units of Pa⁻¹.
- **Standard tests:** ASTM D638 (tensile), D790 (flexural), D2990 (creep), D6048 (relaxation), D256/D6110 (impact), IEC 61215 (PV module qualification).
- **Final design rule:** Always combine **mechanical + thermal + humidity + UV** loading; never analyze a single load in isolation for PV lifetime prediction.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Loading Conditions**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Photovoltaic Generator Current-Voltage Characteristics # ⚡ MODULE 2 — PHOTOVOLTAIC GENERATOR CURRENT-VOLTAGE CHARACTERISTICS ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Photovoltaic (PV) Generator Current-Voltage (I-V) Characteristics** refer to the graphical and mathematical relationship between the **output current** and the **terminal voltage** of a PV cell, module, or array under specified operating conditions of **irradiance (G, W/m²)**, **cell temperature (T, °C or K)**, and **load conditions**. In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Photovoltaic Generator** is the complete current-producing assembly (single cell → module → array), and its **I-V characteristic** is the locus of all possible (V, I) operating points that the device can deliver to an external circuit. The shape of this curve is fundamentally dictated by the **semiconductor p-n junction physics** of the solar cell, mathematically described by the **Shockley Diode Equation** modified to include photogeneration and parasitic losses. The **standard test conditions (STC)** at which PV manufacturers rate their I-V curves are: - Irradiance: **G = 1000 W/m²** (AM 1.5G solar spectrum) - Cell temperature: **T = 25 °C (298.15 K)** - Air mass: **AM 1.5G** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model Imagine a **water tap (faucet) connected to an overhead tank** with a flexible hose at the outlet: | Real Water Tap Analogy | Photovoltaic Cell Equivalent | |---|---| | **Water pressure** at the tap outlet | **Voltage (V)** of the PV cell | | **Water flow rate** through the hose | **Current (I)** of the PV cell | | **Sunlight falling on the panel** | **Water pressure from the overhead tank** | | **A blocked tap (closed fully)** | **Open-circuit condition** — no current flows, but voltage is at its maximum (**Voc**) | | **A fully open tap with no hose attached** | **Short-circuit condition** — maximum current flows (**Isc**) but voltage collapses to zero | | **The tap position that throws water the farthest** | **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** — optimal balance of voltage × current | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Students:** A PV cell is *not* a constant current source nor a constant voltage source — it is a **non-linear, two-terminal, light-sensitive semiconductor device** whose I-V curve has a distinctive "knee" shape. This is the heart of Module 2. ### 1.3 The Three Cardinal Points on Every I-V Curve Any photovoltaic generator's I-V curve is fully characterized by three reference points that KTU examiners love to test: 1. **Short-Circuit Current (Isc)** — The current flowing when the terminals are shorted (V = 0). It is directly proportional to the incident irradiance. 2. **Open-Circuit Voltage (Voc)** — The voltage across the terminals when no current flows (I = 0). It is logarithmically dependent on irradiance and inversely dependent on temperature. 3. **Maximum Power Point (Imp, Vmp)** — The single operating point on the curve where the product **V × I = P** reaches its peak (**Pmax**). > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Recall Mnemonic:** **"S-O-M"** → **S**hort-circuit, **O**pen-circuit, **M**aximum Power Point. Always label these three points on any I-V curve you draw in your exam answer sheet. --- # 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Equivalent Circuit of an Ideal PV Cell A real photovoltaic cell is modelled electrically as a **four-component equivalent circuit**. KTU examiners expect you to draw this circuit and label every element precisely. The four building blocks are: 1. **Photocurrent Source (IL or Iph)** — A current source whose magnitude is directly proportional to the incident photon flux. Symbolized as a circle with an arrow inside. 2. **Diode (D)** — Represents the p-n junction of the semiconductor, biased in forward conduction by the terminal voltage. Models the recombination losses. 3. **Series Resistance (Rs)** — A small resistance in series, modelling the ohmic losses in the semiconductor bulk, metallic contacts, and interconnect ribbons. 4. **Shunt (Parallel) Resistance (Rsh)** — A large resistance in parallel with the diode, modelling the leakage current paths along the cell edges and through crystalline defects. ### 2.2 Derivation of the Governing I-V Equation The fundamental KCL (Kirchhoff's Current Law) applied at the top node of the equivalent circuit gives: $$I = I_L - I_D - I_{sh}$$ **Step-by-Step Logic:** - **I** = terminal current delivered to the external load (the quantity plotted on the y-axis of the I-V curve). - **I_L** = light-generated (photocurrent) current. **I_L ≈ Isc** under strong irradiance. - **I_D** = diode current following the **Shockley Diode Equation**: $I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$ - **I_sh** = current through the shunt resistance: $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ - **I_0** = diode reverse saturation current (dark current), typically **10⁻⁹ to 10⁻¹² A** for crystalline silicon. ### 2.3 Boundary Condition Analysis | Operating Mode | Condition | Simplified Equation | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---| | **Short Circuit** | V = 0 | $I_{sc} = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qI_{sc}R_s}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{I_{sc}R_s}{R_{sh}}$ | For $R_s \to 0$ and $R_{sh} \to \infty$, $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ | | **Open Circuit** | I = 0 | $0 = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}$ | Used to derive the **Voc equation** below | | **Maximum Power** | $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$ | $\dfrac{d(IV)}{dV} = I + V\dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0$ | Used to locate the **MPP** coordinates (Imp, Vmp) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When solving numerical problems, the assumption **$R_s \to 0$** and **$R_{sh} \to \infty$** (the "ideal cell" approximation) is almost always used unless explicitly stated otherwise. Under this assumption, $I_{sc} = I_L$ exactly. ### 2.4 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table) > [!NOTE] > **Notation Convention:** All vertical bars in formulas have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | KTU Usage | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **General PV I-V Equation** | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | I [A], V [V], q = 1.602×10⁻¹⁹ C, k = 1.381×10⁻²³ J/K, n = ideality factor (1–2), T [K] | Master equation — must be memorized | | 2 | **Ideal Cell I-V Equation** (Rs=0, Rsh=∞) | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$ | Same as above | Used in **70% of KTU problems** | | 3 | **Open-Circuit Voltage** | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Voc [V] | Critical for **maximum voltage** calculations | | 4 | **Short-Circuit Current** | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ | Isc [A] | Directly proportional to **irradiance G** | | 5 | **Thermal Voltage** | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV at } 300\text{ K}$ | VT [V] | Used inside the diode exponential | | 6 | **Fill Factor (FF)** | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Dimensionless, 0 < FF < 1 | Measures **"squareness"** of I-V curve | | 7 | **Maximum Power** | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ | Pmax [W] | The peak of the **P-V curve** | | 8 | **Conversion Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}$ | η [%], Pin = G×A [W], A [m²] | Key **figure of merit** for PV cells | | 9 | **Irradiance Scaling (Isc)** | $I_{sc}(G) = I_{sc,STC} \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_{STC}}$ | G [W/m²] | Linear relationship | | 10 | **Temperature Scaling (Voc)** | $V_{oc}(T) = V_{oc,STC} + \dfrac{dV_{oc}}{dT}(T - T_{STC})$ | $\dfrac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx -2.3 \text{ mV/°C per cell}$ (for c-Si) | Voc **decreases** with temperature | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Why does KTU test this topic heavily?** Because the I-V characteristic is the **"fingerprint"** of every solar panel. Engineers use it to: > - **Size inverter ratings** (must handle Voc at coldest expected temperature — a classic Kerala winter morning scenario). > - **Design MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) algorithms** for solar charge controllers and grid-tie inverters. > - **Predict energy yield** of a proposed solar installation under local irradiance profiles (Kerala averages **4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day**). > - **Diagnose faulty modules** — a "squared-off" curve indicates high series resistance, while a "sloped Isc" indicates shunt leakage. --- # 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: From KCL to the Ideal I-V Equation **Starting Point:** KCL at the top node of the equivalent circuit. $$I = I_L - I_D - I_{sh}$$ **Substitute the Shockley Diode Current Expression:** The diode current flowing from p-side to n-side due to the terminal voltage V is: $$I_D = I_0 \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV_D}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$ where the **diode voltage $V_D$** is not equal to the terminal voltage V — it is offset by the voltage drop across the series resistance: $$V_D = V + I R_s$$ **Substitute the Shunt Current Expression:** Ohm's law across the shunt resistor: $$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$ **Assemble the Full Equation:** $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$ This is the **most general** form. For the **ideal cell** ($R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$), the last two terms simplify: $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Open-Circuit Voltage Expression **Condition:** At open circuit, the terminal current is zero: $I = 0$. **Apply to the ideal cell equation:** $$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$ **Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:** $$I_0 \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] = I_L$$ **Divide both sides by $I_0$:** $$\exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 = \dfrac{I_L}{I_0}$$ **Add 1 to both sides:** $$\exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) = \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1$$ **Take the natural logarithm of both sides:** $$\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT} = \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$ **Solve for $V_{oc}$:** $$V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** $V_{oc}$ depends **logarithmically** on $I_L/I_0$. Since $I_0$ is highly temperature-sensitive (it roughly doubles for every **10 °C rise**), Voc drops noticeably with rising temperature — this is why solar panels lose efficiency on hot Kerala afternoons. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Fill Factor and Maximum Power **Definition:** Fill Factor is the ratio of the maximum rectangle area inscribed under the I-V curve to the total bounding rectangle. **Maximum power condition:** $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$ where $P = IV$. **Apply the product rule:** $$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = \dfrac{d(IV)}{dV} = I + V\dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0$$ **For the ideal cell equation**, differentiate $I$ with respect to $V$: $$\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -\dfrac{q I_0}{nkT}\exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) = -\dfrac{q}{nkT}(I + I_L + I_0)$$ **Substitute into the maximum power condition:** $$I_{mp} - V_{mp} \cdot \dfrac{q}{nkT}(I_{mp} + I_L + I_0) = 0$$ This **transcendental equation** cannot be solved algebraically in closed form; it is solved numerically using Lambert W functions or graphically. KTU problems typically **provide** the values of $V_{mp}$ and $I_{mp}$ and ask you to compute FF and $\eta$. **The Fill Factor definition:** $$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$ **The maximum power:** $$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Irradiance and Temperature Scaling Laws ### 3.4.1 Effect of Irradiance on Isc (Linear) The photocurrent $I_L$ is generated by the absorption of photons. Each photon above the bandgap energy generates one electron-hole pair. Hence: $$I_L \propto G \text{ (irradiance in W/m²)}$$ $$I_{sc}(G) = I_{sc,STC} \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_{STC}}$$ **Numerical Example:** If $I_{sc,STC} = 8.0$ A at $G_{STC} = 1000$ W/m², then at $G = 600$ W/m² (cloudy Kerala morning): $$I_{sc}(600) = 8.0 \times \dfrac{600}{1000} = 4.8 \text{ A}$$ ### 3.4.2 Effect of Temperature on Voc (Negative Linear Coefficient) $$V_{oc}(T) = V_{oc,STC} + \beta_{V_{oc}} \cdot (T - T_{STC})$$ For crystalline silicon, $\beta_{V_{oc}} \approx -2.3$ mV/°C **per cell**. For a 36-cell module: $$\beta_{V_{oc},module} = -2.3 \times 36 = -82.8 \text{ mV/°C}$$ **Numerical Example:** Module Voc at STC = 22.0 V; operating temperature = 60 °C: $$V_{oc}(60°C) = 22.0 + (-0.0828)(60 - 25) = 22.0 - 2.90 = 19.10 \text{ V}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Formula to Use?" | Given in KTU Problem | Required Output | Use This Formula | |---|---|---| | $I_L$, $I_0$, n, T, V | I (terminal current) | $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$ | | $I_L$, $I_0$, n, T | $V_{oc}$ | $V_{oc} = (nkT/q)\ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$ | | $V_{mp}$, $I_{mp}$, $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$ | FF, $P_{max}$ | $FF = V_{mp}I_{mp}/(V_{oc}I_{sc})$ | | FF, $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, G, A | $\eta$ | $\eta = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} / (G \cdot A)$ | | Two irradiance levels, $I_{sc,1}$ | $I_{sc,2}$ | Linear scaling: $I_{sc,2} = I_{sc,1}(G_2/G_1)$ | | $V_{oc,STC}$, T, cell count | $V_{oc}$ at operating T | $V_{oc}(T) = V_{oc,STC} + N_s \cdot \beta(T - 25)$ | --- # 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Equivalent Circuit of a Real PV Cell (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR node_src["IL
Photocurrent Source"]:::source node_d["D
Diode"]:::diode node_rs["Rs
Series Resistance"]:::resistor node_rsh["Rsh
Shunt Resistance"]:::resistor termP(["+ Terminal V"]):::terminal termN(["- Terminal"]):::terminal node_src --- termP node_rs --- termP termN --- node_rsh termN --- node_src node_d --- node_rs classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef diode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef terminal fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The photocurrent source $I_L$ drives current upward through the diode branch (containing $R_s$ and the diode D) and the parallel shunt branch (containing $R_{sh}$). The remainder exits at the + terminal as the load current $I$. --- ## 4.2 Functional Architecture: From Cell → Module → Array ```mermaid flowchart TB cell1["Cell 1
0.5 V, 8 A"]:::cell cell2["Cell 2
0.5 V, 8 A"]:::cell cell3["Cell N
0.5 V, 8 A"]:::cell module["MODULE
~36 cells in series
~18 V, 8 A, 144 Wp"]:::module arr1["String 1
10 modules series"]:::string arr2["String 2
10 modules series"]:::string array["PV ARRAY
180 V, 16 A
~2.88 kWp"]:::array inv["INVERTER
MPPT Input"]:::inv cell1 --> cell2 --> cell3 --> module module --> arr1 module --> arr2 arr1 --> array arr2 --> array array --> inv classDef cell fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef module fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef string fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#FF6347,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef array fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef inv fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 I-V Curve Anatomy with Key Reference Points ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ivcurve["I-V CHARACTERISTIC CURVE (I vs V)"] origin["(0, 0)"]:::axis isc["Isc
Y-intercept
V=0, I=Isc"]:::pp voc["Voc
X-intercept
I=0, V=Voc"]:::pp mpp["MPP
Vmp, Imp
Pmax = Vmp x Imp"]:::mpp curve["Curved 'knee' region
exponential decay"]:::curve end origin --- isc isc --- curve curve --- mpp mpp --- voc voc --- origin classDef axis fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px classDef pp fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef mpp fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef curve fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Drawing Tip:** Always draw the I-V curve with **Isc on the y-axis** (when V is on x-axis) OR **invert the axes** (with V on y-axis, I on x-axis). The latter is the European/IEC convention. State your axis convention explicitly at the start of the answer. --- ## 4.4 Effect of Varying Irradiance and Temperature on the I-V Curve ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph irr["Effect of Increasing Irradiance G"] d1["Low G: 200 W/m^2
Isc_small, Voc_slightly_less"]:::low d2["Medium G: 600 W/m^2
Isc_medium"]:::med d3["High G: 1000 W/m^2
Isc_max, Voc_max"]:::high end subgraph temp["Effect of Increasing Temperature T"] t1["Low T: 0 C
Voc_max, Isc_slightly_more"]:::cold t2["Medium T: 25 C"]:::med t3["High T: 75 C
Voc_less, Isc_slightly_more"]:::hot end d1 --> d2 --> d3 t1 --> t2 --> t3 classDef low fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef med fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#FFA500,color:#000 classDef high fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 classDef cold fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef hot fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Trends to Memorize for KTU:** > - ↑ Irradiance ⇒ ↑ Isc (linearly) and ↑ Voc (logarithmically, small effect) > - ↑ Temperature ⇒ ↓ Voc (significantly, ~−2.3 mV/°C per cell) and ↑ Isc (very small positive effect, ~+0.05%/°C) > - Net effect: **Efficiency drops with rising temperature** — important for rooftop solar design in tropical Kerala. --- # 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` **Define the following terms with reference to a photovoltaic cell:** **(i)** Short-circuit current (Isc), **(ii)** Open-circuit voltage (Voc), **(iii)** Fill Factor (FF). **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Term | Definition | Key Point | |---|---|---| | **(i) Isc** | The current delivered by a PV cell when its terminals are connected together (V = 0). | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ (the photogenerated current). It is **directly proportional** to irradiance. | | **(ii) Voc** | The voltage across the terminals of a PV cell when no current flows (I = 0, open circuit). | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$. It is **inversely proportional** to temperature. | | **(iii) Fill Factor** | The ratio of maximum obtainable power to the product of Isc and Voc. | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$. Typical c-Si: 0.7–0.85. | --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **State the equivalent circuit of a real photovoltaic cell and write the governing I-V equation. Mention the significance of Rs and Rsh.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The equivalent circuit consists of a **photocurrent source (IL)** in parallel with a **diode (D)** and a **shunt resistance (Rsh)**, with a **series resistance (Rs)** in the current path. The governing equation is: $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$ - **Rs (Series Resistance):** Models ohmic losses in semiconductor bulk, contacts, and interconnects. **Higher Rs → degrades FF and Pmax** (curve becomes more "rounded" at the knee). - **Rsh (Shunt Resistance):** Models leakage paths along cell edges and pinholes. **Lower Rsh → causes slope in the Isc region** (curve "tilts" downward near the short-circuit point). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Choice 1]` **(a)** Draw the equivalent circuit of a real photovoltaic cell and derive the ideal I-V equation starting from Kirchhoff's Current Law. Explain the significance of each component. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A silicon solar cell has the following parameters at 300 K: $I_L = 3.2$ A, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ A, ideality factor $n = 1.3$, and the cell area is $144$ cm². Calculate: **(i)** Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$, **(ii)** The maximum power assuming a fill factor of 0.78, **(iii)** The conversion efficiency at an irradiance of 1000 W/m². **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Draw the equivalent circuit (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1**. The circuit contains: - A current source $I_L$ (photogenerated current) - A diode D (p-n junction of the cell) - A series resistance $R_s$ (ohmic losses) - A shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ (leakage paths) **Step 2 — Apply KCL (2 marks):** At the top node of the circuit: $$I = I_L - I_D - I_{sh}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute component equations (2 marks):** $$I_D = I_0 \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$ $$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$ Therefore: $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$ **Step 4 — Ideal cell simplification (1 mark):** For an ideal cell, $R_s \to 0$ and $R_{sh} \to \infty$: $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$ **Significance of Each Component:** | Component | Significance | |---|---| | $I_L$ | Represents the **photogenerated carriers** from absorbed sunlight. Sets the **upper bound** on current. | | Diode D | Represents the **p-n junction rectification**; allows the Shockley recombination current. | | $R_s$ | Represents **ohmic/resistive losses**; degrades fill factor and efficiency. | | $R_{sh}$ | Represents **leakage paths**; reduces $V_{oc}$ if too small. | --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks** **Given Data:** - $I_L = 3.2$ A - $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ A - $n = 1.3$ - $T = 300$ K - $A = 144$ cm² $= 144 \times 10^{-4}$ m² $= 0.0144$ m² - $G = 1000$ W/m² - $FF = 0.78$ - Boltzmann constant $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K - Electron charge $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C **Part (i) — Open-Circuit Voltage Calculation (3 marks):** Using the Voc formula: $$V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$ Compute the thermal voltage factor: $$\dfrac{nkT}{q} = \dfrac{1.3 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$ $$\dfrac{nkT}{q} = \dfrac{5.3859 \times 10^{-21}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.03362 \text{ V} = 33.62 \text{ mV}$$ Compute the logarithmic argument: $$\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 = \dfrac{3.2}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} + 1 = 2.1333 \times 10^{9} + 1 \approx 2.1333 \times 10^{9}$$ $$\ln(2.1333 \times 10^{9}) = \ln(2.1333) + 9\ln(10) = 0.7577 + 20.7233 = 21.481$$ Therefore: $$V_{oc} = 0.03362 \times 21.481 = 0.7223 \text{ V}$$ **$\boxed{V_{oc} \approx 0.722 \text{ V}}$** **Part (ii) — Maximum Power Calculation (2 marks):** Since the cell is ideal ($R_s = 0$), $I_{sc} = I_L = 3.2$ A. $$P_{max} = FF \times V_{oc} \times I_{sc} = 0.78 \times 0.7223 \times 3.2$$ $$P_{max} = 0.78 \times 2.311 = 1.803 \text{ W}$$ **$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 1.80 \text{ W}}$** **Part (iii) — Conversion Efficiency (2 marks):** Input power: $$P_{in} = G \times A = 1000 \times 0.0144 = 14.4 \text{ W}$$ Efficiency: $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{1.803}{14.4} = 0.1252 = 12.52\%$$ **$\boxed{\eta \approx 12.5\%}$** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Choice 2]` **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the current-voltage characteristics of a photovoltaic cell. Mark and explain the significance of the maximum power point (MPP), short-circuit current (Isc), and open-circuit voltage (Voc). **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A PV module consists of 36 silicon cells connected in series. At STC, each cell has $V_{oc} = 0.62$ V and $I_{sc} = 3.5$ A. The temperature coefficient of $V_{oc}$ is $-2.3$ mV/°C per cell. If the cell operating temperature rises to 65 °C while irradiance drops to 750 W/m², calculate the **revised module-level $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Sketch the I-V curve (3 marks):** Draw axes with **V on x-axis** and **I on y-axis** (or the inverted convention if you prefer). Plot the characteristic "L-shaped" curve. Mark three cardinal points: - **Isc** at the **y-intercept** (when V = 0) - **Voc** at the **x-intercept** (when I = 0) - **MPP** at the **"knee"** of the curve where $P = VI$ is maximum Also draw the **P-V curve** below the I-V curve, showing a parabolic shape with its peak at the same voltage as MPP. **Step 2 — Explain Isc (1 mark):** $I_{sc}$ is the current when terminals are shorted. Determined entirely by the photogeneration rate, hence: $$I_{sc} \approx q \cdot G \cdot A \cdot \eta_{collection}$$ It scales **linearly with irradiance G**. **Step 3 — Explain Voc (1 mark):** $V_{oc}$ is the terminal voltage under zero current (open circuit). From the diode equation: $$V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$ It depends on the **logarithm of irradiance** and is **strongly affected by temperature**. **Step 4 — Explain MPP (2 marks):** The MPP is the unique point on the I-V curve where the product **P = VI** is maximized. At this point, the load resistance equals the **source resistance** of the cell (impedance matching). It is characterized by coordinates $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ and: $$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$$ MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) controllers in solar inverters continuously adjust the operating point to stay at the MPP under varying irradiance and temperature. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks** **Given Data:** - Number of cells in series: $N_s = 36$ - Per-cell STC values: $V_{oc,cell} = 0.62$ V, $I_{sc,cell} = 3.5$ A - $\beta_{V_{oc}} = -2.3$ mV/°C per cell - New temperature: $T = 65$ °C, with $T_{STC} = 25$ °C - New irradiance: $G = 750$ W/m², with $G_{STC} = 1000$ W/m² **Step 1 — Revised Voc (4 marks):** Temperature difference: $$\Delta T = T - T_{STC} = 65 - 25 = 40 \text{ °C}$$ Change in Voc per cell: $$\Delta V_{oc,cell} = \beta_{V_{oc}} \cdot \Delta T = (-2.3 \times 10^{-3}) \times 40 = -0.092 \text{ V/cell}$$ Revised per-cell Voc: $$V_{oc,cell}(65°C) = 0.62 + (-0.092) = 0.528 \text{ V/cell}$$ Module-level Voc (series connection adds voltages): $$V_{oc,module}(65°C) = N_s \times V_{oc,cell} = 36 \times 0.528 = 19.008 \text{ V}$$ **$\boxed{V_{oc,module}(65°C) \approx 19.01 \text{ V}}$** **Step 2 — Revised Isc (3 marks):** For a series-connected module, the current is the same through all cells and equals the per-cell Isc. Isc scales linearly with irradiance: $$I_{sc}(750) = I_{sc,STC} \times \dfrac{G}{G_{STC}} = 3.5 \times \dfrac{750}{1000}$$ $$I_{sc}(750) = 3.5 \times 0.75 = 2.625 \text{ A}$$ **$\boxed{I_{sc,module}(750 \text{ W/m}^2) \approx 2.63 \text{ A}}$** --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in I-V Characteristic Problems:** > 1. **Confusing axes orientation:** Many students draw V on the y-axis and I on the x-axis without labeling. Always **label axes with units** (Volts, Amperes) to avoid losing 0.5 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the Boltzmann constant units:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K — students often write $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ without the correct exponent, leading to a Voc error by **3 orders of magnitude**. > 3. **Voc scaling wrong for series connection:** Students multiply per-cell Isc by $N_s$ — **NO!** Only **voltages add** in series; current remains equal to the per-cell value. > 4. **Neglecting the "+1" inside the logarithm:** The Voc formula is $\ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$, not $\ln(I_L/I_0)$. Since $I_L/I_0 \gg 1$, this rarely matters numerically, but writing it without the "+1" is conceptually incorrect. > 5. **Mixing up temperature coefficients:** The sign convention is $\beta_{V_{oc}} < 0$ (Voc drops as T rises). Don't accidentally apply a positive sign. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Photovoltaic I-V Characteristics** - ✅ The **general I-V equation** has **four parameters**: $I_L$, $I_0$, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$ — and one **physical constant**: $kT/q$ (thermal voltage ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K). - ✅ **Isc ≈ IL** for an ideal cell; scales **linearly** with irradiance G. - ✅ **Voc** scales **logarithmically** with G and **inversely linearly** with temperature (β ≈ −2.3 mV/°C per c-Si cell). - ✅ **Fill Factor (FF)** is a measure of the **"squareness"** of the I-V curve; typical c-Si cells have FF between **0.70 and 0.85**. - ✅ **Conversion Efficiency** $\eta = (FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}) / (G \cdot A)$ — this is the **headline figure of merit** for any PV cell. - ✅ **Series resistance Rs** rounds off the **knee** of the I-V curve near Voc. - ✅ **Shunt resistance Rsh** tilts the **horizontal portion** of the I-V curve near Isc. - ✅ **Series connection** of cells adds **voltages**; **parallel connection** adds **currents**. - ✅ The **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** is where the load impedance matches the **source (internal) impedance** of the cell. - ✅ **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** G = 1000 W/m², T = 25 °C, AM 1.5G — always mention these when quoting Isc, Voc, and Pmax. - ✅ The diode ideality factor **n** is typically between 1 and 2; n = 1 for ideal diffusion-limited recombination, n = 2 for SRH (Shockley-Read-Hall) recombination-dominated cells. - ✅ **MPPT algorithms** (Perturb & Observe, Incremental Conductance) are mandatory in any commercial PV inverter to keep the system operating at or near (Vmp, Imp). ---

topics.title: Peak Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Peak Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of photovoltaic (PV) technology, **Peak Power** ($P_{max}$ or $P_{p}$) is defined as the maximum electrical power output that a solar cell, module, or PV array can deliver under a specified set of standardized operating conditions. It corresponds to a single unique operating point on the current–voltage (I–V) characteristic curve of the device, known as the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**.

Mathematically, peak power is expressed as:

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$$

where $V_{mp}$ is the voltage at maximum power and $I_{mp}$ is the current at maximum power.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Peak power is conventionally reported under **Standard Test Conditions (STC)**: irradiance $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, cell temperature $T_{cell} = 25^\circ\text{C}$, and air mass **AM 1.5G** spectrum. This standardized rating enables fair comparison between PV modules from different manufacturers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peak Power ≠ Real-World Power.** In field deployment, actual power output is invariably lower than nameplate peak power due to temperature rise, soiling, partial shading, spectral mismatch, and lower-than-STC irradiance.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bucket Faucet Model

Imagine a solar PV module as a **water tank with a smart faucet**:

- The **sunlight (irradiance)** is the water pressure pushing into the tank.
- The **voltage** is how tightly the faucet is closed — at zero voltage, current flows freely (short circuit), but power is zero. At open circuit, the faucet is fully closed — no current flows, power is again zero.
- Somewhere in between, there is a **"sweet-spot" opening** where the actual useful flow (power) is maximum. That sweet-spot is the **Maximum Power Point**.

Just as you would not run a faucet either fully open or fully closed to maximize water delivered to a fixed bucket, a PV system should not operate at $V = 0$ or $V = V_{oc}$. Instead, an **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracker)** electronic controller continuously adjusts the load on the module to lock operation at $V_{mp}$ and $I_{mp}$, delivering $P_{max}$.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (STC) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Irradiance | $G$ | **1000** | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| Cell Temperature | $T_{cell}$ | **25** | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Air Mass | AM | **1.5G** | — |
| Short-Circuit Current Density | $J_{sc}$ | 30–40 | $\text{mA/cm}^2$ |
| Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc}$ | 0.5–0.7 | $\text{V}$ (per cell) |
| Fill Factor | $FF$ | 0.6–0.85 | dimensionless |
| Module Efficiency | $\eta$ | 15–22 | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tag — CO2 / KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly addresses Course Outcome *CO2: Analyse the performance characteristics of photovoltaic devices*, mapped to Bloom's cognitive levels **L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyse)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — The I–V Curve of a Solar Cell.**
Under illumination, a solar cell produces a current that depends non-linearly on the terminal voltage. The illuminated I–V curve lies in the fourth quadrant (current positive, voltage positive) and is described by the **single-diode model (ideal case)**:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

where $I_L$ is the photo-generated current, $I_0$ is the diode reverse saturation current, $q$ is the electron charge, $n$ is the ideality factor, $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

**Step 2 — Locate the Two Boundary Points.**
- At $V = 0$: $I = I_{sc}$ (short-circuit current).
- At $I = 0$: $V = V_{oc}$ (open-circuit voltage), obtained by solving $0 = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV_{oc}/nkT) - 1]$.

**Step 3 — Form the Power Curve.**
The instantaneous electrical power delivered by the cell at any operating point is $P(V) = V \cdot I(V)$. This is the **P–V curve**, a parabola-like shape that begins at zero (short-circuit), rises to a single peak, and returns to zero (open-circuit).

**Step 4 — Identify the Peak.**
The peak of the $P(V)$ curve occurs where $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$. This condition gives the operating point $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$.

**Step 5 — Quantify How "Square" the I–V Curve Is.**
A metric called the **Fill Factor (FF)** compares $P_{max}$ to the theoretical maximum power rectangle $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$. A higher $FF$ means a more rectangular I–V curve and a higher peak power.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Boundary | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Peak power from MPP coordinates | $\text{W}$ |
| 2 | $FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor — quality of I–V curve | dimensionless |
| 3 | $FF \approx \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$ | **Empirical** FF (Green, 1981), where $v_{oc} = qV_{oc}/(nkT)$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_{max} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ | Compact form for $P_{max}$ | $\text{W}$ |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A}$ | Conversion efficiency, $A$ = module area, $G$ = irradiance | dimensionless |
| 6 | $I_{sc} \propto G$ | Short-circuit current scales linearly with irradiance | $\text{A}$ |
| 7 | $V_{oc} \propto \ln(G)$ | Open-circuit voltage depends logarithmically on $G$ | $\text{V}$ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0 \Rightarrow V_{mp}$ | Definition of MPP | — |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dI}{dV}\bigg\vert_{mp} = -\dfrac{I_{mp}}{V_{mp}}$ | Slope of I–V curve at MPP | $\text{S}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** The Fill Factor $FF$ is the single most important *quality indicator* of a solar cell after $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$. A 5% relative increase in $FF$ yields a 5% increase in $P_{max}$ for the same module area — directly translating to higher revenue and lower levelized cost of electricity (LCOE).

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

Peak power governs **every commercial transaction in PV industry**:

1. **Module datasheet rating** — the "$Wp$" (watt-peak) label on every commercial PV module is its STC peak power.
2. **System sizing** — inverter and battery sizing are matched to module peak power.
3. **Financial modelling** — Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) calculations use $P_{max}$ as the rated capacity.
4. **Grid code compliance** — utility-scale plants quote installed peak capacity in MWp (megawatt-peak).
5. **Performance ratio (PR)** — field performance is benchmarked against STC peak power.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation I — Locating the Maximum Power Point Mathematically
