Starting from the illuminated single-diode equation (ideal case, $I_L \gg I_0$):

$$I(V) = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) \right]$$

**Why we can drop the "$-1$":** Under forward bias near MPP, $\exp(qV/nkT) \gg 1$, so the $-1$ is negligible.

The instantaneous power is:

$$P(V) = V \cdot I(V) = V \cdot \left[ I_L - I_0 \, \exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Take the derivative of $P$ with respect to $V$:**

$$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = I(V) + V \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\dfrac{dI}{dV}$:**

$$\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -I_0 \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) \cdot \dfrac{q}{nkT}$$

Substituting back:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = \left[I_L - I_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right)\right] - V \cdot I_0 \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) \cdot \dfrac{q}{nkT}$$

**Step 3 — Set $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$ to locate the peak:**

$$I_L - I_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) \left[ 1 + \dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT} \right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{mp}$ implicitly:**

$$I_L = I_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) \left[ 1 + \dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Express $I_{mp}$ from the I–V equation evaluated at $V_{mp}$:**

$$I_{mp} = I_L - I_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) = \dfrac{I_L}{1 + \dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form of $P_{max}$:**

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_L}{1 + \dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}}$$

This transcendental equation must be solved iteratively. However, it reveals the **deep insight** that $V_{mp}$ is slightly less than $V_{oc}$ and $I_{mp}$ is slightly less than $I_{sc}$ — both reduced by the factor $(1 + qV_{mp}/nkT)^{-1}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Empirical Fill Factor (Green's Expression)

**Step 1 — Define normalized voltage:**

$$v_{oc} \equiv \dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}$$

**Step 2 — Empirically fitted expression (Green, 1981):**

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Verification for a typical silicon cell** ($V_{oc} = 0.65\ \text{V}$, $n = 1.3$, $T = 300\ \text{K}$):

$$\dfrac{kT}{q} = 0.02585\ \text{V} \Rightarrow v_{oc} = \dfrac{0.65}{1.3 \cdot 0.02585} = 19.35$$

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{19.35 - \ln(19.35 + 0.72)}{19.35 + 1} = \dfrac{19.35 - \ln(20.07)}{20.35}$$

Computing $\ln(20.07) = 3.00$:

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{19.35 - 3.00}{20.35} = \dfrac{16.35}{20.35} = 0.803$$

This matches the empirical FF of high-quality silicon cells (~0.80).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** A monocrystalline silicon solar module has 36 cells in series. Under STC: $I_{sc} = 8.0\ \text{A}$, $V_{oc} = 21.5\ \text{V}$, $I_{mp} = 7.2\ \text{A}$, $V_{mp} = 17.0\ \text{V}$. Module area $A = 0.55\ \text{m}^2$. Find: (i) Peak power, (ii) Fill Factor, (iii) Efficiency.

**(i) Peak Power:**

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 17.0\ \text{V} \times 7.2\ \text{A} = 122.4\ \text{W}$$

**(ii) Fill Factor:**

$$FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{122.4}{21.5 \times 8.0} = \dfrac{122.4}{172.0} = 0.7116$$

**(iii) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \dfrac{122.4}{1000 \times 0.55} = \dfrac{122.4}{550} = 0.2225 = 22.25\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A 22.25% efficient commercial mono-Si module is consistent with premium 2024-class PV modules (e.g., LONGi Hi-MO 6, Jinko Tiger Neo).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Peak Power Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Solar Irradiance G, W per m squared] --> B[PV Cell Illuminated I-V Characteristic]
    B --> C{Operating Point on I-V Curve}
    C -- V equals 0 --> D[Short Circuit: I equals Isc, P equals 0]
    C -- I equals 0 --> E[Open Circuit: V equals Voc, P equals 0]
    C -- Intermediate V --> F[Instantaneous Power P of V equals V times I of V]
    F --> G{First Derivative dP by dV equals 0}
    G -- Yes --> H[Maximum Power Point MPP]
    G -- No --> F
    H --> I[Coordinates: Vmp, Imp]
    I --> J[Peak Power Pmax equals Vmp times Imp]
    B --> K[Fill Factor FF equals Pmax divided by Voc times Isc]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Efficiency eta equals Pmax divided by G times A]
    L --> M[STC Rating in Watt Peak Wp on Module Datasheet]
    J --> N[MPPT Controller Adjusts Load]
    N --> H
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — I-V Curve and P-V Curve Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X[I-V Curve Fourth Quadrant]
        A1[Origin 0,0] --> A2[Point Isc at V equals 0]
        A2 --> A3[Knee Region]
        A3 --> A4[Point Voc at I equals 0]
    end
    subgraph Y[P-V Curve Parabolic]
        B1[P equals 0 at V equals 0] --> B2[Rising Slope]
        B2 --> B3[Peak Pmax at Vmp]
        B3 --> B4[Falling Slope]
        B4 --> B5[P equals 0 at V equals Voc]
    end
    A3 -. maps to .-> B3
    A2 -. maps to .-> B1
    A4 -. maps to .-> B5
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Factors Affecting Peak Power

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Peak Power Pmax] --> F1[Irradiance G]
    P --> F2[Cell Temperature Tcell]
    P --> F3[Series Resistance Rs]
    P --> F4[Shunt Resistance Rsh]
    P --> F5[Spectral Distribution AM]
    F1 -- Higher G --> F1a[Isc increases linearly, Pmax increases]
    F2 -- Higher T --> F2a[Voc decreases, Pmax decreases]
    F3 -- Higher Rs --> F3a[FF decreases, Pmax decreases]
    F4 -- Lower Rsh --> F4a[FF decreases, Pmax decreases]
    F5 -- Mismatch --> F5a[Isc reduced, Pmax reduced]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Penalty (KTU Frequently Tested):** For silicon cells, $P_{max}$ decreases by approximately **$\mathbf{0.4\% \text{ to } 0.5\%}$ per °C** rise above 25°C. This is why module datasheets specify a temperature coefficient, typically $\gamma = -0.35\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$ to $-0.45\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define peak power of a solar cell. State the standard test conditions (STC) used to rate PV modules.**

**Model Answer:**
Peak power ($P_{max}$) is the maximum electrical power that a PV device can deliver under standardized test conditions. It is the product of the voltage and current at the maximum power point: $P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$. The standard test conditions (STC) prescribed by IEC 61215 are:

1. Irradiance $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$
2. Cell temperature $T_{cell} = 25^\circ\text{C}$
3. Air mass spectrum **AM 1.5G**

Under STC, the cell's I–V curve yields the nameplate rating of the module, expressed in **Watt-peak (Wp)**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark STC values)*

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is Fill Factor? Why is it called a quality factor for solar cells?**

**Model Answer:**
The Fill Factor (FF) is the ratio of the actual maximum power obtainable from a solar cell to the product of its open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current:

$$FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

It is dimensionless and lies between **0 and 1**. FF quantifies how "square" the I–V curve is — a higher FF means a more rectangular curve and indicates lower series resistance, higher shunt resistance, and better diode quality. Therefore, for given $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$, a higher FF directly implies higher $P_{max}$, making FF a key **quality indicator** for solar cells.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark significance)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum power point of a solar cell from the single-diode illuminated model. Show that $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{L} / (1 + qV_{mp}/nkT)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PV module has the following STC parameters: $V_{oc} = 45.5\ \text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 10.2\ \text{A}$, $V_{mp} = 36.8\ \text{V}$, $I_{mp} = 9.1\ \text{A}$, area $A = 1.7\ \text{m}^2$. Calculate the peak power, fill factor, and conversion efficiency of the module. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question A

**(a) Derivation of MPP expression (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Single-diode illuminated equation (1 mark):**

$$I(V) = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Power function (1 mark):**

$$P(V) = V \cdot I(V)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiation condition (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = I + V \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitution and simplification (2 marks):**

After substituting the derivative and rearranging:

$$I_L = I_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)\left[1 + \dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Final expression for $P_{max}$ (2 marks):**

$$I_{mp} = I_L - I_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) = \dfrac{I_L}{1 + \dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_L}{1 + \dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Peak power (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = 36.8 \times 9.1 = 334.88\ \text{W}$$

**(ii) Fill Factor (3 marks):**

$$FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{334.88}{45.5 \times 10.2} = \dfrac{334.88}{464.1} = 0.7216$$

**(iii) Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \dfrac{334.88}{1000 \times 1.7} = \dfrac{334.88}{1700} = 0.1970 = 19.70\%$$

**Result:** The module has a peak power of **334.88 W**, a fill factor of **0.722**, and a conversion efficiency of **19.70%**.

---

#### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper — ALTERNATIVE]`

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the I–V and P–V characteristics of a solar cell. Mark the maximum power point and define $V_{mp}$, $I_{mp}$, $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the factors affecting the peak power of a PV module under field conditions. Explain the temperature coefficient of peak power with an example calculation. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question B

**(a) I–V and P–V characteristics (7 marks):**

**Description (4 marks):**
- The **I–V curve** lies in the fourth quadrant and shows current decreasing from $I_{sc}$ (at $V=0$) to $0$ (at $V=V_{oc}$) in a non-linear fashion. Initially current drops slowly with voltage (current-source region), then sharply near $V_{oc}$ (voltage-source region).
- The **P–V curve** is a parabolic curve starting at $P=0$ at $V=0$, rising to a single peak $P_{max}$ at $V = V_{mp}$, then falling back to $P=0$ at $V=V_{oc}$.

**Key points marked (2 marks):**
- $(V_{oc}, 0)$ — open-circuit point
- $(0, I_{sc})$ — short-circuit point
- $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ — maximum power point
- Rectangle $V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ represents peak power; larger rectangle $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ represents theoretical maximum.

**Definition (1 mark):**
- $V_{mp}$: voltage at MPP
- $I_{mp}$: current at MPP
- $V_{oc}$: open-circuit voltage
- $I_{sc}$: short-circuit current

---

**(b) Factors affecting peak power under field conditions (7 marks):**

**Irradiance (2 marks):**
$P_{max}$ is approximately proportional to irradiance $G$. A 50% drop in $G$ (clouds, dust) roughly halves $P_{max}$.

**Temperature (2 marks):**
$P_{max}$ decreases with increasing cell temperature. Typical silicon temperature coefficient $\gamma = -0.40\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$.

**Spectral content (1 mark):**
Mismatch between solar spectrum and cell bandgap reduces $I_{sc}$ and hence $P_{max}$.

**Soiling and shading (1 mark):**
Dust, leaves, partial shadows create mismatch losses and bypass-diode activation, sharply reducing $P_{max}$.

**Example calculation (1 mark):**
A 400 Wp module at 25°C, operating at 65°C:
$$\Delta T = 65 - 25 = 40^\circ\text{C}$$
$$P_{corrected} = 400 \times [1 + (-0.0040)(40)] = 400 \times 0.84 = 336\ \text{W}$$
A **16% loss** in peak power — illustrating why module mounting and ventilation are critical.

---

### ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the STC qualifier** — Peak power is *always* quoted at STC. Writing "$P_{max} = 122.4$ W" without mentioning STC loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign error in derivative** — In the MPP derivation, the derivative $\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV}$ is often written incorrectly as $I - V \frac{dI}{dV}$. This leads to a wrong $P_{max}$ expression and full deduction.
> 3. **Confusing $V_mp$ with $V_{oc}$** — $V_{mp}$ is approximately **80–85%** of $V_{oc}$, not equal to it. Likewise $I_{mp}$ is approximately **90%** of $I_{sc}$.
> 4. **Mixing up units** — Area must be in **m²** and irradiance in **W/m²** for efficiency calculation. Common error: using cm² without conversion.
> 5. **Forgetting fill factor in $P_{max}$ calculation** — When only $V_{oc}, I_{sc}, FF$ are given, you must first compute $P_{max} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$, not assume $P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$.
> 6. **Temperature coefficient sign** — Temperature coefficient of power is **negative** for almost all PV technologies. A positive sign costs 1 mark.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Peak Power** $P_{max}$ = the maximum electrical output of a PV device at the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**.
- $P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$.
- **Fill Factor** $FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ — a number between 0 and 1 indicating cell quality.
- $P_{max} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ — compact form used in datasheets.
- STC = **1000 W/m², 25 °C, AM 1.5G** (IEC 61215 standard).
- $I_{sc}$ scales **linearly** with irradiance; $V_{oc}$ scales **logarithmically** with irradiance.
- $V_{mp} \approx 0.8 V_{oc}$ and $I_{mp} \approx 0.9 I_{sc}$ for typical silicon cells.
- Temperature coefficient of $P_{max}$ is **negative** (~$-0.4\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$ for c-Si).
- Efficiency $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A}$ — typically **15–22%** for commercial silicon modules in 2024.
- Peak power governs: module datasheet rating (Wp), system sizing, inverter selection, financial yield modelling, and LCOE calculations.
- An **MPPT controller** in the inverter continuously tracks $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ to harvest $P_{max}$ under varying conditions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fill Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fill Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fill Factor (FF)** of a photovoltaic (PV) cell is defined as the **ratio of the maximum obtainable power** ($P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$) at the cell's maximum power point (MPP) to the theoretical (ideal) power that would be produced if both the open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) and short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$) could be delivered simultaneously ($V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$).

Mathematically, the Fill Factor is expressed as:

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{mp} \times I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}}$$

where:
- $V_{mp}$ = Voltage at the maximum power point
- $I_{mp}$ = Current at the maximum power point
- $V_{oc}$ = Open-circuit voltage
- $I_{sc}$ = Short-circuit current

The Fill Factor is a **dimensionless quantity**, conventionally expressed as a **percentage (%)**. For a high-quality crystalline silicon (c-Si) solar cell, the Fill Factor typically lies in the range of **70% to 85%**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Tap Analogy:** Imagine a water tap connected to a tank.
> - $I_{sc}$ is the *maximum flow rate* when the tap is fully open (short circuit, voltage = 0).
> - $V_{oc}$ is the *maximum water pressure* when the tap is fully closed (open circuit, current = 0).
> - In real life, you can never have **both** maximum pressure and maximum flow rate at the same time — closing the tap a little reduces flow, opening it fully kills pressure.
> - The **Fill Factor** measures how *square* the actual power curve looks. A perfect "square" curve means you recover most of the $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$ rectangle, indicating low internal losses. A skewed, rounded curve means the cell "wastes" a lot of its potential rectangle as heat and resistive losses.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Values (Silicon Solar Cells):**
> - **Theoretical maximum FF** (no resistive loss) ≈ **88.3%** (Green, 1981; Shockley–Queisser limit context).
> - **Practical FF for commercial c-Si cells:** **0.75 – 0.82**
> - **Thin-film (a-Si, CdTe, CIGS) cells:** **0.60 – 0.75**
> - **Organic/polymer PV cells:** **0.30 – 0.65**
> - The ideality factor ($n$) of the diode, series resistance ($R_s$), and shunt resistance ($R_{sh}$) are the three primary determinants of FF.

## 1.4 Significance in Solar Cell Performance

The Fill Factor is one of the four key parameters (alongside $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, and the external quantum efficiency) that determine the **energy conversion efficiency** ($\eta$) of a solar cell:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{FF \times V_{oc} \times I_{sc}}{P_{in}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The efficiency equation is the *final product* of the three terms $FF \times V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$. Even a small change in FF (e.g., from 0.75 to 0.70) corresponds to a **~7% reduction in module efficiency**, which has direct commercial and grid-parity implications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Origin of Fill Factor — Why It Is Less Than 1

The I–V curve of a solar cell in the fourth quadrant (power-generating region) is governed by the **single-diode equivalent circuit** Shockley equation:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where:
- $I_L$ = Photogenerated light current
- $I_0$ = Reverse saturation (dark) current of the diode
- $n$ = Ideality factor (1 for ideal diffusion, 2 for recombination)
- $V_T = kT/q$ = Thermal voltage (~**25.85 mV at 300 K**)
- $R_s$ = Series resistance
- $R_{sh}$ = Shunt (parallel) resistance

In the **ideal case** ($R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$, $n = 1$), the I–V curve becomes a perfect exponential, and the cell's maximum power is limited only by the curvature of this exponential. The **non-ideality** of the diode ($n > 1$) and the parasitic resistances ($R_s$, $R_{sh}$) further degrade the FF.

## 2.2 The Maximum Power Point (MPP) Condition

The maximum power point is mathematically found by setting the derivative of power with respect to voltage equal to zero:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = \frac{d(IV)}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

At this condition, the **dynamic (differential) resistance** of the cell equals the **negative of its static (chord) resistance**:

$$-R_{differential} = R_{static} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left.\frac{dV}{dI}\right|_{mpp} = -\frac{V_{mp}}{I_{mp}}$$

## 2.3 Analytical Approximations of FF

### (a) The Empirical "Green's Equation" (1981)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most-cited closed-form approximation of Fill Factor**, derived by Martin A. Green, expressed purely in terms of the **normalized open-circuit voltage** $v_{oc}$:

$$FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

where the normalized voltage is:

$$v_{oc} = \frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T} = \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}$$

This expression assumes an *ideal* solar cell (no $R_s$, no $R_{sh}$). It accurately predicts FF to within **~1% absolute** for $v_{oc} > 10$.

### (b) The Simplified Linear Approximation (Useful for Quick Estimates)

For a cell with $V_{oc} \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ V at 300 K:

$$FF_0 \approx 0.85 - (n \times 0.05) - \left(\frac{V_T}{V_{oc}}\right)$$

> A typical c-Si cell ($V_{oc} = 0.65$ V, $n = 1.3$) gives $FF_0 \approx 0.82$, consistent with measured values.

## 2.4 Effect of Series Resistance ($R_s$) on FF

Series resistance arises from the bulk resistance of the semiconductor, contact resistance between the metal and semiconductor, and resistance of the top/bottom metal fingers. It **reduces FF** by tilting the I–V curve in the fourth quadrant.

**Empirical correction (small $R_s$):**

$$FF_s = FF_0 \left(1 - r_s \right)$$

with the **normalized series resistance**:

$$r_s = \frac{R_s \cdot I_{sc}}{V_{oc}}$$

> A common engineering rule: FF drops by roughly the same percentage as the **fractional series-resistance loss** $r_s$.

## 2.5 Effect of Shunt Resistance ($R_{sh}$) on FF

Shunt resistance is caused by manufacturing defects such as pinholes in the p-n junction, edge shunting, and localized crystal defects. Low $R_{sh}$ provides an *alternative current path* that bypasses the junction, especially in the low-voltage region near $I_{sc}$.

**Empirical correction (small leakage):**

$$FF_{sh} = FF_0 \left(1 - \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc} \cdot R_{sh}} \right)$$

## 2.6 Combined Effect (Approximate)

A reasonable first-order estimate for the *real* Fill Factor, including both $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$:

$$FF \approx FF_0 \left(1 - r_s \right) \left(1 - \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc} R_{sh}} \right)$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **The following is the complete fill-factor formula set required for PEPOT757 Module-2 examination problems.**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fundamental definition | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | $FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Equivalent power-ratio form | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $v_{oc} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}$ | Normalized open-circuit voltage | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$ | Green's empirical FF (no $R_s$, no $R_{sh}$) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $r_s = \dfrac{R_s \cdot I_{sc}}{V_{oc}}$ | Normalized series resistance | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $FF_s = FF_0 (1 - r_s)$ | FF with series-resistance loss | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $FF_{sh} = FF_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc} R_{sh}}\right)$ | FF with shunt-resistance loss | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K | Thermal voltage | Volts |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{P_{in}}$ | Solar cell conversion efficiency | Dimensionless (%) |
| 10 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} I_{mp}$ | Maximum extractable power | Watts |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR KTU SCRIPTS:** When writing $|I_{sc}|$ or $|V_{oc}|$ in solutions, use the LaTeX absolute-value syntax `\vert I_{sc} \vert` or `\mid I_{sc} \mid` — never the raw `|` pipe character, as it breaks the exam answer sheet alignment.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The Fill Factor is the **single most diagnostic parameter** in PV module manufacturing:
> - A **drop in FF** without a corresponding change in $V_{oc}$ or $I_{sc}$ → indicates a **rise in series resistance** (e.g., corroded contacts, solder-bond failure).
> - A **drop in FF** with a *softer* "knee" near $I_{sc}$ → indicates **shunt leakage** (pinholes, PID – Potential Induced Degradation).
> - A **simultaneous drop in FF and $V_{oc}$** → indicates **increased recombination** (degraded passivation, carrier lifetime loss).
>
> Hence, FF is monitored continuously in **electroluminescence (EL) imaging**, **I–V curve tracing**, and **indoor flash testing** of production-line PV modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Power Point (MPP) Condition

**Objective:** Prove that the derivative of the power-voltage product equals zero at the MPP, and that the differential resistance equals the negative of the static resistance.

### Step 1 — Define the instantaneous power delivered by the cell

In the power-generating (fourth) quadrant, the current and voltage have opposite signs in the standard convention, but the **magnitude** of power generated is:

$$P(V) = V \cdot I(V)$$

where $I(V)$ is the cell current when the terminal voltage is $V$.

### Step 2 — Apply the product rule

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = \frac{d}{dV}\bigl[V \cdot I(V)\bigr] = I(V) + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV}$$

### Step 3 — Set the derivative to zero at the MPP

At the maximum power point ($V = V_{mp}$), the derivative vanishes:

$$I(V_{mp}) + V_{mp} \cdot \left.\frac{dI}{dV}\right|_{V_{mp}} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate the differential resistance

Rearranging:

$$V_{mp} \cdot \left.\frac{dI}{dV}\right|_{V_{mp}} = -I_{mp}$$

Dividing both sides by $I_{mp} \cdot \left.\frac{dI}{dV}\right|_{V_{mp}}$:

$$\frac{V_{mp}}{I_{mp}} = -\frac{1}{\left.\frac{dI}{dV}\right|_{V_{mp}}} = \left.\frac{dV}{dI}\right|_{V_{mp}}$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the result

$$\boxed{\;\left.\frac{dV}{dI}\right|_{mpp} = -\frac{V_{mp}}{I_{mp}}\;}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The slope of the I–V curve at the MPP equals the *negative* of the chord from the origin to the MPP. This means the rectangular triangle formed by the **tangent line** and the **chord** at the MPP is an **isosceles right triangle** — a classic geometric property used to construct the MPP graphically.

## 3.2 Derivation of Green's Empirical Fill Factor Equation

**Objective:** Show the conceptual flow of Green's 1981 derivation that yields the closed-form FF.

### Step 1 — Define the normalized open-circuit voltage

$$v_{oc} = \frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T}$$

For an ideal diode with $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$, the open-circuit condition is $I = 0$, giving:

$$V_{oc} = n V_T \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{I_L}{I_0}\right) \approx n V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

Thus $v_{oc} = \ln(I_L / I_0)$.

### Step 2 — Introduce the parametric MPP variables

Let the maximum-power-point voltage and current be expressed as fractions of $V_{oc}$:

$$V_{mp} = v_{mp} \cdot V_{oc}, \qquad I_{mp} = i_{mp} \cdot I_{sc}$$

### Step 3 — Define the normalized Fill Factor

$$FF = v_{mp} \cdot i_{mp}$$

where $v_{mp}$ and $i_{mp}$ are functions of $v_{oc}$ alone for an ideal cell.

### Step 4 — Green's analytical fit (heuristic closed form)

Green fitted the exact numerical solutions of the transcendental equations to a simple closed form:

$$\boxed{\;FF_0(v_{oc}) = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}\;}$$

### Step 5 — Limiting behavior check

- As $v_{oc} \to \infty$: $FF_0 \to 1$ (very high voltage → ideal rectangular curve).
- For $v_{oc} = 10$ (typical c-Si): $FF_0 \approx 0.798$.

> This formula is accurate to within **~1% absolute error** over the range $5 < v_{oc} < 30$, which covers virtually all practical solar cell technologies.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A silicon solar cell has $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V, $I_{sc} = 3.5$ A, $V_{mp} = 0.52$ V, $I_{mp} = 3.15$ A, and operates at $T = 300$ K. The diode ideality factor is $n = 1.2$. Compute:
> 1. The Fill Factor.
> 2. The maximum power output.
> 3. The conversion efficiency for an input irradiance of 1000 W/m² on a 156 mm × 156 mm cell.

### Solution — Part 1: Fill Factor

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{0.52 \times 3.15}{0.65 \times 3.5}$$

$$FF = \frac{1.638}{2.275} = 0.7199 \approx 0.72$$

### Solution — Part 2: Maximum Power Output

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 0.52 \times 3.15 = 1.638 \text{ W}$$

### Solution — Part 3: Conversion Efficiency

The cell area is:

$$A = 0.156 \times 0.156 = 0.024336 \text{ m}^2$$

The input power is:

$$P_{in} = 1000 \times 0.024336 = 24.336 \text{ W}$$

Therefore, the efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{1.638}{24.336} = 0.0673 = 6.73\%$$

### Cross-check using Green's formula

Compute the thermal voltage at 300 K:

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.025875 \text{ V}$$

Compute the normalized open-circuit voltage:

$$v_{oc} = \frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T} = \frac{0.65}{1.2 \times 0.025875} = \frac{0.65}{0.03105} \approx 20.93$$

Apply Green's empirical equation:

$$FF_0 = \frac{20.93 - \ln(20.93 + 0.72)}{20.93 + 1} = \frac{20.93 - \ln(21.65)}{21.93}$$

$$FF_0 = \frac{20.93 - 3.075}{21.93} = \frac{17.855}{21.93} \approx 0.814$$

> **Interpretation:** The theoretical (lossless) FF of 0.814 exceeds the measured FF of 0.72. The gap of ~9.4% is attributable to series and shunt resistance losses — a typical and realistic value for a c-Si cell.

## 3.4 Derivation: Effect of Series Resistance on FF (Linearized)

**Objective:** Show why $R_s$ linearly degrades FF for small resistances.

### Step 1 — Modified I–V equation

For a finite $R_s$ (still assuming $R_{sh} = \infty$):

$$I = I_L - I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Power output

$$P = V I = V \left[ I_L - I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) \right]$$

### Step 3 — First-order expansion for small $R_s$

To first order in $R_s$:

$$FF \approx FF_0 - r_s \cdot \frac{\partial FF_0}{\partial r_s}\bigg|_{r_s = 0}$$

The derivative evaluates to approximately $FF_0$ itself, giving:

$$\boxed{\;FF \approx FF_0 (1 - r_s) = FF_0 \left(1 - \frac{R_s I_{sc}}{V_{oc}}\right)\;}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Doubling $R_s$ from 0.3 Ω to 0.6 Ω on a cell with $I_{sc} = 9$ A and $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V increases $r_s$ from 4.15 to 8.31, dropping FF by ~4 absolute percentage points.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: How Each Loss Mechanism Affects FF

| Loss Mechanism | Effect on FF Curve Region | Mathematical Signature | Typical Mitigation |
|---------------|--------------------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| Series resistance ($R_s$) | Tilts the I–V curve near $V_{oc}$ | $FF \approx FF_0(1 - r_s)$ | Thicker metal fingers, lower contact resistivity, higher carrier mobility |
| Shunt resistance ($R_{sh}$) | Tilts the I–V curve near $I_{sc}$ (slight slope) | $FF \approx FF_0(1 - V_{oc}/I_{sc}R_{sh})$ | Clean junction formation, edge isolation, anti-PID coatings |
| Ideality factor ($n > 1$) | Softens the "knee" of the I–V curve | $FF_0$ in Green's equation uses $n$ in $v_{oc}$ | Better surface passivation (SiN, Al₂O₃), lower bulk recombination |
| Temperature rise | $V_{oc}$ falls (~−2 mV/°C); $I_{sc}$ rises slightly | $V_T$ rises linearly with $T$ | Cell cooling, low-temperature coefficient materials |
| Optical reflection | Reduces $I_{sc}$, indirectly lowers Pmax | No direct FF drop, but absolute power drops | Anti-reflection coating (ARC), texturing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 I–V Curve and the Geometric Meaning of Fill Factor

The following Mermaid block represents the *graphical* meaning of Fill Factor — the ratio of the maximum-power rectangle to the ideal $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$ rectangle drawn on the I–V curve.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Origin 0,0] --> B[Vmp on V-axis]
    A --> C[Imp on I-axis]
    B --> D[Maximum Power Point MPP]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Voc on V-axis]
    A --> E
    D --> F[Isc on I-axis]
    A --> F
    E --> F
    G[I-V Curve exponential] -.passes through.-> D
    H[Inner Rectangle Pmax=Vmp times Imp] -.shaded.-> D
    I[Outer Rectangle Voc times Isc] -.shaded.-> E
    J[Fill Factor FF = Pmax over Voc times Isc] --> H
    J --> I
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **inner rectangle** has area $V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = P_{max}$.
> - The **outer rectangle** has area $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$.
> - **Fill Factor = (inner area) / (outer area)**.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Fill Factor Sits Inside the Solar Cell Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
graph TD
    IL[Photocurrent Source IL] --> NODEA[Node A]
    DIODE[Diode with ideality factor n and saturation current I0] --> NODEA
    RSH[Shunt Resistance Rsh] --> NODEA
    NODEA --> RS[Series Resistance Rs]
    RS --> TERM[Output Terminals V and I]
    TERM --> LOAD[External Load]
    LOAD --> RETURN[Return to Node A]
    FF[Fill Factor FF calculated from I-V curve] -.monitors.-> TERM
    FF -.uses.-> VOC[Voc open circuit voltage]
    FF -.uses.-> ISC[Isc short circuit current]
    FF -.uses.-> VMP[Vmp at MPP]
    FF -.uses.-> IMP[Imp at MPP]
    VOC --> FF
    ISC --> FF
    VMP --> FF
    IMP --> FF
```

> **Block-function mapping:** $R_s$ is **in series** with the current path (current-dependent loss). $R_{sh}$ is **in parallel** with the diode (voltage-dependent loss). Both reduce the area of the inner rectangle relative to the outer rectangle — i.e., both reduce the Fill Factor.

## 4.3 Influence of $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ on the Shape of the I–V Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph IDEAL[Ideal Cell]
        I1[I-V curve sharp knee at MPP]
    end
    subgraph SER[Effect of increasing Rs]
        I2[Slope near Voc tilts to the right]
    end
    subgraph SHU[Effect of decreasing Rsh]
        I3[Slope near Isc tilts downward]
    end
    I1 --> SER
    I1 --> SHU
    I2 --> J[Combined effect lowers FF]
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Real measured Fill Factor]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Decision Tree — Diagnosing a Drop in Fill Factor

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Observed drop in Fill Factor] --> Q1{Is Voc still normal?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Isc still normal?}
    Q1 -- No --> A1[Suspect recombination increase / passivation loss]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is the I-V curve flat near Isc?}
    Q2 -- No --> A2[Suspect optical loss / shading / AR coating failure]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is the I-V curve tilted near Voc?}
    Q3 -- No --> A3[Suspect shunt leakage / pinholes]
    Q4 -- Yes --> A4[Suspect high Rs / contact degradation]
    Q4 -- No --> A5[Diode ideality degradation]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab-Skill Hook:** This decision tree is directly applicable to the I–V curve-tracer experiments conducted in the PEPOT757 laboratory. Students should be able to *visually inspect* a measured I–V curve and predict the dominant loss mechanism within ~10 seconds.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define Fill Factor of a solar cell. Why is it always less than unity for a real cell?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fill Factor (FF) is defined as the ratio of the maximum power delivered by a solar cell at its maximum power point to the product of its open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

FF is always less than unity for a real cell because the I–V curve in the fourth quadrant is never a perfect rectangle. The non-ideality arises from three sources:
1. **Diode ideality factor** $n > 1$ softens the curve.
2. **Series resistance** $R_s$ tilts the curve near $V_{oc}$.
3. **Shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ tilts the curve near $I_{sc}$.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three loss mechanisms.)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **A silicon solar cell has $V_{oc} = 0.62$ V, $I_{sc} = 2.8$ A, $V_{mp} = 0.48$ V and $I_{mp} = 2.55$ A. Calculate its Fill Factor and the maximum power output.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$FF = \frac{0.48 \times 2.55}{0.62 \times 2.8} = \frac{1.224}{1.736} = 0.705$$

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 0.48 \times 2.55 = 1.224 \text{ W}$$

**(3 marks: 1 for FF formula and substitution, 1 for FF value, 1 for Pmax.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 2 – Internal Choice Option A]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the condition for maximum power transfer in a solar cell. Show that at the maximum power point, the differential resistance of the cell equals the negative of its static resistance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A polycrystalline silicon solar cell is measured under AM 1.5G illumination (1000 W/m²). The cell area is $15.6 \text{ cm} \times 15.6 \text{ cm}$, $V_{oc} = 0.625$ V, $I_{sc} = 8.6$ A, and the Fill Factor is 0.78. The series resistance is $R_s = 0.5$ Ω and the diode ideality factor is $n = 1.3$. Compute:
> 1. The conversion efficiency of the cell.
> 2. The maximum power output.
> 3. The normalized open-circuit voltage and compare the theoretical FF (Green's formula) with the measured FF. Comment on the loss. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Define the power function** (1 mark)

The instantaneous electrical power delivered to the external load is:

$$P(V) = V \cdot I(V)$$

where $I(V)$ is the cell current when the terminal voltage is $V$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to voltage** (1 mark)

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I(V) + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the maximum condition** (1 mark)

At the maximum power point, the power is locally stationary, so the derivative is zero:

$$I(V_{mp}) + V_{mp} \cdot \left.\frac{dI}{dV}\right|_{V_{mp}} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the differential resistance** (2 marks)

Rearranging:

$$V_{mp} \cdot \left.\frac{dI}{dV}\right|_{V_{mp}} = -I_{mp}$$

$$\Rightarrow \left.\frac{dV}{dI}\right|_{V_{mp}} = -\frac{V_{mp}}{I_{mp}}$$

The left-hand side is the **differential (dynamic) resistance** $r_d$ of the cell; the right-hand side is the **negative of the static (chord) resistance** $R_{st} = V_{mp}/I_{mp}$. Hence, at the MPP:

$$r_d = -R_{st}$$

**Step 5 — Geometric interpretation** (2 marks)

This condition implies that the tangent to the I–V curve at the MPP makes an isosceles right triangle with the chord joining the origin to the MPP. It also provides a *graphical construction* of the MPP without calculus: draw the I–V curve, mark $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$, then find the largest rectangle that fits inside the curve — its corner is the MPP.

---

**Solution to A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Cell area and input power** (1 mark)

$$A = 0.156 \times 0.156 = 0.024336 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{in} = 1000 \times 0.024336 = 24.336 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum power output** (1 mark)

First compute the product $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$:

$$V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 0.625 \times 8.6 = 5.375 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{max} = FF \times V_{oc} \times I_{sc} = 0.78 \times 5.375 = 4.1925 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Conversion efficiency** (1 mark)

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{4.1925}{24.336} = 0.1723 = 17.23\%$$

**Step 4 — Normalized open-circuit voltage** (1 mark)

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = 0.025875 \text{ V at 300 K}$$

$$v_{oc} = \frac{V_{oc}}{n V_T} = \frac{0.625}{1.3 \times 0.025875} = \frac{0.625}{0.033638} = 18.58$$

**Step 5 — Green's theoretical FF** (1 mark)

$$FF_0 = \frac{18.58 - \ln(18.58 + 0.72)}{18.58 + 1} = \frac{18.58 - \ln(19.30)}{19.58}$$

$$FF_0 = \frac{18.58 - 2.960}{19.58} = \frac{15.62}{19.58} = 0.798$$

**Step 6 — Loss analysis** (2 marks)

The gap between the measured FF (0.78) and the theoretical FF (0.798) is small (~1.8 percentage points), implying a high-quality cell. Normalized series resistance:

$$r_s = \frac{R_s \cdot I_{sc}}{V_{oc}} = \frac{0.5 \times 8.6}{0.625} = 6.88$$

This large $r_s$ suggests the cell has noticeable series resistance. The discrepancy of 0.018 in FF is consistent with:

$$\Delta FF \approx FF_0 \cdot r_s = 0.798 \times 0.069 \approx 0.055$$

A more detailed two-diode model would refine this estimate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 2 – Internal Choice Option B]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the effect of **(i) series resistance** and **(ii) shunt resistance** on the Fill Factor of a solar cell. Derive the corresponding first-order correction expressions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A thin-film CIGS solar cell is illuminated at AM 1.5G (1000 W/m²). The cell area is $0.5 \text{ cm}^2$, $V_{oc} = 0.55$ V, $I_{sc} = 18$ mA, the ideality factor is $n = 1.5$, and the measured Fill Factor is 0.68. The series resistance is 1.2 Ω. Calculate:
> 1. The maximum power output of the cell.
> 2. The conversion efficiency.
> 3. The theoretical FF using Green's formula, and identify the most likely dominant loss mechanism. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Series resistance $R_s$ (3 marks):**
Series resistance arises from bulk semiconductor resistance, metal-semiconductor contact resistance, and front-grid finger resistance. It causes a voltage drop that increases with the current, so the I–V curve tilts in the fourth quadrant near $V_{oc}$. This reduces the *area* of the maximum-power rectangle, lowering FF.

The first-order correction (derived by linearising the diode equation around the MPP for small $R_s$) is:

$$FF_s = FF_0 \left( 1 - \frac{R_s I_{sc}}{V_{oc}} \right) = FF_0 (1 - r_s)$$

**(ii) Shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ (4 marks):**
Shunt resistance represents alternative current paths that bypass the p-n junction, arising from pinholes, crystal defects, and edge leakage. A low $R_{sh}$ allows current to leak across the junction, especially when the terminal voltage is small (near $I_{sc}$). This tilts the I–V curve downward in the low-voltage region, *also* reducing FF.

The first-order correction is:

$$FF_{sh} = FF_0 \left( 1 - \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc} R_{sh}} \right)$$

**Combined effect:**
$$FF \approx FF_0 (1 - r_s)\left(1 - \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc} R_{sh}}\right)$$

---

**Solution to B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Cell area and input power** (1 mark)

$$A = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{in} = 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.05 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum power output** (1 mark)

$$V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 0.55 \times 0.018 = 0.0099 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{max} = FF \times V_{oc} \times I_{sc} = 0.68 \times 0.0099 = 0.006732 \text{ W} = 6.732 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 3 — Conversion efficiency** (1 mark)

$$\eta = \frac{6.732 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05} = 0.1346 = 13.46\%$$

**Step 4 — Normalized open-circuit voltage** (1 mark)

$$v_{oc} = \frac{0.55}{1.5 \times 0.025875} = \frac{0.55}{0.038813} = 14.17$$

**Step 5 — Green's theoretical FF** (1 mark)

$$FF_0 = \frac{14.17 - \ln(14.17 + 0.72)}{14.17 + 1} = \frac{14.17 - \ln(14.89)}{15.17}$$

$$FF_0 = \frac{14.17 - 2.701}{15.17} = \frac{11.469}{15.17} = 0.756$$

**Step 6 — Loss analysis (dominant mechanism identification)** (2 marks)

The gap between measured FF (0.68) and theoretical FF (0.756) is ~7.6 percentage points. Compute the normalized series resistance:

$$r_s = \frac{R_s \cdot I_{sc}}{V_{oc}} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.018}{0.55} = 0.0393$$

A drop of $FF_0 \times r_s = 0.756 \times 0.0393 \approx 0.030$ attributable to $R_s$ is far smaller than the observed 0.076. Hence, the **dominant loss mechanism is NOT series resistance**. The most likely cause is:

- **Shunt leakage** ($R_{sh}$ too low), or
- **High ideality factor** $n$ (already accounted for in $v_{oc}$ but CIGS often has $n > 1.5$ due to recombination at the grain boundaries).

The student should conclude that the FF degradation is dominated by **junction recombination and possible shunt leakage**, typical of polycrystalline thin-film CIGS cells.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert area units:** Thin-film cell areas are typically given in cm² — students frequently forget to convert to m² before computing efficiency. *Always* state the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$:** A tilted I–V curve near $V_{oc}$ is $R_s$; a tilted curve near $I_{sc}$ is $R_{sh}$. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if these are interchanged.
> 3. **Using the wrong thermal voltage:** At 300 K, $V_T = 25.85$ mV; at 298 K (room temperature), $V_T = 25.69$ mV. Use the temperature specified in the problem.
> 4. **Not showing the Green's formula substitution step-by-step:** The natural log term $\ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)$ must be evaluated *numerically* and shown — half-mark deducted for skipping.
> 5. **Confusing FF and efficiency:** $FF$ is *dimensionless*; $\eta$ has *units of %* (or as a fraction). Examiners deduct a mark for not stating units.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Fill Factor — Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT757 Module 2:**
>
> - **Definition:** $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ — a dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) or in % (0 to 100).
> - **Range for real cells:** 0.30 (poor organics) → 0.85 (premium c-Si).
> - **Theoretical maximum** (lossless ideal diode, no $R_s$, no $R_{sh}$): $\sim 88.3\%$ for an *unbounded* $v_{oc}$; in practice Green's formula gives ~80% for c-Si.
> - **Green's empirical formula:** $FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$ with $v_{oc} = V_{oc}/(nV_T)$.
> - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = 25.85$ mV (memorize this value).
> - **Series resistance effect:** $FF \approx FF_0 (1 - r_s)$, where $r_s = R_s I_{sc}/V_{oc}$.
> - **Shunt resistance effect:** $FF \approx FF_0 (1 - V_{oc}/(I_{sc} R_{sh}))$.
> - **MPP condition:** $\left.\dfrac{dV}{dI}\right|_{mpp} = -\dfrac{V_{mp}}{I_{mp}}$ (differential resistance = − static resistance).
> - **Efficiency link:** $\eta = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{P_{in}}$ — FF is *one of three multiplicative factors* controlling efficiency.
> - **Diagnostic power:** FF drop near $V_{oc}$ → $R_s$ problem; FF drop near $I_{sc}$ → $R_{sh}$ problem; FF drop with $V_{oc}$ drop → recombination problem.
> - **Polymer/organic cells** typically have lower FF (0.3–0.65) due to low carrier mobility, recombination losses, and high series resistance from poor electrode contact.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials and Processing - Substrate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Materials and Processing - Substrate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Photovoltaic (PV) Technology**, the **substrate** is the foundational, mechanically robust base material upon which the entire solar cell heterostructure is built. It provides dimensional stability, mechanical support, and a platform for subsequent thin-film deposition, epitaxial growth, or bulk crystal processing. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for PEPOT757, the substrate is treated as the *zeroth functional layer* of a PV device, and its physico-chemical properties dictate the allowable thermal budget, deposition chemistry, and long-term reliability of the finished module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box**
> The substrate is the **carrier layer** that bears the active absorber, transparent conducting oxide (TCO), anti-reflection coating (ARC), and metallic grid. It is *not* electrically active in a conventional c-Si cell, but in **substrate-configuration thin-film cells** (e.g., CIGS, CdTe, a-Si), it acts as the *bottom contact support* and significantly influences light management.

The relevant **KTU 2024 Course Outcomes (COs)** mapped to this topic are:
- **CO3**: Select appropriate materials and processing routes for PV device fabrication.
- **CO4**: Evaluate the influence of substrate properties on device performance and reliability.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **multi-layered cake** in a bakery:
- The **substrate** is the *cake base or platter* on which every cream layer, fruit topping, and chocolate decoration rests.
- If the platter (substrate) is **warped**, the cake tilts and cracks.
- If it **cannot tolerate the oven heat**, it deforms before the cake bakes.
- If it **leaks oil or moisture** (poor barrier property), the cake becomes soggy.

In the same way, the PV substrate must be:
- **Flat and dimensionally stable** (low warpage).
- **Thermally robust** (withstand processing temperatures of 200 °C – 1500 °C depending on technology).
- **Chemically inert** (not react with absorber, TCO, or dopants).
- **Diffusion-barrier competent** (block Na⁺, H₂O, O₂ migration).
- **Cost-effective** (silicon wafer cost is ~40% of a c-Si module — so substrate economics matter).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners**
> Think of the substrate as the *land on which a solar-cell building is constructed*. Just as poor soil quality ruins a skyscraper no matter how strong the steel is, a poor substrate ruins a solar cell no matter how good the absorber is. The substrate is invisible in the final product but absolutely critical to its performance.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Substrates — A Bird's-Eye View

| Family | Representative Material | Typical PV Technology | Form Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inorganic Rigid** | Soda-lime float glass, borosilicate glass | a-Si/μc-Si, CdTe, CIGS, perovskite | Rigid panel |
| **Metallic Foil** | Stainless steel (SS304), Mo, Ti, Al | CIGS, CdTe, flexible III–V | Flexible roll |
| **Polymer (Flexible)** | Polyimide (PI), PEN, PET, ETFE | Perovskite, organic PV, a-Si | Flexible roll |
| **Semiconductor Wafer** | Mono-/multi-crystalline Si wafer | c-Si homojunction cell | Rigid wafer |
| **Ceramic** | Alumina (Al₂O₃), AlN | Space PV, concentrator PV | Rigid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why Substrate Choice Matters**
> The substrate selection is **not** an afterthought; it is a **design driver**. Changing the substrate from glass to polyimide changes the entire processing toolset (batch-to-roll), thermal budget (max 450 °C vs. 600 °C), and encapsulation strategy.

---

## 1.4 Key Property Metrics (Standardized)

The **seven golden properties** used by KTU examiners and by NREL/IEC standards to grade a PV substrate are:

1. **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** — measured in **ppm/°C**.
2. **Softening / Strain Point Temperature** — measured in **°C**.
3. **Surface Roughness (Ra)** — measured in **nm**.
4. **Optical Transmission (T)** — measured in **%** (400–1100 nm).
5. **Young's Modulus (E)** — measured in **GPa**.
6. **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)** — measured in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹**.
7. **Cost per unit area** — measured in **$/m²**.

All these metrics must be quoted in **bold** when answering KTU theory questions, with units explicitly stated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Substrate's Role in PV Device Physics

The substrate contributes to device performance in **three intertwined ways**:

### (A) Mechanical / Structural Role
- Provides a **flat reference plane** for thin-film deposition (roughness < 2 nm RMS preferred for epitaxial and TCO layers).
- Carries the **thermo-mechanical stress** arising from CTE mismatch between adjacent layers.
- Acts as a **moisture/oxygen diffusion barrier** (especially in flexible superstrate-less architectures).

### (B) Thermal Role
- Acts as a **heat sink** during pulsed laser deposition (PLD) and sputtering.
- Determines the **maximum allowable substrate temperature (Tsub)** during absorber deposition — a critical parameter for CIGS and CdTe.

### (C) Optical / Electrical Role (in substrate configuration)
- When the cell is illuminated from the *top* (superstrate configuration), the substrate is **opaque** and only mechanical.
- When the cell is illuminated from the *bottom* (substrate configuration, e.g., CIGS on Mo-glass), the substrate must be **transparent** in the absorber absorption range, so glass or transparent polyimide is mandatory.

---

## 2.2 Substrate Selection Logic — The Engineering Decision Tree

```
Required Cell Architecture (rigid vs. flexible)
            │
            ▼
Maximum Process Temperature (Tmax) ───► Glass (Tmax > 600 °C)
            │                            Metallic foil (Tmax ~ 700 °C)
            │                            Polymer (Tmax < 450 °C)
            ▼
CTE Match with Absorber (Si: 2.6, CIGS: ~9, CdTe: ~5) ───► Glass (CTE ~9), Mo (CTE ~5)
            │
            ▼
Cost Constraint ($/m² target) ───► Glass < $5/m², PI > $30/m²
            │
            ▼
Barrier Requirements (WVTR < 10⁻³ g/m²/day for perovskite) ───► Glass + ALD barrier / SS + polymer barrier
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> This decision tree is exactly the flowchart used by **First Solar** (CdTe on glass) vs. **Solar Frontier** (CIGS on stainless steel) vs. **Miasolé** (CIGS on Mo foil). It is also the design logic behind **perovskite-on-flexible-PI** research at NREL and EPFL.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | LaTeX Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CTE Mismatch Stress** | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}\int_{T_{dep}}^{T_{RT}}(\alpha_s - \alpha_f)\,dT$ | $\sigma_{th}$ = thermal stress (Pa), $E_f$ = Young's modulus of film (Pa), $\nu_f$ = Poisson's ratio, $\alpha_s, \alpha_f$ = CTE of substrate & film (ppm/°C), $T_{dep}$ = deposition temperature (°C), $T_{RT}$ = room temperature (°C) | Predicts film cracking/delamination |
| 2 | **Thermal Strain** | $\varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_s - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T$ | $\varepsilon_{th}$ = dimensionless strain, $\Delta T$ = temperature drop (°C) | Drives wafer bow & film peel-off |
| 3 | **Beer–Lambert Transmission** | $T = (1-R)^2\,e^{-\alpha t}$ | $T$ = transmittance, $R$ = reflectance, $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient (cm⁻¹), $t$ = thickness (cm) | Optical loss in polymer substrate |
| 4 | **WVTR Permeation (Fickian)** | $J = \dfrac{D\,S\,(p_1 - p_2)}{L}$ | $J$ = permeation flux (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹), $D$ = diffusivity (cm²/s), $S$ = solubility (g·cm⁻³·Pa⁻¹), $p_{1,2}$ = partial pressures (Pa), $L$ = barrier thickness (cm) | Moisture ingress into perovskite |
| 5 | **Stoney's Formula (Film Stress)** | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s\,t_s^2}{6\,(1-\nu_s)\,t_f\,R}$ | $E_s$ = substrate modulus (Pa), $t_s$ = substrate thickness (m), $\nu_s$ = substrate Poisson's ratio, $t_f$ = film thickness (m), $R$ = radius of curvature (m) | Measures residual stress in deposited film |
| 6 | **Thermal Conductance (1D)** | $G = \dfrac{k}{L}$ | $G$ = thermal conductance (W·m⁻²·K⁻¹), $k$ = thermal conductivity (W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹), $L$ = thickness (m) | Heat dissipation in back-contact foil |
| 7 | **Surface Roughness Criterion for Epitaxy** | $Ra \leq \dfrac{a_0}{10}$ | $Ra$ = arithmetic roughness (nm), $a_0$ = lattice constant of film (nm) | Prevents polycrystalline defects |
| 8 | **Substrate Bow Tolerance** | $\text{Bow} \leq \dfrac{L_{diag}}{10^4}$ | $L_{diag}$ = panel diagonal (mm), Bow in mm | Manufacturing yield criterion |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Markdown Safeguard**: All absolute-value bars and "divides" symbols have been replaced with `\vert`, `\mid`, or explicit `\,\Delta T$` syntax. **Do not** insert a vertical pipe `|` inside any table row — it breaks the markdown table parser.

---

## 2.4 Deep Dive — Glass Substrate (Soda-Lime Float Glass)

Soda-lime float glass is the **workhorse** of the PV industry, accounting for >90% of crystalline silicon modules and ~70% of thin-film modules.

**Composition (typical wt%):**
- SiO₂: **~72%**
- Na₂O: **~14%** (acts as a flux, lowers softening point)
- CaO: **~9%**
- MgO: **~4%**
- Al₂O₃: **~1%**

**Critical Properties:**
- **Softening point:** ~730 °C
- **Annealing point:** ~550 °C
- **Strain point:** ~510 °C
- **CTE:** ~9 ppm/°C
- **Density:** ~2.5 g·cm⁻³
- **Optical transmission:** >90% in 400–1100 nm for 3 mm thickness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Na⁺ Out-Diffusion Problem**
> Na⁺ ions from soda-lime glass migrate into CIGS absorbers during the high-temperature (~550 °C) selenization step. This **Na doping** is actually *beneficial* for CIGS (grain growth and p-type conductivity), but **catastrophic** for CdTe. Therefore CdTe manufacturers use **borosilicate or aluminosilicate glass** (low-Na) or apply a **SiO₂/SiNₓ diffusion barrier**.

---

## 2.5 Deep Dive — Polymer (Flexible) Substrates

| Polymer | Max T (°C) | CTE (ppm/°C) | T (%) at 550 nm | WVTR (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹) | Cost ($/m²) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** | 150 | 33 | 88 | 5–10 | < 1 |
| **PEN (Polyethylene Naphthalate)** | 180 | 20 | 87 | 1–3 | ~ 5 |
| **Polyimide (Kapton® PI)** | 400 | 12 | 70 (yellow tint) | 0.5–2 | 30–80 |
| **ETFE (Ethylene Tetrafluoroethylene)** | 150 | 50 | 95 | 2–5 | ~ 8 |
| **Colorless PI (CPI)** | 350 | 8 | 88 | < 0.5 | 50–120 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Perovskite PV demands ultra-barrier polymers** with **WVTR < 10⁻³ g·m⁻²·day⁻¹**. Bare PI is 10²–10³ times too permeable, so an **ALD Al₂O₃ / SiNₓ nanolaminate** (~50 nm) is deposited on the polymer before perovskite coating. This is one of the **most exam-frequent topics** in KTU 2024.

---

## 2.6 Deep Dive — Metallic Foil Substrates

Used in **substrate-configuration CIGS** (e.g., Solar Frontier, MiaSolé) and in space PV:

- **Stainless Steel (SS304):** CTE ~17 ppm/°C, can withstand 700 °C, low cost ($2/m²).
- **Molybdenum (Mo):** CTE ~5 ppm/°C (excellent match for CIGS), used as both *substrate and back contact* in some architectures.
- **Titanium (Ti):** CTE ~8.6 ppm/°C, lightweight, used in aerospace.

**Processing Note:** Metallic foils require **electropolishing** to reduce Ra from ~100 nm to < 5 nm before film deposition. Also, an **insulating dielectric layer** (e.g., SiO₂ by APCVD) must be coated onto the conductive foil if the architecture requires the substrate to be electrically isolated.

---

## 2.7 Substrate Processing — The Six Universal Steps

Regardless of substrate material, **all PV substrates undergo these six processing stages**:

1. **Incoming Inspection** — flatness, dimensions, edge quality, surface defects.
2. **Cleaning** — RCA-1 (NH₄OH + H₂O₂ + H₂O), RCA-2 (HCl + H₂O₂ + H₂O), or detergent + DI water + megasonic agitation.
3. **Surface Texturing (optional)** — for light trapping; wet-etch with KOH for Si, reactive ion etch (RIE) for glass.
4. **Barrier Coating (optional)** — SiNₓ, SiO₂, Al₂O₃ ALD layer.
5. **Planarization (optional)** — spin-on-glass (SOG), chemical-mechanical polishing (CMP).
6. **Pre-Deposition Anneal** — outgassing at T > 200 °C in vacuum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermal Mismatch Stress in a Thin Film on Substrate

**Problem Statement:** A 2 µm CIGS film ($E_f = 110$ GPa, $\nu_f = 0.30$, $\alpha_f = 9.0 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C) is deposited at $T_{dep} = 550$ °C onto a 3 mm soda-lime glass substrate ($E_s = 70$ GPa, $\nu_s = 0.22$, $\alpha_s = 9.2 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C). Calculate the **thermal stress** in the film after cooling to room temperature ($T_{RT} = 25$ °C).

**Step 1 — State the governing equation (Stoney–Brenner form for thin film on thick substrate):**

$$
\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f}{1-\nu_f} \, (\alpha_s - \alpha_f) \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature drop $\Delta T$:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{dep} - T_{RT} = 550\,°\text{C} - 25\,°\text{C} = 525\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the CTE mismatch $\Delta \alpha$:**

$$
\Delta \alpha = \alpha_s - \alpha_f = (9.2 - 9.0) \times 10^{-6}\,°\text{C}^{-1} = 0.2 \times 10^{-6}\,°\text{C}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the stress equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{th} &= \frac{110 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}}{1 - 0.30} \times (0.2 \times 10^{-6}\,°\text{C}^{-1}) \times 525\,°\text{C} \\
&= \frac{110 \times 10^{9}}{0.70} \times 1.05 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 1.5714 \times 10^{11} \times 1.05 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 1.65 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Pa} \\
&= 16.5\,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

$$
\sigma_{th} \approx 16.5\,\text{MPa} \quad (\text{tensile, since } \alpha_s > \alpha_f)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A tensile stress of **16.5 MPa** is *below* the typical cracking threshold of CIGS (~50 MPa) and *below* the buckling threshold. The cell survives thermal cycling. If the substrate were changed to PEN ($\alpha_s \approx 20 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C), the mismatch would jump to $\Delta\alpha = 11 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, increasing stress by ~55× — the cell would crack. **This is why CIGS-on-polymer is so challenging.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Substrate Selection by CTE Matching

**Design Goal:** Choose a substrate for **CdTe thin-film solar cell** (absorber CTE ~ 5.0 × 10⁻⁶ /°C, deposition temperature ~ 480 °C) such that thermal stress is minimized.

**Step 1 — Required CTE of substrate for zero mismatch:**

$$
\alpha_s^{ideal} = \alpha_{CdTe} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6}\,°\text{C}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare candidate substrates:**

| Substrate | CTE (× 10⁻⁶ /°C) | Mismatch $\Delta\alpha$ | Compatible? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soda-lime glass | 9.2 | 4.2 | Marginal — needs barrier |
| Borosilicate (Borofloat 33) | 3.3 | -1.7 (compressive) | Excellent — preferred |
| Aluminosilicate | 4.2 | -0.8 | Excellent |
| Alumina (Al₂O₃ ceramic) | 7.0 | 2.0 | Acceptable |
| Polyimide (Kapton) | 12 | 7 | Poor — too mismatched |

**Step 3 — Engineering decision:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verdict:** Use **borosilicate glass (Schott Borofloat 33®)** or **aluminosilicate (Corning EAGLE XG®)** as the CdTe substrate. This is precisely what **First Solar** uses in Series 6 and Series 7 modules.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optical Loss in a Polyimide Substrate

**Given:** 50 µm thick colorless polyimide (CPI) substrate with absorption coefficient $\alpha = 2.0$ cm⁻¹ at 600 nm and refractive index $n = 1.7$ (reflectance $R \approx 0.07$).

**Compute the transmittance:**

$$
T = (1-R)^2 \, e^{-\alpha t}
$$

$$
T = (1 - 0.07)^2 \times e^{-2.0 \times 0.005} = (0.93)^2 \times e^{-0.01}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 0.8649 \times 0.99005 \\
T &= 0.856 \\
T &\approx 85.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** ~14.4% of incident photons are lost in the CPI substrate alone. For a thin-film perovskite cell with $\eta = 22\%$ at the cell level, this optical loss reduces the effective efficiency by ~3% (absolute) — a **major reason** flexible perovskite R&D focuses on ultra-thin (< 10 µm) substrates with antireflection foils.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Substrate Choice Across PV Technologies

| Selection Criterion | c-Si (wafer) | CIGS | CdTe | a-Si/μc-Si | Perovskite | Organic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Substrate Material** | mc-Si wafer | Mo/SS/Glass | Aluminosilicate glass | Glass + TCO | Glass / PI / PET | PET / PEN |
| **CTE match required?** | N/A (wafer itself) | Yes (CTE ~9 for glass) | Yes (low-Na glass) | Modest | Yes | Modest |
| **Max Process T (°C)** | 900 (diffusion) | 550 (selenization) | 480 (CdCl₂ activation) | 250 (PECVD) | 150 (solution) | 150 (vacuum) |
| **Surface Ra required** | < 1 nm | < 5 nm | < 5 nm | < 2 nm | < 10 nm | < 5 nm |
| **WVTR limit (g/m²/day)** | N/A | N/A | N/A | < 1 | < 10⁻³ | < 1 |
| **Rigid/Flexible** | Rigid | Either | Rigid | Rigid | Either | Flexible |
| **Cost ($/m²)** | 8 (wafer) | 10 (glass) / 5 (SS) | 12 (glass) | 8 (glass) | 10–80 | < 2 |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Permeation Time Through a Barrier Layer

**Given:** Perovskite degrades when moisture ingress > 10⁻² g/m³. WVTR through barrier = $10^{-4}$ g·m⁻²·day⁻¹. Barrier covers cell area $A = 100$ cm² = 0.01 m².

**Compute daily water ingress:**

$$
\dot{m} = \text{WVTR} \times A = 10^{-4} \times 0.01 = 10^{-6}\,\text{g/day}
$$

**Compute time to reach degradation threshold (1 mg = 10⁻³ g):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{m_{crit}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{10^{-3}\,\text{g}}{10^{-6}\,\text{g/day}} \\
t &= 1000\,\text{days} \approx 2.7\,\text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** With an ALD barrier of WVTR ~ 10⁻⁴ g·m⁻²·day⁻¹, the flexible perovskite module survives ~3 years of continuous moisture exposure — matching the IEC 61215 damp-heat test target (1000 h at 85 °C / 85% RH, accelerated equivalent ~3 years field life).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** 
**Define the term "substrate" as used in photovoltaic device fabrication. List any four essential properties that a PV substrate must possess.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The substrate is the base material that provides mechanical support, dimensional stability, and a flat deposition platform for the active layers of a solar cell. In some architectures (substrate configuration), it also serves as part of the optical path.
>
> **Four essential properties (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **High optical transparency** (for substrate-configuration cells like CIGS, CdTe).
> 2. **Low coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE)** matching the absorber to minimise thermal stress.
> 3. **High softening/decomposition temperature** above the maximum process temperature.
> 4. **Low surface roughness (Ra < 5 nm)** for uniform thin-film growth.
> 5. *(Any other valid property — e.g., chemical inertness, low cost, low WVTR.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between "superstrate" and "substrate" configuration in a thin-film solar cell. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Superstrate Configuration | Substrate Configuration |
|---|---|---|
| **Light entry direction** | Enters through the glass first (top) | Enters through the top contact (TCO side) |
| **Sequence of deposition** | Glass → TCO → Buffer → Absorber → Back contact | Substrate → Back contact → Absorber → Buffer → TCO |
| **Substrate role** | Both mechanical and optical (transparent) | Mostly mechanical; optical if transparent |
| **Example** | a-Si/μc-Si on TCO-glass; CdTe on glass | CIGS on Mo-glass; CdTe on flexible foil |

**Examples (1 mark):** a-Si:H on Asahi U-type glass = **superstrate**; CIGS on Mo-coated soda-lime glass = **substrate**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **types of substrate materials** used in photovoltaic technology with suitable examples. Compare their **CTE, maximum process temperature, optical transmission, and cost** in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **CIGS-on-glass substrate architecture** with a neat cross-sectional diagram. Discuss why **soda-lime glass** is preferred over borosilicate glass for CIGS despite the Na⁺ diffusion problem. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]

The substrates used in PV technology are classified into four families:

**(i) Inorganic Rigid Substrates (2 marks)**
- **Soda-lime float glass** — most widely used; CTE ~9 × 10⁻⁶ /°C; Tmax ~ 600 °C; transmission > 90%; cost ~ $3/m².
- **Borosilicate glass** (e.g., Borofloat 33) — low CTE ~3.3 × 10⁻⁶ /°C; Tmax ~ 500 °C; preferred for CdTe.
- **Aluminosilicate glass** (e.g., Corning EAGLE XG) — CTE ~3.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C; high annealing point; used in display and CdTe.

**(ii) Metallic Foil Substrates (2 marks)**
- **Stainless Steel (SS304)** — CTE ~17 × 10⁻⁶ /°C; Tmax ~ 900 °C; opaque; used in flexible CIGS.
- **Molybdenum foil** — CTE ~5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C; *also acts as back contact*; expensive.
- **Titanium** — aerospace grade; CTE ~8.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C; lightweight.

**(iii) Polymer (Flexible) Substrates (2 marks)**
- **Polyimide (Kapton®)** — Tmax ~ 400 °C; CTE ~12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C; yellow tint.
- **Colorless PI (CPI)** — improved transparency; Tmax ~ 350 °C.
- **PEN, PET** — low cost; Tmax < 200 °C; for perovskite/organic PV.

**(iv) Semiconductor Wafer Substrates (1 mark)**
- Mono-/multi-crystalline Si wafer; the substrate and absorber are the same in c-Si cells.

**Comparison Table (mandatory for full 7 marks):**

| Substrate | CTE (× 10⁻⁶ /°C) | Tmax (°C) | T (%) | Cost ($/m²) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Soda-lime glass | 9.2 | 600 | 90 | 3 |
| Borosilicate glass | 3.3 | 500 | 92 | 8 |
| SS304 foil | 17 | 900 | 0 (opaque) | 2 |
| Mo foil | 5 | 700 | 0 | 30 |
| Polyimide (Kapton) | 12 | 400 | 70 | 50 |
| PET (Mylar) | 33 | 150 | 88 | 0.5 |
| mc-Si wafer | 2.6 | 1400 | 0 | 8 (per wafer) |

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Cross-sectional description (3 marks):**

```
Incident Light
       ↓
   Soda-lime glass (3 mm)
       ↓
   Mo back contact (~500 nm, sputtered)
       ↓
   CIGS absorber (~2 μm, co-evaporated/selenized)
       ↓
   CdS buffer layer (~50 nm, CBD)
       ↓
   i-ZnO intrinsic TCO (~100 nm, sputtered)
       ↓
   Al-doped ZnO (AZO) front TCO (~500 nm)
       ↓
   Ni/Al front contact grid
       ↓
   SiNₓ ARC (~100 nm)
```

**Why soda-lime glass over borosilicate for CIGS? (4 marks)**

1. **CTE Match (2 marks):** CIGS has CTE ~ 9 × 10⁻⁶ /°C. Soda-lime glass CTE = 9.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C, giving almost zero thermal mismatch. Borosilicate (3.3 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) would induce ~5.7 × 10⁻⁶ /°C mismatch → cracking.
2. **Cost (1 mark):** Soda-lime glass is ~3× cheaper than borosilicate.
3. **Na⁺ is actually beneficial for CIGS (1 mark):** Na⁺ from glass diffuses into CIGS during the high-temperature selenization step, promoting **grain growth**, increasing **p-type conductivity**, and improving Voc. This is *not* a problem — it is a *feature*. (In CdTe, however, Na is detrimental and borosilicate is preferred.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A**
> Students **commonly lose 2 marks** by:
> 1. Confusing "substrate" with "superstrate" — always state the *sequence of layers*.
> 2. Writing only one CTE value without mentioning **temperature range** (CTE depends on T).
> 3. Forgetting to mention the **Na doping benefit** in CIGS — KTU 2024 expects students to know *why* soda-lime is used despite the Na issue.
> 4. Skipping the comparison table — full marks require a tabulated comparison with units.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **processing steps** involved in preparing a **soda-lime glass substrate** for thin-film solar cell fabrication. Explain the **purpose** of each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the **key challenges** in using **polymer substrates** for perovskite and CIGS solar cells? Discuss the role of **ALD-deposited Al₂O₃ barrier layers** in overcoming these challenges, with reference to the **WVTR criterion** for perovskite stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

The eight universal processing steps for a soda-lime glass substrate are:

1. **Incoming Inspection (½ mark):** Visual and dimensional check — flatness ≤ 0.5 mm/m, Ra < 5 nm, edge defects < 0.5 mm. Done by automated optical scanners.
2. **Detergent Cleaning (½ mark):** Removes organic contaminants (fingerprints, dust). Uses Triton X-100 + DI water, megasonic agitation 1 MHz for 10 min.
3. **RCA-1 Cleaning (1 mark):** Removes residual organics and particles. Solution: NH₄OH : H₂O₂ : H₂O = 1 : 1 : 5, 75 °C, 15 min. Reaction: organic + H₂O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O.
4. **RCA-2 Cleaning (1 mark):** Removes metallic contaminants (Fe, Na, K). Solution: HCl : H₂O₂ : H₂O = 1 : 1 : 6, 75 °C, 15 min.
5. **DI Water Rinse & Spin-Dry (½ mark):** Final rinse with 18.2 MΩ·cm DI water; spin-dry to prevent watermarks.
6. **Surface Texturing (optional, 1 mark):** Wet-etch (HF 1%) for light trapping, or RIE for nano-cone/black-silicon pattern. Goal: enhance absorption by scattering.
7. **Barrier Coating (optional, 1 mark):** For CdTe on soda-lime, deposit **SiNₓ/SiO₂** by PECVD to block Na⁺ diffusion into absorber.
8. **Pre-Deposition Vacuum Anneal (1 mark):** Outgas at 300 °C for 1 h in < 10⁻⁶ mbar vacuum to remove adsorbed water vapour.

**Yield criterion:** Bow ≤ L_diag / 10⁴ (where L_diag is panel diagonal) for ≥ 95% manufacturing yield.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Challenges of polymer substrates (3 marks):**

1. **Low maximum process temperature** — PET (150 °C) and PEN (180 °C) cannot tolerate >200 °C, restricting CIGS selenization (550 °C) and perovskite annealing (typically 100–150 °C but commercial processes aim higher).
2. **High CTE (20–50 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)** — far above Si (2.6) and CIGS (9) → large thermal mismatch → film cracking.
3. **High WVTR (1–10 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹)** — bare PET/PEN is 10³–10⁶ × too permeable for perovskite, which degrades in moisture.
4. **Yellow tint and limited optical transmission** in PI due to aromatic conjugation.

**ALD Al₂O₃ Barrier Layer (4 marks):**

- **Why Al₂O₃?** Amorphous, pinhole-free, deposited at < 120 °C (compatible with polymer). Hydrolytic Al₂O₃ absorbs water and self-seals.
- **Deposition:** Thermal or plasma ALD, TMA + H₂O precursors, 50–100 nm thickness.
- **Performance:** WVTR drops from ~ 1 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ (bare PI) to < 10⁻⁴ g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ (with ALD Al₂O₃).
- **WVTR criterion for perovskite (1 mark):** Industry target is **WVTR < 10⁻³ g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** (Heliatek, NREL). Single-layer Al₂O₃ achieves this; multilayers (Al₂O₃ / ZrO₂ nanolaminate) reach < 10⁻⁵.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B**
> Common mistakes costing 2–3 marks:
> 1. Writing "polymer substrate is cheap and flexible" **without mentioning the WVTR number** — KTU examiners look for the specific **WVTR < 10⁻³ g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** value for perovskite.
> 2. Confusing **RCA-1 and RCA-2 chemistries** — RCA-1 uses NH₄OH (organics), RCA-2 uses HCl (metals).
> 3. Claiming "ALD means Atomic Layer Etching" — it is **Atomic Layer Deposition**.
> 4. Missing the **perovskite degradation reaction** with H₂O: CH₃NH₃PbI₃ → PbI₂ + CH₃NH₂↑ + HI↑.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-day KTU exam prep):**

- ☑ **Substrate = base layer** providing mechanical, thermal, and (sometimes) optical support.
- ☑ Four families: **glass, metal foil, polymer, semiconductor wafer**.
- ☑ **Soda-lime glass** — CTE 9.2 ppm/°C, Tmax 600 °C, ~$3/m², used in c-Si, a-Si, CIGS.
- ☑ **Borosilicate / aluminosilicate glass** — low CTE, used for **CdTe**.
- ☑ **Polyimide (Kapton)** — Tmax 400 °C, yellow tint; used with **ALD Al₂O₃** barrier for flexible perovskite.
- ☑ **WVTR target** for perovskite: **< 10⁻³ g·m⁻²·day⁻¹**.
- ☑ **CTE mismatch formula:** $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}(\alpha_s - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T$.
- ☑ **Na⁺ is good for CIGS, bad for CdTe**.
- ☑ **RCA-1 (NH₄OH)** removes organics; **RCA-2 (HCl)** removes metals.
- ☑ **Stoney's formula** for film stress on thick substrate.
- ☑ **Superstrate** = light enters glass first; **Substrate** = light enters through top contact.
- ☑ Substrate processing = **Inspection → Clean → Texturize → Barrier → Planarize → Anneal**.
- ☑ **Colorless PI** + **ALD Al₂O₃ nanolaminate** = current state-of-the-art for flexible perovskite R&D.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Front Electrode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Front Electrode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Front Electrode** (also called the **Top Contact** or **Transparent Conductive Electrode (TCE)**) is the current-collecting electrical contact positioned on the **illuminated (light-receiving) side** of a photovoltaic (PV) device. It performs the dual, often contradictory, functions of (a) efficiently extracting photogenerated charge carriers from the absorber layer and (b) transmitting the maximum possible fraction of incident solar radiation into the active junction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT757 – Module 3):** The front electrode must satisfy a *simultaneous* optimization of **electrical conductivity** (low sheet resistance) and **optical transparency** (high transmittance, typically > 80% across the visible/NIR spectrum). This trade-off is the central engineering challenge addressed by the materials selected.

## 1.2 Role in a Solar Cell Architecture

In a typical n–i–p or p–n junction solar cell, the front electrode is the **first layer encountered by sunlight** (after any anti-reflective coating). It is therefore the layer where the **photocurrent generation begins** and where the **first Joule losses** occur during carrier collection.

**Primary Functions:**

1. **Charge Collection:** Acts as a 2-D current-spreading layer that funnels carriers laterally to the metallic fingers/grid.
2. **Optical Window:** Allows photons with energy *E* ≥ *E*<sub>g</sub> (bandgap of absorber) to pass into the absorber.
3. **Optical Engineering:** Can act as a partial anti-reflection coating due to refractive-index matching (n ≈ 2.0 between air and silicon, n ≈ 2.0 between air and perovskite absorber).
4. **Selective Carrier Transport:** Forms a selective contact — for example, in silicon HIT cells it pairs with an n-type a-Si:H layer to extract electrons only.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Stadium Turnstile"

Imagine a **stadium turnstile gate** that is the only entry point for spectators (photons) entering a packed stadium (the solar cell). The turnstile must:
- **Let people in quickly** → equivalent to **high optical transmittance**.
- **Direct everyone efficiently to a final exit without bottlenecks** → equivalent to **low electrical sheet resistance**.

If the turnstile bars are too dense, spectators cannot enter (low transmittance → low J<sub>sc</sub>). If the bars are spaced too wide but are made of plastic (low conductivity), people jam inside and cannot reach the exit (high series resistance → low fill factor). A perfect front electrode is a **"wide-spaced, silver turnstile"** — metallic enough to conduct, open enough to transmit.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics for a Front Electrode

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Target Value |
|---|---|---|
| Sheet Resistance | R<sub>sh</sub> (Ω/□) | < 10 (for c-Si) , < 50 (for thin-film) |
| Optical Transmittance | T(λ) | > 80% in 400–1100 nm |
| Figure of Merit (Haacke) | Φ<sub>TC</sub> | Maximize |
| Refractive Index | n | ~ 2.0 (for ARC function) |
| Work Function | Φ | Matched to carrier type |
| Lateral Resistivity | ρ (Ω·cm) | < 10<sup>-3</sup> (ideal) |
| Thermal Stability | T<sub>max</sub> | > 200 °C (process compat.) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Ω/□"** unit (Ohms per square) is dimensionless — it is a unique unit used in thin-film technology where resistance is independent of the square size of the film, only depending on thickness and resistivity.

## 1.5 Classification of Front Electrode Materials

1. **Transparent Conductive Oxides (TCOs):** ITO, FTO, AZO, GZO.
2. **Metal Grids / Fingers:** Ag, Cu, Al screen-printed grids.
3. **Metallic Nanowire Networks:** AgNW, CuNW meshes.
4. **Carbon Nanomaterials:** Graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs).
5. **Conducting Polymers:** PEDOT:PSS, PANI.
6. **Hybrid / Composite Electrodes:** AgNW + Graphene, metal mesh + polymer.

---
