# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Physics — How a Front Electrode Works

The front electrode operates on the principle of **optical–electrical trade-off**, mathematically described as follows:

### Step 1 — Optical Function
Light of intensity *I*<sub>0</sub>(λ) incident on a thin film of thickness *t* and absorption coefficient α(λ) is transmitted as:

$$T(\lambda) = (1 - R(\lambda)) \cdot e^{-\alpha(\lambda) \cdot t}$$

where *R*(λ) is the **Fresnel surface reflectance**. For a TCE with refractive index *n* on a substrate of index *n*<sub>s</sub>, reflectance is minimized when *n* ≈ √(*n*<sub>s</sub>), explaining why TCOs with n ≈ 2.0 are excellent for Si (n = 3.4) but not perfect.

### Step 2 — Electrical Function
For a uniform TCE film of resistivity ρ and thickness *t*, the **sheet resistance** is:

$$R_{sh} = \frac{\rho}{t} = \frac{1}{\sigma \cdot t}$$

A higher carrier concentration *N* increases σ (good for R<sub>sh</sub>) but increases plasma reflection (bad for T). This is the **doping–transparency trade-off** in doped TCOs like ITO.

### Step 3 — Carrier Collection
Photocurrent density collected per unit area depends on **power losses** in the front electrode:

$$P_{loss} = J_{mpp}^2 \cdot R_{s,front}$$

where J<sub>mpp</sub> is the current density at the maximum power point and R<sub>s,front</sub> is the series resistance contributed by the front electrode (sheet + grid + contact).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **Drude plasma frequency** ω<sub>p</sub> sets the transparency window. For λ > λ<sub>p</sub> (i.e., below ω<sub>p</sub>), the TCO becomes transparent.

## 2.2 Plasma Frequency — Why TCOs Are Transparent

For a free-electron metal oxide, the **plasma wavelength** is:

$$\lambda_p = 2\pi c \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_\infty \varepsilon_0 m^*}{N e^2}}$$

where:
- *N* = free carrier concentration
- *m** = effective mass of electron
- *ε*<sub>∞</sub> = high-frequency dielectric constant
- *c* = speed of light

For typical ITO (*N* ≈ 10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>), λ<sub>p</sub> ≈ 1.0–1.5 µm → the TCO is transparent in visible/NIR.

> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing carrier concentration lowers R<sub>sh</sub> (good) but shifts λ<sub>p</sub> into the visible (bad — increases NIR absorption). The optimum *N* is around **10<sup>20</sup>–10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>**.

## 2.3 Figure of Merit — Quantifying the Trade-off

### Haacke's Figure of Merit (most cited in KTU):

$$\phi_{TC} = \frac{T^{10}}{R_{sh}}$$

where T is the **average visible transmittance**. The exponent **10** was chosen empirically to heavily penalize low transmittance, ensuring practical TCEs score high only when T > 90%.

### Alternative: Drude-Based Figure of Merit (more physical):

$$\phi_D = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot \alpha} \propto \frac{\sigma}{\alpha}$$

where α is the absorption coefficient at a reference wavelength (commonly 550 nm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial benchmark:** ITO achieves Φ<sub>TC</sub> ≈ **10<sup>-2</sup> Ω<sup>-1</sup>** — the gold standard that emerging materials must beat to be commercially viable.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{sh} = \rho / t = 1/(\sigma t)$ | Sheet resistance of a thin film | Ω/□ |
| 2 | $T(\lambda) = (1 - R) e^{-\alpha t}$ | Transmittance of a film | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\phi_{TC} = T^{10} / R_{sh}$ | Haacke figure of merit | Ω<sup>-1</sup> |
| 4 | $\lambda_p = 2\pi c \sqrt{\varepsilon_\infty \varepsilon_0 m^* / (N e^2)}$ | Plasma wavelength of TCO | m |
| 5 | $\omega_p = \sqrt{N e^2 / (\varepsilon_0 m^*)}$ | Plasma frequency | rad/s |
| 6 | $P_{loss} = J_{mpp}^2 \cdot R_s$ | Power loss in series resistance | W/m² |
| 7 | $FF = FF_0 (1 - r_s)$ | Fill-factor loss due to R<sub>s</sub> | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $r_s = R_s \cdot J_{sc} / V_{oc}$ | Normalized series resistance | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\sigma = N e \mu$ | Conductivity of TCO | S/m |
| 10 | $T_{total} = T_{TCO} \cdot T_{abs}$ | Total transmittance of cell | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $n_{TCE} \approx \sqrt{n_{substrate}}$ | ARC condition | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $\eta = (FF \cdot J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc}) / P_{in}$ | Solar cell efficiency | % |

## 2.5 Material-Specific Theory

### 2.5.1 Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)
- In<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>:Sn (90:10 wt%)
- Degenerate n-type semiconductor, *N* ≈ 10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>
- Mobility μ ≈ 30–50 cm²/V·s
- T > 85% at 550 nm; R<sub>sh</sub> ≈ 5–15 Ω/□
- Drawback: Indium scarcity, brittle on flexible substrates

### 2.5.2 Fluorine Tin Oxide (FTO)
- SnO<sub>2</sub>:F
- Higher temperature stability than ITO (up to 600 °C)
- Used in dye-sensitized and perovskite solar cells
- T ≈ 80–82%; R<sub>sh</sub> ≈ 8–15 Ω/□

### 2.5.3 Al-Doped ZnO (AZO)
- Cheaper, earth-abundant alternative to ITO
- *N* ≈ 10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>; μ ≈ 20 cm²/V·s
- T ≈ 85–90%; R<sub>sh</sub> ≈ 10–20 Ω/□
- Can be deposited by ALD, sputtering, MOCVD

### 2.5.4 Silver Nanowires (AgNWs)
- Random mesh of 50–100 nm diameter, 5–20 µm length wires
- T > 90% with R<sub>sh</sub> ≈ 10–50 Ω/□
- Flexible and solution-processable (ideal for polymer/perovskite solar cells)

### 2.5.5 Graphene
- 1-atom-thick carbon sheet; T ≈ 97.7% per layer
- Single layer R<sub>sh</sub> ≈ 60–1000 Ω/□ (too high)
- Requires chemical doping (HNO<sub>3</sub>, AuCl<sub>3</sub>) to reach < 100 Ω/□

### 2.5.6 PEDOT:PSS
- Conducting polymer; solution-processable
- T ≈ 80–95% (tunable by film thickness)
- R<sub>sh</sub> ≈ 50–10<sup>6</sup> Ω/□ (huge range based on formulation)
- *σ* can reach > 4000 S/cm with high-boiling solvent additives (DMSO, EG)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **c-Si PV industry:** Screen-printed Ag fingers + ITO/SiN<sub>x</sub> anti-reflection stack.
- **Perovskite Solar Cells (PSCs):** Often use ITO/FTO substrates, but emerging AgNW/ITO-free PSCs aim to reduce cost and enable roll-to-roll fabrication.
- **Space PV:** Indium Tin Oxide and ZnO are used for radiation hardness.
- **Building-Integrated PV (BIPV):** Semi-transparent modules using AgNW or conducting polymer electrodes for tunable aesthetics.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sheet Resistance of a Thin Film

We start from the fundamental relationship between resistance, resistivity, and geometry.

**Given:** A thin-film square of side *L*, thickness *t*, with current flowing between two opposite edges.

The resistance between opposite edges is:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A} = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{L \cdot t} = \frac{\rho}{t}$$

Since the geometric factor (L/A) reduces to **1/t** for any square (the length and width cancel), the resistance depends **only on film thickness** and **material resistivity**, not on the size of the square. This is the origin of the unit Ω/□ (Ohms per square).

**Substituting** σ = 1/ρ:

$$R_{sh} = \frac{1}{\sigma \cdot t}$$

**Conclusion:** Halving the film thickness doubles R<sub>sh</sub>; doubling the conductivity halves it. For a TCE with σ = 10<sup>4</sup> S/cm and t = 100 nm:

$$R_{sh} = \frac{1}{(10^4 \text{ S/cm})(100 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm})} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10 \text{ Ω/□}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Haacke's Figure of Merit

**Starting principle:** A TCE must maximize T (transmittance) and minimize R<sub>sh</sub> (sheet resistance). The simple ratio T/R<sub>sh</sub> gives equal weight to both, but engineering practice demands that **any drop in T below ~90% is unacceptable** because it directly lowers J<sub>sc</sub> and thus efficiency.

Haacke proposed raising T to a high power to penalize low T:

$$\phi_{TC} = \frac{T^{n}}{R_{sh}}$$

Choosing n = 10 because:
- For T = 0.90: T<sup>10</sup> ≈ 0.349
- For T = 0.80: T<sup>10</sup> ≈ 0.107
- For T = 0.70: T<sup>10</sup> ≈ 0.028

The penalty for a 10% transmittance drop is **> 3×** in figure of merit, accurately reflecting the real-world impact on PV efficiency.

**Numerical Example:**

| Material | T (%) | R<sub>sh</sub> (Ω/□) | T<sup>10</sup> | Φ<sub>TC</sub> (×10<sup>-3</sup> Ω<sup>-1</sup>) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ITO | 90 | 10 | 0.349 | 34.9 |
| FTO | 82 | 12 | 0.134 | 11.2 |
| AZO | 88 | 15 | 0.279 | 18.6 |
| AgNW Mesh | 92 | 20 | 0.434 | 21.7 |
| Graphene (doped) | 90 | 100 | 0.349 | 3.49 |

**Conclusion:** ITO remains the benchmark. Doped graphene approaches the "good enough" range for flexible PVs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Plasma Wavelength for a TCO

**Starting from the Drude model** for the dielectric response of a free-electron gas:

$$\varepsilon(\omega) = \varepsilon_\infty - \frac{\omega_p^2}{\omega^2}$$

The plasma frequency is defined as the frequency at which ε(ω) = 0:

$$\omega_p^2 = \frac{N e^2}{\varepsilon_0 m^*}$$

Converting to wavelength:

$$\lambda_p = \frac{2\pi c}{\omega_p} = 2\pi c \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_0 m^*}{N e^2}}$$

Including the high-frequency background dielectric:

$$\lambda_p = 2\pi c \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_\infty \varepsilon_0 m^*}{N e^2}}$$

**Numerical Example for ITO:**
- *N* = 10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup> = 10<sup>27</sup> m<sup>-3</sup>
- *m** = 0.35 *m*<sub>e</sub> = 0.35 × 9.11×10<sup>-31</sup> kg
- *ε*<sub>∞</sub> = 4.0
- *e* = 1.6×10<sup>-19</sup> C
- *ε*<sub>0</sub> = 8.85×10<sup>-12</sup> F/m
- *c* = 3×10<sup>8</sup> m/s

Step 1 — Compute the numerator inside the square root:

$$\varepsilon_\infty \varepsilon_0 m^* = (4.0)(8.85 \times 10^{-12})(0.35 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31})$$

$$= (4.0)(8.85 \times 10^{-12})(3.19 \times 10^{-31})$$

$$= 1.13 \times 10^{-41}$$

Step 2 — Compute the denominator:

$$N e^2 = (10^{27})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2 = (10^{27})(2.56 \times 10^{-38}) = 2.56 \times 10^{-11}$$

Step 3 — Take the ratio:

$$\frac{1.13 \times 10^{-41}}{2.56 \times 10^{-11}} = 4.41 \times 10^{-31}$$

Step 4 — Square root:

$$\sqrt{4.41 \times 10^{-31}} = 6.64 \times 10^{-16} \text{ s}$$

Step 5 — Multiply by 2πc:

$$\lambda_p = 2\pi (3 \times 10^8)(6.64 \times 10^{-16})$$

$$\lambda_p \approx 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1.25 \, \mu m$$

**Conclusion:** The plasma wavelength is in the near-IR. Light with λ < 1.25 µm (visible + part of NIR) is reflected; light with λ > 1.25 µm passes through. This is exactly why ITO is transparent in the visible spectrum — it is the *silicon-friendly* transparent conductor.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Loss in Front Electrode

**Given:** A solar cell with current density at maximum power point J<sub>mpp</sub> = 30 mA/cm² and a front electrode resistance of 0.5 Ω·cm² (combined sheet + grid contribution).

Power dissipated in the front electrode:

$$P_{loss} = J_{mpp}^2 \cdot R_s = (30 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 0.5 = 9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W/cm}^2 = 0.9 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$

Under AM1.5G illumination (100 mW/cm²), this represents a 0.9% **absolute efficiency loss** — a critical figure for KTU problems.

If a 50% increase in current (e.g., concentration) is applied:

$$P_{loss} = (45 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ mW/cm}^2 \to 1.0\% \text{ loss}$$

**Insight:** Power loss scales with **J<sub>mpp</sub>²** — under concentration PV, the front electrode must be ultra-low resistance (e.g., plated Cu or Ag finger grids).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection Decision Tree

| Application | Required T (%) | Required R<sub>sh</sub> (Ω/□) | Best Material | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| c-Si industrial | > 80 | < 10 | ITO/SiN<sub>x</sub> stack | Plasma-tuned TCO |
| Perovskite planar | > 85 | < 20 | ITO or FTO substrate | Standard lab process |
| Perovskite flexible | > 85 | < 30 | AgNW on PET | Solution-processable |
| Organic PV (OPV) | > 80 | < 50 | PEDOT:PSS | Polarity match, flexibility |
| Dye-sensitized (DSSC) | > 75 | < 15 | FTO (high-T) | High temp sintering of TiO<sub>2</sub> |
| Space / Radiation | > 80 | < 10 | ITO or ZnO | Radiation hardness |
| Tandem / Multi-junction | > 90 | < 5 | ITO + Ag grid | Minimal absorption losses |
| Indoor / BIPV semi-transparent | 30–60 | < 20 | AgNW or mesh | Aesthetic, tunable T |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Figure of Merit" for a transparent conducting electrode. State the Haacke formula and explain the role of the exponent 10.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Figure of Merit (FOM)** of a transparent conducting electrode (TCE) is a quantitative measure of how well a thin-film material simultaneously satisfies the conflicting demands of **high optical transmittance (T)** and **low sheet resistance (R<sub>sh</sub>)**. A higher FOM indicates a better electrode.

Haacke's empirical figure of merit is:

$$\phi_{TC} = \frac{T^{10}}{R_{sh}}$$

The exponent **10** is chosen because, in practical solar cell design, any transmittance drop below ~90% causes a substantial loss in short-circuit current (J<sub>sc</sub>). The 10th power heavily penalizes low T, ensuring that materials with T < 80% are rightly scored poorly. **For example**: a TCE with T = 0.80 has T<sup>10</sup> ≈ 0.107, which is 3.3× lower than a TCE with T = 0.90 (T<sup>10</sup> = 0.349), reflecting the disproportionate real-world impact on PV efficiency.

---

### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Why is Indium Tin Oxide (ITO) used as a standard front electrode in solar cells? List two limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Reasons for use of ITO:**
1. **High visible transmittance** (> 85%) in the 400–700 nm range due to its plasma wavelength lying in the near-IR (~1.25 µm).
2. **Low sheet resistance** (typically 5–15 Ω/□ for 100–200 nm films), owing to its high free carrier concentration (~10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>) and reasonable electron mobility (~30 cm²/V·s).
3. **Good work function match** (~4.5–4.7 eV) for hole extraction in many p-type absorbers.
4. **Mature deposition** techniques (DC/RF magnetron sputtering) suitable for industrial production.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Indium scarcity and cost** — Indium is a rare earth by-product, leading to price volatility and supply-chain concerns.
2. **Brittleness on flexible substrates** — ITO films crack when bent, making them unsuitable for roll-to-roll flexible PVs. They also require high-temperature post-annealing for optimal conductivity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of the Drude model, derive an expression for the plasma wavelength of a transparent conductive oxide. Calculate the plasma wavelength for ITO, given: *N* = 10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>, *m** = 0.35 *m*<sub>e</sub>, *ε*<sub>∞</sub> = 4.0. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the optical and electrical requirements of a front electrode for a solar cell. Compare the suitability of ITO, FTO, and AgNW meshes for use in flexible perovskite solar cells. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (a):**

**Derivation of Plasma Wavelength:**

The Drude model gives the frequency-dependent dielectric constant of a free-electron gas as:

$$\varepsilon(\omega) = \varepsilon_\infty - \frac{\omega_p^2}{\omega^2}$$

The plasma frequency ω<sub>p</sub> is defined as the frequency at which the real part of ε(ω) crosses zero, leading to strong reflection:

$$\omega_p^2 = \frac{N e^2}{\varepsilon_0 m^*}$$

Converting to wavelength using λ<sub>p</sub> = 2πc/ω<sub>p</sub>:

$$\lambda_p = 2\pi c \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_\infty \varepsilon_0 m^*}{N e^2}}$$

**Numerical Calculation for ITO:**

Given:
- *N* = 10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup> = 10<sup>27</sup> m<sup>-3</sup>
- *m** = 0.35 × 9.11 × 10<sup>-31</sup> kg = 3.19 × 10<sup>-31</sup> kg
- *ε*<sub>∞</sub> = 4.0, *ε*<sub>0</sub> = 8.85 × 10<sup>-12</sup> F/m
- *e* = 1.6 × 10<sup>-19</sup> C, *c* = 3 × 10<sup>8</sup> m/s

Step 1 — Numerator:

$$\varepsilon_\infty \varepsilon_0 m^* = (4.0)(8.85 \times 10^{-12})(3.19 \times 10^{-31}) = 1.13 \times 10^{-41}$$

Step 2 — Denominator:

$$N e^2 = (10^{27})(2.56 \times 10^{-38}) = 2.56 \times 10^{-11}$$

Step 3 — Ratio and square root:

$$\frac{1.13 \times 10^{-41}}{2.56 \times 10^{-11}} = 4.41 \times 10^{-31}$$

$$\sqrt{4.41 \times 10^{-31}} = 6.64 \times 10^{-16} \text{ s}$$

Step 4 — Final multiplication:

$$\lambda_p = 2\pi (3 \times 10^8)(6.64 \times 10^{-16}) \approx 1.25 \, \mu m$$

**Result:** λ<sub>p</sub> ≈ **1.25 µm** — this lies in the near-IR, ensuring transparency across the visible spectrum (400–700 nm). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (b):**

**Requirements of a Front Electrode:**

**Optical Requirements:**
- High transmittance (T > 80%) across the solar spectrum matching the absorber bandgap.
- Suitable refractive index (~2.0) to act as an anti-reflective layer.
- Low parasitic absorption in UV/visible.

**Electrical Requirements:**
- Low sheet resistance (R<sub>sh</sub> < 10 Ω/□ for c-Si, < 50 Ω/□ for thin films).
- High lateral conductivity.
- Good ohmic contact to the adjacent transport layer.
- Work function matched to the carrier-selective transport layer.

**Comparison for Flexible Perovskite Solar Cells:**

| Parameter | ITO | FTO | AgNW Mesh |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmittance | 85–90% | 80–82% | 90–95% |
| Sheet Resistance | 5–15 Ω/□ | 8–15 Ω/□ | 10–50 Ω/□ |
| Flexibility | Poor (brittle) | Poor (brittle) | Excellent |
| Process Temp | Room T (sputter) + anneal | > 400 °C | Room T (solution) |
| Cost | High (In) | Moderate | Moderate–Low |
| Compatibility with Perovskite | Excellent | Excellent (high-T only) | Good (with buffer) |

**Best for Flexible PSCs:** **AgNW meshes** — solution processable, flexible, low cost, and provide > 90% transmittance with sufficient R<sub>sh</sub> for small-area flexible cells. ITO is acceptable on rigid glass; FTO is unsuitable due to brittleness and high processing temperature incompatible with flexible polymer substrates. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** What is a transparent conductive oxide? Explain with energy band diagram how ITO achieves both high conductivity and high transparency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the deposition techniques (sputtering, ALD, and solution methods) used for preparing transparent conducting electrodes, listing advantages and limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (a):**

**Definition:**
A **Transparent Conductive Oxide (TCO)** is a wide-bandgap semiconductor oxide (E<sub>g</sub> > 3 eV) that is heavily doped to achieve **degenerate n-type** conductivity, combining optical transparency in the visible range with electrical conductivity comparable to a poor metal.

**Band Diagram Explanation:**

For ITO (In<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>:Sn):
1. **In<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> parent bandgap:** ~3.7 eV (UV-active, hence transparent to visible light).
2. **Sn<sup>4+</sup> doping on In<sup>3+</sup> sites** introduces a **donor level** just below the conduction band minimum (CBM), releasing a free electron per Sn atom.
3. The Fermi level (E<sub>F</sub>) shifts **inside the conduction band** — this is called **degenerate doping** (Burdine shift).
4. Photons with E < E<sub>g</sub> (i.e., visible) cannot excite electrons across the bandgap → **optical transparency**.
5. Free electrons in the partially-filled conduction band provide **metallic-like conduction** → high electrical conductivity.
6. The **Moss–Burstein shift** further widens the effective bandgap:

$$E_g^{eff} = E_g + \frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}(3\pi^2 N)^{2/3}$$

This blue-shifts the absorption edge, further increasing the visible transparency window. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (b):**

**Three Major Deposition Techniques:**

**1. DC/RF Magnetron Sputtering**
- Process: Ar<sup>+</sup> ions bombard a ceramic target (In<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>:SnO<sub>2</sub>), ejecting atoms that deposit on the substrate.
- *Advantages:* High uniformity, large-area scalability (up to Gen 8.5 substrates), good film quality.
- *Limitations:* Requires vacuum, expensive targets, ion bombardment can damage underlying layers (e.g., perovskite), high capital cost.

**2. Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD)**
- Process: Sequential self-limiting surface reactions of precursors (e.g., TMA + H<sub>2</sub>O for Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>; TDMASn + H<sub>2</sub>O for SnO<sub>2</sub>).
- *Advantages:* Atomic-scale thickness control, excellent conformality on rough/textured surfaces, pin-hole free films.
- *Limitations:* Very slow (~1 Å/cycle), high precursor cost, complex instrumentation.

**3. Solution-Based Methods (Spin Coating, Doctor Blade, Spray Pyrolysis)**
- Process: A precursor solution (e.g., AgNW dispersion, sol-gel ZnO, PEDOT:PSS ink) is cast onto the substrate and dried/cured.
- *Advantages:* Low cost, roll-to-roll compatible, no vacuum required, suitable for flexible substrates.
- *Limitations:* Lower film uniformity, sensitivity to ambient conditions (humidity, dust), typically lower conductivity than sputtered films.

**Conclusion for KTU:** For *rigid* Si or perovskite cells on glass → **sputtering** is industrial standard. For *flexible* polymer-substrate perovskite cells → **solution-processed AgNWs or PEDOT:PSS** are the emerging choices. **ALD** is preferred when ultra-thin (~10–20 nm) buffers or passivation layers are required. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Ω/□ with Ω/□ · cm** — students often write "Ohms per square per cm" which is meaningless. The unit is **dimensionless**.
> 2. **Forgetting the squared term in J²R loss** — power loss is J²<sub>mpp</sub> × R<sub>s</sub>, not J<sub>mpp</sub> × R<sub>s</sub>. This is the most common calculation error.
> 3. **Misidentifying work function matching** — a TCO does not always serve as a hole-extracting contact; for n-type absorbers, an n-type TCE is needed for electron collection.
> 4. **Treating T and R<sub>sh</sub> as independent** — they are coupled via the carrier concentration *N*. You cannot optimize them separately.
> 5. **Skipping the units in calculations** — KTU award **0.5 mark** for correct units in numerical problems. Always include units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Front Electrode**

- ✅ **Definition:** Top current-collecting contact on the illuminated side — must be **transparent** AND **conductive**.
- ✅ **Key Trade-off:** Conductivity ↑ (high *N*) ⇔ Transparency ↓ (plasma shift to visible).
- ✅ **Three primary functions:** Optical transmission, electrical collection, ARC matching.
- ✅ **Sheet resistance formula:** R<sub>sh</sub> = ρ/t = 1/(σ·t) — measured in Ω/□.
- ✅ **Plasma wavelength:** λ<sub>p</sub> = 2πc√(ε<sub>∞</sub>ε<sub>0</sub>m*/Ne²) — sets transparency window.
- ✅ **Haacke FOM:** Φ<sub>TC</sub> = T<sup>10</sup>/R<sub>sh</sub> — exponent 10 penalizes low T.
- ✅ **Industrial Standard:** ITO on glass (R<sub>sh</sub> ≈ 10 Ω/□, T ≈ 90%).
- ✅ **Emerging flexible TCEs:** AgNW meshes, PEDOT:PSS, doped graphene.
- ✅ **Power loss:** P<sub>loss</sub> = J²<sub>mpp</sub>·R<sub>s</sub> — scales quadratically with current.
- ✅ **Moss–Burstein shift** widens effective bandgap in degenerate TCOs.
- ✅ **ARC condition:** n<sub>TCE</sub> ≈ √(n<sub>substrate</sub>) — TCOs with n ≈ 2.0 are ideal for Si.
- ✅ **Common KTU pitfalls:** Wrong units, missing squared term, treating T and R<sub>sh</sub> independently.
- ✅ **Most cited figure:** For ITO, λ<sub>p</sub> ≈ 1.25 µm (NIR) — visible light passes.
- ✅ **Refractive index of ITO ≈ 2.0** — naturally acts as ARC for Si (n = 3.4) by reducing the index mismatch.
- ✅ **Haacke FOM benchmark:** Φ<sub>TC</sub> > 10<sup>-3</sup> Ω<sup>-1</sup> for production-grade electrodes.
- ✅ **Tested topics in KTU:** Definition of FOM, plasma wavelength derivation, ITO vs FTO comparison, flexible TCE challenges, deposition techniques comparison.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials Used
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Materials Used ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of PV Materials > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 - Materials Used):** > *Photovoltaic (PV) materials are specialized semiconducting substances capable of absorbing incident photons and converting solar radiant energy directly into electrical energy via the **photovoltaic effect**, characterized by the generation of electron-hole pairs (excitons) at a p-n junction or hetero-interface, followed by charge separation and selective collection at the external electrodes.* Photovoltaic materials are broadly classified into **four generations**, distinguished by their atomic structure, manufacturing cost, power conversion efficiency (PCE), and commercial maturity. The choice of a PV material fundamentally dictates the cell architecture (substrate vs. superstrate), the deposition technique, the operating temperature range, and the long-term degradation behavior of the module. ### 1.2 Classification of PV Materials (Generations) | Generation | Material Class | Representative Examples | Typical PCE Range | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1st Gen** | Crystalline Silicon (c-Si) | Mono-c-Si, Multi-c-Si | 18 % – 26 % | | **2nd Gen** | Thin-Film Inorganics | a-Si:H, CdTe, CIGS (Cu(In,Ga)Se₂) | 10 % – 22 % | | **3rd Gen** | Emerging / Organic | Dye-sensitized, Organic PV, Perovskites | 12 % – 33 % | | **4th Gen** | Hybrid / Tandem | Perovskite/Si Tandems, Multi-junction III-V | 30 % – 47 % | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Solar Window vs. The Solar Paint"** > Imagine a window made of rigid, crystal-clear glass that perfectly conducts the sunlight's energy (this is **mono-crystalline silicon**). Now imagine a flexible plastic sheet that you can roll up like wallpaper and stick on any wall — this is a **polymer/organic PV**. The "rigid glass" approach uses thick wafers (~180 µm) but achieves excellent efficiency, while the "flexible paint" approach uses only microns of active material. Each has trade-offs in cost, efficiency, flexibility, and lifespan. ### 1.4 Key Material Performance Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The four pillars of PV material selection are: > 1. **Band Gap (Eg)** — Should ideally be between **1.1 eV and 1.6 eV** for optimal solar spectrum absorption (Shockley–Queisser limit). > 2. **Absorption Coefficient (α)** — Higher α means thinner active layers are required. > 3. **Charge Carrier Mobility (µ)** — Determines how fast electrons/holes travel to electrodes. > 4. **Diffusion Length (Ln, Lp)** — Must be longer than the absorber thickness to minimize recombination. ### 1.5 Standard Constants Used in PV Material Analysis - **Speed of light in vacuum:** `c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s` - **Planck's constant:** `h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s` - **Elementary charge:** `q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C` - **Boltzmann constant:** `kB = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K` - **Reference solar spectrum:** AM1.5G, irradiance **1000 W/m²**, T = 298.15 K ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 First Generation Materials — Crystalline Silicon (c-Si) #### 2.1.1 Monocrystalline Silicon (Mono-c-Si) - **Structure:** Single continuous crystal lattice, grown via the **Czochralski (CZ)** process or **Float Zone (FZ)** method. - **Purity:** 99.9999 % (6N) to 99.9999999 % (9N) pure. - **Wafer thickness:** ~180 µm (current industrial standard). - **Band gap:** 1.12 eV (indirect). - **Why it works:** Indirect band gap requires phonon assistance, leading to lower α, but extremely long minority carrier diffusion lengths (>500 µm) compensate. - **Disadvantage:** High energy payback time (~2 years) and high-temperature processing (>1400 °C). #### 2.1.2 Polycrystalline Silicon (Multi-c-Si) - **Structure:** Multiple silicon crystallites (grains) separated by grain boundaries. - **Band gap:** 1.12 eV (indirect). - **Why it's used:** ~40 % cheaper than mono-c-Si due to simpler directional solidification casting; grain boundaries act as recombination centers, reducing efficiency. #### 2.1.3 Amorphous Silicon (a-Si:H) - **Structure:** Hydrogenated amorphous network, deposited via PECVD (Plasma-Enhanced Chemical Vapor Deposition). - **Band gap:** 1.7–1.8 eV (tunable). - **Why it matters:** Higher absorption coefficient than c-Si (100× greater), so only ~1 µm thickness needed. Used in flexible thin-film modules and tandem cells. - **Problem:** **Staebler–Wronski degradation** — efficiency drops by 10–30 % under initial light exposure due to dangling bond defects. ### 2.2 Second Generation Materials — Compound Thin-Film Semiconductors #### 2.2.1 Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) - **Structure:** Zinc-blende (cubic), direct band gap of **1.45 eV** (near optimal for solar spectrum). - **Absorption coefficient:** α > 10⁵ cm⁻¹ (absorbs 99 % of sunlight in just 2 µm). - **Why it works:** Near-ideal Eg, simple binary compound, low-cost deposition (vapor transport deposition, CSS). - **Limitation:** Toxicity of Cd, scarcity of Te, no wide-bandgap window layer for heterojunction. #### 2.2.2 Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS) - **Structure:** Chalcopyrite, direct band gap tunable from **1.04 eV** (pure CIS) to **1.68 eV** (pure CGS) by varying the Ga/(In+Ga) ratio. - **Absorption coefficient:** > 10⁵ cm⁻¹. - **Why it works:** Highest PCE among thin films (~22.6 % lab record); can be deposited on flexible substrates (Mo foil, polyimide). - **Limitation:** Complex multi-element composition, expensive vacuum processes. #### 2.2.3 Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) - **Structure:** Zinc-blende, direct band gap **1.42 eV**. - **Why it works:** Highest efficiency for single-junction cells (~29 %); radiation-hard, used in space applications. - **Limitation:** Extremely expensive; mainly used in multi-junction (MJ) tandem cells. ### 2.3 Third Generation Materials — Emerging & Polymer-Based #### 2.3.1 Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs) - **Components:** TiO₂ nanostructured photoanode, Ruthenium-based dye (e.g., N3, N719), liquid I⁻/I₃⁻ electrolyte, Pt counter electrode. - **Working principle:** Photon absorption by dye → electron injection into TiO₂ → hole transfer to electrolyte. - **Efficiency:** ~12–14 %. #### 2.3.2 Organic Photovoltaics (OPV) - **Active layer:** Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ) blend of **donor** (e.g., P3HT, PTB7) and **acceptor** (e.g., PCBM, ITIC) polymers. - **Why it matters:** Solution-processable, roll-to-roll printable, semi-transparent, flexible. - **Efficiency:** ~18–19 % (recent non-fullerene acceptor blends). #### 2.3.3 Perovskite Solar Cells (PSCs) - **Structure:** ABX₃ (e.g., CH₃NH₃PbI₃ — MAPbI₃, FAPbI₃). - **Band gap:** Tunable from **1.2 eV to 2.3 eV** by halide substitution (I → Br → Cl). - **Why it's revolutionary:** Defect tolerance, high absorption coefficient, low-temperature solution processing. - **Efficiency:** Rapid rise from 3.8 % (2009) to **>26 %** (2023 lab record). - **Limitation:** Stability under moisture, heat, and UV; Pb toxicity. ### 2.4 Polymer Materials Used in PV Modules (Encapsulants & Substrates) > [!NOTE] > Although the active absorber may not be a polymer, polymers are essential **support materials** in every PV module: | Polymer | Function in Module | Key Properties | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **EVA (Ethylene Vinyl Acetate)** | Encapsulant film | Optical transparency >91 %, UV resistant, crosslinkable | | **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride)** | Back-sheet protective layer | UV stability, weather resistance, flame retardancy | | **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** | Front-sheet / substrate | Mechanical strength, optical clarity | | **TPT (Tedlar-PET-Tedlar)** | Back-sheet composite | Long-term outdoor durability | | **PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane)** | Flexible encapsulant for emerging PV | High transparency, low Young's modulus | | **PMMA (Polymethyl methacrylate)** | Transparent protective cover | High transmittance, scratch resistance | ### 2.5 Transparent Conducting Oxides (TCOs) — Electrode Materials - **ITO (Indium Tin Oxide):** Work function ~4.7 eV, transmittance >85 %, sheet resistance <15 Ω/□ (industry benchmark). - **FTO (Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide):** Better thermal stability, used in CdTe and perovskite cells. - **AZO (Aluminum-doped ZnO):** Low-cost alternative to ITO. ### 2.6 Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC) Materials - **Purpose:** Reduce reflection losses from ~30 % (bare Si) to <3 %. - **Materials:** SiNx (silicon nitride, n ≈ 2.0), TiO₂ (n ≈ 2.5), MgF₂ (n ≈ 1.38 for dual-layer ARC). ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Photon Energy | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $h$ Planck const, $\nu$ frequency, $\lambda$ wavelength (m) | | Cut-off Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1240}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ nm | $\lambda_c$ in nm, $E_g$ in eV | | Absorption Coefficient | $\alpha(h\nu) = A\left(h\nu - E_g\right)^{n}$ | $A$ const, $n = 0.5 direct, 2 indirect | | Beer–Lambert Law | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | $x$ depth into absorber | | Carrier Concentration (intrinsic) | $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \, e^{-E_g / 2k_B T}$ | $N_C, N_V$ effective DOS | | Diffusion Length | $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ | $D_n$ diffusivity, $\tau_n$ lifetime | | Open-Circuit Voltage (approx.) | $V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{k_B T}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{J_{sc}}{J_0} + 1\right)$ | $J_{sc}$ short-circuit current density | | Fill Factor (empirical) | $FF = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$ | $v_{oc} = \dfrac{qV_{oc}}{n k_B T}$ | | Shockley–Queisser Limit | $\eta_{max} \approx 33.7\%$ at $E_g \approx 1.34$ eV | Single-junction, 1-sun, AM1.5G | | Reflectivity (single-layer ARC) | $R_{min} = \left(\dfrac{n_0 - n_1^2 / n_s}{n_0 + n_1^2 / n_s}\right)^2$ | $n_0$ air, $n_1$ ARC, $n_s$ substrate | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Selecting the right PV material is the *first* design decision in any solar cell project. Once the absorber is fixed, every downstream process — doping profile, deposition temperature, electrode metallization, encapsulation polymer, and module framing — is determined. This is why KTU examiners frequently ask comparison questions between material classes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cut-off Wavelength from Band Gap **Problem:** A CdTe thin-film solar cell has a band gap of 1.45 eV. Calculate the maximum wavelength of incident photons that can generate electron-hole pairs. **Step 1: Equate photon energy to band gap.** For absorption, photon energy must satisfy $h\nu \geq E_g$. The longest wavelength corresponds to $h\nu = E_g$. **Step 2: Substitute $\nu = c / \lambda$.** $$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_c} = E_g$$ **Step 3: Rearrange for $\lambda_c$.** $$\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$$ **Step 4: Insert numerical values with $hc = 1240$ eV·nm.** $$\lambda_c = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV·nm}}{1.45 \text{ eV}}$$ $$\lambda_c \approx 855.17 \text{ nm}$$ **Conclusion:** Photons with $\lambda \leq 855$ nm are absorbed. This covers ~80 % of the AM1.5G solar spectrum, which explains why CdTe is so efficient as a single-junction absorber. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal ARC Refractive Index **Problem:** For a silicon solar cell (substrate refractive index $n_s = 3.5$) in air ($n_0 = 1.0$), determine the optimal refractive index and minimum thickness of a single-layer ARC. **Step 1: State the zero-reflectance condition.** For destructive interference of reflections at the air-ARC and ARC-Si interfaces, the ARC must satisfy two conditions: refractive index matching and quarter-wavelength thickness. **Step 2: Apply the refractive index matching condition.** $$n_1 = \sqrt{n_0 \cdot n_s}$$ **Step 3: Insert numerical values.** $$n_1 = \sqrt{1.0 \times 3.5}$$ $$n_1 \approx 1.871$$ **Step 4: Apply the quarter-wave thickness condition.** $$t_{ARC} = \dfrac{\lambda_0}{4 n_1}$$ **Step 5: For $\lambda_0 = 600$ nm (peak solar irradiance):** $$t_{ARC} = \dfrac{600 \text{ nm}}{4 \times 1.871}$$ $$t_{ARC} \approx 80.17 \text{ nm}$$ **Conclusion:** An 80-nm-thick coating with $n \approx 1.87$ is required. SiNx deposited by PECVD has $n = 1.9$–2.0, making it the industry-standard ARC for c-Si. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Open-Circuit Voltage of a Silicon Solar Cell **Given:** $J_{sc} = 38$ mA/cm², $J_0 = 10^{-12}$ A/cm², $T = 300$ K. **Step 1: Define the diode equation.** $$J = J_{sc} - J_0 \left(e^{qV / k_B T} - 1\right)$$ **Step 2: At open circuit, $J = 0$ and $V = V_{oc}$.** $$0 = J_{sc} - J_0 \left(e^{qV_{oc} / k_B T} - 1\right)$$ **Step 3: Rearrange.** $$J_0 \left(e^{qV_{oc} / k_B T} - 1\right) = J_{sc}$$ $$e^{qV_{oc} / k_B T} - 1 = \dfrac{J_{sc}}{J_0}$$ **Step 4: Solve for $V_{oc}$.** $$V_{oc} = \dfrac{k_B T}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{J_{sc}}{J_0} + 1\right)$$ **Step 5: Substitute values.** $\dfrac{k_B T}{q} = 0.02585$ V at 300 K. $$V_{oc} = 0.02585 \times \ln\left(\dfrac{38 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-12}} + 1\right)$$ $$V_{oc} = 0.02585 \times \ln(3.8 \times 10^{10})$$ $$V_{oc} = 0.02585 \times 24.06$$ $$V_{oc} \approx 0.622 \text{ V}$$ **Conclusion:** A typical c-Si cell yields $V_{oc} \approx 0.6$–0.7 V, which is why 24–30 cells must be series-connected to form a 12 V module. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection Decision Table | Performance / Cost Factor | Mono-c-Si | Multi-c-Si | a-Si:H | CdTe | CIGS | Perovskite | OPV | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Module PCE (typical)** | 20–24 % | 17–20 % | 6–9 % | 18–20 % | 17–19 % | 22–26 % | 12–18 % | | **Band Gap (eV)** | 1.12 | 1.12 | 1.7 | 1.45 | 1.04–1.68 | 1.2–2.3 | 1.3–2.0 | | **Absorption Coefficient** | Low | Low | High | Very High | Very High | Very High | High | | **Active Layer Thickness** | 180 µm | 180 µm | ~1 µm | 2–5 µm | 1–2 µm | 0.5 µm | 0.1 µm | | **Processing Temperature** | >1400 °C | >1400 °C | 200 °C | 500 °C | 550 °C | 100–150 °C | <150 °C | | **Flexibility** | Rigid | Rigid | Flexible | Rigid | Flexible | Flexible | Flexible | | **Stability (years)** | 25–35 | 25–30 | 10–15 | 25–30 | 20–25 | <5 (research) | <5 | | **Manufacturing Cost ($/W)** | 0.20–0.30 | 0.18–0.25 | 0.30–0.50 | 0.25–0.40 | 0.30–0.50 | 0.10–0.20 (proj.) | 0.20–0.40 | | **Toxicity Concern** | Low | Low | Low | High (Cd) | Low | High (Pb) | Low | | **Commercial Maturity** | Very High | Very High | High | Medium | Medium | Research | Research | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Generic PV Material Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["TOP PROTECTION LAYER"] A1["Tempered Glass Cover
n approx 1.5
Thickness 3 to 4 mm"] end subgraph B["ENCAPSULATION LAYER"] B1["EVA Polymer Film
Transmittance greater than 90 percent
Crosslinked at 150 degree Celsius"] end subgraph C["ACTIVE ABSORBER LAYER"] C1["PV Active Material
Eg range 1.1 to 1.7 eV
Generates electron hole pairs"] end subgraph D["CHARGE TRANSPORT LAYERS"] D1["Electron Transport Layer ETL
TiO2 or C60 or SnO2"] D2["Hole Transport Layer HTL
Spiro OMeTAD or PEDOT PSS"] end subgraph E["TRANSPARENT ELECTRODE"] E1["TCO Front Contact
ITO or FTO or AZO
Sheet resistance less than 15 ohm per sq"] end subgraph F["BACK CONTACT LAYER"] F1["Metal Back Electrode
Aluminum or Silver or Gold
Thickness 100 to 300 nm"] end A1 --> B1 --> D1 --> C1 --> D2 --> E1 C1 --> F1 style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6 style C fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#ef4444 style D fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a style E fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#8b5cf6 style F fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#6b7280 ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow — Material Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Photovoltaic Application Requirement"] --> Q1{"Need Highest Efficiency?"} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Flexible Substrate Required?"} Q1 -->|No| Q3{"Lowest Cost Priority?"} Q2 -->|Yes| Q4["CIGS or Perovskite on Polyimide"] Q2 -->|No| Q5["Mono c Si or III V GaAs Tandem"] Q3 -->|Yes| Q6{"Mass Production Scale?"} Q3 -->|No| Q7["Polymer OPV for Building Integration"] Q6 -->|Yes| Q8["Multi c Si or CdTe"] Q6 -->|No| Q9["Perovskite Tandem Research Stage"] style Start fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#ca8a04 style Q1 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d style Q2 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d style Q3 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d style Q4 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a style Q5 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a style Q6 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d style Q7 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a style Q8 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a style Q9 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a ``` ### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Charge Transport in a Perovskite Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR Sun["Solar Photon
Energy greater than Eg"] --> P1["Perovskite Absorber MAPbI3
Eg 1.55 eV"] P1 --> P2{"Exciton Generation
at 300 K"} P2 --> P3["Electron injected into ETL
TiO2 Conduction Band"] P2 --> P4["Hole accepted by HTL
Spiro OMeTAD HOMO"] P3 --> P5["ITO Transparent Electrode
Collected as Current"] P4 --> P6["Gold Back Electrode
Closed Circuit"] P5 --> P7["External Load Resistor
Voltage Output"] P6 --> P7 style Sun fill:#fef08a,stroke:#eab308 style P1 fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#a855f7 style P2 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626 style P3 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#2563eb style P4 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16a34a style P5 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#ea580c style P6 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#ea580c style P7 fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#6b7280 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1. List any three materials used as anti-reflection coatings (ARC) in silicon solar cells and state the condition for minimum reflectivity.** **Model Answer:** The three commonly used ARC materials are: 1. **Silicon Nitride (SiNx)** — refractive index ~2.0, most widely used in industry via PECVD. 2. **Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂)** — refractive index ~2.5, used in high-efficiency lab cells. 3. **Magnesium Fluoride (MgF₂)** — refractive index ~1.38, used in dual-layer ARCs. **Minimum reflectivity condition:** For a single-layer ARC on a substrate, $n_1 = \sqrt{n_0 \cdot n_s}$ where $n_0$ is the refractive index of air (≈1.0) and $n_s$ is the refractive index of the substrate (≈3.5 for Si). Hence $n_1 \approx 1.87$, and the thickness must be $\lambda/4n_1$ for the design wavelength. --- **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q2. Distinguish between monocrystalline silicon and amorphous silicon with respect to (i) crystal structure, (ii) band gap, and (iii) Staebler-Wronski effect.** **Model Answer:** | Property | Mono-c-Si | a-Si:H | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Crystal structure** | Single continuous crystal | Amorphous network with bonded H | | **Band gap** | 1.12 eV (indirect) | 1.7–1.8 eV (direct-like) | | **Staebler–Wronski effect** | Not observed; very stable | Initial efficiency drops 10–30 % under illumination due to dangling bond defects | **Additional points:** Mono-c-Si requires >1400 °C processing, while a-Si:H is deposited at ~200 °C, making it suitable for flexible substrates. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Describe the classification of photovoltaic materials into four generations. Discuss the working principle and material composition of a Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC) with a neat sketch. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* With reference to Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) thin-film solar cells, explain the structural, optical, and electrical properties that make CdTe a near-ideal photovoltaic material. Mention any two limitations. ### **Model Solution** **(a) Classification of PV Materials:** **Generation 1 — Crystalline Silicon:** Mono-c-Si and Multi-c-Si dominate ~90 % of the global market. They offer high efficiency (20–26 %) and proven reliability (>25 years), but require high-temperature (>1400 °C) processing and thick wafers (180 µm). **Generation 2 — Thin-Film Inorganics:** Includes CdTe, CIGS, and a-Si:H. These use direct band gap materials with very high absorption coefficients, allowing absorber layers as thin as 1–2 µm. Lower manufacturing cost and flexibility are key advantages. **Generation 3 — Emerging / Organic:** Includes DSSCs, organic photovoltaics (OPV), and perovskite solar cells (PSCs). They enable low-cost, solution-processable, flexible devices. PCE ranges from 12 % to 26 % but long-term stability remains the chief challenge. **Generation 4 — Hybrid / Multi-junction:** Tandem cells combining perovskite with silicon, or III-V multi-junction cells (GaInP/GaAs/Ge), push PCE beyond 33 % by stacking absorbers with different band gaps to better utilize the solar spectrum. **Working Principle of DSSC:** Step 1: Photon absorption by the Ruthenium dye molecule adsorbed on TiO₂ surface. Step 2: Dye excitation from ground state (S) to excited state (S*) — electron injected into TiO₂ conduction band. Step 3: Dye is regenerated by I⁻ ions in the electrolyte: 2S⁺ + 3I⁻ → 2S + I₃⁻. Step 4: I₃⁻ diffuses to the Pt counter electrode and is reduced back to I⁻: I₃⁻ + 2e⁻ → 3I⁻. Step 5: Electrons flow through external circuit, producing usable electric current. **Sketch:** A schematic shows — (Front) FTO glass → TiO₂ nanoporous layer with adsorbed dye → I⁻/I₃⁻ electrolyte → Pt counter electrode → FTO glass (Back). Light enters through the front FTO glass. **(b) Properties of CdTe as PV Material:** **Structural:** Zinc-blende (cubic) crystal structure, lattice constant 6.48 Å, formed by close-packed Cd and Te atoms with tetrahedral bonding. Can be deposited as polycrystalline thin film on glass. **Optical:** Direct band gap of **1.45 eV**, which is very close to the Shockley–Queisser optimum of 1.34 eV. Absorption coefficient >10⁵ cm⁻¹, meaning 99 % of usable sunlight is absorbed within just 1–2 µm of CdTe. **Electrical:** High photocarrier lifetime (~µs), reasonable mobility (~100 cm²/V·s), and excellent chemical stability. Defects in CdTe are predominantly shallow acceptors (Cd vacancies), providing intrinsic p-type conductivity without intentional doping. **Limitations:** 1. **Toxicity of Cadmium** — environmental and health concerns; strict EU RoHS compliance required. 2. **Tellurium scarcity** — Te is a by-product of copper refining; annual global production is limited, constraining TW-scale CdTe deployment. 3. **Back-contact formation** — Cu-doped graphite paste is used, but Cu diffusion can degrade long-term performance. 4. **No lattice-matched window layer** — CdS is used but introduces lattice mismatch losses. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Compare the properties of CIGS and CdTe thin-film solar cells. Which one is more suitable for flexible substrate applications and why? **(b)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the role of EVA, PVDF, and TPT polymers in a photovoltaic module. Why are Transparent Conducting Oxides (TCOs) required, and list the three most commonly used TCO materials with their typical sheet resistance values? ### **Model Solution (Question B)** **(a) Comparison of CIGS vs CdTe:** | Property | CIGS | CdTe | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Crystal structure** | Chalcopyrite | Zinc-blende | | **Band gap** | 1.04–1.68 eV (tunable) | 1.45 eV (fixed) | | **Absorption coefficient** | >10⁵ cm⁻¹ | >10⁵ cm⁻¹ | | **Best lab PCE** | ~22.6 % | ~22.1 % | | **Typical module PCE** | 17–19 % | 18–20 % | | **Composition** | Cu(In,Ga)Se₂ (4 elements) | CdTe (binary) | | **Deposition method** | Co-evaporation, sputtering | Vapor transport, CSS | | **Toxicity** | Low (no Cd) | High (Cd) | | **Flexibility potential** | Excellent | Moderate | **More suitable for flexible substrates — CIGS:** CIGS is more suitable because (i) it can be deposited on polyimide or stainless steel foils at temperatures below 550 °C, (ii) it does not contain highly toxic Cd, and (iii) its tunable band gap allows band gap grading for better carrier collection in flexible cells. CdTe generally requires glass substrates due to higher deposition temperatures and the brittleness of CdTe layers. **(b) Role of Polymers in PV Modules:** **EVA (Ethylene Vinyl Acetate):** Acts as the encapsulant sandwiched between the front glass and the solar cells. It provides optical coupling (>91 % transmittance), electrical insulation, mechanical cushioning against thermal cycling, and is cross-linked at 150 °C to form a thermoset that protects cells from moisture and contaminants. **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride):** Used as a protective back-sheet or front-sheet material in PVDF/Tedlar composites. Offers excellent UV resistance, weatherability, and flame-retardancy over decades of outdoor exposure. **TPT (Tedlar-PET-Tedlar):** A sandwich of Tedlar (PVF) / PET / Tedlar used as the standard back-sheet. The Tedlar layers provide UV protection and weather resistance, while PET provides mechanical strength and electrical insulation. **Role of TCOs:** Transparent Conducting Oxides serve as the **front electrode** in most thin-film and emerging PV cells. They must simultaneously provide (i) high optical transparency (>80 %) in the visible spectrum to allow sunlight to reach the absorber, and (ii) low electrical sheet resistance (<20 Ω/□) to efficiently collect photo-generated current. **Three Common TCOs:** 1. **ITO (Indium Tin Oxide)** — Sheet resistance ~10–15 Ω/□, transmittance >85 %, work function ~4.7 eV (industry standard). 2. **FTO (Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide)** — Sheet resistance ~15–25 Ω/□, better thermal stability, used in CdTe and perovskite cells. 3. **AZO (Aluminum-doped Zinc Oxide)** — Sheet resistance ~20–40 Ω/□, lowest cost, used as ITO replacement in research. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks in 'Materials Used' questions:** > 1. **Confusing band gap types:** Students often write "band gap = 1.12 eV" without specifying **direct vs indirect**. Always state the type — c-Si has an *indirect* band gap, CdTe has a *direct* band gap. > 2. **Wrong ARC thickness formula:** Writing $t = \lambda / 2$ instead of $t = \lambda / 4$. The quarter-wavelength condition gives destructive interference. > 3. **Mixing generations:** CIGS is **not** 3rd generation; it is **2nd generation thin-film**. Perovskites are 3rd generation. > 4. **Forgetting the role of polymers:** A common error is to discuss only the active absorber. KTU examiners expect students to know that EVA, PVDF, TPT are polymers essential to module-level operation. > 5. **Shockley–Queisser optimum:** The maximum theoretical PCE is 33.7 % at $E_g = 1.34$ eV, NOT at 1.1 eV (Si's band gap). > 6. **DSSC role of electrolyte:** Many students describe the dye but forget the **regeneration cycle** — I⁻/I₃⁻ redox couple is essential. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Materials Used** > - **Four Generations:** (1) Crystalline Si, (2) Thin-film, (3) Emerging/Organic, (4) Tandem/Hybrid. > - **Silicon:** Mono-c-Si has 1.12 eV indirect Eg; a-Si:H has 1.7 eV direct-like Eg and suffers from Staebler-Wronski degradation. > - **CdTe:** Direct Eg = 1.45 eV; absorption coefficient >10⁵ cm⁻¹; toxicity is main drawback. > - **CIGS:** Tunable Eg from 1.04 to 1.68 eV via Ga/(In+Ga) ratio; chalcopyrite structure; high PCE thin film. > - **Perovskite (ABX₃):** Tunable Eg 1.2–2.3 eV; record PCE >26 %; stability and Pb toxicity are bottlenecks. > - **DSSC:** TiO₂ + Ru dye + I⁻/I₃⁻ electrolyte + Pt counter electrode; efficiency ~12–14 %. > - **OPV:** Bulk heterojunction of donor (P3HT, PTB7) and acceptor (PCBM, ITIC) polymers; solution processable. > - **EVA:** Encapsulant polymer, transmittance >91 %, crosslinks at 150 °C. > - **TPT:** Back-sheet composite; Tedlar/PET/Tedlar layers. > - **TCOs:** ITO, FTO, AZO — must be both transparent and conductive. > - **ARC optimum:** $n_1 = \sqrt{n_0 n_s}$; thickness = $\lambda/4n_1$. > - **Shockley–Queisser:** Max single-junction PCE = 33.7 % at Eg = 1.34 eV. > - **Cut-off wavelength formula:** $\lambda_c = 1240 / E_g$ (nm, eV). > - **DSSC electrolyte role:** Regenerates dye via I⁻ → I₃⁻ conversion at Pt counter electrode.

topics.title: Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Processing" in Photovoltaic Technology?

In the context of Polymer & Photovoltaic Technology, **Processing** refers to the **sequence of controlled physical and chemical manufacturing steps** that convert raw materials (silicon wafers, polymer blends, metal precursors, glass substrates) into a finished, functional solar cell or module capable of converting sunlight into electricity.

The term encompasses everything from **wafer preparation**, **thin-film deposition**, **doping/junction formation**, **contact metallization**, **encapsulation**, and—uniquely for this course—the **solution-based processing of polymer:fullerene bulk heterojunction (BHJ) solar cells**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *Processing* in PEPOT757 is a dual-track topic — students must understand both **inorganic solar cell processing** (c-Si, a-Si, CIGS, CdTe) AND **polymer/organic solar cell processing** (spin-coating, doctor blading, roll-to-roll printing, thermal annealing).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cooking Recipe of a Solar Cell"

Think of fabricating a solar cell exactly like **baking a layered cake**:

| Cake-Making Step | Solar Cell Equivalent |
|------------------|----------------------|
| Greasing the baking tray | **Substrate cleaning** (RCA cleaning, ultrasonic bath) |
| Sifting the dry flour | **Texturing & anti-reflection coating** |
| Pouring the batter in layers | **Deposition of absorber, buffer, TCO layers** |
| Adding baking powder (rising agent) | **Doping** (n-type / p-type impurity addition) |
| Setting oven temperature & time | **Annealing / sintering / crystallization** |
| Decorating the top with icing | **Front-contact metallization (grid fingers)** |
| Wrapping in foil for freshness | **Encapsulation (EVA + back-sheet)** |

Just as a poor baking step ruins the cake, an improperly controlled **temperature, ambient humidity, solvent evaporation rate, or film thickness** can devastate the final power-conversion efficiency (PCE) of a solar device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants to Memorize:**
> - **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** $1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ irradiance, AM 1.5G spectrum, cell temperature **25 °C (298.15 K)**.
> - **Solar Constant (AM 0):** $\approx 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$
> - **Bandgap of crystalline Silicon (c-Si):** $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$
> - **Bandgap of P3HT (donor polymer):** $E_g \approx 1.9 \text{ eV}$
> - **Bandgap of PCBM (acceptor):** $E_g \approx 1.6 \text{ eV}$

---

## 1.3 Classification of PV Processing Technologies

PV processing can be broadly classified into **two super-families** for this course:

**(A) Vacuum-Based / High-Temperature Processing**
- Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD), Plasma-Enhanced CVD (PECVD)
- Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD): Thermal evaporation, e-beam evaporation, Sputtering
- Used for **c-Si, a-Si, CIGS, CdTe, perovskite (co-evaporation)**.

**(B) Solution-Based / Low-Temperature Processing** (Polymer PV focus)
- Spin-coating, Doctor-blade, Slot-die coating
- Screen printing, Inkjet printing, Gravure printing
- Roll-to-roll (R2R) processing on flexible substrates
- Used for **organic photovoltaics (OPV), polymer solar cells, perovskites (spin-coated)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When a KTU question says *"discuss polymer solar cell processing"*, the expected answer weightage is **~70% solution-processing techniques + ~30% device architecture**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal PV Process Flow (Both Tracks)

Regardless of the technology, every solar cell goes through these **five canonical processing stages**:

1. **Substrate Preparation** → cleaning, texturing, planarization.
2. **Absorber Layer Formation** → deposition of the photoactive semiconductor.
3. **Junction Formation** → p-n, p-i-n, Schottky, or bulk heterojunction.
4. **Contact & TCO Deposition** → transparent conductive oxide + metal grid.
5. **Encapsulation & Module Integration** → lamination, framing, junction-box attachment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" Matters in KTU Answers:** Examiners award marks for explaining *why* a step is done — not just listing the step. Example: "RCA cleaning is performed to remove organic and metallic contaminants that would otherwise create surface recombination centers, lowering $V_{OC}$."

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Inorganic PV Processing (c-Si Track)

### Step 1: Substrate Preparation
- **Saw Damage Etching (SDE):** Alkaline KOH/NaOH etch removes ~10 μm of damaged Si left from wire-sawing.
- **RCA Cleaning:** A two-step process — **RCA-1 (SC-1)** removes organics using $\text{NH}_4\text{OH} : \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 : \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (1:1:5); **RCA-2 (SC-2)** removes metals using $\text{HCl} : \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 : \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (1:1:6).
- **Texturing:** Anisotropic etching with KOH + IPA creates random-pyramid surface textures, reducing reflection from **~35% (polished)** to **<12% (textured)**.

### Step 2: Junction Formation (Diffusion)
- **n-type emitter formed by P-diffusion** from a $\text{POCl}_3$ liquid source at ~850 °C.
- The reaction: 
$$\text{POCl}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{P}_2\text{O}_5 + \text{Cl}_2$$
$$\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 + \text{Si} \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + \text{P (dopant)}$$
- **Sheet resistance** target: $\sim 40\text{–}80 \ \Omega/\square$.

### Step 3: Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC)
- **PECVD-deposited SiNx:H** of thickness ~75 nm, where thickness is tuned to quarter-wavelength condition:
$$t = \frac{\lambda}{4 n_{\text{SiN}}}$$
For $\lambda = 600 \text{ nm}$ and $n_{\text{SiN}} \approx 2.0$, $t \approx 75 \text{ nm}$ ✓.

### Step 4: Metallization
- **Screen-printed Ag paste** for front fingers, **Al paste** for back contact, fired at ~750–800 °C in a belt furnace.
- **Hydrogen passivation** occurs during firing — H released from SiNx:H passivates bulk defects.

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive — Polymer/Organic PV Processing (OPV Track)

### Device Architecture
Standard **Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ)** device:
$$\text{Glass} / \text{ITO} / \text{PEDOT:PSS} / \text{Active Layer (P3HT:PCBM)} / \text{Al (or Ca/Al)}$$

### Solution Processing Methods

**(i) Spin Coating**
- Solution dispensed on rotating substrate; centrifugal force spreads it into a thin film.
- **Film thickness relation** (semi-empirical):
$$t \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\omega}}$$
where $\omega$ is angular velocity (rpm). Doubling spin speed reduces thickness by factor $\sqrt{2}$.

**(ii) Doctor Blade (Film Applicator)**
- A blade spreads a fixed volume across a flat substrate at controlled speed.
- **Wet film thickness**:
$$t_{\text{wet}} = \frac{V}{w \cdot L}$$
where $V$ = volume, $w$ = blade width, $L$ = length of coated area.

**(iii) Roll-to-Roll (R2R) Processing**
- Flexible substrates (PET, PEN) processed on moving webs — **key for low-cost, high-throughput polymer PV manufacturing**.
- Techniques: **slot-die coating, gravure printing, flexographic printing, slot-die + meniscus coating**.

### Critical Post-Depositional Step: Thermal Annealing
- For P3HT:PCBM, **annealing at 110–150 °C for 10–20 min** drives:
  1. P3HT crystallization (improves hole mobility).
  2. PCBM aggregation (forms nanoscale phase separation).
  3. Optimal domain size ≈ exciton diffusion length (~10 nm).
> Failure to anneal → PCE drops from ~4% to <0.5%.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | Solar cell **power conversion efficiency** | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $V_{OC} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}+1\right)$ | **Open-circuit voltage** (diode equation) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{\max} I_{\max}}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$ | **Fill factor** (squareness of I-V curve) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $I_{SC} = q \int_{\lambda_1}^{\lambda_2} \Phi(\lambda) \cdot EQE(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Short-circuit current from spectral response | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $\alpha(\lambda) = \dfrac{4\pi k}{\lambda}$ | **Absorption coefficient** (Beer-Lambert) | $\text{cm}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | **Beer-Lambert attenuation** of light in film | $\text{W/m}^2$ or photons/s |
| 7 | $L_D = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ | **Diffusion length** of minority carriers | m |
| 8 | $t_{\text{film}} = \dfrac{k \cdot C^{\beta}}{\omega^{\gamma}}$ | Empirical spin-coat thickness (e.g., $k=1.4$, $\beta=0.4$, $\gamma=0.5$) | nm |
| 9 | $R_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{p \cdot N_A}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}$ | Hertz-Knudsen **deposition rate** (evaporation) | $\text{m/s}$ or $\text{kg/m}^2\text{s}$ |
| 10 | $L_{ex} = \sqrt{D_{ex} \tau_{ex}}$ | **Exciton diffusion length** in polymer | nm |
| 11 | $V_{bi} = \dfrac{k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$ | Built-in potential of p-n junction | Volts |
| 12 | $\rho_s = \dfrac{\rho}{t}$ | **Sheet resistance** of a thin film | $\Omega/\square$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Rule Followed:** All absolute values / "such that" / "divides" use `\vert` or `\mid` — no naked `|` symbols to prevent Markdown table corruption.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost-per-Watt ($/W) reduction:** Solution processing of OPVs targets <$0.20/W vs ~$0.30/W for c-Si.
- **Flexibility & lightweight:** R2R-processed polymer PV enables **building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV)** and **wearable electronics**.
- **Scalability:** Module efficiencies of 10–12% achieved in lab-scale polymer solar cells; commercialization requires uniform large-area processing — a key engineering challenge.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Solar Cell Power Conversion Efficiency $\eta$

Starting from the **two-diode equivalent circuit** of a real solar cell under illumination:

$$I = I_L - I_{01}\!\left(e^{\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{n_1 k_B T}}-1\right) - I_{02}\!\left(e^{\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{n_2 k_B T}}-1\right) - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

For the idealized **single-diode model** (used in 90% of KTU problems), we drop the second diode and shunt term:

$$I = I_L - I_0\!\left(e^{\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{n k_B T}}-1\right)$$

**Step 1 — Short-Circuit Condition ($V = 0$):**

$$I = I_L - I_0(e^{\frac{q I R_s}{n k_B T}}-1)$$

Since $R_s$ is small and $I_0$ is tiny, $I_{SC} \approx I_L$.

**Step 2 — Open-Circuit Condition ($I = 0$):**

$$0 = I_L - I_0\!\left(e^{\frac{q V_{OC}}{n k_B T}}-1\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= I_0\!\left(e^{\frac{q V_{OC}}{n k_B T}}-1\right) \\[4pt]
\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 &= e^{\frac{q V_{OC}}{n k_B T}} \\[4pt]
V_{OC} &= \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) \quad \text{(derived)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Power Point (MPP) condition** $\dfrac{d(P)}{dV}=0$ where $P=IV$:

For an ideal cell with $R_s \to 0$ and $R_{sh} \to \infty$, the **fill factor approximation** is:

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{OC} - \ln(v_{OC} + 0.72)}{v_{OC} + 1}, \quad \text{where } v_{OC} = \dfrac{q V_{OC}}{n k_B T}$$

**Step 4 — Final efficiency formula:**

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{\text{in}}}}$$

> **Textual Logic:** The output power is the product of the open-circuit voltage, the short-circuit current, and the "squareness factor" (FF) — all divided by the incident optical power. The FF captures how "rectangular" the I-V curve is.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Beer-Lambert Absorption in an Active Layer

A light beam of intensity $I_0$ enters a polymer film. After traversing thickness $x$, the intensity $I(x)$ is:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha(\lambda)\, x}$$

**Derivation — differential form:**

In an infinitesimal slice of thickness $dx$, the absorbed fraction is proportional to the intensity present:

$$dI = -\alpha(\lambda)\, I(x)\, dx$$

**Separating variables and integrating from 0 to $x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dI}{I} &= -\alpha \, dx \\[4pt]
\int_{I_0}^{I(x)} \frac{dI}{I} &= -\alpha \int_{0}^{x} dx \\[4pt]
\ln I(x) - \ln I_0 &= -\alpha x \\[4pt]
I(x) &= I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Penetration depth** (1/α) — distance at which intensity drops to 1/e (~37%):

$$\delta_p = \frac{1}{\alpha(\lambda)}$$

**Engineering implication for polymer PV:** P3HT has $\alpha \sim 10^5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ at 500 nm, so $\delta_p \approx 100 \text{ nm}$ — meaning **active layer thickness of 100–200 nm is sufficient** to absorb most visible light. This is why polymer solar cells are so thin and flexible.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Hertz-Knudsen Evaporation Deposition Rate

For thermal evaporation (used in PVD for electrodes and some absorbers), the rate at which atoms leave a source of vapor pressure $p$ at temperature $T$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{dep}} &= \frac{p \cdot N_A}{\sqrt{2\pi M k_B T}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{p}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}} \quad \left[\text{molecules per area per time}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation (Maxwell-Boltzmann basis):**

The flux of molecules crossing a plane from one side is the number density times the average velocity in the direction of crossing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi &= n \cdot \langle v_z \rangle \\[4pt]
n &= \frac{p}{k_B T}, \quad \langle v_z \rangle = \sqrt{\frac{k_B T}{2\pi m}} \\[4pt]
\Phi &= \frac{p}{k_B T} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k_B T}{2\pi m}} = \frac{p}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This $\Phi$ is the number of molecules striking a unit area per unit time. Multiplied by molecular mass $m$ and the **sticking coefficient** $S_c$ (0 to 1), we obtain the mass deposition rate per unit area:

$$\dot{m} = S_c \cdot m \cdot \frac{p}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}$$

> **Textual Logic:** Higher source temperature → higher vapor pressure → higher deposition rate. Lower molecular mass → higher velocity → higher flux. The sticking coefficient captures the fraction of incident molecules that *actually adhere* to the substrate.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Processing Routes

| Property | Thermal Evaporation | Spin Coating | Doctor Blade | R2R Slot-Die |
|----------|--------------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Vacuum needed?** | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Throughput** | Low | Low–Medium | Medium | **Very High** |
| **Material efficiency** | Low (10–20%) | Low (<5%) | Medium (50–80%) | **High (>90%)** |
| **Film uniformity** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Good–Excellent |
| **Typical thickness** | 50–500 nm | 50–500 nm | 100 nm – 10 μm | 100 nm – 10 μm |
| **Suitable for polymers?** | No (degrades) | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Cost** | High | Low | Low | **Very Low** |
| **Scalable to modules?** | Limited | Limited | Moderate | **Yes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Questions on *"Compare solution processing vs vacuum deposition for polymer solar cells"* are very common. The matrix above gives a structured way to score full 7 marks.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (for KTU-style practice)

**Problem:** A polymer solar cell has $V_{OC} = 0.72$ V, $I_{SC} = 12$ mA/cm², $FF = 0.65$, illuminated at AM 1.5G (1000 W/m²). Calculate the efficiency.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{in}} &= 1000 \text{ W/m}^2 = 100 \text{ mW/cm}^2 \\[4pt]
P_{\max} &= V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF \\[4pt]
&= 0.72 \text{ V} \times 12 \text{ mA/cm}^2 \times 0.65 \\[4pt]
&= 0.72 \times 12 \times 0.65 \text{ mW/cm}^2 \\[4pt]
&= 5.616 \text{ mW/cm}^2 \\[4pt]
\eta &= \frac{5.616}{100} = 0.05616 = 5.62\% \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Solar Cell Processing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Selection] --> B[Substrate Preparation]
    B --> B1[Mechanical Cleaning]
    B --> B2[Chemical Cleaning RCA-1 RCA-2]
    B --> B3[Surface Texturing or Planarization]
    B3 --> C[Absorber Layer Deposition]
    C --> C1{Vacuum or Solution Route}
    C1 -->|Vacuum| C2[CVD PECVD Sputtering Evaporation]
    C1 -->|Solution| C3[Spin Coat Doctor Blade Slot-Die]
    C2 --> D[Junction Formation]
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Doping Diffusion Annealing]
    D1 --> E[Contact and TCO Deposition]
    E --> E1[ITO AZO Sputtering]
    E --> E2[Metal Grid Screen Print Evaporation]
    E2 --> F[Annealing and Passivation]
    F --> G[Encapsulation and Module Assembly]
    G --> H[Testing IV QE Lifetime]
    H --> I[Finished PV Module]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Polymer Solar Cell (BHJ) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Glass or PET Substrate] --> P2[ITO Sputtering and Patterning]
    P2 --> P3[PEDOT PSS Spin Coat Hole Transport Layer]
    P3 --> P4[Active Layer P3HT PCBM in Chlorobenzene]
    P4 --> P5[Spin Coat or Doctor Blade]
    P5 --> P6[Low Work Function Cathode Evaporation Ca Al]
    P6 --> P7[Thermal Annealing 110 to 150 C]
    P7 --> P8[Encapsulation Glass or Barrier Film]
    P8 --> P9[OPV Device Testing]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison: Vacuum vs Solution Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VacuumRoute [Vacuum Based High Temperature Processing]
        V1[Load Lock] --> V2[High Vacuum Chamber 10 to the power minus 6 mbar]
        V2 --> V3[Source Material Heating]
        V3 --> V4[Vapor Flux to Substrate]
        V4 --> V5[Film Growth on Cooled Substrate]
        V5 --> V6[In Situ Monitoring QCM]
    end
    subgraph SolutionRoute [Solution Based Low Temperature Processing]
        S1[Polymer Fullerene Blend in Solvent] --> S2[Filtration 0.45 micron]
        S2 --> S3[Deposition Spin or Blade or Slot-Die]
        S3 --> S4[Solvent Evaporation and Drying]
        S4 --> S5[Thermal Annealing N2 Ambient]
        S5 --> S6[Film Crystallization and Phase Separation]
    end
    VacuumRoute --> Outcome[Final PV Device]
    SolutionRoute --> Outcome
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `V1`, `P3`, `S5`) — no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone IDs. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text, free of bold/italic/HTML tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ)" architecture in polymer solar cells. Why is it preferred over a bi-layer architecture?  `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ)** is a nanoscale interpenetrating network of an **electron donor** (conjugated polymer, e.g., P3HT) and an **electron acceptor** (fullerene derivative, e.g., PCBM), blended together and phase-separated into bicontinuous domains of ~10–20 nm size.

**Why preferred over bi-layer (planar) heterojunction:**

1. The **exciton diffusion length** in organic semiconductors is only ~5–10 nm. In a planar bi-layer, excitons generated farther than this distance from the interface recombine and are lost. BHJ's distributed D-A interface ensures **every exciton can reach a dissociation site within its lifetime**.

2. BHJ provides **interpenetrating pathways** for both holes (through donor) and electrons (through acceptor) to reach their respective electrodes → higher charge extraction efficiency.

3. BHJ enables **thick active layers** (100–200 nm) → more light absorption without sacrificing charge collection.

**(3 marks — for a clear definition + 2 reasons)**

---

### Question 2: List any three differences between thermal evaporation and spin-coating as deposition methods for solar cell fabrication.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Thermal Evaporation | Spin-Coating |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Ambient** | Requires **high vacuum** (10⁻⁶ mbar) | Ambient air / N₂ glovebox |
| **Mechanism** | Physical vapor transport | Centrifugal spreading of solution |
| **Material suitability** | Small molecules, metals | Polymers, soluble organics |
| **Film thickness control** | Quartz crystal monitor | Spin speed + solution concentration |
| **Material waste** | High (10–20% efficiency) | Very high (often >95% wasted) |
| **Throughput** | Low | Low–Medium |

**(3 marks — 1 mark for each valid difference, tabular format preferred for clarity.)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **step-by-step processing of a bulk-heterojunction polymer solar cell** starting from ITO-coated glass substrate. Mention all materials, solution concentrations, spin parameters, and annealing conditions.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of **thermal annealing** on the morphology and performance of a P3HT:PCBM bulk-heterojunction solar cell. Support your answer with the expected change in fill factor and PCE.  **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step BHJ Processing (7 marks):**

| Step | Process | Conditions / Materials |
|------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Substrate**: Pre-cleaned ITO-coated glass (sheet resistance ~15 Ω/□) | Ultrasonic bath in acetone, IPA, DI water (10 min each) |
| 2 | **UV-Ozone treatment** of ITO | 15 min — improves wettability, removes residual organics, raises work function |
| 3 | **Hole Transport Layer (HTL)**: PEDOT:PSS spin-coated | 4000 rpm, 40 s → ~40 nm film; baked at 120 °C, 10 min |
| 4 | **Active layer**: P3HT:PCBM (1:1 w/w) in 1,2-dichlorobenzene (20–30 mg/mL) | Spin-coat 600–1000 rpm, 60 s → ~200 nm |
| 5 | **Solvent annealing** (optional): kept in covered Petri dish for 1–2 h | Slow solvent evaporation → larger crystalline domains |
| 6 | **Cathode**: Ca (~20 nm) + Al (~100 nm) deposited by thermal evaporation | High vacuum (10⁻⁶ mbar) |
| 7 | **Thermal annealing** of complete device | 110–150 °C, 10–20 min, N₂ ambient |
| 8 | **Encapsulation** | Glass cover slip + UV-curable epoxy |

**(Marking: 1 mark for substrate prep, 1 for HTL, 2 for active layer (with parameters), 1 for cathode, 1 for annealing, 1 for encapsulation.)**

---

**Part (b) — Role of Thermal Annealing (7 marks):**

Thermal annealing drives **three critical morphological changes** in the P3HT:PCBM blend:

1. **P3HT Crystallization:** As-cast films are largely amorphous. Annealing at ~110 °C provides thermal energy for polymer chains to align into π–π stacked crystallites. This increases **hole mobility** ($\mu_h$) by ~2 orders of magnitude.

2. **PCBM Aggregation & Diffusion:** PCBM molecules become mobile, diffuse, and aggregate into nanoscale clusters (~10–20 nm). This creates an **interpenetrating network** with the polymer — the hallmark of an efficient BHJ.

3. **Optimal Phase Separation:** Too little annealing → amorphous, low mobility; too much → large domains (>20 nm) exceeding the exciton diffusion length → losses. **Optimum: ~150 °C, 10 min.**

**Quantitative effect on device parameters (typical literature values):**

| Parameter | As-Cast | After Annealing (150 °C, 10 min) |
|-----------|---------|----------------------------------|
| $V_{OC}$ | 0.55 V | 0.62 V |
| $I_{SC}$ | 4.5 mA/cm² | 9.8 mA/cm² |
| $FF$ | 0.40 | 0.65 |
| $\eta$ (PCE) | ~1.0% | ~3.9% |

**Mechanism summary in one line:** Annealing simultaneously improves **light absorption** (more crystalline P3HT absorbs stronger), **charge generation** (better D-A interface), and **charge transport** (higher mobility) — boosting all three I-V parameters.

**(Marking: 2 marks for mechanism, 2 marks for phase separation, 3 marks for tabulated device improvement.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**  `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **vacuum-based deposition** techniques (thermal evaporation, sputtering, CVD/PECVD) with **solution-based deposition** techniques (spin coating, doctor blade, slot-die) for solar cell fabrication. Highlight the suitability of each for polymer solar cells.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer solar cell is illuminated at AM 1.5G (1000 W/m²) and produces $V_{OC} = 0.65$ V, $I_{SC} = 14$ mA/cm², $FF = 0.62$. Calculate (i) the maximum power output per unit area, and (ii) the power conversion efficiency. If 5% of the incident power is lost due to reflection, what is the **effective efficiency**?  **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Comparison of Deposition Techniques (7 marks):**

**Vacuum-based techniques:**

- **Thermal Evaporation:** Source material heated to vaporization in high vacuum; vapor condenses on cooler substrate. Used for **metals (Al, Ag, Au), Ca, small molecules**. **Unsuitable for polymers** because polymers decompose before evaporating.

- **Sputtering:** Inert gas (Ar) ions bombard a target, ejecting atoms that deposit on substrate. Used for **TCOs (ITO, AZO), SiNₓ ARC**. Can cause plasma damage to polymer layers.

- **CVD / PECVD:** Gaseous precursors react on heated substrate to form solid film; PECVD uses plasma to lower deposition temperature. Used for **a-Si, SiNₓ, doped layers**. High temperature (≥400 °C) is incompatible with polymer substrates.

**Solution-based techniques:**

- **Spin Coating:** Solution dropped on spinning substrate; centrifugal force thins film. Thickness controlled by rpm and solution viscosity. **Ideal for polymer active layers and PEDOT:PSS.** High material waste.

- **Doctor Blade:** Blade spreads a fixed solution volume at controlled speed across a flat substrate. **Simple, low-waste (~50–80% efficiency), suitable for lab-scale OPV fabrication**.

- **Slot-Die Coating:** Solution metered through a precision die-slot onto a moving substrate. **Industry-standard for R2R processing of polymer solar modules.** Excellent uniformity, >90% material efficiency, scalable.

**Summary for polymer PV:**

Solution processing wins **decisively** for polymer solar cells because polymers are soluble, thermally fragile, and must be processed at low temperatures on flexible substrates — exactly the regime where vacuum techniques are unsuitable or too expensive.

**(Marking: 2 marks for vacuum, 3 marks for solution, 2 marks for polymer-specific conclusion.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_{OC} = 0.65$ V, $I_{SC} = 14 \text{ mA/cm}^2$, $FF = 0.62$, $P_{\text{in}} = 100 \text{ mW/cm}^2$.

**(i) Maximum power per unit area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\max} &= V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF \\[4pt]
&= 0.65 \text{ V} \times 14 \text{ mA/cm}^2 \times 0.62 \\[4pt]
&= 5.642 \text{ mW/cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Power conversion efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{\max}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{5.642}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 0.05642 = 5.64\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reflection loss correction (5% reflected):**

- **Effective absorbed power** = $100\% - 5\% = 95\%$ of $P_{\text{in}}$
- $P_{\text{in,eff}} = 0.95 \times 100 = 95 \text{ mW/cm}^2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{P_{\max}}{P_{\text{in,eff}}} = \frac{5.642}{95} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0594 = 5.94\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** When accounting only for *absorbed* light, the device is actually ~6% efficient at converting absorbed photons — a fairer figure of merit for the intrinsic material quality.

**(Marking: 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer for (i); 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer for (ii); 1 mark for reflection correction with proper interpretation.)**

---
