# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Photovoltaic (PV) Materials

Photovoltaic (PV) materials are specialized semiconductors or molecular systems that directly convert incident photons (light energy) into electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect**, first observed by Becquerel in 1839 and operationally demonstrated in silicon by Chapin, Fuller, and Pearson at Bell Labs in 1954. In the context of the **KTU PEPOT757** syllabus, the study of "Materials Used" forms the backbone of Module 3 because the choice of material dictates the device architecture, the achievable power conversion efficiency (PCE), the spectral absorption window, the long-term stability, and the manufacturing cost of any solar cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Photovoltaic materials are light-absorbing semiconductors (inorganic, organic, or hybrid) whose electronic bandgap ($E_g$) lies in the solar spectral range (approximately **0.6 eV to 3.5 eV**) and which generate free electron–hole pairs upon photon absorption, enabling charge separation and extraction at junction interfaces.*

## 1.2 The Five Functional Material Families in a PV Cell

A working PV cell is not made of a single substance; it is a layered stack of **five functionally distinct material classes**:

1. **Absorber (Active Layer):** Generates excitons or free carriers.
2. **Transport Layers:** Electron Transport Layer (ETL) and Hole Transport Layer (HTL).
3. **Transparent Conductive Electrode (TCE):** Allows light in and collects current.
4. **Substrate / Backing Electrode:** Provides mechanical support and back contact.
5. **Encapsulant:** Protects against moisture, UV, and mechanical damage.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mail-Room Sun-Catcher"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Think of a Solar Cell as a Specialized Mail Room:**
> Imagine a mail room (the solar cell) that receives letters (photons) of different sizes. The **absorber** is the sorting clerk who only opens envelopes within a specific size range (the bandgap). The **transport layers** are conveyor belts — one for urgent outgoing mail (electrons) and one for incoming replies (holes). The **transparent electrode** is a glass ceiling that lets light in but carries electricity out, and the **encapsulant** is the weather-proof roof keeping everything dry. Choosing the wrong "clerk" (material) means letters pile up unopened — exactly like photons being wasted in a poorly matched solar cell.

## 1.4 The Solar Spectrum and the Bandgap Constraint

The Air Mass 1.5 Global (AM1.5G) solar spectrum peaks at approximately **500 nm (≈ 2.48 eV)** and extends from UV through visible into near-infrared. Only photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ contribute to current. This leads to a fundamental trade-off captured by the **Shockley–Queisser Limit (1961)**, which gives a maximum theoretical efficiency of **≈ 33.7 %** for a single-junction cell with $E_g \approx 1.34 \text{ eV}$.

$$E_g \; (\text{eV}) = \frac{1240}{\lambda \; (\text{nm})}$$

where **1240 eV·nm** is the product of Planck's constant and the speed of light ($hc$).

| Photon Energy (eV) | Wavelength (nm) | Spectral Region | PV Usefulness |
|---|---|---|---|
| $> 3.1$ | $< 400$ | Ultraviolet | Lost as heat (thermalization) |
| 1.1 – 3.1 | 400 – 1100 | Visible + Near-IR | **Useful range for Si (1.12 eV)** |
| $< 1.1$ | $> 1100$ | Infrared | Transparent to Si |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of PV Materials (KTU Board Hierarchy)

For exam purposes, KTU expects students to classify materials along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Classification Axis | Categories | KTU 2024 Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Generations** | 1st (Si wafers), 2nd (Thin films), 3rd (Organics/Perovskites/DSSCs) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Material Class** | Elemental (Si, Ge), Compound (III–V, II–VI), Organic (polymers), Hybrid | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Device Geometry** | Bulk heterojunction, planar junction, tandem | ⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.2 First-Generation Materials: Crystalline Silicon (c-Si)

Crystalline silicon dominates **≈ 95 %** of the commercial PV market. Two sub-categories exist:

- **Monocrystalline Silicon (mono-Si):** Single-crystal lattice grown via the **Czochralski (CZ)** process. Carrier lifetime is high ($\tau \sim 1\text{ ms}$). Efficiency of commercial cells: **20–25 %**.
- **Multicrystalline / Polycrystalline Silicon (mc-Si):** Multiple grains separated by grain boundaries that act as recombination centers. Efficiency: **15–20 %**, but cheaper.

**Doping strategy:**
- **n-type emitter** (top, thin): Phosphorus-doped Si, $n \approx 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$.
- **p-type base** (bottom, thick): Boron-doped Si, $p \approx 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why silicon?** Earth-abundant (28 % of Earth's crust), native oxide (SiO₂) is an excellent passivation layer, well-understood fabrication, bandgap $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$ close to the SQ optimum.

## 2.3 Second-Generation Materials: Thin-Film Inorganic Semiconductors

### 2.3.1 Amorphous Silicon (a-Si:H)
Hydrogenated amorphous silicon deposited via Plasma-Enhanced Chemical Vapor Deposition (PECVD). Direct bandgap $\approx 1.7 \text{ eV}$, absorbs strongly in the visible. Suffers from the **Staebler–Wronski Effect** — light-induced degradation up to **30 %** in initial months.

### 2.3.2 Cadmium Telluride (CdTe)
- Group **II–VI** direct bandgap semiconductor.
- $E_g = 1.45 \text{ eV}$ — almost exactly at the SQ optimum.
- High absorption coefficient ($\alpha > 10^4 \text{ cm}^{-1}$) → only **1–2 µm** of CdTe needed.
- Commercial champion efficiency: **≈ 22.1 %** (First Solar).
- Concerns: **Toxicity of Cd**, scarcity of Te.

### 2.3.3 Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS) / Cu(In,Ga)Se₂
- Chalcopyrite structure.
- $E_g$ tunable from **1.04 eV (pure CIS)** to **1.67 eV (pure CGS)** by varying the Ga/(In+Ga) ratio.
- Champion efficiency: **≈ 23.35 %** (NREL).
- Excellent radiation hardness — preferred for space applications.

### 2.3.4 Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)
- Group **III–V** direct bandgap semiconductor.
- $E_g = 1.42 \text{ eV}$, high electron mobility ($\mu_e \approx 8500 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$).
- Champion efficiency (single-junction): **≈ 29.1 %**.
- Used in multi-junction (tandem) cells for spacecraft — efficiency **> 47 %** under concentration.

## 2.4 Third-Generation Materials: Polymers & Hybrid Systems

This is **most heavily weighted** in PEPOT757 due to the course title.

### 2.4.1 Organic Photovoltaic (OPV) Materials
OPVs use carbon-based conjugated polymers or small molecules. The operating unit is the **bulk heterojunction (BHJ)** — an interpenetrating network of a **donor (D)** and an **acceptor (A)**.

| Material | Type | Function | HOMO / LUMO (eV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P3HT** (Poly-3-hexylthiophene) | Donor polymer | Absorbs 400–650 nm | -5.2 / -3.0 |
| **PCBM** ([6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester) | Acceptor fullerene | Electron acceptor | -6.1 / -3.7 |
| **PTB7** | Low-bandgap donor | Absorbs 400–800 nm | -5.15 / -3.31 |
| **PEDOT:PSS** | Conducting polymer | Hole transport layer | -5.0 / -2.2 |
| **P3HT:ICBA** | Donor:Acceptor blend | Improved LUMO offset | Tunable |

### 2.4.2 Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs)
A photoelectrochemical system using:
- **TiO₂** nanoparticle film (mesoporous, wide-bandgap scaffold).
- **Ruthenium-based dye** (e.g., N719 — cis-bis(isothiocyanato)bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato)ruthenium(II)).
- **Iodide/Triiodide (I⁻/I₃⁻)** redox electrolyte.
- Efficiency: **≈ 11–12 %** under AM1.5G.

### 2.4.3 Perovskite Solar Cells (PSCs)
The structure is **ABX₃**, e.g., **CH₃NH₃PbI₃ (MAPbI₃)**:
- A = Methylammonium (MA⁺), Formamidinium (FA⁺), or Cs⁺.
- B = Pb²⁺ or Sn²⁺.
- X = I⁻, Br⁻, or Cl⁻.

Champion efficiency has rocketed from **3.8 % (2009)** to over **26 % (single junction)** and **> 33 % in tandem with Si**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_g = \frac{1240}{\lambda}$ | Photon–bandgap energy equivalence | eV, nm |
| 2 | $\alpha(h\nu) = A(h\nu - E_g)^{1/2}$ | Indirect bandgap absorption (Si) | cm⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\alpha(h\nu) = A(h\nu - E_g)^{1/2}$ direct | Direct bandgap absorption (GaAs, CdTe) | cm⁻¹ |
| 4 | $L_D = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ | Carrier diffusion length | m |
| 5 | $\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Power conversion efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $FF = \frac{V_m \cdot J_m}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}$ | Fill factor (0 < FF < 1) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{sc}}{J_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage (ideal diode) | V |
| 8 | $t_{absorber} \geq \frac{1}{\alpha}$ | Minimum thickness for >63 % absorption | cm |
| 9 | $E_g^{eff} = E_g^{donor} - E_g^{acceptor}$ | Effective gap in BHJ OPV | eV |
| 10 | $\Delta E_{LUMO} = \vert LUMO_D - LUMO_A \vert \geq 0.3 \text{ eV}$ | Charge-transfer driving force | eV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **direct vs. indirect bandgap** materials. *Indirect bandgap* materials (Si, Ge) require phonon assistance → require thicker absorber (~200 µm). *Direct bandgap* materials (GaAs, CdTe, perovskites) absorb strongly in < 2 µm.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Optimal Bandgap from Shockley–Queisser Principle

The SQ limit considers a solar cell as a black body at temperature $T_S = 6000 \text{ K}$ radiating onto a cell at $T_C = 300 \text{ K}$. The detailed balance gives:

**Step 1: Photocurrent density (current generated per unit area):**

$$J_{ph} = q \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{sun}(E) \, dE$$

where $\Phi_{sun}(E)$ is the photon flux per unit area per unit energy from the AM1.5G spectrum.

**Step 2: Recombination current density (radiative limit):**

$$J_0 = q \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{bb}(E, T_C) \, dE$$

where $\Phi_{bb}(E, T_C)$ is the black-body photon flux emitted by the cell at its operating temperature.

**Step 3: Net current under load (voltage $V$):**

$$J(V) = J_{ph} - J_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT_C}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4: Power and efficiency:**

$$P(V) = V \cdot J(V), \quad \eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}}$$

**Step 5: Numerical optimization:**

Setting $\frac{dP}{dV} = 0$ and solving for $E_g$ in the SQ detailed-balance framework yields the bandgap that maximizes $\eta$. The classical result is:

$$E_g^{opt} \approx 1.34 \text{ eV}$$

which corresponds to a wavelength $\lambda \approx 925 \text{ nm}$.

$$\lambda = \frac{1240}{1.34} = 925.4 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 6: Maximum theoretical efficiency:**

For this optimal bandgap, the SQ analysis gives:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}} \approx 33.7 \%$$

This is the famous **Shockley–Queisser Limit** — the absolute ceiling for any single-junction cell under unconcentrated AM1.5G sunlight.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Absorber Thickness

For Beer–Lambert absorption, the fraction of photons absorbed at depth $x$ is:

$$I(x) = I_0 \left[1 - e^{-\alpha x}\right]$$

For 90 % absorption, we require $e^{-\alpha x} = 0.1$, so:

$$x_{90\%} = \frac{\ln(10)}{\alpha} = \frac{2.303}{\alpha}$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
Given $\alpha = 10^4 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ for CdTe, calculate the thickness for 90 % absorption.

$$x_{90\%} = \frac{2.303}{10^4} = 2.303 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 2.30 \text{ µm}$$

> **Comment:** Only **~2 µm** of CdTe is needed compared to **~200 µm** of silicon. This is why thin-film CdTe cells are so cheap.

For silicon, $\alpha \approx 10^3 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ at 800 nm:

$$x_{90\%} = \frac{2.303}{10^3} = 2.303 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm} = 23 \text{ µm}$$

But because Si is indirect, the weak absorption tail near $E_g$ requires the practical thickness to be **200–300 µm**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Tabulation)

| Property | Mono-Si | CdTe | CIGS | GaAs | MAPbI₃ | P3HT:PCBM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bandgap (eV) | 1.12 | 1.45 | 1.0–1.7 | 1.42 | 1.55 | 1.1 |
| Direct/Indirect | Indirect | Direct | Direct | Direct | Direct | — |
| Champion η (%) | 26.1 | 22.1 | 23.35 | 29.1 | 26.1 | 19.2 (non-fullerene) |
| Stability | > 25 yr | 25 yr | 25 yr | Excellent | < 5 yr | < 2 yr |
| Cost ($/W) | 0.20–0.30 | 0.15–0.25 | 0.30–0.50 | > 5 | 0.30 (lab) | Very low |
| Toxicity | Low | Cd (high) | Low | As (high) | Pb (high) | None |
| Flexibility | No | Limited | Limited | No | Yes | Yes |

## 3.4 Derivation: Why HOMO–LUMO Offsets Matter in OPV

For efficient exciton dissociation at the D/A interface, the LUMO offset must exceed the exciton binding energy (typically **0.3–0.5 eV**).

$$\Delta E_{LUMO} = \vert LUMO_{donor} - LUMO_{acceptor} \vert \geq 0.3 \text{ eV}$$

For P3HT (LUMO = -3.0 eV) and PCBM (LUMO = -3.7 eV):

$$\Delta E_{LUMO} = \vert -3.0 - (-3.7) \vert = 0.7 \text{ eV}$$

This satisfies the requirement (0.7 > 0.3 eV), explaining why P3HT:PCBM is a benchmark OPV system. The effective bandgap of the cell is:

$$E_g^{eff} = LUMO_{acceptor} - HOMO_{donor} = (-3.7) - (-5.2) = 1.5 \text{ eV}$$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Diagram — Generic PV Cell Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1 ["INCOMING SUNLIGHT"]
        SUN[AM1.5G Solar Spectrum]
    end

    subgraph S2 ["FRONT SIDE"]
        AR[Anti-Reflective Coating: SiNx or MgF2]
        TCE[Transparent Conductive Electrode: ITO or FTO]
        ETL[Electron Transport Layer: TiO2 or SnO2]
    end

    subgraph S3 ["ACTIVE LAYER"]
        ABS[Absorber Material: Si / CdTe / MAPbI3 / P3HT:PCBM]
    end

    subgraph S4 ["BACK SIDE"]
        HTL[Hole Transport Layer: PEDOT:PSS or Spiro-OMeTAD]
        BE[Back Electrode: Al or Ag or Au]
        ENC[Encapsulant: EVA plus Glass]
    end

    subgraph S5 ["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT"]
        EXT[Load: External Resistor]
        JCT[Junction: p-n or Bulk Heterojunction]
    end

    SUN --> AR
    AR --> TCE
    TCE --> ETL
    ETL --> ABS
    ABS --> HTL
    HTL --> BE
    BE --> ENC
    ABS -. Photogeneration .-> JCT
    JCT --> EXT
    EXT -. Recombination via circuit .-> ABS
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Material Classification Hierarchy (KTU Board Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Photovoltaic Materials] --> B[First Generation]
    A --> C[Second Generation]
    A --> D[Third Generation]

    B --> B1[Mono Crystalline Silicon]
    B --> B2[Multi Crystalline Silicon]

    C --> C1[Amorphous Silicon a-SiH]
    C --> C2[Cadmium Telluride CdTe]
    C --> C3[Copper Indium Gallium Selenide CIGS]
    C --> C4[Gallium Arsenide GaAs]

    D --> D1[Organic Polymers OPV]
    D --> D2[Dye Sensitized DSSC]
    D --> D3[Perovskite ABX3]
    D --> D4[Quantum Dot Solar Cells]

    D1 --> D1a[Donor: P3HT, PTB7]
    D1 --> D1b[Acceptor: PCBM, ITIC]
    D3 --> D3a[MAPbI3]
    D3 --> D3b[FAPbI3]
    D3 --> D3c[CsPbIBr2]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Operation of a PV Material

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Stage 1 Photon Absorption: Energy greater than Eg] --> P2[Stage 2 Exciton or Electron Hole Pair Generation]
    P2 --> P3{Stage 3 Junction Type Decision}
    P3 --> P4a[Path A: Inorganic p-n Junction]
    P3 --> P4b[Path B: Organic D-A Heterojunction]

    P4a --> P5a[Stage 4a Drift plus Diffusion at Junction]
    P4b --> P5b[Stage 4b Exciton Diffusion to D-A Interface]

    P5a --> P6[Stage 5 Charge Collection at Electrodes]
    P5b --> P6

    P6 --> P7[Stage 6 Output: Voltage times Current equals Power]

    P7 --> P8[Stage 7 Losses: Recombination, Reflection, Thermalization, Transmission]
    P8 -. feedback to improve .-> P1
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Polymer vs. Inorganic Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ORG ["ORGANIC PV ARCHITECTURE"]
        O1[Glass Substrate] --> O2[ITO Electrode]
        O2 --> O3[PEDOT:PSS HTL]
        O3 --> O4[P3HT:PCBM Active Blend]
        O4 --> O5[Ca or Al Cathode]
    end

    subgraph INO ["INORGANIC PV ARCHITECTURE"]
        I1[Glass Substrate] --> I2[FTO Electrode]
        I2 --> I3[Compact TiO2 ETL]
        I3 --> I4[MAPbI3 Perovskite]
        I4 --> I5[Spiro-OMeTAD HTL]
        I5 --> I6[Au Back Contact]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `ORG`). Reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, and `style` were avoided as node IDs. Labels are kept raw (no markdown bold inside quoted strings) to ensure clean rendering in markdown previewers.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]
**"List any three second-generation photovoltaic materials and state one characteristic advantage of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**
1. **Amorphous Silicon (a-Si:H):** Direct bandgap (1.7 eV), absorbs strongly → requires only ~1 µm thickness, can be deposited on flexible substrates.
2. **Cadmium Telluride (CdTe):** Bandgap 1.45 eV almost at the SQ optimum (1.34 eV), high absorption coefficient (>10⁴ cm⁻¹) → very low material usage.
3. **Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS):** Tunable bandgap (1.04–1.67 eV) by adjusting Ga/In ratio, excellent radiation tolerance for space applications. **(1 mark per correct point.)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]
**"What is a bulk heterojunction (BHJ) in organic photovoltaics? Why is it preferred over a planar junction?"**

**Model Answer:**
A **bulk heterojunction (BHJ)** is a nano-scale interpenetrating network formed by blending an electron **donor** (e.g., P3HT) and an electron **acceptor** (e.g., PCBM) in the active layer. It is preferred over a planar (bilayer) junction because the diffusion length of excitons in organic materials is only **~10 nm**, and a BHJ ensures the donor–acceptor interface is everywhere within this distance, drastically improving exciton dissociation efficiency. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for justification.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> *Students routinely lose marks on these questions by: (a) writing generic descriptions without stating bandgap values, (b) failing to draw or describe the device architecture, (c) omitting numerical data for champion efficiencies, and (d) confusing "donor/acceptor" terminology with p-type/n-type. Always state exact numbers, draw labelled device stacks, and use precise KTU terminology.*

---

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**"With the help of a neat device architecture diagram, explain the working of an organic photovoltaic (OPV) cell based on a P3HT:PCBM bulk heterojunction. Discuss the materials used for each functional layer and their roles. Compare the OPV with a perovskite solar cell in terms of efficiency, stability, and scalability."**

#### Part (a) — Device Architecture & Working Principle (7 marks)

**Step 1: Layered architecture (from bottom to top):**

1. **Glass / PET Substrate** — Mechanical support, optically transparent.
2. **ITO (Indium Tin Oxide)** — Transparent conducting anode (work function $\approx -4.7 \text{ eV}$).
3. **PEDOT:PSS** — Hole transport layer (HTL); smoothens ITO roughness, aligns HOMO levels.
4. **P3HT:PCBM Active Layer (~200 nm)** — Light absorption + exciton dissociation.
5. **Aluminum or Calcium Cathode** — Low-work-function electron collector.

**Step 2: Working sequence:**

(i) Photon absorption in P3HT creates a bound electron–hole pair called an **exciton**.
(ii) Exciton diffuses (~10 nm) to the P3HT/PCBM interface.
(iii) **Exciton dissociation:** Electron transfers to PCBM (driven by $\Delta E_{LUMO} = 0.7 \text{ eV}$).
(iv) Charges drift/diffuse to respective electrodes.
(v) External circuit delivers power.

**Step 3: Draw the device stack diagram** (label each layer with thickness and energy levels in eV).

#### Part (b) — Functional Materials, Roles & Comparison (7 marks)

| Layer | Material | Role | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| Substrate | Glass / PET | Mechanical support | Transparent, Tg > 150 °C |
| Anode | ITO | Hole collection | $\rho < 10^{-4} \Omega\cdot$cm, T > 85 % |
| HTL | PEDOT:PSS | Hole transport, work-function matching | HOMO $\approx -5.0$ eV |
| Active | P3HT:PCBM | Absorption + dissociation | Eg $\approx 1.5$ eV (effective) |
| Cathode | Al / Ca | Electron collection | Low work function (~2.9 eV for Ca) |

**Comparison: OPV vs. Perovskite (MAPbI₃):**

| Criterion | OPV (P3HT:PCBM) | Perovskite (MAPbI₃) |
|---|---|---|
| Champion PCE | ~ 4–5 % (P3HT:PCBM); > 19 % (modern non-fullerene) | > 26 % |
| Stability | Poor (< 2 years); degrades in air | Moderate (< 5 years); humidity sensitive |
| Scalability | Excellent — roll-to-roll printing possible | Good — solution processable |
| Toxicity | None | Lead-based (Pb) |
| Flexibility | Yes | Yes |

**Conclusion:** Perovskites currently lead in efficiency; OPVs lead in low-cost, flexible, non-toxic applications. **(7 marks complete: 2 marks for layer-wise material description, 2 marks for energy-level/role, 3 marks for the comparison table with crisp data.)**

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**"Discuss in detail the three generations of photovoltaic materials. Explain why multi-crystalline silicon still dominates the market despite lower efficiency than monocrystalline silicon. With suitable diagrams, describe the working of a perovskite solar cell (MAPbI₃) and identify the specific materials used as ETL, HTL, and electrodes."**

#### Part (a) — Three Generations of PV Materials (7 marks)

**Generation 1 — Crystalline Silicon (c-Si):**
Mono-Si and multi-Si wafers, p-n junction cells. Efficiency 15–26 %, market share ~ 95 %. Requires high-temperature processing (> 1400 °C) and thick absorbers (~ 200 µm).

**Generation 2 — Thin-Film Inorganics:**
a-Si:H, CdTe, CIGS, GaAs. Direct bandgaps → absorption in < 2 µm. Lower material cost. Champion efficiencies 22–29 %.

**Generation 3 — Emerging Materials:**
Organic polymers (OPV), dye-sensitized (DSSC), perovskite (MAPbI₃, FAPbI₃), quantum dots. Solution-processable, flexible, potentially cheap. Efficiency rapidly climbing.

#### Part (b) — Why mc-Si Dominates the Market (3.5 marks)

Multi-crystalline silicon dominates because:
1. **Lower cost:** Casting mc-Si is ~ 3× cheaper than Czochralski mono-Si growth.
2. **Acceptable efficiency:** 18–22 % is sufficient for grid parity.
3. **Mature supply chain:** 50+ years of metallurgical-Si infrastructure.
4. **Tolerance to impurities:** Grain boundaries mask defect sensitivity.
5. **Scalability:** Square wafers pack efficiently into modules.

#### Part (c) — Perovskite Solar Cell Working Diagram (3.5 marks)

**Device stack (n-i-p configuration):**
1. **Glass / FTO substrate.**
2. **Compact TiO₂ (~50 nm) — ETL.**
3. **Mesoporous TiO₂ scaffold infiltrated with MAPbI₃.**
4. **MAPbI₃ perovskite absorber (~500 nm).**
5. **Spiro-OMeTAD — HTL.**
6. **Au back electrode (~80 nm).**

**Working principle:**
(i) Photon absorption in MAPbI₃ (Eg = 1.55 eV) creates free carriers directly (no exciton needed).
(ii) Electrons diffuse to TiO₂ ETL and reach FTO.
(iii) Holes transfer to Spiro-OMeTAD and reach Au.
(iv) Voltage arises from the work-function difference between FTO and Au.

**Key material specifications:**

| Layer | Material | Function | Energy Level (eV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| ETL | Compact TiO₂ | Electron extraction | CB $\approx -4.0$ |
| Absorber | MAPbI₃ | Light harvesting | Eg = 1.55 |
| HTL | Spiro-OMeTAD | Hole transport | HOMO $\approx -5.2$ |
| Back contact | Au | Reflective electrode | WF $\approx -5.1$ |

**(7 + 3.5 + 3.5 = 14 marks complete.)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Key Points to Remember:**

- **Five functional layers** of every PV cell: Substrate → TCE → ETL → Absorber → HTL → Back Electrode → Encapsulant.
- **Silicon (Eg = 1.12 eV)** dominates ~ 95 % of the market; **mc-Si** is cheaper but less efficient than **mono-Si**.
- **CdTe (Eg = 1.45 eV)** is closest single-junction material to the **SQ optimum (1.34 eV)**.
- **CIGS** bandgap is tunable by adjusting the **Ga / (Ga + In)** ratio.
- **GaAs** is preferred for **space and multi-junction tandem** cells (η > 47 %).
- **P3HT:PCBM** is the benchmark **bulk heterojunction (BHJ)** OPV system.
- **Exciton diffusion length in organics ≈ 10 nm** → BHJ architecture is mandatory.
- **LUMO offset ≥ 0.3 eV** is required for efficient charge transfer in OPVs.
- **Perovskite MAPbI₃** has champion efficiency **> 26 %** in < 15 years of research.
- **Shockley–Queisser Limit = 33.7 %** for single-junction cells; multi-junction can exceed 47 %.
- **Transparent conducting oxides (TCO):** ITO (best conductivity), FTO (more thermal stable), AZO (cheap alternative).
- **Hole transport polymer PEDOT:PSS** is standard HTL in OPVs.
- **Spiro-OMeTAD** is the canonical HTL for n-i-p perovskite cells.
- **TiO₂ (compact + mesoporous)** is the canonical ETL for perovskite and DSSC cells.
- **Beer–Lambert law:** $x_{90\%} = \frac{2.303}{\alpha}$; thin films need only 1–2 µm.
- **AM1.5G spectrum** peaks near **500 nm (2.48 eV)**.
- **Photon energy equation:** $E_g = \frac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ — memorize the constant **1240 eV·nm**.
- **Direct vs. indirect bandgap:** Direct = strong absorption, thin absorber possible; Indirect = weak absorption, thick wafer needed.
- **First Generation = Wafers**, **Second Generation = Thin Films**, **Third Generation = Organics/Perovskites/DSSCs**.
- **Flexible substrates:** PET, PEN (for OPV); glass (rigid baseline).
- **Encapsulation:** EVA (ethylene vinyl acetate) is the industry standard; protects against moisture-induced degradation especially critical for perovskites.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 — MATERIALS USED | PEPOT757 | KTU 2024 SCHEME**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Processing (Polymers & Photovoltaics)

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme course **PEPOT757**, the term *Processing* encompasses two distinct, parallel engineering workflows:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Processing** is defined as the set of manufacturing operations by which raw polymeric materials (resins, additives, fillers, reinforcements) are converted into finished or semi-finished products of desired shape, structure, and properties through the controlled application of heat, pressure, shear, and time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photovoltaic (PV) Processing** refers to the sequence of physical, chemical, and electrical fabrication steps used to convert semiconductor materials (typically silicon) into solar cells and modules capable of converting sunlight into electrical energy, including wafer preparation, junction formation, contact metallization, anti-reflection coating, and module encapsulation.

The shared engineering philosophy between the two is the **controlled transformation of matter** — polymers transform viscoelastic melts/solutions into solid articles, while photovoltaics transform raw semiconductor ingots/wafers into functional energy-conversion devices.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Polymer Side):**
> Imagine polymer processing as making **roti (Indian flatbread)**. You take raw dough (polymer melt), apply heat (tava), apply pressure (rolling pin), and shape it within a time window before it hardens. Different "roti-making techniques" — direct on a tava, in a mould, puffed in an oven — correspond to compression molding, injection molding, and blow molding respectively. The "dough" rheology (stickiness, elasticity) is analogous to **melt flow index (MFI)** of a polymer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Photovoltaic Side):**
> Think of fabricating a solar cell as building a **layered sandwich with electrical contact "picks"**. Starting with a thin silicon wafer (the bread), you bake a thin phosphorus-doped layer (n-type cheese spread on top) to create a p-n junction, then spray a transparent anti-reflection coating (mustard oil to reduce glare), and finally poke metal wires (toothpicks) on top and bottom as electrical terminals. Encapsulating the cell under glass is like wrapping the sandwich in cling film to protect it from rain.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Melt Flow Index (MFI) Standard:** **ISO 1133** and **ASTM D1238** — measured in **g/10 min**
> - **Injection Molding Pressure Range:** **50 – 150 MPa** (typical)
> - **Extrusion Temperature Range for Thermoplastics:** **150 °C – 300 °C**
> - **Solar Cell Standard Test Conditions (STC):** Irradiance = **1000 W/m²**, Spectrum = **AM 1.5G**, Cell Temperature = **25 °C**
> - **Band Gap of Crystalline Silicon (c-Si):** **1.12 eV** at 300 K
> - **Solar Constant (Earth):** ≈ **1361 W/m²** (AM 0)
> - **Standard PV Module Efficiency (c-Si):** **15 % – 22 %** (commercial), **> 26 %** (research cells)

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, *Processing* is a **high-weightage, application-oriented topic** in Module 3. Students are expected to:
> - Compare polymer processing techniques (a frequent **14-mark question**)
> - Sketch and explain PV cell fabrication flow
> - Justify selection of processing technique based on material properties
> - Calculate throughput, cycle time, and efficiency in both domains
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymer Processing — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Injection Molding

**Step-by-step logic:**
- **Why:** Produces complex 3-D parts at high volumes with tight tolerances.
- **How:**
  1. Polymer granules are fed from a hopper into a heated barrel.
  2. A rotating screw (or plunger) melts, mixes, and injects the melt into a closed mould cavity.
  3. The melt solidifies under pressure (packing & cooling phase).
  4. The mould opens and the part is ejected.

**Key Parameters:**
- Injection pressure, melt temperature, mould temperature, cooling time, cycle time, shot weight.

### 2.1.2 Extrusion

- **Why:** Continuous profiles (pipes, sheets, films, cables).
- **How:** Polymer melt is forced through a **die** of fixed cross-section by a rotating screw. The extrudate is cooled (air, water bath, chill rolls) and cut/pelletized.

**Mathematical Basis:** The flow inside the die is described by the **power-law fluid model** (non-Newtonian).

### 2.1.3 Blow Molding

- **Why:** Hollow articles (bottles, tanks).
- **How:** A molten parison (extrusion blow molding) or a pre-injected preform (injection blow molding / stretch blow molding) is inflated against a cooled mould using compressed air.

### 2.1.4 Compression Molding

- **Why:** Large, flat, fibre-reinforced thermoset parts.
- **How:** A charge of material is placed in an open heated mould; the mould is closed under pressure (typically **10 – 30 MPa**), curing occurs, and the part is removed.

### 2.1.5 Thermoforming

- **Why:** Thin-walled packaging, trays, disposable cups.
- **How:** A thermoplastic sheet is heated above its **glass transition temperature (T_g)**, then forced (vacuum, pressure, or matched-die) against a mould.

### 2.1.6 Calendaring

- **Why:** Continuous sheets/films of uniform thickness.
- **How:** The melt is passed through a series of heated counter-rotating rolls (**n = 3 to 5**), gap between rolls controls thickness.

### 2.1.7 Other Techniques (Quick Notes)

| Technique | Use Case |
|---|---|
| Rotational Molding | Large hollow tanks, toys |
| Casting | Acrylic sheets, nylon, polyurethane |
| Filament Winding | Composite pressure vessels, pipes |
| Pultrusion | Continuous fibre-reinforced profiles |
| 3D Printing (FDM/SLA) | Prototyping, custom parts |
| Reaction Injection Molding (RIM) | Polyurethane automotive parts |

---

## 2.2 Photovoltaic Processing — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Silicon Wafer Preparation

1. **Metallurgical-Grade Silicon (MG-Si):** ~98 % pure, produced by carbothermic reduction of SiO₂ in an electric arc furnace.
2. **Purification:** Siemens process (chemical vapor deposition of trichlorosilane) → **Solar-Grade Silicon (SoG-Si)**, 99.9999 % (6N) pure.
3. **Czochralski (CZ) Crystal Growth:** A seed crystal is dipped into molten silicon and slowly pulled upward while rotating, growing a single-crystal ingot of **~150 – 200 mm diameter**.
4. **Wafer Slicing:** Ingot is sliced with a **multi-wire saw** using an abrasive slurry into wafers of thickness **~ 180 – 200 μm** (modern: **120 – 160 μm**).

### 2.2.2 p-n Junction Formation (Doping)

- **Emitter formation:** Phosphorus is diffused into a p-type boron-doped wafer at **~ 800 – 900 °C** to form a shallow n⁺ emitter (~ 0.3 – 0.5 μm deep).
- **Alternative methods:** Ion implantation, epitaxial growth, in-situ doping.

### 2.2.3 Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC)

- **Why:** Bare silicon reflects ~ 30 % of incident light.
- **How:** A thin dielectric layer (typically **SiN_x: H** by PECVD, thickness **~ 75 nm**) is deposited. For destructive interference, the optimal thickness satisfies:

  $$t_{\text{ARC}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{4 n_{\text{ARC}}}$$

  where $\lambda_0 = 600 \, \text{nm}$ (peak solar spectrum) and $n_{\text{ARC}} \approx 2.0$ for SiN$_x$.

### 2.2.4 Metallization

- **Front contact:** Screen-printed **silver (Ag) paste**, fired through the ARC.
- **Back contact:** Screen-printed **aluminum (Al)** for **Back Surface Field (BSF)** + **Ag/Ag-Al** soldering pads.
- **Advanced:** Plated Ni/Cu/Ag, HJT uses TCO (ITO).

### 2.2.5 Thin-Film PV Processing (a-Si, CdTe, CIGS)

| Technique | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD / PECVD)** | a-Si:H, μc-Si layers |
| **Sputtering (PVD)** | Mo back contact (CIGS), TCO |
| **Thermal/E-beam Evaporation** | CIGS absorber, CdTe |
| **Close-Space Sublimation (CSS)** | CdTe industrial process |
| **Solvothermal / Co-evaporation** | Perovskite / CIGS research |

### 2.2.6 Module Assembly (Lamination)

The cell-string is laminated between a **front cover (low-iron tempered glass)**, an encapsulant (**EVA — Ethylene Vinyl Acetate**), and a **backsheet (Tedlar/PET)**. Lamination is done in a vacuum laminator at **~ 140 – 150 °C** for **~ 15 min**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Link:** EVA is the **polymer-processing link to photovoltaics** — without polymer encapsulation modules would fail in < 1 year of outdoor exposure. This is a frequently asked integrated question.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Polymer processing drives industries: **packaging, automotive, healthcare, electronics, construction, aerospace, consumer goods**.
- Photovoltaic processing enables the **global solar energy industry (~ USD 250 billion in 2024)**, with c-Si covering **> 95 %** of production.
- Integrated polymer-PV applications: **flexible solar films, polymer-based encapsulation, polymer-based antireflective coatings, building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV)**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Melt Flow Index | $\text{MFI} = \dfrac{m}{t}$ | grams per **10 minutes** |
| 2 | Power-law viscosity (polymer melt) | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | $K$ = consistency, $n$ = power-law index |
| 3 | Hagen-Poiseuille flow in a die (Newtonian approx.) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$ | Volumetric flow rate $Q$ |
| 4 | Injection Molding Cycle Time | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{inj}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{eject}}$ | seconds |
| 5 | Cooling time (semi-empirical) | $t_{\text{cool}} \propto \dfrac{s^2}{\alpha}$ | $s$ = wall thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity |
| 6 | P-N Junction Photocurrent | $J_L = q \int_0^{\infty} \Phi(\lambda) (1 - e^{-\alpha(\lambda) W}) d\lambda$ | $q$ = electron charge, $W$ = depletion width |
| 7 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{OC} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln \!\left(\dfrac{J_L}{J_0} + 1\right)$ | $J_0$ = saturation current density |
| 8 | Fill Factor (FF) | $\text{FF} = \dfrac{V_{MP} I_{MP}}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$ | Dimensionless, $\vert 0 < \text{FF} < 1 \vert$ |
| 9 | Cell Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OC} \, I_{SC} \, \text{FF}}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | $P_{\text{in}} = 1000 \,\text{W/m}^2$ at STC |
| 10 | ARC thickness for destructive interference | $t = \dfrac{\lambda_0}{4 n_{\text{ARC}}}$ | Condition: $n_{\text{ARC}} = \sqrt{n_{\text{Si}}}$ ideal |
| 11 | Wafer Saw Kerf Loss | $\text{Loss \%} = \dfrac{\text{kerf}}{\text{kerf} + t_{\text{wafer}}} \times 100$ | Modern kerf $\sim 80$ – $100 \, \mu m$ |
| 12 | Solar Cell $V_{OC}$ Temperature Coefficient | $\dfrac{dV_{OC}}{dT} \approx -2 \,\text{mV/°C}$ (c-Si) | Negative — efficiency drops with $T$ |
| 13 | EVA Lamination temperature | $T_{\text{lam}} \approx 140$ – $150 \,°\text{C}$ | Crosslinking of EVA |
| 14 | Thermal conductivity of EVA | $k_{\text{EVA}} \approx 0.35 \,\text{W/(m·K)}$ | Used in module thermal model |
| 15 | Energy Payback Time (EPBT) | $\text{EPBT} = \dfrac{E_{\text{embodied}}}{\text{Annual Energy Yield}}$ | Years; c-Si $\sim 1$ – $2$ yrs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the **photo-generated current, V$_{OC}$, FF, and η** equations, and know **one specific numerical value for c-Si** (e.g., $V_{OC} \approx 0.65$ V, $I_{SC} \approx 40$ mA/cm², FF $\approx 0.8$ for a high-quality cell).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Solar Cell Efficiency from Fundamental Equations

We start from the **two-diode model equivalent current-voltage relationship** of a solar cell under illumination:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \!\left[ e^{\frac{qV}{n k_B T}} - 1 \right]$$

where:
- $I_L$ = light-generated current
- $I_0$ = diode saturation current
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (elementary charge)
- $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = ideality factor ($\vert 1 < n < 2 \vert$)

### Step 1: Short-Circuit Current
At $V = 0$ (terminals shorted), $I = I_{SC}$, and the exponential term $\rightarrow 1$:

$$I_{SC} = I_L - I_0 (1 - 1) = I_L$$

So the **light-generated current equals short-circuit current**.

### Step 2: Open-Circuit Voltage
At $I = 0$ (terminals open), solve for $V = V_{OC}$:

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \!\left[ e^{\frac{qV_{OC}}{n k_B T}} - 1 \right]$$

Rearranging:

$$I_0 \!\left[ e^{\frac{qV_{OC}}{n k_B T}} - 1 \right] = I_L$$

Since $I_L \gg I_0$ in a good cell, the $-1$ term is negligible:

$$e^{\frac{qV_{OC}}{n k_B T}} \approx \frac{I_L}{I_0}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\frac{qV_{OC}}{n k_B T} = \ln \!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

Solving for $V_{OC}$:

$$V_{OC} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln \!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

**Engineering insight:** To maximize $V_{OC}$, we need to (a) reduce $I_0$ (better material, lower recombination) and (b) operate at lower $T$ (cool module → higher $V_{OC}$).

### Step 3: Maximum Power Point
Maximum power is found by differentiating $P = I \cdot V$ and setting $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$. The result, by definition, gives the **Fill Factor (FF)**:

$$\text{FF} = \frac{V_{MP} I_{MP}}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$$

A useful **empirical approximation** (Green, 1981) for $\text{FF} \in [0.7, 0.85]$:

$$\text{FF}_0 = \frac{v_{OC} - \ln(v_{OC} + 0.72)}{v_{OC} + 1}$$

where $v_{OC} = \dfrac{qV_{OC}}{n k_B T}$.

### Step 4: Conversion Efficiency
The conversion efficiency is the ratio of maximum electrical power output to incident optical power:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{max}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{V_{OC} \, I_{SC} \, \text{FF}}{P_{\text{in}}}$$

For a cell of area $A$:

$$\eta = \frac{V_{OC} \, J_{SC} \, \text{FF}}{G}$$

where $G = 1000 \, \text{W/m}^2$ at STC and $J_{SC} = I_{SC}/A$.

**Example numerical check (c-Si cell):**  
Take $V_{OC} = 0.65$ V, $J_{SC} = 40$ mA/cm², FF = 0.80:

$$P_{\text{max}} = 0.65 \times 0.040 \times 0.80 = 0.0208 \,\text{W/cm}^2$$

$$\eta = \frac{0.0208 \,\text{W/cm}^2}{0.1 \,\text{W/cm}^2} = 0.208 = 20.8\%$$

This matches commercial c-Si panels of the early 2020s. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Volumetric Flow in a Circular Die

For a non-Newtonian power-law fluid flowing through a cylindrical die (length $L$, radius $R$, pressure drop $\Delta P$), the volumetric flow rate is:

$$Q = \frac{n \pi R^3}{3 n + 1} \left( \frac{R \, \Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n}$$

**Step-by-step derivation logic:**

1. Start with the **power-law constitutive equation**:

   $$\tau_{rz} = -K \left( \frac{du_z}{dr} \right)^{n}$$

2. Apply the **momentum balance** for steady, fully-developed, axisymmetric flow:

   $$0 = -\frac{dp}{dz} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}(r \tau_{rz})$$

3. Integrate twice with the **no-slip boundary condition** $u_z(R) = 0$:

   $$u_z(r) = \frac{n}{n+1} \left( \frac{\Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} R^{(1+n)/n} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{(1+n)/n}\right]$$

4. Integrate $u_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$ from $r = 0$ to $R$ to obtain the volumetric flow rate:

   $$Q = \int_0^R 2\pi r \, u_z(r) \, dr = \frac{n \pi R^3}{3n+1} \left( \frac{R \Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n}$$

**Special case check:** For $n = 1$, $K = \eta$, recover the classical Hagen-Poiseuille equation:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L} \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This is the standard derivation asked when an examiner wants a 7-mark "process engineering" problem. It tests if you understand the link between rheology and equipment design.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Polymer vs PV Processing

| Dimension | Polymer Processing | Photovoltaic Processing |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Material** | Granules, powder, melt, solution | Silicon wafer, thin-film precursors |
| **Key Energy Input** | Heat + mechanical work | Heat (diffusion), photons, plasma |
| **Critical Parameter** | MFI, T_g, viscosity $\eta$ | Doping concentration, $V_{OC}$, $\eta$ |
| **Cycle Time** | Seconds – minutes | Hours – days (for batch processes) |
| **Tolerance** | ± 0.05 mm typical | ± 0.01 mm wafer, ± 1 % electrical |
| **Quality Metric** | Tensile strength, gloss, dimensional stability | Cell efficiency, fill factor, lifetime |
| **Failure Mode** | Short-shot, warpage, sink marks | Microcracks, shunting, PID, hotspots |
| **Scale-up** | Easy (modular machines) | Capital-intensive (cleanrooms) |
| **Cross-Domain Link** | EVA encapsulation, TCO substrates | Polymer-based AR coatings, backsheets |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Injection Molding Machine — Block Architecture

## 4.2 c-Si Solar Cell — Process Flow (Block-Level)

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    rawA[Metallurgical Silicon 98 percent] --> purA[Siemens Process Purification 6N]
    purA --> czA[Czochralski Crystal Growth 150 mm Ingot]

    sawA --> etchA[Acid Alkali Texturing Surface]
    etchA --> diffA[Phosphorus Diffusion n plus Emitter 850 C]

    arcA --> metalA[Screen Print Ag Front and Al Back]

    fireA --> testA[IV Tester Efficiency Sorting]
    testA --> cellA[Finished c Si Solar Cell]

    cmA -->|reinforced| smcA[SMC BMC Molding]

> [!NOTE]

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**List any three polymer processing techniques and give one product example for each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Injection Molding** — used to produce mobile-phone housings, bottle caps, automotive dashboards.
2. **Extrusion** — used for PVC pipes, plastic sheets, plastic films.
3. **Blow Molding** — used for PET water bottles, HDPE fuel tanks.

> [!NOTE]
> A 3-mark answer expects **technique + product + one-line justification**. Avoid bare lists.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**What is the role of an anti-reflection coating (ARC) in a silicon solar cell?**

**Model Answer:**
A bare silicon surface reflects approximately **30 %** of incident sunlight, drastically reducing photocurrent. An anti-reflection coating (typically **SiN$_x$ :H**, refractive index **n ≈ 2.0**, thickness ≈ **75 nm**) is deposited by PECVD. By the principle of destructive interference, the reflected waves from the air–ARC and ARC–Si interfaces cancel, reducing reflection to **< 5 %** at the design wavelength and increasing the light-generated current $I_L$, thereby raising the cell efficiency. The SiN$_x$ layer also provides **hydrogen passivation** of bulk defects.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Polymers

**[a] (7 Marks) Compare the processing techniques of injection molding, extrusion, and blow molding with respect to (i) machine principle, (ii) product geometry, (iii) production volume suitability, and (iv) typical polymers used. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

#### Model Solution:

**Introduction (1 mark):**
All three are processing techniques for thermoplastics, but they differ in how the polymer melt is shaped and solidified.

**(i) Machine Principle (2 marks):**

- **Injection Molding:** A reciprocating screw melts the polymer in a heated barrel and **injects** it under high pressure (~ 50 – 150 MPa) into a closed, cooled mould cavity. The part solidifies by conduction to the mould wall and is ejected.
- **Extrusion:** A rotating screw continuously **conveys** the melt through a heated barrel and **forces** it through a die of fixed cross-section. The extrudate is cooled externally (air, water, chill rolls).
- **Blow Molding:** A molten **parison** (or a pre-injected preform) is **inflated** by compressed air against a cooled mould, producing a hollow article.

**(ii) Product Geometry (2 marks):**

- Injection molding: complex **3-D solid** parts with undercuts, inserts, and high dimensional tolerance.
- Extrusion: **2-D continuous profiles** of constant cross-section (pipes, sheets, films, cables).
- Blow molding: **3-D hollow** parts (bottles, tanks, jerry cans).

**(iii) Production Volume Suitability (1 mark):**

- Injection molding: very high (millions/year).
- Extrusion: continuous, very high throughput.
- Blow molding: very high (PET bottles ~ 500 billion/year globally).

**(iv) Typical Polymers (1 mark):**

- Injection molding: PP, ABS, PA, POM, PMMA.
- Extrusion: HDPE, LDPE, PVC, PP.
- Blow molding: HDPE, PP, PET.

**Conclusion:** Each technique is selected based on part geometry, polymer rheology (MFI), and production volume.

---

**[b] (7 Marks) With a neat flow diagram, explain the fabrication of a crystalline silicon solar cell from metallurgical-grade silicon to a finished module. Highlight at least three stages where polymer materials play a role. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Purification (1 mark):** Metallurgical silicon (~ 98 % pure) is purified by the **Siemens process** (reaction of Si with HCl to form SiHCl₃, distillation, and reduction with H₂) to obtain solar-grade silicon (~ 99.9999 % pure).

**Step 2 — Crystal Growth (1 mark):** A single-crystal ingot is grown by the **Czochralski (CZ) method**: a seed crystal is dipped into molten silicon at **~ 1420 °C** and slowly pulled upward while rotating.

**Step 3 — Wafering (1 mark):** The ingot is sliced with a **multi-wire saw** using SiC abrasive slurry into wafers ~ **180 μm** thick.

**Step 4 — Texturing & Cleaning (0.5 mark):** Alkali (KOH) + acid (HCl/HF) treatment produces a **pyramidal texture** that reduces reflection.

**Step 5 — Junction Formation (0.5 mark):** Phosphorus diffusion at **~ 850 °C** creates an n⁺ emitter on the p-type wafer.

**Step 6 — ARC & Metallization (1 mark):** A **SiN$_x$ :H ARC** (~ 75 nm) is deposited by **PECVD**. **Screen-printed Ag** (front) and **Al** (back) pastes are co-fired.

**Step 7 — Testing & Module Assembly (2 marks):** Cells are IV-tested, then interconnected in **strings of 60 or 72 cells**, laminated between **EVA encapsulant**, **low-iron tempered glass**, and a **Tedlar/PET backsheet** at **~ 145 °C** under vacuum.

**Polymer-related stages (highlighted, 3 points × 0.5 mark = 1.5 marks):**
1. **ARC (SiN$_x$ :H)** — deposited by **PECVD** using silane (SiH₄) and ammonia, a chemical-vapor polymer-precursor route.
2. **EVA encapsulant** — a **poly(ethylene-co-vinyl acetate)** copolymer; the most critical polymer in PV.
3. **Tedlar/PET backsheet** — **poly(vinyl fluoride)** (PVF) and **poly(ethylene terephthalate)** multilayer film for moisture and UV protection.

**Flow diagram (sketch in answer script):**

```
MG-Si → Siemens Purification → CZ Ingot → Wafer Saw → Texturing
       → P-Diffusion → PECVD ARC → Screen Print → Cofiring
       → Cell Test → Stringing → EVA Lamination → Module
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B 14-Mark Questions:**
> 1. **Do not omit a labelled diagram** — a missing sketch costs at least **2 marks** in any 14-mark "explain with diagram" question.
> 2. **Avoid vague statements** like "polymers are used in solar cells". Always specify **which polymer (EVA, Tedlar, PET) and what function (encapsulation, backsheet, ARC precursor)**.
> 3. **Match sub-part marks** — a 7-mark sub-part demands **at least 5 distinct technical points** plus a diagram.
> 4. **Do not confuse techniques** — students frequently write "extrusion" instead of "injection molding" for plastic bottles. The correct answer is **blow molding** (with extruded parison or injection-moulded preform).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[a] (7 Marks) Explain the principle, construction, and working of a single-screw extruder with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for the volumetric output of a non-Newtonian power-law fluid through a circular die. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

#### Model Solution:

**Principle & Construction (3 marks):** A single-screw extruder consists of a **barrel** heated externally (3 to 5 zones), a **rotating screw** (L/D ratio 20 to 30), a **hopper** for feedstock, **heating bands**, **thermocouples**, a **breaker plate + screen pack** (for filtering and building back-pressure), and an **end die** that shapes the extrudate. The screw is divided into three functional zones: **feed (solids conveying)**, **compression (melting)**, and **metering (melt pumping)**.

**Working (1 mark):** Polymer granules from the hopper are conveyed, compacted, melted by conduction and shear, mixed, and pressurized by the rotating screw. The melt is forced through the screen pack, breaker plate, and die, where it takes the die shape. It is then cooled externally.

**Derivation (3 marks):** See Section 3.2 above for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final expression is:

$$Q = \frac{n \pi R^3}{3 n + 1} \left( \frac{R \, \Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n}$$

with the special-case check at $n = 1$ giving $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$.

---

**[b] (7 Marks) Describe the Czochralski process for silicon single-crystal growth. Why is it preferred over the Float-Zone method for photovoltaic applications? List three key wafer properties that influence solar cell efficiency. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

#### Model Solution:

**Czochralski Process (4 marks):**
1. High-purity **polysilicon** is melted in a **quartz crucible** at **~ 1420 °C** under **argon ambient**.
2. A small **seed crystal** (a piece of single-crystal Si) is mounted on a pulling rod, lowered until it just touches the melt surface.
3. The seed is **rotated slowly** (~ 10 – 20 rpm) and **pulled upward** at ~ **1 – 3 mm/min** while the crucible counter-rotates.
4. The molten Si **solidifies epitaxially** on the seed, building up a cylindrical **single-crystal ingot** of diameter ~ 150 – 200 mm and length ~ 1 – 2 m.
5. The ingot is then trimmed and sliced into wafers.

**Why CZ is preferred over Float-Zone (FZ) for PV (2 marks):**

| Criterion | CZ | FZ |
|---|---|---|
| Throughput | High (large ingots) | Low (smaller diameter) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (no crucible; RF heating) |
| Oxygen content | Higher (~ 10¹⁸ cm⁻³) — beneficial for **internal gettering** of metallic impurities | Very low oxygen — needs external gettering |
| Use | **> 95 %** of c-Si solar cells | High-efficiency lab/research cells |

CZ is preferred for **terrestrial PV** because of its **high yield, low cost, and self-gettering** property. FZ is reserved for **high-efficiency (> 24 %) cells** and **power electronics**.

**Three key wafer properties affecting cell efficiency (1 mark):**
1. **Wafer thickness** — thinner wafers save Si but are more fragile and have lower absorption.
2. **Resistivity / doping level** — typically **1 – 3 Ω·cm** for p-type CZ; affects $V_{OC}$ and $I_{SC}$.
3. **Lifetime of minority carriers** — higher lifetime → higher diffusion length → higher $J_{SC}$.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 – Processing:**
> 1. **Confusing "blow molding" with "injection molding"** — both are shaping techniques, but blow molding produces **hollow** parts, injection molding produces **solid** parts.
> 2. **Writing "EVA = poly(vinyl acetate)"** — it is **poly(ethylene-co-vinyl acetate)**.
> 3. **Confusing ARC materials** — SiN$_x$ is for c-Si; MgF$_2$ is for lenses/glasses.
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — **always include units (V, mA/cm², %, μm, °C)**.
> 5. **Writing "Float-Zone is cheaper than CZ"** — the opposite is true.
> 6. **Missing the L/D ratio** of an extruder screw (typically **20 – 30**).
> 7. **Forgetting that $V_{OC}$ is temperature-dependent** with a coefficient of approximately **−2 mV/°C** for c-Si.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Processing: Final 1-Page Revision**
>
> **Polymer Processing:**
> - **Injection Molding:** 3-D solid parts; high pressure (50 – 150 MPa); cycle time = injection + cooling + ejection.
> - **Extrusion:** continuous profiles; $Q = \dfrac{n \pi R^3}{3n+1}\!\left(\dfrac{R\Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{\!1/n}$.
> - **Blow Molding:** hollow parts; uses compressed air to inflate a parison/preform.
> - **Compression Molding:** thermosets, fibre-reinforced parts; lower pressure than injection.
> - **Thermoforming:** heated sheet + vacuum/pressure; T > T_g.
> - **Calendaring:** multi-roll sheet production.
> - **MFI** is the key rheological indicator; **higher MFI → easier flow → thinner walls**.
> - Selection of technique: based on **geometry, polymer type, production volume**.
>
> **Photovoltaic Processing:**
> - **Flow:** MG-Si → Siemens → CZ → Wafer Saw → Texturing → P-diffusion → PECVD ARC → Screen Print → Cofiring → Module Lamination.
> - **CZ is preferred for PV** over FZ (cost, throughput, oxygen gettering).
> - **ARC:** SiN$_x$ :H, **~ 75 nm** thick, $n \approx 2.0$, peak at $\lambda \approx 600$ nm.
> - **EVA** is the encapsulant polymer; **Tedlar (PVF) + PET** form the backsheet.
> - **Standard cell equations:** $V_{OC} = \dfrac{nk_BT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$, $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OC} I_{SC} \text{FF}}{P_{\text{in}}}$.
> - **STC:** $1000 \,\text{W/m}^2$, AM 1.5G, $25\,°\text{C}$.
> - **c-Si benchmarks:** $V_{OC} \approx 0.65$ V, $J_{SC} \approx 40$ mA/cm², FF $\approx 0.80$, $\eta \approx 20$ – $22$ %.
> - **Energy Payback Time** of c-Si module: **1 – 2 years** (lifetime 25 – 30 years).
>
> **Cross-Domain Polymer ↔ PV Connections to Memorize:**
> 1. EVA encapsulant (PV) — polymer processing of encapsulant film.
> 2. Tedlar / PET backsheet — polymer film extrusion + lamination.
> 3. SiN$_x$ :H ARC — CVD/PECVD processing using SiH₄ (a silane gas).
> 4. Transparent conducting oxides (ITO) on thin-film PV — sputter-deposited but embedded in polymer substrates for **flexible PV**.
> 5. **Building-Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV)** — polymer-based encapsulants and front sheets are essential.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability and Characterisation of Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stability and Characterisation of Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Device Stability (KTU 2024 Definition):** In polymer and organic photovoltaic (OPV) technology, *device stability* refers to the ability of a solar cell or module to retain its initial power conversion efficiency (PCE), open-circuit voltage ($V_{OC}$), short-circuit current density ($J_{SC}$), and fill factor (FF) under prolonged exposure to operational stressors such as light, heat, humidity, and oxygen, quantified using lifetime metrics like **T80** and **T50** (the time required for efficiency to drop to 80% or 50% of its initial value, respectively).

> [!NOTE]
> **Device Characterisation (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Characterisation* is the systematic measurement and analysis of a photovoltaic device's electrical, optical, morphological, and chemical properties using techniques such as current–voltage (J–V) measurements, external quantum efficiency (EQE), impedance spectroscopy (IS), ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM), and X-ray diffraction (XRD), performed to evaluate performance, identify degradation modes, and validate material quality.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Real-World Car" Intuition)

Imagine you have just bought a brand-new **electric car** (your newly fabricated polymer solar cell). On day one, you test its driving range, top speed, and battery capacity (this is **characterisation** — measuring initial performance).

Now, after one year of driving in rain, dust, sun, and cold weather, you want to know how much range the car still has and what parts have worn out (this is **stability testing** — long-term reliability assessment).

- **Characterisation** = The "**Day-1 Diagnostic Test Report**" of your device.
- **Stability** = The "**Warranty Performance**" over months and years of real-world operation.
- **T80** = The mileage/time at which your car battery has degraded to **80%** of its original range.
- **Encapsulation** = The **paint coating and rust-proofing** that protects your car body from external damage.

The polymer active layer is analogous to a **delicate fruit** — without proper packaging (encapsulation), it degrades quickly when exposed to oxygen, moisture, and light.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Test Conditions | STC | AM1.5G, 1000 W/m², 25 °C |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| Elementary Charge | $q$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |
| Lifetime Metric | T80 | Time to reach 80% of initial PCE |
| Lifetime Metric | T50 | Time to reach 50% of initial PCE |
| Activation Energy | $E_a$ | Typically **0.6 – 1.2 eV** for OPV |
| Acceleration Factor | AF | Ratio of stress-test to field lifetimes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A device may show high initial efficiency but fail the stability test. Hence, *both* characterisation **and** stability are mandatory for any commercial-grade photovoltaic module certification (e.g., IEC 61215, IEC 61646, ISOS protocols).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Fundamental Degradation Mechanisms in Polymer PV Devices

A polymer photovoltaic device fails because of both **intrinsic** (internal) and **extrinsic** (external) stressors. Understanding each is critical for KTU problem-solving.

**A. Intrinsic Degradation Factors**
1. **Photo-oxidation of the active layer** — Singlet oxygen attacks the conjugated polymer backbone (especially P3HT, PTB7, PM6), breaking π-conjugation.
2. **Morphological instability** — Phase separation between donor and acceptor (e.g., P3HT:PCBM blend) grows over time, reducing the donor–acceptor interface area.
3. **Interfacial degradation** — Oxidation of low-work-function electrodes (Ca, Al) forms insulating oxide layers, increasing series resistance ($R_s$).
4. **Trap state formation** — Deep traps in the bandgap capture charges, reducing carrier mobility ($\mu$) and increasing recombination.
5. **Dimerisation / Cross-linking** — Photo-induced chemical reactions alter the HOMO/LUMO levels.

**B. Extrinsic Degradation Factors**
1. **Water/oxygen ingress** — Diffusion through pinholes in the encapsulation layer.
2. **UV-induced damage** — High-energy photons break polymer chains.
3. **Thermal cycling** — Repeated expansion–contraction induces mechanical fatigue and delamination.
4. **Mechanical stress** — Flexural stress in flexible OPV modules.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why does PCE drop over time?** → Because $V_{OC}$ falls (due to increased recombination), $J_{SC}$ falls (due to reduced absorption and charge extraction), and FF falls (due to rising $R_s$ and shunt leakage $R_{sh}$).
- **How is this quantified?** → By fitting the efficiency decay curve $\eta(t)$ to a **stretched exponential function**, allowing extraction of characteristic lifetime $\tau$ and stretch parameter $\beta$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical**. Memorise the equation, its variables, and its physical meaning.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC}}{P_{in}}$ | Power Conversion Efficiency | % |
| 2 | $FF = \dfrac{V_m \cdot J_m}{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC}}$ | Fill Factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $J(V) = J_{ph} - J_0 \left[ \exp\left( \dfrac{q(V + J R_s)}{n k_B T} \right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + J R_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Single-diode equivalent circuit model | A/m² |
| 4 | $\eta(t) = \eta_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[ -\left( \dfrac{t}{\tau} \right)^{\beta} \right]$ | Stretched exponential decay (lifetime fitting) | % |
| 5 | $t_{80} = \tau \cdot \left[ \ln(1/0.80) \right]^{1/\beta} = \tau \cdot (0.223)^{1/\beta}$ | Time to 80% of initial efficiency | hours |
| 6 | $t_{50} = \tau \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$ | Time to 50% of initial efficiency | hours |
| 7 | $AF = \dfrac{t_{use}}{t_{test}} = \exp\!\left[ \dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left( \dfrac{1}{T_{use}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{test}} \right) \right]$ | Arrhenius Acceleration Factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $EQE(\lambda) = \dfrac{1240 \cdot J_{SC}(\lambda)}{\lambda \cdot P_{in}(\lambda)}$ | External Quantum Efficiency | % |
| 9 | $V_{OC} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{J_{ph}}{J_0} + 1 \right)$ | Open-circuit voltage relation | V |
| 10 | $R_s = -\left.\dfrac{dV}{dJ}\right\vert_{V=V_{OC}}$ | Series resistance | Ω·cm² |
| 11 | $R_{sh} = -\left.\dfrac{dV}{dJ}\right\vert_{J=0}$ | Shunt resistance | Ω·cm² |
| 12 | $L_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu \tau k_B T}{q}}$ | Drift/diffusion length | m |

> [!NOTE]
> In Table above, the symbol $\left\vert\right.$ denotes the **evaluation point** on the J–V curve — strictly do **NOT** use the pipe character $\vert$ alone inside markdown tables, as it breaks syntax. The visible rendering uses $\vert$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial certification labs** (NREL, Fraunhofer ISE) use stretched exponential fitting to extrapolate T80 from a few hundred hours of accelerated testing to thousands of hours of field operation.
- **Module manufacturers** use Arrhenius acceleration to predict 25-year outdoor lifetimes from 1000-hour damp-heat tests at 85 °C / 85% RH.
- **Failure analysis teams** use EQE + J–V + impedance spectroscopy together to pinpoint *which layer* (active layer, interface, electrode) is degrading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stretched Exponential Decay (Kohlrausch–Williams–Watts Function)

The efficiency of a degrading organic photovoltaic device does **not** follow a simple exponential. Instead, it follows a **stretched exponential** due to the broad distribution of relaxation times in disordered polymer films.

**Step 1: Start with a generic first-order decay**

We assume the rate of efficiency loss is proportional to the remaining efficiency, but with a time-dependent rate constant $k(t)$:

$$
\dfrac{d\eta}{dt} = -k(t) \cdot \eta(t)
$$

**Step 2: Introduce the time-dependent rate constant**

Empirically, the rate constant decays in time due to the gradual consumption of reactive species:

$$
k(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\tau}\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^{\beta - 1}
$$

where $\beta$ ($0 < \beta \le 1$) is the **stretch parameter** — lower $\beta$ means broader distribution of degradation pathways.

**Step 3: Substitute back into the differential equation**

$$
\dfrac{d\eta}{dt} = -\dfrac{\beta}{\tau}\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^{\beta - 1} \cdot \eta(t)
$$

**Step 4: Separate variables**

$$
\dfrac{d\eta}{\eta} = -\dfrac{\beta}{\tau}\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^{\beta - 1} dt
$$

**Step 5: Integrate both sides from 0 to $t$**

$$
\int_{\eta_0}^{\eta(t)} \dfrac{d\eta'}{\eta'} = -\dfrac{\beta}{\tau}\int_{0}^{t}\left(\dfrac{t'}{\tau}\right)^{\beta - 1} dt'
$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the integrals**

The left side yields $\ln(\eta(t)/\eta_0)$. The right side becomes $-1 \cdot (t/\tau)^{\beta}$.

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\eta(t)}{\eta_0}\right) = -\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^{\beta}
$$

**Step 7: Exponentiate to obtain the final form**

$$
\eta(t) = \eta_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^{\beta}\right]
$$

This is the **stretched exponential decay law**, the cornerstone of OPV lifetime analysis.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: T80 (Time to 80% Efficiency) in Closed Form

**Step 1: Set $\eta(t)/\eta_0 = 0.80$**

$$
0.80 = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t_{80}}{\tau}\right)^{\beta}\right]
$$

**Step 2: Take natural logarithm of both sides**

$$
\ln(0.80) = -\left(\dfrac{t_{80}}{\tau}\right)^{\beta}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply by $-1$**

$$
-\ln(0.80) = \left(\dfrac{t_{80}}{\tau}\right)^{\beta}
$$

**Step 4: Recognise $-\ln(0.80) = \ln(1/0.80) = \ln(1.25) \approx 0.2231$**

$$
0.2231 = \left(\dfrac{t_{80}}{\tau}\right)^{\beta}
$$

**Step 5: Raise both sides to power $1/\beta$**

$$
(0.2231)^{1/\beta} = \dfrac{t_{80}}{\tau}
$$

**Step 6: Solve for $t_{80}$**

$$
\boxed{t_{80} = \tau \cdot (0.2231)^{1/\beta}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Insight:** If $\tau = 2000$ hours and $\beta = 0.5$, then $t_{80} = 2000 \times (0.2231)^{2} = 2000 \times 0.0498 = 99.6$ hours. A *low* $\beta$ dramatically shortens T80 even when $\tau$ is large — this is why $\beta$ matters as much as $\tau$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Arrhenius Acceleration Factor for Thermal Stress

**Step 1: The Arrhenius rate equation**

The degradation rate constant follows:

$$
k(T) = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Write the rate at two different temperatures**

- Use temperature: $T_{use}$ (typical field, e.g., 298 K)
- Test temperature: $T_{test}$ (accelerated, e.g., 358 K = 85 °C)

$$
k_{use} = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T_{use}}\right), \quad k_{test} = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T_{test}}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Form the ratio $k_{test}/k_{use}$**

$$
\dfrac{k_{test}}{k_{use}} = \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{test}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{use}}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 4: Define the Acceleration Factor (AF)**

By definition, $AF = k_{test}/k_{use}$ because the test runs faster:

$$
AF = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{use}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{test}}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 5: Express field lifetime**

$$
\boxed{t_{field} = AF \cdot t_{test}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** For $E_a = 0.9$ eV, $T_{use} = 298$ K, $T_{test} = 358$ K:
>
> $\dfrac{E_a}{k_B} = \dfrac{0.9 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 298} \approx 3503$ K
>
> $\dfrac{1}{T_{use}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{test}} = \dfrac{1}{298} - \dfrac{1}{358} \approx 6.16 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹
>
> $AF = \exp(3503 \times 6.16 \times 10^{-4}) = \exp(2.158) \approx 8.66$
>
> So a **1000-hour test at 85 °C** corresponds to **≈ 8660 hours (≈ 1 year) at 25 °C**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A polymer solar cell has $\eta_0 = 15\%$. After 500 hours of continuous illumination, the PCE is 12%. The stretch parameter $\beta = 0.6$. Find (a) the characteristic lifetime $\tau$, (b) the T80 value, and (c) the predicted PCE after 2000 hours.

**Solution:**

**(a) Find $\tau$:**

$$
\dfrac{\eta(t)}{\eta_0} = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^{\beta}\right] \Rightarrow \dfrac{12}{15} = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{500}{\tau}\right)^{0.6}\right]
$$

$$
0.8 = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{500}{\tau}\right)^{0.6}\right]
$$

Take natural log:

$$
\ln(0.8) = -\left(\dfrac{500}{\tau}\right)^{0.6}
$$

$$
-0.2231 = -\left(\dfrac{500}{\tau}\right)^{0.6}
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{500}{\tau}\right)^{0.6} = 0.2231
$$

Raise to power $1/0.6 = 1.667$:

$$
\dfrac{500}{\tau} = (0.2231)^{1.667} = 0.0854
$$

$$
\tau = \dfrac{500}{0.0854} \approx 5855 \text{ hours}
$$

**(b) Find T80:**

$$
t_{80} = \tau \cdot (0.2231)^{1/\beta} = 5855 \cdot (0.2231)^{1/0.6} = 5855 \cdot (0.2231)^{1.667}
$$

$$
(0.2231)^{1.667} = 0.0854
$$

$$
t_{80} = 5855 \times 0.0854 \approx 500 \text{ hours}
$$

(This makes sense by construction — at $t = 500$ h, PCE = 80% of initial.)

**(c) PCE after 2000 hours:**

$$
\eta(2000) = 15 \cdot \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{2000}{5855}\right)^{0.6}\right]
$$

$$
\dfrac{2000}{5855} \approx 0.3416
$$

$$
(0.3416)^{0.6} \approx 0.5108
$$

$$
\eta(2000) = 15 \cdot \exp(-0.5108) = 15 \cdot 0.6000 \approx 9.0\%
$$

The device degrades from 15% to **9%** in 2000 hours.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Characterisation Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fabricated PV Device] --> B[Initial Visual Inspection]
    B --> C[J-V Measurement under AM1.5G]
    B --> D[EQE / IPCE Spectroscopy]
    B --> E[UV-Vis Absorption]
    B --> F[AFM / SEM Morphology]
    B --> G[Impedance Spectroscopy IS]
    B --> H[XRD Crystallinity]
    C --> I[Compute PCE, Voc, Jsc, FF]
    D --> J[Spectral Response Curve]
    E --> K[Bandgap Estimation]
    F --> L[Phase Separation Analysis]
    G --> M[Recombination Resistance]
    H --> N[Crystallite Size via Scherrer]
    I --> O[Performance Benchmarking]
    J --> O
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P{Acceptable?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Proceed to Stability Tests]
    P -- No --> R[Fabrication Iteration]
    Q --> S[Continuous Illumination ISOS-L]
    Q --> T[Damp Heat 85/85 ISOS-D]
    Q --> U[Thermal Cycling ISOS-T]
    S --> V[Fit Stretched Exponential]
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Extract T80, T50, beta]
    W --> X[Final Certification Report]
```

### 4.2 Stability Degradation Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Oxygen Ingress] --> D1[Photo-oxidation]
    E2[Moisture Ingress] --> D2[Hydrolysis of Polymer]
    E3[UV Exposure] --> D3[Chain Scission]
    E4[Heat] --> D4[Morphological Reorganisation]
    E5[Mechanical Flex] --> D5[Delamination]
    D1 --> F1[Reduced Jsc]
    D2 --> F1
    D3 --> F2[Lower Voc and FF]
    D4 --> F2
    D5 --> F3[Increased Series Resistance]
    F1 --> X[PCE Loss]
    F2 --> X
    F3 --> X
    X --> Y[Device Failure]
```

### 4.3 ISOS (International Summit on Organic Solar Cell Stability) Test Categories

| ISOS Code | Stress Condition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| ISOS-L-1 | Simulated sunlight, 25 °C, ambient | Laboratory baseline |
| ISOS-L-2 | Light, 65 °C, ambient | Mild acceleration |
| ISOS-L-3 | Light, 85 °C, ambient | High-temperature acceleration |
| ISOS-D-1 | Dark, 85% RH, 25 °C | Humidity-only |
| ISOS-D-3 | Dark, 85% RH, 85 °C | Damp heat (IEC-style) |
| ISOS-T-1 | Thermal cycling, dark | Mechanical fatigue |

### 4.4 Equivalent Circuit of a Photovoltaic Device (Diagnostic Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IL[Photocurrent Source IL] --> A[Node A]
    A --> Rseries[Series Resistance Rs]
    Rseries --> B[Node B]
    B --> D[Diode Ideality Factor n]
    B --> Rshunt[Shunt Resistance Rsh]
    A --> Gnd[Ground]
    B --> Vout[Vout Terminal]
    Gnd --> Vout
```

The single-diode model (with $R_s$, $R_{sh}$, and ideality $n$) is the **universal diagnostic tool** to monitor how $R_s$ increases and $R_{sh}$ decreases over time, signalling specific failure modes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**Define the terms T80 and T50 as applied to photovoltaic device stability. Mention their significance in commercial certification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **T80 (1 mark):** The time, under specified operating conditions, after which the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of a solar cell has degraded to **80% of its initial value**.
- **T50 (1 mark):** The corresponding time for the PCE to fall to **50%** of its initial value.
- **Significance (1 mark):** T80 is the industry-standard benchmark for module warranty (e.g., 25-year warranty typically requires T80 ≥ 25 years under field conditions, often extrapolated from accelerated tests using the Arrhenius model). T50 indicates catastrophic failure and is used for end-of-life estimation.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**List any four characterisation techniques used to evaluate a polymer photovoltaic device and state what each measures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 marks each):**
1. **J–V measurement:** Evaluates $V_{OC}$, $J_{SC}$, FF, PCE, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$, and diode ideality factor $n$.
2. **External Quantum Efficiency (EQE):** Measures the ratio of collected charge carriers to incident photons as a function of wavelength — gives spectral response.
3. **UV–Vis Absorption Spectroscopy:** Determines the absorption spectrum and optical bandgap of the active layer.
4. **Impedance Spectroscopy (IS):** Probes recombination dynamics, charge transport, and interfacial resistance.

*(Alternative accepted: AFM, XRD, SEM, XPS.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**(a)** Derive the stretched exponential decay law $\eta(t) = \eta_0 \exp[-(t/\tau)^{\beta}]$ for polymer solar cell efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer solar cell has initial efficiency 14%. After 800 hours under 1-sun illumination, the efficiency is 11.2%. The stretch parameter $\beta = 0.55$. Calculate the characteristic lifetime $\tau$, the T80 lifetime, and the predicted efficiency at 3000 hours. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the rate equation with time-dependent rate constant:
$$
\dfrac{d\eta}{dt} = -k(t) \cdot \eta(t)
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the empirical form of $k(t)$:
$$
k(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\tau}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^{\beta - 1}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute and separate variables:
$$
\dfrac{d\eta}{\eta} = -\dfrac{\beta}{\tau}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^{\beta-1} dt
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Integrate both sides:
$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\eta(t)}{\eta_0}\right) = -\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^{\beta}
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Exponentiate to obtain final form:
$$
\boxed{\eta(t) = \eta_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^{\beta}\right]}
$$

State physical meaning: $\beta = 1$ gives pure first-order decay; lower $\beta$ indicates a broader distribution of degradation pathways (heterogeneous defect landscape).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Solve for $\tau$.

$$
\dfrac{11.2}{14} = 0.80 = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{800}{\tau}\right)^{0.55}\right]
$$

$$
\ln(0.80) = -0.2231 = -\left(\dfrac{800}{\tau}\right)^{0.55}
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{800}{\tau}\right)^{0.55} = 0.2231
$$

$$
\dfrac{800}{\tau} = (0.2231)^{1/0.55} = (0.2231)^{1.818} \approx 0.0710
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau \approx 11268 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate T80.

$$
t_{80} = \tau \cdot (0.2231)^{1/\beta} = 11268 \cdot (0.2231)^{1/0.55}
$$

$$
(0.2231)^{1.818} \approx 0.0710
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{80} = 11268 \times 0.0710 \approx 800 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Predict efficiency at 3000 h.

$$
\eta(3000) = 14 \cdot \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{3000}{11268}\right)^{0.55}\right]
$$

$$
\dfrac{3000}{11268} = 0.2662
$$

$$
(0.2662)^{0.55} \approx 0.4780
$$

$$
\eta(3000) = 14 \cdot \exp(-0.4780) = 14 \cdot 0.6201
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta(3000) \approx 8.68\%}
$$

---

#### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam - Alternative Module Choice]`

**(a)** With the aid of the single-diode equivalent circuit, derive the standard J–V equation and explain how each parameter ($J_{ph}$, $J_0$, $n$, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$) influences device stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An OPV device is tested at 85 °C (358 K) for 1000 hours and shows a 20% efficiency loss. The activation energy is $E_a = 0.95$ eV. Estimate the equivalent field-operation time at 25 °C (298 K). Comment on whether this device meets a 5-year warranty target. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The single-diode model consists of a photocurrent source $J_{ph}$ in parallel with a diode (with saturation current $J_0$ and ideality factor $n$) and a shunt resistance $R_{sh}$, all in series with $R_s$. Apply Kirchhoff's current law at the output node:

$$
J = J_{ph} - J_{diode} - J_{shunt}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Express diode and shunt currents:

$$
J_{diode} = J_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + J R_s)}{n k_B T}\right) - 1\right]
$$

$$
J_{shunt} = \dfrac{V + J R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Combine to give the complete J–V equation:

$$
J(V) = J_{ph} - J_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + J R_s)}{n k_B T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + J R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Stability implications:
- **Rising $R_s$** → indicates electrode oxidation or interfacial layer growth; reduces FF.
- **Falling $R_{sh}$** → indicates pinhole formation; reduces $V_{OC}$ and FF.
- **Rising $n$** (>2) → indicates trap-assisted recombination; reduces $V_{OC}$.
- **Rising $J_0$** → indicates increased dark current; reduces $V_{OC}$.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply Arrhenius equation:

$$
AF = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{use}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{test}}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute numerical values.

$$
\dfrac{E_a}{k_B} = \dfrac{0.95 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{1.381 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 11019 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_{use}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{test}} = \dfrac{1}{298} - \dfrac{1}{358} \approx 6.16 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\text{Exponent} = 11019 \times 6.16 \times 10^{-4} \approx 6.79
$$

$$
AF = e^{6.79} \approx 882
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate equivalent field time:

$$
t_{field} = AF \cdot t_{test} = 882 \times 1000 = 8.82 \times 10^{5} \text{ hours}
$$

Converting: $8.82 \times 10^{5} / 8760 \approx 100.7$ years of field-equivalent operation.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Conclusion:** Yes, the device comfortably meets a 5-year warranty. The 1000-hour 85 °C test corresponds to ≈ 100 years of normal 25 °C operation, providing a safety factor of ≈ 20×.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** on $\tau$ and $t_{80}$ — always write "hours" or "seconds".
