> 2. **Mixing $E_a/k_B$ in eV/K with $k_B$ in J/K** — convert $E_a$ to Joules before dividing, **OR** keep $E_a$ in eV and use $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K. Pick one convention and stick to it.
> 3. **Sign errors in Arrhenius AF** — the formula has $(1/T_{use} - 1/T_{test})$; reversing the order gives $1/AF$ by mistake.
> 4. **Forgetting to exponentiate** the $(t/\tau)^{\beta}$ term — write it as $\exp[-(t/\tau)^{\beta}]$, not $-(t/\tau)^{\beta}$.
> 5. **Not identifying the activation energy source** — $E_a$ is a fitted parameter, not given a priori. State this explicitly if asked.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Revision):**

- **Stability Metrics:**
  - T80 = time to 80% of initial PCE → **industry benchmark**.
  - T50 = time to 50% of initial PCE → catastrophic-failure marker.
  - $\tau$ = characteristic decay time; $\beta$ = stretch parameter.

- **Decay Law:**
  - $\eta(t) = \eta_0 \exp[-(t/\tau)^{\beta}]$ → always the right answer for fitting OPV ageing.
  - $t_{80} = \tau (0.2231)^{1/\beta}$ → closed-form expression, no integration needed.

- **Arrhenius Acceleration:**
  - $AF = \exp[(E_a/k_B)(1/T_{use} - 1/T_{test})]$.
  - Higher $T_{test}$ and higher $E_a$ → larger AF → shorter required test duration.

- **Characterisation Tools (must memorise):**
  - **J–V** → PCE, $V_{OC}$, $J_{SC}$, FF, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$, $n$.
  - **EQE** → spectral response, $J_{SC}$ cross-check.
  - **UV–Vis** → optical bandgap via Tauc plot.
  - **Impedance Spectroscopy** → recombination resistance, transport time.
  - **AFM/SEM** → morphology.
  - **XRD** → crystallinity / Scherrer grain size.

- **ISOS Protocols (three families):**
  - **L** = Light stress; **D** = Dark/Damp-Heat; **T** = Thermal cycling.
  - Always quote which ISOS-L/D/T level was used when reporting lifetime.

- **Failure Modes (correlate with parameters):**
  - Rising $R_s$ → electrode/interfacial degradation.
  - Falling $R_{sh}$ → pinholes / shunt leakage.
  - Falling $V_{OC}$ → recombination increase.
  - Falling $J_{SC}$ → absorption loss / morphology change.

- **Units Cheat Sheet:**
  - $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
  - $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.
  - Thermal voltage $V_T = k_B T/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K.

- **Exam Quick-Wins:**
  - Whenever a problem gives efficiency vs time, **always** try the stretched exponential fit first.
  - Whenever temperature-accelerated testing is mentioned, **always** use the Arrhenius AF.
  - For certification problems, **always** compute both $t_{field}$ and compare with warranty years.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stability Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Measurement** in polymer and photovoltaic technology is defined as the **systematic, quantitative evaluation of the temporal degradation behavior** of a photovoltaic device (especially polymer-based, organic, or perovskite solar cells) under defined environmental stressors (light, heat, humidity, oxygen, mechanical strain), quantified through key performance metrics—primarily the **Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE)** and its **T₈₀ lifetime** (the time required for PCE to decay to **80% of its initial value**).

In simpler terms, **stability measurement answers one fundamental question**: *"How long will this solar cell actually work in the real world before it stops producing useful electricity?"*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery Health Check" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Car's Fuel Efficiency Over Time:**
> Imagine you buy a new car rated at **20 km/L**. Five years later, due to engine wear, weather, and driving habits, it gives only **16 km/L**. Stability measurement in PV is **exactly the same idea**: we measure how the "mileage" (efficiency) of a solar cell drops with time under specific conditions.
> - The **initial PCE** = the car's showroom mileage.
> - The **T₈₀ lifetime** = the time until mileage drops to 80% (16 km/L).
> - **Accelerated stress tests** (humidity, heat, UV) = driving the car in harsh desert/monsoon conditions to *predict* real-world decay *fast*.

### 1.3 Core Metrics (KTU Mandatory Terminology)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|--------|--------|----------------------|
| Power Conversion Efficiency | **PCE (η)** | **%** (typically 15–25% for research cells) |
| Degradation Threshold | **T₈₀** | **hours / years** |
| Burn-in Loss | — | **% drop in first 100–500 h** |
| Open-circuit Voltage | **Vₒc** | **V** |
| Short-circuit Current Density | **Jₛc** | **mA/cm²** |
| Fill Factor | **FF** | **dimensionless (0–1)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"Define stability of a PV device"*, always mention **T₈₀** explicitly. This single term is the *gold-standard* answer in KTU valuation schemes.

### 1.4 Why Stability Measurement Matters (Engineering Context)

- **Commercial viability**: A solar cell with **25% PCE** but only **1-year lifetime** is commercially worthless compared to a **15% PCE** cell with **25-year lifetime**.
- **Polymer-specific challenge**: Polymer solar cells are especially prone to degradation due to **photo-oxidation**, **moisture ingress**, and **morphological changes** in the active layer.
- **Industry standard**: IEC (International Electrotechnical Commission) standards require minimum **25-year operational warranties**, making stability testing a **mandatory certification step**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of PV Degradation (Structured Logic)

PV device stability is governed by **four interconnected degradation drivers**. Understanding these is critical because **Stability Measurement protocols are designed around stressing each one**:

1. **Photochemical Degradation (Light-Induced)**
   - *Cause*: UV photons break polymer chains (chain scission) and oxidize the active layer.
   - *Affected materials*: Polymer donors (e.g., **P3HT, PTB7, PM6**) and acceptors (e.g., **PC₆₁BM, ITIC, Y6**).
   - *Indicator*: Drop in **Jₛc** and **FF**.

2. **Thermal Degradation (Heat-Induced)**
   - *Cause*: Elevated temperature (60–85 °C) accelerates molecular diffusion, phase separation, and electrode oxidation.
   - *Indicator*: Drop in **FF** and **Vₒc**.

3. **Hydrolytic / Moisture Degradation**
   - *Cause*: Water vapor penetrates encapsulation, reacts with active layer and electrodes.
   - *Indicator*: Rapid PCE drop, often within hours.

4. **Mechanical / Morphological Degradation**
   - *Cause*: Polymer crystallization, demixing of donor/acceptor phases, delamination.
   - *Indicator*: Gradual FF decline over thousands of hours.

### 2.2 The Degradation Mechanism Flow

```
  Light (UV photons)
       │
       ▼
  Excited State (Exciton)
       │
       ▼
  Reaction with O₂ / H₂O  ──►  Photo-oxidation products
       │                          │
       ▼                          ▼
  Chain scission          Bleach of absorption
       │                          │
       └──────────┬───────────────┘
                  ▼
         Loss of Jₛc + FF
                  ▼
         Drop in PCE (η)
                  ▼
       T₈₀ reached → "End of useful life"
```

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains every formula you need for solving KTU Board Exam numerical problems on Stability Measurement.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Power Conversion Efficiency | dimensionless (×100 = %) |
| 2 | $FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}} = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot J_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_{80} = t \; @ \; \eta(t) = 0.80 \, \eta_{initial}$ | Time to reach 80% of initial PCE | hours |
| 4 | $\text{Degradation Rate} = \dfrac{\eta_{final} - \eta_{initial}}{t \cdot \eta_{initial}} \times 100$ | % loss per hour | %/h |
| 5 | $t_{field} \approx t_{accelerated} \times AF$ | Time conversion via Acceleration Factor | hours |
| 6 | $AF = \dfrac{\text{Stress}_{test}}{\text{Stress}_{field}}$ | Acceleration Factor (e.g., 85°C/85% RH test) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\ln(\eta) = \ln(\eta_0) - k \cdot t$ | First-order (exponential) decay law | — |
| 8 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta\eta / \eta_0}{\Delta t}$ | Normalized degradation slope (stability metric) | h⁻¹ |
| 9 | $E_a = -R \cdot \dfrac{\partial \ln(k)}{\partial (1/T)}$ | Activation energy (Arrhenius for thermal aging) | J/mol or eV |
| 10 | $\text{Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)} < 10^{-4}$ | Encapsulation requirement for organic PV | g/m²/day |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Certification**: Stability data is **mandatory** for IEC-61215 (c-Si) and IEC-61646 (thin-film) certification.
- **Insurance & Warranty**: Banks financing solar farms require **T₈₀ ≥ 25 years**.
- **Material screening**: A new polymer candidate is *rejected* if its T₈₀ < 1000 h under ISOS-L-1 conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relating T₈₀ to the Degradation Rate Constant (k)

Starting from the **first-order exponential decay model** for PCE loss (the most commonly used model in polymer PV literature):

$$
\eta(t) = \eta_0 \cdot e^{-k t}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } t = T_{80}: \quad & \eta(T_{80}) = 0.80 \, \eta_0 \\
\text{Substitute:} \quad & \eta_0 \cdot e^{-k T_{80}} = 0.80 \, \eta_0 \\
\text{Divide both sides by } \eta_0: \quad & e^{-k T_{80}} = 0.80 \\
\text{Take natural logarithm:} \quad & -k T_{80} = \ln(0.80) \\
\text{Solve for } T_{80}: \quad & T_{80} = \dfrac{-\ln(0.80)}{k} = \dfrac{\ln(1.25)}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Numerical Value:** $\ln(1.25) \approx 0.2231$, so $T_{80} \approx \dfrac{0.2231}{k}$.
> - If $k = 10^{-4}$ /h, then $T_{80} \approx 2231$ h ≈ 93 days.
> - If $k = 10^{-5}$ /h, then $T_{80} \approx 22{,}313$ h ≈ 2.5 years.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Conversion Efficiency in Terms of Measurable Quantities

The PCE is the ratio of maximum electrical power output to incident solar power:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}}
$$

The maximum power is related to the open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current, and fill factor:

$$
P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}
$$

**Where:**
- $V_{oc}$ = open-circuit voltage (V)
- $J_{sc}$ = short-circuit current density (mA/cm²)
- $FF$ = fill factor (dimensionless, 0–1)
- $P_{in}$ = incident light power density (mW/cm²), typically **100 mW/cm²** under **AM 1.5G standard**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When stability data shows η dropping, you must identify **which parameter dropped first**. This reveals the *degradation mechanism*:
> - $J_{sc}$ drop → photo-bleaching of active layer.
> - $V_{oc}$ drop → trap formation / energy level shifts.
> - $FF$ drop → series resistance increase / shunt leakage.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Acceleration Factor for Damp-Heat Test

The **Acceleration Factor (AF)** relates accelerated test duration to equivalent field exposure:

$$
AF = \dfrac{\text{Degradation rate at stressed condition}}{\text{Degradation rate at field condition}}
$$

Using the Arrhenius assumption for thermal acceleration:

$$
k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF_{thermal} = \dfrac{k(T_{test})}{k(T_{field})} = \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{field}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{test}}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-typical):**
- Field temperature: $T_{field} = 298$ K (25 °C)
- Test temperature: $T_{test} = 358$ K (85 °C)
- Activation energy: $E_a = 0.8$ eV
- $R = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF = \exp\left[\dfrac{0.8}{8.617 \times 10^{-5}}\left(\dfrac{1}{298} - \dfrac{1}{358}\right)\right] \\
AF = \exp[9284 \times (3.356 \times 10^{-3} - 2.793 \times 10^{-3})] \\
AF = \exp[9284 \times 5.63 \times 10^{-4}] = \exp[5.226] \approx 185
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, **1000 hours** of 85 °C exposure ≈ **185,000 hours (≈ 21 years)** of field exposure. This is why **IEC 61215 mandates only 1000 h of damp-heat testing** to certify a 25-year lifetime.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Normalized Degradation Slope (s)

The metric *s* is the **slope of the normalized PCE vs. time curve in the linear (post-burn-in) regime**:

$$
s = \dfrac{\eta(t_2) - \eta(t_1)}{\eta_0 \cdot (t_2 - t_1)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A device with **s < 0.1% per 1000 h** is considered "stable" by KTU/IEEE conventions.

### 3.5 Worked Analytical Matrix — ISOS Protocols

| ISOS Code | Stressor | Temp (°C) | Humidity (%) | Light | Typical Duration |
|-----------|----------|-----------|--------------|-------|------------------|
| ISOS-D-1 | Dark storage | 23 | 50 | None | Long-term shelf-life |
| ISOS-D-3 | Dark + heat | 65 / 85 | 85 | None | Accelerated thermal |
| ISOS-L-1 | Lab light soaking | 25–45 | 30–50 | 1 Sun | 500–2000 h |
| ISOS-L-2 | Outdoor + sun-tracking | ambient | ambient | Real sun | Seasonal |
| ISOS-T-1 | Damp heat (85/85) | 85 | 85 | None | 1000 h |
| ISOS-LC-1 | Light + dark cycling | 25–65 | — | 1 Sun cyclic | Cyclic fatigue |
| ISOS-F-1 | Full encapsulation + outdoor | ambient | ambient | Outdoor | ≥ 1 year |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: A Stability Measurement Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LightSrc["Solar Simulator AM 1.5G 100 mW per cm2"]
    TempCtrl["Temperature Controlled Chamber 25 to 85 C"]
    HumCtrl["Humidity Controller 30 to 85 percent RH"]
    Sample["PV Device Under Test Polymer or Perovskite Cell"]
    Sourcemeter["Keithley Source Measure Unit SMU 2400"]
    IVsoftware["IV Curve Acquisition Software"]
    DataLog["Time Stamped Data Logger every 10 to 60 min"]
    Analysis["Degradation Analysis T80 Burn in s"]
    Decision["Pass or Fail Criterion IEC 61215"]

    LightSrc --> Sample
    TempCtrl --> Sample
    HumCtrl --> Sample
    Sample --> Sourcemeter
    Sourcemeter --> IVsoftware
    IVsoftware --> DataLog
    DataLog --> Analysis
    Analysis --> Decision
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The solar cell is subjected to **simultaneous** controlled stressors (light + heat + humidity). The SMU continuously measures the **I–V curve**, from which software extracts $V_{oc}$, $J_{sc}$, $FF$, and $\eta$. The time-series data is plotted to extract $T_{80}$.

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: From Raw Data to Stability Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RawIV["Raw IV Curve measured at time t"]
    Extract["Extract Voc Jsc FF eta"]
    Store["Store as eta vs time"]
    Plot["Plot normalized eta eta0 vs t"]
    BurnIn["Identify burn in region t less than 500 h"]
    Linear["Fit linear region to extract slope s"]
    T80["Interpolate time where eta equals 0.80 eta0"]
    Report["Report T80 and s as stability metrics"]

    RawIV --> Extract --> Store --> Plot --> BurnIn --> Linear --> T80 --> Report
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Degradation Modes and Their Signatures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Causes
        A1["UV Photon Absorption"]
        A2["Water Vapor Ingress"]
        A3["Temperature Rise"]
        A4["Oxygen Diffusion"]
    end
    subgraph Mechanisms
        B1["Chain scission of polymer"]
        B2["Hydrolysis of bonds"]
        B3["Electrode oxidation"]
        B4["Morphological phase separation"]
    end
    subgraph Signatures
        C1["Jsc drops fast"]
        C2["FF drops fast"]
        C3["Voc drops fast"]
        C4["All parameters drop gradually"]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> C1
    A2 --> B2 --> C2
    A3 --> B3 --> C3
    A4 --> B4 --> C4
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Use:** Draw this type of "cause → mechanism → observable signature" block diagram when asked to *"Explain how you identify the cause of degradation from IV data."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** *Define the term "T₈₀ lifetime" of a photovoltaic device. Why is it used as the standard metric for stability?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **T₈₀ lifetime** is defined as the operational time required for a photovoltaic device's Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE) to decay to **80% of its initial measured value**. It is the internationally accepted standard metric (per IEC 61215 / ISOS protocols) because: (i) it is a **quantitative, dimensionless-anchored** metric (not dependent on absolute efficiency values), allowing fair comparison across devices with different starting PCEs; (ii) it captures the practical "useful life" of the module—beyond T₈₀, the device is considered to have failed commercial operation; and (iii) it is **directly measurable** from accelerated stress tests via the Arrhenius relationship.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List any four ISOS protocols used for stability testing of organic/polymer solar cells and state the stressor used in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **ISOS-D-1**: Dark storage at room temperature — stressor: time alone.
> 2. **ISOS-L-1**: Laboratory light soaking under 1 Sun AM 1.5G — stressor: continuous illumination.
> 3. **ISOS-T-1**: Damp-heat test at **85 °C / 85% RH** — stressor: combined heat + humidity.
> 4. **ISOS-LC-1**: Light-dark cycling — stressor: cyclic thermal/photo fatigue.
> *(1 mark for naming the protocols, 1 mark for correct stressors, 1 mark for accurate conditions)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** *Answer any ONE of the following two questions.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Stability Fundamentals + Degradation Mechanisms*

**(a)** Define Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE) of a solar cell. Derive the expression for PCE in terms of $V_{oc}$, $J_{sc}$, $FF$, and incident power $P_{in}$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The Power Conversion Efficiency $\eta$ of a photovoltaic device is defined as the ratio of maximum electrical power output ($P_{max}$) delivered to the external load to the incident optical power $P_{in}$:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}}
$$

The I–V curve of a solar cell yields $V_{oc}$ (open-circuit voltage), $J_{sc}$ (short-circuit current density), and the maximum power point $(V_{mp}, J_{mp})$. The **Fill Factor** is defined as:

$$
FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot J_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}
$$

Since $P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF$, substituting into the PCE equation gives:

$$
\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}}
$$

For AM 1.5G standard, $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm². *(7 marks: 2 marks definition + 2 marks FF derivation + 3 marks final formula + units)*

---

**(b)** Explain the **four major degradation mechanisms** affecting polymer solar cells. For each, identify which IV parameter (Voc, Jsc, or FF) drops first. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Mechanism | Cause | First IV Parameter to Drop |
|---|-----------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Photo-oxidation** | UV photons + O₂ break polymer conjugation | **$J_{sc}$** (bleaching of absorption) |
| 2 | **Thermal degradation** | Heat-induced phase separation in bulk heterojunction | **$FF$** (increased series resistance) |
| 3 | **Hydrolytic degradation** | H₂O ingress reacts with active layer / electrodes | **$FF$ and $V_{oc}$** (electrode corrosion) |
| 4 | **Morphological change** | Crystallization / demixing of donor-acceptor domains | **$J_{sc}$ and $FF$** (loss of charge generation) |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per mechanism explained + 1 mark for correct IV parameter identification across all four)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Accelerated Testing + Quantitative Analysis*

**(a)** What is an **Acceleration Factor (AF)** in the context of stability testing? With a neat derivation, show how it is computed from the Arrhenius equation for a damp-heat test conducted at 85 °C relative to a field temperature of 25 °C, assuming an activation energy of 0.8 eV. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Acceleration Factor (AF)** is defined as the ratio of the degradation rate observed under an accelerated (harsh) test condition to that observed under normal field conditions. It allows engineers to **extrapolate short-term lab data to predict long-term field lifetimes**.

Assuming the degradation follows Arrhenius kinetics, the rate constant at temperature $T$ is:

$$
k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}
$$

Therefore:

$$
AF = \dfrac{k(T_{test})}{k(T_{field})} = \dfrac{A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT_{test}}}{A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT_{field}}} = \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{field}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{test}}\right)\right]
$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF &= \exp\left[\dfrac{0.8}{8.617 \times 10^{-5}}\left(\dfrac{1}{298} - \dfrac{1}{358}\right)\right] \\
&= \exp\left[9284 \times 5.63 \times 10^{-4}\right] \\
&= \exp(5.226) \approx 185
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, **1000 h** of 85 °C damp-heat testing is equivalent to **~185,000 h (~21 years)** of field operation at 25 °C. *(7 marks: 2 marks AF definition + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks numerical computation)*

---

**(b)** A polymer solar cell has an initial PCE of **18.0%**. After 500 hours of ISOS-L-1 testing, the PCE drops to **15.3%**. Assuming first-order exponential decay, calculate: (i) the degradation rate constant $k$, (ii) the $T_{80}$ lifetime, and (iii) the predicted efficiency after **2000 hours**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The exponential decay model is $\eta(t) = \eta_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$.

**(i) Finding k:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
15.3 &= 18.0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot 500} \\
e^{-500k} &= \dfrac{15.3}{18.0} = 0.850 \\
-500k &= \ln(0.850) = -0.1625 \\
k &= \dfrac{0.1625}{500} = 3.25 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Finding T₈₀:**

$$
T_{80} = \dfrac{\ln(1.25)}{k} = \dfrac{0.2231}{3.25 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 686 \; \text{h}
$$

**(iii) Predicted efficiency at 2000 h:**

$$
\eta(2000) = 18.0 \cdot e^{-3.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 2000} = 18.0 \cdot e^{-0.65} = 18.0 \times 0.522 = 9.40\%
$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for k calculation + 2 marks for T₈₀ + 2 marks for η at 2000 h + 1 mark for correct units)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Stability Measurement Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units of k** — always write $k$ in **h⁻¹** or **s⁻¹**; missing units = **−0.5 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing T₈₀ with T₅₀** — T₈₀ = 80% retained, T₅₀ = 50% retained. Using the wrong threshold gives a **wrong answer and full mark deduction** in Part B.
> 3. **Mixing AM 1.5G and AM 0 conditions** — KTU almost always uses **100 mW/cm²** ($P_{in}$); using 1361 W/m² without conversion = **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Ignoring the Arrhenius factor "R" units** — use **R = 8.617 J/(K·mol) = 8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K** consistently; mixed units = **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the "which IV parameter drops first"** discussion — in mechanism questions, this is a **mandatory sub-step** worth **2 marks** in Part B.
> 6. **Writing "burn-in loss" without defining it** — burn-in = the initial sharp PCE drop in the first **100–500 h** before linear stabilization.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Stability Measurement** = quantitative tracking of PCE over time under controlled stressors.
- ✅ **T₈₀** = time for PCE to drop to **80% of initial value** — *the* universal stability metric.
- ✅ **Exponential decay law**: $\eta(t) = \eta_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ → gives $T_{80} = \ln(1.25)/k$.
- ✅ **Degradation causes**: Photo-oxidation (UV+O₂), Thermal (T), Hydrolytic (H₂O), Morphological.
- ✅ **Parameter-to-cause mapping**: $J_{sc}$ ↓ → bleaching; $V_{oc}$ ↓ → traps; $FF$ ↓ → series R / shunt leak.
- ✅ **ISOS protocols**: D (dark), L (light), T (thermal/damp-heat), LC (light cycling), F (full outdoor).
- ✅ **Damp heat "85/85"** = 85 °C and 85% RH for 1000 h — gold-standard accelerated test.
- ✅ **Arrhenius AF**: $AF = \exp[(E_a/R)(1/T_{field} - 1/T_{test})]$ — converts lab hours to field years.
- ✅ **IEC 61215**: certifies c-Si modules; **IEC 61646**: certifies thin-film modules.
- ✅ **Encapsulation requirement for organic PV**: WVTR < **10⁻⁴ g/m²/day**.
- ✅ **Standard test conditions**: AM 1.5G, **1000 W/m²** = **100 mW/cm²**, 25 °C cell temperature.
- ✅ **Burn-in**: initial rapid degradation (first ~100 h), distinct from long-term linear decay slope **s**.
- ✅ **Activation energy** for PV degradation: typically **0.6–1.2 eV**.
- ✅ **Higher k** = faster degradation = **shorter T₈₀** = less stable device.
- ✅ **Acceleration factor formula**: 1000 h @ 85 °C / 85% RH ≈ 20–25 years of field life (for $E_a \approx 0.8$ eV).

> **End of Notes — Stability Measurement (Module 4, PEPOT757)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacture of Polymer Solar Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Manufacture of Polymer Solar Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Polymer Solar Cell (PSC)**, also known as an **Organic Photovoltaic (OPV)** cell, is a third-generation thin-film photovoltaic device in which the photoactive layer is composed of a conjugated polymer (electron donor) blended with a fullerene or non-fullerene small molecule / polymer (electron acceptor). The active junction is typically realized as a **Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ)** — an interpenetrating nanoscale network of donor and acceptor phases — sandwiched between a transparent anode (usually ITO) and a low-work-function metal cathode. The standardized power conversion efficiency (PCE) of state-of-the-art single-junction PSCs has surpassed **19%** under AM 1.5G illumination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 mandates the study of the complete manufacturing workflow — from substrate preparation to encapsulation — for both **conventional (regular)** and **inverted** polymer solar cell architectures.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Molecular Leaf"

Imagine a real green leaf performing photosynthesis:

- The **chlorophyll** in the leaf absorbs sunlight → a **conjugated polymer** (e.g., P3HT, PTB7, PM6) absorbs a photon and creates a bound electron–hole pair (exciton).
- The leaf's internal membranes separate the charges → the **donor–acceptor interface** in the BHJ dissociates the exciton into free electrons and holes.
- Veins transport water and sugars → **interpenetrating donor/acceptor domains** transport electrons and holes to their respective electrodes.
- The leaf is fragile and needs a cuticle → the OPV needs **encapsulation** (glass or barrier film) to protect against oxygen and moisture.

In short, a polymer solar cell is essentially a **synthetic, ultra-thin, flexible leaf** that converts sunlight into electricity using carbon-based molecules instead of biological pigments.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The four cardinal metrics used globally to benchmark any solar cell (KTU examiners expect these):

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc}$ | Voltage when current is zero | Volts (V) |
| Short-Circuit Current Density | $J_{sc}$ | Current per unit area at zero voltage | mA/cm² |
| Fill Factor | FF | Ratio of max power to $V_{oc} \times J_{sc}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| Power Conversion Efficiency | $\eta$ (or PCE) | Electrical output / optical input | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Condition (STC):** AM 1.5G solar spectrum, irradiance $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm², cell temperature $T = 25$ °C. These are the *exact* conditions KTU problems assume unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Four-Step Photocurrent Dance"

A polymer solar cell generates electricity through a well-defined sequence:

1. **Photon Absorption:** A photon of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ (optical band gap) is absorbed by the conjugated polymer. The $\pi$–$\pi^{*}$ electronic transition excites an electron from the HOMO to the LUMO, creating a **Frenkel-type exciton** (binding energy $\sim$ 0.3–1.0 eV).
2. **Exciton Diffusion:** The bound exciton migrates via Förster/Förster–Dexter energy transfer through the donor phase. It must reach a **donor–acceptor (D–A) interface** within its **exciton diffusion length** $L_D \approx 5\text{–}20$ nm — otherwise it decays (geminate recombination).
3. **Charge Separation (Dissociation):** At the D–A interface, the **LUMO offset** ($\Delta E_{LUMO} \geq 0.3$ eV) and **HOMO offset** ($\Delta E_{HOMO} \geq 0.3$ eV) provide the driving force to split the exciton into a free electron (on the acceptor LUMO) and a free hole (on the donor HOMO).
4. **Charge Transport & Collection:** Free charges hop through percolating donor/acceptor pathways to their respective electrodes — holes via the **donor → anode (HTL side)**, electrons via the **acceptor → cathode (ETL side)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why BHJ?** In a planar bilayer junction, excitons generated farther than $L_D$ from the interface recombine. The **bulk heterojunction** maximizes D–A interfacial area throughout the entire active layer, ensuring *every* exciton finds a dissociation site within $L_D$.

## 2.2 Device Architectures

### A. Conventional (Regular) Architecture
$$\text{Glass} \mid \text{ITO} \mid \text{PEDOT:PSS (HTL)} \mid \text{Active Layer (D:A blend)} \mid \text{Ca or LiF} \mid \text{Al (cathode)}$$

- Light enters through the ITO side.
- **PEDOT:PSS** = poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) — the most common hole transport layer.
- Low-work-function metal (Ca/Al) acts as the cathode.

### B. Inverted Architecture
$$\text{Glass} \mid \text{ITO} \mid \text{ZnO or TiO}_x \text{ (ETL)} \mid \text{Active Layer} \mid \text{MoO}_3 \text{ or PEDOT:PSS} \mid \text{Ag (anode)}$$

- More **air-stable** because the high-work-function Ag anode is on top and the ITO is modified with an n-type oxide.
- Preferred for **roll-to-roll (R2R)** manufacturing on flexible substrates.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Power conversion efficiency | % |
| 2 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot J_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}$ | Fill factor (squareness of J–V curve) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{1}{e}\,\bigl(E_{HOMO}^{D} - E_{LUMO}^{A}\bigr) - 0.3\,\text{eV}$ | Empirical open-circuit voltage | V |
| 4 | $J_{sc} = q \int \Phi_{AM1.5}(E) \cdot EQE(E)\,dE$ | Short-circuit current density (integral form) | mA/cm² |
| 5 | $I(\lambda) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha(\lambda) \cdot d}$ | Beer–Lambert law (absorbed intensity) | W/m² |
| 6 | $\alpha(\lambda) = \dfrac{1}{d}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Absorption coefficient | cm⁻¹ |
| 7 | $L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot \tau}$ | Exciton diffusion length (random walk) | nm |
| 8 | $J_{ph} = J_{sat}\,\bigl[1 - \exp(-V/J_{sat} \cdot kT/q)\bigr]$ | Hecht formula (geminate recombination) | mA/cm² |
| 9 | $P_{dis} = \dfrac{J_{ph}}{J_{sat}}$ | Dissociation probability | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $J_0 = q \cdot N_{c} \cdot N_{v}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{kT}\right)$ | Dark saturation current | mA/cm² |
| 11 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{sc}}{J_0} + 1\right)$ | Diode equation form of $V_{oc}$ | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equations 1–3 are the *first three lines* of any KTU numerical. Equation 4 is the bridge between optical absorption ($EQE$) and electrical output ($J_{sc}$). Equation 7 is the *physical reason* why BHJ morphology is mandatory for polymer solar cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Flexible, lightweight solar panels** (wearables, IoT sensors, building-integrated photovoltaetics).
- **Semi-transparent solar windows** (tunable band gap via molecular engineering).
- **Low-cost, solution-processed, large-area manufacturing** via roll-to-roll printing — the **only solar technology** compatible with newspaper-style printing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Power Conversion Efficiency $\eta$

**Goal:** Starting from fundamental circuit concepts, derive $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc}\cdot J_{sc}\cdot FF}{P_{in}}$.

The illuminated current–voltage characteristic of a solar cell follows the **Shockley diode equation** plus a photocurrent term:

$$J(V) = J_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - J_{ph}$$

**Step 1 — Short-circuit current density.**
At $V = 0$, the exponential term is unity, and the dark current is negligible compared to the photogenerated current:

$$J_{sc} = J(0) = J_0(1 - 1) - (-J_{ph}) = J_{ph}$$

**Step 2 — Open-circuit voltage.**
At $V = V_{oc}$, the net current is zero, so:

$$0 = J_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - J_{ph}$$

Since $J_{ph} \gg J_0$, the "-1" can be dropped:

$$J_{ph} = J_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right)$$

Solving for $V_{oc}$:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{J_{ph}}{J_0} + 1\right)$$

**Step 3 — Maximum power point.**
The maximum power $P_{max} = V_{mp}\cdot J_{mp}$ occurs where $\dfrac{d(VJ)}{dV} = 0$. By definition of the **fill factor**:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp}\cdot J_{mp}}{V_{oc}\cdot J_{sc}}$$

**Step 4 — Power conversion efficiency.**
The efficiency is the ratio of electrical power output to incident optical power:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{mp}\cdot J_{mp}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc}\cdot J_{sc}\cdot FF}{P_{in}}$$

Substituting $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm² for AM 1.5G:

$$\boxed{\eta\,[\%] = \frac{V_{oc}\,[V]\times J_{sc}\,[mA/cm^2]\times FF}{100\,[mW/cm^2]}\times 100\%}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Beer–Lambert Absorption in the Active Layer

**Goal:** Show that the absorbed photon flux depends exponentially on film thickness $d$ and absorption coefficient $\alpha(\lambda)$.

Consider a monochromatic beam of intensity $I_0(\lambda)$ entering a thin film of thickness $d$. Within an infinitesimal slice $dx$ at depth $x$, the fractional loss in intensity is proportional to the local intensity and the absorption coefficient:

$$-\frac{dI}{dx} = \alpha(\lambda)\,I(x)$$

Separate variables and integrate from $x = 0$ to $x = d$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I}\frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha(\lambda) \int_{0}^{d} dx$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha(\lambda)\,d$$

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\boxed{I(d) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha(\lambda)\,d}}$$

The **absorbed intensity** is therefore:

$$I_{abs}(\lambda) = I_0 - I(d) = I_0 \left[1 - e^{-\alpha(\lambda)\,d}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** For a 200 nm active layer with $\alpha \approx 10^5$ cm⁻¹, the optical density $\alpha d \approx 2$, meaning $\sim 86\%$ of incident photons are absorbed in a single pass — this is why PSCs can be ultra-thin compared to silicon ($\sim 200\,\mu\text{m}$).

---

## 3.3 KTU Numerical Worked Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem (KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model):**
A polymer solar cell under AM 1.5G ($P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm²) has $V_{oc} = 0.82$ V, $J_{sc} = 14.5$ mA/cm², and $FF = 0.68$. Calculate (a) the maximum power output per cm², and (b) the power conversion efficiency.

**Step (a) — Maximum power.**

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \times J_{sc} \times FF$$

$$P_{max} = 0.82\,[V] \times 14.5\,[mA/cm^2] \times 0.68$$

Compute stepwise:

$$0.82 \times 14.5 = 11.89$$

$$11.89 \times 0.68 = 8.0852\,[mW/cm^2]$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 8.09\,[mW/cm^2]}$$

**Step (b) — Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{8.0852}{100}\times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 8.09\,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** $8\%$ is a realistic single-junction PSC efficiency. Top lab devices today exceed **19%**, but the typical engineering textbook problem stays in the **5–12%** range.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Fabrication Parameters vs. Performance

| Process Step | Key Parameter | Typical Value | Effect on Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| Substrate cleaning | UV-ozone time | **10–20 min** | Improves ITO work function and wettability |
| PEDOT:PSS spin coating | rpm / acceleration | **3000–5000 rpm** | Controls HTL thickness (30–60 nm) |
| Active layer spin coating | rpm | **1000–2000 rpm** | Determines active layer thickness (80–250 nm) |
| Annealing | Temperature / time | **110–150 °C / 10 min** | Optimizes BHJ phase separation and crystallinity |
| Solvent additive | DIO, CN, ODT volume % | **0.5–3 vol %** | Suppresses large-scale phase separation |
| Cathode evaporation | Vacuum / rate | **< 10⁻⁶ mbar / 0.1–1 nm/s** | Prevents interfacial oxidation |
| Encapsulation | UV-curable epoxy + glass | **N/A** | Extends device lifetime from hours to >1000 h |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conventional Polymer Solar Cell — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sun[Sunlight AM 1.5G 100 mW per cm2] --> Sub
    Sub[Glass Substrate] --> ITOA[ITO Transparent Anode 150 nm]
    ITOA --> HTL[PEDOT PSS Hole Transport Layer 40 nm]
    HTL --> BHJ[Bulk Heterojunction Active Layer 100 to 200 nm Donor Polymer P3HT plus Acceptor PCBM]
    BHJ --> ETP[Exciton Dissociation at Donor Acceptor Interface]
    ETP --> Cath[Al Cathode 100 nm]
    Cath --> Ext[External Circuit Photocurrent Flow]
    Ext --> Meas[Measurement PCE percent]
```

## 4.2 Complete Fabrication Process Flowchart (Conventional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Substrate Preparation ITO Glass] --> P2[Step 2 Ultrasonic Cleaning Detergent plus DI water plus Acetone plus IPA]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 UV Ozone Treatment 15 min]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Spin Coat PEDOT PSS 4000 rpm 30 s]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Anneal PEDOT PSS 130 C 10 min]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Spin Coat Active Layer D plus A blend in Chlorobenzene]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Solvent Anneal or Thermal Anneal 110 to 150 C]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Thermal Evaporation Ca plus Al 10 to 100 nm]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Encapsulation UV Epoxy plus Cover Glass]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 J V Characterization under Solar Simulator]
```

## 4.3 Roll-to-Roll (R2R) Manufacturing — Industrial Scale

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Unwind Flexible PET plus ITO Substrate Roll] --> R2[Slot Die Coat ZnO Nanoparticle ETL]
    R2 --> R3[Slot Die Coat Active Layer D plus A in Non Toxic Solvent]
    R3 --> R4[Slot Die Coat PEDOT PSS or MoO3 HTL]
    R4 --> R5[Vacuum Evaporation Ag Anode 100 nm]
    R5 --> R6[Lamination Encapsulation Barrier Film]
    R6 --> R7[Rewind Finished OPV Module Roll]
    R7 --> R8[Laser Scribing and Module Assembly]
```

## 4.4 Active Layer Morphology — BHJ Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Donor Phase Conjugated Polymer P3HT PTB7 PM6] <--> B[Acceptor Phase Fullerene PCBM or Non Fullerene Y6 ITIC]
    A --> X1[Exciton Generated in Donor]
    X1 --> X2[Diffusion Length LD 5 to 20 nm]
    X2 --> X3[Reach Donor Acceptor Interface]
    X3 --> X4[Charge Transfer Exciton Splits]
    X4 --> Y1[Free Electron on Acceptor LUMO]
    X4 --> Y2[Free Hole on Donor HOMO]
    Y1 --> Z1[Transport to Cathode ETL]
    Y2 --> Z2[Transport to Anode HTL]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ) in the context of polymer solar cells. Why is it preferred over a planar bilayer junction?**

**Model Answer:**
A Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ) is a nanoscale interpenetrating network formed by blending an electron-donor (conjugated polymer) and an electron-acceptor (fullerene or non-fullerene) in a common solvent, creating a phase-separated morphology with domain sizes comparable to the **exciton diffusion length** ($L_D \approx 5\text{–}20$ nm).

It is preferred over a planar bilayer because in a bilayer, excitons generated farther than $L_D$ from the flat interface undergo **geminate recombination** and are lost. The BHJ maximizes the donor–acceptor interfacial area throughout the entire active layer thickness, ensuring that nearly every photogenerated exciton reaches a dissociation site within its diffusion length, dramatically improving the internal quantum efficiency. **(3 marks — Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for $L_D$ link, 1 mark for planar vs BHJ contrast.)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the four key performance metrics of a solar cell. Write the expression for power conversion efficiency (PCE).**

**Model Answer:**
The four metrics are:

1. Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ (V)
2. Short-circuit current density $J_{sc}$ (mA/cm²)
3. Fill factor $FF$ (dimensionless)
4. Power conversion efficiency $\eta$ (%)

The efficiency expression under AM 1.5G illumination ($P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm²):

$$\eta = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}} \times 100\,\%$$

**(3 marks — Award 0.5 mark each for the four metrics, 1 mark for the formula.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Model)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model — Module 4 Choice 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **step-by-step fabrication process** of a conventional polymer solar cell with the structure **Glass / ITO / PEDOT:PSS / P3HT:PCBM / Al**. Include substrate preparation, HTL deposition, active layer deposition, annealing, cathode evaporation, and encapsulation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substrate Preparation:** Patterned ITO-coated glass substrates (sheet resistance $\sim$ 15 $\Omega$/sq) are ultrasonically cleaned sequentially in detergent, deionized water, acetone, and isopropanol for 15 min each, then blow-dried with nitrogen. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — UV-Ozone Treatment:** Substrates are exposed to UV-ozone for 15 min to remove residual organics and to raise the ITO work function (from $\sim$ 4.5 eV to $\sim$ 4.9 eV), improving hole extraction. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — PEDOT:PSS (HTL) Deposition:** A filtered aqueous PEDOT:PSS solution is spin-coated at 4000 rpm for 30 s, producing a uniform film of $\sim$ 40 nm thickness. The film is annealed at 130 °C for 10 min in ambient air to remove water. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Active Layer Deposition:** A solution of P3HT (donor) and PCBM (acceptor) in chlorobenzene (1:1 weight ratio, 20 mg/mL each) is spin-coated at 1000 rpm for 60 s inside a nitrogen glovebox. A typical active-layer thickness is $\sim$ 100–200 nm. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Solvent / Thermal Annealing:** The film is thermally annealed at 110–150 °C for 10 min to optimize BHJ morphology — promoting P3HT crystallization and forming interpenetrating donor/acceptor domains of $\sim$ 10–20 nm scale. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Cathode Evaporation:** Aluminum (100 nm) is thermally evaporated through a shadow mask at a vacuum of $< 10^{-6}$ mbar and a deposition rate of 0.5–1 nm/s. Optionally, a thin Ca or LiF layer is pre-deposited to improve electron extraction. **(1 mark)**

**Step 7 — Encapsulation:** A UV-curable epoxy and a cover glass are applied on top to protect the cell from oxygen and moisture, extending its operational lifetime. **(1 mark)**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **conventional** and **inverted** polymer solar cell architectures in terms of structure, electrode polarity, charge-transport layers, and air-stability. Why is the inverted architecture preferred for roll-to-roll manufacturing?

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Conventional | Inverted |
|---|---|---|
| **Stack Order** | Glass / ITO / HTL / Active / ETL / Metal | Glass / ITO / ETL / Active / HTL / Metal |
| **Anode** | ITO (bottom, light-facing) | Ag (top) |
| **Cathode** | Al or Ca/Al (top) | ITO (bottom) |
| **HTL** | PEDOT:PSS | MoO₃ or PEDOT:PSS |
| **ETL** | Ca or LiF (or none) | ZnO or TiOₓ nanoparticles |
| **Light Entry** | Through ITO (transparent) | Through ITO (transparent) |
| **Air Stability** | Poor (PEDOT:PSS is hygroscopic, Ca oxidizes) | **Excellent** (ZnO is stable, Ag is inert) |
| **Roll-to-Roll Suitability** | Limited | **Preferred** |

**Why Inverted for R2R:** The conventional architecture uses acidic PEDOT:PSS and low-work-function Ca — both degrade in ambient air and corrode ITO, which is a fatal flaw for a continuous printing process. The inverted architecture uses solution-processable ZnO nanoparticles as the ETL and high-work-function Ag as the top anode, both of which are **chemically robust** and compatible with **slot-die coating and gravure printing** on flexible PET substrates. This dramatically improves operational stability, throughput, and yield — the three pillars of industrial R2R manufacturing. **(Award 4 marks for table, 3 marks for explanation.)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model — Module 4 Choice 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four sequential steps** by which a polymer solar cell converts incident photons into usable electrical current. For each step, identify the physical location in the device and the relevant length scale or energy criterion.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Photon Absorption (in the donor phase).**
A photon of energy $h\nu \geq E_g^{opt}$ is absorbed by the conjugated polymer, exciting an electron from the HOMO to the LUMO. This creates a **Frenkel exciton** — a tightly bound electron–hole pair with binding energy $E_b \approx 0.3\text{–}1.0$ eV. The optical absorption coefficient $\alpha$ is typically $10^5$ cm⁻¹, so a 200 nm film absorbs $\sim 86\%$ of incident photons (Beer–Lambert law). **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 2 — Exciton Diffusion (within the donor phase).**
The bound exciton migrates via Förster energy transfer through the donor domains. It must reach a **donor–acceptor interface** within its **exciton diffusion length** $L_D = \sqrt{D\cdot\tau} \approx 5\text{–}20$ nm; otherwise it decays via geminate recombination (radiative or non-radiative). This is why BHJ domain sizes are engineered to be $\leq L_D$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Charge Dissociation (at the D–A interface).**
At the interface, the **LUMO offset** $\Delta E_{LUMO} \geq 0.3$ eV and **HOMO offset** $\Delta E_{HOMO} \geq 0.3$ eV provide the thermodynamic driving force to split the exciton into a free electron (transferred to the acceptor LUMO) and a free hole (remaining on the donor HOMO). The driving force overcomes the exciton binding energy $E_b$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Charge Transport and Collection (through percolating networks).**
Free electrons hop through the acceptor phase to the **cathode** (via the ETL), and free holes hop through the donor phase to the **anode** (via the HTL). Charges are collected at the external circuit, producing a photocurrent. The transport is governed by the carrier mobilities ($\mu_e, \mu_h$) and the built-in electric field from the work-function difference of the electrodes. **(2 marks)**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **major deposition techniques** used to coat the polymer:fullerene active layer in both lab-scale and industrial-scale PSC fabrication. Compare their suitability for large-area manufacturing.

**Model Solution:**

| Technique | Scale | Mechanism | Film Thickness Control | Suitability for R2R |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Spin Coating** | Lab (cm²) | Centrifugal force spreads solution | Excellent (via rpm) | **Not suitable** — batch process |
| **Doctor Blade** | Lab/pilot (cm²) | Blade spreads paste on substrate | Moderate | Pilot only |
| **Slot-Die Coating** | Pilot → Industrial (m²) | Solution extruded through slit onto moving web | **Excellent** (via flow rate & web speed) | **Highly suitable** |
| **Inkjet Printing** | Lab/pilot | Droplet-on-demand | Pattern-defined | Limited throughput |
| **Gravure Printing** | Industrial (m²) | Engraved cylinder transfers ink | Good | **Highly suitable** |
| **Spray Coating** | Pilot | Aerosol deposition | Moderate | Suitable |
| **Screen Printing** | Industrial | Stencil + squeegee | Good (thick films) | Suitable for HTL/ETL |

**Lab-scale:** Spin coating is the workhorse because it is fast, reproducible, and gives uniform films on cm² substrates. However, it wastes $\sim 95\%$ of the solution and cannot be scaled.

**Industrial-scale:** **Slot-die coating** and **gravure printing** dominate because they enable continuous, patternable deposition on flexible PET foils moving at $\sim$ 10 m/min — true roll-to-roll production. The use of **non-toxic, non-halogenated solvents** (e.g., o-xylene, 2-methyltetrahydrofuran) is critical for environmental and worker-safety reasons. **(Award 4 marks for table, 3 marks for scale discussion.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing BHJ with bilayer** — BHJ is a *blend*, bilayer is a *stack*. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sim 0.3$ eV empirical correction** in the $V_{oc}$ formula $V_{oc} = (1/e)(E_{HOMO}^D - E_{LUMO}^A) - 0.3$ eV. Examiners deduct marks for omitting the 0.3 eV. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Using wrong units** — $J_{sc}$ is mA/cm², $P_{in}$ is 100 mW/cm². Mixing A with mA leads to $\eta$ off by $10^3$. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Skipping the encapsulation step** in the fabrication flow — mandatory for full marks on "manufacture" questions. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Stating $V_{oc} = E_g / e$** — wrong! $V_{oc}$ is limited by the **donor HOMO − acceptor LUMO** gap, not the donor's optical band gap. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Spin-coating at atmospheric air** — degrades the active layer; must be in a **nitrogen glovebox**. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Architecture:** Conventional = Glass/ITO/PEDOT:PSS/Active/Al · Inverted = Glass/ITO/ZnO/Active/MoO₃/Ag.
- **BHJ = mandatory** because exciton diffusion length $L_D$ is only 5–20 nm.
- **Key driving forces:** $\Delta E_{LUMO} \geq 0.3$ eV and $\Delta E_{HOMO} \geq 0.3$ eV for charge dissociation.
- **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = (V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF) / P_{in} \times 100\%$.
- **$V_{oc}$ formula:** $V_{oc} = (1/e)(E_{HOMO}^D - E_{LUMO}^A) - 0.3$ eV (empirical).
- **Beer–Lambert law:** $I(d) = I_0 e^{-\alpha d}$ — governs how much light a thin film absorbs.
- **Fabrication steps in order:** Clean → UV-ozone → Spin/slot-die HTL → Spin/slot-die active layer → Anneal → Evaporate cathode → Encapsulate → Measure.
- **Spin coating = lab only; slot-die / gravure = industrial R2R.**
- **All spin-coating of the active layer must be done inside a nitrogen glovebox** (O₂ and H₂O degrade the active layer).
- **Standard test condition:** AM 1.5G, $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm², $T = 25$ °C.
- **Encapsulation is non-optional** — without it, a PSC degrades in minutes of air exposure.
- **State-of-the-art efficiency:** $\eta > 19\%$ (single-junction, lab scale, 2024).
- **Vocabulary:** BHJ = Bulk Heterojunction; HTL = Hole Transport Layer; ETL = Electron Transport Layer; PCE = Power Conversion Efficiency; EQE = External Quantum Efficiency; R2R = Roll-to-Roll.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photoactive Wide Band Gap Donor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Photoactive Wide Band Gap Donor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photoactive Wide Band Gap Donor (WBG Donor):** A conjugated polymer (or oligomer/small molecule) that functions as the **electron-donating** (p-type) semiconductor in the **bulk heterojunction (BHJ) photoactive layer** of an organic photovoltaic (OPV) device, characterized by an **optical band gap (E_g) ≥ 1.8 eV** (typically 1.8–2.5 eV). It absorbs high-energy (short-wavelength, blue-green) photons, generates excitons, and transfers electrons to a complementary low band gap non-fullerene acceptor (NFA) under suitable energy-level offsets.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the photoactive layer is the **engine** of an OPV device where photons are converted into free charge carriers. A *wide band gap* donor absorbs **only the higher-energy portion** of the solar spectrum (λ ≈ 400–650 nm), leaving the red/NIR region to be absorbed by a low-band-gap acceptor — a strategy known as **complementary absorption** or **tandem-style spectral splitting within a single junction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Band-gap classification (commonly adopted in OPV literature & KTU syllabus):**
> - **Wide band gap (WBG):** E_g > 1.8 eV
> - **Medium band gap (MBG):** 1.5 eV ≤ E_g ≤ 1.8 eV
> - **Low/Narrow band gap (LBG):** E_g < 1.5 eV

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Netter Fishermen" 🌊

Imagine a lake (the solar spectrum) full of fish (photons) of different sizes:
- **Big fish** = high-energy (blue/UV) photons
- **Medium fish** = green photons
- **Small fish** = red/NIR photons

A **narrow band gap donor** can catch only *small fish* (red/NIR) — its net is wide (low E_g) so high-energy photons slip through.  
A **wide band gap donor** can catch *big and medium fish* (blue-green) — its net is fine (high E_g) so red fish slip through.

To catch **all sizes**, two fishermen must work together: the **WBG donor** (catching blue-green) hands off the electrons to a **LBG acceptor** (catching red-NIR). The combined "catch" (photocurrent) is huge, and because the WBG donor has a *deep HOMO*, the **voltage (V_OC)** across the cell is also high. That's the essence of WBG donor design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key constants used throughout this module:**
> - **Planck's constant:** h = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - **Speed of light:** c = **3.0 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - **Elementary charge:** e = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - **hc:** ≈ **1240 eV·nm** (most-used constant in OPV band-gap math)

**Standard benchmark metrics for any photoactive donor:**
| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (WBG OPV) |
|---|---|---|
| Power Conversion Efficiency | PCE (η) | 10 – 19 % |
| Open-Circuit Voltage | V_OC | 0.85 – 1.20 V |
| Short-Circuit Current Density | J_SC | 15 – 26 mA/cm² |
| Fill Factor | FF | 0.65 – 0.80 |
| Optical Band Gap | E_g^opt | 1.80 – 2.50 eV |
| HOMO Level | E_HOMO | −5.10 to −5.80 eV |
| LUMO Level | E_LUMO | −3.00 to −3.80 eV |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The 6-Step Working Cycle of a WBG-Donor OPV

A photon entering the bulk heterojunction containing a wide band gap donor undergoes the following sequence (each step must occur efficiently for high PCE):

1. **Photon Absorption (Step 1 — Donor-Driven):**
   The WBG donor absorbs photons with energy **hν ≥ E_g^Donor**. Because E_g is large, only the **UV–blue–green window** is harvested.

2. **Exciton Generation (Step 2):**
   A bound electron–hole pair (**Frenkel exciton**, binding energy ≈ 0.3–1.0 eV) is created on the polymer backbone.

3. **Exciton Diffusion (Step 3):**
   The exciton migrates via Förster/Förster-Dexter energy transfer over a typical **diffusion length L_D ≈ 5–20 nm**. If it does not reach a D/A interface within L_D, it recombines — *a major loss channel*.

4. **Charge Transfer at D/A Interface (Step 4 — Critical):**
   At the donor–acceptor heterojunction, the exciton is split. Electron transfers to the LUMO of the acceptor; hole remains on the donor HOMO. **Required LUMO-offset ≥ 0.3 eV** for efficient dissociation (empirical rule from KTU/OPV literature).

5. **Charge Transport (Step 5):**
   - **Holes** travel through donor domains (need **high hole mobility μ_h ≥ 10⁻⁴ cm²/V·s**).
   - **Electrons** travel through acceptor domains.

6. **Charge Collection (Step 6):**
   Carriers reach the respective electrodes (ITO/PEDOT:PSS anode, Al/Ag cathode), producing **photocurrent**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "wide" band gap specifically?**
> - **Higher V_OC** (because V_OC scales with E_HOMO^Donor − E_LUMO^Acceptor).
> - **Lower voltage loss** (ΔE_loss = E_g − eV_OC); WBG donors push this loss down toward the **Shockley–Queisser radiative limit ≈ 0.25–0.30 eV**.
> - **Complementary absorption** when paired with a low-E_g NFA — both blue-green AND red-NIR photons are harvested in a *single* junction, mimicking a tandem cell at half the complexity.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam grade**. Memorize symbol definitions and units — not just the equation shape.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbol Meaning & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon Energy – Wavelength Relation | $E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{onset}}$ | E_g in eV, λ_onset in nm |
| 2 | Quick eV↔nm Conversion | $E_g(\text{eV}) = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}(\text{nm})}$ | h·c product = 1240 eV·nm |
| 3 | Open-Circuit Voltage (empirical) | $V_{OC} = \dfrac{1}{e}\bigl(E_{HOMO}^{Donor} - E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}\bigr) - \Delta E_{loss}$ | E in eV, V_OC in V, ΔE_loss ≈ 0.25–0.6 eV |
| 4 | Energy Loss Definition | $\Delta E_{loss} = E_g - e V_{OC}$ | All in eV |
| 5 | Power Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | P_in = 100 mW/cm² (AM 1.5G) |
| 6 | Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC}} = \dfrac{J_{max} \cdot V_{max}}{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC}}$ | Dimensionless, 0 ≤ FF ≤ 1 |
| 7 | Tauc Equation (Direct Allowed) | $(\alpha h\nu)^{2} = A(h\nu - E_g)$ | α = absorption coeff. (cm⁻¹), A = const |
| 8 | Tauc Equation (Indirect Allowed) | $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2} = A(h\nu - E_g)$ | Used if polymer is amorphous |
| 9 | Exciton Diffusion Length | $L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot \tau}$ | D = diffusivity (cm²/s), τ = lifetime (s) |
| 10 | External Quantum Efficiency | $EQE(\lambda) = \eta_{abs} \cdot \eta_{diff} \cdot \eta_{diss} \cdot \eta_{coll}$ | Product of 4 sub-efficiencies |
| 11 | Urbach Energy (Disorder) | $\alpha(E) = \alpha_0 \exp\bigl[(E - E_0)/E_U\bigr]$ | E_U small = low disorder |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Table-Safety Rule:** In KTU exams (and in your notes), never write absolute values with `|` inside markdown table cells — it breaks rendering. Use `\vert x \vert` instead. Example: `\vert E_{HOMO}^{Donor} - E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor} \vert`.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wide band gap donors are **not academic curiosities** — they are the reason modern **non-fullerene OPVs crossed the 19% PCE barrier** (e.g., PM6, D18, PTQ10 blended with Y6/BTP-eC9 acceptors). Their practical utilities include:

- **Semi-transparent OPVs** for building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV): WBG donors transmit red/IR → windows that *generate power AND let visible light through*.
- **Tandem solar cells**: The front sub-cell uses a WBG donor, the back sub-cell uses an LBG donor.
- **Indoor / IoT photovoltaics**: Under LED lighting (which peaks at 450 nm and 550 nm), WBG donors are *perfectly matched*.
- **Voltage-biased photodetectors & sensors**: High V_OC enables self-powered operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photon Energy and the Optical Band Gap

**Starting principle:** A photon is absorbed only when its energy is *at least* equal to the material's band gap.

$$
E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

For the *onset* of absorption (the lowest-energy photon the donor can absorb), λ = λ_onset, so:

$$
E_g = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{onset}}
$$

**Plug in the constants** h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s and c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s. But we want the answer in **electron-volts**, not joules. Use the conversion **1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**.

$$
E_g\,[\text{J}] = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.0 \times 10^{8})}{\lambda_{onset}\,[\text{m}]}
$$

$$
E_g\,[\text{eV}] = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.0 \times 10^{8})}{\lambda_{onset}\,[\text{m}] \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}
$$

**Simplify the numerical prefactor:**

$$
E_g\,[\text{eV}] = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{\lambda_{onset}\,[\text{m}] \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = \frac{1.2408 \times 10^{-6}}{\lambda_{onset}\,[\text{m}]}
$$

Convert m → nm (multiply denominator by 10⁹):

$$
E_g\,[\text{eV}] = \frac{1240.8}{\lambda_{onset}\,[\text{nm}]} \;\approx\; \frac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}\,[\text{nm}]}
$$

**Worked Example (typical KTU numerical):** A WBG polymer donor shows an absorption onset at λ_onset = 620 nm. Find its optical band gap.

$$
E_g = \frac{1240}{620} = 2.00\ \text{eV}
$$

✅ Result: **E_g = 2.00 eV** (clearly a wide band gap, since 2.00 > 1.8 eV).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — V_OC from Energy Levels (with Loss Term)

**Starting principle:** Under open-circuit conditions, no current flows. The maximum voltage a cell can deliver equals the splitting between the donor HOMO and the acceptor LUMO, **minus** unavoidable energy losses.

$$
eV_{OC} = \bigl(E_{HOMO}^{Donor} - E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}\bigr) - \Delta E_{loss}
$$

Solve for V_OC:

$$
V_{OC} = \frac{1}{e}\bigl(E_{HOMO}^{Donor} - E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}\bigr) - \frac{\Delta E_{loss}}{e}
$$

Since ΔE_loss is already in eV, the /e is implicit:

$$
V_{OC} = \frac{E_{HOMO}^{Donor} - E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}}{e} - \Delta E_{loss}
$$

Plugging numbers (PTB7-TH donor + Y6 acceptor, textbook example):
- E_HOMO^Donor = −5.20 eV
- E_LUMO^Acceptor = −4.10 eV
- ΔE_loss = 0.55 eV (radiative + non-radiative)

$$
V_{OC} = (-5.20) - (-4.10) - 0.55 = 1.10 - 0.55 = 0.55\ \text{V}
$$

(Modern WBG donors like PM6 push V_OC up to **0.85–1.05 V** with state-of-the-art NFAs.)

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tauc Plot Extrapolation to Extract E_g

For an **amorphous polymer** (which most WBG donors are), use the **indirect Tauc relation**:

$$
(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2} = A(h\nu - E_g)
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Measure UV-Vis absorbance A(λ) of a thin film on a glass substrate.
2. Convert absorbance to absorption coefficient: $\alpha = 2.303 \, A / d$, where d = film thickness (cm).
3. Convert wavelength to photon energy: $h\nu\,[\text{eV}] = 1240 / \lambda\,[\text{nm}]$.
4. Compute y = (α · hν)^(1/2) and plot it against x = hν.
5. The plot shows a *linear region* in the absorption edge.
6. **Extrapolate** the linear fit to the x-axis (where y = 0).
7. The x-intercept = **E_g**.

**Worked numeric example (Tauc linear fit for a hypothetical WBG polymer):**

| λ (nm) | hν (eV) | α (cm⁻¹) | (α·hν)^(1/2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 500 | 2.480 | 4.0 × 10⁴ | 446.0 |
| 520 | 2.385 | 3.2 × 10⁴ | 398.0 |
| 540 | 2.296 | 2.5 × 10⁴ | 350.0 |
| 560 | 2.214 | 1.7 × 10⁴ | 286.0 |
| 580 | 2.138 | 1.0 × 10⁴ | 215.0 |
| 600 | 2.067 | 4.0 × 10³ | 128.0 |

Linear fit on the last 4 points gives slope ≈ 760, intercept ≈ 2.00 eV. Extrapolate to y = 0:

$$
h\nu = E_g \;\Rightarrow\; E_g = 2.00\ \text{eV}
$$

(Consistent with Derivation 1.)

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Full PCE from a J-V Curve

The J-V curve under illumination follows the diode equation in its simplest form:

$$
J(V) = J_{ph} - J_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{eV}{n k_B T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V}{R_{sh}}
$$

Where:
- J_ph = photogenerated current density
- J_0 = reverse saturation current
- n = ideality factor
- k_B T/e = thermal voltage ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K
- R_sh = shunt resistance

**Key points read directly from J-V curve:**
- J_SC = J(V = 0)
- V_OC = V(J = 0)
- (J_max, V_max) = the *knee point* that maximizes J·V
- FF = (J_max × V_max) / (J_SC × V_OC)
- η = (J_SC × V_OC × FF) / P_in

**Worked example:** A WBG donor cell gives:
- J_SC = 22 mA/cm²
- V_OC = 0.95 V
- J_max = 19 mA/cm² at V_max = 0.78 V

$$
FF = \frac{19 \times 0.78}{22 \times 0.95} = \frac{14.82}{20.90} = 0.709
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{22 \times 0.95 \times 0.709}{100} = \frac{14.82}{100} = 0.1482 \;\Rightarrow\; \eta \approx 14.8\%
$$

✅ This is consistent with state-of-the-art PM6:Y6-type single-junction OPVs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of a BHJ OPV with WBG Donor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sunlight AM1.5G] --> B[Glass Substrate]
    B --> C[ITO Transparent Anode]
    C --> D[PEDOT:PSS Hole Transport Layer]
    D --> E["Bulk Heterojunction Photoactive Layer (WBG Donor + LBG NFA)"]
    E --> F[PFN or PDINN Electron Transport Layer]
    F --> G[Al or Ag Cathode]
    G --> H[External Circuit Load]
    H --> C
```

**Node roles:**
- **ITO**: Indium-Tin-Oxide, transparent conducting oxide (anode, work function ≈ −4.7 eV).
- **PEDOT:PSS**: Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) — smooths ITO work function to ≈ −5.0 eV and aids hole extraction.
- **BHJ layer**: The heart of the device — interpenetrating networks of WBG donor (e.g., PM6) and LBG acceptor (e.g., BTP-eC9).
- **PFN/PDINN**: Cathode interlayer that aligns the LUMO of the acceptor to the Al work function (≈ −4.1 eV).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Level Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ANODE_SIDE ["ANODE SIDE - Hole Extraction"]
        A1[ITO Work Function -4.7 eV]
        A2[PEDOT:PSS -5.0 eV]
    end
    subgraph ACTIVE_LAYER ["BULK HETEROJUNCTION ACTIVE LAYER"]
        D1["WBG Donor HOMO -5.30 eV"]
        D2["WBG Donor LUMO -3.20 eV"]
        D3["WBG Eg opt 2.10 eV"]
        A3["LBG Acceptor LUMO -3.90 eV"]
        A4["LBG Acceptor HOMO -5.60 eV"]
    end
    subgraph CATHODE_SIDE ["CATHODE SIDE - Electron Extraction"]
        C1[PFN Interlayer -4.0 eV]
        C2[Al Cathode -4.1 eV]
    end
    D2 --> A3
    A1 --> A2 --> D1
    D1 -.Hole transport.-> A2
    A3 -.Electron transport.-> C1 --> C2
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU-board style):**
- The **vertical drop** from donor LUMO to acceptor LUMO (≈ 0.7 eV) is the **driving force** for electron transfer.
- The **vertical gap** between donor HOMO and acceptor HOMO blocks hole transfer to the acceptor (energy barrier).
- The whole stack is a **cascade of energy levels** designed so that electrons fall downhill toward Al and holes fall uphill toward ITO.

## 4.3 Working-Mechanism Flowchart (6 Functional Stages)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Stage 1: Photon Absorption by WBG Donor"] --> S2["Stage 2: Exciton Generation on Polymer Backbone"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage 3: Exciton Diffusion to D-A Interface"]
    S3 --> S4{"Within Diffusion Length L_D ?"}
    S4 -- No --> S5["Recombination Loss Heat or Photon"]
    S4 -- Yes --> S6["Stage 4: Charge Transfer at D-A Interface"]
    S6 --> S7["Stage 5: Charge Transport through Donor and Acceptor Phases"]
    S7 --> S8["Stage 6: Charge Collection at ITO and Al Electrodes"]
    S8 --> S9["Photocurrent Output to External Load"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This 6-stage model is the **standard KTU answer template** for "Explain the working of an organic solar cell" questions — practice drawing it from memory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Set B]**
**Define a "photoactive wide band gap donor" as used in organic photovoltaics. Give two examples and state the typical optical band-gap range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **photoactive wide band gap donor** is a conjugated polymer (or small molecule) that acts as the *electron-donating* semiconductor in the bulk-heterojunction (BHJ) photoactive layer of an organic solar cell, with an optical band gap **E_g^opt > 1.8 eV** (typically 1.8 – 2.5 eV). It absorbs high-energy (blue-green) photons and transfers photogenerated electrons to a complementary acceptor.

**Two examples:**
1. **P3HT** — poly(3-hexylthiophene), E_g ≈ 1.90 eV.
2. **PM6** — poly[(2,6-(4,8-bis(5-(2-ethylhexyl)thiophen-2-yl)benzo[1,2-b;4,5-b']dithiophene))-alt-(5,5-(1',3'-di-2-thienyl-5',7'-bis(2-ethylhexyl)benzo[1',2'-c:4',5'-c']dithiophene-4,8-dione)], E_g ≈ 1.85 eV.

(Marks split: 1 for definition, 1 for range, 1 for examples.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Why is a wide band gap donor preferred when aiming for high open-circuit voltage (V_OC) in a BHJ organic solar cell?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

V_OC is approximately given by:
$$
V_{OC} \approx \frac{1}{e}\bigl(E_{HOMO}^{Donor} - E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}\bigr) - \Delta E_{loss}
$$

For high V_OC:
1. The **donor must have a deep (more negative) HOMO level**, which typically requires a *wide band gap* donor with reduced intramolecular charge transfer.
2. The energy loss ΔE_loss = E_g − eV_OC is *smaller* when E_g is larger (assuming V_OC is held constant), giving less voltage deficit.
3. Wide band gap donors paired with low band gap acceptors enable **complementary absorption** without sacrificing voltage, leading to the simultaneous optimization of J_SC and V_OC. *(Marks: 1 per point.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample QP aligned to PEPOT757 Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the molecular design strategies adopted to engineer wide band gap polymer donors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labelled energy-level diagram, explain the donor–acceptor energy alignment requirements for efficient exciton dissociation in a BHJ OPV. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(a): Design Strategies for WBG Polymer Donors**

The most successful strategy for designing WBG polymer donors is the **donor–acceptor (D-A) alternating copolymer** approach, where electron-rich (D) and electron-deficient (A) units are alternated along the polymer backbone.

**Key design rules:**

1. **Weakened intramolecular charge transfer (ICT):**
   The band gap in D-A copolymers is largely determined by how strongly the HOMO of D overlaps with the LUMO of A. To *widen* the gap, choose **moderately electron-rich D units** (e.g., benzodithiophene with alkyl side chains rather than strongly donating alkoxy groups) and **weakly electron-deficient A units** (e.g., ester- or amide-substituted thiophene rather than strongly withdrawing diketopyrrolopyrrole).

2. **Increased aromatic / reduced quinoidal character:**
   A more aromatic backbone resists bond-length alternation, *raises* the band gap. Examples: using fused aromatic units like fluorene, carbazole, or indacenodithiophene with limited quinoidal stabilization.

3. **Side-chain engineering:**
   - **Alkyl side chains** (e.g., 2-ethylhexyl, n-octyl) tune solubility without disturbing π-conjugation.
   - **S, F, or Cl substitutions** on the aromatic ring deepen HOMO without narrowing E_g.

4. **Reduced backbone planarity (controlled):**
   Slight torsional twist via steric bulk can *increase* E_g by partially breaking conjugation — useful when targeting 1.9–2.1 eV.

5. **Molecular weight control:**
   Higher M_w redshifts absorption (lowers E_g); mid-range M_w (~30–80 kDa) is typical for WBG polymers to retain solubility.

**Representative examples:** PM6, D18, P3HT, PTQ10, PffBT4T-2OD.

*(Marks: 1 each for rules 1–5, plus 2 for citing 2 named examples and their E_g.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(b): Energy-Level Alignment for Exciton Dissociation**

**Required energy offsets (KTU/OPV literature rule-of-thumb):**

- **LUMO_offset = ΔE_L = E_LUMO^Donor − E_LUMO^Acceptor ≥ 0.3 eV**  
  This drives exciton dissociation at the D/A interface by providing the electron with a downhill energy gradient into the acceptor phase.

- **HOMO_offset = ΔE_H = E_HOMO^Donor − E_HOMO^Acceptor ≥ 0.1 eV**  
  This confines the hole to the donor phase (prevents hole transfer to the acceptor).

- **ΔE_CT < 0.05 eV** (small charge-transfer state offset)  
  Ensures low non-radiative recombination losses.

**Labelled energy-level diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
Energy (eV)
  -3.0 |                LUMO Donor (-3.20 eV)
       |                 | ΔE_L ≥ 0.3 eV
  -3.5 |                LUMO Acceptor (-3.90 eV)
       |
  -4.0 | PFN            |                        Al
       |                |
  -4.5 | ITO
       |
  -5.0 | PEDOT:PSS      |
       |                HOMO Donor (-5.30 eV)
  -5.5 |                 ΔE_H ≥ 0.1 eV
       |                HOMO Acceptor (-5.60 eV)
```

**Electron flow path:** Donor LUMO → Acceptor LUMO → PFN → Al cathode.  
**Hole flow path:** Donor HOMO → PEDOT:PSS → ITO anode.

*(Marks: 2 for stating 3 offset rules, 2 for the diagram, 2 for explaining electron & hole paths, 1 for conclusion on V_OC formula.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample QP aligned to PEPOT757 Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe the working mechanism of a bulk-heterojunction organic solar cell employing a wide band gap donor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare wide band gap donors with narrow band gap donors in terms of absorption profile, voltage, current, and stability. Give at least one drawback of each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(a): Working Mechanism of a BHJ OPV with WBG Donor**

*(Draw the standard 6-stage working diagram from Section 4.3 — 3 marks for diagram.)*

**Six-stage description:**

1. **Photon absorption (1 mark):**  
   Sunlight (AM 1.5G, 100 mW/cm²) enters through the ITO/glass side. The WBG donor absorbs photons with energy hν ≥ E_g^Donor (typically 1.9–2.1 eV, λ = 590–650 nm).

2. **Exciton generation (1 mark):**  
   The absorbed photon promotes a π-electron from HOMO to LUMO, creating a bound electron-hole pair (Frenkel exciton) with binding energy 0.3–1.0 eV.

3. **Exciton diffusion (0.5 mark):**  
   The exciton hops via Förster energy transfer along the polymer chain. Its diffusion length L_D = √(D·τ) is ~5–20 nm. **If the BHJ domain size exceeds L_D, the exciton decays** — driving the need for nano-scale phase separation.

4. **Charge transfer at D/A interface (1 mark):**  
   At the interface with the LBG acceptor (e.g., Y6), electron transfer occurs on a sub-picosecond timescale, driven by the LUMO_offset ≥ 0.3 eV. The hole remains on the donor.

5. **Charge transport (0.5 mark):**  
   Holes hop between donor chains (μ_h ~10⁻⁴ cm²/V·s), electrons traverse acceptor domains. Balanced μ_h/μ_e is essential for high FF.

6. **Charge collection (1 mark):**  
   Holes are collected at ITO/PEDOT:PSS, electrons at the Al/PFN cathode. Photocurrent flows through the external circuit.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(b): WBG vs LBG Donors — Comparison Table**

| Property | **Wide Band Gap Donor** | **Narrow Band Gap Donor** |
|---|---|---|
| Optical Band Gap | 1.8 – 2.5 eV | < 1.5 eV |
| Absorption Window | Blue–green (400–650 nm) | Red–NIR (650–900 nm) |
| V_OC | **High** (0.85–1.20 V) | Low (0.50–0.75 V) |
| J_SC | Moderate (limited spectral coverage) | **High** (broad coverage) |
| HOMO Depth | Deep (stable against oxidation) | Shallower (less stable) |
| Photo-stability | Generally **better** | Often degrades faster |
| Best Use-Case | Front cell of tandem; BIPV; indoor PV | Single-junction NIR harvesting |
| **Drawback 1** | Limited J_SC due to narrow absorption window | Lower V_OC from shallower HOMO |
| **Drawback 2** | Requires LBG acceptor to harvest red/NIR → more complex blend | Higher non-radiative loss; smaller ΔE_loss tolerated |

*(Marks: 5 for the table, 1 each for the two named drawbacks, 1 for a concluding engineering recommendation.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU examiners routinely dock marks on "WBG Donor" questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing optical band gap (E_g^opt, from UV-Vis absorption edge) with electrochemical band gap (E_g^ec = E_LUMO − E_HOMO from CV).** They differ by the exciton binding energy. *Always state which one your number refers to.*
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the LUMO_offset ≥ 0.3 eV rule.** Without this, exciton dissociation fails and J_SC collapses — the cell becomes a *photodiode that doesn't work*.
> 
> 3. **Stating "more absorption = more efficiency"** without distinguishing current vs. voltage trade-offs. Wide band gap donors often *lose* J_SC because they absorb less of the spectrum.
> 
> 4. **Drawing energy levels without numerical values or proper sign convention.** KTU demands: HOMO negative, LUMO less negative, arrows showing electron flow *downward* in energy.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the donor–acceptor (D-A) copolymer design principle** when asked "design strategies" — this is worth 3+ marks by itself.
> 
> 6. **Writing ΔE_L as |E_LUMO^Donor − E_LUMO^Acceptor| using pipe `|` symbols** in exam scripts — not a marks issue but breaks readability. Use clear phrasing like "the offset equals 0.30 eV".

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the WBG Donor topic:**

- ⭐ **Definition:** WBG donor = E_g^opt ≥ 1.8 eV, used as the p-type semiconductor in the BHJ photoactive layer.
- ⭐ **Primary purpose:** Achieve high V_OC via deep HOMO; enable complementary absorption with LBG acceptor.
- ⭐ **Key constant:** 1240 eV·nm (use this in **E_g = 1240 / λ_onset** calculations).
- ⭐ **V_OC formula:** $V_{OC} \approx (E_{HOMO}^{D} - E_{LUMO}^{A})/e - \Delta E_{loss}$.
- ⭐ **Energy loss:** $\Delta E_{loss} = E_g - eV_{OC}$; aim for ≤ 0.6 eV (radiative limit ≈ 0.25–0.30 eV).
- ⭐ **Exciton diffusion length:** $L_D \approx 5-20$ nm → drives BHJ morphology requirement.
- ⭐ **LUMO offset rule:** ΔE_L ≥ 0.3 eV for charge transfer; HOMO offset ≥ 0.1 eV.
- ⭐ **Tauc plot:** $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2}$ vs. hν → linear-region x-intercept = E_g (for indirect/amorphous polymer).
- ⭐ **PCE formula:** $\eta = (V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC} \cdot FF) / P_{in}$, with P_in = 100 mW/cm².
- ⭐ **Design rule:** D-A alternating copolymers with weakened ICT → wider band gap.
- ⭐ **Examples to memorize:** PM6 (E_g ≈ 1.85 eV), D18 (E_g ≈ 1.95 eV), P3HT (E_g ≈ 1.90 eV), PTQ10 (E_g ≈ 1.92 eV).
- ⭐ **Trade-off mantra:** "Wide band gap = high V_OC, low J_SC; narrow band gap = low V_OC, high J_SC."
- ⭐ **Modern record:** Single-junction OPV with WBG donor + LBG NFA (e.g., PM6:BTP-eC9) → PCE ≈ 19%.
- ⭐ **Six-stage working cycle:** Absorb → Excite → Diffuse → Dissociate → Transport → Collect.
- ⭐ **Shockley–Queisser radiative V_OC loss** = 0.25–0.30 eV — the floor no OPV can beat.
- ⭐ **Device stack memory aid:** Glass / ITO / PEDOT:PSS / Active layer / PFN / Al.
- ⭐ **Examiner's pet peeve:** Never quote band gap without specifying **optical** vs. **electrochemical**.

> 📌 **Final KTU tip:** For a 14-mark question, **always draw the energy-level diagram with numerical values** (HOMO, LUMO, work functions, offsets) — it is worth at least 3–4 marks by itself and prevents loss of partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small Band Gap Donor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Small Band Gap Donor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
