In the context of polymer and organic photovoltaics, a **Small Band Gap Donor (Low Band Gap Donor, LBG Donor)** is defined as a conjugated polymeric or oligomeric electron-donor material whose optical energy gap ($E_g^{opt}$) typically lies below **1.8 eV** (and in modern high-performance systems, between **1.2 – 1.6 eV**). 

Formally, the band gap is the energetic separation between the **Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO)** of the donor and its **Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO)**:

$$E_g = E_{LUMO} - E_{HOMO}$$

For an organic donor to be classified as a "small band gap" material in OPV (Organic Photovoltaic) literature, the following working criteria are used:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Working Definition of Small Band Gap Donor:**
> A conjugated donor (π-conjugated polymer or small molecule) whose $E_g^{opt} \leq 1.8 \text{ eV}$, achieved through intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) between electron-rich (D) and electron-deficient (A) units, enabling absorption beyond 700 nm into the near-infrared (NIR) region.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **photovoltaic absorber as a fishing net**:

- A **wide band gap polymer** (e.g., P3HT, $E_g \approx 1.9$ eV) is like a fishing net with very large holes — it can only catch **big, high-energy fish** (blue and green photons). The smaller, more abundant red and infrared fish simply slip through the net.
- A **small band gap donor** is like a **finer, redesigned net** with smaller mesh size. By engineering the polymer backbone, we shrink the "holes" (reduce the band gap) so the net can now capture **red and infrared photons** as well.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world implication:** The sun delivers ~50% of its energy in the infrared region (>700 nm). Capturing these photons is the single biggest reason researchers pursue small band gap donors — they directly address the **spectral mismatch problem** in organic solar cells.

## 1.3 Why the Band Gap Must Be "Small" — Physical Origin

In π-conjugated systems, the band gap is determined by:

1. **Bond length alternation (BLA)** in the ground state.
2. **Resonance energy** of aromatic vs. quinoid forms.
3. **Intramolecular charge transfer (ICT)** strength between donor and acceptor moieties.
4. **Planarity & π-orbital overlap** along the backbone.

By incorporating alternating **electron-rich (D)** and **electron-deficient (A)** units along the polymer chain, the HOMO is destabilized (pushed up) and the LUMO is stabilized (pushed down), shrinking $E_g$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants / Metrics (Bold):**
> - **Planck's Constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s**
> - **Speed of Light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s**
> - **Threshold wavelength for absorption: $\lambda_{onset} = \frac{1240}{E_g^{opt} (\text{eV})}$ nm**
> - **Solar photon flux peak: ~1.77 eV (700 nm)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Design Principles for Small Band Gap Donors

The construction of a small band gap donor proceeds through four well-established molecular engineering strategies:

### Strategy 1: Donor–Acceptor (D–A) Alternation
- Alternate **electron-rich (D)** units (e.g., benzodithiophene, dithienosilole, cyclopentadithiophene) with **electron-deficient (A)** units (e.g., thieno[3,4-b]thiophene, diketopyrrolopyrrole, benzothiadiazole).
- This creates a **push–pull** electronic structure that promotes intramolecular charge transfer (ICT).

### Strategy 2: Quinoid Stabilization
- Force the backbone into a **quinoid resonance form** instead of aromatic.
- Quinoid structures have smaller bond length alternation → smaller band gap.
- Example: thieno[3,4-b]thiophene (TT) backbone favors quinoid character.

### Strategy 3: Side-Chain Engineering
- Alkyl side chains do not directly shrink the band gap, but they govern:
  - **Solubility** (processability)
  - **π–π stacking distance** (charge transport)
  - **Crystallinity** (morphology of BHJ blend)

### Strategy 4: Fused-Ring / Rigid Planar Systems
- Fusing aromatic rings (e.g., indacenodithiophene, benzodithiophene) flattens the backbone, increasing conjugation length and reducing $E_g$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Strategy

| Design Lever | Effect on $E_g$ | Reason |
|--------------|----------------|--------|
| Add D–A alternation | ↓ Reduces $E_g$ | ICT lowers LUMO, raises HOMO |
| Quinoid backbone | ↓ Reduces $E_g$ | Smaller bond-length alternation |
| Fused aromatic ring | ↓ Reduces $E_g$ | Extended π-conjugation, planarity |
| Alkyl side chains | ≈ Neutral | Affect morphology, not $E_g$ |
| Stronger acceptor | ↓ Reduces $E_g$ | Deeper LUMO stabilization |

## 2.3 Energy Level Alignment in a BHJ Solar Cell

For an efficient polymer-fullerene or polymer-non-fullerene solar cell, the following **energy cascade** must be satisfied:

$$\Delta E_{LUMO} = E_{LUMO}^{Donor} - E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor} \geq 0.3 \text{ eV}$$

$$\Delta E_{HOMO} = E_{HOMO}^{Donor} - E_{HOMO}^{Acceptor} \geq 0.3 \text{ eV}$$

These offsets drive **exciton dissociation** at the D/A interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Crucial Trade-off:** Smaller $E_g$ → broader absorption → higher $J_{SC}$. BUT if $E_g$ is too small, the open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$ collapses because $V_{OC} \propto (E_{LUMO}^{Donor} - E_{HOMO}^{Acceptor})$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Typical Units | Notes |
|--------------------|-----------|---------------|-------|
| Optical band gap | $E_g^{opt} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}}$ | eV | $\lambda_{onset}$ in nm |
| Open-circuit voltage | $V_{OC} = \dfrac{1}{e}(E_{HOMO}^{D} - E_{LUMO}^{A}) - 0.3$ | V | Empirical correction |
| Short-circuit current | $J_{SC} = q \int \Phi_{AM1.5}(\lambda) \cdot EQE(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | mA/cm² | Depends on $E_g$ |
| Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{MP} \cdot J_{MP}}{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC}}$ | dimensionless | Morphology dependent |
| Power Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | % | $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm² (AM1.5G) |
| Exciton binding energy | $E_b \approx 0.3 - 1.0$ eV | eV | Must be overcome at D/A interface |
| Scharber model PCE | $\eta \approx 0.3 \cdot (E_g - 0.3) \cdot FF$ | % | Empirical, KTU-relevant |

> [!NOTE]
> For absolute values in tables above, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — vertical bars in markdown would break table syntax.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Band Gap from Absorption Onset

The optical band gap of a polymer film is experimentally obtained from the **Tauc plot** (for direct allowed transitions):

$$(\alpha h\nu)^2 = A(h\nu - E_g^{opt})$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient (cm⁻¹)
- $h\nu$ = photon energy (eV)
- $A$ = constant
- $E_g^{opt}$ = optical band gap

**Step-by-step procedure:**

**Step 1 — Measure UV-Vis-NIR absorption spectrum.**
Record absorbance $A(\lambda)$ of a thin polymer film.

**Step 2 — Convert wavelength to photon energy.**
Use the identity $h\nu (\text{eV}) = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda (\text{nm})}$.

**Step 3 — Compute $\alpha$.**
For thin films: $\alpha = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot A}{d}$, where $d$ is the film thickness.

**Step 4 — Construct the Tauc plot.**
Plot $(\alpha h\nu)^2$ on the y-axis vs. $h\nu$ on the x-axis.

**Step 5 — Extrapolate the linear region.**
The x-intercept of the linear fit gives $E_g^{opt}$.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A small band gap donor polymer shows an absorption onset at $\lambda_{onset} = 820$ nm. The acceptor (PCBM) has $E_{LUMO} = -4.0$ eV. The donor's $E_{HOMO} = -5.1$ eV. Calculate:
(a) The optical band gap of the donor.
(b) The donor LUMO (assuming $E_g = E_{LUMO} - E_{HOMO}$).
(c) The LUMO offset between donor and acceptor.
(d) The theoretical maximum $V_{OC}$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Optical band gap:**
$$E_g^{opt} = \frac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}} = \frac{1240}{820}$$
$$E_g^{opt} = 1.512 \text{ eV}$$

**(b) Donor LUMO level:**
$$E_{LUMO}^{D} = E_{HOMO}^{D} + E_g = -5.1 + 1.512$$
$$E_{LUMO}^{D} = -3.588 \text{ eV}$$

**(c) LUMO offset:**
$$\Delta E_{LUMO} = E_{LUMO}^{D} - E_{LUMO}^{A} = -3.588 - (-4.0)$$
$$\Delta E_{LUMO} = 0.412 \text{ eV}$$

Since $0.412 \text{ eV} \geq 0.3 \text{ eV}$ ✓ — the offset is sufficient for exciton dissociation.

**(d) Theoretical $V_{OC}$:**
$$V_{OC} = \frac{1}{e}(E_{HOMO}^{D} - E_{LUMO}^{A}) - 0.3$$
$$V_{OC} = (-5.1) - (-4.0) - 0.3$$
$$V_{OC} = 0.8 \text{ V}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Scharber Model for Maximum Achievable PCE

For a donor:fullerene bulk heterojunction, **Scharber et al. (2006)** proposed an empirical relation:

$$\eta_{max} = 0.3 \cdot (E_g - 0.3) \cdot FF_{assumed}$$

with $FF_{assumed} = 0.65$ and $E_g$ in eV.

**Step-by-step evaluation for our polymer with $E_g = 1.512$ eV:**

$$\eta_{max} = 0.3 \cdot (1.512 - 0.3) \cdot 0.65$$
$$\eta_{max} = 0.3 \cdot 1.212 \cdot 0.65$$
$$\eta_{max} = 0.2363$$
$$\eta_{max} \approx 8.9\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 1.5 eV small band gap donor can in principle deliver ~9% PCE in a single-junction BHJ cell. Modern non-fullerene systems exceed this benchmark.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Common Small Band Gap Donors

| Polymer | $E_g^{opt}$ (eV) | $\lambda_{onset}$ (nm) | HOMO (eV) | LUMO (eV) | $\eta_{max}$ (Scharber) |
|---------|------------------|------------------------|-----------|-----------|------------------------|
| P3HT (reference) | 1.90 | 653 | -5.0 | -3.1 | ~5.2% |
| PTB7 | 1.60 | 775 | -5.15 | -3.55 | ~8.1% |
| PCDTBT | 1.85 | 670 | -5.45 | -3.60 | ~5.5% |
| PTB7-Th (PffBT4T-2OD) | 1.58 | 785 | -5.20 | -3.62 | ~8.4% |
| DPP-TBTT | 1.40 | 886 | -5.20 | -3.80 | ~9.6% |
| PM6 (modern benchmark) | 1.80 | 689 | -5.45 | -3.65 | ~6.6% |

> [!NOTE]
> The "sweet spot" is $E_g \approx 1.3 - 1.6$ eV for single-junction OPV. Below ~1.2 eV, $V_{OC}$ loss dominates; above ~1.8 eV, $J_{SC}$ becomes limited.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: D–A Small Band Gap Donor in a BHJ Solar Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sun[Sunlight AM1.5G Spectrum] --> Absorb[Photon Absorption by Donor]
    Absorb --> Exciton[Exciton Generation on Donor Backbone]
    Exciton --> Diffuse[Exciton Diffusion to D-A Interface]
    Diffuse --> Dissociate[Charge Transfer at Donor-Acceptor Interface]
    Dissociate --> Electron[Electron transferred to Acceptor LUMO]
    Dissociate --> Hole[Hole remains on Donor HOMO]
    Electron --> ET[Electron Transport via Acceptor Phase]
    Hole --> HT[Hole Transport via Donor Phase]
    ET --> Electrode1[Top Electrode - Cathode]
    HT --> Electrode2[Bottom Electrode - Anode]
    Electrode1 --> Circuit[External Circuit - Current Flow]
    Electrode2 --> Circuit

    DonorUnit[Electron Rich Donor Unit - e.g. Benzodithiophene] --> Backbone[Conjugated Polymer Backbone]
    AcceptUnit[Electron Deficient Acceptor Unit - e.g. Thienothiophene] --> Backbone
    Backbone --> SmallEG[Effective Low Band Gap 1.2 to 1.8 eV]
    SmallEG --> Absorb

    classDef photon fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef charge fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef material fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef electrode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class Sun photon
    class Exciton,Diffuse,Dissociate,Electron,Hole charge
    class DonorUnit,AcceptUnit,Backbone,SmallEG,Absorb material
    class Electrode1,Electrode2 electrode
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Molecular Design Strategy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Target: Reduce Optical Band Gap of Conjugated Polymer] --> Step1[Step 1: Identify Electron Rich D Unit]
    Start --> Step2[Step 2: Identify Electron Deficient A Unit]
    Step1 --> Combine[Step 3: Alternate D and A along Polymer Backbone]
    Step2 --> Combine
    Combine --> ICT[Step 4: Generate Intramolecular Charge Transfer ICT]
    ICT --> HOMO[LUMO Down Shifted, HOMO Up Shifted]
    HOMO --> Shrink[Step 5: Effective Band Gap Shrinks]
    Shrink --> NIR[Step 6: Absorption Extends into NIR Region]
    NIR --> Tune[Step 7: Tune HOMO LUMO to Match Acceptor Energy Levels]
    Tune --> Device[Step 8: Fabricate Bulk Heterojunction BHJ Device]

    classDef target fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outcome fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class Start,Device target
    class Step1,Step2,Combine,ICT,HOMO,Shrink,NIR,Tune step
```

## 4.3 Energy Level Cascade Diagram (ASCII Schematic)

```
Energy (eV)
    0.0  ──────────────────────────────  Vacuum Level
         │
  -3.10  │        ┌─────────────────┐
         │        │ Acceptor LUMO   │  ← PCBM / ITIC family
  -3.58  │  ┌─────┤ Donor LUMO      │  ΔELUMO ≥ 0.3 eV (driving force)
         │  │ICT  │   (E_g reduced) │
  -5.10  │  │     ├─────────────────┤
         │  │     │ Donor HOMO      │
  -5.45  │  │     │ Acceptor HOMO   │  ΔEHOMO ≥ 0.3 eV
         │  │     └─────────────────┘
         │  │
         └──┴──── Small Band Gap Donor Stack
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, notice how the D–A alternation creates an internal "ICT ratchet" that pulls the LUMO down and pushes the HOMO up — this is the molecular origin of the small band gap.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define a "small band gap donor" in the context of polymer photovoltaics. What optical criterion is used to classify a conjugated polymer as such?**

**Model Answer:**
A small band gap donor is a π-conjugated polymer or oligomer used as the electron-donor phase in a bulk heterojunction (BHJ) organic solar cell, whose optical band gap ($E_g^{opt}$) is ≤ 1.8 eV. Such materials absorb light well beyond 700 nm, into the near-infrared region, due to intramolecular charge transfer between electron-rich (D) and electron-deficient (A) units. Classification criterion: $E_g^{opt} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}} \leq 1.8$ eV, where $\lambda_{onset}$ is the absorption onset wavelength measured from the UV-Vis-NIR spectrum. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Donor's LUMO–Acceptor's LUMO offset requirement for efficient exciton dissociation. Why is this offset necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
The LUMO offset $\Delta E_{LUMO} = E_{LUMO}^{Donor} - E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}$ must be at least **0.3 eV**. This offset is necessary because the exciton binding energy in organic semiconductors is typically 0.3–1.0 eV. Without sufficient LUMO offset, the Coulombically bound electron–hole pair cannot dissociate into free charges at the D/A interface, leading to severe geminate recombination and low $J_{SC}$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Choose Either A or B

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat energy-level diagram, explain the **Donor–Acceptor (D–A) alternation strategy** for designing small band gap conjugated polymers. Show how this strategy modifies the HOMO and LUMO of the resulting polymer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small band gap donor polymer has $\lambda_{onset} = 780$ nm and $E_{HOMO} = -5.20$ eV. The acceptor is PC$_{71}$BM with $E_{LUMO} = -4.0$ eV and $E_{HOMO} = -6.0$ eV. Calculate:
- (i) Optical band gap of the donor.
- (ii) LUMO of the donor.
- (iii) LUMO offset and HOMO offset between donor and acceptor.
- (iv) Theoretical open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$ (use the empirical 0.3 eV loss term). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The D–A alternation strategy involves polymerizing electron-rich (D) and electron-deficient (A) units in a regular alternating sequence along the π-conjugated backbone.

- **D units** (e.g., benzodithiophene, dithienosilole) have high-lying HOMO.
- **A units** (e.g., thieno[3,4-b]thiophene, diketopyrrolopyrrole) have low-lying LUMO.
- Upon hybridization, the donor's HOMO mixes with the acceptor's HOMO to form a **new, destabilized HOMO** of the copolymer (pushed upward).
- The donor's LUMO mixes with the acceptor's LUMO to form a **new, stabilized LUMO** (pulled downward).
- The net result is a **reduction in $E_g = E_{LUMO} - E_{HOMO}$**.
- An intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) excited state emerges at lower energy, extending absorption into the NIR.
- This is the **molecular foundation** of all modern small band gap donors (PTB7, PM6, etc.).

**Energy-level diagram (schematic):**

```
   ┌────────────┐     Hybridization     ┌────────────┐
   │  D-HOMO    │ ───────────────────► │   New HOMO  │ ↑ (destabilized)
   └────────────┘                       └────────────┘
                                          ↕  E_g reduced
   ┌────────────┐     Hybridization     ┌────────────┐
   │  A-LUMO    │ ───────────────────► │   New LUMO  │ ↓ (stabilized)
   └────────────┘                       └────────────┘
```

**(b) Step-by-step calculation:**

**(i) Optical band gap:**
$$E_g^{opt} = \frac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}} = \frac{1240}{780} = 1.59 \text{ eV}$$

**(ii) Donor LUMO:**
$$E_{LUMO}^{D} = E_{HOMO}^{D} + E_g = -5.20 + 1.59 = -3.61 \text{ eV}$$

**(iii) Energy offsets:**
$$\Delta E_{LUMO} = E_{LUMO}^{D} - E_{LUMO}^{A} = -3.61 - (-4.00) = 0.39 \text{ eV}$$
$$\Delta E_{HOMO} = E_{HOMO}^{A} - E_{HOMO}^{D} = -6.00 - (-5.20) = 0.80 \text{ eV}$$

Both ≥ 0.3 eV ✓ — efficient charge transfer is feasible.

**(iv) Theoretical $V_{OC}$:**
$$V_{OC} = (E_{HOMO}^{D} - E_{LUMO}^{A})/e - 0.3$$
$$V_{OC} = (-5.20) - (-4.00) - 0.3 = 0.50 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing $E_g^{opt}$ (from absorption onset) with $E_g^{electrochem}$ (from CV — HOMO–LUMO difference). KTU expects $E_g^{opt}$ unless stated otherwise.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Computing $V_{OC}$ without the empirical 0.3 eV loss term — this will cost 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Mixing sign conventions. HOMO is negative, LUMO is negative; $E_g$ is positive. Always recheck sign logic.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Drawing D–A alternation diagram without labeling the new (hybridized) HOMO and LUMO — explicit arrows showing up/down shift are mandatory for full marks.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Discuss the **role of the small band gap donor** in extending the spectral absorption of a polymer solar cell. Use the Scharber model to justify how reducing $E_g$ from 1.9 eV to 1.4 eV affects the theoretical maximum PCE. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A novel D–A copolymer has the following frontier orbital energies: $E_{HOMO} = -5.30$ eV, $E_{LUMO} = -3.80$ eV. The acceptor (ITIC) has $E_{LUMO} = -3.90$ eV. Determine:
- (i) Optical band gap and $\lambda_{onset}$.
- (ii) Is the LUMO offset sufficient for exciton dissociation?
- (iii) Maximum theoretical $V_{OC}$.
- (iv) Maximum theoretical PCE using the Scharber relation (assume $FF = 0.65$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Role of small band gap donor in spectral extension:

The AM1.5G solar spectrum delivers substantial photon flux in the 600–900 nm range. A wide band gap donor like P3HT ($E_g = 1.9$ eV) absorbs only up to ~650 nm, missing >30% of available solar photons. A small band gap donor ($E_g = 1.4$ eV) absorbs up to ~886 nm, capturing this red/NIR portion.

**Scharber model calculation:**

For $E_g = 1.9$ eV:
$$\eta_{max} = 0.3 \cdot (1.9 - 0.3) \cdot 0.65 = 0.3 \cdot 1.6 \cdot 0.65 = 0.312 \approx 5.1\%$$

For $E_g = 1.4$ eV:
$$\eta_{max} = 0.3 \cdot (1.4 - 0.3) \cdot 0.65 = 0.3 \cdot 1.1 \cdot 0.65 = 0.2145 \approx 8.5\%$$

**Justification:** Reducing $E_g$ from 1.9 eV to 1.4 eV theoretically increases maximum PCE by ~67%. The trade-off is that $V_{OC}$ decreases (smaller HOMO–LUMO gap of donor), but the increase in $J_{SC}$ from broader absorption dominates. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Step-by-step:**

**(i) Optical band gap and onset wavelength:**
$$E_g^{opt} = E_{LUMO} - E_{HOMO} = -3.80 - (-5.30) = 1.50 \text{ eV}$$
$$\lambda_{onset} = \frac{1240}{E_g^{opt}} = \frac{1240}{1.50} = 826.7 \text{ nm}$$

**(ii) LUMO offset check:**
$$\Delta E_{LUMO} = E_{LUMO}^{D} - E_{LUMO}^{A} = -3.80 - (-3.90) = 0.10 \text{ eV}$$

Since $0.10 \text{ eV} < 0.3 \text{ eV}$ ✗ — **the LUMO offset is insufficient** for efficient exciton dissociation. The device would suffer severe geminate recombination.

**(iii) Maximum $V_{OC}$:**
$$V_{OC} = (E_{HOMO}^{D} - E_{LUMO}^{A})/e - 0.3 = (-5.30) - (-3.90) - 0.3 = 0.30 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) Maximum PCE (Scharber):**
$$\eta_{max} = 0.3 \cdot (E_g - 0.3) \cdot FF = 0.3 \cdot (1.50 - 0.3) \cdot 0.65$$
$$\eta_{max} = 0.3 \cdot 1.20 \cdot 0.65 = 0.234 = 8.1\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (for Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Failing to check whether $\Delta E_{LUMO} \geq 0.3$ eV — this is a **mandatory energy-cascade check** and is worth 2 marks alone.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Applying the Scharber model to **non-fullerene acceptors (NFAs)** without noting it was originally derived for **fullerene acceptors**. State this caveat explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Reporting PCE without units (%) — KTU strictly requires **percentage** in the final answer.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to convert 1240/nm·ev constants in band-gap–wavelength conversions — losing 1 mark on trivial arithmetic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second Revision Block:**

- ✅ **Small band gap donor**: $E_g^{opt} \leq 1.8$ eV; absorbs up to ~700–900 nm.
- ✅ **Core formula**: $E_g^{opt} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}}$ (eV, with $\lambda$ in nm).
- ✅ **D–A alternation** is the master strategy — destabilizes HOMO, stabilizes LUMO.
- ✅ **LUMO offset ≥ 0.3 eV** is the gatekeeper for exciton dissociation.
- ✅ **HOMO offset ≥ 0.3 eV** ensures hole transfer to the donor phase.
- ✅ **$V_{OC} = (E_{HOMO}^{D} - E_{LUMO}^{A})/e - 0.3$** — empirical Scharber-type formula.
- ✅ **$\eta_{max} = 0.3(E_g - 0.3) \cdot FF$** — Scharber limit (originally for fullerenes).
- ✅ **Spectral sweet spot**: $E_g = 1.3 - 1.6$ eV balances $J_{SC}$ vs. $V_{OC}$.
- ✅ **Quinoid structures** and **fused aromatic rings** both lower $E_g$.
- ✅ **Alkyl side chains** don't reduce $E_g$ but govern morphology and processability.
- ✅ **Modern examples**: PTB7, PTB7-Th, PM6, DPP-based copolymers (DPP-TBTT, etc.).
- ✅ **Why care**: ~50% of solar energy is in the NIR — small band gap donors unlock this energy.
- ✅ **Trade-off**: smaller $E_g$ → higher $J_{SC}$ but lower $V_{OC}$.
- ✅ **Mandatory KTU check**: $\Delta E_{LUMO}$ and $\Delta E_{HOMO}$ must BOTH be ≥ 0.3 eV in any valid BHJ design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymer Tandem Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Polymer Tandem Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Polymer Tandem Cell** (also called a **multi-junction polymer solar cell** or **stacked organic photovoltaic cell**) is a photovoltaic device architecture in which two or more polymer-based sub-cells with **complementary absorption spectra** are optically and electrically integrated in series (or in parallel) to harvest a broader portion of the incident solar radiation. By stacking a wide-bandgap (WBG) front sub-cell on top of a narrow-bandgap (NBG) rear sub-cell, photons that pass unabsorbed through the top active layer are recovered by the bottom active layer, thereby surpassing the **Shockley–Queisser detailed-balance limit (~33.7 %)** that constrains any single-junction organic cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> Tandem (multi-junction) architecture is the principal strategy taught in PEPOT757 to overcome the trade-off between short-circuit current density ($J_{SC}$) and open-circuit voltage ($V_{OC}$) inherent to organic semiconductors whose exciton binding energies (~0.3–0.5 eV) limit single-junction efficiencies to ~11–13 %.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Two-Catcher Baseball Team":**
> Imagine a tall outfielder and a short outfielder standing one behind the other. The tall one (WBG front cell) catches the high-arcing balls (high-energy blue photons, λ < 650 nm), while the balls he misses (low-energy red photons) drop down and are caught by the short outfielder (NBG rear cell). A single fielder (single junction) would either let the reds fall past him (low $J_{SC}$) or strain to reach only the blues (low $V_{OC}$). Stacking two fielders recovers **both** current and voltage — this is the tandem principle.

## 1.3 Classification of Polymer Tandem Architectures

| Architecture | Terminals | Inter-cell Connection | Voltage / Current Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Series-connected (2T)** | 2-terminal | Recombination / tunnel junction | $V_{OC,tot} = V_{OC,1} + V_{OC,2}$ ; $J_{SC,tot} = \min(J_{SC,1}, J_{SC,2})$ |
| **Parallel-connected (3T or 4T)** | 3 or 4-terminal | Transparent intermediate electrode | $J_{SC,tot} = J_{SC,1} + J_{SC,2}$ ; $V_{OC,tot} = \min(V_{OC,1}, V_{OC,2})$ |

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (must know for KTU exams)

- **PCE (Power Conversion Efficiency)** — bold metric ≈ **15–19 %** (state-of-art polymer tandem, 2023–2024 literature).
- **$V_{OC}$** — typically **0.6 V–1.8 V** for a 2-junction tandem.
- **$J_{SC}$** — usually **8–14 mA/cm²** per sub-cell (must be matched in series tandems).
- **FF (Fill Factor)** — typically **0.65–0.78**; recombination layer losses can reduce this.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Spectrum Splitting

A polymer tandem exploits three physical principles simultaneously:

1. **Spectral complementarity** — the front cell's donor polymer absorbs the high-energy (short-λ) part of the AM 1.5G spectrum; the rear cell absorbs the low-energy (long-λ) remainder.
2. **Optical field redistribution** — the thin-film interference pattern inside the stack is engineered (active-layer thickness ~80–250 nm) so that each junction receives a maximally flat photon flux.
3. **Charge recombination at the inter-layer** — electrons from the front cell recombine with holes from the rear cell through an **Inter-Connecting Layer (ICL)** — typically composed of **PEDOT:PSS / ZnO** or **MoO$_3$ / Al / LiF** nano-laminates.

## 2.2 Why Tandems Are Needed — The Loss Mechanisms in a Single Polymer Cell

A single-junction bulk-heterojunction (BHJ) polymer cell suffers from four unavoidable losses (these are exactly what the tandem attempts to cancel):

- **Thermalisation loss** — hot carriers (E$_{photon}$ $\gg$ E$_g$) relax to band edge, wasting $\Delta E = h\nu - E_g$.
- **Transmission loss** — sub-bandgap photons ($h\nu < E_g$) pass through unabsorbed.
- **CT-state binding energy loss** — the charge-transfer (CT) state energy is typically **0.3–0.6 eV** below the donor LUMO–acceptor LUMO offset, capping $V_{OC}$.
- **Bimolecular & trap recombination** — reduces FF.

A tandem **directly attacks losses (i) and (ii)** by dividing the spectrum into two narrower energy slices, each handled by a material whose $E_g$ is closer to the photon energy, minimising the *sum* of thermalisation + transmission losses.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table before attempting any KTU numerical — every line is exam-worthy.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{PCE} = \dfrac{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Power conversion efficiency | $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm² (AM 1.5G) |
| 2 | $V_{OC}^{tandem} = V_{OC,1} + V_{OC,2}$ | Voltages add in series tandem | volts (V) |
| 3 | $J_{SC}^{tandem} = \min(J_{SC,1}, J_{SC,2})$ | Current is matched (limiting) | mA/cm² |
| 4 | $FF = \dfrac{V_m \cdot J_m}{V_{OC} \cdot J_{SC}}$ | Rectangularity of J–V curve | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $V_{OC} = \dfrac{1}{q}(E_{HOMO,D} - E_{LUMO,A}) - 0.3$ | Empirical for organics (eV offset of CT state) | V |
| 6 | $J_{SC} = q \int_{\lambda_{min}}^{\lambda_{g}} \eta_{EQE}(\lambda) \cdot N_{ph}(\lambda)\, d\lambda$ | Spectral current integral | mA/cm² |
| 7 | $\lambda_g (\text{nm}) = \dfrac{1240}{E_g (\text{eV})}$ | Absorption edge conversion | nm |
| 8 | $n_{ph}(\lambda) = \dfrac{2\pi c}{\lambda^4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1}$ | Black-body photon spectral density | photons·s⁻¹·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·m⁻¹ |
| 9 | $P_{max} = P_{in}\,\eta_{opt}\,\eta_{abs}\,\eta_{IQE}$ | Maximum extractable power | W/m² |
| 10 | $\eta_{Shockley-Queisser} \approx 33.7\%$ | Single-junction radiative limit at $E_g \approx 1.34$ eV | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In every KTU numerical, write $P_{in} = 100 \text{ mW/cm}^2$ in bold under the formula. Marks are awarded for explicitly citing the AM 1.5G input power.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Field

- **Roll-to-roll (R2R) flexible modules** — polymer tandems deposited on PET / PEN substrates enable lightweight, semi-transparent building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV).
- **Indoor IoT harvesting** — tandem bandgaps (~1.6 eV + 1.1 eV) match LED spectra used in offices, achieving >30 % PCE under 500 lux.
- **Space & aerospace** — radiation tolerance of organic bulk heterojunctions is being actively researched for low-earth-orbit applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photocurrent from the AM 1.5G Spectrum

We derive the short-circuit current density produced by a single sub-cell of bandgap $E_g$.

**Starting point:** The number of incident photons per unit area per unit wavelength from the AM 1.5G standard spectrum is $N_{ph}(\lambda)$ (ASTM G173). Each absorbed photon produces **one** exciton (ideal internal quantum efficiency assumption).

$$
J_{SC} \;=\; q \int_{0}^{\lambda_g} N_{ph}(\lambda)\,\eta_{abs}(\lambda)\,\eta_{IQE}(\lambda)\,d\lambda
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the upper integration limit.** The absorption edge wavelength $\lambda_g$ is the longest wavelength that can promote an electron across the bandgap:

$$
\lambda_g \;=\; \frac{hc}{E_g} \;=\; \frac{1240\,\text{nm}\cdot\text{eV}}{E_g\,(\text{eV})}
$$

**Step 2 — For a perfectly absorbing active layer ($\eta_{abs} = 1$) and 100 % IQE,** the integral collapses to the photon flux below $\lambda_g$:

$$
J_{SC}^{\text{ideal}} \;=\; q \int_{0}^{\lambda_g} N_{ph}(\lambda)\,d\lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Numerically integrate over the AM 1.5G standard spectrum** (ASTM G173 global tilt). For $\lambda_g = 650$ nm (corresponding to $E_g = 1.91$ eV):

$$
J_{SC}^{\text{ideal}} \;\approx\; 21.4\;\text{mA/cm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Account for realistic $\eta_{IQE} \approx 0.85$ and $\eta_{abs} \approx 0.75$:**

$$
J_{SC}^{\text{real}} \;=\; \eta_{abs}\,\eta_{IQE}\,J_{SC}^{\text{ideal}} \;=\; 0.75 \times 0.85 \times 21.4 \;\approx\; 13.6\;\text{mA/cm}^2
$$

This value (~13.6 mA/cm²) is the realistic upper bound for a single wide-bandgap polymer cell and is the **target value the front sub-cell of a tandem must reach**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Current-Matching Condition for a 2-Junction Series Tandem

In a 2-terminal series tandem the **same current must flow through both sub-cells** because the ICL is a series element.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law at the ICL.** Let $J_1$ and $J_2$ be the photocurrent densities generated by the front and rear sub-cells respectively (positive when flowing out of the device):

$$
J_{ext} \;=\; J_1 \;-\; J_{recomb} \;=\; J_2
$$

**Step 2 — An ideal recombination layer removes all charge carriers at its interface**, so $J_{recomb} = J_1$ (for the front cell) and $J_{recomb} = J_2$ (for the rear cell). Therefore:

$$
J_{ext} \;=\; J_1 \;=\; J_2
$$

**Step 3 — Realistically the two cells produce different currents.** The external current is limited by the smaller:

$$
J_{ext} \;=\; \min(J_1,\, J_2)
$$

This is the **current-matching condition**, a centrepiece of every KTU problem on tandems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tandem PCE from Sub-Cell Parameters

**Step 1 — Write PCE for each sub-cell:**

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{V_{OC,1}\,J_{SC,1}\,FF_1}{P_{in}}, \qquad \eta_2 = \frac{V_{OC,2}\,J_{SC,2}\,FF_2}{P_{in}}
$$

**Step 2 — For a 2-terminal series tandem the total voltage adds and the total current equals the matched (minimum) current:**

$$
V_{OC}^{tandem} \;=\; V_{OC,1} + V_{OC,2}, \qquad J_{SC}^{tandem} \;=\; \min(J_{SC,1}, J_{SC,2})
$$

**Step 3 — Total efficiency under matched conditions:**

$$
\eta_{tandem} \;=\; \frac{\left(V_{OC,1} + V_{OC,2}\right) \cdot \min(J_{SC,1}, J_{SC,2}) \cdot FF_{tandem}}{P_{in}}
$$

**Step 4 — The recombination-layer voltage penalty** $V_{loss,ICL}$ reduces the sum:

$$
V_{OC}^{real,tandem} \;=\; V_{OC,1} + V_{OC,2} - V_{loss,ICL}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration (KTU-style):** A front cell has $V_{OC,1} = 0.85$ V, $J_{SC,1} = 12$ mA/cm²; a rear cell has $V_{OC,2} = 0.65$ V, $J_{SC,2} = 14$ mA/cm²; $FF_{tandem} = 0.72$; $V_{loss,ICL} = 0.05$ V. Compute:

$$
V_{OC}^{real} \;=\; 0.85 + 0.65 - 0.05 \;=\; 1.45\;\text{V}
$$

$$
J_{SC}^{matched} \;=\; \min(12,\, 14) \;=\; 12\;\text{mA/cm}^2
$$

$$
\eta_{tandem} \;=\; \frac{1.45 \times 12 \times 0.72}{100} \;\times\; 1000 \;=\; 12.53\,\%
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Loss Analysis Table

| Loss Channel | Magnitude (single junction) | Mitigation in Tandem | Residual in Tandem |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermalisation | 23 % of $P_{in}$ | Split spectrum (2 junctions) | ~9 % |
| Transmission | 24 % of $P_{in}$ | Capture sub-gap photons in rear cell | ~6 % |
| CT-state voltage offset | ~0.4 V per cell | Low-offset donor/acceptor pairs | ~0.25 V per cell |
| Recombination-layer $V_{loss}$ | n/a | New loss introduced | ~0.05 V |
| Series-resistance $R_s$ | 5–10 Ω·cm² | TCO optimisation | 3–6 Ω·cm² |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Detailed-Balance Limit for an Infinite Tandem

**Step 1 — Shockley–Queisser derivation generalised to N sub-cells:** Each sub-cell of bandgap $E_{g,i}$ accepts photons with $E_g \leq h\nu \leq E_{g,i-1}$ (with $E_{g,0} = \infty$).

**Step 2 — In the limit $N \to \infty$:**

$$
\eta_{N\to\infty} \;=\; \eta_{Carnot}\left(1 - \frac{T_{cell}}{T_{sun}}\right) \;\approx\; 0.66 \times 0.95 \;\approx\; 0.68
$$

i.e., **68 %** is the upper bound. Even a 2-junction organic tandem (theoretical ~42 %) surpasses the single-junction 33.7 % limit by a wide margin.

**Step 3 — Practical state-of-art (2024) polymer tandem ≈ 19 %**, meaning there remains ~23 % absolute efficiency headroom — the rationale for active KTU-level research in this domain.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Cross-Sectional Architecture of a 2-Terminal Polymer Tandem

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    B --> C[Transparent Anode ITO or AgNW]

    D --> E[Front Active Layer Donor WBG plus PCBM or Y6 derivatives]
    E --> F[Front Cell ETL ZnO or PFN]
    F --> G[Recombination Layer ICL MoO3 plus Ag plus ZnO or PEDOT PSS plus ZnO]
    G --> H[Rear Cell HTL PEDOT PSS or MoO3]
    H --> I[Rear Active Layer Donor NBG plus IEICO 4F or PTB7 Th]
    I --> J[Rear Cell ETL PFN or ZnO]
    J --> K[Metal Cathode Al or Ag]
    K --> L[External Circuit]

    subgraph "Front Sub-Cell"
        D

    end
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Block Architecture of Tandem Photocurrent Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Photon Flux Splitting Logic] --> Q1[Front Cell Absorbs UV and Visible 350 to 650 nm]
    P1 --> Q2[Rear Cell Absorbs Red and NIR 650 to 900 nm]
    Q1 --> R1[Exciton Generation at Donor Acceptor Interface]
    Q2 --> R2[Exciton Generation at Donor Acceptor Interface]
    R1 --> S1[Charge Separation at BHJ]
    R2 --> S2[Charge Separation at BHJ]
    S1 --> T1[Carrier Transport to ICL]
    S2 --> T2[Carrier Transport to ICL]
    T1 --> U[Recombination at Inter Connecting Layer]
    T2 --> U
    U --> V[Net External Current min of Jsc1 and Jsc2]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Design ICL] --> B{Polarities Match at Interface}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use MoO3 plus Ag plus ZnO Nano Laminate]

    C --> E{Require Optical Spacer}
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F[Insert ZnO or SnO2 Optical Coupling Layer]
    E -- No --> G[Proceed to Vacuum Deposition]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Measure JV Curve and EQE]
    H --> I{Jsc front approx Jsc rear}
    I -- Yes --> J[Match Achieved]
    I -- No --> K[Adjust Active Layer Thickness and Iterate]

    style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style K fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description of Optical Field Distribution

In a polymer tandem the standing-wave optical field $|E(z)|^2$ shows **two maxima** — one inside the front active layer, one inside the rear active layer. The thickness of each active layer and of the ICL is engineered so that both maxima coincide with the corresponding donor:acceptor BHJ plane. This is the **transfer-matrix optical modelling** output that KTU examiners may ask you to sketch.

| Layer | Approximate Thickness (nm) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| ITO / AgNW | 100–150 | Transparent anode |
| PEDOT:PSS | 30–40 | Hole transport |
| Front active (e.g., PM6:Y6) | 100–130 | UV–Vis absorption |
| ZnO / PFN | 20–40 | Electron transport |
| ICL (MoO$_3$/Ag/ZnO) | 5–15 | Recombination zone |
| Rear active (e.g., PTB7-Th:IEICO-4F) | 100–150 | NIR absorption |
| PFN-Br | 5 | Electron transport |
| Al / Ag | 100 | Cathode |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define a polymer tandem solar cell. Why is it preferred over a single-junction polymer cell?

**Model Answer:**
A polymer tandem solar cell is a multi-junction organic photovoltaic device that stacks two or more polymer-based sub-cells with **complementary absorption spectra** in optical and electrical series (or parallel). It is preferred over a single-junction polymer cell because:

1. It **divides the solar spectrum** between two (or more) active layers, reducing **thermalisation loss** of high-energy photons and **transmission loss** of low-energy photons simultaneously.
2. The **open-circuit voltages of the sub-cells add**, yielding $V_{OC}^{tandem} = V_{OC,1} + V_{OC,2}$ without sacrificing $J_{SC}$, thereby exceeding the **Shockley–Queisser detailed-balance limit (~33.7 %)** that constrains any single organic junction.
3. It allows independent optimisation of bandgaps and morphologies of each sub-cell, enabling **PCE > 15 %** with solution-processable polymers — the central theme of KTU Module 4.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` What is the role of the inter-connecting layer (ICL) in a 2-terminal polymer tandem? Name any two ICL material systems.

**Model Answer:**
The inter-connecting layer (ICL) in a 2-terminal polymer tandem serves three simultaneous roles:

1. **Electrical function:** It acts as a **recombination zone** where electrons from the front sub-cell recombine with holes from the rear sub-cell, allowing series current continuity.
2. **Optical function:** It can serve as an **optical spacer**, redistributing the electromagnetic field $|E(z)|^2$ between the two active layers to maximise absorption.
3. **Chemical / energetic function:** It aligns the energy levels at the interface, minimising the **$V_{loss,ICL}$** (typically 0.03–0.10 V).

**Two ICL material systems:**
- **MoO$_3$ / Ag / ZnO** (vacuum-deposited nano-laminate).
- **PEDOT:PSS / ZnO** (solution-processed hybrid).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a labelled cross-sectional diagram, explain the architecture and working of a **2-terminal series-connected polymer tandem solar cell**. Discuss the function of the **recombination (inter-connecting) layer**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **current-matching condition** for a series tandem and show that the **maximum tandem PCE** is obtained when $J_{SC,1} = J_{SC,2}$. Compute the tandem PCE for the following sub-cell data: $V_{OC,1} = 0.84$ V, $J_{SC,1} = 13.0$ mA/cm², $V_{OC,2} = 0.66$ V, $J_{SC,2} = 14.5$ mA/cm², $FF_{tandem} = 0.71$, $V_{loss,ICL} = 0.04$ V. Take $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm². **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Architecture:** A 2-terminal series polymer tandem consists of the following layers stacked from the substrate side to the top side:

1. **Substrate** (Glass or PET) → provides mechanical support.
2. **Transparent anode** (ITO, ~150 nm) → collects holes.
3. **Front sub-cell** → consists of a **HTL (PEDOT:PSS ~30 nm)**, a **wide-bandgap BHJ active layer** (~110 nm, e.g., PM6:Y6), and an **ETL (ZnO ~30 nm)**.
4. **Recombination layer (ICL)** → MoO$_3$ (~10 nm) / Ag (~1 nm) / ZnO (~20 nm). This is the heart of the device.
5. **Rear sub-cell** → HTL (PEDOT:PSS ~30 nm), **narrow-bandgap BHJ** (~140 nm, e.g., PTB7-Th:IEICO-4F), ETL (PFN-Br ~5 nm).
6. **Cathode** (Al or Ag, ~100 nm).

**Working Principle:**

- Photons with $\lambda < \lambda_{g,front}$ are absorbed in the front cell, generating excitons that dissociate at the BHJ interface.
- Photons with $\lambda_{g,front} < \lambda < \lambda_{g,rear}$ pass through the front cell and are absorbed in the rear cell.
- Electrons from the front cell and holes from the rear cell **recombine inside the ICL**, ensuring series current continuity.
- The total $V_{OC}$ is the **sum** of the sub-cell open-circuit voltages.

**Function of the ICL (Recombination Layer):**
- Provides a **low-resistance recombination contact** between the two sub-cells.
- Prevents **chemical / physical damage** to the underlying active layer during the deposition of the next sub-cell.
- Acts as an **optical spacer** to enhance light absorption in both active layers.
- Minimises the **voltage loss** $V_{loss,ICL}$.

*(A labelled diagram earns 2 of the 7 marks — refer to SECTION 4.1.)*

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Derivation of Current-Matching Condition:**

For a 2-terminal series tandem, by Kirchhoff's current law, the external current density $J_{ext}$ must equal the current generated in both sub-cells after recombination at the ICL.

$$
J_{ext} \;=\; J_{1} \;=\; J_{2} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; J_{1} = J_{2}
$$

When the two sub-cells are imperfectly matched, the external current is limited by the smaller one:

$$
J_{ext} \;=\; \min(J_{1}, J_{2})
$$

The **photocurrent density** in each sub-cell depends on the **active-layer thickness** $d_i$, the **absorption coefficient** $\alpha_i(\lambda)$, and the **optical field distribution**. For a given spectrum $N_{ph}(\lambda)$:

$$
J_i \;=\; q \int N_{ph}(\lambda)\left[1 - e^{-\alpha_i(\lambda)\, d_i}\right]\eta_{IQE,i}\, d\lambda
$$

By adjusting $d_1$ and $d_2$, one tunes $J_1$ and $J_2$ until they are equal. This is the **current-matching condition**.

**Why maximum PCE occurs at match:** If $J_1 > J_2$, then $J_{ext} = J_2$, and the excess carriers in cell 1 recombine at the ICL — wasting optical current. Symmetrically, if $J_1 < J_2$ then cell 1 is limiting. Therefore the **PCE is maximised** only when $J_1 = J_2$.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $V_{OC,1} = 0.84$ V, $J_{SC,1} = 13.0$ mA/cm², $V_{OC,2} = 0.66$ V, $J_{SC,2} = 14.5$ mA/cm², $FF = 0.71$, $V_{loss,ICL} = 0.04$ V, $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm².

Step 1 — Matched current:

$$
J_{ext} = \min(13.0,\;14.5) = 13.0\;\text{mA/cm}^2
$$

Step 2 — Net open-circuit voltage:

$$
V_{OC}^{tandem} = 0.84 + 0.66 - 0.04 = 1.46\;\text{V}
$$

Step 3 — Tandem PCE:

$$
\eta_{tandem} = \frac{V_{OC}^{tandem}\cdot J_{SC}^{tandem}\cdot FF}{P_{in}} = \frac{1.46 \times 13.0 \times 0.71}{100} \times 1000
$$

$$
\eta_{tandem} = \frac{13.4818}{100} \times 1000 = 13.48\,\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\eta_{tandem} \approx 13.48\,\%}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the four major loss mechanisms in a single-junction polymer solar cell. Show with a mathematical argument why a 2-junction tandem can reduce these losses by a factor of ~2. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a 2-terminal polymer tandem intended for AM 1.5G illumination. Propose suitable donor polymers for the front and rear sub-cells with their bandgaps and justify the choice of an inter-connecting layer. Estimate the theoretical $V_{OC}$ if the front-cell donor is PM6 ($E_{HOMO} = -5.50$ eV) combined with acceptor Y6 ($E_{LUMO} = -4.10$ eV), and the rear cell uses PTB7-Th ($E_{HOMO} = -5.20$ eV) with IEICO-4F ($E_{LUMO} = -3.95$ eV). Take the empirical CT-offset as 0.30 V. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Four major loss mechanisms in a single-junction polymer cell:**

1. **Thermalisation loss** — carriers excited by photons with $h\nu \gg E_g$ relax to the band edge, dissipating the excess energy as heat. Energy lost per high-energy photon: $\Delta E_{therm} = h\nu - E_g$.
2. **Transmission loss** — photons with $h\nu < E_g$ pass through the active layer without being absorbed.
3. **Voltage deficit (CT-state loss)** — the charge-transfer state energy $E_{CT}$ lies 0.3–0.6 eV below the donor-LUMO / acceptor-LUMO energy gap, capping $V_{OC} \leq (E_{CT}/q) - 0.3$.
4. **Bimolecular & trap recombination** — Langevin-type bimolecular recombination of free carriers and Shockley–Read–Hall recombination via trap states reduce FF.

**Mathematical Argument for Tandem Reduction:**

Let the solar spectrum be divided into two sub-spectra $S_1$ and $S_2$ corresponding to $h\nu > E_{g,1}$ and $E_{g,2} < h\nu < E_{g,1}$. For each sub-spectrum the average thermalisation loss is:

$$
\langle \Delta E_{therm}\rangle_i \;=\; \frac{\int_{E_{g,i}}^{E_{g,i-1}} (h\nu - E_{g,i})\,N_{ph}(h\nu)\,d\nu}{\int_{E_{g,i}}^{E_{g,i-1}} N_{ph}(h\nu)\,d\nu}
$$

Because each sub-spectrum spans a narrower energy range than the full spectrum, $\langle \Delta E_{therm}\rangle_i$ is smaller. Similarly, **transmission loss** is reduced because the rear cell absorbs photons with $E_{g,2} < h\nu < E_{g,1}$. Adding the two cells' outputs, the total loss is approximately halved. Hence:

$$
P_{loss}^{tandem} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{2}\,P_{loss}^{single}
$$

This is the quantitative foundation of tandem superiority.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Material Design:**

| Sub-cell | Donor Polymer | Acceptor | Bandgap ($E_g$) | Spectral Window |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Front (WBG) | **PM6** | Y6 | ~1.40 eV | 350–880 nm |
| Rear (NBG) | **PTB7-Th** | IEICO-4F | ~1.20 eV | 350–1050 nm |

PM6:Y6 is selected for the front cell because its high $V_{OC}$ (~0.85 V) and broad visible absorption maximise photon harvesting in the high-energy part of the spectrum. PTB7-Th:IEICO-4F is selected for the rear cell because its NIR absorption (extending to ~1050 nm) recovers the sub-gap photons that pass through PM6:Y6.

**Choice of ICL — MoO$_3$ / Ag / ZnO:**

- The front cell (PM6:Y6) terminates in an **electron-transporting layer (ZnO)**. The rear cell starts with a **hole-transporting layer (PEDOT:PSS or MoO$_3$)**.
- A **recombination contact** must collect electrons from ZnO and holes from MoO$_3$ simultaneously. The **MoO$_3$/Ag/ZnO** trilayer provides this: the ultra-thin Ag (~1 nm) forms nano-islands that act as recombination centres.
- Alternative solution-processed ICL: **PEDOT:PSS / ZnO** is acceptable for R2R fabrication.

**Theoretical $V_{OC}$ Estimation:**

Using the empirical relation $V_{OC} = \dfrac{1}{q}(E_{HOMO,D} - E_{LUMO,A}) - 0.30$ V:

Front cell (PM6 / Y6):

$$
V_{OC,1} = (E_{HOMO,PM6} - E_{LUMO,Y6}) - 0.30 = (-5.50 - (-4.10)) - 0.30 = 1.40 - 0.30 = 1.10\;\text{V}
$$

Rear cell (PTB7-Th / IEICO-4F):

$$
V_{OC,2} = (E_{HOMO,PTB7-Th} - E_{LUMO,IEICO-4F}) - 0.30 = (-5.20 - (-3.95)) - 0.30 = 1.25 - 0.30 = 0.95\;\text{V}
$$

Tandem $V_{OC}$ (assuming ICL loss = 0.05 V):

$$
V_{OC}^{tandem} = 1.10 + 0.95 - 0.05 = 2.00\;\text{V}
$$

This **2.00 V** open-circuit voltage is a hallmark of an optimally designed polymer tandem and is consistent with the state-of-the-art 2024 literature reports.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{V_{OC,1} = 1.10\;\text{V},\;V_{OC,2} = 0.95\;\text{V},\;V_{OC}^{tandem} = 2.00\;\text{V}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ICL voltage penalty** ($V_{loss,ICL}$): The total tandem voltage is **NOT** simply $V_{OC,1} + V_{OC,2}$. You MUST subtract $V_{loss,ICL} \approx 0.03$–0.10 V.
> 2. **Current matching**: In a 2-terminal series tandem, $J_{ext} = \min(J_{SC,1}, J_{SC,2})$ — many students mistakenly **add** the currents. This is wrong.
> 3. **Forgetting the units**: $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm² = $0.1$ W/cm². Numerical answers in % require careful unit conversion. Always write units in every step.
> 4. **Confusing series and parallel tandems**: Series = 2 terminals, voltages add, current is matched. Parallel = 3/4 terminals, currents add, voltage is the minimum.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram**: In a 7-mark question on architecture, at least 2 marks are reserved for a **labelled cross-sectional sketch**. Do not omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Polymer tandem cell** = stack of 2 or more polymer sub-cells with complementary bandgaps.
- ✅ **Goal** = exceed Shockley–Queisser limit (~33.7 %) of single-junction devices.
- ✅ **Architecture types**: 2-terminal (series), 3-terminal (parallel-like), 4-terminal (mechanically stacked, independent).
- ✅ **Voltage addition (series)**: $V_{OC}^{tandem} = V_{OC,1} + V_{OC,2} - V_{loss,ICL}$.
- ✅ **Current matching (series)**: $J_{ext} = \min(J_{SC,1}, J_{SC,2})$.
- ✅ **Recombination (ICL) materials**: MoO$_3$/Ag/ZnO, PEDOT:PSS/ZnO, or PFN/Ag/PFN.
- ✅ **Current-matching condition** = maximum PCE condition for series tandem.
- ✅ **Front cell**: wide-bandgap (~1.6–1.9 eV) — examples: PM6:Y6, P3HT:PC$_{71}$BM, PM7.
- ✅ **Rear cell**: narrow-bandgap (~1.0–1.3 eV) — examples: PTB7-Th:IEICO-4F, PTB7-Th:PC$_{71}$BM.
- ✅ **Optical modelling**: Transfer-Matrix Method (TMM) used to design active-layer thicknesses.
- ✅ **State-of-art (2024)**: ~19 % PCE for polymer tandems; target for Module-4 viva.
- ✅ **Voc formula**: $V_{OC} = (E_{HOMO,D} - E_{LUMO,A})/q - 0.3$ V.
- ✅ **Bandgap-wavelength relation**: $\lambda_g (\text{nm}) = 1240 / E_g (\text{eV})$.
- ✅ **Loss channels reduced by tandem**: thermalisation + transmission; **new loss introduced**: ICL voltage drop.
- ✅ **AM 1.5G standard input power** $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm² = 1000 W/m².
- ✅ **KTU numerical must-show steps**: write $P_{in}$, write units, show min/max logic explicitly, state ICL loss.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small Molecule Tandem Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Small Molecule Tandem Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SMALL MOLECULE TANDEM CELLS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Small Molecule Organic Solar Cell (SM-OSC)** is an organic photovoltaic device in which the photoactive layer is composed of well-defined, low-molecular-weight organic semiconductors (typically with molecular weight < 1000 g/mol), as opposed to the long-chain polymeric semiconductors used in bulk-heterojunction (BHJ) polymer solar cells. Common examples include phthalocyanines (ZnPc, CuPc), subphthalocyanines (SubPc), oligothiophenes, squaraines, diketopyrrolopyrroles (DPP), and merocyanine dyes, blended with molecular acceptors such as C₆₀, C₇₀, or non-fullerene small molecules (e.g., ITIC, Y6 derivatives).

A **Tandem Solar Cell** is an advanced multi-junction photovoltaic architecture in which two or more sub-cells (each with distinct absorption profiles) are stacked vertically and electrically coupled through a charge-recombination or interconnecting layer (ICL). In the specific context of **Small Molecule Tandem Cells**, this architecture integrates small-molecule-based sub-cells to achieve broader solar-spectrum harvesting, reduced thermalization losses, and elevated open-circuit voltages beyond the Shockley–Queisser limit of single junctions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Recall:** A tandem cell is essentially a "two-cell sandwich" — a wide-bandgap front cell captures high-energy (blue/green) photons, while a narrow-bandgap rear cell captures transmitted low-energy (red/near-IR) photons, separated by a recombination layer that electrically couples them in series.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a single solar cell as a single fishing net that can only catch one *type* of fish (specific light wavelengths). If the net's mesh is too fine, big fish (high-energy photons) tear through; if it's too coarse, small fish (low-energy photons) slip away. **You lose energy in both cases.**

A **tandem cell** is like stacking two nets — a fine mesh on top and a coarse mesh below. The fine net catches large fish, the coarse net catches the small fish below, and **a thin layer in between (the recombination layer) acts like a conveyor belt that combines the catches.**

A **small molecule tandem cell** specifically uses precisely-engineered "discrete Lego-block molecules" rather than tangled polymer chains — meaning every molecule is identical, well-characterized, and reproducible (like uniform Lego bricks vs. spaghetti strands).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following are the **board-relevant constants and metrics** for this topic:

- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3.0 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Standard solar spectrum:** AM 1.5G, $P_{\text{in}} = \mathbf{100\ \text{mW/cm}^2}$
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = k_B T / q \approx \mathbf{25.85\ \text{mV}}$
- **Shockley–Queisser limit (single junction, 1.34 eV bandgap):** $\approx 33.7\%$ for PCE
- **Record small molecule tandem PCE (as of literature):** $> 19\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Loss in Photovoltaics:**
> - **Thermalization Loss** — Excess photon energy above bandgap is lost as heat.
> - **Transmission Loss** — Sub-bandgap photons pass through unabsorbed.
> - **Tandem cells mitigate BOTH losses simultaneously** by spectral splitting.

### 1.4 Why Small Molecules (vs. Polymers)?

| Property | Small Molecules | Polymers |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight | Discrete, < 1000 Da | Distributed, 10⁴–10⁶ Da |
| Purity | High, reproducible batch-to-batch | Batch-to-batch variation common |
| Synthesis | Multi-step, but well-defined | Step-growth, polydispersity |
| Film morphology | Easier to control via vacuum deposition | More sensitive to processing |
| Charge mobility | Often higher (ordered crystals) | Lower, dependent on packing |
| Device fabrication | Often vacuum evaporation | Predominantly solution processing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** While polymers dominate the OPV research literature, small molecule OSCs have gained renewed interest due to their **synthetic reproducibility, well-defined structures, and suitability for multi-junction (tandem) architectures** — making them a high-yield topic in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Small Molecule Tandem Cell

A series-connected (2-terminal) tandem cell consists of the following stack (from top to bottom, illuminated side first):

1. **Substrate** — typically glass or flexible PET
2. **Transparent anode** — usually ITO (Indium Tin Oxide)
3. **Front sub-cell** — wide bandgap donor:acceptor blend (e.g., SubPc:C₆₀, ~2.0 eV)
4. **Interconnecting Layer (ICL) / Recombination Layer** — typically a p–n junction (e.g., MoO₃/PEDOT:PSS or BCP/LiF/Al-based)
5. **Rear sub-cell** — narrow bandgap donor:acceptor blend (e.g., ZnPc:C₆₀ or DPP-based, ~1.5 eV)
6. **Cathode** — typically Al or Ag

> [!NOTE]
> The **Interconnecting Layer (ICL)** is the heart of the tandem cell. It must:
> - Be electrically conductive (ohmic contact to both sub-cells)
> - Permit efficient electron–hole recombination at the junction
> - Be optically transparent (avoid parasitic absorption)
> - Provide energy-level alignment

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Photon Absorption in Front Cell:** High-energy photons ($E_{ph} > E_{g,\text{front}}$) are absorbed in the wide-bandgap front cell, generating excitons.

**Step 2 — Exciton Dissociation:** Excitons diffuse to the donor:acceptor interface where they dissociate into free electrons (in acceptor LUMO) and holes (in donor HOMO).

**Step 3 — Charge Transport:** Electrons travel through the acceptor phase to the **front cell's cathode-side** (toward ICL); holes travel through the donor phase toward the **front cell's anode (ITO)**.

**Step 4 — Recombination at ICL:** Electrons from the front cell meet holes from the rear cell at the ICL and **recombine**. This is the key step that allows series electrical coupling while maintaining independent optical absorption.

**Step 5 — Rear Cell Operation:** Low-energy transmitted photons are absorbed in the rear cell. Holes from the rear cell drift to the ICL (to recombine), and electrons travel to the **rear cell's cathode (Al)**.

**Step 6 — External Circuit:** Holes from the front cell exit through ITO, pass through the load, and return to the rear cell's Al cathode, completing the circuit.

## 2.3 The Two Dominant Tandem Configurations

### (a) Series-Connected (2-Terminal) Tandem

- Sub-cells are stacked electrically in series.
- The ICL acts as a recombination zone.
- **V_oc** of the tandem = **sum** of individual V_oc values.
- **J_sc** of the tandem = **minimum** of the two sub-cell J_sc values (current-matching constraint).
- Most common configuration; single external load.

### (b) Parallel-Connected (3 or 4-Terminal) Tandem

- Sub-cells are independently connected to external contacts.
- **V_oc** = limited by the **lower** sub-cell.
- **J_sc** = **sum** of the two sub-cell J_sc values.
- No current-matching constraint, but more complex external circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Series tandem:** $V_{oc,\text{tandem}} = V_{oc,1} + V_{oc,2}$
> - **Series tandem:** $J_{sc,\text{tandem}} = \min(J_{sc,1}, J_{sc,2})$
> - **Parallel tandem:** $V_{oc,\text{tandem}} = \min(V_{oc,1}, V_{oc,2})$
> - **Parallel tandem:** $J_{sc,\text{tandem}} = J_{sc,1} + J_{sc,2}$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all board-critical equations. **NO pipe symbols are used; vertical bars use `\vert` instead.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda\ (\text{nm})}\ \text{eV}$ | Bandgap–wavelength relation | $E_g$ in eV, $\lambda$ in nm |
| 2 | $\text{PCE} = \dfrac{J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Power conversion efficiency | $P_{in} = 100\ \text{mW/cm}^2$ |
| 3 | $FF = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc}} = \dfrac{J_{\max} \cdot V_{\max}}{J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc}}$ | Fill factor | Dimensionless, $0 < FF \le 1$ |
| 4 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{1}{q}\left(\vert E_{\text{HOMO}}^{\text{donor}} - E_{\text{LUMO}}^{\text{acceptor}} \vert\right) - 0.3\ \text{eV}$ | Empirical V_oc relation | Empirical loss $\approx 0.3$ eV |
| 5 | $V_{oc,\text{tandem}}^{\text{series}} = V_{oc,1} + V_{oc,2}$ | Series tandem V_oc | Additive |
| 6 | $J_{sc,\text{tandem}}^{\text{series}} = \min(J_{sc,1},\ J_{sc,2})$ | Current-matching condition | Limited by weaker cell |
| 7 | $J_{sc,\text{tandem}}^{\text{parallel}} = J_{sc,1} + J_{sc,2}$ | Parallel tandem J_sc | Additive |
| 8 | $V_{oc,\text{tandem}}^{\text{parallel}} = \min(V_{oc,1},\ V_{oc,2})$ | Parallel tandem V_oc | Limited by weaker cell |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{tandem}} = \dfrac{\left(J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF\right)_{\text{tandem}}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Tandem efficiency | $P_{in} = 100\ \text{mW/cm}^2$ |
| 10 | $J_{\text{photo}} = q \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi_{\text{AM1.5}}(E)\ \eta_{\text{EQE}}(E)\ dE$ | Photocurrent from EQE | Spectral integration |
| 11 | $\text{EQE}(\lambda) = \eta_{\text{abs}} \cdot \eta_{\text{diff}} \cdot \eta_{\text{dis}} \cdot \eta_{\text{tr}} \cdot \eta_{\text{cc}}$ | External quantum efficiency product | Five sub-efficiencies |
| 12 | $\Delta V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{ph}}{J_0} + 1\right)$ | Diode V_oc relation | $n$ = ideality factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For series tandem, the cell delivering the **lower current** limits the entire device — this is the *current-matching* bottleneck. Always verify $J_{sc,1} \approx J_{sc,2}$ in design calculations.

## 2.5 Why Are Small Molecules Preferred for Tandems?

1. **Vacuum-Deposition Compatibility:** Many small molecules can be thermally evaporated in vacuum, enabling precise thickness control of each sub-cell and clean interfaces.
2. **Reproducibility:** Identical molecules every batch → predictable device parameters.
3. **Orthogonal Solubility:** Easier fabrication of multilayer stacks without intermixing.
4. **Tunable HOMO/LUMO:** Side-group chemistry tunes energy levels for complementary absorption.
5. **Sharp Absorption Edges:** Crystalline small molecules give sharp, well-defined absorption onsets — useful for spectral splitting in tandems.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Utility |
|---|---|
| **Building-Integrated PV (BIPV)** | Semi-transparent tandem cells can be tuned for aesthetic + power |
| **Indoor IoT** | Tandems harvest both visible and IR for low-light operation |
| **Space PV** | Tandems offer radiation resistance + high specific power |
| **Wearable Electronics** | Flexible small-molecule tandems on plastic substrates |
| **Agricultural PV** | Tuned transparency for greenhouse-compatible spectrum |

## 2.7 Critical Challenges in Small Molecule Tandems

- **ICL optical losses** — parasitic absorption in ICL reduces photocurrent.
- **Current matching** — requires precise thickness tuning.
- **Interlayer mixing** — solvent from rear cell can dissolve front cell (orthogonal solvent selection critical).
- **Voc losses** — recombination at ICL introduces additional voltage deficit.
- **Stability** — ICL degradation under illumination and oxygen.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Series Tandem Open-Circuit Voltage

We derive why the open-circuit voltage of a series tandem cell is the **sum** of the two sub-cell V_oc values.

### Step 1 — Define the ICL Boundary Condition

At the interconnecting layer, the **net current is zero** in open-circuit condition:

$$
J_{\text{front}}(V) + J_{\text{rear}}(V) = 0
$$

where $J_{\text{front}}$ is the current density flowing out of the front cell into the ICL, and $J_{\text{rear}}$ is the current density flowing out of the rear cell into the ICL.

### Step 2 — Express Each Sub-cell Current via Diode Equation

For each sub-cell, the current-voltage relation under illumination is:

$$
J_i(V_i) = J_{0,i}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q V_i}{n_i k_B T}\right) - 1\right] - J_{ph,i}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Apply Open-Circuit Condition

At the ICL, the voltage across each sub-cell is $V_1$ and $V_2$, with total tandem voltage $V_T = V_1 + V_2$:

$$
J_{0,1}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q V_1}{n_1 k_B T}\right) - 1\right] - J_{ph,1} + J_{0,2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q V_2}{n_2 k_B T}\right) - 1\right] - J_{ph,2} = 0
$$

### Step 4 — Assume Identical Ideality Factors

For ideal matching, let $n_1 = n_2 = n$:

$$
J_{0,1} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q V_1}{n k_B T}\right) + J_{0,2} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q V_2}{n k_B T}\right) = J_{ph,1} + J_{ph,2} + J_{0,1} + J_{0,2}
$$

### Step 5 — Extract the Open-Circuit Voltage

For each individual sub-cell under illumination alone, $V_{oc,i}$ satisfies $J_{0,i}\exp(qV_{oc,i}/nkT) = J_{ph,i} + J_{0,i}$, giving:

$$
V_{oc,i} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{ph,i}}{J_{0,i}} + 1\right)
$$

### Step 6 — Sum the Sub-cell Voltages

In the ideal case where both sub-cells experience equal current (current-matched), the tandem open-circuit voltage becomes:

$$
\boxed{V_{oc,\text{tandem}} = V_{oc,1} + V_{oc,2} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{ph,1}}{J_{0,1}} + 1\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{ph,2}}{J_{0,2}} + 1\right)\right]}
$$

This additive behavior is the **fundamental advantage** of series-connected tandem architectures.

## 3.2 Derivation: Numerical Tandem PCE Calculation

**Problem:** A series tandem solar cell has the following sub-cell parameters:

| Parameter | Front Cell | Rear Cell |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{oc}$ | 0.95 V | 0.65 V |
| $J_{sc}$ | 8.5 mA/cm² | 10.2 mA/cm² |
| $FF$ | 0.65 | 0.70 |

### Step 1 — Identify the Current-Matching Bottleneck

For a series tandem, the operating $J_{sc}$ equals the **smaller** of the two sub-cell currents:

$$
J_{sc,\text{tandem}} = \min(J_{sc,1},\ J_{sc,2}) = \min(8.5,\ 10.2) = 8.5\ \text{mA/cm}^2
$$

The **front cell limits the current.**

### Step 2 — Add the Open-Circuit Voltages

$$
V_{oc,\text{tandem}} = V_{oc,1} + V_{oc,2} = 0.95 + 0.65 = 1.60\ \text{V}
$$

### Step 3 — Estimate Tandem Fill Factor

The fill factor of a tandem is not strictly the average. A common engineering approximation (assuming similar ideality factors) is:

$$
FF_{\text{tandem}} \approx \dfrac{FF_1 \cdot J_{sc,1} \cdot V_{oc,1} + FF_2 \cdot J_{sc,2} \cdot V_{oc,2}}{(J_{sc,1} + J_{sc,2}) \cdot (V_{oc,1} + V_{oc,2})/2}
$$

For our case:

$$
\text{Numerator} = (0.65 \cdot 8.5 \cdot 0.95) + (0.70 \cdot 10.2 \cdot 0.65)
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 5.2488 + 4.6410 = 9.8898\ \text{mW/cm}^2
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = (8.5 + 10.2) \cdot 1.60 / 2 = 18.7 \cdot 0.8 = 14.96\ \text{mW/cm}^2
$$

$$
FF_{\text{tandem}} \approx \dfrac{9.8898}{14.96} \approx 0.661
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Tandem PCE

$$
\text{PCE}_{\text{tandem}} = \dfrac{J_{sc,\text{tandem}} \cdot V_{oc,\text{tandem}} \cdot FF_{\text{tandem}}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{PCE}_{\text{tandem}} = \dfrac{8.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.60 \cdot 0.661}{100 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{PCE}_{\text{tandem}} = \dfrac{8.987 \times 10^{-3}}{0.100} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PCE}_{\text{tandem}} \approx 8.99\%}
$$

### Step 5 — Compare with Single-Junction Equivalent

If the rear cell were used alone:

$$
\text{PCE}_{\text{single}} = \dfrac{10.2 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.65 \cdot 0.70}{0.100} \times 100\% = 4.64\%
$$

The tandem nearly **doubles** the PCE through voltage addition and complementary absorption.

## 3.3 Derivation: Recombination-Layer Quasi-Fermi Level Analysis

To understand how the ICL works, we analyze quasi-Fermi levels ($E_{F,n}$ for electrons, $E_{F,p}$ for holes).

**At the ICL under operation:**

- Electrons arriving from the front cell (cathode side) and holes arriving from the rear cell (anode side) recombine.
- The recombination rate must equal the generation rate in each sub-cell.
- The energy-level alignment of the ICL must ensure **no barrier** for either carrier.

For efficient recombination, the ICL typically has a structure of:

- **n-type electron transport layer (ETL)** on the front-cell side — accepts electrons
- **p-type hole transport layer (HTL)** on the rear-cell side — accepts holes
- A high-recombination-rate interface between ETL and HTL

Common combinations:

$$
\text{MoO}_3\ (\text{HTL}) \mid \text{PEDOT:PSS}\ (\text{HTL}) \mid \text{BCP or LiF}\ (\text{ETL}) \mid \text{Al}\ (\text{cathode})
$$

The energy-level alignment across the ICL must ensure:

$$
E_{F,n}^{\text{front}} \approx E_{F,p}^{\text{rear}}\quad \text{(at ICL, for recombination equilibrium)}
$$

This is achieved by carefully choosing HTL and ETL work functions such that the quasi-Fermi levels coincide at the ICL interface.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sub-cell Selection for Tandems

| Sub-cell Position | Required Property | Recommended Materials | Typical $E_g$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Front (Wide bandgap)** | High $V_{oc}$, absorbs blue–green | SubPc (2.0 eV), DBP (1.9 eV), TAPC | 1.8–2.0 eV |
| **Rear (Narrow bandgap)** | Low $E_g$, absorbs red–NIR | ZnPc (1.5 eV), DPP-based, squaraines | 1.3–1.6 eV |

## 3.5 Derivation: Loss Analysis — Why Two Cells Are Better Than One

### Step 1 — Single-Junction Loss Channels

For a single junction with bandgap $E_g$:
- **Thermalization loss:** Photons with $E > E_g$ lose excess energy as heat.
- **Transmission loss:** Photons with $E < E_g$ pass through unabsorbed.
- **Total entropy loss** → limits efficiency to $\approx 33\%$ (Shockley–Queisser).

### Step 2 — Tandem Loss Reduction

For a 2-cell tandem with bandgaps $E_{g,1}$ (front) and $E_{g,2}$ (rear) where $E_{g,1} > E_{g,2}$:

- Front cell absorbs $E_{g,1} \le E_{ph} \le E_{g,2}$ — captures the high-energy photons.
- Rear cell absorbs $E_{g,2} \le E_{ph} \le E_{g,1}$ — captures the remaining low-energy photons.
- **Average thermalization loss is reduced** because each sub-cell's bandgap is closer to the absorbed photon energy.

### Step 3 — Detailed-Balance Limit for 2-Cell Tandem

For an infinite-junction tandem (concentrator limit), the detailed-balance efficiency reaches $\approx 42\%$. For a practical 2-cell tandem under AM1.5G:

$$
\eta_{\text{tandem, ideal}} \approx 42\%\quad \text{(vs. 33.7\% single junction)}
$$

This explains why **tandem architectures are the most promising pathway to high-efficiency organic PV.**
