# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define a small molecule tandem solar cell. List any two advantages of using small molecules over polymers in OPV.**

**Model Answer:**
A small molecule tandem solar cell is a multi-junction organic photovoltaic device in which two or more sub-cells, each comprising low-molecular-weight organic semiconductors (MW < 1000 g/mol, e.g., SubPc, ZnPc, DPP derivatives) as the active layer, are stacked vertically and electrically coupled through an interconnecting layer (ICL). The sub-cells have complementary absorption spectra — a wide-bandgap front cell absorbs high-energy photons while a narrow-bandgap rear cell absorbs low-energy photons.

**Two advantages of small molecules over polymers:**

1. **Defined molecular structure and reproducible synthesis** — Small molecules have a single, well-defined molecular weight and structure, leading to consistent batch-to-batch device performance, unlike polymers which exhibit polydispersity.

2. **Higher charge carrier mobility** — Crystalline packing of small molecules often yields higher and more isotropic charge mobilities compared to amorphous polymer films.

3. *(Additional valid point)* Better suitability for vacuum deposition and multilayer stacking.

**(3 marks)**

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: What is an interconnecting layer (ICL) in a tandem solar cell? State its two essential functions.**

**Model Answer:**
An interconnecting layer (ICL), also called a recombination layer, is a thin functional layer placed between the two sub-cells of a series-connected tandem solar cell. It serves as the electrical and optical bridge that couples the front and rear sub-cells.

**Two essential functions:**

1. **Charge recombination** — It enables electrons from the front sub-cell to recombine with holes from the rear sub-cell, ensuring zero net current accumulation at the junction and allowing series electrical coupling.

2. **Optical transparency** — It must be optically transparent (low parasitic absorption) so that the unabsorbed photons from the front cell can reach the rear cell for additional current generation.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Tandem Cell Questions:**
> Examiners deduct marks when students: (i) forget the **current-matching constraint** in series tandems, (ii) confuse series vs. parallel tandem formulas, (iii) fail to mention the role of the ICL, and (iv) write V_oc as additive without justification. Always show the formula AND a brief physical reasoning.

---

### Question B-A (Choice A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat energy-band diagram, explain the working of a series-connected small molecule tandem solar cell. Discuss the role of the interconnecting layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A series-connected small molecule tandem solar cell has the following sub-cell parameters:
- Front cell: $V_{oc,1} = 0.90\ \text{V}$, $J_{sc,1} = 9.5\ \text{mA/cm}^2$, $FF_1 = 0.68$
- Rear cell: $V_{oc,2} = 0.70\ \text{V}$, $J_{sc,2} = 11.0\ \text{mA/cm}^2$, $FF_2 = 0.72$

Calculate the tandem $V_{oc}$, $J_{sc}$, $FF$, and PCE under AM1.5G illumination ($P_{in} = 100\ \text{mW/cm}^2$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Working of Series-Connected Small Molecule Tandem Cell:**

A series tandem cell stacks two sub-cells (front wide-bandgap, rear narrow-bandgap) with an ICL in between. The energy-band diagram shows the relative positions of HOMO and LUMO levels of donors and acceptors in each sub-cell, separated by the ICL.

**Step 1 — Front Cell Operation:** High-energy photons ($E_{ph} > E_{g,\text{front}}$) are absorbed in the wide-bandgap front cell. Excitons dissociate at the donor:acceptor interface. Electrons travel through the acceptor LUMO to the **cathode side** of the front cell, i.e., toward the ICL. Holes travel through the donor HOMO toward the **ITO anode** and exit to the external circuit.

**Step 2 — ICL Recombination:** At the ICL, electrons from the front cell and holes from the rear cell **recombine**. The ICL must have aligned energy levels such that electrons from the front sub-cell's acceptor LUMO can easily recombine with holes from the rear sub-cell's donor HOMO.

**Step 3 — Rear Cell Operation:** Low-energy photons transmitted through the front cell ($E_{g,\text{rear}} < E_{ph} < E_{g,\text{front}}$) are absorbed in the narrow-bandgap rear cell. Holes travel to the ICL and recombine; electrons travel to the rear Al cathode.

**Role of the Interconnecting Layer (ICL):**

1. Provides an energy-level-matched recombination site (e.g., MoO₃/PEDOT:PSS as HTL + BCP as ETL).
2. Must be optically transparent to permit transmission of unabsorbed photons.
3. Must form ohmic contacts with both sub-cells to minimize voltage losses.
4. Typical materials: MoO₃, PEDOT:PSS, BCP, LiF, Al, or their combinations such as MoO₃/Ag/MoO₃ or PEDOT:PSS/BPhen.

**(7 marks — band diagram sketch, working steps, ICL roles)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Front Cell | Rear Cell |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{oc}$ | 0.90 V | 0.70 V |
| $J_{sc}$ | 9.5 mA/cm² | 11.0 mA/cm² |
| $FF$ | 0.68 | 0.72 |

**Step 1 — Tandem $V_{oc}$ (Series, additive):**

$$
V_{oc,\text{tandem}} = V_{oc,1} + V_{oc,2} = 0.90 + 0.70 = 1.60\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Tandem $J_{sc}$ (Series, current-matching, limited by smaller value):**

$$
J_{sc,\text{tandem}} = \min(J_{sc,1},\ J_{sc,2}) = \min(9.5,\ 11.0) = 9.5\ \text{mA/cm}^2
$$

The **front cell limits the current** because it has lower $J_{sc}$. The rear cell produces excess carriers that are lost via recombination at the ICL.

**Step 3 — Tandem $FF$ (Weighted approximation):**

Using the engineering approximation:

$$
FF_{\text{tandem}} \approx \dfrac{FF_1 \cdot J_{sc,1} \cdot V_{oc,1} + FF_2 \cdot J_{sc,2} \cdot V_{oc,2}}{(J_{sc,1} + J_{sc,2}) \cdot (V_{oc,1} + V_{oc,2}) / 2}
$$

Numerator:

$$
(0.68 \cdot 9.5 \cdot 0.90) + (0.72 \cdot 11.0 \cdot 0.70) = 5.814 + 5.544 = 11.358\ \text{mW/cm}^2
$$

Denominator:

$$
(9.5 + 11.0) \cdot 1.60 / 2 = 20.5 \cdot 0.80 = 16.40\ \text{mW/cm}^2
$$

$$
FF_{\text{tandem}} \approx \dfrac{11.358}{16.40} \approx 0.693
$$

**Step 4 — Tandem PCE:**

$$
\text{PCE}_{\text{tandem}} = \dfrac{J_{sc,\text{tandem}} \cdot V_{oc,\text{tandem}} \cdot FF_{\text{tandem}}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{PCE}_{\text{tandem}} = \dfrac{(9.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 1.60 \cdot 0.693}{100 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{PCE}_{\text{tandem}} = \dfrac{10.534 \times 10^{-3}}{0.100} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PCE}_{\text{tandem}} \approx 10.53\%}
$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Tandem Value |
|---|---|
| $V_{oc}$ | 1.60 V |
| $J_{sc}$ | 9.5 mA/cm² |
| $FF$ | 0.693 |
| **PCE** | **≈ 10.53%** |

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B-B (Choice B — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare series-connected and parallel-connected tandem solar cells. Derive the expressions for $V_{oc}$ and $J_{sc}$ for each configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the design considerations for selecting donor materials for the front and rear sub-cells of a small molecule tandem solar cell. Give two examples of small molecule donors for each sub-cell position. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Series-Connected (2-Terminal) Tandem:**

In a series tandem, the two sub-cells are stacked and connected electrically through a recombination layer (ICL). The same current flows through both sub-cells.

**Derivation of $V_{oc,\text{tandem}}$:** At open circuit, the total voltage is the sum of the two sub-cell voltages, since they are in series:

$$
V_{oc,\text{tandem}} = V_{oc,1} + V_{oc,2}
$$

This follows from the diode equation analysis at the ICL: at $J = 0$, the voltage drop across each sub-cell is $V_{oc,i}$, and the total external voltage is additive.

**Derivation of $J_{sc,\text{tandem}}$:** In a series circuit, the same current must flow through both sub-cells. Hence:

$$
J_{sc,\text{tandem}} = \min(J_{sc,1},\ J_{sc,2})
$$

The cell with the lower photocurrent limits the entire device. This is the **current-matching condition** — a critical design constraint.

**Parallel-Connected (3 or 4-Terminal) Tandem:**

In a parallel tandem, the two sub-cells share the same voltage but contribute independent currents through separate external contacts.

**Derivation of $V_{oc,\text{tandem}}$:** The voltage across both sub-cells must be equal. Since they share the same voltage:

$$
V_{oc,\text{tandem}} = \min(V_{oc,1},\ V_{oc,2})
$$

The lower-V_oc sub-cell limits the device voltage.

**Derivation of $J_{sc,\text{tandem}}$:** The currents from each sub-cell add independently:

$$
J_{sc,\text{tandem}} = J_{sc,1} + J_{sc,2}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Series Tandem | Parallel Tandem |
|---|---|---|
| Terminals | 2 | 3 or 4 |
| $V_{oc}$ | Sum ($V_1 + V_2$) | Min ($V_1$ or $V_2$) |
| $J_{sc}$ | Min ($J_1$ or $J_2$) | Sum ($J_1 + J_2$) |
| Current matching | Required | Not required |
| External circuit | Simple (single load) | Complex (independent loads) |
| Voltage losses | Sum of sub-cell V_oc | Lower sub-cell dominates |
| Optical losses | ICL must be transparent | Middle contact must be semi-transparent |
| Practical preference | Most common | Less common |

**(7 marks — comparison, derivation, table)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Design Considerations for Donor Materials:**

**For the Front Sub-Cell (Wide Bandgap):**

1. **High bandgap** (1.8–2.0 eV) to absorb high-energy (UV/blue/green) photons.
2. **High LUMO offset** with acceptor to ensure sufficient $V_{oc}$.
3. **High hole mobility** for efficient charge transport.
4. **Photostability** under high-energy illumination.
5. **Compatible processing** — must not dissolve during rear-cell deposition.

**Two Example Donors for Front Cell:**

- **SubPc (Subphthalocyanine)** — $E_g \approx 2.0$ eV, absorbs blue-green, sharp absorption edge, often paired with C₆₀.
- **TAPC (1,1-bis[(di-4-tolylamino)phenyl]cyclohexane)** — wide bandgap (~3.0 eV) used as donor or HTL.

**For the Rear Sub-Cell (Narrow Bandgap):**

1. **Low bandgap** (1.3–1.6 eV) to absorb transmitted red/NIR photons.
2. **Complementary absorption** — minimal spectral overlap with the front cell.
3. **High external quantum efficiency (EQE)** in the 600–900 nm range.
4. **Suitable LUMO offset** with acceptor for charge generation.
5. **Solution or vacuum processability** compatible with ICL.

**Two Example Donors for Rear Cell:**

- **ZnPc (Zinc Phthalocyanine)** — $E_g \approx 1.5$ eV, absorbs red-NIR, vacuum-depositable.
- **DPP-based small molecules** (e.g., DPP(TBFu)₂) — $E_g \approx 1.4–1.5$ eV, broad red-NIR absorption, often solution-processable.

**Additional Considerations:**

- **Spectral complementarity** — front cell's absorption tail should not overlap rear cell's main absorption band.
- **Energy-level matching** — the ICL's HOMO/LUMO levels must align with both sub-cells to minimize voltage losses.
- **Morphological stability** — both sub-cells must be resistant to intermixing during multilayer processing.

**(7 marks — design considerations, examples)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 4 Rapid Revision Checklist: Small Molecule Tandem Cells**

- [x] **Small molecule OSCs** use discrete, well-defined organic semiconductors (MW < 1000) such as phthalocyanines, subphthalocyanines, DPPs, squaraines, and oligothiophenes.
- [x] **Tandem cells** stack sub-cells with complementary bandgaps to broaden spectral coverage and reduce thermalization + transmission losses.
- [x] **Series tandem V_oc** = $V_{oc,1} + V_{oc,2}$ (additive).
- [x] **Series tandem J_sc** = $\min(J_{sc,1}, J_{sc,2})$ (current-matching required).
- [x] **Parallel tandem V_oc** = $\min(V_{oc,1}, V_{oc,2})$.
- [x] **Parallel tandem J_sc** = $J_{sc,1} + J_{sc,2}$ (no current matching needed).
- [x] **ICL (Interconnecting Layer)** is the recombination zone — must be optically transparent, electrically conductive, and energy-level matched.
- [x] **Common ICL materials**: MoO₃, PEDOT:PSS, BCP, LiF, Al — used in n/p or p/n heterojunction stacks.
- [x] **Front cell** = wide $E_g$ (~2.0 eV), absorbs blue-green; example: SubPc.
- [x] **Rear cell** = narrow $E_g$ (~1.5 eV), absorbs red-NIR; example: ZnPc.
- [x] **PCE formula:** $\text{PCE} = (J_{sc} \cdot V_{oc} \cdot FF)/P_{in} \times 100\%$ with $P_{in} = 100\ \text{mW/cm}^2$.
- [x] **Shockley–Queisser limit** for single junction ≈ 33.7%; 2-cell tandem ideal ≈ 42%.
- [x] **Voltage losses in ICL** reduce practical $V_{oc}$ below the ideal sum — minimize via proper ICL engineering.
- [x] **Small molecule advantages:** synthetic reproducibility, defined structure, vacuum-depositable, often higher mobility, orthogonal solubility for multilayer stacks.
- [x] **EQE decomposition:** $\text{EQE} = \eta_{abs} \cdot \eta_{diff} \cdot \eta_{dis} \cdot \eta_{tr} \cdot \eta_{cc}$ (five sub-efficiencies).
- [x] **Bandgap–wavelength relation:** $E_g\ (\text{eV}) = 1240 / \lambda\ (\text{nm})$.
- [x] **Current matching is the #1 design constraint** in series tandems — always verify $J_{sc,1} \approx J_{sc,2}$.
- [x] **Interlayer mixing** is a key challenge — orthogonal solvent selection or vacuum deposition used.
- [x] **Applications**: BIPV, indoor IoT, space PV, wearable electronics, agrivoltaics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semi-Transparent Tandem Solar Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Semi-Transparent Tandem Solar Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Semi-Transparent Tandem Solar Cells

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> A **Semi-Transparent Tandem Solar Cell** is a multi-junction photovoltaic device in which two (or more) sub-cells with complementary absorption spectra are optically stacked in series (typically a wide-bandgap "front" sub-cell and a narrow-bandgap "back" sub-cell), connected via a recombination/intermediate layer, and engineered so that a measurable portion of the incident visible light is transmitted through the stack while unabsorbed photons are converted into electricity with high power conversion efficiency.

In the polymer photovoltaic context (which is the focus of PEPOT757), such devices are most often realized as **all-polymer or polymer:small-molecule tandem stacks** on transparent conducting electrodes (e.g., ITO/PEDOT:PSS/Active/ETL/AgNW or ITO), enabling use as **energy-generating windows**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> The module pairs the *materials chemistry* of conjugated polymers (donors/acceptors, non-fullerene acceptors) with *device architectures*. Semi-transparent tandems specifically test the student's ability to combine (a) spectrum splitting, (b) recombination-layer engineering, (c) current matching, and (d) the dual figure-of-merit (PCE × AVT) framework.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layered Greenhouse Roof"

Imagine a **two-layer tinted roof** on a greenhouse:

- The **top tinted layer** (front sub-cell, wide bandgap ~1.8 eV) absorbs only the harsh ultraviolet and blue photons that plants don't need much of — and converts them to electricity.
- The **bottom tinted layer** (back sub-cell, narrower bandgap ~1.4 eV) captures the red and near-infrared photons that did not get used by the top layer — also converting them to electricity.
- A fraction of **green-yellow light** simply passes through both layers into the greenhouse, where plants use it for photosynthesis. From outside, the window still *looks like a window* — yet every surface is generating power.

This is precisely the operating philosophy of a semi-transparent tandem: **selectively harvest photons that humans/plants do not need for vision or photosynthesis**, while transmitting the useful visible band.

> [!TIP]
> **Key intuition:** Tandem ≠ simply stacking two cells. The *complementary bandgaps*, the *intermediate recombination layer*, and the *current-matching condition* are what distinguish a tandem from a useless series-stacked mess.

### 1.2 Why Semi-Transparent? Why Tandem?

| Driver | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| **Building-Integrated PV (BIPV)** | Windows/facades need visible light transmission. |
| **Greenhouses & Agrivoltaics** | Plants use PAR (400–700 nm); transmit green, harvest UV + NIR. |
| **Vehicles, IoT, Wearables** | Transparent surfaces (curved glass) become power sources. |
| **Higher PCE** | Single-junction Shockley–Queisser limit ≈ **33.7%**; an unconstrained tandem (two-junction) ≈ **42%** (detailed-balance). |
| **Thermal management** | NIR harvesting reduces heating; less IR transmitted indoors. |

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in This Topic (Bold = Standard KTU Symbol)

- **PCE (η)**: Power Conversion Efficiency, in **%**.
- **J<sub>SC</sub>**: Short-circuit current density, in **mA·cm⁻²**.
- **V<sub>OC</sub>**: Open-circuit voltage, in **V**.
- **FF**: Fill Factor, dimensionless, **0 < FF ≤ 1**.
- **AVT**: Average Visible Transmittance (400–700 nm), dimensionless.
- **LUE**: Light Utilization Efficiency = **PCE × AVT**.
- **CRI**: Color Rendering Index of transmitted light.
- **E<sub>g</sub>**: Optical bandgap of the active layer, in **eV**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step by Step

1. **Photon Incidence (AM 1.5G spectrum, 100 mW·cm⁻²).**
   Sunlight $h\nu \geq 1.1\ \text{eV}$ strikes the transparent front electrode (ITO, ~80–85 % T).

2. **Front Sub-Cell Absorption.**
   The wide-bandgap donor:acceptor (D:A) blend (e.g., PM6:Y6, E<sub>g,front</sub> ≈ 1.8 eV) absorbs the **UV + blue** portion of the spectrum. Photons with $E > E_{g,\text{front}}$ generate excitons, which diffuse to the donor/acceptor interface, dissociate into free charges, and are collected at the electrodes.

3. **Selective Transmission.**
   Photons with energy **E < E<sub>g,front</sub>** (the green–red–NIR region) pass through the front cell — provided the front cell's absorption tail and electrode parasitics are minimized.

4. **Recombination / Intermediate Layer.**
   Electrons from the front sub-cell and holes from the back sub-cell **recombine** in the middle layer (e.g., ZnO/PEDOT:PSS, SnO<sub>x</sub>/PEDOT:PSS, or a thick metal cluster such as Ag ≈ 1 nm). This recombination is essential — without it, charges would accumulate and oppose current flow.

5. **Back Sub-Cell Absorption.**
   The narrow-bandgap blend (e.g., PTB7-Th:IEICO-4F, E<sub>g,back</sub> ≈ 1.3 eV) absorbs the **red + NIR** photons. Charges are extracted at the back transparent electrode (e.g., AgNW, ITO/Ag/ITO multilayer).

6. **Dual Output.**
   The series stack delivers one combined current (limited by the *smaller* sub-cell J<sub>SC</sub>) and the **sum** of open-circuit voltages:
   $$V_{\text{OC,tandem}} \;=\; V_{\text{OC,front}} + V_{\text{OC,back}}$$

## 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" at Each Step

| Step | "Why" | "How" |
|---|---|---|
| Wide-bandgap front cell | Lets visible light through; harvests high-energy UV/blue | Use D:A with high E<sub>g</sub> (PM6, P3HT, TPD-3F) |
| Narrow-bandgap back cell | Captures transmitted red/NIR; recovers lost current | Use NIR non-fullerene acceptors (Y6, IEICO-4F, COi8DFIC) |
| Recombination layer | Forces series connection, avoids S-kink | n-type oxide (ZnO/SnO<sub>x</sub>) + p-type polymer (PEDOT:PSS) junction |
| Transparent electrodes | Allow transmitted light to exit | ITO, AgNW, conductive polymers, dielectric-metal-dielectric (DMD) |
| Current matching | Maximizes tandem J<sub>SC</sub> | Tune active-layer thickness & blend ratio in each sub-cell |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects **every equation** a KTU 2024 answer for this topic may legitimately require. Master these — they appear in 3-mark, 7-mark, and 14-mark questions alike.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Statement | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{J_{\text{SC}}\,V_{\text{OC}}\,FF}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | P<sub>in</sub> = 100 mW·cm⁻² (AM 1.5G); η in % |
| 2 | Open-circuit voltage (empirical) | $V_{\text{OC}} = \dfrac{1}{q}\!\left(E_{\text{g}} - \Delta E_{\text{loss}}\right)$ | ΔE<sub>loss</sub> ≈ 0.5–0.6 eV for OSCs |
| 3 | Series-tandem V<sub>OC</sub> | $V_{\text{OC,tandem}} = V_{\text{OC,1}} + V_{\text{OC,2}}$ | Voltages **add** |
| 4 | Series-tandem J<sub>SC</sub> (current matching) | $J_{\text{SC,tandem}} = \min(J_{\text{SC,1}},\,J_{\text{SC,2}})$ | Currents are **equal** — limited by smaller sub-cell |
| 5 | Current-matching condition | $J_{\text{SC,1}} = J_{\text{SC,2}}$ | Adjust thicknesses / bandgaps |
| 6 | Average Visible Transmittance | $\text{AVT} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\int_{400}^{700} T(\lambda)\,S(\lambda)\,d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{400}^{700} S(\lambda)\,d\lambda}$ | Dimensionless, 0–1 |
| 7 | Light Utilization Efficiency | $\text{LUE} = \eta \times \text{AVT}$ | Best dual-metric; LUE is the **modern KTU benchmark** |
| 8 | Color Rendering Index (qualitative) | CRI ≈ 80 → "neutral" window | t<sub>color</sub>-related metrics: CIE 1931 (x, y) |
| 9 | Detailed-balance (Shockley–Queisser) | $\eta_{\text{SQ,2J}} \approx 42\%$ | Two-junction SQ limit |
| 10 | Absorption edge (Tauc) | $\alpha h\nu \propto (h\nu - E_{\text{g}})^{n}$ | n = 1/2 direct, 2 indirect |
| 11 | Recombination current density | $J_{\text{rec}} = J_{0}\!\left(e^{qV/nkT} - 1\right)$ | n = ideality factor (1–2 in OSCs) |
| 12 | Series-resistance loss in FF | $\Delta FF \approx \dfrac{J_{\text{mpp}}\,R_{\text{S}}}{V_{\text{OC}}}$ | R<sub>S</sub> from J–V slope near V<sub>OC</sub> |
| 13 | External Quantum Efficiency | $\text{EQE}(\lambda) = \dfrac{hc}{q\lambda}\,\text{SR}(\lambda)$ | SR = spectral response in A·W⁻¹ |
| 14 | Beer–Lambert transmission | $T(\lambda) = e^{-\alpha(\lambda)\,d}$ | d = active-layer thickness |

> [!WARNING]
> **Ktu Pitfall:** Students frequently write $J_{\text{SC,tandem}} = J_{\text{SC,1}} + J_{\text{SC,2}}$. **This is wrong** for a series (two-terminal) tandem. The two sub-cells carry the **same** current; it is the *voltages* that add.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Real-World Context

| Application | Required LUE | Typical η (state-of-art, 2024–25) | Typical AVT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power-generating window (offices) | ≥ 3 % | 12–17 % | 25–40 % |
| Agrivoltaic greenhouse | ≥ 2.5 % | 10–14 % | 20–35 % |
| Vehicle windshield | ≥ 2 % | 8–12 % | 30–70 % |
| IoT/self-powered sensor | ≥ 1 % | 6–10 % | 30–50 % |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Series-Connected Tandem V<sub>OC</sub> and J<sub>SC</sub>

We model each sub-cell as an **illuminated diode**. Let sub-cell *i* obey:
$$J_i(V) \;=\; J_{\text{ph},i} \;-\; J_{0,i}\!\left(e^{qV_i / n_i k_B T} - 1\right) \;-\; \frac{V_i}{R_{\text{sh},i}}$$

In a **two-terminal series stack**, the same current flows through both cells:
$$J_{\text{ext}} \;=\; J_1(V_1) \;=\; J_2(V_2)$$
and the total terminal voltage is:
$$V_{\text{total}} \;=\; V_1 + V_2$$

**Step 1.** At open-circuit, $J_{\text{ext}} = 0$, so:
$$0 = J_{\text{ph},1} - J_{0,1}\!\left(e^{qV_{\text{OC},1}/n_1 k_B T} - 1\right)$$

Neglecting the $-1$ term (since $J_{\text{ph}} \gg J_0$):
$$V_{\text{OC},1} \;=\; \dfrac{n_1 k_B T}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{\text{ph},1}}{J_{0,1}}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Same logic for sub-cell 2:
$$V_{\text{OC},2} \;=\; \dfrac{n_2 k_B T}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{\text{ph},2}}{J_{0,2}}\right)$$

**Step 3.** Because the cells are in series and *no external current flows* at $V_{\text{OC,tandem}}$, the total voltage adds:
$$V_{\text{OC,tandem}} \;=\; V_{\text{OC},1} + V_{\text{OC},2}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{\text{OC,tandem}} \;=\; \dfrac{n_1 k_B T}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{\text{ph},1}}{J_{0,1}}\right) + \dfrac{n_2 k_B T}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{J_{\text{ph},2}}{J_{0,2}}\right)\,}$$

**Step 4.** For the short-circuit current, again enforcing $J_1(V_1) = J_2(V_2) = J_{\text{SC,tandem}}$. The current is set by the *weakest* sub-cell (the one whose photo-generated current cannot be sustained by the other). At short-circuit, the limiting cell's current ≈ its own $J_{\text{ph}}$, while the over-generating sub-cell has to forward-bias the limiting cell. The result (in the ideal matched case):
$$\boxed{\,J_{\text{SC,tandem}} \;=\; \min\!\left(J_{\text{SC},1},\; J_{\text{SC},2}\right)\,}$$

In the **current-matched** design condition, $J_{\text{SC},1} = J_{\text{SC},2}$, and the tandem operates at the maximum possible current.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Light Utilization Efficiency (LUE)

**Step 1.** Start from definitions:
$$\eta \equiv \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}, \qquad \text{AVT} \equiv \dfrac{\int_{400}^{700} T(\lambda)\,S(\lambda)\,d\lambda}{\int_{400}^{700} S(\lambda)\,d\lambda}$$

where $S(\lambda)$ is the AM 1.5G photon-weighted spectral irradiance (W·m⁻²·nm⁻¹) and $T(\lambda)$ is the wavelength-dependent transmission of the stack.

**Step 2.** Define LUE as the *simultaneous* achievement of power generation and light delivery:
$$\text{LUE} \;=\; \eta \times \text{AVT}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute typical numbers: a high-performance semi-transparent tandem with $\eta = 14\%$ and AVT = 30 %:
$$\text{LUE} = 0.14 \times 0.30 = 0.042 = 4.2\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{LUE} = \eta \times \text{AVT}\,}$$

This single scalar encodes *both* the photovoltaic performance and the optical transparency — it is the figure-of-merit most often requested in KTU answers.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Current-Matching by Active-Layer Thickness

**Step 1.** For a single sub-cell, the short-circuit current density from EQE integration is:
$$J_{\text{SC}} \;=\; q \int_{\lambda_1}^{\lambda_g} \text{EQE}(\lambda)\,\Phi_{\text{ph}}(\lambda)\,d\lambda$$

where $\Phi_{\text{ph}}(\lambda)$ is the incident photon flux (photons·m⁻²·s⁻¹·nm⁻¹) and $\lambda_g = hc/E_g$.

**Step 2.** In an OSC, the internal quantum efficiency (IQE) is nearly unity above $E_g$ once the layer is thick enough — so EQE is dominated by absorption:
$$\text{EQE}(\lambda) \;\approx\; 1 - e^{-\alpha(\lambda)\,d} - R(\lambda)$$

**Step 3.** Setting $J_{\text{SC},1}(d_1) = J_{\text{SC},2}(d_2)$ and solving numerically (or graphically, on a $J$ vs $d$ plot) gives the **matched thicknesses** $d_1^{\star}$ and $d_2^{\star}$. For a typical PM6:Y6/PTB7-Th:IEICO-4F tandem:
$$d_1^{\star} \approx 80\text{–}120\ \text{nm}, \qquad d_2^{\star} \approx 100\text{–}160\ \text{nm}$$

**Step 4.** The tandem J–V relation (in the matched, ideal case) is:
$$\eta_{\text{tandem}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\left(J_{\text{SC}}^{\star}\right)\left(V_{\text{OC},1}+V_{\text{OC},2}\right)\text{FF}_{\text{tandem}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Device Architecture & Charge Flow

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid block-diagram conveys the same information as a cross-sectional device schematic would, while satisfying the KTU visual-clarity expectation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    light[Sunlight AM1.5G] --> glass[Glass Substrate]
    glass --> ito[ITO Front Electrode]
    ito --> ebl1[Electron Blocking Layer - PEDOT:PSS]
    ebl1 --> front[Front Sub-cell Wide Bandgap PM6-Y6 Eg 1.8 eV]
    front --> rl[Recombination Layer ZnO - PEDOT:PSS]
    rl --> back[Back Sub-cell Narrow Bandgap PTB7-Th-IEICO-4F Eg 1.3 eV]
    back --> ebl2[Hole Blocking Layer - PDINN or PFN-Br]
    ebl2 --> agnw[AgNW or ITO-Ag-ITO Top Electrode]
    agnw --> transmitted[Transmitted Visible Light to Room or Plants]

    e1[Electron]:::charge --> front
    h1[Hole]:::charge --> front
    e2[Electron]:::charge --> back
    h2[Hole]:::charge --> back

    rl_note[Recombination - electrons from front meet holes from back]:::note -.- rl

    classDef charge fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#a07b00,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Spectrum Splitting Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sun[AM1.5G Spectrum]:::src --> uv[UV and Blue 350 to 500 nm]
    sun --> green[Green to Yellow 500 to 600 nm]
    sun --> red[Red and NIR 600 to 900 nm]

    uv --> front_abs[Absorbed by Front Wide Eg Sub-cell]:::absorb
    green --> trans[Transmitted Through Stack]:::transmit
    red --> back_abs[Absorbed by Back Narrow Eg Sub-cell]:::absorb

    front_abs --> elec1[Electricity - High V]
    back_abs --> elec2[Electricity - Lower V]
    trans --> usage[Visible Light for Humans or Plants]

    classDef src fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef absorb fill:#fab1a0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef transmit fill:#81ecec,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Photon Management Layer - Anti-reflection Coating] --> B[Transparent Electrode 1]
    B --> C[Front Sub-cell - Wide Eg Active Layer]
    C --> D[Recombination Junction]
    D --> E[Back Sub-cell - Narrow Eg Active Layer]
    E --> F[Transparent Electrode 2]
    F --> G[Output to Load or Grid]

    C -->|exciton generation| H[Charge Generation and Transport]
    E -->|exciton generation| H
    H --> I[Recombination at Junction]

    C -.->|parasitic absorption| J[Loss Channel]
    F -.->|reflected NIR| J

    classDef loss fill:#ff7675,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class J loss
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Architecture (Text-Schematic Equivalent)

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark answers, sketch this as a labelled cross-section in the answer sheet:

```
   Sunlight  ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ Glass (1.1 mm)                          │
   ├────────────────────────────────────────┤
   │ ITO (~150 nm)                           │  ◄── Front transparent electrode
   ├────────────────────────────────────────┤
   │ PEDOT:PSS (~30 nm)                      │  ◄── Hole transport layer
   ├────────────────────────────────────────┤
   │ PM6:Y6 (~100 nm)   E_g ≈ 1.8 eV         │  ◄── Front sub-cell (wide)
   ├────────────────────────────────────────┤
   │ ZnO (~20 nm) / PEDOT:PSS (~15 nm)       │  ◄── Recombination layer
   ├────────────────────────────────────────┤
   │ PTB7-Th:IEICO-4F (~130 nm)  E_g ≈ 1.3 eV│  ◄── Back sub-cell (narrow)
   ├────────────────────────────────────────┤
   │ PDINN (~10 nm)                          │  ◄── Electron transport layer
   ├────────────────────────────────────────┤
   │ AgNW (~100 nm)  OR  ITO/Ag/ITO (DMD)    │  ◄── Back transparent electrode
   ├────────────────────────────────────────┤
   │ Encapsulation                           │
   └────────────────────────────────────────┘
            ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓  (transmitted visible light)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
> **Define a semi-transparent tandem solar cell. Why is the Light Utilization Efficiency (LUE) a preferred figure of merit over PCE alone for such devices?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A semi-transparent tandem solar cell is a series-connected, two-terminal multi-junction device composed of a wide-bandgap front sub-cell and a narrow-bandgap back sub-cell separated by a recombination layer, such that a portion of visible light (typically quantified by AVT) is transmitted through the device while the absorbed photons are converted to electricity with high PCE.

**LUE is preferred** because a semi-transparent cell serves a **dual purpose** — power generation *and* light transmission. A device with $\eta = 15\%$ but AVT = 5 % is useless as a window, whereas a device with $\eta = 10\%$ and AVT = 35 % (LUE = 3.5 %) is excellent. LUE = $\eta \times$ AVT captures **both** objectives in one scalar, allowing fair comparison among BIPV-grade devices. **(1 mark definition, 2 marks justification.)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> **Explain the role of the recombination layer in a polymer tandem solar cell. Name two commonly used material combinations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The recombination (or intermediate) layer is sandwiched between the two sub-cells. Its role is to **enable ohmic-like series connection** by providing a site where electrons extracted from the front sub-cell recombine with holes extracted from the back sub-cell. Without it, charges accumulate at the interface, building a counter-field that opposes current flow and produces an **S-shaped kink** in the J–V curve, degrading FF and PCE.

Two common combinations: **(i)** ZnO nanoparticles / PEDOT:PSS (n-type oxide + p-type conducting polymer) — the most widely used in all-polymer and polymer:NFA tandems, and **(ii)** SnO<sub>x</sub> / PEDOT:PSS — a thermally robust alternative allowing thicker processing windows. **(2 marks for role + 1 mark for materials.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** With the help of a labelled cross-sectional diagram, describe the architecture of a semi-transparent polymer tandem solar cell. Identify the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the open-circuit voltage relation for a two-terminal series-connected tandem and discuss the **current-matching condition**, including how it is experimentally realized. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks)**

Cross-section (top to bottom) and functions:

| Layer | Material Example | Thickness | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Substrate | Glass / flexible PET | 1.1 mm / 100 µm | Mechanical support |
| Front transparent electrode | ITO (sputtered) | ~150 nm | Hole collection + light entry |
| Hole transport layer | PEDOT:PSS | ~30 nm | Hole selectivity, energy alignment |
| **Front sub-cell** | PM6:Y6 | ~100 nm | Absorbs UV + blue; $E_g \approx 1.8$ eV |
| **Recombination layer** | ZnO NPs / PEDOT:PSS | ~20 nm / ~15 nm | Series connection via e–h recombination |
| **Back sub-cell** | PTB7-Th:IEICO-4F | ~130 nm | Absorbs red + NIR; $E_g \approx 1.3$ eV |
| Electron transport layer | PDINN / PFN-Br | ~10 nm | Electron selectivity, blocks holes |
| Back transparent electrode | AgNW or ITO/Ag/ITO (DMD) | ~100 nm | Electron collection + light exit |

Additional functional requirements: anti-reflection coating (optional), encapsulation (UV-curable epoxy), edge sealing. Marks allocated: ~1 for diagram, ~3 for layer-wise description, ~1 for choosing transparency-aware materials, ~2 for completeness (energy-level alignment discussion).

**Part (b) — V<sub>OC</sub> derivation and current matching (7 marks)**

Following **Derivation 1** in Section 3.1:

For each sub-cell:
$$V_{\text{OC},i} = \frac{n_i k_B T}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{J_{\text{ph},i}}{J_{0,i}}\right)$$

For a series two-terminal tandem:
$$\boxed{V_{\text{OC,tandem}} = V_{\text{OC},1} + V_{\text{OC},2}}$$

(3 marks for derivation steps including logarithmic expressions.)

**Current-matching condition:** Because the same current flows through both sub-cells, $J_{\text{SC,tandem}} = \min(J_{\text{SC},1}, J_{\text{SC},2})$. To maximise output:
$$J_{\text{SC},1}(d_1) = J_{\text{SC},2}(d_2)$$

Experimental realisation (4 marks):

1. **Adjust active-layer thickness** — thicker front cell absorbs more blue but reduces transmission; an optimum exists.
2. **Tune donor:acceptor ratio** — affects morphology, crystallinity, and thus $\alpha(\lambda)$.
3. **Use complementary bandgaps** — choose front $E_g$ such that its absorption spectrum splits with the back cell near the green region (~550 nm).
4. **Optical spacers** — a thin high-refractive-index layer (e.g., ZnO) can redistribute the optical field inside the stack.
5. **Iterative characterisation** — measure EQE of each sub-cell under selective bias-light, plot $J_{\text{SC}}$ vs $d$, find the matched thickness.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Define **AVT** and **LUE** mathematically. Discuss why simultaneous optimisation of both is a *trade-off*, and explain how a tandem design relaxes this trade-off compared with a single-junction semi-transparent cell. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare four transparent-electrode technologies (ITO, AgNW, conducting polymer PEDOT:PSS, DMD ITO/Ag/ITO) for use in semi-transparent tandems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — AVT, LUE and the tandem trade-off (7 marks)**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
$$\text{AVT} = \frac{\int_{400}^{700} T(\lambda)\,S(\lambda)\,d\lambda}{\int_{400}^{700} S(\lambda)\,d\lambda}, \qquad \text{LUE} = \eta \times \text{AVT}$$

**Trade-off in a single-junction semi-transparent cell (3 marks):**
A single-junction cell needs to absorb light to generate current, but to be transparent it must transmit light — these are opposite demands. The simplest way to raise AVT is to **thin the active layer**, but $J_{\text{SC}} \propto (1 - e^{-\alpha d})$, so thinning reduces $J_{\text{SC}}$, $V_{\text{OC}}$ (slightly, logarithmically), and FF (via increased $R_S$ relative to photocurrent). The resulting $\eta$–AVT curve peaks around $\eta \approx 8\text{–}10\,\%$ at AVT ≈ 25 %; pushing AVT higher crushes PCE.

**How tandem relaxes the trade-off (2 marks):**
In a tandem, the *front* sub-cell is made deliberately **thin and wide-bandgap**, so it absorbs only a fraction of the spectrum (UV/blue) — preserving high AVT for the green-yellow band. The *back* sub-cell harvests the remaining red/NIR without competing for visible photons. Hence a tandem can deliver higher $\eta$ at the same AVT, or higher AVT at the same $\eta$, than a single-junction. State-of-art LUE values for tandems (≈ 4–6 %) are roughly **2× those of single-junction semi-transparent cells**.

**Part (b) — Transparent Electrode Comparison (7 marks)**

| Property | ITO (sputtered) | AgNW | PEDOT:PSS (PH1000) | DMD (ITO/Ag/ITO) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sheet resistance | 10–20 Ω/sq | 10–30 Ω/sq | 50–200 Ω/sq | 5–10 Ω/sq |
| Transmittance (550 nm) | 85 % | 90 % | 85 % | 80–88 % |
| Flexibility (Bend radius) | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate |
| Solution processable | No (sputter) | Yes | Yes | Partial |
| Cost | Moderate–High | Low | Low | Moderate |
| Stability | Excellent | Moderate (oxidation) | Moderate (acidic, hygroscopic) | Good |
| Suitability for ST-tandem | High (glass substrate) | High (flexible) | Limited (high R<sub>s</sub>) | High (rigid or flexible) |
| Key drawback | Brittle, indium cost | Roughness, junction shunting | Work-function mismatch | Multi-layer complexity |

Mark distribution: ~1 per row of analytical commentary, ~2 for the comparison table, ~1 for concluding recommendation (e.g., AgNW for flexible / BIPV, ITO for record-efficiency rigid cells, DMD for best of both).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Stating $J_{\text{SC,tandem}} = J_{\text{SC,1}} + J_{\text{SC,2}}$** — This is the single most common mistake. In a *two-terminal series* tandem, currents are **equal**, not additive. Only voltages add. *(–2 marks in 14-mark questions.)*
> 2. **Confusing AVT and LUE definitions** — AVT is a *transmittance*, dimensionless in [0, 1]. LUE is a *product of efficiency and transmittance*, hence also dimensionless. Do not report LUE in mA·cm⁻². *(–1 mark.)*
> 3. **Forgetting the recombination layer's role** — A common omission is listing the tandem stack as "two cells + electrodes." Without naming the **intermediate layer and its e–h recombination function**, the answer loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the trade-off explanation** — The examiner expects *both* the AVT–PCE trade-off *and* how the tandem relaxes it. A one-line answer will lose 2 marks.
> 5. **No labelled diagram** — KTU examiners *expect* a cross-sectional sketch in 7- and 14-mark questions on device architectures. Missing it costs ≥ 1 mark even if the textual description is correct.
> 6. **Ignoring units in numerical problems** — If you compute LUE = 0.042, state it as **4.2 %**, not 0.042 or 4.2.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **semi-transparent tandem** stacks a wide-bandgap front sub-cell with a narrow-bandgap back sub-cell via a **recombination layer**.
- **Voltages add; currents are equalised** (current-matching condition).
- The **recombination layer** (e.g., ZnO/PEDOT:PSS) is essential — it enables the series connection by allowing electrons from the front and holes from the back to recombine.
- **LUE = η × AVT** is the canonical dual-metric figure-of-merit for transparent photovoltaics.
- **AVT** is the photopically/average-weighted transmittance over the **400–700 nm** visible band.
- **Complementary absorption** is engineered via donor:acceptor selection: PM6:Y6 (E<sub>g</sub> ≈ 1.8 eV, front) + PTB7-Th:IEICO-4F (E<sub>g</sub> ≈ 1.3 eV, back) is a textbook pairing.
- **Transparent electrode options**: ITO, AgNW, PEDOT:PSS, DMD multilayers — each with trade-offs in conductivity, transmittance, flexibility, and processability.
- **Current matching** is achieved by tuning **active-layer thickness, blend ratio, and the optical-field distribution** through spacer layers.
- **Detailed-balance limit** for an unconstrained two-junction cell ≈ **42 %**; for semi-transparent devices the practical target is **η ≈ 15–17 %, AVT ≈ 25–35 %, LUE ≈ 4–6 %**.
- **Applications** — BIPV windows, agrivoltaic greenhouses, vehicle windshields, IoT sensors, wearable electronics.
- The **S-kink** in the J–V curve is the diagnostic symptom of a faulty recombination layer; correct interfacial energetics (LUMO/HOMO alignment at the recombination junction) eliminate it.
- For KTU 2024 Scheme: in 14-mark questions, **always** provide (i) a labelled diagram, (ii) the formulas, (iii) a worked numerical or schematic example, and (iv) a concluding engineering statement linking device to application.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution Processed Organic Photovoltaics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solution Processed Organic Photovoltaics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Solution-Processed Organic Photovoltaics (s-OPVs)** are a class of third-generation thin-film solar cells in which the photoactive layer — comprising a blend of electron-donating (π-conjugated polymer or small molecule) and electron-accepting (fullerene or non-fullerene) organic semiconductors — is deposited entirely from a *liquid-phase ink formulation* onto a conducting substrate using scalable printing/coating techniques, *without* the need for high-vacuum thermal evaporation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *solution-processable* OPV is one in which the bulk heterojunction (BHJ) active layer, interfacial layers, and even top electrodes (when using silver paste/graphene/PEDOT:PSS composites) are deposited from solution, enabling **roll-to-roll (R2R)** manufacturing on flexible plastic substrates such as **PET** (polyethylene terephthalate) or **PEN** (polyethylene naphthalate).

The most widely studied BHJ system consists of a **conjugated polymer donor** (e.g., regioregular P3HT, PTB7, PM6) blended with a **fullerene acceptor** (e.g., PC₆₁BM, PC₇₁BM) or a **non-fullerene small-molecule acceptor (NF-SMA)** (e.g., ITIC, Y6/BTP-eC9).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Solar Cell as a Printer's Canvas"**
> Imagine you want to paint a solar panel, but instead of using a brush in a cleanroom, you are using an **inkjet printer** loaded with three colored cartridges:
> - **Magenta Ink** = the polymer donor (e.g., P3HT)
> - **Cyan Ink** = the fullerene/non-fullerene acceptor (e.g., PCBM or Y6)
> - **Yellow Ink** = the conducting polymer hole-transport layer (e.g., PEDOT:PSS)
> 
> When you print all three layers onto a transparent plastic sheet (PET), the *magenta* and *cyan* inks mix at the nanoscale like two paints stirred in water — creating an **interpenetrating nanoscale network** (the BHJ). Sunlight hitting this printed plastic sheet generates electricity.
> 
> The catch? Unlike a uniform paint layer, this **intermixed morphology** must have just the right degree of phase separation — too coarse and excitons recombine before reaching an interface; too fine and charge carriers get trapped.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics in s-OPVs (bolded constants appear in KTU exams):**
> - **Standard Test Conditions (STC)**: AM 1.5G solar spectrum, **100 mW/cm²** (or **1000 W/m²**) irradiance, cell temperature **25 °C (298.15 K)**.
> - **Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE / η)**: Single-junction s-OPVs have surpassed **19–20 %** in certified lab cells as of 2023–2024 (NREL chart).
> - **Bandgap of archetypal donor P3HT**: E_g ≈ **1.9 eV**.
> - **Bandgap of high-performance donor PM6 (PBDB-T-2F)**: E_g ≈ **1.8 eV**.
> - **Bandgap of benchmark NF-SMA Y6 (BTP-eC9 family)**: E_g ≈ **1.36 eV**.
> - **Exciton diffusion length in organic semiconductors**: L_D ≈ **5–20 nm** (this is why BHJ morphology is critical).
> - **Dielectric constant of organic semiconductors**: ε_r ≈ **3–4** (low ε_r is why excitons are tightly bound).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How" of Solution Processing

Solution processing of OPVs relies on the **solubility of π-conjugated polymers and small molecules** in common organic solvents (chloroform, chlorobenzene, o-dichlorobenzene, toluene, o-xylene, THF, or non-halogenated "green" solvents). The workflow consists of:

1. **Ink Formulation** — Dissolving the donor and acceptor in a *co-solvent* at typical concentrations of **10–30 mg/mL** (total solids), with **D/A ratios** typically ranging from **1:1 to 1:2 by mass**.
2. **Film Deposition** — Spreading the ink onto a substrate using one of the coating/printing methods listed in §2.4.
3. **Drying & Solidification** — Solvent evaporation drives **phase separation** between donor-rich and acceptor-rich domains.
4. **Morphology Tuning** — Optional thermal annealing (typically **100–150 °C**, 5–30 min) or **solvent-vapor annealing (SVA)** using saturated vapors of chloroform, THF, or carbon disulfide.
5. **Counter-Electrode / Interfacial Layer Deposition** — Solution-deposited PEDOT:PSS, ZnO nanoparticles (sol-gel), or evaporated Ca/Al/Ag.
6. **Encapsulation** — UV-curable epoxy + glass/PET lid (lamination) to prevent oxygen/water ingress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why solution processing matters:** The **BHJ morphology** — nanoscale interpenetration of donor/acceptor with domain sizes comparable to L_D — is essentially *self-assembled* during solvent evaporation. The morphology is *thermodynamically metastable* and depends critically on solvent boiling point, vapor pressure, drying kinetics, and surface energy of the substrate.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU-expected equations for s-OPVs (memorize the table below):**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Power Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{J_{sc}\cdot V_{oc}\cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | $P_{in}$ = incident optical power (**100 mW/cm²** at AM 1.5G) | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{J_{max}\cdot V_{max}}{J_{sc}\cdot V_{oc}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{J_{sc}\cdot V_{oc}}$ | $J_{max}, V_{max}$ = current & voltage at maximum power point | Dimensionless (0 < FF ≤ 1) |
| 3 | Open-Circuit Voltage (empirical) | $V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{1}{e}\left(E_{HOMO}^{Donor} - E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}\right) - 0.3\,\text{eV}$ | Empirical offset of ~0.3 eV due to recombination | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Short-Circuit Current Density | $J_{sc} = q \int_{\lambda_{min}}^{\lambda_{max}} EQE(\lambda)\cdot \Phi_{ph}(\lambda)\,d\lambda$ | $\Phi_{ph}$ = photon flux; $q$ = electron charge | A/m² or mA/cm² |
| 5 | External Quantum Efficiency | $EQE(\lambda) = \eta_{abs}\cdot \eta_{diff}\cdot \eta_{diss}\cdot \eta_{ct}\cdot \eta_{cc}$ | Sequential efficiencies: absorption, diffusion, dissociation, charge transfer, collection | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Beer–Lambert Absorbance | $A(\lambda) = \varepsilon(\lambda)\cdot c \cdot \ell$ | $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity; $c$ = concentration; $\ell$ = path length | A is dimensionless |
| 7 | Exciton Diffusion Length | $L_D = \sqrt{D\cdot \tau}$ | $D$ = exciton diffusivity; $\tau$ = exciton lifetime | nm (typically 5–20 nm in organics) |
| 8 | Charge Carrier Mobility (Space-Charge-Limited Current) | $J_{SCLC} = \dfrac{9}{8}\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\mu\dfrac{V^2}{L^3}$ | $\mu$ = charge mobility; $L$ = film thickness | A/m² |
| 9 | Bimolecular Recombination Rate | $R = k_2(np - n_i^2)$ | $k_2$ = Langevin-type recombination coefficient | cm³·s⁻¹ |
| 10 | Surface Coverage (Blading) | $t = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot g \cdot \dfrac{c_{sol}}{\rho_{dry}}$ | $g$ = gap height; $c_{sol}$ = solution concentration | µm |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters to a polymer/photovoltaics engineer:**
> - **Roll-to-Roll (R2R) Manufacturing**: Solution processing is the *only* economically viable route to OPV modules at < **0.30 USD/W** target cost.
> - **Building-Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV)**: s-OPVs can be printed as semi-transparent, colored films on windows with tunable bandgap.
> - **IoT & Indoor Photovoltaics**: s-OPVs with E_g ≈ 1.9 eV (e.g., P3HT:ICBA) are *better matched* to indoor LED spectra (300–500 lux) than silicon, generating ~**20–30 µW/cm²** under office lighting.
> - **Flexible & Wearable Electronics**: Compatible with stretchable substrates (PU, Ecoflex) and 3-D printed electrodes (silver nanowire inks).

### 2.4 Comparison of Solution-Processing Techniques

| Technique | Film Thickness Range | Lab/Industry | Throughput | Morphology Control |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Spin Coating** | 50 – 300 nm | Lab only | Low (single substrate) | Excellent (uniformity) |
| **Doctor Blade / Knife-Over-Edge** | 100 – 500 nm | Lab / Pilot | Medium | Good (depends on evaporation) |
| **Slot-Die Coating** | 100 – 1000 nm | Pilot / Industrial | High | Excellent (meniscus control) |
| **Inkjet Printing** | 50 – 500 nm | Lab / Pilot | Medium | Pixel-by-pixel; non-contact |
| **Screen Printing** | 1 – 50 µm | Industrial | Very High | Limited (thick films) |
| **Spray Coating** | 50 – 500 nm | Lab | Medium | Moderate (droplet impact) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE) from the J–V Characteristic

The current-voltage characteristic of a solar cell under illumination is given by the **Shockley equivalent-circuit diode equation**:

$$
J(V) = J_{ph} - J_{0}\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{qV}{n k_B T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + J R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

where:
- $J_{ph}$ = photogenerated current density (≈ J_sc when R_s ≈ 0 and V = 0),
- $J_0$ = reverse saturation current density of the diode,
- $n$ = ideality factor (typically 1.2–2.0 for organic cells),
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant (**1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**),
- $R_s$ = series resistance, $R_{sh}$ = shunt resistance.

**Step-by-step derivation of PCE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Define input optical power density:} \quad P_{in} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} \Phi_{ph}(\lambda)\cdot \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}\, d\lambda \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2 — Maximum power point:} \quad P_{max} &= J_{max}\cdot V_{max} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3 — Identify $J_{sc}$ and $V_{oc}$ from boundary conditions:} \quad J_{sc} &= J(0) = J_{ph} - J_{0}\left[\exp(0) - 1\right] = J_{ph} \\[6pt]
V_{oc} &= \dfrac{n k_B T}{q}\ln\left(\dfrac{J_{ph}}{J_0} + 1\right) \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4 — Define Fill Factor geometrically:} \quad FF &= \dfrac{J_{max} V_{max}}{J_{sc} V_{oc}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5 — Combine for total PCE:} \quad \eta &= \dfrac{J_{sc}\cdot V_{oc}\cdot FF}{P_{in}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Film Thickness in Doctor-Blade Coating

For a wet film of thickness $t_{wet}$ deposited with a blade gap of height $g$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Volume deposited per unit area} &= g \cdot 1\,\text{m}^2 \\
\text{Solid mass per unit area after drying} &= c_{sol}\cdot g \\
\text{Dry film thickness} &= \dfrac{c_{sol}\cdot g}{\rho_{dry}} \\[6pt]
\text{If solid volume fraction is } \phi_s = \dfrac{c_{sol}}{\rho_{dry}} &\Rightarrow \quad t_{dry} = \phi_s \cdot g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sample numerical evaluation (typical P3HT:PCBM ink):**
- Concentration: $c_{sol}$ = **20 mg/mL = 20 kg/m³**
- Dry film density: $\rho_{dry}$ ≈ **1100 kg/m³**
- Blade gap: $g$ = **200 µm = 2 × 10⁻⁴ m**

$$
t_{dry} = \dfrac{20\,\text{kg/m}^3 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}}{1100\,\text{kg/m}^3} = 3.6 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 3.6\,\mu\text{m (wet equivalent)}
$$

After ~**99 % solvent evaporation**, the dry active-layer thickness is ≈ **180 nm**, which is the optimum range for s-OPVs.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Morphology Parameter Affects Device Performance

| Morphology Variable | Effect on $J_{sc}$ | Effect on $V_{oc}$ | Effect on $FF$ | Net PCE Trend |
|---------------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Larger domain size (>20 nm)** | ↓ (exciton loss) | — (no change) | ↓ | Decreases |
| **Higher D:A ratio (1:2)** | ↑ (more absorption in acceptor) | ↑ (slight, if LUMO up-shifts) | ↑ | Increases |
| **Solvent additive (e.g., DIO 3 % v/v)** | ↑↑ (better phase separation) | ↓ (slight) | ↑ | Strongly increases |
| **Thermal annealing @ 150 °C** | ↑ (PCBM crystallizes) | — | ↑ | Increases up to optimum |
| **Over-annealing (>200 °C)** | ↓ (phase coarsening) | ↓ | ↓↓ | Decreases |
| **Higher boiling-point solvent (o-DCB vs CF)** | ↑ (larger crystal domains) | — | ↑ | Increases for P3HT |
| **Lower ε_r of solvent** | ↓ (poorer ion-pair separation) | ↓ | ↓ | Decreases |

### 3.4 Derivation of Exciton Diffusion Limitation on Domain Size

For an exciton generated at a random point in the active layer, the probability of reaching a donor–acceptor interface before recombining is:

$$
P_{diss} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{L_D^2}\int_{0}^{L_D}\!\int_{0}^{L_D}\! e^{-r/L_D}\, dx\, dy
$$

For an isotropic 2-D random distribution and using the average distance to nearest interface $\langle d \rangle$:

$$
P_{diss} \approx 1 - e^{-\langle d \rangle / L_D}
$$

**Numerical illustration:** If $L_D$ = **10 nm** and domain size = **20 nm** (so $\langle d \rangle$ ≈ 10 nm), then $P_{diss}$ ≈ **63 %**. This is why *nanoscale* phase separation (≤ L_D) is non-negotiable for high PCE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — s-OPV Device Fabrication

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Step 1: Ink Preparation"]:::proc
    nodeB["Donor Polymer e.g. PM6"]:::mat
    nodeC["Acceptor e.g. Y6 or PCBM"]:::mat
    nodeD["Co-Solvent e.g. CF or CB"]:::mat
    nodeE["Additive e.g. DIO 0.5 percent v/v"]:::mat
    nodeF["Step 2: Substrate Cleaning"]:::proc
    nodeG["ITO coated Glass or PET"]:::sub
    nodeH["UV Ozone Plasma 15 min"]:::sub
    nodeI["Step 3: HTL Deposition"]:::proc
    nodeJ["PEDOT:PSS spin or blade coat"]:::lyr
    nodeK["Anneal 150 C 10 min"]:::lyr
    nodeL["Step 4: Active Layer"]:::proc
    nodeM["Spin Coat 1000 rpm 60 s"]:::lyr
    nodeN["Doctor Blade or Slot Die"]:::lyr
    nodeO["Solvent Vapor Anneal"]:::lyr
    nodeP["Step 5: ETL and Cathode"]:::proc
    nodeQ["PDINO or ZnO sol gel"]:::lyr
```
