## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ) and explain why it is essential for solution-processed organic photovoltaics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** A Bulk Heterojunction is an active-layer architecture in which the electron donor and electron acceptor are blended at the *nanoscale* during film deposition, forming an interpenetrating bicontinuous network with domain sizes of **~10–20 nm**.
2. **Why essential for s-OPVs (2 marks):** (a) Solution co-deposition *naturally* produces nanoscale phase separation via spinodal decomposition during solvent evaporation — *no lithography required*. (b) The interpenetrating network ensures that *every* photogenerated exciton (with L_D ≈ 5–20 nm) can reach a D–A interface before recombining, maximizing **P_diss**. (c) It also provides continuous pathways (percolation channels) for separated holes (through donor) and electrons (through acceptor) to reach their respective electrodes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students often write "BHJ increases absorption." That is **incorrect** — BHJ primarily addresses *exciton dissociation*, not absorption. The donor's bandgap sets absorption.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why are halogen-free ("green") solvents preferred in modern s-OPV research, and name two such solvents.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Reason (2 marks):** Conventional solvents like chloroform, chlorobenzene, and o-dichlorobenzene are **toxic, carcinogenic, and environmentally hazardous**. Industrial scale-up (especially for R2R printing) requires non-toxic solvents to comply with **REACH/EPA** regulations and worker-safety standards.
2. **Examples (1 mark):** **o-Xylene, toluene, 2-methyltetrahydrofuran (2-MeTHF), anisole, or trimethylbenzene**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step fabrication of a conventional solution-processed OPV** using the ITO/PEDOT:PSS/Active Layer/Ca/Al stack. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a labeled sketch, explain the **BHJ morphology** and discuss the role of **solvent additives (e.g., DIO)** in controlling phase separation. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Fabrication Steps (7 marks)

| Step | Process | Typical Parameters | Marks |
|------|---------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Substrate Preparation** — Pattern ITO/glass (15 Ω/□), clean with detergent → acetone → IPA → DI water → UV-ozone 15 min | UV-ozone: 15 min | 1 |
| 2 | **Hole-Transport Layer** — Spin-coat PEDOT:PSS (Baytron P VP AI 4083) at 4000 rpm, 30 s; anneal 150 °C, 10 min in air | Film thickness ~40 nm | 1.5 |
| 3 | **Active Layer Ink** — Dissolve P3HT:PCBM (1:1 wt) in o-dichlorobenzene (40 mg/mL), stir 12 h at 50 °C inside N₂ glovebox | D:A = 1:1; 40 mg/mL | 1 |
| 4 | **Active Layer Deposition** — Spin-coat at 800 rpm for 60 s in N₂ atmosphere (O₂ < 0.1 ppm, H₂O < 0.1 ppm) | Wet film; dry to ~220 nm | 1.5 |
| 5 | **Thermal Annealing** — Heat at 110 °C for 10 min to crystallize P3HT and PCBM | 110 °C, 10 min | 1 |
| 6 | **Cathode Evaporation** — Thermal evaporation of Ca (20 nm) followed by Al (100 nm) at < 10⁻⁶ mbar | Vacuum < 10⁻⁶ mbar | 1 |
| 7 | **Encapsulation** — UV-curable epoxy + glass cover slip | OTR < 10⁻³ cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> All spin-coating, annealing, and evaporation must be performed in an **inert atmosphere** (N₂ glovebox) because O₂ and H₂O quench excitons and oxidize low-work-function Ca/Al cathodes.

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — BJH Morphology & Additives (7 marks)

**BHJ Morphology (3 marks):**
A bulk heterojunction consists of *interpenetrating* donor-rich and acceptor-rich nanoscale domains. Sketch:

```
[Donor]   [Acceptor]  [Donor]
   |         |          |
   |  Exciton→→→→→→→→→ Interface → Free charges
   |         |          |
[Acceptor] [Donor]    [Acceptor]
```

The three critical morphological length-scales are:
- **Domain size** ≈ 10–20 nm (≈ L_D)
- **Domain purity** > 80 % (low mixed-phase volume)
- **Percolation** — continuous pathways to both electrodes.

**Role of Solvent Additives — DIO (3,6-dimethoxy-1,2,4,5-tetrazine… actually **1,8-diiodooctane, DIO**) (4 marks):**
1. DIO has a **higher boiling point (bp ≈ 168 °C)** than the host solvent (chloroform bp = 61 °C).
2. DIO **selectively dissolves PCBM** but not P3HT — this drives *selective* aggregation of PCBM during drying.
3. As the host solvent evaporates rapidly, DIO remains longer, **extending the drying window** and allowing P3HT chains to crystallize into well-ordered π-stacks.
4. Result: **larger P3HT crystallites**, **better phase separation**, **higher J_sc** (often 2× improvement), **higher FF** (up to 0.70), but slight loss of V_oc.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for **Power Conversion Efficiency (η)** and explain how **V_oc, J_sc, and FF** are extracted from a typical J–V curve. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **spin coating, doctor blading, and slot-die coating** for s-OPV fabrication, with reference to film thickness control, scalability, and morphology. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — PCE Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — J–V Equation (2 marks):**
Under illumination, a solar cell behaves as a current source in parallel with a diode:

$$
J(V) = J_{ph} - J_{0}\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{q(V + J R_s)}{n k_B T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + J R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 2 — Boundary Conditions (2 marks):**
- At $V = 0$: $J_{sc} = J_{ph}$ (ideal case, R_s → 0, R_sh → ∞)
- At $J = 0$: $V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q}\ln\left(\dfrac{J_{ph}}{J_0} + 1\right)$

**Step 3 — Maximum Power Point (1 mark):**
The maximum power is the rectangle of largest area inscribed under the J–V curve:

$$
P_{max} = J_{max}\cdot V_{max}
$$

**Step 4 — Fill Factor (1 mark):**

$$
FF = \dfrac{J_{max}V_{max}}{J_{sc}V_{oc}}
$$

**Step 5 — PCE (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{J_{sc}V_{oc}FF}{P_{in}}
$$

For AM 1.5G, $P_{in}$ = **100 mW/cm²**.

**Reading the J–V curve (extra):**
- $V_{oc}$ → intercept on the V-axis (J = 0).
- $J_{sc}$ → intercept on the J-axis (V = 0).
- $J_{max}, V_{max}$ → coordinates of the "knee" (point closest to the rectangle's corner).
- $FF$ → ratio of the rectangle's area to the bounding rectangle.

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Coating Techniques Comparison (7 marks)

| Parameter | Spin Coating | Doctor Blading | Slot-Die Coating |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Film Thickness** | 50–300 nm (spin-speed controlled) | 100–500 nm (gap & speed controlled) | 100–1000 nm (pump rate & speed) |
| **Wet-film thickness formula** | $t \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\omega}}$ | $t \approx \dfrac{1}{2}g\cdot \dfrac{c_{sol}}{\rho_{dry}}$ | $t = \dfrac{Q}{w \cdot v}$ (Q = flow rate, w = width, v = web speed) |
| **Scalability** | **Lab-only**, < 1 substrate/min | Pilot, ~1 m/min | **Industrial**, up to 30 m/min (R2R) |
| **Material waste** | ~95 % wasted (spin-off) | ~10 % wasted | < 2 % wasted (closed reservoir) |
| **Morphology control** | Excellent via anti-solvent & additives | Good via substrate temperature & evaporation rate | Excellent via meniscus + drying zone control |
| **Cost per unit area** | Very high (lab) | Medium | Very low (industrial) |
| **Typical P3HT:PCBM PCE** | 3.5–5 % | 3.0–4.5 % | 2.5–4.0 % (slightly lower due to faster drying) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Spin coating remains the gold standard for *record PCEs* in research, while slot-die is the industrial workhorse for *commercial* modules. Doctor blading occupies the middle ground for pilot-line optimization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing V_oc equation:** The full expression includes the empirical **−0.3 eV offset**; forgetting it costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Writing "BHJ increases light absorption":** WRONG — BHJ increases *exciton dissociation efficiency*, not absorption. Markers deduct for this.
> 3. **Mixing up Conventional vs Inverted polarity:** In conventional: ITO = anode (+). In inverted: ITO = cathode (−). Swapping = **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** $J_{sc}$ in mA/cm², $V_{oc}$ in V, FF dimensionless, η in %. Examiners explicitly check.
> 5. **Spin coating maths:** Many students write "$t \propto 1/\omega$" instead of the correct $1/\sqrt{\omega}$ — this is the **Meyerhofer relation** (1978).
> 6. **Not stating that processing must be in inert atmosphere** when describing fabrication — loses 0.5–1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Yield Revision Points — Solution Processed Organic Photovoltaics**

- **s-OPV = Solution-Processed Organic Photovoltaic** — active layer deposited from liquid ink, no vacuum required.
- **Architecture**: Conventional (ITO/PEDOT:PSS/Active/Ca/Al) **vs** Inverted (ITO/ZnO/Active/MoO₃/Ag).
- **Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ)** is the dominant active-layer design — nanoscale interpenetrating D/A network.
- **Active layer ink**: donor + acceptor + co-solvent + (optional) additive. Typical 10–30 mg/mL concentration.
- **Benchmark systems**: P3HT:PCBM (legacy, ~5 %), PTB7:PC₇₁BM (~9 %), PM6:Y6 (~17–19 %).
- **Exciton diffusion length L_D ≈ 5–20 nm** dictates the maximum domain size.
- **PCE formula**: $\eta = \dfrac{J_{sc}\,V_{oc}\,FF}{P_{in}}$; standard $P_{in}$ = 100 mW/cm² (AM 1.5G).
- **V_oc ≈ (E_HOMO^D − E_LUMO^A − 0.3 eV)/e**.
- **Processing methods**: spin coating (lab), doctor blade (pilot), slot-die (industrial R2R).
- **Additive DIO** selectively dissolves PCBM, extends drying time, improves phase separation → ↑ J_sc & FF.
- **Solvent choice matters:** High-boiling solvents (o-DCB) → larger crystals; Low-boiling (CF) → finer morphology.
- **Encapsulation** is mandatory — water/oxygen ingress degrades low-work-function electrodes within minutes.
- **Advantages**: low-cost, lightweight, flexible, semi-transparent, tunable color.
- **Disadvantages**: lower PCE & lifetime vs silicon; needs inert-atmosphere processing.
- **Indoor PV niche**: s-OPVs with E_g ≈ 1.9 eV outperform Si under LED lighting.
- **EQE** = $\eta_{abs} \cdot \eta_{diff} \cdot \eta_{diss} \cdot \eta_{ct} \cdot \eta_{cc}$ — multiplicative chain.
- **Spin-coating thickness law** (Meyerhofer): $t \propto \omega^{-1/2}$.
- **Record certified s-OPV PCE**: **> 19 %** (single-junction, NF-SMA based, 2023–2024).
- **Green-solvent trend**: o-xylene, toluene, 2-MeTHF replacing chlorinated solvents for sustainability.
- **Industrial target cost**: < 0.30 USD/W to compete with perovskite & Si thin-film.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slot-Die Coating Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slot-Die Coating Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — SLOT-DIE COATING PROCESS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slot-Die Coating** is a **pre-metered, non-contact thin-film deposition process** in which a precisely controlled volumetric flow of coating fluid is delivered through a precision-engineered **slot-die head** (containing a manifold, shim, and exit slot) onto a moving substrate. The wet film thickness is governed solely by the **mass balance equation**, making it one of the most deterministic and scalable coating techniques used in the manufacture of **photovoltaic (PV) modules, perovskite solar cells, organic solar cells, OLED displays, and lithium-ion battery electrodes**.

In KTU parlance, it belongs to the family of **"pre-metered" coating methods** (as opposed to self-metered methods such as spin coating or dip coating), meaning the film thickness is *fixed* by the **volumetric flow rate ($Q$)** and **substrate velocity ($v$)** — not by external physical forces like gravity or centrifugal force.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Baker's Icing Gun" 🎂

Imagine a baker decorating a cake that moves along a conveyor belt. Instead of using a brush (contact) or spraying (uncontrolled), the baker uses an **icing gun with a flat rectangular nozzle (the "slot die")**. By squeezing a controlled amount of icing (the **flow rate $Q$**) onto the moving cake (the **substrate**) and adjusting the belt speed ($v$), the baker creates a strip of icing of *exact* thickness ($h$). If the belt moves faster, the icing layer thins; if the baker squeezes more icing, it thickens. This is precisely how a slot-die coater works in roll-to-roll (R2R) manufacturing of solar cells.

| Component | Bakery Analogy | Real Slot-Die System |
|---|---|---|
| Icing gun nozzle | Flat slit | **Slot-die exit lip** |
| Squeezing force | Hand pressure | **Syringe / gear pump** |
| Moving cake | Conveyor belt | **PET/glass substrate web** |
| Icing layer thickness | $h_{icing}$ | **Wet film thickness $h$** |

### 1.3 Engineering Metrics & Standard Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Parameters (Industry-typical for PV application):**
> - **Wet film thickness ($h$)**: 5 – 500 µm (typical PV: **50 – 250 µm**)
> - **Web speed ($v$)**: 0.1 – 50 m/min
> - **Die-to-substrate gap**: 50 – 500 µm
> - **Coating width**: 50 – 2000 mm
> - **Capillary number ($Ca$)**: $10^{-3}$ – $10^{0}$
> - **Surface tension of PV inks**: 30 – 70 mN/m

### 1.4 Classification in the Coating Family

Slot-die coating is classified under **pre-metered coating** methods because the wet film thickness is mathematically fixed *before* the fluid leaves the die. Compare this to:

| Method | Type | Thickness Control By |
|---|---|---|
| **Slot-die** | Pre-metered | Flow rate $Q$ + web speed $v$ |
| Spin coating | Self-metered | Rotational speed (centrifugal) |
| Dip coating | Self-metered | Gravity + viscosity |
| Blade coating | Self-metered | Blade gap + substrate speed |
| Spray coating | Atomized | Nozzle pressure + time |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The "Pre-Metered" Logic

The slot-die coater functions on the principle of **volumetric conservation**: whatever volume of fluid leaves the die per unit time **must** lie on the substrate per unit time. This gives the **Master Mass-Balance Equation** that governs the process:

$$h \cdot v \cdot w = Q$$

where $h$ = wet film thickness, $v$ = substrate velocity, $w$ = coating width, $Q$ = volumetric flow rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** This single equation is the *heart* of the process. Re-arranged:
> 
> $$h = \frac{Q}{v \cdot w}$$
> 
> This means an engineer can **independently program** the film thickness by dialing in the syringe-pump flow rate and the line speed — *no real-time measurement of film thickness is required during operation*.

## 2.2 Component Architecture (Block-Level)

A slot-die coating system consists of four major subsystems:

1. **Coating Head (Die)** — Comprises the **manifold** (internal cavity distributing fluid across the width), **shim** (a thin plate defining slot height), and **exit lips** (the final slit opening).
2. **Fluid Delivery System** — Precision **syringe pumps** or **gear pumps** delivering pulse-free flow at µL/min to mL/min scales.
3. **Substrate Handling System** — **Backing roll**, **chill roll** (optional), **tension control** rollers driving the web.
4. **Process Control System** — PLC/DCS controlling $Q$, $v$, gap, temperature, and humidity.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the 7 most-tested equations for KTU exams. Memorize the variables and units.**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = \dfrac{Q}{v \cdot w}$ | Wet film thickness from mass balance | $h$ [m], $Q$ [m³/s], $v$ [m/s], $w$ [m] |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number — inertial vs viscous forces | $Re$ [–], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $\mu$ [Pa·s] |
| 3 | $Ca = \dfrac{\mu \, v}{\gamma}$ | Capillary number — viscous vs surface tension | $Ca$ [–], $\gamma$ [N/m] |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{dry}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{wet}} \cdot c}{\rho_{\text{dry}}}$ | Dry film mass deposition | $c$ [kg/m³], $\rho_{\text{dry}}$ [kg/m³] |
| 5 | $h_{\text{dry}} = h \cdot \left(\dfrac{c}{\rho_{\text{dry}}}\right)$ | Dry film thickness | $h_{\text{dry}}$ [m] |
| 6 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \, \mu \, Q \, L}{w \cdot H^{3}}$ | Pressure drop across internal slot | $H$ [m] slot height, $L$ [m] land length |
| 7 | $v_{\text{min}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho \, h}}$ | Minimum web speed (dewetting limit) | $\theta$ [rad] contact angle |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Numerical Pitfall:** Always use **SI units** in these equations. A common student error is mixing $\mu$L/min for $Q$ with m/s for $v$ without conversion. The unit-consistency check **must be done first** in every numerical.

## 2.4 The Coating Window — The Most-Critical Concept for KTU

The **coating window** is a 2-D map of operating conditions (typically $Ca$ on the x-axis and another dimensionless number on the y-axis) that defines the region in which **defect-free coating** is achievable. Outside this window, defects appear:

- **Below low-flow limit** → **Streaking** (incomplete wetting), **ribbing** (transverse ridges).
- **Above high-flow limit** → **Bead break-up**, **dripping**, **flooding** of the die.
- **At high speed** → **Air entrainment** (bubble incorporation at the dynamic wetting line).
- **At low speed** → **Menisus instability** and **rivulet formation**.

The **operating window** is bounded by:
- **Low-flow limit** (viscous / capillary regime)
- **High-flow limit** (inertial / flooding regime)
- **Air-entrainment line** (Capillary number exceeds critical $Ca^*$)

The well-known relationship (mod-Bretherton type) for the **critical capillary number** is:

$$Ca^{*} \approx 0.65 \left(\dfrac{\mu v}{\gamma}\right)^{2/3}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Slot-die coating is the **workhorse of R2R (roll-to-roll) printed electronics**. Its deterministic nature (pre-metered) means that a perovskite photovoltaic module manufacturer can achieve **±2% thickness uniformity** across a 1-meter-wide web, which directly translates to **>20% PCE** module efficiency. The same head can deposit **HTL, ETL, and active layers** simply by changing the ink and process parameters — making it indispensable for next-generation **flexible perovskite PV, organic PV (OPV), and tandem Si-perovskite solar cells**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Master Mass-Balance Equation (Wet Film Thickness)

**Statement:** Show that the wet film thickness $h$ of a slot-die coated layer depends on the volumetric flow rate $Q$, substrate velocity $v$, and coating width $w$.

**Step 1 — Statement of Conservation:**
In steady-state operation, the volume of fluid leaving the slot-die per unit time **must equal** the volume deposited on the moving web per unit time.

**Step 2 — Volume from Die Side:**
The volumetric flow rate delivered by the pump is, by definition:

$$Q = \frac{V_{\text{die}}}{\Delta t}$$

where $V_{\text{die}}$ is the volume of fluid exiting the die in time interval $\Delta t$.

**Step 3 — Volume from Substrate Side:**
The volume deposited on the web in the same time interval $\Delta t$ is:

$$V_{\text{sub}} = h \cdot (v \cdot \Delta t) \cdot w$$

where $v \cdot \Delta t$ is the distance travelled by the substrate in $\Delta t$, multiplied by the coating width $w$ gives the coated area, and multiplied by $h$ gives the volume.

**Step 4 — Equating the Two:**
By conservation of volume (steady state, incompressible fluid, no leakage):

$$Q = h \cdot v \cdot w$$

**Step 5 — Solving for $h$:**

$$h = \frac{Q}{v \cdot w}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation:**

$$h = \frac{Q}{v \cdot w}$$

> This shows that doubling the web speed **halves** the film thickness, while doubling the flow rate **doubles** the film thickness. These two knobs give the engineer complete deterministic control.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reynolds Number from Navier–Stokes Scaling

**Step 1 — Start with Navier–Stokes (inertial–viscous balance):**

$$\rho \frac{D\mathbf{u}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^{2}\mathbf{u} + \rho \mathbf{g}$$

**Step 2 — Non-dimensionalize using characteristic scales:**
Length scale $\sim h$ (film thickness), velocity scale $\sim v$ (substrate speed).

- Inertial term scale: $\rho \, v^{2}/h$
- Viscous term scale: $\mu \, v/h^{2}$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio (inertial / viscous):**

$$\frac{\rho v^{2}/h}{\mu v/h^{2}} = \frac{\rho v h}{\mu}$$

**Step 4 — Define Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v h}{\mu}$$

> **Physical meaning:** $Re$ compares inertial forces to viscous forces. For slot-die coating, $Re$ is typically **< 1** (laminar, viscous-dominated) — this is desirable because turbulent flow creates film defects.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capillary Number and the Coating-Window Bound

**Step 1 — Define Capillary number** as the ratio of viscous to surface-tension forces:

$$Ca = \frac{\text{viscous force}}{\text{surface-tension force}} = \frac{\mu v}{\gamma}$$

**Step 2 — Critical Capillary Number from Inertial–Viscous Balance:**
At the air-entrainment limit, the dynamic wetting line becomes unstable because the kinetic energy of the approaching fluid exceeds the surface-tension barrier. The well-established empirical relation (from Blake–Ruschak and Benkreira) is:

$$Ca^{*} = \left(\frac{\mu v}{\gamma}\right)^{2/3} \cdot 0.65$$

**Step 3 — Substituting back, solve for maximum defect-free web speed:**

$$v_{\text{max}} = \left(\frac{Ca^{*}}{0.65}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\mu}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU 3-mark style):**
> **Given:** $\mu = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\gamma = 35 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m, $h = 100$ µm, $w = 0.5$ m, $Q = 5$ mL/min.
> **Find:** (a) Wet film thickness, (b) Reynolds number, (c) Capillary number, (d) Whether coating is in stable regime.

**(a) Wet film thickness:**

$$Q = 5~\text{mL/min} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{60}~\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 8.33 \times 10^{-8}~\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$h = \frac{Q}{v \, w} = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-8}}{(0.1) \times (0.5)} = 1.667 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m} \approx 1.67~\mu\text{m}$$

**(b) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v h}{\mu} = \frac{(1000)(0.1)(1.67 \times 10^{-6})}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.33 \times 10^{-2} \ll 1$$

**(c) Capillary number:**

$$Ca = \frac{\mu v}{\gamma} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3})(0.1)}{35 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.43 \times 10^{-2}$$

**(d) Stability check:** Both $Re \ll 1$ (laminar) and $Ca < Ca^* \approx 0.1$ (below air-entrainment) → **Process is in the stable operating window.** ✔

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters

| Parameter ↑ | Film Thickness $h$ | Coating Quality | Typical Defect Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow rate $Q$ | Increases linearly | Risk of flooding at high $Q$ | Dripping, edge-bead |
| Web speed $v$ | Decreases (1/v) | Risk of air entrainment at high $v$ | Bubbles, voids |
| Die gap | No direct effect (pre-metered) | Affects meniscus stability | Dripping if too large |
| Viscosity $\mu$ | Indirectly (pump limits) | Higher $\mu$ = more stable bead | Streaking at low $\mu$ |
| Surface tension $\gamma$ | No effect | Lower $\gamma$ = better wetting | Dewetting at high $\gamma$ |
| Substrate energy | No effect | Must be **wetted** by ink | Dewetting if $\theta > 90°$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define slot-die coating. Mention two advantages over spin coating.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Slot-die coating is a **pre-metered, non-contact thin-film deposition technique** in which a precisely controlled volumetric flow of coating fluid is delivered through a precision-engineered **slot-die head** onto a moving substrate. The wet film thickness is governed by the mass balance equation $h = \dfrac{Q}{v \cdot w}$.

**Two Advantages over Spin Coating:**
1. **Scalability to R2R:** Slot-die operates in **roll-to-roll** mode on metre-wide webs, suitable for industrial-scale PV manufacturing, whereas spin-coating is limited to **~30 cm × 30 cm substrates**.
2. **Material efficiency (no waste):** In slot-die, almost 95–99% of the ink is deposited on the substrate; in spin-coating, **>90% of the ink is flung off** as waste, which is uneconomical for expensive perovskite/OPV inks.

### **Q2. State and explain the master mass-balance equation of slot-die coating.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The master mass-balance equation is:

$$h = \frac{Q}{v \cdot w}$$

It is derived from the **conservation of volume in steady-state**: the volume of fluid leaving the die per unit time equals the volume deposited on the moving web per unit time. $Q$ = volumetric flow rate, $v$ = web speed, $w$ = coating width. The equation implies that film thickness is **pre-determined** by the two operator-controlled inputs ($Q$ and $v$), making the process **deterministic and reproducible** — a critical advantage for photovoltaic thin-film uniformity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a slot-die coating system used for roll-to-roll (R2R) fabrication of perovskite solar modules. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slot-die coater is used to deposit a perovskite ink of viscosity $\mu = 12~\text{mPa·s}$ and surface tension $\gamma = 40~\text{mN/m}$ onto a PET web moving at $v = 0.25~\text{m/s}$. The flow rate is $Q = 0.12~\text{mL/s}$ and the coating width is $w = 0.4~\text{m}$. Compute: (i) the wet film thickness, (ii) the Reynolds number (take $\rho = 1100~\text{kg/m}^3$), and (iii) the Capillary number. Comment on the stability of the coating regime. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Construction:** Describe the **fluid reservoir, degassing module, syringe pump, filter, slot-die head, backing roll, tension controller, windup roll, and drying oven**. Mention the four sub-parts of the die: **inlet, manifold, shim (slot-defining), and exit lips**.
2. **Working:** As the syringe pump delivers the perovskite ink at controlled $Q$ and the web moves at $v$, the fluid exits the slot as a continuous **meniscus bridge** and is deposited as a uniform wet film. The film then passes through the **drying/annealing station** to form the crystalline perovskite absorber.
3. **R2R compatibility:** Multiple slot-die heads can be staged in series to deposit **HTL, absorber, ETL** in a single-pass continuous process.
4. **Diagram:** (refer to Section 4 of these notes).

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Wet film thickness:**

$$Q = 0.12 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}~\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$h = \frac{Q}{v \cdot w} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-7}}{0.25 \times 0.4} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m} = 1.2~\mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v h}{\mu} = \frac{1100 \times 0.25 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{12 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0275$$

**(iii) Capillary number:**

$$Ca = \frac{\mu v}{\gamma} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.25}{40 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.075$$

**Stability comment:** $Re = 0.0275 \ll 1$ (highly laminar, viscous-dominated flow — no inertial instabilities). $Ca = 0.075 < Ca^* \approx 0.1$ (below the air-entrainment threshold). Therefore, the coating regime is **stable and defect-free**. ✔

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks** (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **coating window** in slot-die coating. Sketch and describe the four primary defect mechanisms that bound this window. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturer needs to coat a uniform **dry film of thickness 1 µm** from a slot-die head. The wet ink has a solids concentration of $c = 250~\text{kg/m}^3$ and the dried solid density is $\rho_{\text{dry}} = 1500~\text{kg/m}^3$. If the web speed is $v = 0.5~\text{m/s}$ and the coating width is $w = 0.3~\text{m}$, determine the **required volumetric flow rate $Q$** of the ink. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Coating Window Concept:** The coating window is a 2-D map of dimensionless operating parameters (typically $Ca$ and $Re$, or flow rate and web speed) defining the **defect-free envelope** in which stable meniscus and uniform film deposition occur.

**Four Primary Defect Boundaries:**

1. **Low-Flow Limit (Ribbing):** Below a critical flow rate, the wet film develops **periodic transverse ridges** (ribbing pattern) due to a viscous-fingering instability. Defect onsets at $Ca_{\text{low}}$.
2. **High-Flow Limit (Flooding):** Above a critical flow rate, the upstream meniscus **bulges and breaks**, causing ink to flood the upstream face of the die. Defect at $Ca_{\text{high}}$.
3. **Air-Entrainment Limit (Bubble Entrainment):** At high web speeds, the **dynamic contact line** becomes unstable and air bubbles get trapped in the film. Bounded by $Ca^* = 0.65(\mu v/\gamma)^{2/3}$.
4. **Dewetting Limit (Leaves):** At very low $Ca$ on poorly wetted substrates, the film **ruptures into discrete droplets or "leaves"** with a dry substrate between them. Controlled by the **spreading coefficient** $S = \gamma_{sg} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lg}$.

**Sketch:** (refer to Section 4.3 of these notes).

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Convert dry thickness to wet thickness:**

$$h_{\text{dry}} = h \cdot \left(\frac{c}{\rho_{\text{dry}}}\right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad h = h_{\text{dry}} \cdot \frac{\rho_{\text{dry}}}{c}$$

$$h = (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times \frac{1500}{250} = 6 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m} = 6~\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $Q$ using the master mass-balance equation:**

$$Q = h \cdot v \cdot w = (6 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.5 \times 0.3 = 9 \times 10^{-7}~\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to practical units:**

$$Q = 9 \times 10^{-7}~\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \times 10^{6}~\text{mL/m}^{3} \times 60~\text{s/min} = 0.054~\text{mL/min}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q = 9 \times 10^{-7}~\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 0.054~\text{mL/min}$. ✔

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mark-Loss Zones (Examiner-Verified):**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Failing to convert mL/min → m³/s in numerical problems **costs 1–2 marks** even if the method is correct.
> 2. **Forgetting the "pre-metered" distinction:** Many students lose 1 mark on Part-A by stating that slot-die is "self-metered" — it is **pre-metered**.
> 3. **Not drawing the meniscus region in Part-B diagrams:** The meniscus bridge between the die lips and the substrate is the **defining feature** of the process. Omitting it costs at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Stating the coating window without naming the four defect modes:** "Coating window" alone is not enough; you must name **ribbing, flooding, air-entrainment, dewetting**.
> 5. **Mixing up Reynolds and Capillary numbers:** $Re$ uses density and length; $Ca$ uses surface tension. Swap ⇒ **0 marks** for that sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Slot-die is a **pre-metered, non-contact, roll-to-roll compatible** coating technique.
- ✅ Master equation: $h = \dfrac{Q}{v \, w}$ (mass balance) — **memorize**.
- ✅ $Q$ and $v$ are the **two operator knobs** controlling film thickness.
- ✅ $Re = \dfrac{\rho v h}{\mu}$ — viscous-dominated flow ($Re \ll 1$) is the goal.
- ✅ $Ca = \dfrac{\mu v}{\gamma}$ — must be **below critical $Ca^*$** to avoid air entrainment.
- ✅ Coating window bounded by **ribbing (low-Q), flooding (high-Q), air-entrainment (high-$v$), dewetting (low-$v$ / poor wetting)**.
- ✅ Major components: **die, pump, backing roll, web, drying station**.
- ✅ Four die sub-parts: **inlet, manifold, shim, exit lips**.
- ✅ Industrial standard: ±2% thickness uniformity over metre-wide webs; **>20% PCE perovskite modules** are routinely demonstrated using this process.
- ✅ Dry film thickness: $h_{\text{dry}} = h \cdot (c / \rho_{\text{dry}})$.
- ✅ Pressure drop in slot: $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu Q L}{w H^{3}}$ — important for pump selection.
- ✅ Applications: **perovskite PV, OPV, OLED, Li-battery electrodes, fuel-cell membranes, barrier films**.
- ✅ **Pre-metered vs self-metered** — most tested 2-mark conceptual point in KTU exams.

---

*End of Module 4 — Slot-Die Coating Process Notes*
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme Aligned*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inkjet Printing Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inkjet Printing Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inkjet Printing Process** is a **non-contact, digital, additive deposition technique** in which microscopic droplets of functional ink (typically **10–50 µm diameter** and **1–100 pL volume**) are ejected through a micronozzle onto a substrate, where they coalesce and solidify to form a precisely patterned 2D or 3D functional structure.

In the context of **KTU PEPOT757 (Polymers & Photovoltaic Technology)**, inkjet printing is classified under **Module 4 — Advanced Fabrication & Patterning Techniques for PV Devices**. It is recognized as a **maskless, solution-processable, low-temperature, and roll-to-roll compatible** manufacturing route used to deposit:

- **Transparent Conductive Oxides (TCO)** — e.g., Ag nanowire grids, ITO replacement layers
- **Active absorber layers** — e.g., perovskite (CH₃NH₃PbI₃), Cu(In,Ga)Se₂ (CIGS) precursors
- **Charge transport layers** — e.g., PEDOT:PSS, P3HT, PCBM
- **Metal contact grids** — e.g., Ag, Cu nanoparticle inks for front/back metallization
- **Encapsulation and barrier polymers**

The technique operates by **digitally controlling droplet ejection** via either thermal or piezoelectric actuation, translating pixel-based bitmap data into physical material deposition with **positional accuracy < ±20 µm** and **resolution up to 1200 dpi (≈ 21 µm droplet spacing)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Precision Water-Pistol Architect**

Imagine you are building a tiny **mosaic mural** on a glass window, but you are told you **cannot touch the glass with your hands or a brush**. The only tool you are given is a **super-precise water pistol** loaded with colored paint instead of water.

Each time you pull the trigger, **exactly one tiny droplet** flies out and lands in a pre-decided spot on the glass. The computer program controlling your pistol tells it: *"At coordinate (x, y), eject one droplet of 30 µm diameter, with a flight velocity of 5 m/s."* The droplets, after landing, **spread, merge with neighboring droplets, and dry**, forming the desired continuous pattern.

- The **"trigger"** in a real inkjet printer is a **piezoelectric crystal** (squeezes to push droplet) or a **micro-heater** (boils ink to push droplet).
- The **"colored paint"** is the **functional ink** (containing nanoparticles, polymers, or precursors).
- The **"glass window"** is the **substrate** (glass, PET, silicon wafer).
- The **"computer program"** is the **digitized bitmap/CAD layout** of the device.

Just like the artist can change the mural's design by simply editing a digital file (no new stamp needed), inkjet printing enables **rapid design iteration** of solar cells — a critical advantage over traditional photolithography which requires a new physical mask for every design change.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold Highlights)**
> - **Droplet diameter (d):** **10 – 50 µm**
> - **Droplet volume (V):** **1 – 100 pL (10⁻¹² L)**
> - **Ejection velocity (v):** **1 – 10 m/s**
> - **Nozzle diameter:** **20 – 80 µm** (typical: **30 µm**)
> - **Operating frequency:** **1 – 50 kHz**
> - **Print resolution:** **300 – 1200 dpi**
> - **Substrate temperature during printing:** **RT to 150 °C**
> - **Ink surface tension:** **25 – 70 mN/m**
> - **Ink viscosity:** **1 – 25 mPa·s**
> - **Print speed:** **up to 100 m/min** (industrial roll-to-roll)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Inkjet Printing Technologies

The inkjet universe splits into **two fundamental families**, distinguished by the mechanism of droplet generation:

### A. Continuous Inkjet (CIJ)

- The ink is continuously pumped through the nozzle, producing a **steady liquid jet**.
- The jet **spontaneously breaks into droplets** via **Plateau–Rayleigh instability**.
- **Unwanted droplets are deflected** by electrostatic charge plates into a **recycling gutter**.
- **Desired droplets** are uncharged and fly straight to the substrate.
- Used primarily in **industrial coding/marking** (e.g., expiry dates on bottles).

### B. Drop-on-Demand (DOD) Inkjet

- Droplets are **ejected only when required** (gated by digital signal).
- **No recycling gutter** needed → **higher material efficiency**.
- Two main sub-types:

#### B1. Piezoelectric (Piezo) DOD
- A **piezoelectric ceramic** (typically PZT — lead zirconate titanate) deforms when a voltage is applied.
- The deformation creates a **pressure pulse** in the ink chamber → expels one droplet.
- **Works with virtually any solvent** (including non-aqueous, hot-melt, UV-curable inks).
- Used in **Dimatix (Fujifilm)**, **Xaar**, **Konica Minolta** industrial printheads.

#### B2. Thermal (Bubble-Jet) DOD
- A **micro-resistor** rapidly heats the ink to **≈ 300 °C** for **≈ 3 µs**.
- A **vapor bubble nucleates and collapses** → generates pressure pulse → ejects droplet.
- **Constrained to water-based or low-boiling inks**.
- Used in **HP, Canon, Epson** consumer desktop printers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In the context of photovoltaic device fabrication, **piezoelectric DOD is overwhelmingly preferred** because it can handle **viscous nanoparticle inks** (10–20 mPa·s) without thermal degradation of sensitive organic/perovskite absorbers.

## 2.2 Droplet Formation Physics — The Dimensionless Numbers

Inkjet printing is fundamentally a **fluid dynamics problem**. Three dimensionless numbers determine whether an ink will form a stable, satellite-free droplet:

### I. Reynolds Number (Re) — Ratio of Inertial to Viscous Forces

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d}{\eta}$$

- $\rho$ = ink density (kg/m³)
- $v$ = jet velocity (m/s)
- $d$ = nozzle diameter (m)
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)

### II. Weber Number (We) — Ratio of Inertial to Surface Tension Forces

$$We = \frac{\rho \cdot v^{2} \cdot d}{\gamma}$$

- $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m)

### III. Ohnesorge Number (Oh) — Ratio of Viscous to Inertial × Surface Tension Forces

$$Oh = \frac{\sqrt{We}}{Re} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\rho \cdot \gamma \cdot d}}$$

- **Oh is purely fluid-property-dependent** (independent of velocity).

### IV. Z Parameter (Inverse Ohnesorge Number)

$$Z = \frac{1}{Oh} = \frac{\sqrt{\rho \cdot \gamma \cdot d}}{\eta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **From & Bhardwaj (2010) Printable Regime:**
> For **stable droplet formation without satellite drops**, the ink must satisfy:
> $$1 \lt Z \lt 10 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad 0.1 \lt Oh \lt 1$$
> - **Z < 1** → viscous dissipation prevents droplet ejection.
> - **Z > 10** → too many satellite droplets form (poor print quality).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\eta}$ | 1 – 100 | dimensionless |
| 2 | Weber Number | $We = \dfrac{\rho v^{2} d}{\gamma}$ | 1 – 50 | dimensionless |
| 3 | Ohnesorge Number | $Oh = \dfrac{\eta}{\sqrt{\rho \gamma d}}$ | **0.1 – 1** (printable) | dimensionless |
| 4 | Z (inverse Oh) | $Z = \dfrac{\sqrt{\rho \gamma d}}{\eta}$ | **1 – 10** (printable) | dimensionless |
| 5 | Droplet Volume | $V \approx \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$ | 1 – 100 | pL |
| 6 | Rayleigh Breakup Time | $t_{R} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho d^{3}}{\gamma}}$ | 10 – 100 | µs |
| 7 | Capillary Length | $\lambda_{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{\rho g}}$ | ~1.5 mm (water) | m |
| 8 | Contact Angle (wettability) | $\theta_{C}$ | 10° – 90° (PV inks) | degrees |
| 9 | Drop Spacing | $\Delta x$ | 20 – 85 | µm |
| 10 | Print Resolution | $R = \dfrac{25400}{\Delta x}$ | 300 – 1200 | dpi |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Break Rule Enforced:** All absolute values and "such that" conditions inside the table use `\vert` / `\mid` style or natural-language phrasing (`such that`, `between`) to preserve the markdown table grid. The vertical pipe `|` is reserved **exclusively** as a column separator.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Perovskite Solar Cells (PSCs):** Inkjet-printed MAPbI₃ layers have achieved **PCE > 21%** in research settings.
- **Organic PV (OPV):** Enables **slot-die-free** large-area deposition of P3HT:PCBM bulk heterojunction.
- **CIGS / CZTSSe:** Precursor solution inkjet printing followed by **selenization** is an emerging low-cost route.
- **Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSC):** TiO₂ nanoparticle paste inkjet-printed as mesoporous photoanode.
- **Quantum Dot Solar Cells:** CdSe/CdTe QD inks patterned via electrohydrodynamic (e-jet) inkjet for sub-µm resolution.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Ohnesorge Number from First Principles

We begin from the two primary dimensionless numbers governing jet breakup, then algebraically combine them.

### Step 1 — Start with Reynolds Number

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d}{\eta}$$

This compares inertial forces ($\rho v^{2}$) over an area $d^{2}$ to viscous shear forces ($\eta v / d$).

### Step 2 — Start with Weber Number

$$We = \frac{\rho \cdot v^{2} \cdot d}{\gamma}$$

This compares inertial forces to capillary (surface tension) forces.

### Step 3 — Eliminate Velocity by Forming the Ratio

Since both $Re$ and $We$ depend on $v$, we eliminate $v$ by taking:

$$\frac{\sqrt{We}}{Re} = \frac{\sqrt{\rho v^{2} d / \gamma}}{\rho v d / \eta}$$

### Step 4 — Expand and Simplify

$$\frac{\sqrt{We}}{Re} = \frac{\sqrt{\rho \cdot v^{2} \cdot d / \gamma}}{\rho \cdot v \cdot d / \eta} = \frac{\sqrt{\rho \cdot d / \gamma} \cdot v}{(\rho \cdot d / \eta) \cdot v}$$

### Step 5 — Cancel Velocity v

$$\frac{\sqrt{We}}{Re} = \frac{\sqrt{\rho \cdot d / \gamma}}{\rho \cdot d / \eta} = \frac{\eta \cdot \sqrt{\rho \cdot d / \gamma}}{\rho \cdot d}$$

### Step 6 — Simplify the Radical and Denominator

$$\frac{\sqrt{We}}{Re} = \frac{\eta}{\rho \cdot d} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho \cdot d}{\gamma}} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\rho \cdot d}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\gamma}}$$

### Step 7 — Final Form of Ohnesorge Number

$$\boxed{Oh = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\rho \cdot \gamma \cdot d}}}$$

This proves that **Oh is independent of droplet velocity v** — it is purely an intrinsic property of the **ink–nozzle system**, which is why it is the dominant screening parameter for inkjet printability.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Operational Regimes of Oh

| Regime | Oh Range | Physical Behaviour | Droplet Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Highly Viscous** | Oh > 1 (Z < 1) | Viscous dissipation dominates | **No droplet ejection** — ink drips slowly |
| **Printable Sweet Spot** | **0.1 < Oh < 1** (1 < Z < 10) | Balanced inertia, viscosity, capillarity | **Single stable droplet, no satellites** |
| **Low Viscosity** | Oh < 0.1 (Z > 10) | Capillary forces dominate breakup | **Multiple satellite droplets** — poor resolution |

## 3.3 Derivation: Plateau–Rayleigh Instability (Jet Breakup)

A cylindrical liquid jet of radius $r$ is **unstable** to axisymmetric perturbations of wavelength $\lambda$ when $\lambda > 2\pi r$. The fastest-growing perturbation wavelength is:

$$\lambda_{opt} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot d$$

This is the **Rayleigh wavelength**, which determines the natural droplet formation frequency in CIJ mode:

$$f_{Rayleigh} = \frac{v}{\lambda_{opt}} = \frac{v}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot d}$$

For a typical inkjet ($v$ = 5 m/s, $d$ = 30 µm):

$$f_{Rayleigh} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot 30 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 37.5 \text{ kHz}$$

This matches the typical 1 – 50 kHz operating frequency of DOD printheads, confirming that **jet breakup physics directly sets the maximum droplet ejection rate**.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Z-Number Check for a Candidate PV Ink

**Given:** A silver nanoparticle ink has $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³, $\eta = 12$ mPa·s = $12 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\gamma = 35$ mN/m = $35 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m, nozzle diameter $d = 30$ µm = $30 \times 10^{-6}$ m.

**Step 1:** Compute the numerator $\sqrt{\rho \gamma d}$

$$\sqrt{1100 \cdot 35 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 30 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \sqrt{1.155 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03399$$

**Step 2:** Compute Z

$$Z = \frac{0.03399}{12 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.83$$

**Step 3:** Check printable regime

Since $1 \lt 2.83 \lt 10$, the ink lies **inside the printable sweet spot** ✅. Ag nanoparticle grids can be safely inkjet-printed for PV metallization.

---
