<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a Transparent Conductive Oxide (TCO)? Name two commonly used TCO materials and state two essential properties required for solar cell application.*

**Model Answer:**
A Transparent Conductive Oxide (TCO) is a degenerately doped wide-bandgap semiconductor that simultaneously exhibits high electrical conductivity (σ > 10³ S/cm) and high optical transparency (> 80%) in the visible spectrum. It functions as a top electrode and anti-reflection coating in thin-film solar cells.

**Two common TCO materials:**
1. **ITO** (Indium Tin Oxide): 90% In₂O₃ + 10% SnO₂.
2. **FTO** (Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide).

**Two essential properties:**
- Low sheet resistance **R_sh < 10 Ω/□** for efficient current collection.
- Refractive index **n ≈ 1.9–2.1** for anti-reflection matching with Si (n ≈ 3.5).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the role of a buffer layer in a CIGS thin-film solar cell. Why is intrinsic ZnO (i-ZnO) preferred over heavily doped ZnO?**

**Model Answer:**
The **buffer layer** in a CIGS solar cell is placed between the CdS window and the TCO (ZnO:Al) to:
- Protect the CIGS absorber from sputter damage during TCO deposition.
- Reduce shunt paths and pinholes.
- Engineer the conduction band offset (CBO) for optimal carrier transport.

**Why i-ZnO is preferred:**
Heavily doped ZnO (ZnO:Al) introduces a high density of free electrons that tunnel directly into the CIGS absorber, increasing interface recombination. Intrinsic ZnO has very low carrier density, forming a thin insulating barrier that allows only photogenerated electrons to pass via thermionic emission, thus suppressing recombination at the TCO/absorber interface.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the various functional layers in a typical CIGS thin-film solar cell with a neat cross-sectional diagram. Explain the role of each layer and the materials used.

**Model Solution:**

A CIGS (Cu(In,Ga)Se₂) thin-film solar cell consists of the following functional layers (from top to bottom, in superstrate or substrate configuration):

1. **Substrate (Soda-Lime Glass, ~3 mm):** Provides mechanical support. Na diffuses from glass into CIGS, improving grain growth and p-type conductivity.

2. **Back Contact — Molybdenum (Mo, ~0.5–1 μm):** DC-sputtered Mo forms an ohmic contact with p-CIGS. During deposition, a thin MoSe₂ layer forms, providing a good valence band alignment.

3. **Absorber Layer — CIGS (Cu(In₁₋ₓGaₓ)Se₂, ~1.5–2.5 μm):** p-type semiconductor with direct bandgap tunable from 1.04 eV (CIS) to 1.68 eV (CGS) by varying Ga/(In+Ga) ratio. Optimal x ≈ 0.3 gives E_g ≈ 1.15 eV, matching the AM1.5G spectrum.

4. **Buffer Layer — CdS (~50 nm):** Chemical bath deposition (CBD). n-type, E_g = 2.42 eV. Provides lattice matching and conduction band alignment with CIGS.

5. **Intrinsic ZnO (i-ZnO, ~50–100 nm):** Sputtered high-resistivity ZnO that prevents shunt currents.

6. **TCO — Al-doped ZnO (ZnO:Al, ~300–500 nm):** RF-sputtered conductive oxide serving as the front contact and anti-reflection coating (n ≈ 1.9).

7. **Anti-Reflection Coating (Optional, e.g., MgF₂):** Further reduces front-surface reflection.

```
Glass | Mo | CIGS | CdS | i-ZnO | ZnO:Al
```

**Role of each layer:**
- **Mo:** Back electrode + optical reflector.
- **CIGS:** Photon absorption (α > 10⁵ cm⁻¹ for E > E_g).
- **CdS:** Window + junction partner.
- **i-ZnO:** Buffer + shunt protection.
- **ZnO:Al:** Current collection + ARC.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the working of an anti-reflection coating on a silicon solar cell. Derive the conditions for zero reflectance at wavelength λ₀.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
When light strikes a bare Si surface, ~34% of incident light is reflected due to the large refractive index mismatch (n_Si ≈ 3.5, n_air = 1.0). A single-layer anti-reflection coating (ARC) of thickness d and refractive index n_f is applied to suppress this reflection via destructive interference of the two reflected rays (R₁ at air/film and R₂ at film/Si).

```
Air  | n_air = 1.0
Film | n_f, thickness d
Si   | n_Si = 3.5
```

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** For destructive interference, the optical path through the film must equal λ₀/2:

$$2 \, n_f \, d = \dfrac{\lambda_0}{2} \implies d = \dfrac{\lambda_0}{4 \, n_f}$$

**Step 2:** The amplitude reflectance at each interface must be equal. The amplitude coefficients are:

$$r_1 = \dfrac{n_f - 1}{n_f + 1}, \quad r_2 = \dfrac{n_Si - n_f}{n_Si + n_f}$$

**Step 3:** Setting $r_1 = r_2$:

$$\dfrac{n_f - 1}{n_f + 1} = \dfrac{n_Si - n_f}{n_Si + n_f}$$

Cross-multiplying and simplifying:

$$n_f^2 = n_{air} \cdot n_Si = 1.0 \times 3.5 = 3.5$$

$$n_f = \sqrt{3.5} \approx 1.87$$

**Step 4:** For λ₀ = 600 nm, d = 600 / (4 × 1.87) ≈ **80 nm**.

In practice, **Si₃N₄ (n ≈ 1.9–2.0)** is used with d ≈ 75–80 nm, providing < 2% reflection at 600 nm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the materials used for encapsulation of photovoltaic modules. Compare EVA, POE, and ionomer encapsulants in terms of their properties and performance.

**Model Solution:**

Encapsulation protects PV modules from **moisture ingress, UV radiation, mechanical stress, and thermal cycling** (IEC 61215 standard: -40 °C to +85 °C cycling for 200 cycles).

**Material Requirements:**
- Optical transmittance > 90% in 400–1100 nm.
- UV stability (ΔT < 1% over 25 years).
- Moisture vapor transmission rate (MVTR) < 1 g/m²/day.
- Volume resistivity > 10¹⁴ Ω·cm.
- Adhesion to glass and back sheet.
- Crosslinkable for thermal stability.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Property** | **EVA** | **POE (Polyolefin Elastomer)** | **Ionomer (Surlyn®)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Composition | Ethylene-vinyl acetate copolymer | Polyolefin elastomer | Ethylene-methacrylic acid, Na/Zn salt |
| Transmittance | ~91% | ~92% | ~92% |
| UV stability | Good (with stabilizers) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Moisture barrier | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| PID resistance | Low (acetic acid release) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | High | High |
| Crosslinking | Peroxide-based | No crosslinking needed | No crosslinking needed |
| Typical use | Standard c-Si modules | High-efficiency / bifacial | Space / premium |

**EVA (most common):** Crosslinked EVA has been industry standard since the 1980s. However, it releases acetic acid during UV exposure, corroding cell metallization and inducing Potential-Induced Degradation (PID).

**POE:** No acetic acid release, high PID resistance, used in bifacial and PERC modules.

**Ionomer:** Highest moisture barrier, used in space-grade and flexible PV.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of a back contact in a solar cell. Discuss the materials used for back contacts in c-Si and CIGS cells with suitable justifications.

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Back Contact:**
1. **Carrier collection:** Collects majority carriers (holes in p-type, electrons in n-type).
2. **Ohmic contact:** Low contact resistivity (ρ_c < 10⁻³ Ω·cm²).
3. **Optical reflector:** Reflects unabsorbed photons back into the absorber, increasing path length (light trapping).
4. **Adhesion:** Mechanical integrity of the cell.

**Materials for c-Si cells:**

- **Aluminum (Al):** Most common for p-type c-Si. Forms Al-BSF (Back Surface Field) upon alloying (~577 °C eutectic). Creates a p⁺ layer reducing rear surface recombination velocity. Limitation: not suitable for n-type cells (no BSF formation).

- **Silver (Ag):** Used in HJT and TOPCon cells. Lower contact resistance to n-type Si.

- **Copper (Cu):** Low cost alternative but prone to diffusion into Si (requires barrier layer like TiN or Ni).

**Materials for CIGS cells:**

- **Molybdenum (Mo):** Industry standard. Forms a thin MoSe₂ layer at the Mo/CIGS interface, providing ohmic contact and good adhesion. Work function ≈ 4.6 eV matches p-CIGS valence band.

**Justification for Mo in CIGS:**
- Stable at high CIGS deposition temperature (~550 °C).
- Forms ohmic, not Schottky, contact.
- High reflectivity in the IR region, increasing optical path length in CIGS absorber.

**Limitations of Mo:**
- MoSe₂ formation must be controlled (excess causes shunting).
- Expensive for large-scale production — research is ongoing into alternative back contacts like **W, Mo/Cu bilayers, and transparent back contacts (TBC)** for bifacial CIGS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse window and buffer layers.** Many students interchange them. The **window** is the primary wide-bandgap n-type layer (e.g., CdS) that forms the junction. The **buffer** is a thin interfacial layer (e.g., i-ZnO) that prevents shunts and protects during TCO sputtering.
> 2. **Always mention both refractive index AND thickness conditions** for ARC derivations. Writing only $n_f = \sqrt{n_{air} \cdot n_{sub}}$ without the d = λ/4n condition will cost you 2–3 marks.
> 3. **In EVA-related questions**, explicitly mention **acetic acid release** and **PID (Potential-Induced Degradation)** — this is a favorite KTU examiner area.
> 4. **For CIGS back contacts**, do not say "Molybdenum forms a Schottky contact." It is an **ohmic contact via MoSe₂ formation** — this distinction is worth marks.
> 5. **Numerical units:** Cut-off wavelength is in μm, not nm. Cross-check the formula $\lambda_c = 1.24 / E_g$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Functional layers** in a PV cell are: **Substrate → Back Contact → Absorber → Buffer → Window → TCO → Encapsulant**.
- **Absorber bandgap** must be in the range **1.0–1.6 eV** for optimal solar spectrum utilization (Shockley–Queisser limit).
- **ARC optimum condition:** $n_{ARC} = \sqrt{n_{air} \cdot n_{sub}}$ and $d = \lambda_0 / (4 n_{ARC})$.
- **TCO requirements:** T > 80%, R_sh < 10 Ω/□, n ≈ 1.9–2.1.
- **ITO, FTO, AZO** are the three industrial-standard TCOs.
- **CdS** is the classical window layer; **i-ZnO** is the buffer layer in CIGS.
- **Mo** is the standard back contact for CIGS due to **ohmic MoSe₂ formation**.
- **Al** is the standard back contact for p-type c-Si (forms **Al-BSF**).
- **EVA** is the most common encapsulant but suffers from **acetic acid release → PID**.
- **POE and ionomers** are premium alternatives with superior **PID resistance and moisture barrier**.
- **Shockley–Queisser limit:** η_max ≈ 33.7% at E_g = 1.34 eV.
- **Cut-off wavelength:** $\lambda_c = 1.24 / E_g$ (μm, eV).
- **Reflectance at bare Si-air interface:** R ≈ 34% (huge optical loss without ARC).
- **Polymer PV (Module 4 integration):** Conducting polymers like **PEDOT:PSS, P3HT, PCBM** are used as HTL/ETL in organic and perovskite cells.
- **Spectral response SR(λ)** is the link between optical absorption and electrical output.
- **Standard test conditions:** AM1.5G, 1000 W/m², 25 °C cell temperature (IEC 60904).
- **IEC 61215** is the standard qualification test for c-Si terrestrial PV modules (thermal cycling, humidity freeze, mechanical load, hail impact).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexible Substrates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT757 - POLYMERS & PHOTOVOLTAIC TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flexible Substrates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flexible Substrates** in photovoltaic (PV) technology are defined as thin, mechanically compliant base materials (typically polymeric films or thin metal foils) upon which the entire active photovoltaic stack — including back contact, absorber layer, buffer/window layers, transparent conducting oxide (TCO), and front contact — is deposited to fabricate lightweight, bendable, and conformable solar cells. Unlike conventional rigid substrates (e.g., soda-lime glass with thickness ≥ 1 mm), flexible substrates have a total thickness generally in the range of **25 µm to 250 µm** and can withstand repeated bending strains (ε) typically of **0.1% to 1%** without functional degradation.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, flexible substrates are classified as a critical enabler of **third-generation and emerging PV technologies**, including Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS), Cadmium Telluride (CdTe), organic photovoltaics (OPV), dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC), and perovskite solar cells (PSCs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEPOT757 Module 4:**
> Flexible substrates are a sub-topic under *Thin-Film and Flexible Photovoltaic Devices*. Students must understand substrate types, material properties, deposition compatibility, and the engineering trade-offs between polymer films and metal foils.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine a **flexible transparent plastic sheet** like the wrapping film used to cover a notebook. Now, instead of just being a passive wrapper, this sheet becomes the *foundation* of an entire working solar cell. Photons (light particles) strike one side, and electricity flows out the other — yet the whole thing can be **rolled up like a yoga mat**.

In a traditional solar panel, the silicon wafer sits on a thick, brittle glass plate — heavy, breakable, and rigid (like a ceramic tile). A flexible substrate, in contrast, acts like a **bendable, ultra-thin, and durable plastic or metal foil canvas** that allows the solar cell to be:
- Rolled into a tube for transport
- Stuck onto curved surfaces (car roofs, building facades, drone wings)
- Manufactured in continuous roll-to-roll (R2R) processes like printing newspapers

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Example:** The *Solar Cloth* developed by UK companies uses CIGS cells on polyimide substrates — flexible enough to be sewn into tents and awnings, yet efficient enough to charge phones in sunlight.

## 1.3 Key Material Categories

Flexible substrates fall into three primary classes:

| Category | Common Materials | Typical Thickness | Max Process Temp. |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Polymer Films** | PET, PEN, Polyimide (PI/PKapton), Polyethylene Naphthalate | 25–125 µm | 150–450 °C |
| **Metal Foils** | Stainless Steel (SS), Titanium (Ti), Aluminum (Al), Molybdenum (Mo) | 25–125 µm | > 600 °C |
| **Ultra-thin Glass** | Alkali-free glass, Willow Glass® | 30–200 µm | ~ 600 °C |

## 1.4 Why Flexible Substrates Matter — Engineering Motivation

1. **Weight reduction** — Flexible CIGS modules weigh ~ **0.5–2 kg/m²** vs. ~ **10–15 kg/m²** for glass-based modules.
2. **Roll-to-Roll (R2R) manufacturing** — Enables high-throughput, low-cost production with potential module costs below **\$0.30/W**.
3. **Application diversity** — Building-Integrated PV (BIPV), Vehicle-Integrated PV (VIPV), aerospace, portable electronics, IoT sensors.
4. **High specific power** — Power-to-weight ratio can exceed **500 W/kg** for advanced flexible cells.
5. **Reduced installation cost** — No heavy mounting structures required.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Substrate Selection

Every flexible substrate must satisfy a stringent set of physical, chemical, thermal, optical, and mechanical criteria. A deficiency in any one pillar can cause catastrophic device failure.

### Pillar 1 — Thermal Stability
The substrate must survive the highest process temperature encountered during device fabrication. For CIGS cells, the absorber deposition (co-evaporation) requires substrates to withstand **400–600 °C**. For perovskite and OPV, the limit is much lower (**≤ 150 °C**). Exceeding the **glass transition temperature (Tg)** or **melting point (Tm)** causes irreversible deformation.

### Pillar 2 — Mechanical Flexibility
Quantified using the **bending strain** formula. For a film of thickness $t$ bent to a radius of curvature $R$, the maximum strain at the outer surface is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{t}{2R}$$

For the substrate to remain functional, $\varepsilon$ must be below the **critical cracking strain** of the brittle TCO/active layers on top (typically **0.2%–1%** for ITO).

### Pillar 3 — Optical Transparency
Required for **superstrate** configurations (light enters through the substrate, as in OPV and some CIGS cells). For **substrate** configurations (light enters from the top, CIGS on metal foil), transparency is unnecessary — opaque metal foils are preferred.

### Pillar 4 — Chemical & Barrier Properties
The substrate must be a barrier to **water vapor (WVTR)** and **oxygen (OTR)**. Required rates for perovskite/OPV:
- **WVTR** ≤ $10^{-4}$ g/m²/day
- **OTR** ≤ $10^{-3}$ cm³/m²/day

Polymers naturally fail here and require **multi-layer barrier coatings** (e.g., Al₂O₃/SiO₂ nanolaminates).

### Pillar 5 — Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) Match
Mismatch between substrate CTE and film CTE generates thermal stress:

$$\sigma_{thermal} = E_{film} \cdot (\alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{film}) \cdot \Delta T$$

Where $E$ is Young's modulus, $\alpha$ is CTE, and $\Delta T$ is the temperature excursion. For CIGS on Mo-coated SS foil ($\alpha_{SS} \approx 17 \times 10^{-6}$ /K), the mismatch with CIGS ($\alpha \approx 9 \times 10^{-6}$ /K) can cause film cracking — mitigated by **stress-relief layers**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master cheat sheet** for solving any KTU numerical or conceptual question on Flexible Substrates.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | Significance |
|---|----------|------------------|---------------|------|--------------|
| 1 | Bending strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{t}{2R}$ | 0.1 – 1 | % | Mechanical flexibility |
| 2 | Critical bend radius | $R_{min} = \dfrac{t}{2\varepsilon_{crack}}$ | 5 – 50 | mm | Minimum safe bend |
| 3 | Thermal stress | $\sigma = E_{f} \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | 10 – 500 | MPa | CTE mismatch stress |
| 4 | Specific power | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{m/A}$ | 100 – 1000 | W/kg | Power-to-weight ratio |
| 5 | Sheet resistance | $R_{sh} = \dfrac{\rho}{t_{film}}$ | 5 – 50 | Ω/□ | TCO performance |
| 6 | Optical transmission | $T(\lambda) = \dfrac{I_{out}}{I_{in}}$ | > 80 (visible) | % | Window/substrate |
| 7 | Power conversion efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}}$ | 5 – 22 (flex) | % | Device performance |
| 8 | CTE mismatch strain | $\varepsilon_{CTE} = \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | 0.1 – 1 | % | Cracking risk |
| 9 | Young's modulus ratio | $\dfrac{E_{sub}}{E_{film}}$ | 0.1 – 10 | — | Stress partition |
| 10 | Bending stiffness | $D = \dfrac{Et^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$ | N·m | Flexural rigidity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note:** In KTU exam answers, always state units explicitly. For a formula like $\sigma = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$, the units are: $\text{MPa} = \text{GPa} \times (10^{-6}/\text{K}) \times \text{K}$. Always convert before substituting.

## 2.3 Engineering Trade-offs — Decision Logic

| If the PV technology is… | Preferred substrate | Why? |
|--------------------------|---------------------|------|
| CIGS (high-temp co-evap.) | Stainless steel / Mo foil | Survives 500–600 °C |
| CIGS (low-temp process) | Polyimide (Kapton®) | Adequate for < 450 °C |
| CdTe | Glass, PI, or metal foil | Good for 400–500 °C |
| Perovskite | PET / PEN / Ultra-thin glass | Low-temp solution process |
| OPV (organic) | PET / PEN | Low cost, light weight |
| DSSC | TCO-coated PET/Glass | Transparent, conductive |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flexible substrates enable entirely new product categories:
- **Aerial drones** with solar wings (specific power > 300 W/kg)
- **Solar car roofs** (e.g., Hanergy, Tesla Solar Roof extensions)
- **BIPV facades** — adhered to curved concrete/metal
- **Disaster-relief power blankets**
- **Spacecraft solar arrays** — flexible blankets deployed via roll-out booms
- **Wearable PV** — sewn into backpacks, jackets

The global market for flexible PV is projected to exceed **USD 500 million by 2030**, driven primarily by BIPV and portable applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Bending Radius

**Given:** A CIGS cell on a 50 µm thick stainless steel substrate (Young's modulus $E_{SS} = 200$ GPa) coated with a 1 µm thick ITO top layer (Young's modulus $E_{ITO} = 110$ GPa, critical cracking strain $\varepsilon_{cr} = 0.4\%$).

**To find:** The minimum safe bending radius $R_{min}$ before ITO fracture.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant geometry.**
The bending strain in the outermost (ITO) layer is governed by the composite thickness (since ITO is the topmost brittle layer):

$$t_{total} = t_{SS} + t_{ITO} = 50~\mu m + 1~\mu m = 51~\mu m$$

> Conversion Logic: The ITO layer is on top of the SS substrate, so the neutral axis lies near the SS mid-plane. The distance from the neutral axis to the ITO surface is approximately $t_{total}/2$.

**Step 2 — Apply the bending strain formula.**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{t_{total}}{2R}$$

**Step 3 — Set the strain equal to the critical cracking strain.**

$$\varepsilon_{cr} = \frac{t_{total}}{2R_{min}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_{min}$.**

$$R_{min} = \frac{t_{total}}{2 \cdot \varepsilon_{cr}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert units and substitute.**

$$\varepsilon_{cr} = 0.4\% = 0.004$$

$$R_{min} = \frac{51 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m}}{2 \times 0.004}$$

$$R_{min} = \frac{51 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerical result.**

$$R_{min} = 6.375 \times 10^{-3}~\text{m} = 6.375~\text{mm}$$

> **Result:** The CIGS/SS/ITO cell can be safely bent to a radius of about **6.4 mm** before the ITO layer cracks. This is the **engineering design limit** for roll-up solar sheets.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Stress from CTE Mismatch

**Given:** A CIGS absorber is deposited on a 100 µm Mo foil (interlayer) atop a 50 µm stainless steel substrate. The deposition occurs at 550 °C and cools to 25 °C. The relevant CTE values are:

- $\alpha_{CIGS} = 9.0 \times 10^{-6}$ /K
- $\alpha_{SS} = 17.3 \times 10^{-6}$ /K
- $E_{CIGS} = 70$ GPa

**To find:** The thermal stress generated in the CIGS layer on cooling.

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature difference.**

$$\Delta T = T_{deposition} - T_{room} = 550 - 25 = 525~\text{K}$$

> Conversion Logic: The film solidifies and bonds to the substrate at 550 °C. On cooling, both film and substrate want to contract, but the CTE mismatch creates in-plane stress.

**Step 2 — Calculate the differential CTE.**

$$\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{SS} - \alpha_{CIGS}$$

$$\Delta \alpha = (17.3 - 9.0) \times 10^{-6} = 8.3 \times 10^{-6}~\text{/K}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the biaxial strain induced.**

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\varepsilon_{th} = 8.3 \times 10^{-6} \times 525$$

$$\varepsilon_{th} = 4.358 \times 10^{-3} = 0.4358\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute the thermal stress (assuming elastic behavior).**

$$\sigma_{th} = E_{CIGS} \cdot \varepsilon_{th}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 70 \times 10^{9}~\text{Pa} \times 4.358 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 305.1 \times 10^{6}~\text{Pa} \approx 305~\text{MPa}$$

> **Result:** A thermal stress of **~ 305 MPa** develops in the CIGS film. Since CIGS has a fracture strength of only **~ 200–500 MPa**, this stress is significant and can cause micro-crack formation. This is why **Mo interlayers** are essential — they act as compliant stress-relief layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering mitigation:** Inserting a thin (0.5–1 µm) Mo layer with intermediate CTE (≈ 5 × 10⁻⁶ /K) reduces the differential strain to tolerable limits.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Major Substrate Classes

| Property | Polyimide (Kapton®) | Stainless Steel (304) | PET (Mylar®) |
|----------|--------------------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Thickness (typical)** | 25–125 µm | 25–125 µm | 50–250 µm |
| **Density** | 1.42 g/cm³ | 8.0 g/cm³ | 1.38 g/cm³ |
| **Tg / Tm** | Tg ≈ 360 °C (Kapton HN) | Tm ≈ 1400 °C | Tg ≈ 70 °C, Tm ≈ 260 °C |
| **Max process temp.** | ~ 450 °C (short) | > 800 °C | ~ 150 °C |
| **CTE (× 10⁻⁶ /K)** | 20–30 | 17.3 | 60–70 |
| **Tensile strength** | 200–300 MPa | 500–700 MPa | 50–80 MPa |
| **Optical transparency** | Amber (semi-transparent) | Opaque | Transparent |
| **WVTR (uncoated)** | ~ 1 g/m²/day | 0 (perfect barrier) | ~ 5–10 g/m²/day |
| **Electrical** | Insulator | Conductor (needs insulating layer) | Insulator |
| **Cost (relative)** | High | Medium–High | Very Low |
| **Surface roughness** | Low (Ra < 50 nm) | High (needs polishing) | Low |
| **Best-suited PV tech.** | CIGS, OPV, Perovskite | CIGS, CdTe | OPV, Perovskite (low-T) |
| **Flexibility (ε at R=10mm)** | < 0.2% strain | < 0.2% strain | < 0.4% strain |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem — Specific Power Calculation

**Given:** A flexible perovskite module of dimensions **1 m × 0.5 m** produces **50 W** under AM 1.5G illumination. The substrate is **PET (100 µm thick)**, the active layers add another **2 µm**. Total device density is dominated by PET ($\rho = 1.38$ g/cm³).

**Step 1 — Compute the substrate mass per unit area.**

$$m_{sub} = \rho_{PET} \cdot t_{PET} = 1.38~\text{g/cm}^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-4}~\text{cm}$$

$$m_{sub} = 1.38 \times 10^{-2}~\text{g/cm}^2 = 0.0138~\text{g/cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kg/m².**

$$m_{sub} = 0.0138 \times 10^{4}~\text{g/m}^2 = 138~\text{g/m}^2 = 0.138~\text{kg/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute total area.**

$$A = 1 \times 0.5 = 0.5~\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute total mass (ignoring active layers).**

$$M = m_{sub} \cdot A = 0.138 \times 0.5 = 0.069~\text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the specific power.**

$$P_{sp} = \frac{P_{out}}{M} = \frac{50~\text{W}}{0.069~\text{kg}}$$

$$P_{sp} = 724.6~\text{W/kg}$$

> **Result:** The specific power is **~ 725 W/kg**, nearly **10× higher** than conventional glass-based modules (~ 70 W/kg). This enables UAV and aerospace applications.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Substrate Classification Mind-Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FLEXIBLE SUBSTRATES] --> B[Polymer Films]
    A --> C[Metal Foils]
    A --> D[Ultra-Thin Glass]
    A --> E[Composite Hybrids]

    B --> B1[PET - Polyethylene Terephthalate]
    B --> B2[PEN - Polyethylene Naphthalate]
    B --> B3[PI - Polyimide Kapton]
    B --> B4[Fluoropolymers - ETFE PVDF]

    C --> C1[Stainless Steel SS304/SS430]
    C --> C2[Titanium Foil]
    C --> C3[Aluminum Foil]
    C --> C4[Molybdenum Foil]

    D --> D1[Willow Glass Corning]
    D --> D2[Alkali-Free Glass AF32]

    E --> E1[PI coated Metal Foil]
    E --> E2[Barrier-coated Polymer]
    E --> E3[Laminate Stack]
```

## 4.2 Device Cross-Section — CIGS on Flexible Substrate

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Incident Light] --> B[Anti-Reflection Coating MgF2]
    B --> C[Front Contact ZnO:Al TCO]
    C --> D[Window Layer i-ZnO or CdS]
    D --> E[Absorber CIGS]
    E --> F[Back Contact Mo]
    F --> G[Insulating Layer SiO2 or Na compound]
    G --> H[Flexible Substrate SS or PI]
    H --> I[Encapsulant EVA]
    I --> J[Backsheet Polymer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Light enters from the top. Each layer serves a precise photovoltaic function. The flexible substrate (H) bears all mechanical and thermal load. The Mo layer (F) doubles as a stress-relief and back-contact reflector.

## 4.3 Substrate Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Choose Flexible Substrate] --> B{Max Process Temperature?}
    B -->|Below 150 C| C[Polymer Film Zone]
    B -->|150 to 450 C| D[Polyimide or PEN]
    B -->|Above 450 C| E[Metal Foil Required]

    C --> C1{Transparent Needed?}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2[PET or PEN]
    C1 -->|No| C3[Opaque Laminated Film]

    D --> D1{Optical transparency?}
    D1 -->|Yes| D2[PEN with barrier coating]
    D1 -->|No| D3[Polyimide Kapton]

    E --> E1{Need insulating surface?}
    E1 -->|Yes| E2[SS with SiO2 insulating layer]
    E1 -->|No| E3[Titanium or Molybdenum foil]

    C2 --> F[End: Choose substrate]
    C3 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
```

## 4.4 Roll-to-Roll (R2R) Manufacturing Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[Unwinding Roll Substrate] --> S1[Cleaning Station]
    S1 --> S2[Back Contact Deposition Sputtering]
    S2 --> S3[Absorber Deposition Co-evap or Printing]
    S3 --> S4[Buffer Layer CBD or ALD]
    S4 --> S5[Window Layer TCO Sputtering]
    S5 --> S6[Patterning Laser Scribing]
    S6 --> S7[Encapsulation Lamination]
    S7 --> S8[Module Cutting]
    S8 --> R2[Winding Roll Finished Product]
```

> **Engineering note:** The R2R process transforms a roll of flexible substrate into a roll of finished solar module — analogous to printing a newspaper. Throughputs can exceed **30 m/min** in industrial lines.

## 4.5 Failure Mode Causal Chain — Why Substrates Fail

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Substrate Failure Root Causes] --> B[Thermal Overload]
    A --> C[Mechanical Over-flex]
    A --> D[Moisture Ingress]
    A --> E[CTE Mismatch]

    B --> B1[Tg or Tm Exceeded]
    B1 --> B2[Shrinkage or Wrinkling]
    B2 --> B3[Film Delamination]

    C --> C1[Bend Radius Too Small]
    C1 --> C2[Cracking in TCO]
    C2 --> C3[Shunt Leakage Paths]

    D --> D1[Insufficient WVTR Barrier]
    D1 --> D2[Oxidation of Absorber]
    D2 --> D3[Efficiency Degradation]

    E --> E1[Lateral Stress Buildup]
    E1 --> E2[Micro-crack Network]
    E2 --> B3
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define a flexible substrate in the context of photovoltaic technology. Mention any two commonly used polymer substrates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A flexible substrate is a thin, mechanically compliant base material (thickness ~ 25–250 µm) on which the complete PV device stack is deposited, enabling bendable, lightweight, and conformable solar cells. It must provide thermal stability, mechanical flexibility, and barrier properties to moisture/oxygen.
*Common polymer substrates (any two):*
1. **Polyimide (PI / Kapton®)** — high Tg (~360 °C), used for CIGS.
2. **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET / Mylar®)** — low cost, transparent, used for OPV/perovskite.
3. (Acceptable) Polyethylene Naphthalate (PEN) — better thermal stability than PET.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark for first substrate, 1 mark for second substrate with reason.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State the formula for bending strain in a thin film and explain how the minimum safe bending radius is determined for a flexible solar cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The bending strain at the outermost surface of a film bent to radius $R$ is:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{t}{2R}$$
where $t$ is the total stack thickness. The **minimum safe bending radius** is determined by equating this strain to the **critical cracking strain** of the most brittle top layer (typically the TCO):
$$R_{min} = \frac{t_{total}}{2 \cdot \varepsilon_{crack}}$$
Below this radius, the brittle TCO/active layer cracks, creating shunt paths and degrading efficiency. For example, with $t = 50~\mu m$ and $\varepsilon_{cr} = 0.4\%$, $R_{min} = 6.25~\text{mm}$.

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark derivation of $R_{min}$, 1 mark worked example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, describe the device architecture of a CIGS thin-film solar cell on a flexible stainless steel substrate. Explain the function of each layer.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The flexible substrate is 50 µm thick stainless steel (Young's modulus E = 200 GPa, CTE = 17.3 × 10⁻⁶ /K). The CIGS absorber (E = 70 GPa, CTE = 9.0 × 10⁻⁶ /K) is deposited at 550 °C and cooled to 25 °C. Calculate the thermal stress developed in the CIGS absorber.

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram (2 marks):**

| Layer | Material | Function |
|-------|----------|----------|
| 1 | MgF₂ (anti-reflection) | Reduces reflection losses |
| 2 | ZnO:Al (TCO) | Front transparent electrode |
| 3 | i-ZnO / CdS | Window/buffer, p-n junction partner |
| 4 | CIGS (p-type) | Light absorber (Eg ≈ 1.1–1.7 eV) |
| 5 | Mo (back contact) | Hole collection, stress relief |
| 6 | SiO₂ or NaF | Na diffusion barrier, insulation |
| 7 | Stainless steel (50 µm) | Flexible mechanical support |
| 8 | EVA encapsulant | Moisture protection |
| 9 | Polymer backsheet | Mechanical/UV protection |

**Description of each layer (5 marks):**
1. **Anti-reflection coating (MgF₂):** Minimizes reflection losses at the air-cell interface.
2. **TCO (ZnO:Al):** Allows light in, collects current laterally.
3. **Buffer (CdS/i-ZnO):** Forms p-n junction with CIGS, band alignment.
4. **CIGS absorber:** Converts photons to electron-hole pairs.
5. **Mo back contact:** Ohmic contact, reflects unabsorbed photons.
6. **Insulating layer (SiO₂):** Prevents shunting between Mo and SS, controls Na diffusion.
7. **Stainless steel substrate:** Provides mechanical support, high-temp stability.
8. **Encapsulation (EVA + backsheet):** Long-term environmental protection.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Temperature difference (1 mark):**
$$\Delta T = 550 - 25 = 525~\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Differential CTE (1 mark):**
$$\Delta \alpha = (17.3 - 9.0) \times 10^{-6} = 8.3 \times 10^{-6}~\text{/K}$$

**Step 3 — Induced thermal strain (1 mark):**
$$\varepsilon_{th} = \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 8.3 \times 10^{-6} \times 525 = 4.358 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Thermal stress calculation (2 marks):**
$$\sigma_{th} = E_{CIGS} \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = 70 \times 10^{9} \times 4.358 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\sigma_{th} = 305.1 \times 10^{6}~\text{Pa} \approx 305~\text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
- Since CIGS fracture strength is ~ 200–500 MPa, the induced stress of 305 MPa is significant and risks micro-crack formation.
- **Mitigation:** Use a thin Mo interlayer (CTE ≈ 5 × 10⁻⁶ /K) as a stress-relief buffer between SS and CIGS.
- **Engineering implication:** Suggests the need for a controlled cooling ramp and compliance interlayers in commercial CIGS-on-SS devices.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** [7 Marks] List the essential properties required of a flexible substrate for photovoltaic applications. Compare the suitability of **polyimide (Kapton®)** and **stainless steel (SS 304)** as flexible substrates for CIGS solar cells.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A flexible OPV cell is fabricated on a PET substrate of thickness 100 µm and density 1.38 g/cm³. The cell area is 0.5 m² and the output power is 12 W under standard illumination. Calculate the **specific power (W/kg)** of this device. If the substrate is bent to a radius of 10 mm, what is the maximum strain in the substrate? Comment on whether ITO (with $\varepsilon_{cr} = 0.4\%$) will crack.

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Essential properties of a flexible substrate (3 marks — list any 5):**
1. High thermal stability (Tg / Tm above process temperature)
2. Mechanical flexibility (low bending strain at small radii)
3. Chemical inertness (resistance to acids, alkalis, solvents)
4. Low surface roughness (Ra < 50 nm for thin-film deposition)
5. Good barrier properties (low WVTR, low OTR)
6. CTE matched to active layers
7. High optical transparency (for superstrate configuration)
8. Light weight and low cost

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Polyimide (Kapton®) | Stainless Steel (SS 304) |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Max process temp. | ~ 450 °C | > 800 °C |
| Density | 1.42 g/cm³ (light) | 8.0 g/cm³ (heavy) |
| Optical | Semi-transparent amber | Opaque |
| Electrical | Insulator (good) | Conductor (needs insulator) |
| CTE | 20–30 × 10⁻⁶ /K | 17.3 × 10⁻⁶ /K |
| Surface | Smooth (Ra < 50 nm) | Needs polishing |
| Cost | High | Medium |
| Best for CIGS? | Yes (low-temp route, < 450 °C) | Yes (high-temp route, 550–600 °C) |

**Conclusion:** For high-efficiency CIGS with co-evaporation at 550 °C, **SS 304 is preferred** despite its weight penalty. For low-temperature (< 450 °C) CIGS or flexible OPV/perovskite, **polyimide is the preferred choice** due to its light weight, electrical insulation, and surface smoothness.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Mass per unit area of PET (1 mark):**
$$m_{sub} = \rho_{PET} \cdot t = 1.38 \times 1.38~\text{g/cm}^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-4}~\text{cm}$$
$$m_{sub} = 1.38 \times 10^{-2}~\text{g/cm}^2 = 138~\text{g/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total mass (1 mark):**
$$M = 138 \times 0.5 = 69~\text{g} = 0.069~\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Specific power (1 mark):**
$$P_{sp} = \frac{P_{out}}{M} = \frac{12}{0.069} = 173.9~\text{W/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Bending strain at R = 10 mm (2 marks):**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{t}{2R} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$\varepsilon = \frac{10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-2}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5\%$$

**Step 5 — Cracking analysis (2 marks):**
- Computed strain: $\varepsilon = 0.5\%$
- ITO critical cracking strain: $\varepsilon_{cr} = 0.4\%$
- Since $0.5\% > 0.4\%$, the ITO layer **will crack** at a 10 mm bend radius.
- **Recommended minimum bend radius:**
$$R_{min} = \frac{t}{2\varepsilon_{cr}} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 0.004} = 12.5~\text{mm}$$
- The device must be bent to a radius **≥ 12.5 mm** to preserve ITO integrity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion is the #1 mark-loser.** Students frequently forget to convert µm to m and mm to m. Always write $100~\mu m = 100 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m}$ explicitly in your solution.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse bending strain with thermal strain.** They use similar symbols but have entirely different physical origins.
> 3. **For multi-layer stacks**, the strain must be calculated using the *total* stack thickness, not just the substrate alone.
> 4. **Always state the cracking criterion clearly** — examiners look for the explicit comparison $\varepsilon$ vs. $\varepsilon_{cr}$.
> 5. **In Question A part (a)**, marks are awarded for both the *diagram* and the *layer functions*. A diagram without labels scores only 1 out of 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Flexible substrates are thin (25–250 µm) base layers enabling bendable PV cells; key for CIGS, CdTe, OPV, perovskite, DSSC.
- [ ] **Core formula (must memorize):** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{t}{2R}$ and $R_{min} = \dfrac{t}{2\varepsilon_{cr}}$
- [ ] **Thermal stress formula:** $\sigma = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$
- [ ] **Three substrate classes:** Polymer (PET/PEN/PI), Metal foil (SS/Ti/Mo), Ultra-thin glass.
- [ ] **Polyimide (Kapton®):** Tg ~ 360 °C, amber, insulator, smooth, expensive — used for CIGS/OPV.
- [ ] **PET:** Tg ~ 70 °C, transparent, cheap, low-temp only — used for OPV/perovskite.
- [ ] **Stainless steel:** High-temp tolerant (> 600 °C), opaque, conductive, heavy — best for high-temp CIGS.
- [ ] **CIGS preferred substrate:** Stainless steel for high-temp, polyimide for low-temp.
- [ ] **Barrier requirement:** WVTR < $10^{-4}$ g/m²/day for perovskite/OPV.
- [ ] **CTE mismatch** is the root cause of micro-cracks; mitigated by Mo interlayer.
- [ ] **Roll-to-Roll (R2R)** manufacturing is the key cost-reduction enabler for flexible PV.
- [ ] **Specific power** of flexible cells: 100–1000 W/kg vs. ~ 70 W/kg for rigid Si modules.
- [ ] **Critical cracking strains:** ITO ~ 0.4%, AZO ~ 0.3%, CIGS thin film ~ 0.5–1%.
- [ ] **Always quote units** in numerical answers (µm, mm, MPa, W/kg, /K).
- [ ] **Light weight advantage:** Flexible PV weighs ~ 0.5–2 kg/m² vs. 10–15 kg/m² for rigid.
- [ ] **Real-world applications:** BIPV, VIPV, UAV wings, spacecraft, portable power.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEPOT757 – Polymers & Photovoltaic Technology**, **Silver Back Electrodes** refer to the metallic rear-side current-collecting contact of a photovoltaic (PV) cell, fabricated by screen-printing a silver-containing thick-film paste (typically a blend of **Ag micro-flakes**, **lead-borosilicate glass frit**, an **organic binder/vehicle**, and **solvents**) onto the rear surface of a silicon wafer (most commonly a p-type **mono- or multi-crystalline Si** wafer with an Al back-surface field, Al-BSF) followed by a co-firing step in an infrared belt furnace at peak temperatures of approximately **700 °C – 850 °C**.

The silver back electrode fulfils three engineering functions:
1. **Mechanical / Adhesion Function**: It forms a robust solderable termination to the rear tabbing ribbon during module assembly.
2. **Electrical Function**: It collects the **hole-current** (majority carriers in p-type Si) laterally and provides a low-resistance ohmic pathway to the external circuit.
3. **Optical / Reflective Function (in bifacial cells)**: In **bifacial PV modules**, the rear silver is engineered to reflect infrared light back into the absorber, increasing photocurrent generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Snapshot (verbatim-grade):**
> *"The rear-side metallization in a crystalline-silicon solar cell is screen-printed using Ag paste containing glass frit for ohmic contact formation and is co-fired with the front Ag grid and the Al rear layer to produce a solderable, low-resistivity back electrode."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **shopping mall with thousands of cash registers** (these are the p–n junctions across the wafer, each generating a tiny photocurrent). All these cash registers are connected by a **paved road network on the back of the building** (the silver back electrode) that funnels money to a single, central bank vault (the external tabbing wire).

- If the road is made of **mud** → huge electrical losses (high resistivity).
- If the road is made of **concrete with potholes** → poor adhesion, ribbons peel off.
- A **smooth silver road with a sealed tarmac (glass-frit bonding layer)** is exactly what a silver back electrode is — a low-loss, well-adhered highway for current collection.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bold Constants to Memorise)

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Resistivity of bulk silver (Ag)** | **1.59 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** |
| **Resistivity of fired Ag paste film** | **~ 3 – 6 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** |
| **Specific contact resistivity (ρ_c)** | **≤ 5 mΩ·cm²** (good ohmic) |
| **Silver loading per cell** | **80 – 150 mg** |
| **Firing peak temperature** | **700 – 850 °C** |
| **Drying temperature (post-print)** | **150 – 200 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> Pure silver has the **lowest electrical resistivity of any metal at room temperature**, which is precisely why even expensive Ag paste is preferred over cheaper Cu paste (which oxidises and requires barrier layers such as Ni).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Silver Back Electrode

The fired silver back contact is **not a monolithic layer of silver**. After co-firing through a thin **SiN_x :H ARC / passivation layer** (where present on bifacial cells), it resolves into the following sub-layers from the silicon outward:

1. **Ag crystallites / nano-Ag-rich layer** — in direct physical contact with the p-Si, forming the ohmic junction.
2. **Glass-frit bonding layer** — a thin (50–200 nm) lead-borosilicate glassy region containing dissolved Ag colloids.
3. **Bulk porous Ag matrix** — sintered silver flakes providing mechanical strength.
4. **Outer Ag skin** — dense silver layer enabling wire/ribbon soldering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **glass-frit mechanism of contact formation** is critical for KTU Module 4. During firing, the **PbO–B₂O₃–SiO₂** frit **etches through the SiN_x ARC**, dissolves a thin Si layer underneath, and re-deposits Ag nano-crystallites in the etched pits — these nano-Ag precipitates form **point-contact ohmic connections** to the p-type emitter / BSF region.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Firing Mechanism

- **Step 1 — Drying (150–200 °C)**: Organic vehicle evaporates, Ag flakes form a porous skeleton.
- **Step 2 — Burn-out (~ 400 °C)**: Remaining organics combust; pore network stabilises.
- **Step 3 — Frit softening (~ 550 – 650 °C)**: Glass frit becomes viscous; begins to **wet** the underlying dielectric.
- **Step 4 — Etching of SiN_x (~ 650 – 750 °C)**: PbO in the frit reacts with SiN_x:

$$ \text{PbO} + \text{SiN}_x \longrightarrow \text{Pb-silicate (liquid)} + \text{N}_2 \uparrow $$

- **Step 5 — Ag precipitation**: Metallic Ag dissolves into the molten glass:

$$ \text{Ag}_2\text{O} + \text{PbO} \rightleftharpoons \text{PbO}\cdot\text{Ag (silicate melt)} $$

On cooling, Ag **re-nucleates** as nano-crystallites at the Si/glass interface — these are the actual electrical contact points.

- **Step 6 — Solidification (~ < 600 °C)**: Glass vitrifies; Ag matrix densifies.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_c = \left( \dfrac{dV}{dJ} \right)_{V=0}$ | Specific contact resistivity | $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$ | Quality of ohmic contact |
| 2 | $R_s = R_{\text{emitter}} + R_{\text{contact,front}} + R_{\text{bulk}} + R_{\text{contact,back}} + R_{\text{metal,back}}$ | Total series resistance | $\Omega$ | Efficiency loss calculation |
| 3 | $\Delta \eta_{\text{series}} = \dfrac{J_{mp}^2 \, R_s}{V_{oc} \, J_{sc}}$ | Efficiency penalty from $R_s$ | dimensionless | KTU numerical problems |
| 4 | $R_{\text{metal,back}} = \dfrac{\rho_{Ag}}{t} \cdot \dfrac{1}{W}$ | Sheet resistance of Ag back film | $\Omega/\square$ | Back contact design |
| 5 | $FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc}+0.72)}{v_{oc}+1}$ | Ideal fill factor (empirical Green) | — | Used as baseline before $R_s$ penalty |
| 6 | $FF = FF_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{R_s J_{sc}}{V_{oc}} \right)$ | Fill factor with $R_s$ loss | — | Computes real FF |
| 7 | $m_{\text{Ag,total}} = N_{\text{cells}} \times (m_{\text{front}} + m_{\text{back}})$ | Ag consumption per module | g | Cost analysis |
| 8 | $L_{\text{diff}} = \sqrt{D_p \, \tau_p}$ | Minority carrier diffusion length | cm | Back-surface field effect |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{BSF}} \approx 1 - \dfrac{S_{\text{eff}} L_{\text{diff}}}{D_p}$ | BSF rear passivation effectiveness | — | With Al-BSF |
| 10 | $R_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{q \cdot k_{\text{Ag}}}$ | Thermal resistance via Ag | K/W | Reliability studies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always use `\vert` instead of `|` (pipe) inside any markdown-table cell when writing absolute values, e.g., write `\vert V_{oc} \vert` not `|V_{oc}|`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial c-Si PV manufacturing**: > 95 % of all commercial silicon solar cells currently use Ag-paste screen printing for *both* front and rear metallisation. The rear Ag is integral to module-level **solderability** with Cu ribbons coated with Sn/Ag/Sn or Sn/Pb alloys.
- **Bifacial PV**: The rear Ag layer is deposited over the **Al-BSF or PERC/PERT rear dielectric**, often as a discontinuous pattern of Ag pads with Al covering the rest — this combines **internal optical reflection** with **solderable bond pads**.
- **TOPCon and SHJ (Silicon Heterojunction)**: In these next-gen architectures, Ag paste is *still* the workhorse back contact, though SHJ requires **low-temperature (< 200 °C) Ag pastes with Ag-flake + acrylic binder** — a major topic of recent industrial R&D.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Power Loss Due to Back Silver Electrode Resistance

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark derivation):**
*"A 156 mm × 156 mm pseudo-square c-Si solar cell has a rear full-area Ag contact of resistivity $\rho_{Ag} = 5 \times 10^{-8} \ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$, fired thickness $t = 25 \ \mu\text{m}$. The cell delivers $J_{mp} = 33 \ \text{mA/cm}^2$ at the maximum power point. If the rear tabbing ribbon is soldered only along one edge (worst-case lateral current path), estimate the additional power loss."*

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup**

The cell is modelled as a square of side $a = 156 \ \text{mm} = 0.156 \ \text{m}$. Worst-case lateral path = half the cell side:

$$ L_{\text{lat}} = \dfrac{a}{2} = \dfrac{0.156}{2} = 0.078 \ \text{m} $$

The cross-section of the Ag layer carrying the current:

$$ A_{\text{Ag}} = a \cdot t = 0.156 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 3.9 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2 $$

**Step 2 — Compute Lateral Resistance of the Back Ag Film**

Using $R = \rho L / A$:

$$ R_{\text{back,lat}} = \dfrac{\rho_{Ag} \cdot L_{\text{lat}}}{A_{Ag}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.078}{3.9 \times 10^{-6}} $$

$$ R_{\text{back,lat}} = \dfrac{3.9 \times 10^{-9}}{3.9 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \ \Omega $$

So $R_{\text{back,lat}} \approx 1 \ \text{m}\Omega$.

**Step 3 — Convert Current Density to Total Cell Current**

Cell area:

$$ A_{\text{cell}} = a^2 = (0.156)^2 = 0.024336 \ \text{m}^2 = 243.36 \ \text{cm}^2 $$

Current at MPP:

$$ I_{mp} = J_{mp} \cdot A_{\text{cell}} = 33 \times 10^{-3} \times 243.36 = 8.031 \ \text{A} $$

**Step 4 — Power Loss**

$$ P_{\text{loss,back}} = I_{mp}^2 \cdot R_{\text{back,lat}} = (8.031)^2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ P_{\text{loss,back}} = 64.50 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0645 \ \text{W} $$

**Step 5 — Efficiency Penalty**

Assuming the cell's nominal output at MPP is $P_{mp} = 4.5 \ \text{W}$ (a typical value for this cell size):

$$ \Delta \eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{loss,back}}}{P_{mp}} = \dfrac{0.0645}{4.5} \approx 1.43 \ \% $$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is why industrial cells always use multiple busbars (MBB — 9 to 16 BB architecture) on the rear** — the lateral path $L_{\text{lat}}$ is divided by the number of busbars, reducing $R_{\text{back,lat}}$ proportionally.

### 3.2 Derivation — Minimum Specific Contact Resistivity for a Silver Back Electrode

**Goal:** Show that the *ohmic* requirement on $\rho_c$ at the Ag / p-Si interface can be derived from the requirement that voltage drop across the contact be less than 1 % of $V_{oc}$.

$$ V_{\text{drop,contact}} = J_{mp} \cdot \rho_c \leq 0.01 \cdot V_{oc} $$

Rearranging for $\rho_c$:

$$ \rho_c \leq \dfrac{0.01 \cdot V_{oc}}{J_{mp}} $$

Substituting typical values $V_{oc} = 0.65 \ \text{V}$, $J_{mp} = 33 \ \text{mA/cm}^2 = 0.033 \ \text{A/cm}^2$:

$$ \rho_c \leq \dfrac{0.01 \times 0.65}{0.033} = \dfrac{0.0065}{0.033} = 0.197 \ \Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2 $$

Therefore, an *acceptable* contact should have $\rho_c \leq 0.2 \ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$; **best-in-class silver back contacts achieve $\rho_c \leq 5 \ \text{m}\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$**, two orders of magnitude better than the threshold.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Silver Back vs Alternative Back Contacts

| Property | **Ag Screen-Printed (Standard)** | **Cu Plated (Experimental)** | **Al Full-BSF (Legacy)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistivity ($\mu\Omega\cdot$cm) | **3 – 6** | **1.7** | **2.7** |
| Solderable? | ✅ Yes | ❌ Requires Ni/Au barrier | ❌ Difficult |
| Cost per cell | High | Low | Low |
| Firing temp (°C) | 700 – 850 | RT (electroless) | 700 – 850 |
| Reliability | Excellent | Emerging concern | Adhesion limited |
| Compatible with bifacial? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Silver-saving potential | — | 100 % | 100 % |
